days_plan.itin_title: Southern Corsica Travel Guide: Corsican Mountains & Gulf Heritage Itinerary
Complete Tour of Corsica

visits.text: The kitchen garden of Bonnétable is one of Sarthe's most pleasant surprises. Laid out in 1885 over 13,000 m² by brothers Denis and Eugène Bühler—the landscape architects who also designed Thabor Park in Rennes—it was commissioned by the Duke of La Rochefoucauld-Doudeauville for his Château de Bonnétable. Surrounded by walls covered with old roses and clematis, it houses a heritage orchard, heirloom vegetables, a Mediterranean garden, a medicinal herb garden, and magnificent greenhouses. At the back of the park, a Bollée windmill from the late 19th century—a wind turbine invented in 1868 by Ernest Sylvain Bollée to pump water—is one of the finest examples still standing in France. Free access during tourist office hours.

The Château de Bonnétable, built in the 15th century then remodeled in the 19th for the Dukes of Doudeauville, dominates the village with its re-dug moats and park. The old stables, with their preserved stalls and saddlery, now house the tourist office and a temporary exhibition hall.

The Church of Saint-Sulpice, whose origins date back to the 11th century, preserves a wooden organ from 1850 and beautiful 19th-century stained glass windows illustrating the life of Saint Sulpice. The 1856 covered market, on Place du Vieux Château, deserves a look for its campanile with two clocks.

activities.text: The Transvap is a tourist railway operated by passionate volunteers who run steam locomotives and vintage railcars on the 17 km of the former Mamers-Saint-Calais line—nicknamed the Dukes' line because it was originally financed by the region's château owners. The journey between Bonnétable and Beillé crosses the Jousse valley through the Sarthe bocage. Trains run in summer on Sundays and holidays. Check the calendar before coming—the Transvap sometimes has technical closures.

The Forest of Bonnétable extends over several hundred hectares north of the village. Marked walking and cycling circuits allow you to stroll beneath centuries-old oaks and beeches. It's an ideal outing to finish this circuit gently, away from the bustle, before rejoining Le Mans 30 km away.

days_plan.itin_title: Sarthe Road Trip: Le Mans and the Loir Valley

visits.text: Bonneval, nicknamed the Little Venice of Beauce for its canals fed by the Loir. This medieval town founded in 857 around Saint-Florentin Abbey has preserved its water-filled moats, ramparts, and picturesque streets. An oasis of greenery in the heart of the Beauce plain.

Former Benedictine Abbey of Saint-Florentin-et-Saint-Hilaire, classified as a historic monument since 1883. The pointed-arch entrance gate from the 13th century remains, topped by the 15th-century abbot's lodging, with its checkerboard wall of white stones, bricks, and flint flanked by three towers.

Church of Notre-Dame de Bonneval, a jewel of early Gothic architecture built between the 12th and 13th centuries. It houses two paintings from the abbey's former refectory, including a copy of a canvas by Nicolas Poussin. Its bell tower dominates the medieval town's rooftops.

Medieval fortifications of Bonneval with the 15th-century Porte Saint-Roch and the 13th-century King's Tower, a former 22-meter-high keep. The ramparts still encircle the old town, offering a walk along the flower-lined moats.

activities.text: Electric boat ride on Bonneval's canals, a unique experience to discover the town from the water. No license required, these small boats allow you to pass under medieval bridges and admire ancestral wash houses. In the evening, the illuminations of 'Bonneval, lights along the water' enhance the journey.
Walk along Bonneval's medieval moats, where ducks and moorhens come to greet strollers. The 19th-century wash houses punctuate the route while old houses reflect in the calm waters of the Loir.
Tasting of local Beauce products at Vergers de Beauce, a shop offering exclusively local products in short supply chains. A gourmet stop to discover the authentic flavors of Eure-et-Loir.

days_plan.itin_title: Eure-et-Loir Circuit: Chartres, Châteaux & Proust

visits.text: Bonneval-sur-Arc is the only Savoyard village classified among France's Most Beautiful Villages. Perched at 1,800 m altitude at the far end of Haute-Maurienne, it lines up stone houses with 80-cm-thick walls, roofed with lauze slabs on larch timbers. No antenna or satellite dish disturbs the village silhouette.

The parish church raises its tower bell-spire of stone above the rooftops. Originally a simple chapel, it was enlarged in the 16th century to accommodate the parish newly established by the Bishop of Maurienne.

Push on to the hamlet of l'Écot, 4 km from the village (2,000 m). Classified as a historic site in 1971, this cluster of abandoned chalets and its Chapelle Sainte-Marguerite (Romanesque, adorned with medieval frescoes) served as a film set for Belle et Sébastien adaptations.

The Espace Neige et Montagne recounts daily life in the high mountains of yesteryear: wood crafts, pottery, weaving, and the beginnings of mountaineering at Bonneval. The old-timers heated themselves with "grébons," molded briquettes made from sheep dung.

Stop at the cooperative fromagerie to taste Bleu de Bonneval, a local blue-veined cheese, and Beaufort AOP d'alpage. Cows graze here between 1,800 and 2,500 m altitude, in the pastures of Vanoise National Park.

activities.text: Hike to the Cirque des Évettes. From l'Écot, the trail climbs alongside the Arc torrent to a glacial amphitheater dominated by the Albaron (3,637 m). Facing the Évettes glacier, silence and immensity reward every step.
Family walk on the Sentier des Agneaux, between the village and the hamlet of l'Écot. The path follows pastures where flocks spend the summer, with views of the border peaks of Italy.
Canyoning in the gorges of l'Écot. One of the Alps' finest spots: descent in the Arc's waters with natural slides, jumps, and rappels supervised by instructors.

days_plan.itin_title: Savoie from Lakes to Glaciers: Yenne to Haute-Maurienne

visits.text: Drive to Bordeaux (3h15 via the A10). Get settled and set out to explore the historic center, a UNESCO World Heritage site since 2007. Start with the Place de la Bourse, designed by architect Jacques Gabriel between 1730 and 1755—it's the first open square in the history of French urbanism. Opposite, the Water Mirror (3,450 m², the world's largest) reflects the 18th-century façades. Every 15 minutes, 900 misters create a fog effect—magical late in the day.

Walk up to the Saint-André Cathedral, begun in the 11th century and expanded through the 15th. This is where Eleanor of Aquitaine married the future Louis VII in 1137. Climb to the top of the Pey-Berland tower (66 meters, 231 steps) for the panorama over Bordeaux's rooftops.

Continue to the Grand Théâtre (1780), one of Europe's finest. Its 12 Corinthian columns and monumental staircase inspired Charles Garnier for the Paris Opera. Guided tours are rare—check availability.

Morning at the Marché des Capucins, the "belly of Bordeaux." This covered market operates daily except Monday. You'll taste Arcachon oysters, entrecôte bordelaise, canelés, and cheeses. The atmosphere is lively and working-class—Bordelais do their shopping here.

Head to La Cité du Vin, opened in 2016 on the banks of the Garonne. It's not a conventional museum but an immersive experience in the world of wine. The glass-and-aluminum building designed by XTU Architects evokes a twisted vine trunk. The interactive journey lasts 2 hours. The belvedere on the 8th floor offers a panoramic view and includes a tasting with your ticket.

Late afternoon, wander down rue Sainte-Catherine, Europe's longest pedestrian street (1.2 km). Pass beneath the Grosse Cloche, a medieval belfry from the 15th century with its two 40-meter towers—the 7,800 kg bell rings only for special occasions. The Saint-Pierre quarter behind it is the most charming in old Bordeaux.

Head to Saint-Émilion this morning, 45 minutes from Bordeaux. This medieval village perched amid the vines has been a UNESCO World Heritage site since 1999—the world's first "viticultural jurisdiction" to receive this distinction. Start with the Monolithic Church, entirely carved out of limestone rock between the 8th and 12th centuries. It's Europe's largest underground church—38 meters long, 20 wide. Accessible only by guided tour (departure from the tourist office).

Stroll the village's cobbled lanes—the view from the King's Tower (13th-century keep) sweeps across vineyards to the horizon. The ramparts, cloisters, and golden stone houses create an atmosphere that the tourist crowds can't quite spoil.

Day at the Arcachon Basin, 1 hour from Bordeaux. Start with the Dune of Pilat—Europe's highest sand dune: 100 to 110 meters high depending on the year (it moves!), 2.9 km long, 616 meters wide. Climb via the wooden staircase (installed March to November) or on foot through the sand. The panorama from the summit sweeps across the ocean, pine forest, and Banc d'Arguin. Arrive early—the parking lot fills quickly in summer.

Head down to the oyster-farming village of Gujan-Mestras or Le Canon, on the Cap-Ferret peninsula. The colorful huts of the oyster farmers serve oysters straight from the basin, with a glass of white wine and rye bread. It's simple, fresh, and the best value of your trip.

Final morning in Bordeaux. If you haven't yet, visit the Bassins des Lumières—the world's largest digital art center, installed in the former German submarine base from World War II. The immersive exhibitions change regularly. The water basins reflect the projections and create a dizzying effect.

Cross the Pont de Pierre to the right bank and the Darwin quarter, a former military barracks converted into an alternative eco-district—skatepark, urban farm, craft brewery, organic restaurants. It's the Bordeaux that's on the move, far from the classicism of the left bank.

activities.text: Treat yourself to a late-afternoon stroll along the Garonne quays. The 4.5 km of quays redeveloped since 2009 have become Bordeaux locals' favorite gathering place—joggers, cyclists, families. The Pont de Pierre (1822), with its 17 brick-and-stone arches, is the most photogenic seen from the quays. Legend has it that the number of arches corresponds to the letters in "Napoléon Bonaparte"—an appealing story, but historians have debunked it.
In the evening, try a wine bar downtown—Bordeaux has dozens, from the most traditional to the most trendy. Ask for a glass of Saint-Émilion or Pessac-Léognan to stay local.

Book a tasting at one of the wine châteaux around Saint-Émilion. Most estates welcome visitors by appointment—allow 1h to 1h30 with a tour of the cellars and tasting of 3-4 wines. Saint-Émilion Grand Cru appellations are among the world's most prestigious.

On the way back, if you enjoy vineyard landscapes, make a detour via Pomerol—Bordeaux's most discreet appellation. No village, no monumental château, just vines and understated estates producing some of the world's most expensive wines.

If weather permits, embark on a tour by pinasse (traditional flat-bottomed boat) around the Bird Island and its cabanes tchanquées—two stilt cabins that have become the symbol of the Basin. Operators offer 2-hour outings from the port of Arcachon or Cap-Ferret.
Treat yourself to a final café on the quays before departure. Bordeaux-Mérignac airport is 20 minutes from downtown, and the Saint-Jean station offers TGV trains to Paris in 2h04.

days_plan.itin_title: Gironde Road Trip: Bordeaux, Vineyards & Arcachon Bay

visits.text: A 12th-century medieval village classified among France's Most Beautiful Villages, Bormes unfolds its flowered alleys, 'cuberts' (vaulted passages), and ochre-tinted facades. Over 700 plant species color the village—bougainvillea in summer, mimosa cascades in winter.

Climb up to the 13th–14th-century castle, a historic monument since 1931. From the village's highest point, the panorama embraces Lavandou Bay, the islands of Levant and Port-Cros, Favière harbor, and Cap Bénat—a view that captures the entire coast.

Saint-Trophyme church, built in the 18th century in Romanesque style, replaced the old castle church that had become too small. Its Chapel of Saint Francis honors the saint who reportedly delivered the town from plague in 1483.

Parc Gonzalez, classified as a Remarkable Garden, brings together 3,000 m² of plants from Australia and around the world, with 250 species arranged by landscape designer Gilles Augias. The terraces offer views over the Mediterranean.

The Museum of History and Art of Bormes, housed in the former town hall, offers an augmented reality tour via HistoPad tablets. The rooms reveal the village's secrets through the centuries with immersive storytelling.

activities.text: Fort de Brégançon, a state residence overlooking the sea, can be glimpsed from the coast. Head to Cabasson beach below: white sand, crystalline waters, and a direct view of the fort—presidential luxury from a beach towel.
Nine wine estates and one cooperative cellar offer tastings of AOC Côtes de Provence. The 'Vignobles & Découvertes' label guarantees quality hospitality in the cellars, where the rosé flows freely.
From Favière harbor, boat excursions head to Port-Cros, Porquerolles, or offshore to observe dolphins. Departures run from April to October, weather permitting.

days_plan.itin_title: Var Road Trip: French Riviera & Green Provence

visits.text: Boucieu-le-Roi is the "village of a thousand secrets" as its inhabitants call it. Its name comes from its history: in 1291, Philip IV le Bel created here a royal court of justice whose jurisdiction covered all of Haut-Vivarais, from the Eyrieux to the north of the department. The village was thus a small judicial and commercial capital. The Maison du Bailli, listed as a Monument Historique, is the emblematic building from this period—its projecting watchtower is instantly recognizable. It housed the high court of justice. 16th-century Wars of Religion ended this prosperity.

Go down to the Pont du Roi, listed as a Monument Historique, probably built in the 15th century to ensure the link with northern Vivarais. It spans the Doux in a wild setting below the village—allow 15 min on foot. Below flows the Doux, a river classified as Espace Naturel Sensible since 2015, renowned for its water quality and protected species (Grand Murin bats, orchids). Climbing to the convent on the heights, you visit the Musée Pierre Vigne, devoted to the Ardèche missionary priest (1670-1740) beatified by John Paul II in 2004, who found in Boucieu a resemblance to Jerusalem and in 1714 built a Stations of the Cross with 39 station-chapels.

The small exhibition room at the village entrance traces the history of Boucieu and Vivarais through photos and old documents—useful context before wandering the lanes. The craft art exhibition "Les Arts à la Cure" in July-August opens the doors of one of the village's oldest houses.

activities.text: The Vélorail des Gorges du Doux offers panoramic descents of 8, 12 or 20 km on the historic Train de l'Ardèche line (built 1891), above the gorges. You pedal on 2- or 4-person rail bikes, the climb by train. The route is accessible to all and landscapes are cut off from the road—the only places where you'll see the Doux gorges from the bank. Absolutely book ahead in July-August.

The Train de l'Ardèche—nicknamed the Mastrou—is a historic steam train linking Tournon-sur-Rhône to Lamastre over 28 km climbing the wild Doux gorges. Boucieu-le-Roi is one of the intermediate stations. Half a day suffices for the Étroits or Viaduct descent from Boucieu. An original experience to discover otherwise inaccessible landscapes.

days_plan.itin_title: The 18 Most Beautiful Villages of Ardèche

visits.text: 18th century castle.
Stables.
English-style park.

activities.text: Guided tours.
Gardens.
Near Valençay.

days_plan.itin_title: Indre, France: Secret Berry Region and Vallée Noire Rural Villages

visits.text: Boulbon is a village with tight-knit streets clinging to its limestone promontory. Its vaulted passages at the hill's foot — typical of medieval Rhône-side architecture — create an atmosphere from another time. Climb to the château ruins (11th-12th centuries) for a view simultaneously embracing the Rhône to the west, the Montagnette to the north, and the Alpilles ridges to the southeast. Few villages of this size offer such amplitude of landscape.

The Saint-Marcellin chapel, at the village's base, is an 11th-century Romanesque structure of remarkable sobriety. It's known for the "procession of bottles" held here every June 1st for centuries: the village men carry wine bottles and have them blessed to protect the vines from diseases and storms. This archaic ritual linked to the cult of Saint Marcellin is one of the last of its type in Provence.

activities.text: Climb to Boulbon's castle promontory: a steep 20-minute path from village center leads to the château ruins. The panorama over the Rhône, Tarascon, and Alpilles is exceptional. Bring good shoes and arrive late afternoon for golden light on the Alpilles.

Chapel loop around Boulbon: several 16th-17th century rural oratories and chapels dot the paths between Boulbon and Barbentane. A marked heritage route of 7 km explores these discreet structures against a backdrop of garrigue and pine forests.

days_plan.itin_title: Alpilles, Aix-en-Provence & Camargue Circuit

visits.text: The Musée des Années 30 displays 3,000 m² of Art Deco and modern art. Paintings, sculptures, and furniture by Ruhlmann and Leleu testify to Boulogne's artistic ferment in the interwar years.
Île Seguin housed the Renault factory from 1929 to 1992, employing 30,000 workers. La Seine Musicale, inaugurated in 2017, now occupies the downstream tip, a work by Japanese architect Shigeru Ban.
Stroll along the Seine embankments between Boulogne and Sèvres. The redesigned quays offer views of Île Seguin and former industrial sites.

activities.text: The 4-hectare Albert Kahn gardens compose landscape scenes: a Japanese garden with tea pavilion brought over in 1909, Vosges forest, English garden, and French formal garden. The museum preserves 72,000 autochrome photographs from the Archives of the Planet.
Parc de Boulogne stretches across the southern commune. Perfect for a nature break.

days_plan.itin_title: Hauts-de-Seine Road Trip: Royal Heritage & Gardens

visits.text: Nausicaá, Europe's largest aquarium, presents 60,000 animals in 16,000 cubic meters of water. The High Seas basin with its 100-square-meter viewing window shelters hammerhead sharks and manta rays.
Notre-Dame Basilica's 19th-century dome soars 101 meters above the upper town. Its architecture draws inspiration from Rome's St. Peter's and Paris's Les Invalides.
The 1,400-square-meter crypt, one of France's vastest, unfolds a labyrinth of 20 underground chambers. Walls and vaults covered with 19th-century frescoes narrate Christianity's history.
Walk the 13th-century ramparts, northern France's only intact medieval enceinte. The 360° views span the Boulonnais region and the fortified old town.
The 13th-century count's castle, built on a Roman camp, now houses Boulogne Museum. Eclectic collections range from ancient Egypt to indigenous arts.
The belfry, a 37-meter former 12th-century keep, has been UNESCO-listed since 1926. It symbolizes the city's communal independence.

activities.text: Stroll the Promenade du Général San Martin along the beach. It stretches from Nausicaá to Wimereux, perfect for walkers and cyclists.
Visit the fish market in France's leading fishing port. Guaranteed freshness and authentic atmosphere.
Discover the ephemeral garden on Place Godefroy de Bouillon. This green space transforms each season around a new botanical theme.
Climb the Column of the Grande Armée, erected in Napoleon's glory. Fifty-three meters high, 263 steps, and breathtaking views over the Boulonnais.

days_plan.itin_title: Pas-de-Calais Road Trip: Opal Coast & Historic Belfries

visits.text: The Bourbon castle ruins (13th-15th centuries) crown the town from their rocky spur. These impressive vestiges of the ancestral fortress of the Bourbon dynasty include the Qui-Qu'en-Grogne keep built by Louis II around 1400. Climb the 70 steps to reach the terrace offering panoramic views over Bourbonnais bocage and Tronçais forest.

The classified Belle Époque thermal baths (1886) perpetuate a tradition dating back to Romans. The preserved Neo-Byzantine architecture with polychrome brick facades houses modern facilities treating rheumatism and respiratory conditions. The springs rich in sulfur and minerals have attracted curistes for over 2000 years.

The Saint-Georges church (12th-13th centuries) displays Romanesque architecture with fortified elements. The interior shelters frescoes and carved capitals illustrating medieval art. The massive bell tower dominates old Bourbon testifying to the church's strategic and spiritual role.

Old Bourbon reveals its paved streets lined with stone houses (15th-18th centuries) and half-timbered facades. The town preserves authentic medieval character with ancient ramparts still visible. Stroll discovering artisan shops and café terraces in preserved historical setting.

activities.text: The thermal park extends 15 hectares of landscaped gardens around thermal baths. Marked paths wind through century-old trees, flowerbeds and ponds. The peaceful setting invites relaxation walks away from urban bustle. Benches and pavilions offer rest spots.

The Tronçais Forest surrounds Bourbon offering hiking and biking opportunities. Marked trails explore this exceptional oak forest planted by Colbert in the 17th century. The forest roads lead to remarkable trees, ponds and glades perfect for picnics and nature observation.

days_plan.itin_title: Allier Road Trip: Vichy, Thermal Baths and Bourbonnais Bocage

visits.text: Bourbonne's thermal springs have drawn visitors for 2,000 years. Mineral-rich water surges naturally from deep underground at 66°C, prized since Roman times for its healing properties.
The spa center specializes in two therapeutic areas: rheumatology for treating arthritis and joint pain, and respiratory treatments for asthma and chronic sinusitis.
The Parc des Sources provides shaded refuge in the town center. The 18th-century former military hospital stands as testament to the spa's storied past as a healing destination for soldiers.
The medieval château park overlooks the town from elevated ground. A 16th-century archway opens onto tree-lined paths shaded by centuries-old specimens.
The municipal museum displays Gallo-Roman archaeological finds, fine art collections, and ethnographic curiosities chronicling Bourbonne's thermal heritage across the ages.

activities.text: Treat yourself to a spa package at the thermal center. Hydromassage baths, steam hammam, and relaxation massages complement the therapeutic treatments.
Wander the historic quarter between the thermal park and Notre-Dame church. 18th-century architecture defines the spa town's character.
Explore marked hiking trails through the surrounding forest. The rolling Champagne countryside offers peaceful landscapes ideal for leisurely walks.

days_plan.itin_title: Haute-Marne Discovery: Langres & Historic Heritage

visits.text: The Royal Monastery of Brou stands like a Bressan Taj Mahal, built out of love between 1506 and 1512. A masterpiece of Flamboyant Gothic architecture, it captivates with its stone lacework, three princely tombs, and poignant history: Margaret of Austria had this mausoleum built for her husband Philibert the Fair, who died too soon. The three cloisters and the Fine Arts Museum complete the visit to this listed monument.

Notre-Dame Church reveals Flamboyant Gothic architecture typical of the Savoy Bresse region. Built with carrons, those characteristic Savoy bricks, it bears witness to the town's medieval past. Its bell tower has watched over the city center for centuries, offering a visible landmark from the surrounding cobblestone streets.

Stroll through the historic center's narrow streets where half-timbered houses tell the story of this former capital of Bresse. Place Carriat and its terraces invite you to observe the daily ballet of the Burgiens. Colorful facades and arcades shelter artisan shops that perpetuate local craftsmanship.

The Ferme du Sougey, a listed historic monument, is one of the oldest half-timbered farms in Bresse, dating from the 15th century. Transformed into a local heritage museum, it showcases traditional Bressan habitat and houses a breeding farm for AOC Bresse poultry. The surrounding 35 hectares of bocage landscape bear witness to ancestral local agriculture.

activities.text: Discover Bresse poultry AOC, the only poultry in the world to benefit from a protected designation of origin since 1957. In local farms and restaurants, taste this fine, marbled meat from chickens raised free-range in the Bressan bocage. Local gastronomy highlights this blue-legged chicken, the region's crown jewel.

Get lost in the cobblestone streets of the Brou district. Old houses, secret passages, and small interior courtyards create a preserved medieval atmosphere. Every facade tells a story, every stone bears witness to the passage of centuries in this city of weavers and merchants.

The market halls buzz every Saturday morning with the activity of local producers. Bresse poultry, mountain cheeses, sausages, thick fresh cream: the stalls overflow with local products. The friendly atmosphere and discussions with producers keep the Bressan agricultural tradition alive.

Visit the apothecary of the former Brou hospital, arranged in the early 18th century. This old pharmacy preserves an exceptional collection of Meillonnas earthenware jars and period medical instruments. Carved woodwork and secret drawers testify to medicine of yesteryear.

days_plan.itin_title: Ain Road Trip: Bresse Plains and Bugey Mountains
Grand Tour of the French Alps: From the Thousand Ponds to Mont Blanc
Auvergne-Rhône-Alpes Road Trip: Monasteries, Mountains & Lakes

visits.text: Bourges Cathedral surprises from the entrance: no transept, five naves stretching in one line for 120 meters. This extremely rare feature for a Gothic cathedral creates a dizzying perspective found nowhere else in France. Built between 1195 and 1230, it rivals Notre-Dame de Paris. The central portal of the Last Judgment deserves a closer look: smiling resurrected souls, grimacing damned, carved with striking realism.

The 13th-century stained glass windows are among the finest preserved medieval collections. In the ambulatory, the Last Supper and Saint John's vision haven't moved in 800 years. The crypt houses the marble effigy of Duke Jean de Berry (1422) and remnants of the rood screen destroyed during the Revolution. Guided tour required, departures every hour.

396 spiral steps in the Butter Tower lead to the panoramic terrace. The climb is steep but the 360° view over Bourges, medieval rooftops and the Berry countryside rewards the effort. You can make out the marshes to the north and the first hills of Sancerre to the east. Access limited to July-August, fixed hours, limited capacity.

The Palais Jacques-Cœur is one of France's finest Gothic civil buildings. Built between 1443 and 1451 by Charles VII's treasurer, it has 43 rooms over 4000 m². Jacques Cœur only slept there 8 nights before his disgrace. The street facade shows two sculpted figures leaning on false windows — they seem to have been watching passersby for 600 years. Inside, the upturned boat-hull roof frame and monumental fireplaces testify to the splendor of the era.

Jacques Cœur's motto — "To a valiant heart, nothing is impossible" — is carved throughout the palace, accompanied by sculpted shells and hearts. The chapel preserves a stained glass window depicting one of his merchant ships. The banquet hall, with its musicians' gallery and fireplace bearing royal arms, gives an idea of the lavish receptions he never got to host.

activities.text: The Archbishop's Garden, attributed to Le Nôtre, offers an open view of the cathedral's chevet. French-style lawns, geometric flowerbeds and shaded benches: the ideal spot for a break after visits. Free access year-round.

Bourges' historic center is easily walkable. Half-timbered houses on rue Bourbonnoux, Hôtel Lallemant (Renaissance), Notre-Dame church with its contemporary stained glass. The cobbled streets around the cathedral have kept their medieval layout.

Les Nuits Lumière de Bourges (mid-June to mid-September): free nighttime route with projections on historic facades. The cathedral, Palais Jacques-Cœur and several private mansions light up at nightfall. Allow 2 hours for the complete circuit.

days_plan.itin_title: Cher Circuit: Romanesque Berry and Jacques-Cœur Route

visits.text: The castle of Boussac stands on a cliff overlooking the Petite Creuse. Built in the 15th century by Jean de Brosse, companion-in-arms of Joan of Arc, it was the site of a major discovery: it was here that George Sand identified the Lady and the Unicorn tapestries during her stays between 1840 and 1870. The writer convinced her friend Prosper Mérimée, Inspector of Historic Monuments, to classify them. The six panels joined the Cluny Museum in 1882.

George Sand's bedroom in the castle has been reconstructed with touching attention to detail: open book on the table, slippers at the foot of the bed, hair tongs on the nightstand. The writer drafted her pastoral novel Jeanne here. A reproduction of the 'À mon seul désir' (To my only desire) tapestry recalls the medieval masterpiece discovered within these walls.

The castle now houses a remarkable collection of more than 40 tapestries, from the 15th century to contemporary times. An entire room is devoted to Dom Robert, a Benedictine monk whose colorful cartoons depict joyful fauna. The works of Jean Lurçat, including the 'Liberté' (Freedom) tapestry inspired by Paul Éluard's poem, testify to the renewal of textile art in the 20th century.

activities.text: The Pierres Jaumâtres, a natural granite chaos on Mont Barlot, offers a fantastic landscape of strangely shaped rocks. George Sand set a scene from her novel Jeanne here. The blocks stacked by erosion evoke petrified creatures. A developed trail winds between the rock formations, with panoramic views over the Marche mountains.

The town of Boussac preserves some old houses and a 12th-century church. A stroll through the alleys allows appreciation of the atmosphere of this small Creuse town. The weekly market animates the central square.

days_plan.itin_title: Creuse Circuit: Valley of the Painters and Aubusson Tapestries

places.name: Bouxwiller

visits.text: Bouxwiller is the secret market Alsatians jealously guard for themselves. Former capital of the County of Hanau-Lichtenberg, this small Bas-Rhin town preserves exceptional architectural heritage — half-timbered houses, colorful façades, cobbled streets — and its Christkindelsmärik was voted 'most authentic Christmas market in Alsace' by France Bleu Alsace listeners. The reason? Here, the 200 exhibitors are exclusively Alsatian, no imported products appear on stalls, and selection is absolutely rigorous. Just three days in December (Friday-Saturday-Sunday), 50,000 visitors, and a soul that big markets have sometimes lost.

The Musée du Pays de Hanau presents a 'Christmas Window' each year: figures and decorations fashioned by the territory's glass artisans, with Christmas tree ornaments dating from the 19th and 20th centuries. The 'Christmas Treasure Hunt' exhibition in the old town streets allows discovering heritage while having fun — booklet available at the tourist office. Bouxwiller also organizes the Grand Est's only labeled truffle market, coinciding with the Christkindelsmärik — a gastronomic curiosity not to be missed.

activities.text: The wanderings of the Hàns Tràpp and the Christkindel are Bouxwiller market's signature entertainments. The Christkindel, this young girl dressed in white wearing a fir crown on her head, distributes sweets to good children at day's end. The Hàns Tràpp — a dark and frightening character, the Alsatian counterpart of Père Fouettard — is meant to scare naughty children. The small steam train that traverses Place du Château carrying the winemaker from the Three Witches estate completes the festive tableau.

The fire show 'The Dragon's Legend' on Place du Château at day's end is one of the route's most spectacular moments — flames, music, and narration in the Alsatian night. Pastry workshops (bredele making) and floral workshops are offered throughout the weekend, perfect for families with children.

days_plan.itin_title: Alsace's Most Beautiful Christmas Markets

map_keywords: Musée du Pays de Hanau Bouxwiller

visits.text: City of Molière.
Saint-Nazaire Cathedral.

Art crafts.

days_plan.itin_title: Hérault, France: Mediterranean Beaches and Languedoc Wine Country

places.name: Bouzonville

visits.text: Begin the trail in Schwerdorff, right up against the German border—the first German villages lie less than two kilometers away. The neo-Romanesque Church of Notre-Dame-de-l'Assomption, built in 1886, holds a surprise: its carved oak woodwork incorporates the epitaphs of the Lords of Haen and the Villers family, allowing you to trace the entire history of the village's castle lords. During the Christmas season, an exhibition of miniature nativity scenes from around the world is presented here, accompanied by pieces played on the restored Dalstein-Haerpfer organ. Take advantage of your visit to glimpse the Gallo-Roman vestiges visible within the commune's boundaries.

The Abbey Church of Sainte-Croix in Bouzonville is the flagship monument of this first day. It was founded in 1033 by Count Adalbert of Alsace—little-known fact: his direct descendants became the founders of the Ducal House of Lorraine and the city of Nancy. The current building was entirely reconstructed in Gothic style between 1342 and 1375 after a fire. Listed as a Historical Monument since September 8, 1999, the abbey church astonishes with the sobriety of its sandstone façade, contrasting with the splendor of its interior. The Christmas nativity scene here is one of the most remarkable on the route: its figures come from Oberammergau in Bavaria, a village world-renowned for its Passion Play. Don't miss the Last Supper restored in 2020, created in 1925 by a local craftsman, nor the Garnier organ, a unique instrument in the region.

Make the detour to the Maison Lorraine d'Oberdorff in Odenhoven (17 km via D23 and D63). In this vaulted-room dwelling from the 15th century, you'll find an old nativity scene from Saint-Martin Church in Odenhoven dating to the 18th century, several nativity scenes from the Pays de Nied—in wax, terracotta, wood, and paper—and a collection of angels and shepherds on the fireplace mantle. A concentrated glimpse of Lorraine Christmas craftsmanship in an authentic setting.

activities.text: From the abbey church, follow the Stations of the Cross that climb to Belle Croix, the town's highest point. This walk passes through the historic center and by the Town Hall built in 1719, topped with a bulbous belfry typical of Lorraine. It's a lovely way to see the 18th-century houses of the "Pearl of the Nied" illuminated by Christmas decorations.

In December, Bouzonville hosts its Christmas market and brings Saint Nicholas by carriage to the Town Hall forecourt. The concerts at the abbey church—notably the December Christmas concert with organ and soprano—are free and worth the trip. Check the town's program before your visit.

days_plan.itin_title: Christmas Nativity Trail through Moselle

map_keywords: Chemin de croix Belle Croix Bouzonville

visits.text: Brancion is perched on a barred spur overlooking the pass linking Cluny to the Saône — a strategic position that explains everything. The castle alone concentrates a thousand years of Burgundian military architecture: the year 1000 dwelling (early 11th century, one of the rare examples of Carolingian great seigneurial halls in France), the 12th-13th century seigneurial castle with its 23-meter keep, and the 14th-century ducal lodge with its trefoil bays. In 1259, the Brancion family — ruined by the crusades — sold their castle to the Duke of Burgundy Hugues IV, who made it a ducal castellany for two centuries. The castle has been classified as a historical monument since 1977. From the top of the keep, the view over the Saône-et-Loire plain is exceptional.

The Saint-Pierre church, at the end of the spur, is a Romanesque masterpiece from the second half of the 12th century. It houses late-13th-century wall paintings and the recumbent statue of Josserand de Brancion, who died at the Battle of Mansurah in 1250 during the 7th Crusade alongside Saint Louis — before selling the castle, the family had already spent everything on the crusades. Next door, the medicinal garden reconstructed by a local association features 80 medieval plants.

activities.text: Loop of viewpoints and edges above the village (2–3 km, ~1 hour). The trail follows the Mâconnais vineyards and offers alternating views over the Grosne valley and the hills of Southern Burgundy.

Interpretation tour of the castle site and church with the audioguide available in 4 languages (included in the castle entrance ticket). On Easter weekend and in autumn, medieval festivals animate the site.

days_plan.itin_title: Loop Around Lyon: Villages and Golden Stones
Burgundy's 15 Most Beautiful Villages

visits.text: Erbalunga spreads its fishermen's houses tightly across a rocky peninsula. The ruined 16th-century Genoese tower dominates the small harbor while salt-weathered facades reveal authentic charm. The baroque-fronted Church of Saint-Érasme protects the processional crosses used during Holy Week.

The hamlets of Brando climb in tiers through the valley: Castello with its feudal castle ruins, Pozzo and its flagstone quarries, Mausoleu and its cipolin marble quarries. The Chapel of Notre-Dame-des-Neiges preserves 14th-century frescoes. A journey back in time among fountains and dry-stone washhouses.

The Lavasina sanctuary has drawn pilgrims for centuries. The ex-voto museum can be visited by reservation and tells the story of the miracle. The former Franciscan convent overlooks a small pebble beach facing the sea.

activities.text: The coastal path from Erbalunga winds along coves between maquis and black rocks. Kayak rentals available at the harbor for exploring hidden caves and wild inlets. Diving clubs offer trips to the protected waters of the Cap Corse Marine Natural Park.
Erbalunga beach and Campinca cove offer swimming in a pristine setting. Fine sand and transparent waters bordered by rocks. Family-friendly atmosphere far from the crowds of beach resorts.

days_plan.itin_title: Upper Corsica Road Trip: Cap Corse and Balagne

visits.text: Legend credits Charlemagne with founding Brantôme's Benedictine abbey in 769 AD. Whether or not the emperor passed through, the 11th-century Romanesque bell tower—among France's oldest—still stands on the rock outcrop above the abbey church. Monks carved living spaces directly into the limestone cliff: a dovecote, Saint Sicaire's miraculous fountain, and austere monastic cells that speak to centuries of devotion.

The Cave of the Last Judgment, hewn into the cliff face during the 15th century, harbors two monumental bas-reliefs: Christ in glory surrounded by apocalyptic scenes. The atmosphere inside is haunting and profound. Nearby, the remnants of troglodyte dwellings reveal where monks lived as early as the 9th century—before the abbey was even built.

Brantôme earned the nickname 'the Venice of Périgord' for good reason: the Dronne River winds through the town, crossed by arched bridges and lined with riverside terraces. The angled medieval bridge, the Renaissance pavilion, and the abbey mill create postcard-perfect reflections in the water. It's a place made for strolling and lingering.

activities.text: The Dronne River offers gentle canoeing and kayaking—Class I water suitable for beginners and families. Routes range from one to five hours, paddling beneath Brantôme's bridges and alongside limestone cliffs. Local outfitters provide gear and shuttle service back to town.

days_plan.itin_title: Dordogne Road Trip: Cave Art, Medieval Fortresses & Périgord Delights
Nouvelle-Aquitaine Loop: Medieval Villages, Vineyards & Dordogne Valley

visits.text: The 11th-century castle of the Lords of Beaumont unfolds 700 meters of walls punctuated by 38 towers on a rocky promontory.
Follow the restored ramparts and admire the romantic ruins of the 15th-century manor house.
The 19th-century neo-Gothic mansion hosts temporary exhibitions and sculpture symposiums.
The Church of Notre-Dame, founded by Thibault de Beaumont in the 11th century, features 12th-century Angevin domed vaults.
The 56-meter Renaissance bell tower inspired by Italy dominates the city. 19th-century frescoes adorn the choir.

activities.text: From the castle esplanade, enjoy the view over the Chapel of Saint-Cyprien and the 19th-century former slaughterhouses.
Participate in the Terra Aventura treasure hunt within the castle grounds to help Dame Jeanne.

days_plan.itin_title: Deux-Sèvres Circuit: Marais Poitevin and Romanesque Heritage

visits.text: The Pink Granite Coast stretches 30 km between Perros-Guirec and Trébeurden, and only three places in the world have such pink granite: in Brittany, Corsica, and China. The color comes from iron oxide impurities in the crystal structure. Start at Saint-Guirec beach in Ploumanac'h — a neighborhood attached to Perros-Guirec, voted France's Favorite Village in 2015. On the beach, a pink granite oratory houses the statue of Saint Guirec, whose nose was long pierced with pins by young women seeking husbands — the original 14th-century wooden statue, too damaged, was replaced with a granite version in 1904.

The Men Ruz lighthouse ('red stone' in Breton) is the emblem of the Pink Granite Coast. Standing 15 meters tall, built entirely of local pink granite, it was erected between 1946 and 1948 to replace the 1860 lighthouse that German troops destroyed in 1944. The architecture was designed to blend into the surrounding granite chaos — from the Customs Officers' Trail, it's hard to distinguish from the surrounding rocks until you're right on it. It's not open for visits, but the approach along the trail through the chaos is worth the trip.

From Trestraou beach in Perros-Guirec to the port of Ploumanac'h, the Customs Officers' Trail (GR34) follows the coast's most spectacular granite chaos. The rocks take strange shapes that locals have named: the Turtle, the Bear, the Mushroom. In sunny weather, the golden pink light on the turquoise water and granite blocks is rare beauty. Plan on good shoes — the path requires hopping from rock to rock in places — and allow between 2 hours for brisk hikers and 4 hours for photographers.

Île Renote, at Trégastel, is a peninsula accessible on foot from the beaches of Grève Blanche and Grève Rose. The 3 km coastal loop circles the island through granite chaos and offers successive panoramas across the coast and islets. Grève Rose beach gets its name from an unusual sandy tint — the grains of pink quartz and feldspar from granite erosion give it a slightly rosy color. Allow 90 minutes for the loop.

Roscoff, a labeled Petite Cité de Caractère, is one of northern Finistère's best-preserved towns. The church of Notre-Dame-de-Croaz-Batz was built between 1522 and 1545 by Roscoff shipowners — its Breton name means 'cross of the landing stage for Batz island.' The building's distinctive feature: the exterior walls are carved with stone caravels, gifts from the merchants who financed the edifice and sought divine protection for their fleet. The Renaissance bell tower dates from 1575-1576. The church earned historic monument status as early as 1886 — look for the 18 figured corbels representing people and animals on the facades. Across from it, the harbor pier — a 600-meter walkway into the sea — takes you all the way to the embarkation dock for Batz island.

The Maison des Johnnies, a small museum in Roscoff, traces the history of Roscoff onion merchants who crossed the Channel in the 19th and 20th centuries by bicycle to sell their onion strings in England. These traveling merchants were nicknamed 'Johnnies' by the British — a tradition that lasted over 150 years and helped popularize the pink Roscoff onion, now a protected geographical designation.

The Keremma dunes at Plouescat form one of Brittany's most extensive and preserved dune massifs — 4 km of dunes protected by the Coastal Conservancy overlooking the Bay of Kernic. Behind the dunes, the bay welcomes thousands of migrating waders (autumn-winter). A wooden walkway crosses the dunes without damaging them.

Brignogan-Plages owes its reputation to the Pontusval lighthouse and its chaos of granite rocks emerging from the sea — the automated lighthouse sits atop one of these rocks, visible from the beach. The town is also known for the Christianized standing stone of Men Marz (2.9 meters tall), topped with a cross, which you'll find roadside at the entrance to the village.

The village of Meneham at Kerlouan is one of the most singular coastal sites on the Coast of Legends. This restored hamlet literally built itself between and beneath the rocks: one house is framed by two granite blocks weighing several tons that form its walls. The site was inhabited by shipwreck watchers in the 18th century and is now classified. Arrive early morning in summer — tour buses arrive by 10 am.

The Île Vierge lighthouse, visible from the Plouguerneau coast, is Europe's tallest stone lighthouse at 82.5 meters. Built between 1897 and 1902, it comprises 365 spiral steps — one for each day of the year. The island is accessible by boat from Plouguerneau in season; from the coast, the lighthouse's silhouette on the horizon is already spectacular.

Pointe de Corsen, at Plouarzel, is the westernmost point of mainland France. From the viewpoint, the Iroise Sea stretches to the horizon — on clear days, you can see the islands of Ouessant and Molène. A marker signals this edge of the territory. Brief visit (20 to 30 minutes) but symbolic.

Pointe Saint-Mathieu, at the tip of the Kermorvan Peninsula, combines two heritages in one. On one side, the lighthouse built in 1835, still active, whose base is constructed within the ruins of a 6th-century Benedictine abbey — the residual Gothic arches encircle the lighthouse tower in striking fashion. On the other, the cliffs plunge vertically into the Iroise Sea. It's also the starting point for walks toward Blancs Sablons beach, a long white sand beach protected from winds by the dune.

The Petit Minou lighthouse, at Plouzané, photographed from its arched bridge spanning the passage between the mainland and the rock, is one of Brittany's most photographed lighthouses. It has watched over the Brest roadstead since 1848 (rebuilt in 1848 after an initial 1848 version). From neighboring viewpoints, the view across the Brest roadstead with maritime traffic is spectacular — hundreds of vessels pass through this corridor each week.

Locronan is a village classified among France's Most Beautiful Villages, and its medieval architecture is so well preserved that it has served as a period film set. The central square, surrounded by granite dwellings with slate roofs, owes its coherence to the hemp industry: between the 15th and 18th centuries, Locronan manufactured sailcloth for the great European navies, including the Royal Navy and Louis XIV's fleet. This prosperity enabled building in stone for eternity. Arrive before 9 am or after 6 pm in summer — tour buses fill the square from mid-morning.

Saint-Ronan church, built between 1420 and 1480 thanks to donations from the Dukes of Brittany Jean V, Pierre II, and François II, is a masterpiece of Breton Gothic. The ascending nave of six bays, 43 meters long, culminates in a large 1480 window depicting Christ's Passion. The Pénity chapel, built from 1485 by Pierre Le Goaraguer — the same architect as Quimper cathedral — houses Saint Ronan's cenotaph in Kersanton stone, with his recumbent figure resting on six angel caryatids. The pulpit, dated 1706, illustrates the saint's legend in ten carved medallions.

The Baie des Trépassés (Bay of the Departed), framed between Pointe du Raz and Pointe du Van, is one of Cap Sizun's largest wild beaches. Its name evokes a legend that currents deposited the bodies of shipwreck victims here. Today it's mainly a recognized surf spot — the waves are regular and powerful even in moderate wind. The beach is accessible from the parking (paid in season) at the entrance.

Pointe du Raz is Brittany's most visited Grand Site de France, with about 800,000 visitors per year. Its cliffs rise 72 meters above the Raz de Sein — a powerful tidal current notorious for its breakers and shipwrecks. Opposite, the Vieille lighthouse (lit in 1887, automated in 1995) and Sein island are visible in clear weather. A statue of Our Lady of the Shipwrecked watches over the site from the cliff. Leave your car at the parking (paid from late March to early November) and follow the secured trails toward the point — 90 minutes for the complete loop. The Site House offers guided commentary tours all season.

Pointe de la Torche at Plomeur is Finistère's main surf spot, with a long beach open to the Atlantic exposed to swells from the open ocean. Local operators offer surf and kitesurf lessons. Beyond water sports, the walk on the dune offers panoramic views across Audierne Bay on one side and Penmarc'h Bay on the other.

Bénodet, a seaside resort at the mouth of the Odet River, is a soothing stop before heading back. Trez cove beach is lined with villas dating from the Belle Époque — bourgeois families from Quimper came to bathe here as early as the late 19th century. From the seafront promenade, the Combrit point lighthouse reflects in the calm estuary waters. Boats regularly shuttle from Bénodet to Sainte-Marine on the opposite shore.

activities.text: The photographic walk through Ploumanac'h's granite chaos follows the Customs Officers' Trail from the Sémaphore parking above Trestraou beach to the port of Ploumanac'h. The rock formations are especially dramatic in raking light — favor sunrise or sunset. Walking poles should be fitted with rubber tips on this trail. Round trip circuit or shuttle in season between Ploumanac'h and Perros-Guirec.

Operators offer boat trips from Perros-Guirec to the bird sanctuary of the Sept Îles archipelago — one of France's most important seabird reserves, with colonies of northern gannets, Atlantic puffins, and European shags. Trips last about 2 hours. Book ahead in summer.

The Coast of Legends driving route from Brignogan to Plouguerneau follows a succession of wild beaches, rock chaos, and small fishing ports. Stop at Boutrouilles beach at Kerlouan (large wild beach with no facilities) and at the Plouguerneau aber for views over the estuary.

Short walk from the Petit Minou parking to Minou beach (15 minutes on foot), at the foot of the lighthouse. The beach is framed by cliffs and offers a direct view of the Brest channel with French Navy vessels maneuvering. It's also a good spot to watch the sunset over the ocean.

The walking loop of Locronan around the central square and through the medieval lanes takes about 45 minutes. If you have more time, climb up to Locronan mountain (285 meters elevation) on the marked trail — the view over Douarnenez Bay and the village below is worth the effort (90 minutes round trip from the square).

Coastal walk on the GR34 at Pointe du Raz: from the Site House, follow the marked trail toward the point itself then toward the north coast (Pointe de Castel-Koz) before returning. Allow 90 minutes to 2 hours for the complete loop. Binoculars are available on loan at the Site House to observe seabirds nesting on the cliffs.

days_plan.itin_title: Hautes-Alpes Majesty: High Mountain Passes, Ski Resorts & Alpine Valleys

places.name: Briare

visits.text: The Briare Canal Bridge carries barges across the Loire for 2,172 feet—one of the longest navigable aqueducts in the world. Built by Gustave Eiffel's workshops in 1896, this marvel of steel and cast iron suspends the canal 36 feet above the royal river.

The Musée des Deux Marines traces river navigation and Loire boatmanship through the ages. Scale models, vintage photographs, and authentic equipment evoke the life of the mariniers who guided flat-bottomed boats along the treacherous currents.
The Église Saint-Étienne raises its neo-Gothic bell tower above the town center. The 19th-century interior glows with colorful stained glass.

activities.text: Cruise the Canal latéral à la Loire in an electric boat—no license required. Discover the canal bridge from the water, gliding through its graceful arches.
Watch the barges navigate the locks. The canal links the Loire and Seine rivers, cutting through the Loiret on its journey between France's two great waterways.

days_plan.itin_title: Loiret Road Trip: Orléans & Loire Valley Châteaux

map_keywords: Canal latéral à la Loire Briare

visits.text: Brienne-le-Château earned its fame as Napoleon Bonaparte's military school city. From 1779 to 1784, the young Corsican studied at the Royal Military School, now the Museum of Napoleon and the Revolution. Period collections, imperial souvenirs, weapons, documents retrace the emperor's youth. The castle, rebuilt after WWI bombings, houses temporary exhibitions. Allow 1h30 for the complete visit.

The Saint-Pierre-et-Saint-Paul church houses an exceptional treasure: the Girardon Altarpiece, 17th-century sculpted masterpiece by François Girardon, Louis XIV's official sculptor. White marble composition depicting the Assumption, remarkable for its movement and expressiveness. One of France's most beautiful baroque altarpieces. Free admission but irregular opening hours — check before visiting.

activities.text: Walk around Lac de la Forêt d'Orient: this artificial lake created in 1966 extends over 2,500 hectares. Marked trails allow walking or cycling the shores. Water activities: swimming from supervised beaches, sailing, kayaking, stand-up paddle. Nautical base rents equipment. Popular in summer, bring picnic and swimwear.

Every Wednesday and Saturday, Brienne-le-Château holds its market under the covered hall. Local products: vegetables, cheeses, charcuterie, honey. Then explore the town's shops and cafés. Lunch at a restaurant offering Champagne regional cuisine: andouillette, pig's trotters, chaource, Champagne-braised meat dishes. Authentic local flavors.

days_plan.itin_title: Aube Circuit: Champagne, Medieval Heritage and Orient Forest Lakes

visits.text: Le Corbusier's Cité Radieuse rises from the forest since 1961. This vertical village measures 110 meters long and 56 high, containing 339 duplex apartments across 17 floors.
Rough concrete frames the colorful loggias. André Wogenscky led the construction following Le Corbusier's Modulor principles.
Six interior streets traverse the building constructed on stilts. The association La Première Rue opens model apartments furnished in 1960s style.
The 13th-century Église Saint-Gengoult testifies to Briey's medieval past. This Gothic gem, a listed monument, dominates the old town.
Briey-Haut's historic center preserves ramparts and remains of the county castle. Cobbled lanes wind between old houses.

activities.text: Lac de la Sangsue offers swimming and water activities. The 2.6-kilometer trail circles the lake.
The forest trails of Mance and Mancieulles offer loops from 5 to 15 kilometers. The forest surrounds the Cité Radieuse.

days_plan.itin_title: Meurthe-et-Moselle Road Trip: Art Nouveau Nancy & Fortified Cities

visits.text: The Basilica of Saint-Julien is the largest Romanesque church in Auvergne, built between the 11th and 12th centuries over a 4th-century martyr's tomb. Its polychromy of red sandstone, basalt, granulite, and gray marbles is utterly unique.
Look up: roughly 300 Romanesque capitals populated with foliage, symbolic animals, and knights follow one another down the nave. The sculpted cycle in the choir evokes the Passion and Resurrection.
Look down at the pavement of basalt and quartz pebbles, rediscovered in 1963. Arranged in flowers, rosettes, and waves, these river stones form a two-tone mineral carpet.

The 37 contemporary stained-glass windows by Father Kim En Joong, created by the Loire Workshops of Chartres in 2008, flood the nave with colored light. The dialogue between Romanesque and contemporary art is striking.
Take rue du 4-Septembre to admire the Maison de Mandrin (12th–20th c.), its half-timbered brick façade with corbelling, and its vaulted room with painted coats of arms.
The Saint-Julien Fountain, on Avenue d'Auvergne, marks according to legend the site of the saint's beheading. Its capitals reportedly date back to the 10th century.

activities.text: Brioude sits on the Via Arverna linking Clermont-Ferrand to Santiago de Compostela. Walk a stretch of this thousand-year-old pilgrimage route in the surrounding area.
Wander the lanes of this "Petite Cité de Caractère" (Small Heritage Town): mansions, turrets, half-timbered houses, and shaded squares punctuate your stroll.
Need some fresh air? Head down to the banks of the Allier. The river, one of the last wild rivers in Europe, flows at the town's doorstep at the entrance to its gorges.

days_plan.itin_title: Haute-Loire Road Trip: Le Puy-en-Velay & Volcanic Peaks

visits.text: Brissac — "Green Lady".

activities.text: Access: public (Maillezais)

days_plan.itin_title: Loire Valley Phantoms: Tragic Histories and Haunted Castles of France

visits.text: The Château de Brissac is France's tallest, soaring 7 stories and 204 rooms high—earning it the nickname 'Giant of the Loire Valley.' At 50 meters, it dominates the Aubance valley with unmistakable vertical drama.

Two medieval towers stand alongside the Renaissance façade—evidence of an unfinished transformation. Charles II de Cossé commissioned a new palace in 1606, but his death in 1621 permanently halted construction, freezing this architectural paradox in time.

The Belle Époque theater was built in 1890 by Jeanne Say, Marquise de Brissac and renowned opera singer. This intimate opera house welcomed the artistic elite of Paris to performances in the Loire countryside.

Gold-leaf ceilings, precious tapestries, and period furnishings create sumptuous interiors. The Louis XIII chamber preserves the memory of the 1620 reconciliation between the king and Marie de Médicis—a moment when royal family drama played out in these very rooms.
The 16th-century chapel features frescoes painted by Coppin Delf. Sixteen musical angels glorify the Virgin Mary and Christ in celestial harmony.

A 250-meter underground channel diverts the Aubance beneath the park. Its schist vault reflects in the water, creating an illuminated subterranean spectacle where stone and water mirror each other in darkness.

activities.text: The 70-hectare romantic park invites leisurely exploration. Century-old cedars, the mausoleum avenue, Jeanne Say's cascade, and vineyards compose a bucolic landscape that changes with each season.
The village of Brissac-Quincé nestles within vineyards bordered by the Aubance. The 16th-century church and wine-growers' houses testify to generations of local viticultural tradition.
The Montgolfiade hot air balloon festival gathers thousands of visitors each late August to the château park, where dozens of balloons rise into the sunset sky in a magical display.

days_plan.itin_title: Maine-et-Loire Road Trip: Anjou and Loire Valley Châteaux

visits.text: Labenche Museum. Located in a 16th-century Renaissance mansion listed as Historic Monument, this art and history museum presents 17 rooms. Unique collection in France of 10 tapestries from the English Royal Mortlake Manufactory (17th century). Also features Claude Debussy's piano, prehistoric and Gallo-Roman archaeological collections, and sections on local traditions.
Saint-Martin Collegiate Church. 12th-century Romanesque church in the heart of the old town. The 6th-century baptistery, discovered during excavations, testifies to the site's antiquity.
Saint-Libéral Chapel. 15th-century Gothic building converted into a contemporary art space, programmed by the Labenche Museum.

activities.text: Brive Market. On Saturday morning, it's one of the most renowned in the region. Under the Georges Brassens hall and on the surrounding squares, local producers and market gardeners offer regional specialties. Don't miss the violet mustard of Brive, a specialty made with grape must.

Tasting of Corrèze specialties. The region offers generous gastronomy: farcidures (grated potato dumplings with bacon), mique (poached dough ball served with salted pork), millassou (potato pancake), tourtous (buckwheat crepes), potato pie, flognarde (apple flan).

days_plan.itin_title: Corrèze Road Trip: Villages and Dordogne Valley

visits.text: Place des Halles in Brou, the beating heart of this town between Beauce and Perche. The current market hall from 1846 replaces the former 16th-century wooden structure that housed one of the region's most important calf markets. 15th-century half-timbered houses frame this lively square.

Church of Saint-Lubin in Brou, built in the 12th century and expanded in the 15th and 16th centuries. Its 18th-century furnishings and religious art statues testify to this trading town's past prosperity. The bell tower dominates the historic center's rooftops.

Classified half-timbered houses of Brou, notably the 15th-century one on Place des Halles and the houses on rue des Changes with their carved figurative beams. These last witnesses of the medieval town have miraculously survived through the centuries.

activities.text: Weekly market in Brou on Wednesday mornings, one of the region's most important. Place des Halles, rue des Changes, and rue de la Chevalerie come alive with local producers offering cheeses, charcuterie, and vegetables from Beauce and Perche.

Mill race walk along the banks of the Ozanne, where wash houses from the late 19th century follow one another in a bucolic setting. This river that crosses Brou from west to east joins the nearby Loir, offering peaceful rural perspectives.

Explore the Perche-Gouët terroir around Brou, a transitional land between the cereal plains of Beauce and the bocage hills of Perche. Traditional farms and Percheron horse breeding operations dot this authentic landscape.

days_plan.itin_title: Eure-et-Loir Circuit: Chartres, Châteaux & Proust

visits.text: Noirlac Abbey is one of the best-preserved Cistercian abbeys in Europe. Founded in 1136, it escaped revolutionary destruction because it was used as a porcelain factory until 1950. The church, cloister, chapter house, dormitory, refectory: everything is intact or faithfully restored. The stained glass windows by Jean-Pierre Raynaud (1976-2011) — blue, white, gray — respect the Cistercian spirit while bringing contemporary light.

The cloister is the abbey's heart. Four galleries surrounding a square garden. Romanesque columns, sculpted capitals, ribbed vaults. The monks walked here in silence, hands in sleeves, eight times a day between prayers. The chapter house, where daily decisions were made, has kept its acoustics: a whisper at one end is heard at the other.

Noirlac hosts classical music concerts year-round, taking advantage of the church's exceptional acoustics. The Rencontres Musicales de Noirlac (June-September) feature baroque, sacred music, contemporary. Check the program: attending mass or vespers sung by a vocal ensemble in this Cistercian setting is an unforgettable experience.

activities.text: The Borne de l'Empire (Empire Milestone) stands 500 meters from the abbey. Erected in 1757, it marks the geographic center of France under the Ancien Régime — equidistant from Dunkirk, Perpignan, Brest and Strasbourg. A Roman milestone, it's become a pilgrimage site for lovers of symbolic geography.

The abbey shop sells products made by monks from other communities: honey, jams, herbal teas, candles. Also books on Cistercian spirituality, sacred music CDs, local crafts. Part of the proceeds supports abbey restoration.

days_plan.itin_title: Cher Circuit: Romanesque Berry and Jacques-Cœur Route

visits.text: The Old Castle, built in the 13th century on a Roman castrum, dominates a 90-meter cliff. Its Renaissance gallery opens sheer above the Aveyron Gorges with vertiginous panoramas.
The Young Castle was erected between 1485 and 1510 by Maffre de Comminges. Its restored rooms host an exhibition on prehistory and the filming of Le Vieux Fusil with Romy Schneider.
Climb Queen Brunehaut's tower from the 12th century. Legend attributes the village's founding to this Merovingian queen.
Walk cobbled lanes where limestone dwellings from the 15th, 16th, and 17th centuries follow one another. Climbing roses and lilacs adorn the facades.
Discover the exhibition dedicated to Bruniquel Cave. Arranged structures dating to 176,500 years ago make this site the oldest human construction in an underground environment.

activities.text: Explore the terraced gardens spreading across 6 hectares around the castles. Views embrace the Aveyron and Vère valleys.
Reach the panoramic belvedere from the Corniche road. The Bône cirque reveals the Aveyron's meanders embedded in limestone cliffs.
Visit artisan workshops installed in the village. Painters, sculptors, and ceramicists animate this bastide classified among France's Most Beautiful Villages since 1994.

days_plan.itin_title: Tarn-et-Garonne Road Trip: Medieval Bastides and Orchard Country

visits.text: Walk along the Planches de Cabourg, the iconic 4 km promenade stretching along the sandy beach. The Belle Époque atmosphere permeates this seaside resort with its unique fan-shaped layout. The villas with their bow windows, loggias and colourful ceramics showcase the architectural refinement of the 1900s.

The Grand Hôtel de Cabourg stretches its 300-metre façade facing the sea. Opened in 1907, this Belle Époque palace welcomed Marcel Proust, who stayed each summer in room 414 from 1907 to 1914. The writer composed passages of In Search of Lost Time here, renaming Cabourg 'Balbec'.

activities.text: Explore the Villa du Temps retrouvé, a museum evoking the Belle Époque, housed in the former Villa Bon-Abri. Exhibitions, collections and recreations transport you to the art of living in 1900.
Follow the Marcel Proust promenade named after the writer. The Méridien de l'Amour, a giant sundial on the sand, marks this romantic spot cherished by couples.
Try your luck at the Cabourg casino, next to the Grand Hôtel. Rebuilt in 1911 in a neoclassical style, it carries on the tradition of elegant entertainment at the resort.

days_plan.itin_title: Calvados Circuit: D-Day Beaches and Normandy

visits.text: Pech Merle — Paintings & echoes.
Cénevières — Apparitions & chains.

activities.text: Access: public (Pech Merle)
Access: public (Cénevières)

days_plan.itin_title: Occitanie Mysteries Road Trip: Cathar Legends & Hidden Secrets of Southern France

visits.text: The Abbaye aux Dames, founded around 1060 by Matilda of Flanders, houses the queen's tomb beneath a black marble slab in the Trinity Abbey Church. A masterpiece of Norman Romanesque art, its capitals carved with fantastical animals and its crypt with its forest of columns transport you to the Middle Ages. The 18th-century conventual buildings, now the seat of the Normandy Region, can be visited with enthusiastic guides.

The Caen Memorial, the leading museum on World War II, presents a gripping journey from the D-Day landings to the Cold War. Period films, reconstructions and testimonies plunge you into 20th-century history with immersive staging. Temporary exhibitions enrich this essential place of remembrance.

William the Conqueror's Castle, one of the largest medieval fortified enclosures in Europe, commands the city centre from its thousand-year-old ramparts. Inside, the Normandy Museum and the Museum of Fine Arts house remarkable collections. The ruins of the ducal palace evoke the power of the conqueror of England.

Explore the Abbaye aux Hommes, where William the Conqueror has rested since 1087. The Saint-Étienne abbey church masterfully combines Romanesque and Gothic architecture. The 18th-century cloister, now the town hall, can be discovered during daily guided tours.

activities.text: Wander through the Vaugueux quarter, the medieval heart of Caen. Its narrow lanes lined with half-timbered houses are home to traditional restaurants and lively terraces.
Visit the marina right in the city centre. Accessible from Ouistreham via the Orne Canal, it offers an unexpected maritime atmosphere just steps from the castle.
Savour Caen's specialities in a traditional cider house. Tripes à la mode de Caen, andouille de Vire and teurgoule are enjoyed with a farmhouse cider from the Pays d'Auge.

days_plan.itin_title: Grand Tour of Normandy: From D-Day Landing Beaches to Étretat's White Cliffs
Calvados Circuit: D-Day Beaches and Normandy
Complete Normandy Road Trip: Cliffs, D-Day Beaches and Mont-Saint-Michel

places.name: Cagnes Sur Mer

visits.text: Climb to Haut-de-Cagnes, the medieval quarter perched on its rocky outcrop. Renoir, Soutine, Modigliani, and Klein fell in love with it. The Grimaldi Castle-Museum, erected in 1309 by Rainier Grimaldi (Admiral of France appointed by Philip the Fair) then transformed into a Baroque palace around 1620, now houses the Olive Museum, a donation of 40 portraits of cabaret singer Suzy Solidor painted by Foujita, Van Dongen, Lempicka, and Cocteau, and trompe-l'œil painted ceilings on the theme of Phaethon struck down by Zeus. Take the free shuttle no. 44 from the bus station to avoid the uphill walk.

Renoir Museum at the Domaine des Collettes: in 1907, Pierre-Auguste Renoir purchased this plot of centuries-old olive trees in Cagnes, seduced by the light. He lived there until his death in 1919, painting despite severe rheumatism that had fused his fingers—some witnesses report that he had brushes tied to his hand. The house-studio has remained in its original state: studio, kitchen, olive grove, everything breathes the end of a life devoted to painting. Open every day except Tuesday.

activities.text: Promenade de la Plage along the seafront: Cagnes has 3.5 km of promenade along the Baie des Anges between Cagnes-sur-Mer and Saint-Laurent-du-Var. Panoramic view of the snow-capped Mercantour in winter. Accessible by bike (rental on-site).

If the calendar permits, the Hippodrome de la Côte d'Azur (one of France's rare seaside racetracks) organizes trotting and galloping races in winter. The esplanade offers at all times an interesting view over the bay.

days_plan.itin_title: The 15 Jewels of the French Riviera and Its Hinterland

map_keywords: Musée Renoir Domaine des Collettes Cagnes-sur-Mer

visits.text: The Pont Valentré has spanned the Lot River since 1308. Three defensive towers rise 40 meters above the water, linked by eight stone arches stretching 172 meters. Look for the carved devil perched atop the central tower—a reminder of the legend about the architect's pact with Satan. The bridge is UNESCO-listed as part of the Way of Saint James pilgrimage routes.

Step inside the Cathedral of Saint-Étienne, consecrated in 1119. Two massive domes on pendentives cover the nave—the largest medieval domes in southwest France, soaring over 30 meters high. The western dome preserves 14th-century murals depicting the stoning of Saint Stephen and eight prophets. The northern portal, carved between 1140 and 1160, illustrates Christ's Ascension in intricate detail.

Behind the cathedral, the Flamboyant Gothic cloister was built between 1493 and 1553. Its galleries feature delicately carved arches sheltering sculptures and coats of arms of successive bishops. The Chapel of Saint-Gausbert, in the former chapter house, contains the Holy Coif—a relic believed to have wrapped Christ's head.

Wander the narrow streets of old Cahors: half-timbered medieval houses, Renaissance twin windows, and soleilhos—those distinctive open-air attics typical of Quercy. Rue Nationale, Place Saint-Urcisse, and the Badernes quarter showcase the finest facades. Beneath the underground parking, you can glimpse the remains of a 1st-century Gallo-Roman amphitheater.

Climb Mont Saint-Cyr for the most breathtaking panorama of Cahors. From 260 meters elevation, your gaze sweeps across the entire meander of the Lot, the cathedral domes, and the Pont Valentré far below. The walk takes 45 minutes with 120 meters of elevation gain.

activities.text: Follow the towpath along the Lot River at the foot of Pont Valentré. The trail passes the Chartreux Fountain—a natural spring—and the Maison de l'Eau, a converted 19th-century pumping station. Stunning views of the bridge's arches from the riverbank.

Visit Cahors market on Wednesday and Saturday mornings. Stalls fill the area around the cathedral and market hall—ranked among France's exceptional markets. Find Rocamadour cheese, Périgord walnuts, Quercy lamb, melons, saffron, local pastries, and Cahors wines.

The Cahors AOC vineyards spread across the hillsides surrounding the town. Malbec, the region's signature grape, produces deep, full-bodied wines nicknamed "black wine." Several estates offer tastings by appointment.

days_plan.itin_title: Lot Itinerary: Cahors, Rocamadour & the Lot Valley

visits.text: The 75-meter UNESCO belfry has dominated the town since 1925. Climb for 360° panoramas over Calais and surroundings. The colorful clock faces recall communal mastery of time.
The Burghers of Calais, Rodin's monumental sculpture inaugurated in 1895, commemorates six notables' sacrifice in 1347. Six draped figures, ropes around their necks, immortalize heroism.
The town hall, built 1911–25, blends Flemish Renaissance and Art Nouveau styles. Jules Desbois's sculptures symbolize lace and fishing, the economic pillars.
The Museum of Fine Arts holds works from the 16th to 20th centuries. In 2025, a new Rodin gallery dedicated to the Burghers presents sketches, models, and archives.
The International City of Lace and Fashion occupies a former lace factory. Nineteenth-century machines, craft history, and haute couture collections.

activities.text: Stroll the sandy beach. An ideal space to relax, watch ferries come and go, and contemplate the English coastline.
Visit the lighthouse at the end of Jetée Carnot, classified as a sea lighthouse. Cylindrical white and green tower, accessible at low tide.
Discover the Saint-Pierre-lès-Calais quarter and its lace heritage. Former factories testify to the industry that made the town world-famous.

days_plan.itin_title: Pas-de-Calais Road Trip: Opal Coast & Historic Belfries

visits.text: Calvi's citadel dominates the bay from its granite spur—a 15th-century Genoese fortress whose motto inscribed on the ramparts, "Semper Fidelis," says everything about the city's attachment to Genoa. In the paved lanes of the upper town, a plaque on some ruins claims "Christopher Columbus was born here in 1441." Historians agree the explorer was probably Genoese, but the Calvais won't hear anything else—the legend is far more entertaining. Also visit the Saint-Jean-Baptiste cathedral in the citadel's center, which houses an organ and artworks from the Genoese period.

Notre-Dame de la Serra, 5 km from Calvi via a road climbing through the maquis, is a chapel perched on a rocky peak at 200 meters altitude. The view from its forecourt embraces the entire Bay of Calvi, the citadel below, Monte Cinto in the background, and on clear days the Ligurian coast on the horizon. The walk up from the road takes 20 minutes.

The Revellata peninsula, beyond the beach, contains a lighthouse and several wild coves accessible on foot or by kayak. It's also one of Balagne's best diving spots, with posidonia meadows and rocky bottoms down to 30 meters. Tour operators offer diving outings from Calvi harbor.

activities.text: Calvi's beach stretches 4 km of white sand between the harbor and the Revellata peninsula. It's well equipped with lifeguard stations, water sports rental, and beach bars. Arrive before 9:30 AM to get space; in July–August the parking fills in an hour.

The Bonifatu forest, 18 km east of Calvi, is the trailhead for hikes toward Balagne's high peaks. An easy 2-hour trail follows the Figarella river to natural pools for swimming. The pine and oak forest offers welcome coolness after the beaches.

days_plan.itin_title: Upper Corsica Road Trip: Cap Corse and Balagne
Complete Tour of Corsica

visits.text: Tristan Island.
Sables Blancs Beach.
Saint-Corentin Cathedral.

activities.text: Locronan Mountain (panoramic view).
Weaving.
Cornouaille Festival (July).

days_plan.itin_title: Finistère, Brittany Road Trip: Breton Headlands, Ponant Islands & Coastal Heritage

visits.text: Cambrai's belfry, a UNESCO World Heritage site, towers 62 meters above the city. The former bell tower of Saint-Martin Church dates from 1447-1474 and served as a watchtower until 1934—lookouts were still posted to signal fires.

Step inside Notre-Dame-de-Grâce Cathedral, a vast 18th-century edifice housing Fénelon's tomb. This funerary monument, sculpted by David d'Angers, depicts the archbishop kneeling, draped in his chasuble.

Saint-Géry Church preserves a Rubens Entombment in a sumptuous baroque altarpiece. Built between 1698 and 1745, it features richly ornamented interiors with a monumental rood screen and 18th-century carved woodwork.

The Porte de Paris, built between 1685 and 1692, was the fortified city's main entrance. A remnant of Spanish fortifications, this imposing stone and brick arch attests to the defensive role of Cambrai, an episcopal seat since the 6th century.

What remains of old Cambrai? The Spanish House, dated 1595, is the city's last half-timbered house. Its timber framing and overhanging stories contrast with surrounding brick façades. It now houses the tourism office.

CambraiScope, an interpretation center in the former Jesuit college, unveils the city's history through relief maps and immersive displays. The models visualize how the city appeared within its ramparts.

The Museum of Fine Arts occupies a 1720 private mansion. Its rooms present a collection of 17th-century Dutch and Flemish paintings, sculptures and archaeological objects found in the Cambrésis region.

activities.text: Descend into the catiches, underground passages carved in white chalk beneath downtown. Quarried since the Middle Ages then used as civilian shelters until 1944, these cool galleries maintain a constant temperature year-round.

The Bêtise de Cambrai, born around 1830 from a manufacturing error, is a caramelized sugar candy striped with mint. Watch confectioners work the paste into long ribbons—artisan know-how passed down for nearly two centuries in the city.

Circle the ramparts along the tree-lined boulevards encircling the historic center. The route follows the former moats, passes the Porte de Paris and offers clear views of the city's steeples—Cambrai is labeled a City of Art and History.
Taste Cambrai specialties in a downtown estaminet. Local cuisine blends Flemish and Picard influences: maroilles flamiche, potjevleesch, carbonnade—generous dishes suited to the northern climate.

days_plan.itin_title: Northern France Circuit: Lille, Belfries and Flemish Heritage
