The dedicated 100-square-meter shop delights collectors with figurines, plush toys, and exclusive collectibles. This playful visit enchants families and awakens adult nostalgia.

Electric boat rides and golf carts on the canal and through Cheverny park. The 115-meter canal dug in the 18th century allows peaceful gliding between shaded banks. Electric golf carts traverse the estate's pathways offering comfortable exploration of the 100-hectare park without fatigue. Changing perspectives on the château reveal classical architectural harmony. The 2-hectare vineyard producing white wine reveals itself along the route through the outbuildings.

Discovering the surrounding Sologne with its characteristic landscapes of heaths, ponds, and pine forests. Winding roads cross villages with typical red-brick houses and châteaux hidden behind foliage. Shimmering ponds attract varied bird life with herons, egrets, and wild ducks. This game-rich region has always been the privileged hunting ground of kings and nobles. Sologne farms testify to preserved vernacular architecture. These landscapes inspired writer Maurice Genevoix.

days_plan.itin_title: Loir-et-Cher Itinerary: Royal Châteaux and the Loir Valley

visits.text: The royal fortress of Chinon, perched on its rocky spur, unfurls its ramparts for nearly a third of a mile. Three enclosures—Fort Saint-Georges, Château du Milieu, Fort du Coudray—chronicle ten centuries of history. Henry II Plantagenet stored his treasury here in the 12th century. It was here, on February 25, 1429, that Joan of Arc recognized Charles VII among his courtiers and convinced him to march to Reims for his coronation.

The royal lodgings, restored after 200 years as ruins, recreate the apartments of Charles VII and Marie d'Anjou. The Histopad tablet offers 360° augmented reality reconstructions: Henry II Plantagenet's Christmas court, the Recognition scene with Joan of Arc, medieval war machines.

The old town of Chinon, at the fortress's foot, lines up half-timbered houses and tufa mansions in a maze of cobbled lanes. Rue Voltaire, the main artery, leads to Place Jeanne-d'Arc. François Rabelais, native son, set several episodes of Gargantua here. The collegiate church of Saint-Mexme preserves a 10th-century nave.

Stroll the quays of the Vienne for panoramic views of the fortress crowning the town. The river winds between vineyards—Chinon's 5,700-acre vineyard, on both banks, produces some of the Loire's most renowned cabernet franc reds.

activities.text: Visit to troglodyte wine caves and AOC Chinon tasting. Ancient tufa quarries, carved into the hillside, house naturally cool cellars. Chinon's cabernet franc reds are the perfect match for Tours rillettes and fouées.
Chinon market on Thursday morning, Place Jeanne-d'Arc. Goat cheeses, rillettes, fouées, garden vegetables and Chinon wines. Authentic Touraine market atmosphere.
Cruise or canoe on the Vienne. The calm river invites lazy paddles with views of the fortress and vineyard slopes. Several outfitters offer routes from one hour to half a day.

days_plan.itin_title: Indre-et-Loire Road Trip: Loire Valley Châteaux, Secret Valleys & Touraine Vineyards

visits.text: Chiroubles, the highest cru of Beaujolais perched at 400 meters altitude, produces elegant and delicate wines nicknamed the 'flowers of Beaujolais.' The village clings to granite hillsides overlooking valleys dotted with vineyards and forests. This elevated position creates a unique microclimate that gives wines their distinctive finesse and floral character.

The Terrasse de Chiroubles offers a breathtaking 360-degree panorama over the entire Beaujolais region. On clear days, the view extends to Mont Blanc and the Alps in the east, the Monts du Forez in the west. Information panels identify the different Beaujolais crus visible from this exceptional belvedere.

The Church of Saint-Étienne, with its Romanesque bell tower, watches over the village from its elevated position. The interior houses a remarkable collection of religious furniture and ex-votos testifying to popular devotion. The stained glass windows filtered by the sun create a mystical atmosphere conducive to meditation.

The Victor Pulliat Museum, housed in a former wine storehouse, pays tribute to this ampelographer who revolutionized viticulture in the 19th century. Pulliat studied grafting to save French vines from phylloxera. Tools, documents, and explanatory panels retrace his essential work for world viticulture.

activities.text: Discover Chiroubles wines through tastings at independent winegrowers who cultivate steep hillsides. This lightest and most aromatic cru of Beaujolais reveals notes of violet, peony, and red fruits. Producers explain the influence of altitude on ripeness and the delicate balance they seek. Small family estates, often spanning 5-10 hectares, welcome visitors with legendary Beaujolais conviviality.

Hike the Sentier des Crus trail that crosses vineyards while offering spectacular views of the Alps. The 8 km marked loop passes through different climats and explains the geological particularities of granite terroir. Resting areas allow you to contemplate the landscape while enjoying a snack. The gentle slopes make this walk accessible to families.

Visit during the Fête des Crus in April, when the entire village celebrates the new vintage. Producers open their cellars for free tastings, street entertainment animates the streets, and regional specialties are served on every corner. The festive and authentic atmosphere showcases Beaujolais conviviality.

Take a mountain bike ride on trails winding through vineyards and forests. Local circuits offer all difficulty levels, from beginner to expert. The varied terrain alternates between forest paths and vineyard roads. Bike rental shops in the village provide quality equipment and detailed maps.

days_plan.itin_title: Ain Road Trip: Bresse Plains and Bugey Mountains

visits.text: The Museum of Art and History devotes its history gallery to the Choletais region's defining moments from Gallo-Roman times forward. Extensive focus on the Vendée Wars that profoundly marked the city in blood and memory.

The Textile and Fashion Museum occupies the former linen bleaching factory built in 1881. Demonstrations on century-old looms reveal the manufacturing stages that made Cholet's textile industry famous.
The museum workshop still weaves Cholet's famous red handkerchief. Red for the blood spilled during the Vendée Wars, white for the monarchy—this local symbol has been sung about since 1900.
Notre-Dame Church rises in the city center. Its architecture testifies to Cholet's reconstruction after the Revolutionary Wars' devastation.
The Oriental Park of Maulévrier, just kilometers from Cholet, is Europe's largest Japanese garden at 29 hectares—a landscape of contemplation where Eastern philosophy meets French countryside.

activities.text: Puy du Fou lies 25 km from Cholet. Voted Best Theme Park in the World, it presents grandiose historical spectacles and enchanting settings. The nighttime Cinéscénie gathers 2,400 volunteer actors in an unforgettable epic performance.
Cholet's Saturday market ranks among Anjou's most important. Local terroir products and regional specialties fill the stalls with authentic flavors.
Parc de Moine and Lac de Ribou offer natural spaces for restoration and recreation. Hiking trails and water activities provide outdoor escape.

days_plan.itin_title: Maine-et-Loire Road Trip: Anjou and Loire Valley Châteaux

visits.text: The Choranche cave is the most spectacular natural site in the northern Vercors. In 1871, drought drove villagers to follow a dry streambed; at the foot of the Presles cliffs (300 meters high), they entered a cavity from which water flowed. It wasn't until 1897 that Oscar Descombaz, speleology pioneer, conducted the first explorations. Open to the public since 1967. Choranche's distinctive feature: thousands of tubular stalactites—"calcite straws" exceeding 3 meters—cover the vaults. They've grown only 5 cm in 100 years. No site in Europe concentrates so many. Bring a sweater: it's permanently 10°C.

Along two underground rivers, you'll encounter olms in their aquariums: blind, depigmented cave amphibians from Slovenia, capable of fasting for months and living up to 80 years. The visit concludes in the Cathedral Room—40 meters in diameter, 25 meters high—with a sound and light show projected onto millennia-old walls. Book ahead in high season.

The cave's outdoor site offers exceptional views of the Presles cliffs and limestone cirque. Before or after the visit, walk the developed path at the foot of the walls: karst geology is visible to the naked eye, with Jurassic limestone strata exposed for hundreds of meters.

activities.text: The balcony trail overlooking the Bourne valley starts from Choranche village and climbs 3-5 km in marked aerial passages along the cliffs. Across the way, the Presles walls with their climbing routes, and below, the river emerging from rock. One of the Vercors' most striking routes—bring hiking shoes.

days_plan.itin_title: Royans-Vercors Road Trip: Hilltop Villages and Gorges

places.name: Cite Architecture Patrimoine

visits.text: The Cité de l'Architecture et du Patrimoine, in the east wing of Palais de Chaillot, is a surprising museum: its gallery of plaster casts presents life-size reproductions of cathedral portals, sculptures, and frescoes from great French monuments—from Chartres to Vézelay, from Conques to Notre-Dame de Paris. You literally walk through the façade of Chartres at 1:1 scale. The gallery of murals and stained glass, contemporary architecture collections, and a direct view of the Eiffel Tower from the windows complete the ensemble. Allow at least 2 hours.

activities.text: Palais de Chaillot, built for the 1937 Universal Exposition on the site of the former Trocadéro, also houses the Maritime Museum and the Museum of Mankind—plan a second visit if these themes interest you. The central terrace between the two wings offers the most perfect view of the Eiffel Tower and the Champ-de-Mars.

days_plan.itin_title: The Great Museums of Paris

map_keywords: Cité de l'Architecture et du Patrimoine Paris
Galerie des Moulages Cité Architecture Paris

places.name: Cite Des Sciences

visits.text: The Cité des Sciences et de l'Industrie, inaugurated in 1986 in the former La Villette slaughterhouses transformed by Adrien Fainsilber, is Europe's largest science museum: 30,000 m² of permanent and temporary exhibitions, a planetarium, a real submarine to visit, and the Géode—a steel sphere 36 meters in diameter with a 1,000 m² hemispherical screen. The interactive exhibitions are calibrated for all age groups. If you're coming with children, block out the entire day. Without children, 2 hours allows you to see the essentials.

activities.text: Parc de la Villette (55 hectares), Paris's largest urban park, surrounds the Cité des Sciences. It houses the Philharmonie de Paris, the Grande Halle, red follies designed by Bernard Tschumi, and themed gardens. In summer, the outdoor cinema is free. A walk from the Cité to the Canal de l'Ourcq takes 30 minutes.

days_plan.itin_title: The Great Museums of Paris

map_keywords: Cité des Sciences et de l'Industrie Paris Villette

visits.text: Saint-Nicolas Church in Civray boasts one of the most ornate Romanesque façades in Poitou, rivaling Notre-Dame-la-Grande. Built in the 12th century, it displays the Wise and Foolish Virgins, the Assumption, the Labors of the Months, and the signs of the Zodiac across two tiers.

Step inside to see the 13th-century fresco recounting the legend of Saint Giles in the south transept. The rest of the interior was painted in the 19th century by Amédée Brouillet, creating a highly colorful atmosphere beneath the octagonal lantern tower.
The Hôtel de la Prévôté, built in the late 15th century, hosted King Louis XIII and his young bride in 1616. Several other private mansions line the town's streets.

Fifteen minutes from Civray, the village of Charroux preserves a tall octagonal tower—a remnant of the 11th-century Saint-Sauveur Abbey—plus a cloister and chapter house. The village is labeled a Small Town of Character.

activities.text: Walk along the Charente River in Civray. A path leads to the leisure area and Île Foin, a 10-minute walk from the center. The town is designated a Green Resort.
Visit local producers in the Civray region: farmhouse cheeses, artisanal apple juice, and Poitevin charcuterie can be found within a few kilometers.

days_plan.itin_title: Vienne Road Trip: Futuroscope and Romanesque Heritage

visits.text: Clairvaux Abbey, founded June 25, 1115 by Saint Bernard, embodied Cistercian spiritual power for centuries. The mother abbey spawned 80 daughter abbeys across Europe. Transformed into prison in 1808, it held up to 2,000 inmates and closed definitively in 2023. Guided tours reveal this dual history: 12th-century Lay Brothers' Building (74m long, exceptional), 18th-century refectory-chapel, 19th-century disciplinary quarter. Heavy atmosphere marked by centuries of confinement.

The Clairvaux Historical Circuit extends around the abbey: Bernard of Clairvaux Museum in Ville-sous-la-Ferté (childhood house), medieval hospice, Val des Choues where Bernard experienced his mystical visions. Interpretation trail connecting key sites of Cistercian history. Allow a full day to complete the circuit including abbey visit.

activities.text: The Clairvaux forest surrounds the abbey with marked hiking trails. Peaceful paths through oak and beech woods, clearings, streams. Easy to moderate circuits from 5 to 15 km. Maps available at Clairvaux tourist office. Meditative walking in the footsteps of Cistercian monks who found solitude and prayer here.

Clairvaux celebrates its Cistercian heritage through cultural events: medieval market in summer, classical music concerts in the abbey, temporary exhibitions on monastic history. Check the calendar before your visit. Special atmosphere in these centuries-old walls. Book tickets in advance for concerts — limited seating.

days_plan.itin_title: Aube Circuit: Champagne, Medieval Heritage and Orient Forest Lakes

visits.text: Tour of the Clairvaux lakes, ideal site for swimming and water activities
Discovery of the Chalain archaeological site, UNESCO World Heritage listed

activities.text: Bike ride around the Clairvaux lakes on the Jurassic Vélo Tour routes
Relaxing break by the lake with views of the Jura mountains

days_plan.itin_title: Jura Lakes & Mountains: Turquoise Waters, Waterfalls & Alpine Cheese Routes

visits.text: Saint-Seine Church.
Museum of the Seven-Year Term (Mitterrand).
Costume Museum.

activities.text: Nivernais Canal.
Paths to Compostela.

days_plan.itin_title: Nièvre, France: Morvan Hills and Ducal Castle Heritage Routes

visits.text: Droiteval Abbey keeps a low profile deep in the Darney forest. Founded in 1130 by lord Aubert de Darney — who wanted, according to texts, to atone for his faults — it's a Cistercian abbey that became a priory in the 15th century, then national property at the Revolution. A royal forge was installed there, then a scythe factory. Restored in the 19th century, it became private property again in 1987 and has since the 2000s housed an Art and Culture Center. Check opening hours before coming — they vary according to seasons and current exhibitions.

activities.text: The Darney forest surrounds the abbey site and offers forest paths easily walkable on foot. If you have energy, continue to Châtillon-sur-Saône, about twenty kilometers south: it's an exceptionally preserved Renaissance village, with 16th and 17th-century mansions that have barely moved since. Little known, rarely crowded — go in the afternoon.

days_plan.itin_title: Vosges Abbey Trail: Monastic Heritage Unveiled

visits.text: Leaving the Mont-Saint-Michel area, stop in Mortain for the Grandes Cascades - the highest in western France, with a 25-meter drop in a green setting. The access path descends through a beech forest. Right next door, the Abbaye Blanche (12th century), a former Cistercian abbey, can be visited from outside - the terraced gardens offer a view of the Cance valley.

Welcome to Suisse Normande - don't expect the Alps, but the steep Orne valleys have surprises in store. The Rocher de la Houle, above Clécy, offers a viewpoint over the Orne's meanders and wooded hills. It's the sector's most photographed vantage point.

Continue to Rocher des Parcs, another viewpoint accessible by a short ridge trail. The view plunges to the river below and the schist cliffs bordering the valley. In autumn, the colors are spectacular.

The Roches d'Oëtre are Suisse Normande's most impressive natural site. From the viewpoint, you overlook the Rouvre gorges from 118 meters. The rock profile, seen from the right angle, draws a human face - look for it. A marked trail circuits the site in 45 minutes.

activities.text: Hike the Orne ridges on a marked route starting from Clécy. You'll follow the high points with views over the valley, river and bocage meadows. This is Normandy's green heart, far from beaches and tourists.

days_plan.itin_title: Grand Tour of Normandy: From D-Day Landing Beaches to Étretat's White Cliffs
Complete Normandy Road Trip: Cliffs, D-Day Beaches and Mont-Saint-Michel

visits.text: Saint-Samson Church raises its Rayonnant Gothic façade from the 13th and 14th centuries. Sculpted portal and rose window dominate the Place d'Armes from the hilltop overlooking the Brèche valley.
The communal belfry has stood since the 12th century as a symbol of urban freedoms. Rebuilt in the 16th century, it marks the town's independence from the Counts of Clermont.
The Town Hall occupies a 16th-century Renaissance building. Its stone and brick façades testify to communal prosperity during the Wars of Religion.

activities.text: Follow the vestiges of medieval ramparts around the historic center. The 12th-century fortifications protected the city perched on its limestone spur.

days_plan.itin_title: Oise Circuit: Imperial Châteaux, Ancient Forests & Living History

visits.text: Notre-Dame-de-l'Assomption Cathedral crowns the historic center's hilltop with its striking black lava silhouette. Construction began in 1248 under master builder Jean Deschamps, making it the first major edifice built entirely from Volvic stone, whose exceptional strength allowed remarkably slender pillars to soar skyward. Inside, a unique collection of stained glass from the 12th to 19th centuries floods the nave with colored light. The twin 315-foot spires, added by Viollet-le-Duc in the 19th century, signal the monument from every quarter of the city.

In the old merchant quarter of Portus, Notre-Dame-du-Port Basilica radiates the warm glow of blonde arkose stone—a striking contrast to the dark volcanic tones that dominate elsewhere. Built in the first third of the 12th century and inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site on the Santiago de Compostela pilgrimage routes, it ranks among the five great Romanesque churches of Auvergne. Its pyramidal apse adorned with lava stone mosaics, 250 narrative capitals, and sculpted portal stand as some of the finest examples of Romanesque art in France. In the crypt, the Black Madonna has drawn pilgrims since 1614.

The Bargoin Museum houses one of Auvergne's richest archaeological collections. The archaeology department spans from prehistory to the Gallo-Roman era with remarkable pieces including the Bronze Age Manson Treasure, a monumental bronze foot, and a unique collection of Gaulish ex-votos from the Roches spring at Chamalières—unmatched anywhere in Europe. The second department showcases non-European textiles from the 18th to 21st centuries, with pieces from Asia, Africa, and Central America.

Across 21,500 square feet of scenographic space, L'Aventure Michelin traces more than a century of industrial history born in Clermont-Ferrand. From the Michelin brothers' first pneumatic tires to contemporary innovations, the journey mingles spectacular vehicles, interactive screens, cinema experiences, and exploration notebooks. The saga of Bibendum, the company's planetary mascot, unfolds through archival documents and unexpected objects. The museum offers bilingual French-English signage with audio guides in multiple languages.

Place de Jaude, Clermont-Ferrand's grandest esplanade, concentrates the city's energy around its fountains and magnolia trees. Bartholdi's equestrian statue of Vercingetorix—created by the sculptor of the Statue of Liberty—dominates the square with its triumphant posture. The 19th-century theater, Saint-Pierre-des-Minimes church, and Galeries de Jaude shopping arcades frame this plaza where strollers, café terraces, and seasonal markets converge.

Vulcania plunges visitors into the heart of the telluric forces that shaped Auvergne. This European volcano park, nestled in the Chaîne des Puys, combines immersive experiences, shows, and educational workshops around volcanoes, geology, and natural phenomena. Highlights include The Chaîne des Puys Story, Awakening of the Giants, and First Flight—a spectacular aerial journey over the volcanoes. The park is accessible by shuttle from Clermont-Ferrand's train station.

Puy de Dôme rises to 4,806 feet, the highest point in the Chaîne des Puys UNESCO World Heritage Site. The Panoramique des Dômes electric rack railway climbs 1,640 feet of elevation in fifteen minutes with departures every 20 to 40 minutes depending on season. At the summit, 360-degree panoramas embrace 80 dormant volcanoes, the Limagne plain, and on clear days, the Alps themselves. Three themed trails traverse the site: the Pilgrims' Path, the Conquerors' Path, and the Curious Path.

The Temple of Mercury ruins atop Puy de Dôme constitute the largest mountain sanctuary in Roman Gaul. Built in the 2nd century, this monumental temple testifies to the site's religious importance since antiquity. An interactive museum space presents the sanctuary's history and archaeological discoveries. A three-level treasure hunt game, The Temple Relics, offers a playful way to discover the site.

activities.text: Wander through the twin historic centers of Clermont and Montferrand, united from two rival cities in 1630. In Clermont, medieval lanes spiral around the cathedral past Renaissance townhouses, sculpted fountains, and volcanic stone facades. Montferrand's protected quarter preserves its bastide grid plan and exceptional private mansions including the celebrated Maison de l'Apothicaire.

Discover Clermont's street art on an urban trail that leads from mural to mural through downtown streets. Five major works coexist with more discreet interventions scattered along the lanes, like the one on rue des Bohêmes evoking Lascaux's cave paintings. The Such'Art route, designed by the Sommet Urbain Clermontois association, guides the curious among art galleries, antique dealers, and wall creations.

Taste Auvergne's signature dishes in the historic center's restaurants. Truffade, Auvergne potée, stuffed cabbage, tripoux, and potato pâté form the local culinary repertoire. Protected designation of origin cheeses—Saint-Nectaire, Bleu d'Auvergne, Cantal, Fourme d'Ambert—accompany meals or stand alone as worthy tastings.

Hike the Muletiers Trail to reach Puy de Dôme's summit on foot. This historic 3-mile path offers a sporty but accessible climb with 1,378 feet of elevation gain. The route crosses moorland and alpine meadow landscapes before opening onto the summit panorama.

Witness the Brocken spectre phenomenon from Puy de Dôme's summit when conditions align. This optical illusion projects the observer's shadow onto the sea of clouds, surrounded by a circular rainbow. Free seasonal activities include guided walks with interpreters, summit concerts, and yoga sessions with volcano views.

Paraglide from Puy de Dôme's summit for thrill-seekers. The site ranks among France's most renowned for free flight thanks to regular updrafts and exceptional panoramas over the volcanic chain. Tandem discovery flights supervised by certified instructors.

days_plan.itin_title: VOLCANOES, ROMANESQUE STONE AND AUVERGNE TRADITIONS
VOLCANOES, ROMANESQUE STONE AND AUVERGNE TRADITIONS
Auvergne's Volcanic Villages Trail
Auvergne Volcanoes Road Trip: Puy de Dôme and Lakes

places.name: Clisson

visits.text: The Château de Clisson has guarded the Sèvre Nantaise since the 12th century. Sprawling across ten acres, this frontier fortress of the Duchy of Brittany bristles with arrow slits and cannon ports carved into weathered stone.

The Church of Notre-Dame crowns its bell tower with a Tuscan campanile—a surprising touch added in the 1800s. After the Vendée Wars razed the town, Clisson was rebuilt in an Italianate style inspired by Tuscany's sun-washed villages.
The medieval wooden market halls rank among France's oldest. Their massive oak beams once sheltered bustling trade, a window into Clisson's prosperous medieval past.
Cross the ancient stone bridge and wander cobbled lanes lined with ochre houses and terracotta roofs. Clisson weaves together medieval bones and Italian soul in an unlikely but enchanting mix.

On the far bank, the Domaine de la Garenne Lemot invites rambling exploration. Temples, follies, and carefully framed views of Clisson evoke the Roman countryside, a romantic fantasy frozen in stone and greenery.

activities.text: The Nantes wine country grows Muscadet on granite slopes. Visit local cellars to taste Clisson cru, an age-worthy Muscadet with smoky notes and candied citrus that defies the grape's simple reputation.
Follow the Sèvre Nantaise on foot or by canoe. The river carves through vineyards, slipping between granite cliffs and past watermills that once powered the valley's industries.

Each June, Hellfest transforms Clisson into the world capital of heavy metal. The legendary festival draws hundreds of thousands of metalheads from across the globe to this unlikely medieval-Italian town.

days_plan.itin_title: Loire-Atlantique Road Trip: Nantes and the Jade Coast

map_keywords: Domaine de la Garenne Lemot Gétigné

visits.text: Cluny Abbey, founded in 910 by William the Pious, became the headquarters of the largest monastic order in the West. At its peak around 1100, the Cluniac order ruled over 10,000 monks and 1,500 monasteries across Europe. The Cluny III abbey church, the largest in Christendom before St. Peter's in Rome, measured 614 feet long.

Of the immense abbey church, only 8% remains after Revolutionary destruction—sold as a stone quarry in 1798 and dynamited by an unscrupulous entrepreneur. The great transept, the bell tower of the Holy Water, and the world's tallest Romanesque vaults still testify to this gigantism.

The Farinier, a 13th-century monastic building, houses the capitals from the ambulatory of Cluny III's choir—carved around 1095, they represent the eight tones of Gregorian chant and the seasons. The 3D digital reconstruction lets you visualize the abbey church in its original dimensions.

The Palais Jean de Bourbon, Museum of Art and Archaeology, is a rare example of flamboyant Gothic seigneurial lodgings. Monumental fireplaces, state rooms, and medieval sculptures from the town and abbey. Don't miss the full-scale reconstruction of the Great Portal of Cluny III.

Climb the 120 steps of the Tour des Fromages, a former abbey enclosure tower, for an exceptional panorama over the abbey-city, Cluny's rooftops, and the Burgundian countryside. The monastic borough below aligns Romanesque and Gothic houses—a rare urban ensemble.

activities.text: Guided tour of the abbey. Your guide reconstructs the scale of the vanished edifice through the vestiges, augmented reality terminals, and an immersive 3D film. The screens act as windows revealing past within present.
Festival of the Grands Crus de Bourgogne. Every summer, concerts in the monks' Farinier and the abbey cloister. Classical and early music in an exceptional medieval setting.

Stroll through the town of Cluny. Romanesque and Gothic houses, two parish churches, the Hôtel-Dieu, and the national stud form a dense heritage landscape. The École Nationale Supérieure d'Arts et Métiers occupies the 18th-century conventual buildings.

days_plan.itin_title: Saône Valley, France: Medieval Abbeys, Wine and Comtois Heritage
Saône-et-Loire Road Trip: Bibracte, Cluny, Solutré & the Mâconnais Vineyards
Burgundy's 15 Most Beautiful Villages

visits.text: The Hennessy house offers the most spectacular tour: solar-powered boat crossing of the Charente, ageing cellars where the 'angel's share' floats in the air, guided tasting. Booking essential.

Martell, the oldest of the great houses (1715), has renovated its historic site with its Art Deco tower. Tour of the centuries-old cellars and the founder's house. A more intimate atmosphere than Hennessy.
The Château de Cognac, birthplace of Francis I, houses Baron Otard cognacs. Renaissance vaulted cellars, rooftop view over the Charente. French history entwined with the story of cognac.
Old Cognac is best explored on foot: medieval houses with walls blackened by the cognac fungus (the famous Torula), merchants' townhouses, quays where gabares loaded barrels.
The Musée des Arts du Cognac traces the history of this spirit: stills, cooperage, vintage labels, retro advertisements. A good complement to cellar tours. Housed in two townhouses.

activities.text: Tour of a major cognac house. Hennessy for the immersive experience, Martell for intimacy, Rémy Martin for connoisseurs (expert tours available). Allow 1h30 to 2h including tasting.
Cruise on the Charente. Traditional gabares with commentary (1h30) or licence-free electric boat hire. The river at the pace of the barrels of old.
Cycling along the Charente. The Flow Vélo (national route) passes through Cognac. Bike hire available in town. Flat route to Jarnac (15 km) or Saint-Brice (8 km).

days_plan.itin_title: Charente Circuit: Cognac and Romanesque Heritage in 9 Days

visits.text: Perched on the cliff overlooking Omaha Beach, the American Cemetery at Colleville-sur-Mer spreads its 9,387 white crosses in perfect rows across an emerald lawn. The headstones face westward toward the homeland, while at the center, the bronze statue 'The Spirit of American Youth Rising from the Waves' watches over this haven of peace. The chapel, the Garden of the Missing and the Visitor Center movingly recount Operation Overlord and the sacrifice of an entire generation.

Walk down to Omaha Beach, the scene of 'Bloody Omaha' on June 6, 1944. The fine sand stretches peacefully today, but the remains of blockhouses and memorials recall the 2,500 American soldiers who fell in a single day. The calm sea contrasts with the memory of waves laden with men and equipment, creating a uniquely contemplative atmosphere.

At Pointe du Hoc, the 30-metre cliffs bear the indelible marks of bombardment. Moon-like craters, shattered bunkers and gaping casemates bear witness to the heroic assault by Colonel Rudder's 225 Rangers, who scaled these walls at dawn on D-Day. The site, managed by the American administration, offers a striking panorama over the English Channel and illustrates the ferocity of the fighting.

activities.text: Walk the coastal path between Omaha Beach and Pointe du Hoc. Orientation tables line this historic route offering spectacular views of the cliffs and the sea.
Attend the flag-lowering ceremony at the American Cemetery. Every day at 4 pm, the American flag is retired to the sound of Taps in reverent silence.

days_plan.itin_title: Calvados Circuit: D-Day Beaches and Normandy

visits.text: The Royal Château plunges its stones into the Mediterranean, standing since the 13th century. Built by the Kings of Majorca then remodeled by Vauban, it offers a circular view over the bay and the Albères mountains.

The Church of Our Lady of the Angels rises from the waves with its 17th-century lighthouse-bell tower. Its baroque altarpiece by Joseph Sunyer, covered in gold leaf, features 97 figurines and statues.
Fort Saint-Elme dominates the bay with 360° views. This 1538 fortress controlled the passage between Collioure and Port-Vendres.
The Fauvism Showcase displays reproductions and documents about Matisse and Derain. It's the starting point for the Fauvism Trail.
Step inside the Chapel of Saint Vincent, perched on its rocky outcrop. It houses the patron saint's relics brought back from Rome in 1700.

activities.text: Follow the Fauvism Trail. Reproductions mark the exact spots where Matisse and Derain set up their easels in 1905.
Get lost in the narrow streets of Mouré and Port d'Avall. The ochre-pink facades capture the light that seduced the painters.
Admire the colorful Catalan fishing boats on the pebble beach. Fishermen still haul in their nets from 5 a.m. onward.
Taste Collioure's AOC anchovies. The 16th-century cellars preserve Banyuls and Collioure wines, fortified on site.

days_plan.itin_title: Pyrénées-Orientales Road Trip: French Catalonia and the Vermilion Coast

visits.text: Saint-Pierre Church and its sculpted tympanum. Romanesque building from the 11th century with a rare detail: the tympanum representing Christ's Ascension is made of white Turenne limestone, which contrasts with the surrounding red sandstone. The octagonal gabled bell tower exceeds 20 meters. The church was divided in two during the Wars of Religion to accommodate both religious services simultaneously.

Penitents' Chapel (Saint-Maximin). 14th-century Gothic chapel, it served as a burial place for noble families including the Maussacs (coat of arms visible on a vault keystone). From the mid-17th to the late 19th century, the Black Penitents brotherhood met here. Saved from ruin in 1927 by the Friends of Collonges, it houses a stained glass window by Francis Chigot (1937) depicting the Resurrection, and two contemporary stained glass windows by Didier Mencoboni and Olivier Juteau (2016).

Vassinhac Castle. Built in 1583, this manor belonged to the lords of Collonges who were also governors of the Viscounty of Turenne. The facade features typical Renaissance mullioned windows, and two round towers frame the structure. Not open for interior visits.

Mermaid House. 16th-century residence that owes its name to the mermaid sculpted to the right of the entrance door. It houses a small museum of folk arts and traditions with a reconstruction of a traditional Corrèze interior.

16th-century grain hall. Under this covered hall, the wine and walnut oil market was held. The floor is paved with red sandstone. It was here that Charles Ceyrac, former mayor, launched the association of Most Beautiful Villages of France in 1982. Collonges was the first village to receive the label.

activities.text: Walk through the streets with 25 towers. Allow 2 hours for the complete tour. The noble houses with blue schist or slate roofs were built by officers of the Viscounty of Turenne between the 15th and 16th centuries. Vines climb the red facades - the green/red contrast is striking. The red sandstone comes from a geological fault between Brive and Meyssac, rich in iron oxide.
Tasting of Corrèze straw wine. This sweet wine is produced from grapes dried on straw racks. A few local producers offer tastings in the village or around Meyssac.
Meyssac Market. 1.5 km from Collonges, the neighboring village holds a market on Tuesdays and Fridays year-round, and on Sundays in July-August.

days_plan.itin_title: Corrèze Road Trip: Villages and Dordogne Valley
Nouvelle-Aquitaine Loop: Medieval Villages, Vineyards & Dordogne Valley

visits.text: Little Venice follows the Lauch River for a few hundred meters — half-timbered houses in ochre, rose, and blue reflect in the canal, geraniums spill from every balcony, flat-bottomed boats glide beneath the bridges. It's the most photographed site in Alsace, and the crowds know it. Arrive early in the morning if you want the reflections to yourself.

The Unterlinden Museum occupies a former 13th-century Dominican convent. Its centerpiece is the Isenheim Altarpiece, painted by Matthias Grünewald between 1512 and 1516 for the Antonine commandery at Isenheim — a hospital treating victims of Saint Anthony's Fire. This seven-panel polyptych ranks among the most expressive works in Western painting: the Crucifixion depicts Christ's body with wounds meant to comfort the sick by making them feel understood in their suffering. It survived two world wars hidden in banks and Périgord châteaux. Book your ticket in advance — queues can be long in season.

The Collegiate Church of Saint-Martin lifts its two unequal spires above the old town, a project spanning from 1235 to 1365. Look up at the carved west portal and its gargoyles before entering to see the stained glass and the Alsatian Gothic nave.

The Pfister House, built in 1537 for a draper named Ludwig Scherer, is one of the finest Renaissance residences in Alsace. Its corner oriel, wall frescoes, and long carved wooden gallery give it the air of stone lacework. Note that it now houses a wine cellar — you can go inside for a tasting.

Detour through the Tanners' Quarter on rue de la Poissonerie. These narrow half-timbered houses, built in the 16th and 17th centuries, had their upper floors open to the air for drying hides. Also check out the Maison des Têtes at 3 rue des Marchands: its 1609 façade is decorated with over a hundred grotesque faces.

activities.text: Embark on a flat-bottomed boat ride along the Lauch from Little Venice. Operators offer these trips in season — expect about twenty minutes gliding past the colorful façades from the water. Book ahead or wait for a boat at the Poissonerie quay.

Wander the lanes of old Colmar hunting for details that go unnoticed: the carved draper figure on a façade along rue des Marchands, the wrought-iron shop signs, the medieval interior courtyards. The tourist office offers themed walking routes that ensure you miss nothing.

Late in the day, push open the door of a winstub — these traditional Alsatian wine taverns — to taste a flammenkueche (tarte flambée) or baeckeoffe paired with a glass of Riesling or Pinot Gris. It's the best moment to catch the local atmosphere away from the tourist rush hour.

days_plan.itin_title: Alsace's Most Beautiful Christmas Markets
Upper Rhine Road Trip: Colmar and the Southern Wine Route

visits.text: Colmars-les-Alpes sits at 1,250 meters altitude in the Upper Verdon valley, on the border between alpine and Provençal influences: medieval ramparts, flowered fountains, sundials on facades, and tall houses with colorful shutters. Founded by Romans under the name Collis Martis, the town received its first fortifications in 1382 when the neighboring village of Allos passed to the Duchy of Savoy. Classified as one of France's Most Beautiful Villages in September 2025, first stroll its lanes and the 500-meter rampart walk that encircles the town.

Fort de Savoie is the landmark monument: built between 1693 and 1696 by engineer Creuzet de Richerand (following Vauban's plans, though Vauban never visited the site), it guarded the border with Savoy. Classified as a Historic Monument since 1923, it houses an immersive exhibition with projections and reconstructed scenes about Colmars's history. Guided tours last about 1h30 — offered in July-August. Fort de France, on the downstream side, can't be visited but is clearly visible from the ramparts.

In the rampart walk house-museum, a reconstructed 19th-century bourgeois apartment brings you into the daily life of the fortified town. And if you have 20 minutes, head to the Apothecary's Garden: dedicated to the 17th-century village apothecary, it brings together aromatic and medicinal plants from the Upper Verdon — a peaceful and fragrant garden just steps from the ramparts.

activities.text: The Lance waterfall is reached on foot from the village in 20 minutes (easy marked trail, accessible to families). Its natural pools are suitable for swimming in summer — the stream is cold and the spot remains uncrowded. On the way back, take the Adroit Canal trail (4 km loop, 2h, interpretive signs about fortified heritage) for a balcony view of the ramparts and Mercantour peaks.

Colmars stands at the gates of Mercantour National Park: in summer, half-day hikes lead to alpine pastures and high-altitude lakes. Operators also offer canyoning outings in the nearby gorges — a fine option if the weather is warm.

days_plan.itin_title: Forcalquier & Verdon Gorges Road Trip

visits.text: The Charles de Gaulle Memorial nestles at the foot of the Cross of Lorraine. Across 1,600 square meters, exhibitions trace 20th-century history through the General's extraordinary life.
Giant-screen films, immersive environments, and archival footage recreate the atmosphere of each era. A panoramic elevator connects the memorial to the cross above.
The pink granite Cross of Lorraine soars 44 meters high. Visible from 30 kilometers away, it was inaugurated on June 18, 1972—the anniversary of de Gaulle's historic 1940 appeal.
La Boisserie, purchased in 1934, preserves the salon, library, and study where the General penned his War Memoirs. The tower offers views across the Haute-Marne plains he loved.
The de Gaulle family tomb rests in the village cemetery. Its stark simplicity contrasts powerfully with the monumental cross on the hillside.

activities.text: Visit the Memorial in early afternoon. Allow two hours to explore the chronological sequences and thematic spaces documenting France's turbulent century.
Stroll through La Boisserie's grounds. The General cherished these landscapes he described in his memoirs as embodying France's essence.

days_plan.itin_title: Haute-Marne Discovery: Langres & Historic Heritage

visits.text: Chateaubriand Castle.
Tranquil Lake.
Old town.

activities.text: Lake (walk).
Fishing).
Literary heritage.

days_plan.itin_title: Ille-et-Vilaine, Brittany: Saint-Malo and Medieval Walled Towns
Brittany's Mystical Coast: Ancient Megaliths, Abbey Ruins & Celtic Legends
Brittany Road Trip: Historic Villages, Sea and Heritage

places.name: Commercy

visits.text: Château Stanislas de Commercy, classified as a Historic Monument, was embellished by the Prince of Vaudémont then by Stanislas Leszczynski, former King of Poland and Louis XV's father-in-law. Architects Boffrand and Dorbay gave it its current appearance. Partly destroyed in 1944, it was identically rebuilt from 1957 to 1977.

Cross the semicircular Place du Fer à Cheval, bordered by two symmetrical buildings extending the castle. The stables redesigned by architect Héré recall the pavilions of Place Stanislas in Nancy: continuous rustication, semicircular bays, and balustrades adorned with trophies.
Saint-Pantaléon church blends a 17th-century nave with a 19th-century choir in Champagne Gothic style. Its sober interior contrasts with the richness of liturgical furnishings.

The Museum of Ceramics and Ivory is housed in the former municipal bathhouses in Art Deco style (1930), inspired by Stanislas's royal pavilion. It holds one of France's most important ivory collections, from the 17th to 19th centuries, complemented by porcelains and earthenware.
Place Charles de Gaulle houses the former Town Hall built under Stanislas. Nearby, a 1903 pharmacy with Art Nouveau decor testifies to the town's elegance at the turn of the century.

activities.text: Step into an artisanal madeleine factory to watch the making of these small shell-shaped cakes, born in the kitchens of Stanislas's castle in the 18th century. Tasting on site.

Walk along the Meuse following the Promenade Stanislas, which crosses the town's different districts within 18th-century boundaries. The river draws meanders bordered by willows and classical architecture.
Visit the Priory of Breuil, founded in the 11th century, which occupies an important place in Commercy's religious history. Guided tours trace its successive transformations.

days_plan.itin_title: Meuse Circuit: Verdun and the Citadels

map_keywords: Place du Fer à Cheval Commercy

visits.text: The Château de Compiègne unfolds its neoclassical façades built between 1751 and 1788 by Ange-Jacques Gabriel. Louis XV, Louis XVI, then Napoleon I and Napoleon III made it a major residence alongside Versailles and Fontainebleau.

The Imperial Apartments showcase the unified First Empire décor of 1808-1810. Architect Louis-Martin Berthault completely remodeled the interior with Jacob-Desmalter furnishing and Girodet creating painted decorations.

The Ballroom Gallery was built in 1810 for Marie-Louise of Austria's arrival. Napoleon welcomed his future empress here on March 27, 1810, exactly as Marie-Antoinette had been received forty years earlier.

The Second Empire Museum evokes Napoleon III's famous Séries de Compiègne. Each autumn, one hundred guests succeeded each other for four to six weeks of hunting, concerts, and theatrical performances in quasi-imperial intimacy.

The Armistice Clearing preserves the railway car where the 1918 and 1940 armistices were signed. On November 11, 1918 at 5:15 AM, Marshal Foch received Germany's capitulation in dining car 2419D of the Wagons-Lits Company.

The Armistice Memorial displays car 2439D, fitted out identically after the original was destroyed in 1945. Hitler had demanded that the June 1940 armistice be signed in the same place to avenge the 1918 humiliation.

activities.text: Wander through the Forest of Compiègne among beech groves and centuries-old oaks. Fourteen thousand hectares of forest unfold just steps from the château.

days_plan.itin_title: Oise Circuit: Imperial Châteaux, Ancient Forests & Living History

visits.text: Fortified walled town.
Fishing port.
Fishing Museum.

activities.text: Blue Nets Festival (August).
Beaches.
Fish auction.

days_plan.itin_title: Finistère, Brittany Road Trip: Breton Headlands, Ponant Islands & Coastal Heritage
Brittany Road Trip: Historic Villages, Sea and Heritage

visits.text: Condom's Saint-Pierre Cathedral has watched over the Baïse River since the 16th century. Built on the site of an 11th-century abbey church, it follows the classic plan of southern Gothic churches: a single wide nave flanked by lateral chapels squeezed between buttresses. Its 19th-century choir draws inspiration from Albi's Sainte-Cécile Cathedral. Bossuet briefly served as bishop here in 1669-1670.

The 16th-century cloister adjoining the cathedral unfolds vast galleries with elegant flamboyant Gothic arcades. It once led bishops to their private chapel. The eastern gallery showcases three pointed arches adorned with nine painted keystones bearing the coats of arms of the lord bishops. On the square outside, the Musketeers statue freezes the moment: Athos, Porthos, Aramis and d'Artagnan crossing swords.

The Armagnac Museum occupies the outbuildings of the former bishop's palace. Built around a monumental 19th-century beam press, the rooms trace the complete production cycle of Gascony's signature brandy: viticultural techniques, alembic distillation and barrel-making. France's oldest eau-de-vie certainly deserves its own museum.

Wander through the lanes of the old episcopal city. The 17th and 18th-century townhouses—Polignac, Cugnac—display their stone façades and wrought iron flourishes, witnesses to the wealth of armagnac merchants. The Baïse, once nicknamed the "silver river," carried barrels downstream to Bordeaux.

Head to the marina quays along the Baïse. Developed in the 18th century for river trade, the waterway remains navigable and offers a peaceful setting bordered by plane trees. Condom is listed among Occitanie's Grand Sites under the title "Armagnac, Abbey and Cities."

Larressingle, nicknamed the "little Carcassonne of Gers," is France's smallest fortified city. Its 13th-century ramparts stretch 900 feet and rise 20 to 26 feet high, enclosing a castle-keep, a Romanesque church dedicated to Saint Sigismond and a handful of golden stone houses. Cross the restored drawbridge and step back seven centuries.

Larressingle's Saint-Sigismond Church displays sober Romanesque architecture with a typically Gascon bell-wall. Curious detail: the saint's statue, commissioned from a Toulouse workshop, is actually a scaled-down version of Aimé Millet's Vercingétorix. A rather odd mustachioed Gaul watches over this medieval village.

Flaran Abbey, a Cistercian monastery founded in 1151 in the Baïse valley, ranks among the best-preserved in southwestern France. Romanesque church, cloister, chapter house, refectory and gardens compose a monastic ensemble of remarkable legibility. In 1913, the Gers archaeological society prevented its cloister from being dismantled and shipped to New York's Cloisters museum.

In the former monks' dormitory at Flaran, the Simonow Collection displays over 300 works of European art from the 16th to 20th centuries. Cézanne, Renoir, Picasso, Monet, Braque, Rodin—a small Gascon Louvre assembled by an Anglo-Saxon philanthropist who fell in love with the Gers. The department's most visited site.

Montréal-du-Gers, Gascony's first bastide, founded in 1255 by Alphonse de Poitiers, brother of Saint Louis. Perched on a rocky promontory, it preserves its grid layout, stone arcades, ogival fortified gate and half-timbered houses. Classified as one of France's Most Beautiful Villages.

A few miles from Montréal, the Gallo-Roman villa of Séviac reveals an immense 4th-century palace. Enfilade of rooms, marble colonnades, lavish baths and polychrome mosaic pavements—unique in Europe for their number and state of preservation. The motifs of flowers, vine scrolls and birds unfold like sumptuous carpets.

activities.text: Guided tour of the episcopal city. The tourist office offers commentary-led circuits unveiling the secrets of the cathedral, cloister, townhouses and the Way of Saint James that passes through town.

Armagnac tasting in the town's cellars. Producers open their doors to explain the distillation process and offer guided tastings. Floc de Gascogne—a blend of grape must and armagnac—is served chilled as an aperitif.

Stroll along the Armagnac greenway, built on a former railway line. This flat, shaded path is perfect for cycling or walking, connecting Condom to neighboring villages through vineyards and hillsides.

Medieval siege camp at the gates of Larressingle. Life-size catapults, trebuchets, stone-throwers and crossbows: operate medieval war machines. Firing demonstrations and activities for the whole family.
Hike from Larressingle to Condom on the GR 65, the Way of Saint James. Three miles through vineyards and hillsides, passing the UNESCO-listed Artigues bridge. A pilgrim's path accessible to everyone.
Montréal-du-Gers market on Friday mornings. Under the bastide's arcades, producers offer foie gras, shepherd's cheeses, seasonal vegetables and Côtes de Gascogne wines. Guaranteed Gascon atmosphere.

days_plan.itin_title: Haute-Garonne Road Trip: From Toulouse to the Pyrenees
Gers Road Trip: Gascon Bastides, Armagnac & Musketeers
Gers Road Trip: Gascon Bastides, Armagnac & Musketeers

visits.text: Condrieu, ancient city of river merchants on the Rhône's right bank, had connections with the Phocaeans of Massalia. Its steep hillsides carry one of the Rhône Valley's most prestigious appellations: 540 acres of Viognier clinging to granite rock on narrow terraces supported by dry-stone walls—the 'chaillées.'

Condrieu's vineyard, an AOC since 1940, nearly vanished in the 20th century: after phylloxera, wars, and industrialization, only about 25 acres remained in the 1950s. Passionate winegrowers saved it by replanting abandoned hillsides in the 1980s. Viognier expresses itself here like nowhere else.

The Rhône riverbanks at Condrieu offer a peaceful walk between river and vineyards. The landscape of terraced hillsides, supported by centuries-old walls, ranks among the most spectacular in French wine country. Across the river on the left bank, the village of Les Roches-de-Condrieu reflects in calm waters.

Three miles north, Ampuis is the birthplace of Côte-Rôtie appellation, one of the world's greatest Syrah crus. Slopes steepen to 60%! The 'cheys,' stone walls supporting the vines, sometimes date to Roman times. The landscape bears the Vignobles & Découvertes label.

activities.text: Tasting of Condrieu and Côte-Rôtie in winegrowers' cellars. Condrieu, 100% Viognier, unfolds aromas of peach, apricot, and honeysuckle. Côte-Rôtie, Syrah on vertiginous slopes, is an age-worthy red of remarkable depth.

Walk through Condrieu's terraced vineyards. Trails between dry-stone chaillées lead to natural viewpoints over the Rhône valley. Work is almost entirely manual—mechanization is impossible on these slopes.

Water sports on the Rhône and Condrieu lake. Paddleboarding, kayaking, wakeboarding, and inflatable obstacle courses at the leisure base. The water ski cable system lets you learn water skiing without a boat.

days_plan.itin_title: Rhône Road Trip: Lyon, Golden Stone Villages of Beaujolais & Condrieu Vineyards

visits.text: The Pont-Vieux in Confolens has spanned the Vienne since the 11th century (on ancient foundations). Postcard view of half-timbered houses reflected in the water. This is where the Goire meets the Vienne — hence the name 'confluent'.
The old town climbs the hill in winding lanes. Medieval half-timbered houses, craft shops, small shaded squares. The Saint-Barthélemy quarter keeps its old-world atmosphere.
The church of Saint-Maxime overlooks the upper town. Sculpted Romanesque doorway, square bell tower typical of Poitou. Inside, carved historiated capitals and contemporary stained glass. Free entry.

activities.text: Walk along the Vienne. The towpath leads from the Old Bridge towards the countryside. Anglers, grey herons, abandoned locks. Peaceful atmosphere guaranteed.
Canoeing on the Vienne. The watersports centre offers routes from 2 hours to a full day. Calm water, no technical difficulty. Hire includes life jackets and dry bags.

Confolens Festival (mid-August). Since 1958, France's biggest folklore festival. Troupes from around the world, costumed parades, free concerts. If you're passing through at this time, it's unmissable.

days_plan.itin_title: Charente Circuit: Cognac and Romanesque Heritage in 9 Days

visits.text: The Sainte-Foy Abbey Church was built from 1041 by Abbot Odolric. The Last Judgement tympanum features 124 sculpted figures on 24 blocks of yellow limestone. The interior contains 250 Romanesque capitals spread over two levels.

The Treasury houses the reliquary statue of Saint Foy, a unique piece of Carolingian goldsmith work set with gold and precious stones. This collection bears witness to nine centuries of pilgrimage on the Way of Saint James.

Pierre Soulages worked from 1987 to 1994 to create 104 stained glass windows for the abbey church. He carried out nearly 700 trials with master glassmaker Jean-Dominique Fleury to achieve a unique translucent glass. The light changes throughout the day thanks to variations in grain size.

activities.text: The Conques Nocturnes take place from May to October to illuminate the polychrome tympanum. The reimagined polychromy reveals the restored medieval colours.

Visit the abbey church galleries to get up close to the Soulages stained glass windows. The guided tour lasts 45 minutes and explores the painter's creative approach. Booking recommended, open to visitors aged 12 and above.

days_plan.itin_title: Aveyron Road Trip: Causses, Tarn Gorges and Villages

visits.text: Conques-en-Rouergue - The village appears around a bend, nestled in a shell-shaped valley ('conca' in Occitan) above the Dourdou. Classified as one of France's Most Beautiful Villages since 1982, it's a major stop on the Santiago de Compostela pilgrimage route (via the Le Puy route). Wander the sloping streets among the half-timbered houses and slate roofs. The place was 'discovered' by Prosper Mérimée in 1837, who launched the restoration of the abbey church.

Abbey Church of Sainte-Foy - Take your time here, it's one of the masterpieces of French Romanesque art. Built from 1041 by Abbot Odolric, it served as a prototype for the great pilgrimage churches (like Saint-Sernin in Toulouse or Santiago de Compostela Cathedral). The tympanum of the Last Judgment, sculpted in the 12th century on 24 limestone blocks, shows 124 figures distributed across three registers - the saved on the left, the damned on the right, Christ in the center. Look for traces of the original polychromy. Inside, 250 sculpted Romanesque capitals on two levels. Since 1994, the 104 stained glass windows by Pierre Soulages - born in Rodez in 1919, 40 km away - bathe the nave in a unique translucent light. Soulages spent eight years (1986-1994) and hundreds of trials to create this special glass with variable grain size. If you're there between May and October, come back for the Nocturnes: illumination of the tympanum at 10:15 PM, visit of the galleries with music at 9:30 PM.

Treasury of Conques - Don't leave Conques without visiting the south wing of the cloister. This collection of medieval goldsmithing is the richest in France. The centerpiece: the Majesty of Sainte Foy, a Carolingian-era reliquary statue covered in gold, silver and precious stones. The relics of the young martyr from Agen (beheaded in 303) were 'borrowed' by a Conques monk in the 9th century - a pious theft that made the place's fortune. Allow 30 to 45 minutes to tour it.

activities.text: Bancarel viewpoint - For the best overall view of Conques, descend from the abbey via the GR 62 toward the Dourdou, cross the footbridge and climb about a hundred meters. From this promontory facing the village, you take in the entire medieval city, the slate roofs, the abbey church and the valley. This is also the classic postcard viewpoint.

Pilgrims' Bridge over the Dourdou - Descend to this medieval bridge (called 'Roman') below the village. The walk along the Dourdou is peaceful and gives you a different perspective on the abbey church that dominates the valley. This is where pilgrims arrived - and still arrive - on the Compostela route.

visits.text: The 19th-century Grands Moulins dominate the Essonne River with their metallic industrial architecture. Saint Louis built his château here before departing for the Crusades.
Founded in 957 to house the relics of Saint Exuperius, the Cathedral of Saint-Spire blends Romanesque and Gothic styles. Its bell tower dominates the river valley.
The marble effigy of Count Haymon rests in the choir. This 14th-century sculpture rivals those at Saint-Denis for its exquisite craftsmanship.
The 17th-century organ was restored after its destruction by Prussian forces. The stained glass windows trace the history of the relics preserved in the primitive church.
The former Church of Saint-Jean-en-L'Isle housed a commandery of the Knights of Malta. Though deconsecrated, it has been a classified historic monument since the 12th century.

activities.text: The Juine and Louette rivers flow through town, creating peaceful small canals. Follow the paths along the right bank past the old mills.
Corbeil-Essonnes Jazz Festival takes place between June and July with several days of open-air concerts throughout the town.
La Nacelle Park and Chantemerle Park offer over 200 hectares of green spaces. The town has earned three flowers in the Flowered Towns competition.

days_plan.itin_title: Essonne Journey: Castles and River Valleys

days_plan.itin_title: Nièvre, France: Morvan Hills and Ducal Castle Heritage Routes

visits.text: Cordes-sur-Ciel reveals itself on foot as you climb its cobbled lanes through five fortified walls. Founded in 1222 as a bastide by Count Raymond VII of Toulouse, it's one of Occitanie's oldest hilltop towns.

Stop before the Maison du Grand Fauconnier: its 13th-century Gothic façade is carved with fantastical creatures and mythological scenes. Next door, the Maison du Grand Veneur displays hunting bas-reliefs of remarkable precision.
The church of Saint-Michel dates from 1269. Its southern Gothic architecture with a single nave was expanded twice in the 14th and 15th centuries. The side chapels deserve careful attention.

Step inside the Charles-Portal Museum, housed in the Porte des Ormeaux, a classified medieval military structure. Here you'll find carved falcons, an old 16th-century town hall door, and films about the market hall's well, which plunges more than 100 meters deep.

Under the 14th-century market hall—Cordes' commercial heart for seven hundred years—locate the vertiginous well that drops more than 100 meters beneath your feet. The surrounding terraces remain the townspeople's gathering place.

The Jardin des Paradis, classified as a Remarkable Garden, occupies the summit of the citadel behind a white stone wall. Botanical squares, forgotten species, and refreshing pools compose a timeless retreat.

Forty minutes by road, Bruniquel perches atop a cliff overlooking the Aveyron gorges. Two castles face each other: the Old (13th century) and the Young (15th century), separated by a wall born of family feuds lasting three centuries.

In the Old Castle, the Renaissance gallery overlooks the Aveyron 90 meters below. The knights' hall preserves 13th-century paired windows and sculpted capitals. One room recounts the discovery of Bruniquel Cave, whose structures date back 176,500 years.

activities.text: Follow Cordes' heritage trail (roughly 2-hour loop). The route offers views of the citadel from surrounding hills and passes through the five remaining fortified gates out of the original fifteen.

Rise early to admire Cordes from the Cérou valley: on certain mornings, mist envelops the hill and only the citadel emerges from the clouds. This spectacle inspired its name 'sur-Ciel' (in the sky) in 1993.
Artisan workshops—ceramicists, jewelers, wood sculptors, leather craftspeople—line the upper town's lanes. Push open their doors and discuss their craftsmanship.
The AOC Gaillac vineyard spreads at Cordes' foot, with over 2,000 years of winemaking history. Nearby estates offer tastings of reds, dry whites, sparkling wines, and rosés.

days_plan.itin_title: Occitanie's 13 Most Beautiful Villages
Tarn Road Trip: Albi & Hilltop Villages

visits.text: Château de Cormatin, built between 1605 and 1616, preserves the most sumptuous Louis XIII painted apartments in France. The marquise's cabinet of curiosities, with blue-and-gold ceilings covered in allegorical scenes, is a jewel of Mannerism. The French and English gardens, boxwood mazes, and embroidered parterres complete the picture.

The gardens of Château de Cormatin, award-winning on multiple occasions, unfold across 30 acres of greenery. The flowering kitchen garden, labyrinth, and herb garden are organized around water features and classical perspectives. An ideal setting for a stroll after touring the interiors.

Brancion, a fortified village perched on a rocky spur, dominates the Grosne valley. Its castle (10th–14th century), medieval lanes, and Romanesque church of Saint-Pierre (12th century) with interior frescoes form a remarkably preserved ensemble. No cars in the village—you climb on foot.

Brancion's Romanesque church, simple and luminous, preserves 12th-century frescoes depicting Christ in Majesty and the apostles. The recumbent effigy of a lord of Brancion, in armor, lies in the nave. From atop the castle keep, views extend to the Bresse, the Jura, and sometimes the Alps.

activities.text: Guided tour of Château de Cormatin. Guides reveal the details of the painted ceilings, the symbols hidden in the allegories, and the turbulent history of this residence that passed through multiple owners' hands.

Exploration of Brancion. The village is explored on foot, from the parking area below up to the keep. The path crosses the ruins of the covered market, fortified gates, and medieval houses. Allow 90 minutes for the castle, church, and panorama.
Wine tasting from the Côte Chalonnaise. The appellations Mercurey, Givry, and Rully produce distinctive Pinot Noirs and Chardonnays. Wine cellars abound in the surrounding villages.

days_plan.itin_title: Saône-et-Loire Road Trip: Bibracte, Cluny, Solutré & the Mâconnais Vineyards

visits.text: Perched on its rocky peak above the Cèze, Cornillon is a medieval village listed in the inventory of protected sites, still little known. Its history is directly linked to the turbulence of the Avignon papacy: in the 14th century, Guillaume II Roger de Beaufort, brother of Pope Clement VI, purchased the lordship. At his death in 1380, the castle inventory revealed 40 rooms, two chapels and a 6-level keep tower. But in 1632, the Duke Henri II de Montmorency, owner of the castle, was beheaded in Toulouse for conspiring with Gaston d'Orléans against Richelieu. His property was destroyed: Cornillon castle was dismantled. The remaining walls, pierced with large windows, still dominate the valley from the village heights.

Walk along the ramparts to Place du Barry: an orientation table allows you to identify hamlets and reliefs in the verdant Cèze plain. The current church was built in one of the castle chapels whose external wall is formed by the old ramparts. Below the village, near the washhouse, you can see medieval sarcophagi found near the old church — destroyed by Protestant troops in the 16th century.

Since 2013, the festival 'Cornillon se la joue au château' takes over the courtyard each first week of August for open-air theater performances in a unique setting. Outside the season, the ruins are freely accessible from the outside — the view from the courtyard over the valley alone justifies the detour.

activities.text: The trail that descends from the village towards the Cèze gorges (4 km loop, ~1h30, easy level) runs along abandoned agricultural terraces, dry stone walls and olive trees. The river below offers swimming spots in limestone pools — much less crowded than those at Aiguèze or La Roque-sur-Cèze.

A few kilometers from Cornillon, the Saint-Sauveur chapel in the hamlet of Saint-Gély (12th century) is accessible on foot via marked trails. It's characteristic of rural Romanesque art in Gard, simply built in light limestone.

days_plan.itin_title: Cèze Valley and Ardèche Gorges Circuit

places.name: Correncon En Vercors

visits.text: Corrençon-en-Vercors separated from Villard-de-Lans in 1857 to form its own commune. It's the southernmost village of the Villard-Corrençon ski domain—the Vercors' largest alpine ski domain, linked since 1984, with 125 km of trails from 1,143 to 2,050 meters altitude. The alpine village is smaller and quieter than Villard, with its chalets, flagstone walls, and small squares. It's a good starting point for the High Plateaus, a Nature Reserve since 1985.

Clearings and trail heads to high combes open just outside the village. The view of the Grande Moucherolle and Roc Cornafion (2,049 m) from the pastures above Corrençon is one of the most beautiful in the Isère Vercors. From these heights you understand this massif's uniqueness: a plateau closed on all sides by cliffs, a natural fortress that explains the 1944 maquis.

activities.text: The panoramic loop at the High Plateaus edge (6-8 km) starts from Clos de la Balme, Corrençon's ski base, and climbs to the first ridges of the Nature Reserve. The path to Grand Veymont (Vercors' highest point at 2,341 m) is glimpsed in the distance. This walk is the perfect conclusion to a Royans-Vercors circuit: you look back toward the Isère plain you left 14 days earlier.

The heritage walk through the village—flagstone and wood-shingle roofs, dry-stone walls, fountains, and squares—takes 30-45 minutes and reveals the vernacular architecture of the Isère Vercors before leaving the massif. A map is available at the Villard-de-Lans tourist office, 10 km away.

days_plan.itin_title: Royans-Vercors Road Trip: Hilltop Villages and Gorges

map_keywords: Réserve naturelle des Hauts Plateaux Vercors

visits.text: Correns is France's first entirely organic-certified village — a revolution launched in 1997 when its mayor, a winemaker and agronomist, proposed that all farmers convert together to organic agriculture during the height of the wine crisis. Since then, the local economy has thrived on organic Côtes-de-Provence vineyards, chicken and goat farming, honey and olive oil. Wander the cobbled lanes that spiral upward around Fort Gibron, a 12th-century priory of the Abbey of Montmajour in Arles — its 2-meter-thick walls of Correns stone are a fine example of Provençal Romanesque art. The Fort now houses a cultural center with concerts and exhibitions. At the village's base, the 14th-century Porte Saint-Germain with its portcullis gives access to the old quarter — legend has it that Saint Germain stopped here on his return from Rome.

The single-arch stone bridge spanning the Argens connects the old village on the right bank with the suburb on the left. From the bridge, several houses in the center look directly onto the river — the typical image of Correns in summer, with gardens reaching down to the water. The 18th-century baroque church conceals in its choir a gilded wooden high altar topped with a glory, an altarpiece of rare quality for the region.

activities.text: The Vallon Sourn, between Correns and Châteauvert, is one of the Var's most beautiful natural sites. The Argens winds for 4 km at the foot of limestone cliffs renowned for their climbing routes, through protected riparian woodland. Shaded pebble beaches line the river — ideal for family swimming away from crowds. Local outfitters offer canoe trips on the Argens from Correns, descending the valley and passing the Benedictine lock. Check ahead on access conditions during fire-risk periods.

Several organic wine estates in Correns open their cellars for tastings and visits. The white wines of the Côtes-de-Provence appellation are the local specialty. Check with the tourist office for estates that welcome visitors without reservation.

days_plan.itin_title: The 10 Most Beautiful Villages of the Haut-Var

places.name: Corte

visits.text: Corte is the only inland city—and Corsica's former capital between 1755 and 1769, when Pascal Paoli made it the seat of his government and founded a university there. The citadel, perched on a rocky spur 100 meters above the confluence of the Restonica and Tavignano, is the only one of Corsica's seven citadels built inland. Climb to the belvedere for the 360° view over both valleys and the triangular bell tower of the Annunciation church. The Museum of Corsica, in the Serrurier barracks at the citadel's heart, houses 3,000 objects of traditional Corsican ethnography collected by Father Doazan—opened in 1997, it's the island's best museum for understanding insular culture. Your Corte train ticket entitles you to a discount.

Place Gaffory, in the old town, contains the house of General Jean-Pierre Gaffory—the man who liberated Corte from the Genoese before being assassinated in 1753. The bullet impacts visible on his house's façade aren't decoration: they date from the 1750 Genoese siege, when Gaffory was defending his own home with his wife while the Genoese held his son hostage.

The Gorges de la Restonica begin 2 km from Corte's center via a narrow mountain road. In summer, a shuttle runs from the town parking lot (the road is often closed to cars in July–August). The pools and natural basins between the gorges and the Grotelle shepherds' hut are among Corsica's finest—the water is icy even in full heat wave.

activities.text: Lake Melo hike from the Grotelle shepherds' hut: from the Restonica valley terminus parking lot (1,375 m), count on 1 hour each way to reach Lake Melo at 1,711 m. Rocky marked trail, 300 m elevation gain. The lake is framed by cliffs and its water is an intense cold blue. In summer, the local shepherds sell their tomme cheese directly at the hut—don't miss this break.

Swimming in the Restonica's pools on the return from Grotelle. The best basins are mid-valley, accessible from parking areas on the D623. The water descends directly from Lake Melo—count on 10°C in midsummer.

days_plan.itin_title: Upper Corsica Road Trip: Cap Corse and Balagne
Complete Tour of Corsica

map_keywords: Citadelle Corte musée de la Corse

Gate of Burgundy.
Cistercian abbey.
