days_plan.itin_title: Nièvre, France: Morvan Hills and Ducal Castle Heritage Routes

visits.text: Cotignac earned its label as one of France's Most Beautiful Villages in 2022 — and the moment you first glimpse the 80-meter-high, 400-meter-wide tufa cliff towering over the village rooftops, you'll understand exactly why. This ochre rock face, carved by the Cassole River during the Quaternary era, is riddled with cave dwellings hollowed directly into the stone. A secured footpath lets you explore these cavities from the base of the cliff — allow a full hour for the complete visit. You'll discover former stables, olive oil mills and even grain silos, all sculpted into the tufa by medieval inhabitants. If you can, join one of the guided tours offered in summer: the stories about daily life in these caves are absolutely worth it.

At the cliff's summit stand the two so-called Saracen towers, built in the 12th century — they're actually the remnants of the feudal castle constructed from 1033 onwards by Boniface de Castellane. Climb up to them via the cobbled paths for a 360° panorama sweeping across the entire plain stretching to Carcès and Entrecasteaux. The medieval village below is equally worth exploring on foot: its 16th and 17th-century stone lanes open onto flower-decked squares, and its 17 fountains — including the famous Cascade fountain — bear witness to the water abundance that built Cotignac's prosperity.

About 1.5 km from the center, the Notre-Dame de Grâces sanctuary is well worth the detour. In 1519, the Virgin Mary reportedly appeared here to a woodcutter on Mont Verdaille. Louis XIV himself made a pilgrimage on February 21, 1660, accompanied by his mother Anne of Austria, to give thanks to Mary for the birth of the Dauphin. The site is peaceful, shaded and offers lovely views over the surrounding hills.

activities.text: Rise early on Tuesday morning for the Cotignac market, held on Cours Gambetta beneath the centuries-old plane trees. This is one of the most flavorful markets in the Haut-Var: bottarga, lavender honey, hillside olive oil, goat cheese and all-natural soaps. Arrive before 9:30 AM to have your pick and chat with the producers without the crowds.

Head out for an hour's walk in the hills around Cotignac to see the vineyards (Coteaux Varois en Provence appellation) and olive groves encircling the village. Local estates offer tastings — check with the tourist office for opening hours.

days_plan.itin_title: Var Road Trip: French Riviera & Green Provence
The 10 Most Beautiful Villages of the Haut-Var

visits.text: Coucy Castle raises its spectacular ruins on a wooded spur. Built in the 13th century by Enguerrand III, this medieval fortress dominated the region with its 54-meter keep before German troops dynamited it in 1917. The massive ramparts and imposing towers still testify to the grandeur of this feudal palace. The site offers one of the finest examples of medieval military architecture.

The medieval town surrounds the castle with its ramparts and fortified gates. The Porte de Laon and Soissons Gate open the enclosure protecting the upper town. Cobblestone streets lead to Saint-Sauveur church and half-timbered houses.
The underground cellars dug beneath the castle served as storage and refuge. Vaulted galleries carved into rock reveal medieval construction techniques. These spaces now host cultural events.
The Saint-Aubin Park surrounds the castle over several hectares. Wooded paths wind through greenery offering viewpoints over ruins and surrounding countryside. Picnic areas invite outdoor breaks.

activities.text: The castle historical show brings the medieval era to life with jousts, falconry and knight combats. This spectacle recreates 13th-century atmosphere with period costumes and authentic weapons. Performances occur during summer and certain weekends.
Walk the marked forest trails surrounding Coucy. Several loops cross the massif through beech and oak groves. Paths lead to panoramic viewpoints over the valley.
Visit the museum evoking castle and town history. Collections include medieval objects, weapons, period models and documents. Interactive displays explain fortress construction and strategic role.

days_plan.itin_title: Aisne Circuit: Gothic Cathedrals and Picardy Nature

visits.text: The Commanderie des Templiers de Coulommiers, built between the 12th and 15th centuries, is the best preserved north of the Loire. The rectangular courtyard serves the Tithe Barn, dovecote, commander's lodging, and Sainte-Anne Chapel.
Enter the Sainte-Anne Chapel: its 13th-century wall paintings are among the rare preserved medieval decorations in the region. A permanent exhibition traces the history of the Knights Templar.

The Parc des Capucins, considered one of the most beautiful parks in Île-de-France, occupies the site of the former château of the Duchess of Longueville, who inspired "La Princesse de Clèves". The château remnants and Notre-Dame-des-Anges Church are classified Historic Monuments.
The Capucins Municipal Museum, housed in the former convent chapel, preserves the last vestiges of the tannery industry that made Coulommiers prosper for 800 years.

The Halle aux Fromages (Cheese Market Hall), labeled Heritage of Regional Interest, was built in 1887 in cast iron modeled after Paris's Les Halles. It was the trading and sales place for Brie cheeses.

activities.text: Walk along the Grand Morin's brassets, small canals created in the Middle Ages by Champagne tanners. Thirty-six bridges and footbridges span these waterways that earn Coulommiers the nickname "Little Venice".
The Commanderie's medieval garden recreates a vegetable patch, medicinal plants, and cut flowers cultivated in the Middle Ages. A 4,000-square-meter space to explore at your own pace.
Taste Coulommiers cheese at one of the local fromageries. Smaller and creamier than Brie de Meaux, this soft cheese is best enjoyed when aged on-site.

days_plan.itin_title: Seine-et-Marne Circuit: Fontainebleau and Royal Châteaux

visits.text: The Maison du Marais Poitevin occupies the former Maison de la Coutume on the square of the same name. The illuminated maraiscope traces the draining of the marsh.
The Church of the Holy Trinity dates from 830. Its watchtower on the side and its 18th-century funeral frieze testify to a turbulent history.
The narrow streets reveal low houses with whitewashed walls and blue or green shutters.

activities.text: Board a traditional flat-bottomed boat from the Quai Louis Tardy. The boatmen tell stories of the marsh, its wildlife, and the mystery of fire on water.
Rent a boat or canoe to navigate independently through the maze of canals bordered by centuries-old pollarded ash trees.

days_plan.itin_title: Deux-Sèvres Circuit: Marais Poitevin and Romanesque Heritage

visits.text: Acquired in 1552 by Cosme Clausse, the Château de Courances was rebuilt between 1622 and 1630. Baron de Haber restored it in the 19th century, adding the horseshoe staircase inspired by Fontainebleau.
Step into the Monkey Gallery adorned with tapestries where playful primates perform antics. The guided tour reveals six rooms still inhabited by the de Ganay family.
Fourteen springs feed the park. The 600-meter Grand Canal predates Fontainebleau's by a century. King Louis XIII came here to paddle his canoe.
Cascading water features, the Mirror reflecting the château, the decagonal Gerbe fountain, and sculpted gargoyles dramatize the Renaissance hydraulic network.
The Japanese garden created in 1930 by the Marquise de Ganay sits beside the 3-hectare vegetable garden. Organic fruits and vegetables are grown for sale throughout Île-de-France.

activities.text: Wander the 75-hectare park along grass-lined alleys. The great gardener de Noailles described it as France's only garden that is both classical and romantic.
Visit the Foulerie tea room, housed in the former hemp mill. The building reflects beautifully in the canal of the same name.
Landscape architects Achille Duchêne redesigned the park in the early 20th century to restore its classical layout. Each generation of owners has left their mark.

days_plan.itin_title: Essonne Journey: Castles and River Valleys

visits.text: Notre-Dame Cathedral of Coutances is a masterpiece of 13th-century Norman Gothic, built over an 11th-century Romanesque structure still visible beneath the stone facing. Its vertiginous lantern tower floods the transept crossing with ethereal light. The facade's twin spires soar to 246 and 253 feet.

Take the guided tour of the cathedral's upper reaches. The route winds through the Romanesque tower interiors, the galleries, medieval timber frames, and the nave's upper walkways. Up here, 13th-century stained glass reveals itself at eye level, its jeweled colors blazing.

The Jardin des Plantes, a Historic Monument since 1992, blends French, English, and Italian garden styles on a single terraced hillside. Italian-style terraces, English-style groves, and a living labyrinth succeed one another. The 16-foot-circumference purple beech and the tulip tree rank among its notable specimens.

The Quesnel-Morinière Museum occupies a 17th-century mansion bequeathed to the city in 1850. Its seven permanent galleries present paintings from the 17th to 20th centuries, Norman ceramics, and works by 'Le Pou qui grimpe' ('The Climbing Flea'), an early 20th-century local art movement. Admission is free.

Saint-Pierre Church, rebuilt after the Hundred Years' War in the 15th century, illustrates the transition from Flamboyant Gothic to Renaissance. Its tracery windows and sculpted portal deserve a stop.
Founded before 1238 by Hugues de Morville, Saint-Nicolas Church stands outside the old city walls. The 13th-century chapel was expanded in the 14th century to become the parish church of the suburb.

activities.text: The Jazz sous les Pommiers (Jazz Under the Apple Trees) festival takes over Coutances each Ascension week since 1982. More than 50 concerts unfold in the cathedral, theater, halls, and schoolyards. Jazz, soul, world music, and street performances pulse through the town for five days.
Wander the medieval lanes around the cathedral. Interior courtyards, vaulted passages, and half-timbered houses sketch an old center that survived the 1944 bombardments remarkably intact.
Coutances market runs every Thursday morning, on place Saint-Nicolas and around the cathedral. Cotentin cheeses, teurgoule rice pudding, Isigny butter, and coastal fish crowd the stalls.
The Jardin des Plantes' tradition of mosaic culture, practiced since the late 19th century, unfolds across the flower beds. Geometric compositions change with each season, a living art form.

days_plan.itin_title: Manche Road Trip: Cotentin Peninsula & Mont-Saint-Michel

visits.text: Craon Castle, nicknamed 'little Versailles of Mayenne,' stands on Guinefol hill overlooking the town. This elegant late 18th-century tuffeau edifice, built by the Marquis d'Armaillé on the ruins of a feudal lodge destroyed by the Wars of Religion then by Henri IV, is still inhabited by the same family.

The guided tour opens salons with finely carved woodwork and furnishings that belonged to Marie-Antoinette. The architecture, inspired by Versailles' Petit Trianon, illustrates the art of living in the Age of Enlightenment.

The French formal garden surrounds the castle with perfect symmetry. Water mirrors reflect the façade and light. The English-style park, planted in 1830, unfolds 47 hectares of greenery and over 150 plant species along 6 km of tree-lined alleys with century-old specimens.
Don't miss the large walled kitchen garden (over a hectare), the 19th-century greenhouses, the ice house, the wash-laundry and the dovecote—all preserved in their original state.

In town, Saint-Nicolas church and the market halls merit a detour. Craon was also a major center for Mayenne horse racing, a tradition rooted in this horse-breeding land symbolized by Pegasus, the red winged horse, emblem of the department.

activities.text: Walk the castle park following the 6 km of alleys. A treasure hunt is offered to children. Along the paths, encounter horses, goats, swans, geese and perhaps deer or squirrels that populate the estate.
Follow the Oudon river that borders the park. Its two bridges offer romantic viewpoints of the castle through the grove.

days_plan.itin_title: Mayenne Circuit: Hedgerows and Medieval Towns

visits.text: The Gallé-Juillet Museum showcases fine earthenware from Creil and Montereau produced between 1797 and 1895. Black stencil-printed and polychrome services illustrate the golden age of Creil manufacturing.
The former earthenware factories lined the banks of the Oise from the late 18th century. The ceramic industry made Creil's reputation with patterns inspired by Antiquity and the Orient.
Saint-Médard Church raises its Flamboyant Gothic bell tower from the 16th century. Rebuilt after the Hundred Years' War, it preserves Renaissance stained glass and furnishings.

activities.text: Stroll along the banks of the Oise where 19th-century industry once thrived.
Discover the town's industrial heritage through its ceramic-era architecture.

days_plan.itin_title: Oise Circuit: Imperial Châteaux, Ancient Forests & Living History

visits.text: Don't miss Crémieu's market hall — it's the centerpiece of the visit. Dendrochronologically dated to 1434 through analysis of the timber framework, it's unique in Isère and hasn't changed appearance in six centuries. At the back, you'll notice four carved stone troughs: these are the grain measures from medieval fairs (the "bichet" equaled 19.5 liters). A stone's throw away, the Augustinian convent, founded in the 14th century by Dauphin Jean II, houses the current town hall. But enter the church: it preserves a set of Augustinian paintings uncovered since the 2000s, unique in France, notably representing a double apostolic and prophetic Creed. The organ mentioned in the archives in 1785 is the oldest in Isère. On July 20, 1315, Dauphin Jean II had granted Crémieu a charter of franchises — one of the most complete in Dauphiné — marking the beginning of two centuries of prosperity.

Climb to the 12th-century Dauphin castle, perched on the Saint-Laurent hill overlooking the town. While the castle interior isn't open to visitors, the climb through the medieval lanes gives a beautiful panorama over the Dauphiné plain. Along the way, observe the flat limestone slab roofs — a local architectural specificity that's gradually disappearing.

activities.text: Intramural circuit of gates and ramparts (3 km, ~90 minutes): the Porte de la Loi, restored in 2023, marks the main entrance to the town. A free numbered map, available at the tourist office, ensures you miss nothing. In September, the "Médiévales" plunge the town back into its Dauphinois past with market, combats, and entertainment.

Walk in the Isle Crémieu beyond the walls: this "triangle" between Rhône, ponds, and limestone cliffs shelters more than 1,300 plant species and 187 bird species. Marked trails depart from the town toward the gorges and limestone caves of the surroundings.

days_plan.itin_title: Loop Around Lyon: Villages and Golden Stones

visits.text: Climb the 257 steps leading to the summit of France's tallest keep. Standing 52 meters high, the Tower of Crest is an architectural masterpiece comprising three nested towers. Transformed into a state prison from the 17th to 19th centuries, it held Protestants, libertines, and insurgents. From the top, enjoy exceptional 360° panoramas over the Drôme valley, Saou forest, and Vercors mountains.

Wander the narrow, winding alleys of the medieval old town that snake beneath the tower. Discover calades paved with river stones, covered passages, and remnants of ancient ramparts. The Saint-Sauveur church with its Romanesque bell tower stands as a remarkable edifice.

activities.text: Experience something unique with the immersive audio system. A 19th-century guard guides you through nine centuries of history. Discover prisoners' living conditions, escape attempts, and hundreds of poignant graffiti covering the walls.
For the truly daring, attempt the 44-meter rappel down the east wall, reliving Philippe Rivoire's 1789 escape attempt. Supervised by certified instructors, you'll enjoy an incredible view over Crest.
Take the Ramparts Path that climbs from downtown to the Tower. This steep ascent offers beautiful valley views. Below, stroll along the emerald-watered Drôme riverbanks.

days_plan.itin_title: Provence Drôme Road Trip: Medieval Villages & Local Terroir

visits.text: Créteil Lake spans 42 hectares, born from a gypsum quarry that operated from 1940 to 1976 — when excavators hit the Champigny aquifer, pools formed naturally and urban planners had the wisdom to turn it into a lake. The result: 1,500 meters long, 6 meters deep, with a 4 km shoreline that's been fully accessible on foot since 2019. Be warned in summer: cyanobacteria regularly invade the surface, the water turns greenish, and some activities get suspended. Check the lake's status before visiting.

MAC VAL in Vitry-sur-Seine is France's only museum entirely devoted to French contemporary art from the 1950s to today — not a single Spanish Picasso or American Warhol. 2,500 works including pieces by Kader Attia, Christian Boltanski, Niki de Saint Phalle, and Agnès Varda. Jacques Ripault's white building (2005) opens onto a 10,000 m² garden where sculptures from the collection sit as if they'd always been there. No barriers between you and the artworks — museum policy. Allow at least 2 hours; the exhibition rotates every 18 months. Take tram T9 to Musée MAC VAL stop.

activities.text: The Créteil leisure island has surrounded the lake since 1980: kayaking, windsurfing, catamaran sailing, dinghy sailing — everything happens on the lake between June and August with certified instructors. Swimming in the lake has been banned since 1982 (prefectural decree), but the 1,300 m² wave pool compensates. Check seasonal hours before heading out.

Late afternoon, walk around the lake's east side and look up: Créteil's residential towers nicknamed "the cabbages" — for their petal-shaped balconies — date from the 1970s and have become an inadvertent symbol of the new town. The lake reflects between the facades. Strange view, somewhat sci-fi, totally Créteil.

days_plan.itin_title: Discovering Val-de-Marne in 10 Days
Val-de-Marne Road Trip: Castles and River Marne Banks

visits.text: The fortress of Crozant rises on a rocky spur at the confluence of the Creuse and Sédelle, a strategic site that fascinated George Sand from 1827. The romantic ruins of the Lusignan castle, built in the 12th-13th centuries, stretch over a kilometer of ramparts punctuated by ten towers. The Colin tower, figurehead above the precipice, offers a striking silhouette. Listed as a Historic Monument and a stop on the path to Santiago de Compostela.

The Hôtel Lépinat, former meeting place for Impressionist painters from 1880, has become the Museum of the Valley of the Painters. In this legendary inn where Guillaumin, Picabia, and many others stayed, an immersive 90-minute journey plunges visitors into the universe of the Crozant School through new technologies, atmospheric settings, and more than 100 original works. Discover how 500 painters followed one another here from 1850 to 1950.

The Moulin de la Folie (Mill of Madness), on the banks of the Sédelle, was one of the most painted motifs in the valley. Paul Madeline depicted it realistically, Francis Picabia drew abstract-edged works from it. The mill and its stone bridge remain, even if vegetation has replaced the rocky debris that fascinated the artists. A trail follows the watercourse to the mill.

activities.text: The Painters' Trail of Crozant offers a circuit punctuated by interpretation posts presenting the paintings created at each viewpoint. Enameled stoneware medallions reproduce details of works, engraved plaques tell the valley's history, and glass windows invite visual games with the landscape. A discovery booklet extends the experience with practical exercises.

A narrated boat ride allows discovery of the Creuse gorges from the water. From Crozant to the Éguzon dam, the 90-minute route offers a unique perspective on the cliffs and lush vegetation that replaced the mineral landscapes painted by the Impressionists before the waters rose in 1926.

days_plan.itin_title: Creuse Circuit: Valley of the Painters and Aubusson Tapestries

visits.text: Vauban Tower (UNESCO).
Notre-Dame-de-Rocamadour Chapel.
Port museum.

activities.text: Pointe du Toulinguet.
Film festival.
Canneries.

days_plan.itin_title: Finistère, Brittany Road Trip: Breton Headlands, Ponant Islands & Coastal Heritage

visits.text: Cruis is a discreet village perched on the south face of Montagne de Lure — the 'little sister of Mont Ventoux' dear to Jean Giono. Its large Church of Notre-Dame-et-Saint-Martin is actually the former church of an abbey founded around the year 1000 by Augustinian canons. Around it, vestiges of the cloister and traces of the abbey recall that the village owes its development to this institution, which was very wealthy until the Hundred Years' War and the Great Plague ruined it in the 15th century. Wander the stone lanes, calm and cool in summer — the tall houses with carefully crafted lintels betray the past prosperity of peddler-druggist families.

A few kilometers north of Cruis, the Abbey of Notre-Dame de Lure (12th century) nestles at the bottom of the Morteiron valley, beneath century-old walnut and linden trees. It was one of the required stops for peddler-druggists who traveled the mountain to gather medicinal plants — lavender, wormwood, thyme — and resell them as far as the Beaucaire fair. This trade was so developed here that the Pays de Forcalquier counted 45 peddler-druggists in 1779 (versus 3 in 1707) — their descendants became pharmacists in the 19th century.

activities.text: Three marked routes 'On the path of plants of Montagne de Lure' allow discovery of aromatic and medicinal plants in their natural environment (Graves site, Lure ridges). The discovery guide is available at the Forcalquier tourist office. If you're there between June and August, the Ronde des Jas (10 km, 4h30, starting from Cruis) reaches the alpine pastures and high-altitude shepherds' huts with exceptional views of the Alps.

A tasting of essential oils and Provençal herbs at local producers in the Pays de Forcalquier is a good introduction to the region's flavors — some estates near Cruis offer visits to lavender distilleries in season.

days_plan.itin_title: Forcalquier & Verdon Gorges Road Trip

visits.text: Battle Museum (Czechoslovakia 1918).
Church.

activities.text: 14-18 Heritage.
Forest.
Vosges Saône.

days_plan.itin_title: Vosges Mountains, France: Alpine Lakes, Spa Towns and Forest Heritage

visits.text: Dauphin sits on its hilltop between the Luberon and the Valensole plateau, 9 km from Forcalquier. The village established itself there in the Middle Ages for protection from marauders, leaving below the vestiges of Gallo-Roman villas and Via Domitia mosaics that crossed the commune. Pass under the Pourcheirieu gate — sole remnant of the fortified walls — and climb to the old castle viewpoint: to the north, the Observatory domes of Saint-Michel, the village of Mane, and the Forcalquier citadel; to the south, the wooded Luberon. The view is one of the most complete over the Pays de Forcalquier.

The Church of Saint-Martin, a former castle chapel that became parochial in 1524, houses an exceptional baroque organ: restored and enriched with multiple stops by organ builder Michel Deluz, it's renowned for its acoustics and attracts organists each summer for concerts (Bach, Buxtehude, Handel, Frescobaldi). Concerts take place from July to September. If you're not there for a concert, the nave is worth visiting for its 19th-century canvas paintings by Esprit Michel Gibelin and its mullioned windows.

In December, the church displays one of the oldest nativity scenes in the Pays de Forcalquier: 11 dressed santons from the 18th century in their original state, whose clothing allows dating the crèche to the July Monarchy period.

activities.text: The village has active craftspeople — potters, ceramicists, art blacksmiths, wood turners. A late-morning stroll through the cobbled lanes often allows entering their workshops. The Iron and Fire festival (last weekend of March) is the main event celebrating these crafts.

The Via Domitia runs along the commune: from the village, it's easy to join the GR 653 (Way of St. James) that follows the old Roman route. A 2-hour walk through olive groves and oak forests, heading north toward the Forcalquier plateaus, offers beautiful panoramas.

days_plan.itin_title: Forcalquier & Verdon Gorges Road Trip

visits.text: Fontaine Chaude: Gushing at 64°C since Roman times, this spring feeds the thermal establishments of Dax, France's premier spa town and one of Europe's most important. The neoclassical building erected between 1814 and 1818 houses the Nèhe spring. The péloïde, mud derived from Adour River silt, has been used for rheumatological treatments for 2,000 years.

Archaeological crypt: Remains of a 1st-century basilica bearing witness to ancient Aquae Tarbellicae (waters of the Tarbelli). The mosaics and Roman foundations reveal the importance of this ancient thermal city. Guided tours organized by the Borda Museum on Tuesday, Thursday and Saturday at 3pm from February to December.

Notre-Dame Cathedral: Built between 1662 and 1894 in an imposing neoclassical style. The Apostles Portal, a 13th-century Gothic remnant, contrasts with the overall architecture. The Gallo-Roman ramparts are still visible nearby, recalling the ancient city's fortifications.

Art Deco heritage: The Atrium and Hotel Le Splendid testify to the golden age of the 1930s. These buildings designed by Parisian architects Granet and Expert illustrate the splendor of grand spa resorts. The Parc du Sarrat, a remarkable 3.5-hectare garden, shelters over 1,000 trees and 130 species.

activities.text: Heritage circuit: 2-hour urban route between Fontaine Chaude, Gallo-Roman ramparts and cathedral. The tourist office offers themed guided tours (Art Deco, thermal heritage, arenas). Dax has been labeled a City of Art and History since 2019.

Dax markets: The renovated halls (2021) offer local products Saturday and Sunday mornings. Fattened duck, Landes asparagus, Tursan wines and Bayonne ham. The friendly atmosphere and local producers make these markets essential stops.

Banks of the Adour: Walk along the river at Parc Théodore-Denis. The landscaped banks offer a peaceful setting for walking or running. Fishing possible with permit. Sunsets over the water create a special atmosphere.

Feria de Dax in August: 5 days of entertainment with course landaise, bandas, concerts and parades. The city transforms completely during this festive period. Accommodation booking essential several months beforehand. The arenas host corridas and bullfighting shows.

days_plan.itin_title: Landes Road Trip: Atlantic Coast, Pine Forests & Gascon Heritage

visits.text: The Planches de Deauville, a legendary 643-metre boardwalk of azobé wood, line the beach with multicoloured parasols. The 450 bathing cabins bear the names of stars who attended the American Film Festival. The Art Deco Bains Pompéiens complex completes this postcard setting immortalised by Claude Lelouch.

Admire the Hôtel Normandy Barrière with its green half-timbered Anglo-Norman style. This five-star palace built in 1912 embodies the elegance of the Côte Fleurie. Just steps away, the Casino Barrière has been glowing with golden lights for over a century.

Visit Les Franciscaines, an innovative cultural hub set in a former convent. Museum, temporary exhibitions, media library and documentation spaces explore horses, cinema, photography and the Norman art of living.

activities.text: Attend horse racing at the Deauville-La Touques racecourse. Inaugurated in 1864 by the Duc de Morny, this temple of racing hosts the most prestigious Group 1 events during the August meeting.
Enjoy the beach and its water sports. Land sailing, swimming and sunbathing set the rhythm of summer days on Deauville's fine sand.
Browse the luxury boutiques in the town centre. Deauville cultivates the art of elegant shopping, with fashion houses and designers side by side.
Unwind at the spa or thalassotherapy centre. Deauville's wellness establishments offer marine treatments and massages overlooking the sea.

days_plan.itin_title: Calvados Circuit: D-Day Beaches and Normandy

visits.text: Saint-Aré Church.
Medieval Loire bridge.
Priory.

activities.text: Loire-Aron confluence.
River heritage.

days_plan.itin_title: Nièvre, France: Morvan Hills and Ducal Castle Heritage Routes

visits.text: The international station at Delle, reopened in 2018, serves the Belfort-Bienne line via Delémont. Just 1,600 meters from Boncourt in Switzerland, it marks the terminus for CFF trains and provides connections to TGV services from Belfort-Montbéliard.

The railway heritage testifies to Delle's strategic role since 1868. After the annexation of Alsace in 1870, the line was rerouted around German territories to maintain the Franco-Swiss connection without passing through Mulhouse.

activities.text: Cross the border on foot or by bike to Boncourt. Explore the Swiss canton of Jura: Porrentruy lies 15 km away with its medieval old town and castle.

The Malsaucy leisure base (30 minutes from Delle) offers sailing, kayaking, and stand-up paddleboarding from April to October. A free 6,000 m² supervised beach opens from June to August. The Eurockéennes festival draws 100,000 spectators in early July.

days_plan.itin_title: Territoire de Belfort Road Trip: The Citadel and the Lion

visits.text: Admire these exceptional 3rd-century ramparts, witnesses to the grandeur of Dea Augusta Vocontiorum. These fortifications extend nearly 2 kilometers with a thickness of 3.6 meters and rise up to 14 meters high. The Saint-Marcel Gate, the only Roman monument still standing in the department, ranks among France's best-preserved.

Visit this listed cathedral built between the 12th and 13th centuries atop the vestiges of an ancient Roman temple. The building blends Romanesque and Gothic architecture. The remarkable 12th-century Romanesque portal ranks among the finest in the Auvergne-Rhône-Alpes region.

Discover an exceptional treasure in this chapel of the former episcopal palace: the 12th-century Romanesque mosaic of the Four Rivers. This unique altar carpet in France offers a symbolic representation of the universe according to medieval cosmogony.

Housed in an 18th-century mansion, the municipal museum designated a Museum of France holds archaeological collections dating back to the Neolithic. The Gallo-Roman period shines through tauroboic altars listed as Historical Monuments.

activities.text: Head to the cellars to taste the famous Clairette de Die, France's first sparkling wine to receive PDO status in 1942. This naturally effervescent wine is crafted using the ancestral Diois method dating back to Gallo-Roman times. The Jaillance Cooperative Cellar, founded in 1950, brings together over 240 winemakers.

Wander through the medieval cobbled streets of the historic center with its colorful old houses. Admire the Protestant temple housed in a former 18th-century Jesuit chapel. Enjoy the Wednesday and Saturday morning markets to discover Clairette, Picodon PDO, and local products.

A few kilometers from Die, visit the Notre-Dame de Valcroissant Abbey, a listed monument founded in the late 12th century. Though partially ruined, it still presents a cloister surrounded by an abbey church in a preserved natural setting testifying to over 800 years of history.

days_plan.itin_title: Provence Drôme Road Trip: Medieval Villages & Local Terroir

visits.text: Dieppe Castle — This 15th-century fortress perched on a cliff houses a museum exhibiting one of France's finest collections of carved ivories. Dieppe was Europe's main ivory working center in the 17th and 18th centuries.

Saint-Jacques Church — This 13th-16th century church mixes Gothic and Renaissance styles. Its sculpted frieze representing a procession of Brazilian Indians commemorates the 1520 expedition that brought exotic wood from Brazil to Dieppe.

Puits Salé Square — This central square lined with cafes hosts Saturday morning's famous fish market where fishermen sell their catch directly. An authentic and lively place appreciated by Dieppe residents and visitors.

activities.text: Dieppe Beach — This kilometer-long pebble beach is France's oldest seaside resort. The 1930s seafront promenade, Belle Époque villas and beach cabins bear witness to 400 years of bathing history. Kite flying is a local tradition.

Pollet District — This former fishermen's district clinging to the cliff preserves narrow cobbled alleys and sailors' houses. The stairs climbing to the castle offer changing perspectives on the port and the English Channel.

August 19, 1942 Memorial — This museum commemorates the Dieppe Raid when 6,000 Allied soldiers, mainly Canadians, attempted a landing. Photographs, testimonies and objects evoke this tragic episode that cost 3,600 lives.

days_plan.itin_title: Seine-Maritime Road Trip: Cliffs of Étretat, Rouen and Jumièges Abbey

visits.text: Stroll down the pedestrian shopping street running from the Protestant temple to the clock tower. Designated a City of Art and Crafts, Dieulefit concentrates workshops, boutiques, museums, and galleries. The village sits beside the Jabron River. The riverside house façades have been repainted in pastel tones creating a colorful, joyful atmosphere.

Discover the 40 pottery and ceramics workshops, heirs to a 2,000-year tradition. Dieulefit's pottery history begins in Gallo-Roman times thanks to clay renowned for its qualities. Today, the Dieulefit region perpetuates this tradition with a revival of contemporary production. Enter galleries to admire original creations bearing the Dieulefit Original label.

Visit this cultural center created in 1995 in Baume Park that presents the region's pottery history. The museum space displays over 200 pieces illustrating local production evolution, particularly the great 19th-century era. Discover pottery's various uses through the ages and manufacturing techniques employed.

Spend the morning completing your artisan souvenir shopping in village boutiques. Utilitarian or decorative pottery, unique pieces created by local ceramists stamped with the Dieulefit Original label, local products like Picodon PDO, lavender honey, and essential oils.

activities.text: Get hands-on during a pottery initiation workshop offered by the House of Ceramics or a local potter. Discover coiling, slab building, modeling, and throwing. You can create your own unique piece guided by passionate artisans.

Tour the workshop circuit open to the public in downtown and surroundings. Engage with artisans in their workspaces, observe shaping techniques, discover kilns and various firing methods. Each artisan has developed their own style and specialty.

Enjoy the orienteering sport space set up by the department to discover the old village in a fun family way. The goal is to find wooden markers shown on the map. Three routes of varying difficulty exist. Cool off along the Jabron's banks, whose source lies in neighboring Comps.

Savor one last stroll through Dieulefit's colorful alleys, enjoy a café terrace along the Jabron's banks, soak in the peaceful atmosphere of this artists' village nestled in Drôme Provençale. If departing on Sunday, enjoy the small local market.

Hit the road home carrying memories of this magnificent journey through Drôme Provençale. From Roman ramparts to lavender fields, from medieval villages to sun-drenched olive groves, you've discovered this region's exceptional terroir and heritage.

days_plan.itin_title: Provence Drôme Road Trip: Medieval Villages & Local Terroir
Provence Drôme Road Trip: Medieval Villages & Local Terroir

visits.text: The Geological Reserve of Haute-Provence reveals its fossil treasures. Marine imprints emerge from limestone rock, witnesses of a vanished ocean 300 million years ago. The geological trail leads you through ammonite slabs and fossilized ichthyosaurs.

The Gassendi Museum combines contemporary art and natural sciences in an unexpected dialogue. Naturalist collections sit alongside installations by resident artists. Zenith light floods the main gallery where Andy Goldsworthy's works and geological specimens interact.

Discover the limestone rock that rises above Digne-les-Bains like a stone ship. Steep trails lead to the summit where views embrace the spa town and surrounding valleys. The perched chapel has watched over the city since the Middle Ages.

The Digne Thermes have been exploiting sulfurous waters gushing at 42°C since 1930. The spa offers treatments and balneotherapy in a preserved Art Deco setting. The thermal gardens provide a relaxing haven in the shade of century-old plane trees.

The old town displays its ochre facades and shaded squares. Stroll through medieval alleys where fountains murmur their freshness. Boulevard Gassendi brings the city center to life with its Provençal craft shops and welcoming terraces.

activities.text: The Train des Pignes winds from Digne to Nice through the pre-Alps. The historic route uses vertiginous tunnels and viaducts. Landscapes shift from olive trees to fir trees, offering a unique railway spectacle in Provence.
Digne-les-Bains is the ideal starting point to explore the Verdon gorges. Scenic roads lead toward Castellane and the canyon entrances. Plan a full day for this memorable excursion.
Hiking trails crisscross the surrounding hills. Mont Saint-Pancrace offers a moderate ascent rewarded by a 360-degree panorama. Oaks and Aleppo pines punctuate the routes with their welcome shade.

days_plan.itin_title: Alpes-De-Haute-Provence Circuit: Lavender and Perched Villages
Forcalquier & Verdon Gorges Road Trip

visits.text: Take the Route des Grands Crus (D122) between Beaune and Dijon—37 miles of UNESCO-listed vineyards (the "Climats of Burgundy," inscribed in 2015). You'll pass through Pommard, Volnay, Meursault, Nuits-Saint-Georges, Vosne-Romanée, and Gevrey-Chambertin. Stop at the Clos de Vougeot: this 125-acre walled estate was created by Cistercian monks in the 12th century. The road is narrow and winding—take your time.

In Dijon, head for the Palace of the Dukes and States of Burgundy, which dominates the Place de la Libération (designed by Hardouin-Mansart between 1681 and 1686). The Musée des Beaux-Arts, housed in the palace, is one of France's oldest museums—and it's free. If you have the stamina, climb the 316 steps of the Tour Philippe le Bon (15th century, 150 feet high) for panoramic views over the entire city.

Don't miss the Church of Notre-Dame in Dijon—you'll see the Jacquemart, an automaton stolen from Courtrai in 1382 by Philip the Bold. In 1651, the people of Dijon added a wife (Jacqueline), then a son in 1714 (Jacquelinet) and a daughter in 1884 (Jacquelinette). On a buttress of the church, stroke the little carved owl with your left hand while making a wish—it's been a tradition since the 15th century.

Second day in Dijon—start with Saint-Bénigne Cathedral. You'll descend into the 11th-century Romanesque crypt, one of the few of its type still standing in France. Three levels descend into the ground around a columned rotunda. It's austere and powerful.

Head to the Dijon Market Halls, built in a metal style attributed to Gustave Eiffel (opinions differ, but the structure is typical of his work). The Tuesday, Friday, and Saturday morning market is spectacular: Époisses cheeses, mustard, gingerbread, snails, blackcurrants. If you're not here on a market day, the permanent shops beneath the halls are still worth a visit.

Visit the Cité Internationale de la Gastronomie et du Vin—you'll discover the Gastronomic Meal of the French (inscribed on UNESCO's intangible heritage list in 2010) through interactive exhibitions, cooking workshops, and a tasting cellar. Opened in 2022 in the former General Hospital. Allow at least 90 minutes.

If you love sculpture, continue to the Chartreuse de Champmol, a former monastery founded by Philip the Bold in 1383. Only the church portal (with statues by Claus Sluter) and the Well of Moses remain—six life-size prophets carved in stone, with astonishing realism for the 14th century.

activities.text: Follow the Owl Trail—22 stages marked on the ground by bronze plaques that guide you through Dijon's protected heritage district (240 acres, UNESCO-listed). You'll pass the private mansions of Rue des Forges, the 15th-century Maison Millière, the Law Courts, and Saint-Bénigne Cathedral. Pick up the guidebook at the Tourist Office. Allow an hour of walking.

End the day with a tasting of crème de cassis—you'll learn that Dijon has been the birthplace of blackcurrant liqueur since the 19th century. Try a kir (Aligoté white wine + crème de cassis): it was Canon Kir, former mayor of Dijon, who popularized this cocktail in the mid-20th century.

days_plan.itin_title: Burgundy Road Trip: Cathedrals, Vineyards & Medieval Villages
Burgundy Road Trip: Cathedrals, Vineyards & Medieval Villages

visits.text: Dinan's ramparts stretch 2.7 km around the medieval city—the longest preserved ramparts in Brittany. Built between the 13th and 15th centuries, they still feature 10 towers and 4 monumental gates. The walkway along the ramparts offers sweeping views over the Rance valley below.

Dinan Castle, erected between 1380 and 1384 by Duke Jean IV of Brittany, served both as a residence tower and fortress. The keep houses a museum chronicling the town's history from the Middle Ages to today. Climb to the top for a complete panorama of the rooftops and the Rance river.

Follow rue du Jerzual, the steep cobblestone street that has connected the upper town to the port for ten centuries. Half-timbered houses shelter artist workshops, galleries, and artisan boutiques. Weavers and tanners once worked here—the creative energy remains unchanged.

The Saint-Sauveur Basilica, constructed in the second half of the 12th century, blends Romanesque and Gothic influences across its western facade. It preserves the heart of Bertrand Du Guesclin, Constable of France, in a reliquary in the north chapel. The Romanesque portal displays Oriental-Byzantine motifs rarely seen in Brittany.

Dinan's port, nestled at the foot of the ramparts, has been a trading crossroads since the 11th century. Commerce with England, Flanders, and later Spain built the town's prosperity. Now a marina, its quays lined with terraces maintain a peaceful morning atmosphere that turns lively by evening.

The Clock Tower, a 15th-century belfry, dominates the town from its 46-meter height. Duchess Anne's bell still rings today. Climb the stairs for a 360° panorama over the slate rooftops and surrounding countryside.

activities.text: Guided tour of the medieval city. Expert guides reveal stories behind the 130 half-timbered houses, the murder holes, gun ports, and counter-scarps of the ramparts. A journey from daily medieval life to the present day.

Cruise on the Rance from Dinan port. The boat sails upriver toward Léhon or downriver to Saint-Malo, gliding between wooded banks and granite manor houses. The ramparts viewed from the river offer a spectacular perspective.

Dinan's weekly market, Thursday mornings. Stalls fill Place du Guesclin and adjacent streets. Local Breton products: galette-sausage, farmhouse cider, salted butter, buckwheat crêpes. A lively and colorful atmosphere.

days_plan.itin_title: Brittany Road Trip: Historic Villages, Sea and Heritage
Côtes-d'Armor Road Trip: Dinan, Pink Granite Coast, Bréhat Island & Guerlédan Lake

days_plan.itin_title: Ille-et-Vilaine, Brittany: Saint-Malo and Medieval Walled Towns

visits.text: Disneyland Park, opened in 1992, centers around Sleeping Beauty Castle. Five themed lands recreate worlds ranging from the Victorian Wild West to science fiction, with attractions for all ages.
Walt Disney Studios Park plunges into the backstage of cinema and animation. Immersive attractions recreate large-scale movie sets.

activities.text: Disney Village, a free-access zone between the two parks, offers restaurants, shops, and evening shows. The atmosphere comes alive especially after the parks close.
The nighttime spectaculars project onto Sleeping Beauty Castle a show blending fireworks, fountains, lasers, and video projections.

days_plan.itin_title: Seine-et-Marne Circuit: Fontainebleau and Royal Châteaux

visits.text: Cathedral.
Mont-Dol (panorama).
Champ-Dolent Menhir.

activities.text: Dol Marshes.
Cathedraloscope (sound and light show).

visits.text: Pasteur's birthplace.
Pasteur House.

activities.text: Walk along the Doubs.

Straw wine).

days_plan.itin_title: Jura Mountains Road Trip, France: Comtois Traditions & Emerald Lakes

visits.text: The ruins of Domfront Castle rise on a sandstone spur at 692 feet. The 12th-century keep, 82 feet tall, the shell keep, and the curtain walls testify to a stronghold that controlled routes to Mont-Saint-Michel, Maine, and Brittany. Dismantled in 1610 by order of Sully.

At the fortress's foot, the Church of Notre-Dame-sur-l'Eau is a jewel of Norman Romanesque art, dating to the late 11th century. Its choir welcomed illustrious guests: Louis IX and Louis XI on pilgrimage, Thomas Becket, and Henri II Plantagenet, who baptized his daughter here in 1162.

The Church of Saint-Julien, built in 1924–1926, is a unique structure: reinforced concrete, square plan, and neo-Byzantine decoration. Its mosaics in geometric gold-and-blue forms, by Gaudin, create a striking atmosphere. Listed as a historic monument in 1993.

Roam the cobbled lanes of the medieval town, one of the few in Orne to preserve its medieval urban plan. Covered passageways, interior courtyards, half-timbered houses, and townhouses follow one after another.
The 11 towers and gates of the old town punctuate the Rue des Fossés Pusson. These remnants of the ramparts frame a preserved medieval urban fabric.

activities.text: From the Place du Panorama and the castle's orientation table, views extend from the Maine landscapes to the borders of Brittany. The Domfront bocage spreads below, dotted with centuries-old pear trees.
Domfront is the capital of poiré, a fermented pear drink with AOP status. Visit a producer to discover Domfront calvados as well, which blends pears and apples—a unique particularity.
Domfront sits at the crossroads of the Vélo Francette (Ouistreham–La Rochelle) and the Véloscénie (Paris–Mont-Saint-Michel). Ride a section through Norman bocage.
The Medieval Festival of Domfront, held every two years, plunges the town into the fervor of the 12th century: shows, activities, and historical reenactments.

days_plan.itin_title: Orne Road Trip: Norman Bocage and Prestigious Stud Farms

visits.text: Domme is a royal bastide founded in 1281 by Philip III the Bold, perched 150 meters above the Dordogne on a rocky promontory. Unlike the checkerboard layout typical of bastides, Domme's trapezoidal plan adapts ingeniously to the clifftop terrain. The Porte des Tours—a gateway flanked by two massive towers—preserves enigmatic graffiti carved between 1307 and 1318.

The graffiti inside the Porte des Tours have long been attributed to Knights Templar imprisoned here after Philip the Fair dissolved the order in 1307. Crosses, human figures, and mysterious symbols cover the stone walls. Historians continue to debate whether these carvings are truly Templar—or the work of other prisoners—but the mystery endures.

Domme Cave, accessible directly from the central market square, is the largest developed cave in Périgord Noir. Its 450 meters of galleries are adorned with stalactites, stalagmites, and flowing draperies. The exit is through a panoramic elevator carved into the cliff, emerging at the belvedere with stunning views across the valley.

activities.text: The Belvedere de la Barre offers one of the finest panoramas in all of Périgord: a sweeping 180-degree view over the Dordogne Valley, the villages of La Roque-Gageac and Beynac, and the castles of Castelnaud and Marqueyssac. The esplanade sits right at the cliff edge—bring your camera.

Domme's market convenes Thursday morning at Place de la Halle, where local producers sell foie gras, walnuts, seasonal truffles, and Domme wine. The vineyard, replanted in 1990 after phylloxera devastated it a century earlier, now produces wine again—a small triumph of agricultural revival.

days_plan.itin_title: Dordogne Road Trip: Cave Art, Medieval Fortresses & Périgord Delights

visits.text: The Basilica of Bois-Chenu is not medieval — people often forget that. The first stone was laid on November 3, 1881 on the hillside where Joan, then a shepherdess, is said to have heard her voices. She was not yet canonized at the time, and the building was initially dedicated to Saint Michael. Designed by architect Paul Sédille then completed by the Demay family until 1926, it blends pink Vosges granite and white Euville stone in a striking polychrome. Inside, mosaics cover everything from floor to vault — a decorative program carried out from 1891 to 1934. In the crypt, look for the statue of Notre-Dame de Bermont, the Virgin whom Joan came to pray to every week 3 km from here. The building is illuminated every evening — if you pass at nightfall, it's striking from the valley.

500 meters from the basilica, Joan of Arc's birthplace (born around 1412) is one of the most visited monuments in the Vosges. What's remarkable: it survived thanks to a tax exemption granted by the Duchy of Lorraine from the 15th century to preserve the dwelling. Joan spent her entire childhood here before leaving in 1429 to liberate Orléans. An adjoining museum traces the Maid's history with original documents and objects.

activities.text: Climb to the Bois-Chenu hillside after visiting the basilica and walk on the heights overlooking the Meuse valley. If you have time, continue to the chapel of Notre-Dame de Bermont in Greux, 3 km north: this is the pilgrimage site that Joan frequented every Saturday from Domrémy, in all weathers. A quiet, almost forgotten place, which changes from the tourist sites of the village.

days_plan.itin_title: Grand Est Road Trip: Vineyards, Ramparts and Forests
Vosges Abbey Trail: Monastic Heritage Unveiled
