Seine-Maritime Road Trip: Cliffs of Étretat, Rouen and Jumièges Abbey
Complete Normandy Road Trip: Cliffs, D-Day Beaches and Mont-Saint-Michel

map_keywords: Chapelle Notre-Dame de la Garde Étretat

visits.text: Évaux-les-Bains, Limousin's only thermal spa town, traces its history to Antiquity. Around 50 BC, Caesar's legions were already exploiting the hot springs of Ivaonum, a name derived from Ivaos, Gallic divinity of waters. The remains of Gallo-Roman baths, classified as Historic Monuments as early as 1840, testify to this ancient occupation. The ancient capture system is still in use.

The Abbey Church of Saint-Pierre-et-Saint-Paul, a masterpiece of Romanesque art, was built in the 11th century on the relics of Saint-Marien, hermit protector of the town. Its five-story bell tower-porch, covered with chestnut shingles, dominates the town. The interior surprises with its grandeur and sobriety. Partially destroyed by fire in 1942, it remains the pride of Evahoniens.

The modern thermal establishment, completely renovated in 2001, occupies an Art Deco building with red and white bricks in geometric patterns. The waters spring forth at 60°C and treat rheumatism and venous conditions. The 'Les Bains d'Evahona' spa, recently inaugurated, offers a hammam, sauna, jacuzzi, and outdoor thermal pool, the gentlest in France.

activities.text: The Rochers de Bord circuit, 6 kilometers from the tourist office, crosses a remarkable natural site. Allow two hours to discover the rock formations and enjoy an impressive view of the Tardes Viaduct, a metal structure built between 1882 and 1885 under the direction of Gustave Eiffel.

The thermal park, a green setting in the heart of the town, invites strolling. The century-old trees of the former convent and shaded alleys offer a restful setting between treatments or after a visit. The Grand Hôtel, a characteristic building of spa towns, overlooks the park.

days_plan.itin_title: Creuse Circuit: Valley of the Painters and Aubusson Tapestries

visits.text: Explore this spectacular natural amphitheater in the Sixt-Fer-à-Cheval nature reserve. The cirque forms a 4-5 kilometer horseshoe with 500-700 meter vertical walls. More than thirty waterfalls cascade from its cliffs—particularly dramatic in spring when snowmelt sends torrents plunging down the rock faces.

Visit the legendary spring discovered in 1789 that launched Évian's worldwide fame. The magnificent Art Nouveau Cachat Pavilion, built in 1905, crowns the site with its glazed tile dome. The water flows continuously at a constant 52.9°F (11.6°C)—the pure Alpine water that became an international icon.

Marvel at this sumptuous building constructed in 1902 as a thermal spa. The former spa transformed into a cultural center in 2007 still impresses with its Belle Époque architecture—especially the immense glass dome soaring more than 30 meters high, flooding the interior with Alpine light.

Discover the former summer residence of the Lumière family, inventors of cinema, which became Évian's Town Hall in 1927. Rich in woodwork, bronze, and marquetry, the building can be visited freely during office hours—a glimpse into Belle Époque elegance.

activities.text: Follow the family-friendly trail that climbs the valley floor from the parking area to the Bout du Monde (End of the World). This virtually flat walk traverses grandiose scenery beneath vertiginous walls and multiple cascading waterfalls—nature's drama on an epic scale.

Stroll the flower-lined lakefront promenade stretching nearly 3 kilometers along Lake Geneva's shores. Manicured gardens, fountains, and a music kiosk dot the route. The sunsets paint the lake and mountains in impossible colors—pure Alpine magic.

Ride the historic funicular, free to use and in service since 1907. This gem of railway heritage links the town center to the heights over 750 meters of track. At the upper station, you can visit the historic engine room—Victorian engineering still working perfectly.

days_plan.itin_title: Haute-Savoie Road Trip: Alpine Lakes & Mont Blanc
Grand Tour of the French Alps: From the Thousand Ponds to Mont Blanc

visits.text: The Gorges de Spelunca connect Evisa to the village of Ota via a 5 km mule track carved into the gorges of the Porto and Tavulella rivers. The descent winds between orange granite walls and crystal-clear pools. Along the way you'll cross the Zaglia bridge—a 15th-century Genoese arch listed as a historic monument, built of pink granite over the river. It's one of Corsica's most beautiful low-mountain trails; count on 2.5 hours descending. Bring water and a swimsuit.

If you still have energy after the gorges, climb up into the Aïtone forest above Evisa: laricio pines 40 to 50 meters tall, centuries old, filtering the light into a green and gold checkerboard. A marked trail follows the waterfalls and natural pools of the torrent—the water is icy even in mid-August. It's the most frequented swimming spot in the region, so avoid Sunday morning.

activities.text: Swimming in the natural pools of the Gorges de Spelunca: midway along the trail between Ota and Evisa, several basins carved in granite allow you to plunge into transparent water. It's a popular family picnic spot on weekends—come during the week or arrive early in the morning for peace and quiet.

Hike in the Aïtone forest from the village of Evisa: the trail gradually climbs under the pines to viewpoints over the Gulf of Porto. The forest is cool even in midsummer—ideal if you're looking for an alternative to the coastal heat. Count on 2.5 hours for a complete loop.

days_plan.itin_title: Complete Tour of Corsica

visits.text: Notre-Dame Cathedral of Évreux. A jewel of Gothic art, this vast sanctuary brings together styles from the 11th to 16th centuries. Its 70 stained glass windows constitute a masterpiece of glass art, including the famous 'Évreux Yellow'—a 14th-century silver-salt color whose golden brilliance has never been equaled. The flamboyant north portal by Jehan Cossart and the lantern tower with its 'silver steeple' complete this treasure. The contemporary organ by Pascal Quoirin sports a futuristic case designed by Bruno Decaris.

Évreux Belfry (Clock Tower). The sole vestige of medieval fortifications, this 44-meter flamboyant Gothic belfry built between 1490 and 1497 houses the 'Louyse' bell cast in 1406. Unique in Normandy, it symbolizes the communal freedoms of the Middle Ages and offers a panorama over the city.

Saint-Taurin Abbey Church. Founded in the 10th century by Richard the Fearless, this former Benedictine abbey preserves a priceless treasure: the shrine of Saint Taurin, a masterpiece of 13th-century medieval goldsmithing, considered one of France's most precious.

Museum of Art, History, and Archaeology. Housed in the former 15th-century episcopal palace flanking the cathedral, this museum traces Évreux's history from Gallo-Roman times (when it was called Mediolanum Aulercorum) to modern Reconstruction.

Iton riverbank promenade. This developed path along the river crosses Évreux's historic heart. Old washhouses, rampart remains, stone bridges, and a reflecting pool offer a romantic perspective on the cathedral and episcopal palace.

activities.text: Gisacum Archaeological Site. A few kilometers from Évreux, this Gallo-Roman site reveals the vestiges of an exceptional 2,000-year-old city. The interpretation center offers guided tours, children's workshops, and themed events.

Évreux–Le Bec-Hellouin Greenway. This 42 km pastoral route follows an old railway line through woods, bocage, and villages. Accessible on foot, by bike, or on rollerblades, it passes near Château du Champ de Bataille.

days_plan.itin_title: Eure Road Trip: From Giverny to Normandy's Finest Medieval Villages

visits.text: The Basilica of Notre-Dame de l'Épine in Évron is Mayenne's most significant medieval religious building. According to legend, a 7th-century pilgrim returning from Jerusalem deposited a relic of the Virgin's milk in a hawthorn bush; its miraculous overnight flowering led Saint Hadouin to found a monastery here in 642.

The architecture offers a complete panorama of medieval religious art: a 10th-century crypt, an 11th-century Romanesque nave, a 12th-century fortified tower-porch, and a 13th-century Gothic choir with soaring proportions. Listed as a Historic Monument since 1840.

Don't miss the 12th-century Saint-Crépin chapel, a stunning Arabo-Byzantine jewel inspired by Spanish Moorish architecture—unique décor in western France. Also admire the mural paintings, stained glass and ornate reliquary containing the Virgin's milk.

The basilica's history is turbulent: destroyed by Bretons and Vikings in the 9th century, rebuilt in the 10th, pillaged during the Wars of Religion in 1562. A Festival of Sacred Arts brings the naves to life each summer in July.

activities.text: Hike in the Charnie Forest, southeast of Évron. This forest massif is one of Mayenne's most extensive, offering numerous marked trails through oak and beech groves.
The Coëvrons territory around Évron offers over 500 km of marked trails for hiking, mountain biking or horseback riding through hedgerow countryside and green hills.

days_plan.itin_title: Mayenne Circuit: Hedgerows and Medieval Towns

visits.text: The only cathedral built in France during the 20th century. Its 38-meter brick cylinder is crowned by 24 silver lime trees symbolizing the hours of the day and resurrection.
Ascend into the keep to discover the 12 stained glass windows in different colors, the work of Father Kim En Joong. Each evokes one of the twelve apostles.
The white Carrara marble altar stands at the center of the choir beneath a modern stained glass window depicting a tree. The cylindrical baptistery allows for baptism by immersion.
Place des Droits de l'Homme brings together three modern brick buildings: cathedral, town hall, and chamber of commerce. Pools and water fountains animate the esplanade.

activities.text: Walk along the confluence of the Seine and Essonne rivers at Coquibus Park. The two rivers meet in a wooded setting accessible on foot.
The Évry 2 shopping center and Agora border the cathedral district. Perfect for a shopping break in this new town development.

days_plan.itin_title: Essonne Journey: Castles and River Valleys

visits.text: Climb to the summit of old Eygalières village through cobbled streets lined with limestone houses — no pastel tones here, only the raw rock of the Alpilles. At the top, the Clock Tower built in 1672 with stones from the château symbolizes the village's independence. Its story: in 1660, the villagers paid the ransom of their lord, the Duke of Guise, taken hostage by the Spanish. In exchange, the duke ceded them the seigneurial rights. The tower, erected 12 years later, celebrates this emancipation.

Of the medieval keep (13th century), only the vaulted ground floor and the base of a tower remain, topped with a statue of the Virgin placed in 1893 to commemorate an apparition reported by a young village girl. Beside it, the Pénitents Chapel from 1581 served as a charnel house during the great plague of 1720. It now houses the Maurice Pezet Local History Museum.

1.4 km east of the village, the Saint-Sixte chapel, perched on a knoll and framed by cypresses, is one of the most photographed images of Provence. Its sober Romanesque style, built in 1581, contrasts with the surrounding olive fields. The climb is easy and the view from its forecourt over the Alpilles ridges is splendid. A pilgrimage is still held here on Easter Tuesday, a centuries-old tradition.

activities.text: Provençal market on Friday morning: one of the best markets in the Alpilles, heavily frequented by residents of surrounding farmhouses. PDO Vallée des Baux olive oils, IGP Alpilles wines, goat cheeses, and broken olives flavored with wild fennel. Arrive before 9 AM to enjoy the local atmosphere before passing visitors.

Loop around the Calanquet from Eygalières: marked route of about 10 km that descends into the olive plain along the ridges. The trail captures the geological structure of the Alpilles — a compressed limestone massif whose arid crests emerge abruptly from fertile plains. Allow 3 to 3h30.

days_plan.itin_title: Alpilles, Aix-en-Provence & Camargue Circuit

visits.text: Eyguières takes its name from the abundance of its springs — "aiguières" in Provençal — and several fountains punctuate its streets and squares as testimony. The village sits at the foot of the Alpilles massif, within which its entire territory lies in the Regional Natural Park. Its medieval feudal gate, its cobblestone Rue Calade with ancient paving, and the remains of the château of the de Sade family (11th century) deserve a leisurely hour-long stroll.

2 km north of the village, the Castellas de Roquemartine is a medieval fortress from the 12th-13th centuries that belonged to the d'Albe family of Tarascon — the same who controlled the strategic passage between the Durance and the south of the Alpilles massif. The architectural ensemble includes a double curtain of ramparts and a church dedicated to Saint-Sauveur. The ruins are accessible on foot and offer a commanding view over the plain.

For geology enthusiasts, don't miss the Tour des Opies, the Alpilles' highest point at 498 meters, visible from the village. It was a medieval watchtower that allowed surveillance of movements across the entire plain. From this summit, the panorama covers the Maures, Sainte-Victoire, Mont Ventoux, and, on clear days, the Mediterranean.

activities.text: Hike to Tour des Opies (498 m): from the village, a marked trail climbs to the Alpilles' highest point in about 2h30 round trip. The panorama over the entire limestone massif, the Crau plains, and distant Camargue is well worth the effort. Hiking shoes and water essential.

Eyguières market on Tuesday morning, Place Monier. Small village market very pleasant for stocking up on Provençal products — PDO Alpilles olive oil, seasonal fruits, local crafts. The atmosphere is more local than touristy, which is rare in the region.

days_plan.itin_title: Alpilles, Aix-en-Provence & Camargue Circuit

visits.text: Eyragues is a village of traditional Provence with quiet charm — its ochre-rendered houses and plane-tree-shaded squares welcome you to an atmosphere of deep Provence, far from tourist flows in the neighboring Alpilles. Wander the center's streets, spot the sculpted doorways of bourgeois houses and stone fountains. This is where Frédéric Mistral situated certain characters from his Provençal works.

A few minutes from the center, a 4.5-hectare bamboo forest opens its doors: a signposted path through a collection of varied bamboos forms a surprising plant tunnel, complemented by a wide diversity of trees and flowering shrubs blooming year-round. An unusual detour in this agricultural plain landscape.

activities.text: Cycling loop toward Maillane (quiet roads, flat terrain): the D5 and secondary roads linking Eyragues to Maillane cross a peaceful agricultural plain — orchards, wheat and corn fields, cypress hedges. Allow 30 minutes by bike one way to reach Mistral's native village.

Photo tour of Eyragues doorways and small squares: the old center's houses often have carved door frames and dated lintels from the 17th-18th centuries. A walking tour will reveal architectural details that few visitors notice — sculpted heads, acroteria, worn coats of arms.

days_plan.itin_title: Alpilles, Aix-en-Provence & Camargue Circuit

visits.text: Begin your journey in Villefranche-sur-Mer, whose turquoise bay is considered one of the finest natural anchorages in the Mediterranean. Climb up to the Citadelle Saint-Elme, a bastioned fortress built between 1554 and 1570 on the orders of Emmanuel-Philibert of Savoy after Barbarossa's galleys ravaged the town in 1543. Entry is free. From the ramparts, the view over the bay and Cap Ferrat is spectacular. Down on the Quai Courbet, don't miss the Chapelle Saint-Pierre, a tiny fishermen's chapel whose interior walls were entirely painted by Jean Cocteau in 1957 as a gesture of friendship to the local fishermen—Charlie Chaplin came to visit at its inauguration on June 30 of that same year. In the narrow streets, seek out the Rue Obscure, a vaulted medieval passageway classified as a historic monument that runs through the entire town in darkness.

In Saint-Jean-Cap-Ferrat, the Villa Ephrussi de Rothschild stands as the most extravagant Belle Époque mansion on the French Riviera. Baroness Béatrice de Rothschild had it built between 1907 and 1912 on the narrowest part of the peninsula—a plot she'd snatched from King Leopold II of Belgium by making an immediate cash offer. She created nine themed gardens around the villa, each in a different style: French, Spanish, Florentine, Japanese, Provençal, and exotic. Upon her death in 1934, she bequeathed everything to the Academy of Fine Arts, which opened it as a museum in 1938. Allow 1.5 hours for the villa and gardens, and if possible book outside peak season—parking is free but fills up quickly in summer.

Perched at 429 meters altitude between Nice and Monaco, Èze is one of the most spectacular medieval villages on the Côte d'Azur. The entire village is pedestrian-only—leave your car in the parking lot below and walk up. At the summit, the Jardin Exotique occupies the ruins of the medieval castle and brings together more than 400 species of succulent plants. The view from the terraces extends across the entire coast, from Saint-Tropez to Italy. Down below at the village entrance, historic perfume houses offer free guided tours on the making of Provence fragrances—a chance to understand the journey from lavender and jasmine to bottling before leaving with an olfactory souvenir.

activities.text: The Plage des Marinières in Villefranche-sur-Mer is the town's only sand beach—the others are pebble. The water here is particularly clear due to the depth of the bay. Arrive early in the morning to get a spot; in July-August, cruise ship ferries disgorge thousands of passengers starting at 10am.

The Nietzsche Path links Èze-sur-Mer to the hilltop village along a route that philosopher Friedrich Nietzsche used to walk during his stays on the coast in 1883, and where he apparently drew part of the inspiration for "Thus Spoke Zarathustra." The climb takes about 1 hour (not the 45 minutes announced on the sign at the bottom—beware). The path is quite steep with rocky sections: bring good shoes and water, and avoid climbing in full sun during July-August. The descent toward Èze-sur-Mer from the village takes about 40 minutes.

days_plan.itin_title: The 15 Jewels of the French Riviera and Its Hinterland
Around Nice Circuit: Coast and Hilltop Villages

visits.text: Falaise Castle, birthplace of William the Conqueror born in 1027, raises its three imposing keeps above the town. The medieval fortress combines Caen stone and Norman architecture with majesty. The virtual tour recreating the castle in the time of the dukes offers a spectacular plunge into the 11th century.
Explore the historic centre of Falaise and its medieval remains. The ramparts, Saint-Gervais Church and half-timbered houses recount the history of this strategic ducal city.

activities.text: Visit the Civilians in Wartime Memorial, the only one of its kind in France. Set in the castle's underground passages, it tells the story of everyday life under the Occupation and during the Liberation.
Discover the Automates Avenue museum, home to an astonishing collection of Belle Époque automata and antique toys. The magic of these mechanisms delights visitors of all ages.

days_plan.itin_title: Calvados Circuit: D-Day Beaches and Normandy

visits.text: Felletin shares with Aubusson the tapestry tradition since the 15th century. This small working-class town has preserved some workshops where weavers perpetuate ancestral gestures. The Church of Moutier, in Romanesque style, merits a visit for its sculpted capitals. The Creuse river crosses the town, offering charming perspectives on old weavers' houses.

The Felletin diamond is the largest rough diamond ever found in France. Discovered in the Creuse in 1477, it weighed 21 carats before being cut. A replica is displayed in the local museum, testimony to a little-known geological richness of the Massif Central.

activities.text: The tapestry workshops of Felletin sometimes open their doors to visitors. Observing a weaver working on their low-warp loom, choosing threads from thousands of shades, interpreting an artist's cartoon, is to understand why this know-how is inscribed on UNESCO's intangible heritage list. Inquire at the tourist office for possible visits.

The Millevaches plateau, of which Felletin is one of the gateways, offers landscapes of moors, peat bogs, and forests. Hiking trails allow discovery of this 'water tower' of France where several rivers are born. The altitude (up to 1000 meters) guarantees coolness and pure air.

days_plan.itin_title: Creuse Circuit: Valley of the Painters and Aubusson Tapestries

visits.text: Fère-en-Tardenois Castle raises its Renaissance ruins on a wooded height. The 16th-century vestiges testify to refined architecture commissioned by princes. Towers and curtain walls emerge from vegetation in a romantic atmosphere.

The fortified Saint-Macre church displays its defensive keep and loopholes characteristic of Thiérache. Though located further south, it shares the religious military architecture of the border region.
The nearby Templar Chapel recalls the military order's presence in the region. This small medieval building merits a visit for its history.
The picturesque village preserves rural charm with its old houses and quiet streets. The verdant setting invites leisurely strolls.

activities.text: Fère Forest unfolds its hiking trails around the village. Forest paths follow streams and clearings in a preserved massif. Several marked loops allow 2 to 4 hour walks.
Surrounding ponds welcome anglers in peaceful settings. Predator fish and coarse fish populate these developed waters. Fishing permit required.
Country roads link Tardenois villages through rolling landscapes and grain fields. Rural heritage reveals itself at the slow pace of cycling.

days_plan.itin_title: Aisne Circuit: Gothic Cathedrals and Picardy Nature

places.name: Ferrette

visits.text: Ferrette is one of southern Alsace's best-kept secrets. Nestled in a small cluse of the Alsatian Jura, this former capital of the Counts of Ferrette is dominated by the ruins of its castle built in 1105 by Frederick of Montbéliard, first Count of Ferrette. For two centuries, seven counts ruled over a territory that was nearly a sovereign state — the county passed to the Habsburgs in 1324 through the marriage of Joan of Ferrette to Albert II of Austria. Today, Prince Albert II of Monaco still bears the title Count of Ferrette, through a chain of inheritances passing through Mazarin and the Grimaldi family. The ruins are freely accessible year-round — count 10 minutes' climb and 45 to 60 minutes for the entire site.

The upper castle, the oldest, preserves a residential tower carved directly into the limestone on several levels — one of the first of its type in Alsace. From the summit platform at 612 meters altitude, the panorama is exceptional: the rolling hills of Sundgau, the Alsace plain, the Vosges to the north and, on clear days, the Swiss Alps to the south. The lower castle, built from a 12th-century keep, was remodeled in 1488 and has several round towers still standing.

In the lower town, the 1572 Town Hall is an elegant example of Rhine Renaissance with trefoil windows — its small belfry houses a 1592 bell cast in Basel that still rings curfew at 10 p.m. The two coats of arms on the facade tell the story: on the left, the House of Austria; on the right, the two fish of the Counts of Ferrette. The Church of Saint Bernard of Menthon, classified as a Historical Monument since 1902, completes the historic center's heritage.

activities.text: Head out on the trail leading to the Cave of the Dwarves — a widespread legend throughout Sundgau tells of dwarves with webbed feet who inhabited these limestone gorges and helped residents with their chores, until the day the villagers mocked their feet and drove them away forever. The hike to the cave takes 40 minutes from the village. The complete 6 km loop, passing by the castle, cave, and viewpoints over the Jura, is the best discovery route of the site.

If you have energy left, climb to the Rossberg tower — a belvedere a few kilometers from Ferrette offering a 360° view of three countries: the Alsace plain, Swiss Jura, and German Black Forest. The Sundgau's gastronomic specialty is fried carp — several regional inns serve it year-round in a lightly vinegared sauce.

days_plan.itin_title: The 15 Most Beautiful Villages of Alsace

map_keywords: Château de Ferrette ruines piton rocheux
Hôtel de Ville Ferrette Renaissance rhénane

places.name: Figeac

visits.text: The Champollion Museum—Writings of the World occupies the birthplace of the man who deciphered hieroglyphics. Its contemporary facade is inlaid with a thousand writing symbols in copper and glass. Inside, 5,300 years of writing history unfolds: Sumerian cuneiform, Egyptian hieroglyphics, Chinese ideograms, Mayan glyphs. The oldest piece, a Sumerian pictographic tablet, dates back to 3200 BCE.

Just beside the museum, Place des Écritures features a giant reproduction of the Rosetta Stone cast in black granite by American artist Joseph Kosuth in 1991. In the adjacent courtyard, the French translation is engraved on a glass plate suspended above a map of the Nile Delta.

On Place Champollion, the Maison du Griffon dates from the mid-12th century—the town's oldest dwelling. All around, half-timbered medieval houses and typical Quercy soleilhos compose an intact historic setting. The 14th-century former hospice completes the ensemble.
The Hôtel du Viguier du Roy, a 14th-century palace, served as the royal representative's residence. Its interior courtyard, cloister, formal French gardens, and royal tower are open to visitors.
The Church of Saint-Sauveur grew from a 9th-century Benedictine abbey. Romanesque and Gothic styles blend through successive renovations. The carved capitals and austere atmosphere merit a stop.

Wander Figeac's historic center, designated a Town of Art and History. Merchants' houses from the 12th–15th centuries with ogival arcades, open soleilhos beneath the roofs, Renaissance mansions, and 18th-century bourgeois facades form a remarkably preserved ensemble.

activities.text: Follow the Clés de Figeac circuit, a marked route through the old town. An illustrated booklet guides you from one monument to the next in a coherent loop.
Figeac's market fills Place Carnot every Saturday morning beneath the 19th-century metal hall. Find cabécous, Rocamadour cheeses, walnuts, Quercy saffron, and fouace—a local orange-blossom brioche.

days_plan.itin_title: Lot Itinerary: Cahors, Rocamadour & the Lot Valley

map_keywords: Hôtel du Viguier du Roy Figeac

visits.text: Le Corbusier UNESCO World Heritage Site. Europe's largest Le Corbusier architectural ensemble brings together five groundbreaking buildings in the Firminy-Vert district. The Maison de la Culture, the only structure completed during the architect's lifetime (1961-1965), features a façade tilted at 53 degrees with undulating panels designed by composer Xenakis. The curved roof floats on unstressed cables—a technical marvel. Inside, a 5,000-square-meter complex houses auditoriums and art spaces that still pulse with cultural life.

Saint-Pierre Church. This truncated concrete cone soars 33 meters skyward, completed between 1973 and 2006 to realize Le Corbusier's vision posthumously. Inside, an 11-second echo creates otherworldly acoustics. The constellation of Orion, pierced through the eastern wall, floods the interior with golden light at sunrise—a celestial clock that transforms throughout the day. The lower level, originally designed for parish activities, now hosts exhibitions featuring Le Corbusier's original maquettes and sculptures.

Unité d'Habitation. The fifth and final Unité worldwide, completed in 1967 by André Wogenscky after Le Corbusier's death. This residential ship stretches 130 meters long and rises 56 meters high on massive pilotis. Its 414 apartments (ranging from 25 to 113 square meters) house roughly 600 residents affectionately known as 'the Corbus.' Seven color-coded interior streets serve the dwellings—yellow, red, blue corridors that bring joy to daily life. The entire design follows the Modulor system, proportions based on a 1.83-meter human figure. Guided tours only.

Stadium and Swimming Pool. The stadium, finished in 1968, is France's only sports facility classified as a historic monument. Its 4,000 seats cascade down from the rim like a Greco-Roman arena—spectators enter from above and descend toward the field. The municipal pool, built 1969-1971 by Wogenscky and restored in 2006, completes Le Corbusier's vision of harmonizing physical culture with intellectual and spiritual life—a modernist ideal made concrete.

activities.text: Firminy-Vert District Discovery. Created in the 1950s to address the housing crisis in this former mining town, the district embodies modernist urban planning at its most ambitious. Social housing, schools, shops, and garages form a self-contained 'city within a city'—more spacious, healthier, and functional than the cramped 19th-century workers' quarters. The site draws 20,000 architecture pilgrims annually from around the globe.

days_plan.itin_title: Loire Discovery: Wild Gorges and Industrial Heritage

visits.text: Upon arrival in Flavigny, a sweet scent of anise welcomes you. Anis de Flavigny candies have been made since the 16th century in the former Benedictine abbey—an anise seed coated for 15 days in flavored sugar syrup. Visit the factory housed in the abbey's Carolingian crypts: it's free and you'll leave with pockets full of dragées flavored with rose, violet, or lemon.

Wander the cobbled streets of this hilltop village, classified among France's Most Beautiful Villages. Medieval ramparts, fortified gates, Renaissance houses with ornate facades—Flavigny served as the setting for the film Chocolat with Juliette Binoche and Johnny Depp. The 13th-century Saint-Genest church, with its carved choir stalls, deserves a detour.

Walk around the ramparts to discover views over the valleys of the Ozerain. The village sits on an isolated plateau at 400 meters altitude—the panoramas over the Burgundian countryside open 360 degrees.

activities.text: Take the rural trail that descends toward the Ozerain valley and climbs back through the orchards. Along the way, you'll cross the Alesia archaeological site—the plateau where Vercingetorix surrendered to Julius Caesar in 52 BC is just 8 km away. If the subject fascinates you, the MuséoParc Alésia is worth the detour.
In the evening, settle onto the ramparts with a glass of Crémant de Bourgogne. The sunset over the hills of Auxois is one of the most beautiful along the route.

days_plan.itin_title: Burgundy's 15 Most Beautiful Villages

visits.text: Fleurance is a bastide town founded in 1274. Its central market hall with Gothic arcades dates from the 14th century and still hosts the market today. The Notre-Dame church features beautiful contemporary stained glass windows created by Arnaud Moles, the same artist who worked on Auch cathedral.
The archaeological museum displays Gallo-Roman discoveries from the region. You'll see mosaics, ceramics, and everyday objects that bring Roman-era life to vivid reality.

activities.text: Tuesday morning market is one of the liveliest in the Gers. Local producers sell foie gras, duck, cheeses, and vegetables. It's the perfect time to discover regional products and chat with the Gersois.
Stroll through the arcaded streets surrounding the central square. The typical bastide architecture is clear here with the grid layout and central plaza. Half-timbered houses add charm to the scene.

days_plan.itin_title: Haute-Garonne Road Trip: From Toulouse to the Pyrenees

visits.text: Fleurie, the 'Queen of Beaujolais,' produces arguably the most elegant and refined wines of the region. The 880 hectares of vines unfold on pink granite hillsides that give the wine its characteristic finesse. The village itself charms with its golden stone houses and Romanesque chapel overlooking the vineyards. Fleurie embodies the perfect balance between viticultural excellence and preserved architectural heritage.

The Madonna Chapel, perched on the hill overlooking Fleurie, offers a breathtaking panorama of the vineyards stretching to the horizon. Built in the 19th century, it became a place of pilgrimage for winegrowers seeking protection for their harvests. The stained glass windows depict wine-related scenes, celebrating the sacred bond between faith and viticulture. The walk to reach it crosses the most beautiful parcels of the appellation.

The village center preserves remarkable wine architecture with vaulted cellars, sculpted doorways, and period wineries. Golden stone facades reflect warm light at dusk, creating a romantic atmosphere. The central square and its century-old plane trees provide a shaded meeting place where producers and visitors exchange impressions on the latest vintage.

The Caveau des Producteurs du Cru Fleurie groups together sixty independent winegrowers under one roof. This cooperative tasting room allows discovery of wine diversity despite the common appellation. Each parcel, each climat brings subtle nuances that passionate producers decipher. Modern architecture contrasts with ancient cellars while respecting the site.

activities.text: Taste Fleurie wines at renowned estates like Clos de la Roilette or Domaine de la Grand'Cour. These elegant wines, with silky tannins and aromas of iris and violet, represent the quintessence of Beaujolais. Producers explain the influence of pink granite on finesse and the winemaking techniques that preserve aromatic delicacy. Some estates offer vertical tastings of several vintages to understand evolution over years.

Walk the Wine Trail that crosses the most beautiful climats of the appellation: La Roilette, Grille-Midi, Les Moriers. Information panels explain the influence of exposure, slope, and soil composition on wine character. The 5 km loop offers spectacular views of Mont Blanc on clear days and passes through century-old vines.

Attend a wine and cheese pairing workshop organized by local sommeliers. Young Beaujolais cheeses, goat cheeses from nearby mountains, and aged Comté accompany different cuvées of Fleurie. Professionals explain harmony principles between wine tannins and cheese textures. The convivial format encourages exchanges between participants.

Cycle through vineyards on the Beaujolais Wine Route. The gentle slopes make cycling accessible while offering exceptional panoramas. The route connects Fleurie to neighboring villages, passing through varied landscapes of vineyards, forests, and small valleys. Electric bike rental makes climbs effortless.

days_plan.itin_title: Ain Road Trip: Bresse Plains and Bugey Mountains

places.name: Florac

visits.text: Florac's château, beside the Vibron stream, houses the headquarters of Cévennes National Park. Its courtyard, shaded by centuries-old plane trees, provides a peaceful setting. The building hosts exhibitions on Cévenol heritage.

In a quiet park, the Pêcher spring gushes from a rocky scree through several outlets. It channels water from the Méjean causse toward the Tarnon. Its name derives from the Occitan "pesquièr" (fish pond), inherited from the old mills that once lined the watercourse.

Cross the stone Pont du Tarn, Florac's architectural emblem. It offers unobstructed views of the river and surrounding cliffs. Downstream, the metal bridge of the old station, built in 1890 following Eiffel's design, recalls when trains opened up the Cévennes.

The Tourism and National Park Information Center occupies the renovated old train station building. An interactive exhibition presents the Park, its ecosystems, and conservation challenges. Nature shop and bookstore on-site.

Florac-Trois-Rivières lives up to its name: the Tarn, Tarnon, and Mimente converge here. Stroll the Vibron's banks between schist houses, the 1833 neoclassical temple on the Esplanade, and Parc Paul Arnal.

activities.text: Paddle down the Tarn by canoe from Florac to Sainte-Énimie. The gorges gradually tighten, cliffs rise higher, pebble beaches invite breaks. Rental companies provide shuttle service back.
The Rochefort via ferrata offers two intertwining routes along the river, with an optional 50-meter zipline. Swimming breaks possible between passages.

The GR70, Robert Louis Stevenson's trail, passes through Florac. Follow a section to reach Col de Jalcreste or Barre-des-Cévennes, a hilltop village with history-laden facades and panoramic views over the National Park.

days_plan.itin_title: Lozère Circuit: Tarn Gorges and Cévennes

map_keywords: Maison du Parc national des Cévennes Florac

visits.text: Place des Couverts.
Half-timbered houses.
Cathedral (UNESCO).

activities.text: Stroll through the narrow streets.
Discovery of Couserans.

days_plan.itin_title: Ariège Pyrenees Road Trip, France: Cathar Castles & Prehistoric Cave Art
Ariège Circuit: Cathar Castles, Prehistoric Caves & Mountain Lakes

places.name: Fondation Louis Vuitton

visits.text: The Fondation Louis Vuitton, built by Frank Gehry and inaugurated in 2014 in the Bois de Boulogne, is one of Paris's most spectacular buildings: 12 glass sails blown around an iceberg structure of white concrete, comprising 3,600 curved glass panels across 11 galleries. The foundation exhibits a permanent collection of modern and contemporary art—Basquiat, Richter, Hirst, Koons—and organizes major temporary exhibitions. The terrace at the top offers a 360° view of the Bois de Boulogne, the Eiffel Tower, and La Défense. Book your ticket online. Free shuttles depart from Porte Maillot every 20 minutes.

activities.text: The Bois de Boulogne surrounds the foundation—take advantage of it for a walk or bike ride around the Lac Inférieur after your visit. The Bagatelle gardens, a 10-minute walk away, are renowned for their rose garden with 1,200 varieties in bloom from May to July.

days_plan.itin_title: The Great Museums of Paris

map_keywords: Fondation Louis Vuitton Paris Bois de Boulogne
Collections art contemporain Fondation Vuitton Paris

visits.text: The Odeillo Solar Furnace stands 50 meters tall. Built in 1969 with 1,000 adjustable mirrors, it concentrates solar energy to reach 3,500°C at the focal point. Research center for high-temperature materials.
The Hermitage of Notre-Dame de Font-Romeu dates from 1693. This baroque chapel on the Camino de Santiago houses a 13th-century statue of the Virgin. The sacred fountain gives it its name.
The 20th-century Church of Saint Louis dominates the center. Built in concrete in 1733 on the model of the citadel chapel, it features a monumental statue of Christ the King.
The National High-Altitude Training Center has welcomed Olympic athletes since 1967. Its facilities are open to the public: swimming pool, ice rink, weight room.
The Grand Hotel, built between 1910 and 1914, dominates the resort. Listed as a historical monument, this Belle Époque establishment symbolizes the rise of climate therapy.

activities.text: Ski on the Font-Romeu Pyrénées 2000 domain. France's sunniest resort with 43 slopes between 1,600 and 2,213 meters altitude, 500 snow cannons, and 110 km of cross-country skiing.
Take the Yellow Train from Font-Romeu station. Century-old line connecting Villefranche to Latour-de-Carol through Cerdagne landscapes.
Hike on the Cerdagne plateau. Hundreds of kilometers of marked trails traverse pine forests, lakes, and clearings at 1,800 meters altitude.
Relax in the sulfurous hot spring baths. Llo, Dorres, and Saint-Thomas offer havens of peace in pristine nature.

days_plan.itin_title: Pyrénées-Orientales Road Trip: French Catalonia and the Vermilion Coast

visits.text: Fontaine-de-Vaucluse gave its name to the department: "Vallis Clausa," the enclosed valley, attested as early as 979. The Sorgue spring that gushes at the foot of a 230-meter limestone cliff is France's most powerful resurgence, with an average annual flow of 630 million cubic meters. It's fed by waters from Mont Ventoux, the Albion plateau, and the Vaucluse Mountains. The first explorations of the chasm began in 1878; in 1985, divers reached a depth of -308 meters without finding the bottom. The walk from the village to the chasm takes 15 to 20 minutes on foot. The view is most spectacular in spring, when the waters boil at high flow.

The Musée-Bibliothèque François Pétrarque, located on the left bank of the Sorgue, is dedicated to the Tuscan poet who settled in Fontaine-de-Vaucluse from 1338 onward to escape the tumult of the papal court in Avignon. Here he wrote much of his Canzoniere—366 poems dedicated to Laure de Sade, who died of the Plague in 1348. The museum also exhibits a modern art collection including names like Braque, Picasso, Miró, and Giacometti, all connected to the work of local poet René Char.

The Church of Saint-Véran, classified as a Historic Monument since 1840, is built on the remains of a Roman temple. It's one of the oldest in the diocese of Cavaillon, with an 11th-century Romanesque nave. It houses the sarcophagus of Saint Véran, 6th-century bishop of Cavaillon, reputed to have slain a legendary beast—the Coulobre—in the Sorgue waters.

activities.text: Round trip to the chasm from the parking lot (15-20 min walk each way). The path follows the Sorgue and its waterwheels. Note: access has been reduced for safety reasons (possible rockfall), the observation point has been moved 50 meters upstream.

Trail along the left bank of the Sorgue, shaded section under the plane trees (45 min). The emerald green waters of the Sorgue allow kayaking and aquatic hiking—providers offer these activities from the village.

days_plan.itin_title: Vaucluse: Avignon, Luberon Villages & Ventoux Routes
The 15 Most Beautiful Villages of the Luberon

visits.text: The Château de Fontainebleau commands 130 hectares of parks and gardens. Eight centuries of monarchs lived here continuously, from the 12th to the 19th century. With 1,500 rooms, it's the most furnished royal palace in France.
Walk through the François I Gallery, 60 meters long. The frescoes and stuccowork by Rosso Fiorentino depict mythological scenes commissioned by the king to transform Fontainebleau into a "new Rome".

The Napoleon I Museum occupies the Louis XV wing. Uniforms, weapons, imperial jewels, and personal items trace the emperor's life—he called the château "the true home of kings, the house of centuries".
Step into Empress Eugénie's Chinese Museum. Cloisonné vases, jade, and porcelain form an Asian cabinet of curiosities installed during the Second Empire.

The Imperial Theatre, commissioned by Napoleon III and inaugurated in 1857, was rediscovered after 150 years of neglect. Its refinement echoes that of the Versailles opera house. Accessible by guided tour only.

Descend to the Court of Honor, known as the "Farewell Court". The horseshoe staircase marks the exact spot where Napoleon bid farewell to the Imperial Guard on April 20, 1814, before his exile to Elba.

activities.text: Stroll through the English Garden, landscaped under Napoleon I, then follow the Grand Canal dug under Henri IV. Rowboats let you glide across the water facing the château.

The Fontainebleau Forest stretches across 22,000 hectares. Its sandstone boulders are the world reference for bouldering, drawing climbers from across the globe. Over 30,000 problems across 270 sectors are mapped, from beginner to expert.
Follow the marked forest trails: the GR 1, 2, 11, and 13 cross the massif. Landscapes alternate between sandstone chaos, oak groves, and sandy plateaus.

days_plan.itin_title: Seine-et-Marne Circuit: Fontainebleau and Royal Châteaux

visits.text: Fontenay-le-Comte's Renaissance old town, Vendée's only City of Art and History, unfolds its private mansions along cobbled streets. Maison Billaud, the Hôtel de la Sénéchaussée, and Hôtel Grimouard bear witness to 16th-century splendor.
Château de Terre-Neuve, built in the late 16th century for poet Nicolas Rapin, is a Renaissance jewel. Its grand salon houses gilded sunbursts from Louis XIV's bedroom at Château de Chambord.

Notre-Dame Church, a 15th-century Gothic edifice, conceals an 11th-century Romanesque crypt. Its bell tower soars over 80 meters. Renaissance chapels, a classical altarpiece, and an 18th-century pulpit punctuate the nave.
Descend a few steps to discover the Fontaine des Quatre Tias, built in 1542. This listed monument, shaped like an ancient temple with pediment and Doric columns, bears the city's Latin motto.
Around Place Belliard, arcaded 17th-century houses recall the commercial prosperity of this former Vendée capital. Look for caryatids tucked into the façades.
The Musée de Fontenay, housed in the former Notre-Dame presbytery, preserves one of France's richest collections of Gallo-Roman glassware.

activities.text: Follow the circuit of 42 heritage interpretation panels, from the Quartier des Illustres to the Faubourg des Loges. Two loops departing from the Tourist Office cover medieval and Renaissance treasures.
Climb to Parc Baron, site of the former medieval fortress. Remnants of the Romanesque keep, Guinefolle bastion, and Tour de la Boulaye offer the finest view over Fontenay's rooftops.
Embark on a flat-bottomed boat ride in the Marais Poitevin from landing stages near Fontenay. Canals shaded by ash and poplar trees wind through the 'Green Venice.'

days_plan.itin_title: Loire Valley Phantoms: Tragic Histories and Haunted Castles of France
Vendée Tour: Noirmoutier Island, Puy du Fou & Coast

visits.text: The Royal Abbey of Fontevraud, founded in 1101, ranks among Europe's largest monastic cities, sprawling across 13 hectares. UNESCO-listed and remarkable for its history, it was governed for 700 years by 36 abbesses—an extraordinary matriarchy in medieval Christendom.

In the abbey church lie the polychrome effigies of Eleanor of Aquitaine, Henry II Plantagenet, Richard the Lionheart, and Isabelle of Angoulême. This royal necropolis bears witness to the Plantagenet empire that once stretched from Scotland to the Pyrenees.
The 12th-century Romanesque kitchens are architecturally unique. Twenty stone chimneys rise beneath an octagonal structure crowned with slate scales—a medieval engineering marvel.
The Grand-Moûtier cloister invites contemplation. Its 16th-century galleries frame a garden where silence reigns, much as it has for centuries of monastic life.
The chapter house served as the nuns' daily meeting place. Converted into a prison in the 19th century, it has been restored to reveal its Gothic beauty once more.

activities.text: The abbey welcomes artists in residence, contemporary art exhibitions, and concerts within its history-laden spaces where ancient stones meet modern creativity.
The village of Fontevraud-l'Abbaye, classified among France's Most Beautiful Villages, reveals itself on foot. White tuffeau stone houses line flower-filled lanes that seem untouched by time.
Saumur vineyards surround the abbey. Several estates within a few kilometers offer tastings where you can sample the terroir that has been cultivated here for centuries.

days_plan.itin_title: Maine-et-Loire Road Trip: Anjou and Loire Valley Châteaux

visits.text: Fontvieille owes its fame to Alphonse Daudet and his "Letters from My Windmill." But know this: Daudet never lived in this windmill. He stayed at the nearby Château de Montauban, whose owner was his friend. The mill called Daudet's, built in 1814 and stopped in 1915 (when wheat was requisitioned for war), served as his setting and inspiration. Now a museum, it sits on a hill of olive trees and pines with panoramic views of the Alpilles. The walk from village center takes 20 minutes.

Fontvieille's white limestone quarries have been exploited since the 15th century. The same blonde stone dressing the village served to build the Arles and Nîmes arenas, Château de Tarascon (1429-1434), and even Marseille's Palais Longchamp and Stock Exchange. The large underground galleries — some converted into Carrières de Lumières at Les Baux — are a geological feature of the Alpilles limestone massif.

The Roman aqueduct of Barbegal, 2 km from Fontvieille, is an exceptional archaeological site: a series of 8 water wheels superimposed on two parallel channels, fed by water from the Caume mountain. Built in the 2nd century AD, it's the largest collective hydraulic mill known from antiquity — it produced enough flour daily to feed 12,000 people. Free access, roadside parking.

activities.text: Loop of Fontvieille windmills (marked trail, 2h): the trail starts from Daudet's mill and follows garrigue hills on rocky tracks to Château de Montauban, where Daudet wrote. Several viewpoints over the Arles plain and Alpilles punctuate the route.

Discovery of capitelles and oratories around Fontvieille: capitelles are small circular dry-stone constructions, built without mortar, used as shelters by shepherds and wine growers. The Alpilles area is particularly rich in them. Several marked circuits reveal these witnesses to Provençal rural architecture.

days_plan.itin_title: Alpilles, Aix-en-Provence & Camargue Circuit

visits.text: The citadel crowns the hill above Forcalquier. Medieval ruins dominate the town and plain with their austere walls. Notre-Dame-de-Provence chapel watches from the summit, offering a circular panorama over the Alps, Luberon and Montagne de Lure.

The Cordeliers convent displays its 13th-century Franciscan monastic architecture. The Gothic cloister with vaulted galleries surrounds a simple garden. Temporary exhibitions and concerts regularly animate this carefully restored historic place.

Notre-Dame-du-Bourguet cathedral blends Romanesque and Provençal Gothic. The 13th-century sculpted portal opens onto a harmoniously proportioned nave. Side chapels house richly decorated Baroque altarpieces.

Walk through old town streets where mansions and Renaissance residences line up. Medieval gates punctuate the former ramparts' layout. Restored fountains and washhouses mark shaded squares where Provençal gentleness reigns.
Saint-Michel cemetery offers a peaceful haven away from town. Terraced tombs follow the hillside slope. Cypresses stand like sentinels in this serene place.

activities.text: Forcalquier market brings the town to life every Monday morning. Considered one of Provence's most beautiful markets, it spreads its colorful stalls across several squares. Local producers, artisans and market gardeners offer goat cheeses, lavender honey, olives and local specialties.

Montagne de Lure rises north of Forcalquier. Forest roads wind through beech groves and alpine meadows to the 1826-meter summit. The astronomical observatory benefits from clear skies for its observations.
Hiking trails radiate around Forcalquier. The Montagne des Mourres tour offers a moderate route through garrigue and oaks. Medieval ruins and Alpine panoramas punctuate the itineraries.

days_plan.itin_title: Alpes-De-Haute-Provence Circuit: Lavender and Perched Villages

visits.text: The fortress of Montbazon raises its 11th-century keep on a rocky spur overlooking the Indre. Erected by Foulques Nerra, the formidable Count of Anjou, this 28-meter square tower is one of France's oldest stone keeps.
Climb to the top of the keep to embrace with your gaze the Indre valley, the village rooftops, and the Touraine countryside. The panorama extends several kilometers on clear days.
The fortified enclosure shelters the remains of a seigneurial residence and a chapel. Explanatory panels retrace the history of Foulques Nerra, fortress builder and major figure of medieval Anjou.

activities.text: Medieval animations and demonstrations regularly take place within the enclosure: crossbow shooting, blacksmithing, calligraphy, and knightly combat bring the fortress alive during school holidays.

days_plan.itin_title: The Essential Châteaux of the Loire Valley

visits.text: The royal fortress of Chinon stretches 500 meters along the top of a rocky spur dominating the Vienne. Three fortified enclosures succeed one another: Fort Saint-Georges, the Middle Castle, and Fort du Coudray.
In the great hall of the royal apartments, Joan of Arc recognized Charles VII among his courtiers in March 1429. Interactive scenography retraces this founding episode of French history.
The Tour du Coudray preserves graffiti carved by Templars imprisoned here in 1308 on the order of Philippe le Bel. Crosses, inscriptions, and mysterious symbols cover the tufa walls.

The royal apartments have been recreated through a digital device that projects furniture, hangings, and characters onto the walls. The contrast between the ruins and the virtual reconstruction is striking.
From the ramparts, the view plunges onto the Vienne, the slate roofs of old Chinon, and the hillsides planted with vines that produce the famous Chinon red wine.

activities.text: Descend into old Chinon via the cobbled lanes. Half-timbered houses from the 15th and 16th centuries, troglodyte caves dug into the tufa, and shaded squares compose a preserved historic center.
The troglodyte caves in Chinon's hillsides house wine producers who offer tastings of the local cabernet franc, the appellation's emblematic grape.

days_plan.itin_title: The Essential Châteaux of the Loire Valley

Ramparts Walk.

days_plan.itin_title: Ille-et-Vilaine, Brittany: Saint-Malo and Medieval Walled Towns

visits.text: Nicéphore Niépce Museum (photography).
Saint-Vincent Cathedral.

activities.text: Comtois heritage.
Battle Museum (1871).
Ognon.

days_plan.itin_title: Saône Valley, France: Medieval Abbeys, Wine and Comtois Heritage

visits.text: Fourcès is one of France's rare circular bastides, listed among the Most Beautiful Villages. Founded in the 13th century, it's organized around a round square bordered by arcades. Half-timbered houses and flower-laden façades create a picturesque atmosphere. The 15th-century château overlooks the village.
The church of Saint-Laurent has a fortified bell tower typical of the region. Inside, 15th-century frescoes are still visible.

activities.text: In late April, the flower market transforms the circular square into an ephemeral garden. It's one of the Southwest's most beautiful flower markets with hundreds of exhibitors. The atmosphere is festive and vibrant.
Wander beneath the arcades of the round square. The galleries shelter small shops and restaurants. Sit at a terrace to admire this unique architecture.

days_plan.itin_title: Haute-Garonne Road Trip: From Toulouse to the Pyrenees

places.name: Fox Amphoux

visits.text: Fox-Amphoux is a twin village: the "new Fox" in the plain groups the school, town hall and post office; Old Fox, perched at 540 meters altitude on a promontory, preserves its entire medieval soul. The ruins of the Blacas castle, first mentioned in 1200, crown the stairway lanes. Don't miss the 400-year-old hackberry tree on the church square, measuring 5 meters in circumference — it provides shade even in mid-July. The panorama from the viewing platform on rue de la Prison embraces the entire Var region as far as Sainte-Baume and the Alpilles on clear days. This is also the birthplace of Paul Barras, born here in 1755: this revolutionary viscount voted for Louis XVI's death, helped bring down Robespierre in 1794 and served in the Directory before being sidelined by Napoleon.

Descend from Old Fox on a cobbled path to the Chapel of Notre-Dame du Bon Secours, set within a grotto at the foot of a limestone tufa cliff. The way is marked by several oratories and a peaceful forest atmosphere. Inside the grotto, behind the gates, the altar is adorned with flowers and the vault covered with small ex-votos. A discreet and touching place, off the tourist circuits.

activities.text: At the former cooperative cellar at the village's base, the DinoFox Museo displays fossils and dinosaur bones discovered in the area — notably the Variraptor mechinorum, discovered between 1992 and 1995 by amateur paleontologists, which provided the first proof of a dromaeosaur (velociraptor cousin) in Europe. The visit is free and fascinating for the whole family.

In late afternoon, settle on the Old Fox viewpoint to watch the sun descend over the wooded hills of the Haut-Var. The perched village then stands out as a dark silhouette against the horizon — one of the most distinctive views in the backcountry.

days_plan.itin_title: The 10 Most Beautiful Villages of the Haut-Var

map_keywords: Chapelle Notre-Dame du Bon Secours Fox-Amphoux grotte

visits.text: The Roman amphitheater, built in the 1st century, could hold 10,000 spectators who came to watch gladiatorial combat. Its tiers, corridors, and vomitoria can be visited freely. The venue still hosts concerts and shows—ancient stones echoing with modern music.

Saint-Léonce Cathedral forms, along with the 5th-century Paleo-Christian baptistery, one of France's oldest episcopal complexes. The octagonal baptistery, supported by recycled ancient columns, preserves its original baptismal font—a tangible link to early Christianity.

The 13th-century Romanesque cloister is a medieval jewel. Its 14th-century carved larch wood ceiling presents 300 painted panels of fantastic creatures, saints, and daily life scenes. A bestiary unique in France—look up and lose yourself.

The Roman aqueduct stretched 42 kilometers with a 481-meter drop and 36 aqueduct bridges. At La Roche Taillée, a 50-meter canal was carved directly into the rock. Classified as a historic monument since 1886, its vestiges can be visited on foot from downtown.

The Porte des Gaules, one of the four main entrances to the Roman city, preserves its monumental pillars overgrown with wild capers. Just beside it, a section of Roman paved road testifies to the past of Forum Julii.

The archaeological museum, behind the cloister, displays the results of local excavations. The Hermès Bicéphale, a two-faced bust that has become Fréjus's emblem, is the centerpiece—ancient mystery in marble.

activities.text: Stroll through the pedestrian lanes of the historic center, between Place Formigé with its terraces, Rue Sieyès with its good addresses. The Provençal market animates the squares every morning with local flavor.
Villa Aurélienne and its 24-hectare park offer a green break in town. This Palladian-style residence, surrounded by umbrella pines, hosts temporary exhibitions in a setting of aristocratic elegance.
Visit the Roman theater, more modest than the amphitheater but fascinating. Its restored tiers allow you to imagine the performances of comedies and poetry from Antiquity—sit where Romans once sat.

days_plan.itin_title: Var Road Trip: French Riviera & Green Provence

visits.text: Fresnay-sur-Sarthe has perched on a rocky spur above the Sarthe since the 9th century—William the Conqueror besieged it several times in the 11th century. Labeled Petite Cité de Caractère since 1995, it represented the Pays de la Loire region on the television show France's Favorite Village in 2021. Its absolute jewel: the carved oak door of Notre-Dame church, created in 1528 by the Dienis brothers, two local artists. The left panel illustrates the Tree of Jesse (Old Testament), the right the Twelve Apostles (New Testament). The church itself, built in warm-toned russet stone, was constructed in the second half of the 12th century by the counts of Maine.

Climb to the château park for two discoveries: the ruins of the old medieval keep and the plunging view over the Sarthe meanders and the Creusot quarter where weavers' houses still line up. These houses have very particular architecture—the weaving workshop was installed slightly underground to maintain the humidity necessary for hemp thread solidity, the living room upstairs, accessible by an external staircase. The château's postern, two 14th-century circular towers, marks the entrance to the public garden.

The Bonnet Museum exhibits 180 19th-century Sarthe bonnets—from the simple bonnette to the Bise-moi vite—restored and presented on hat stands by the local association's seamstresses. Each bonnet tells an era and social status: the most elaborate, entirely handmade in lace, could represent weeks of work. A precious ethnographic document on Sarthe women's lives.

activities.text: Descend rue du Bourgneuf, partly paved, which runs along the Sarthe at the foot of the promontory. Typical houses open directly onto the river and benches allow you to sit and watch the water pass. It's one of the most peaceful and authentic streets in Sarthe—take your time.

The Coteau des Vignes, accessible from a marked path from the bottom of the village, offers a fantastic view over the Sarthe valley and its meanders. This sensitive natural site hosts rare plant species specific to limestone grasslands—its almost Mediterranean airs surprise in Maine.

days_plan.itin_title: Sarthe Road Trip: Le Mans and the Loir Valley

visits.text: Fresnes Prison was built between 1895 and 1898 by Henri Poussin to replace Paris's old Mazas, Sainte-Pélagie, and Grande-Roquette prisons. At its opening, it was the world's largest prison: 1,800 individual cells, running water, steam heating, electric lighting. Its "telephone pole" layout — cell wings perpendicular to a 200-meter central corridor — was copied for a century in the United States, notably at Rikers Island. During the Occupation, the Germans detained resistance fighters and British SOE agents there. The prison was liberated on August 24, 1944, by Leclerc's 2nd Armored Division. You can't go inside — it's an active prison — but the exterior and history are worth the detour. It's been listed as national cultural heritage since 1995.

The Parc de Sceaux, a few kilometers away in Hauts-de-Seine, is one of the best-preserved French gardens in Île-de-France. Le Nôtre designed it for Colbert — grand cascades, grand canal, embroidered parterres. Allow 1h30 for the full circuit.

activities.text: The Verrières forest, at Fresnes's doorstep, is one of the best-preserved peri-urban forests south of Paris. Several marked trails allow walking or running in an environment that contrasts sharply with the concrete and prison walls nearby.

Fresnes's Saint-Sulpice church dates partially from the 12th century. The village has preserved some old buildings around it that recall that Fresnes was a rural hamlet before the prison transformed its identity in 1898.

days_plan.itin_title: Discovering Val-de-Marne in 10 Days
Val-de-Marne Road Trip: Castles and River Marne Banks

visits.text: Fresselines preserves the imprint of Claude Monet's stay from March to May 1889. The Impressionist master created 23 canvases here, including ten representing the confluence of the two Creuse rivers nicknamed 'Les Eaux Semblantes' (The Similar Waters). This series is considered his first true exploration of the same motif under different lights, a technique he would perfect with the Haystacks and then the Cathedrals.

The Monet-Rollinat Space, an art center inaugurated in 2018, pays tribute to the two personalities who marked the village. A memorial room is dedicated to Monet and presents reproductions of his Creuse canvases. The other evokes Maurice Rollinat, poet of Névroses who lived here for twenty years and received artists and writers. Temporary contemporary art exhibitions animate this creative space.

On the wall of the Fresselines church, a bas-relief by Rodin entitled 'The Poet and His Muse' pays homage to Maurice Rollinat. The sculptor, a friend of the poet, offered this work to the town after Rollinat's death in 1903. Inside, contemporary stained glass windows created by a local glass artist add a modern artistic touch to the building.

activities.text: The 'In Monet's Footsteps' trail starts from the village and winds 3 kilometers along the Petite Creuse. A suspended footbridge crosses the river to reach the painter's favorite viewpoints. Interpretation panels present the paintings created at each location, allowing comparison of the current landscape with the Impressionist vision of 1889.

The Rocher de la Fileuse (Spinner's Rock), in the neighboring commune of Saint-Plantaire, offers the most beautiful panorama of the valley and the confluence of the Creuse and Sédelle. It was here that Monet created his series of 24 canvases. A 3-kilometer hiking trail leads through the woods. The late afternoon light magnifies the colors the master sought to capture.

days_plan.itin_title: Creuse Circuit: Valley of the Painters and Aubusson Tapestries

visits.text: Saint-Michel Abbey, founded in 972, dominates the Tarn riverbanks. In its vaulted cellars, the museum traces Gaillac's history through a Gallo-Roman mosaic discovered on site, sacred art, and guild craftsmanship.
Head to the Quai Saint-Jacques quarter, the oldest part of town. From here, gabarres loaded with pastel and wine once departed, sailing down the Tarn to the Gironde and European ports.
Continue to the Portanelle quarter. Its corbelled houses, half-timbered structures, and walls of raw or fired brick mixed with river pebbles illustrate typical Tarn riverside architecture.
Château de Foucaud houses the Fine Arts Museum. Its classified Italian-style garden descends in terraces to the Tarn's banks, with small pools and open views.
Saint-Pierre Church, built in the 10th century, features a 14th-century stone portal and sober façade. Inside, marble altars and the nave's proportions warrant a visit.

activities.text: Visit a wine estate for a tasting introduction. Gaillac's vineyard, one of France's oldest with over 2,000 years of history, produces powerful reds, dry whites, sweet wines, and sparkling varieties.
Follow a wine-hiking trail through the Gaillac AOC vineyards. These marked walks cross rolling landscapes nicknamed 'Occitan Tuscany.'
Stop at Gaillac's weekly market to discover regional products: foie gras, duck confit, local saffron, and sheep's milk cheeses from the Lacaune Mountains.

days_plan.itin_title: Tarn Road Trip: Albi & Hilltop Villages

visits.text: Take the road that climbs the Fango valley for 15 km from Galéria—a forest of cork oaks and chestnuts descending from the heights of Monte Cinto (2,706 m, Corsica's highest point) down to the sea, classified as a UNESCO biosphere reserve. Solitude is almost total on weekdays. The Fango's waters are among the island's most transparent, shifting from blue to green depending on depth and light.

Don't leave without stopping at the Fango's natural pools—a series of basins and potholes carved in granite between two Genoese stone bridges. The main swimming spot is about 5 km from the village, past the old bridge. The water is cool and limpid even in August.

activities.text: Rental companies offer sea kayaks and canoes in Galéria for paddling up the lower Fango in season (April–October). The section between the sea and the first pools takes about 90 minutes. Best in the morning, before the offshore wind picks up.

Picnic beside the Fango pools: bring your provisions from Galéria (the village grocery is the only option in the valley). Set up on the flat rocks by the water—the shade's coolness and the torrent's sound do the rest. The spot is quiet on weekdays; packed on July Saturdays.

days_plan.itin_title: Complete Tour of Corsica

Ski resort.

Lake.

days_plan.itin_title: Hautes-Alpes Majesty: High Mountain Passes, Ski Resorts & Alpine Valleys

Raoul Castle.

activities.text: Guided tours.
Near Valençay.
Banks of the Indre.

days_plan.itin_title: Indre, France: Secret Berry Region and Vallée Noire Rural Villages

visits.text: The Cirque de Gavarnie, inscribed on the UNESCO World Heritage List since 1997 as part of the cross-border ensemble "Pyrénées-Mont Perdu," is a natural amphitheater 6.5 km in circumference whose limestone walls rise to 1,700 meters high. Sculpted by glacial erosion approximately 50 million years ago, its concentric tiers of striking symmetry are surrounded by peaks exceeding 3,000 meters. Victor Hugo called it an "impossible and extraordinary object."

The Grande Cascade de Gavarnie, with its 423-meter drop, is the highest waterfall in mainland France. Fed by snow and the Marboré glacier, it unfolds in long white veils on the cirque wall. Its flow varies considerably by season, with the spring melt months offering the most powerful spectacle.

The Brèche de Roland, at 2,804 meters altitude, is a natural notch 40 meters wide and 100 meters high in the rock wall separating France from Spain. According to legend, the knight Roland, Charlemagne's nephew, opened it with a blow of his sword Durandal to destroy it so it wouldn't fall into enemy hands.

The village of Gavarnie, starting point toward the cirque, preserves traditional Pyrenean architecture of stone houses and slate roofs. A historic stop on the paths to Santiago de Compostela, it also constitutes the birthplace of Pyreneanism, that current of Pyrenean exploration and ascent born in the 18th century.

The Refuge des Sarradets, at 2,587 meters altitude below the Brèche de Roland, dates from 1955-1956. Its terrace offers one of the most striking panoramas of the Pyrenees, with the Cirque de Gavarnie and the Grande Cascade as a backdrop. It serves as a stop for hikers climbing toward the Brèche.

The Cirque de Gavarnie is part of a set of three glacial cirques with Estaubé and Troumouse, forming the "Pyrénées-Mont Perdu" site recognized by UNESCO. This sector shelters remarkable fauna: chamois on the slopes, marmots in high-altitude meadows, bearded vulture (Europe's largest raptor with 2.80 m wingspan) and golden eagle in the updrafts.

activities.text: From the village, walk about 1h30 on the main trail to the foot of the cirque to see the Grande Cascade up close. The path, without technical difficulty, crosses meadows and follows the gave - it can also be done on horseback with village outfitters.

For seasoned hikers, reach the Brèche de Roland from the Col des Tentes (2,208 m) via the Sarradets refuge. Allow 5 to 6 hours round trip for this demanding hike (approximately 1,000 m elevation gain, possible passages on snowfields even in summer).
Tour the medieval village of Gavarnie observing the stone architecture of Pyrenean houses and barns, and stop at one of the small restaurants to taste garbure or valley sheep cheese.
Stay until evening if you can: the sunset light on the cirque walls transforms the rock into gradients of ochre and pink, a spectacle that photography struggles to reproduce.

days_plan.itin_title: Hautes-Pyrénées Circuit: From Tarbes to Cirques and Valleys

visits.text: Discover the Ecomusée de la Baie, an enlightening interpretation center dedicated to the natural wonders of the Mont-Saint-Michel bay and its protected ecosystems. Spread across 400 square meters, this engaging museum features films, detailed scale models, and interactive terminals that explain the unique environment of Europe's greatest tidal bay. The museum offers unobstructed views toward the iconic silhouette of the mount.

Explore the charming coastal village of Genêts, a quintessentially Norman settlement that serves as the traditional starting point for guided bay crossings. This picturesque harbor community retains its authentic maritime character with stone cottages, fishing boats, and a peaceful atmosphere that contrasts beautifully with the drama of the surrounding tidal flats.

activities.text: Embark on the legendary barefoot crossing of the Mont-Saint-Michel bay, an unforgettable adventure through one of the world's most dramatic tidal landscapes. Accompanied by an expert guide, wade through shallow channels, discover the phenomenon of quicksand safely, and experience the vast scale of this UNESCO-listed natural site. The bay witnesses Europe's highest tidal variations, creating an ever-changing seascape of extraordinary beauty.

Observe the remarkable wildlife that thrives in the bay's unique ecosystem, including elegant egrets, graceful seals, and the famous pré-salé sheep that graze the salt marshes. The herbus-coastal meadows regularly covered by tides-support an extraordinary biodiversity and create the distinctive flavored lamb that is a regional culinary treasure.

days_plan.itin_title: Normandy's Bay of Mont-Saint-Michel: Coastal Drives & the Sacred Abbey

visits.text: Discovery of the Louron valley and Lake Génos-Loudenvielle, surrounded by verdant peaks.
Walk through the typical village of Loudenvielle, between traditional architecture and mountain atmosphere.

activities.text: Wellness session at Balnéa, thermal center with Roman, Japanese and Amerindian baths.
Evening walk around the lake to admire the sunset over the Pyrénées.

visits.text: The Château de Gien commands the Loire from its red-brick heights. Inside, the International Hunting Museum displays trophies, antique firearms, and animal paintings spanning five centuries—from 15th-century tapestries to 19th-century oils.
The stone bridge has arched across the Loire since the 18th century. Sixteen elegant spans connect both riverbanks directly beneath the château.

The Gien Faïencerie perpetuates centuries of pottery craftsmanship dating back to 1821. The factory outlet and museum unveil historic collections and offer glimpses into the working studios where artisans still paint by hand.

activities.text: The Saturday market animates the Loire quays with local flavor. Farmers' stalls and Gien pottery stands line the riverfront opposite the château.
Follow the Faïence Route connecting Gien, Briare, and surrounding villages. Workshops, studios, and outlet stores dot the scenic circuit.

days_plan.itin_title: Loiret Road Trip: Orléans & Loire Valley Châteaux

visits.text: Saint-Pardoux Church. Church from the late 15th century housing an exceptional treasure: the reliquary shrine of Saint Stephen, a masterpiece of 12th-century Limoges enamel. Also several ancient statues and liturgical objects.
Notre-Dame du Rosaire Chapel. White Penitents' chapel located on the village's main street.
Ruins of Roche Haute Castle. Medieval ruins overlooking the village, visible from the main street.

activities.text: Gimel Waterfalls - Vuillier Park. The Montane river plunges in three spectacular falls totaling 143 meters: the Grand Saut (45 m), the Redole (38 m) and the Queue de Cheval (60 m) which dives into the "Inferno abyss". Painter Gaston Vuillier developed this park between 1893 and 1899 with staircases and observation points. Abel Hugo, Victor's brother, was the first to describe the site's beauty in "Picturesque France" (1883). Listed site since 1912.

Hike to the 4th waterfall and Pont du Péage. Free circuit of about 1h30 one way to discover an additional waterfall outside the paid park. The "cascadelles" are also accessible via the Short Circuit in the forest.

days_plan.itin_title: Corrèze Road Trip: Villages and Dordogne Valley

visits.text: At the summit of the Ballon d'Alsace (1,247 m), the statue of Jeanne d'Arc erected in 1909 marks the border between three departments. The orientation table built in 1888 by the Alpine Club offers views stretching to the Alps on clear days.
Fort Dorsner was built in 1875 as part of the Séré de Rivières system to guard the Savoureuse valley. This pink sandstone fortress towers over Giromagny and the Ballon d'Alsace road.
The Église Saint-Jean-Baptiste in Giromagny houses a remarkable Verschneider organ. Its 19th-century neoclassical architecture is worth a visit.

activities.text: Hike the GR5 from the Ballon d'Alsace down to Giromagny. This 13-km stage with 224 m descent follows the red rectangle blazes of the Club Vosgien. Pass through the mountain farmhouse inns of La Chaumière and Wissgrut.
The mining trail winds through former lead-silver extraction sites. Discover the industrial history of the Upper Savoureuse valley from the Middle Ages to the 20th century.

days_plan.itin_title: Territoire de Belfort Road Trip: The Citadel and the Lion

visits.text: Giverny — Monet's house & gardens (water lilies, Japanese bridge).

activities.text: Village art galleries & studios.

days_plan.itin_title: Normandy's Classic Coast: Étretat Cliffs and Bocage Countryside Villages
Grand Tour of Normandy: From D-Day Landing Beaches to Étretat's White Cliffs
Complete Normandy Road Trip: Cliffs, D-Day Beaches and Mont-Saint-Michel

visits.text: Fort Charlemont. This stone colossus erected by Charles V in 1555 then remodeled by Vauban dominates the Meuse from an 80-meter rocky spur. The 60 hectares of successive fortifications testify to five centuries of military architecture evolution. Underground galleries, casemates, and bastions offer striking panoramas across the valley and nearby Belgian border.

Old Town of Givet. The Meuse separates the town into two historic quarters: Grand-Givet and Petit-Givet linked by the Pont de l'Europe. Cobbled lanes reveal old houses, Saint-Hilaire Church, and remnants of urban fortifications designed by Vauban. Lively quays border the river where barges and cruise boats dock at the river port.

Collegiate Church of Saint-Hilaire. Built in the 15th century, this Gothic church preserves a remarkable sculpted Renaissance rood screen and Baroque organ case. Restored stained glass and liturgical furnishings testify to the artistic wealth of this frontier town. Engraved tombstones tell the history of Givet families.

Vauban Towers and urban enclosure. Remnants of fortifications encircling Givet illustrate the genius of the royal architect who made the town impregnable. Monumental gates, watchtowers, and dried moats punctuate an urban route steeped in history. The Mont d'Haurs fortified camp completed the defensive system capable of accommodating 20,000 men.

activities.text: Hike in the Ardennes forest. Marked trails traverse the Pointe de Givet nature reserve surrounding Fort Charlemont. Limestone grasslands, rocky outcrops, and dense forests shelter remarkable fauna and flora. Viewpoints over the Meuse meanders reward climbs through fragrant undergrowth.

Meuse river cruise. Board the boat Le Charlemagne for a river discovery of the valley and fort from the water. Commentary enriches the navigation between limestone cliffs and riverside villages. Promenade packages, gourmet meals, or musical evenings depart from the port.

Ardennes gastronomy. Local restaurants showcase regional specialties including the famous boudin blanc de Rethel (white pudding), Ardennes pâté, and forest game. Craft breweries offer local beers including La Charlemont brewed in neighboring Belgium. Markets feature farm products, cheeses, and local cured meats.
