Provence, France: Lavender Fields, Hilltop Villages and Riviera Towns
Vaucluse: Avignon, Luberon Villages & Ventoux Routes
The 15 Most Beautiful Villages of the Luberon
France's Most Beautiful Villages: Alps to Luberon in 7 Days

visits.text: Gorron is a small character town set on the granite foundations of the Armorican Massif, at the historic crossroads of the Breton and Norman marches. The Colmont river flows through it, creating a green setting bordered by old wash houses.

Saint-Martin church, rebuilt from 1868 to 1874 in neo-Romanesque style, dominates the town from its tall square bell tower that forms the entrance porch. Inside, an altarpiece depicting the stoning of Saint Stephen deserves attention.

Rue des Chauvinettes, quite steep, runs within the outline of the old fortress built around 1162 by Henry II Plantagenet, King of England. The granite flanks of the Colmont riverbanks served as natural fortification. The castle, contested between the lords of Mayenne and the English crown, was destroyed during the Hundred Years' War.

Stroll the lanes to spot the town hall housed in the old market halls, 19th-century bourgeois houses and the metal footbridge above the street—remnant of the former furniture factory that brought industrial fame to Gorron after the clog makers.

activities.text: The Colmont trail (n°11, yellow markers, FFRandonnée certified) starts from the church and circles the town between sunken lanes, hedgerows and Colmont riverbanks. Along the way, the Roche menhir has stood since approximately 4,000 BC beside a stream: 4.40 meters tall, classified Historic Monument. Legend says that on Christmas night, at the twelve strokes of midnight, the stone turns twelve times on itself.
The Colmont Leisure Park offers in season treetop adventure courses, kayaking, swin-golf, outdoor laser tag and paintball in a preserved natural setting.

days_plan.itin_title: Mayenne Circuit: Hedgerows and Medieval Towns

visits.text: Goudargues fully deserves its nickname of 'little Venice of Gard.' The La Fontaine canal that runs through the village is fed by a Vaucluse spring with constant flow (1,400 m³/h) and stable temperature at 14°C year-round. The Benedictine monks who developed the site in the Middle Ages chose their location well. Roman remains attest to human presence from the 3rd century — the village name itself comes from 'Gordanicus,' the name of a Roman villa that occupied the site. The plane trees lining the canal were planted in 1821. Sit on a terrace on the quay and watch the water flow between the plane trees: it's the most representative image of southern Gard.

The Notre-Dame-et-Saint-Michel abbey church is a surprise for those expecting a simple village church: it's a 12th-century abbey building, listed as a Historic Monument, which ranks among the most interesting Romanesque buildings in Languedoc. It developed around a Benedictine cloister — the refectory and chapter house have survived and now host cultural events. The choir and apse still date from the original Romanesque period.

Walk through Goudargues' narrow streets: the large washhouse with its emblematic bronze frog, the fountains, the bridges over the canal, the shaded squares. The village has local craft shops and tasting cellars for wines from the Gard Côtes du Rhône. In summer, the Wednesday evening market is one of the week's most pleasant moments.

activities.text: The bike loop from Goudargues to the surrounding wine-growing hamlets (15 to 20 km, gently rolling terrain) is one of the best ways to explore the Gard countryside. The roads between vineyards and garrigue have virtually no traffic.

The Cèze is accessible on foot from Goudargues in 10 minutes via the path that starts from the washhouse. Swimming spots in limestone pools upstream and downstream from the village allow you to enjoy the clear water in summer, with fewer crowds than at La Roque-sur-Cèze.

days_plan.itin_title: Cèze Valley and Ardèche Gorges Circuit

visits.text: Goult is worth the effort: less known than Gordes or Roussillon, this village perched in the heart of the Luberon has retained genuine authenticity. Its château (private) belonged to the powerful Agoult family, whose name gave the village its identity. As you wander through its lanes with vaulted passages, arcades and old archways, you'll discover stone and ochre facades that change color with the light. The Thursday morning market on the Place de la Libération is a good excuse to meet local producers.

The Moulin de Jérusalem is the village's crowning glory. Perched atop the hill behind the château, this late-17th-century windmill operated until 1919. It owes its unique name to the surrounding quarter, named in reference to the Crusades in which the lords of Agoult participated. After serving as an astronomy teaching center for the National Geographic Institute from 1947 to 1990, it was restored to its original state by the municipality between 1994 and 1997. It still has its sails and interior mechanism—a rarity in Provence. Panoramic view over the Calavon valley from the summit.

activities.text: Heritage trail up to the windmill and the old communal oven (30 min from the center). The path is well marked and offers beautiful views over the Claparèdes plateau and the Luberon.

Trail between dry-stone terraces, olive trees and vines—a short loop (approximately 1h) starting from the Conservatoire des terrasses de cultures, a restored landscape site showcasing local agricultural heritage. Make the detour to the Romanesque Chapel of Saint-Véran, nestled in a pine forest away from the village.

days_plan.itin_title: The 15 Most Beautiful Villages of the Luberon

visits.text: Goumois is a Franco-Swiss village split in two by the Treaty of Vienna in 1815, with the Doubs serving as the border. Nestled in the valley hollow, it is surrounded by the Corniche road, the Rocher du Singe, and the ruins of the medieval castle of Franquemont. Jean-Jacques Rousseau came to botanize here as early as 1748 with botanist Gagnebin. The bridge over the Doubs connects the two namesake communes.

The Corniche de Goumois viewpoint offers an exceptional panorama over the Doubs gorges and the Swiss Jura. The very picturesque road that leads there dominates the gorges and offers a series of varied viewpoints. It is the meeting place for lovers of wild and authentic nature.

The Maîche plateau extends between 800 and 1,000 meters altitude, bounded by the deep valleys of the Doubs and Dessoubre. This characteristic area of the Haut-Doubs offers vast expanses dotted with old Comtoise farms. Maîche, the canton's chief town near the Swiss border, is integrated into the Doubs Horloger regional natural park.

activities.text: Goumois is an internationally renowned fishing spot for trout and grayling. The Franco-Swiss Doubs offers 150 km of marked hiking trails. The most daring will take the Échelles de la Mort, installed in a wild and spectacular site near Charquemont.

Le Russey, at nearly 1,000 meters altitude on the Maîche plateau, offers panoramas over the surrounding plateaus. The 19th-century town hall is listed as a Historic Monument. Saint-Nicolas church and Saint-Roch chapel testify to the local religious heritage. It is the birthplace of the Almanach Vermot, founded by Joseph Féréol Vermot born here in 1828.

days_plan.itin_title: Doubs Circuit: Watchmaking and Swiss Border

places.name: Gourdon

visits.text: Gourdon is one of France's most spectacular perched villages: clinging at 760 meters to a rocky spur above the Loup gorges, it's part of the "Most Beautiful Villages of France" network. Its origins date back to the 9th century, when the counts of Provence fortified this promontory. Queen Victoria herself made the detour on March 28, 1891—the central square still bears her name. If you arrive early in the morning, you'll have the lanes to yourself before day-trippers invade the village.

Gourdon Castle, an 11th-17th century fortress rebuilt in 1610, impresses with its cylindrical towers and Renaissance gateway. The castle is privately owned and not open for tours, but the French formal gardens designed by André Le Nôtre—the same landscape architect who worked at Versailles—are accessible from April to September. The centuries-old boxwoods trimmed into topiaries, the terraces suspended above the gorges, and the view of the Mediterranean from this balcony are breathtaking.

From Place Victoria, the panorama stretches 80 kilometers without interruption—from Nice to Théoule, Cap d'Antibes, the Lérins Islands, and the Estérel massif. On exceptionally clear days, you can even make out the first contours of Corsica. Look for the orientation table to identify each summit. Paragliders regularly take off from Gourdon—the spectacle from the ground is already dizzying.

Gourdon's lanes are animated by artisan workshops: blown glass, perfumed soaps, hill honey, nougat, and gingerbread. The "Gourdouillat," a local cake with almonds and honey, is sold in the village shops. Don't leave without trying a piece.

activities.text: The Chemin du Paradis (Path of Paradise) connects the hamlet of Pont-du-Loup below to Gourdon village: this former mule trail still used until 1900 climbs 500 meters elevation through Mediterranean vegetation. The name is a promise—the view at the summit rewards the effort. Allow 2 to 2.5 hours round trip from Pont-du-Loup.

Drive down to the Loup gorges for the Saut du Loup and Courmes waterfalls: the river has carved natural basins called "giants' kettles" into the limestone. Trails run along the gorges between Pont-du-Loup and Bramafan. In summer, canyoning enthusiasts launch into the pools—local outfitters offer half-day guided trips.

days_plan.itin_title: Road Trip Through the Nice & Grasse Backcountry
Lot Itinerary: Cahors, Rocamadour & the Lot Valley
The 15 Jewels of the French Riviera and Its Hinterland
Nice Hinterland Circuit: Perched Villages and Mountain Trails

activities.text: Access: mixed (ruins/roadway)

days_plan.itin_title: Haunted Île-de-France: Occult Paris and Royal Ghost Stories

visits.text: Start with Écalgrain Bay in La Hague. It's one of the wildest panoramas in the Cotentin - schist cliffs plunging into turquoise water, with the Channel Islands as a backdrop on clear days. The coastal path along the ridge offers breathtaking views. Bring a windbreaker - even in summer, the wind blows hard here.

Continue to Nez de Jobourg, the highest point of the Norman cliffs - up to 128 meters above the sea. From the viewpoint, you'll see sea caves carved into the rock below. In rough weather, waves crash into them with thunder. A marked path allows you to walk safely along the ridge.

Make a stop at the Vauville mare nature reserve, protected by a thin dune cordon. From the Thot viewpoint, you overlook this 67-hectare coastal pond owned by the Coastal Conservatory. It's a remarkable Natura 2000 site for observing migratory birds - binoculars recommended.

Heading south, stop at Flamanville Castle. Built in the 17th century from local granite, it's visited mainly for its gardens and park - the castle itself is private. The view of the coast from the terraces is worth the detour.

Finish at Gouville-sur-Mer, where colorful beach cabins lined up on the sand are the classic photo spot of the Cotentin's west coast. At low tide, oyster parks stretch as far as the eye can see - if you meet an oyster farmer, don't hesitate to buy a dozen directly from them.

activities.text: Coastal hike between dunes and tidal flats on a marked section of the GR 223. You'll alternate between sandy paths, passages over rocks, and views of oyster parks. Allow 1 to 1.5 hours depending on your pace.

days_plan.itin_title: Grand Tour of Normandy: From D-Day Landing Beaches to Étretat's White Cliffs
Complete Normandy Road Trip: Cliffs, D-Day Beaches and Mont-Saint-Michel

visits.text: The Grand Palais—240 meters long, a nave 45 meters high topped with 15,000 m² of glass canopy—is one of Paris's largest exhibition spaces. Built in 1897-1900 for the Universal Exposition, it hosts major temporary exhibitions at the Galeries Nationales and significant cultural events. Its central nave, the largest in France, is open to the public during certain events. Check the current program before coming—exhibitions change every two to three months and require advance booking.

activities.text: Walk or rent a bike up the Champs-Élysées to the Arc de Triomphe—1.9 km of perspective from Place de la Concorde. The panorama from the top of the Arc (284 steps, open daily) embraces the 12 radiating avenues and, on clear days, the towers of La Défense on the horizon.

days_plan.itin_title: The Great Museums of Paris

visits.text: The trip's highlight: Mont-Saint-Michel. Arrive early—before 9am if possible. Park at the main car park (fee) and take the free shuttle or walk the 2.5 km causeway. The Mont reveals itself gradually—first its silhouette in the distance, then the ramparts, then the village, then the abbey at the summit. UNESCO-listed since 1979, it receives 3 million visitors a year.

First climb through the medieval village via the Grande-Rue—narrow lanes, half-timbered houses, shops (lots of tourism, but the ensemble is authentic). Then climb to the abbey. The audio-guided tour lasts about 1h30. The story begins in 708 when the Archangel Michael supposedly appeared in a dream to Bishop Aubert to command him to build a sanctuary on this 92-meter-high rock. Benedictine monks settled here in 966. The "Merveille"—the 13th-century Gothic building clinging to the north face—is an architectural marvel, with its three stacked levels of chambers.

Walk the circuit of the ramparts. Built from the 12th to 14th centuries, they enabled the Mont to withstand an English siege from 1423 to 1434 during the Hundred Years' War—one of the rare Norman sites never to have been taken. The western terrace in front of the abbey church offers the most open view over the bay.

A day in the Norman bocage. Thirty minutes from Granville, Villedieu-les-Poêles has been the "copper city" since the 12th century. The Cornille-Havard bell foundry, operating since 1865, is one of France's last—it cast bells for Notre-Dame de Paris. The museum tour shows you the manufacturing stages, from the clay mold to the bronze pouring at 1,150°C.

Wander through Villedieu's artisan lanes—you'll pass workshops of coppersmithing (working copper), pottery and lacemaking. The Cour du Foyer, former cloister of the Hospitallers, is a haven of calm in the center of town. Push open the doors of workshops that are open—artisans readily demonstrate their skills.

Continue to Mortain-Bocage (25 min). The Grande Cascade, 25 meters high, is the tallest waterfall in western France. You reach it via an 800-meter path from the town center—easy. The Petite Cascade, 200 meters further, is less known but equally lovely. Don't miss the Abbaye Blanche, founded in the 12th century at the foot of the cascade.

Final day in Granville, the "Monaco of the North." Begin with the fortified Haute-Ville, perched on a rocky promontory. Pass through the Grande Porte and climb the granite lanes to the Église Notre-Dame-du-Cap-Lihou. The ramparts offer a panorama over the harbor, the Chausey Islands offshore and, in fine weather, Jersey on the horizon.

At the far tip, the Pointe du Roc—a rocky spur battered by waves where the old signal station stands. It's the best spot to watch the great tides. The Granville lighthouse, steps away, has guided boats to the harbor since 1828.

Don't miss the Musée Christian Dior, housed in the villa Les Rhumbs, the couturier's childhood home. Purchased by the Dior family in 1906, it overlooks the cliff facing the sea and the Channel Islands. His mother Madeleine created an English-style garden there, and young Christian designed the pergola and reflecting pool around 1920. The museum hosts a renewed thematic exhibition each year—haute couture dresses, accessories, perfumes. The garden is freely accessible year-round.

If you have time, push on to the Musée d'Art Moderne Richard Anacréon in the Haute-Ville. Richard Anacréon, a Parisian bookseller born in Granville, bequeathed his hometown a collection of rare books and works signed by Derain, Vlaminck, Utrillo or Signac. Small museum, quick visit but quality.

activities.text: If the tide schedule allows, witness the rising waters from the causeway bridge or from the ramparts. Mont-Saint-Michel Bay experiences the greatest tides in continental Europe—up to 15 meters of tidal range during spring equinox tides. Legend says the sea rises "at the speed of a galloping horse"—in reality it's about 6 km/h, but it's still impressive.

For a different experience, book a guided bay crossing on foot. Licensed guides take you 7 km across the tidal flats (14 km round-trip) telling tales of the Mont's legends and showing you how to avoid quicksand. Departure from Genêts or Bec d'Andaine—plan on going barefoot and wearing clothes that can get wet.

From the Mortain viewing point (beside the Chapelle Saint-Michel), you sweep your gaze across the entire Norman bocage—and in clear weather, the silhouette of Mont-Saint-Michel appears on the horizon. It's one of Normandy's highest points. In August 1944, the German counteroffensive at Mortain (Operation Lüttich) was one of the fiercest battles of the Battle of Normandy.

End the journey with a stroll along the Plat Gousset seawall and the beach of the same name. This fine-sand beach in an arc, protected by the seawall, is Granville's family beach. The Art Deco casino and bathing cabins complete the seaside picture. If you're there on a Saturday morning, Granville's covered market is one of the best in La Manche—fish, seafood, salt-marsh lamb.

places.name: Grasse

visits.text: Grasse has been the world capital of perfume since the 17th century: local know-how was inscribed on UNESCO's intangible cultural heritage list in 2018. Lose yourself in the old town's ochre and yellow-facade lanes, where 17th and 18th-century buildings adjoin 19th-century townhouses. The air itself is fragrant — fields of roses and jasmine surround the town from May to October.

The Musée International de la Parfumerie is the largest of its kind in the world: created in 1989 and renovated in 2008, its 3,500 m² trace 4,000 years of perfume history, from Egyptian canopic jars to Lalique bottles. Over 50,000 objects are preserved in its collections. The multi-sensory route with a hundred essences to smell is a unique experience — allow 1h30.

Visit one of the historic perfumeries — Fragonard (1926), Galimard (1747, the oldest) or Molinard (1849): copper alembics, enfleurage basins, creation laboratories and packaging rooms are accessible to visitors. Visits are free at all three houses — they naturally hope you'll buy from the boutique.

The Cathédrale Notre-Dame du Puy raises its 12th-century Romanesque walls at the historic center's heart: it preserves a painting by Fragonard (The Washing of the Feet), a Rubens and canvases by Louis Bréa. It's one of the rare places where you can see old masters' works in their original context, without display cases or protection.

Climb to the village of Gourdon, one of France's most beautiful villages perched at 760 meters on a vertiginous pinnacle: the 12th-century castle, remodeled in the 17th, is surrounded by gardens designed after Le Nôtre's model. The panorama from the terraces embraces the Mediterranean, Cap d'Antibes and — in exceptional weather — Corsica. Allow 20 minutes' drive from Grasse.

activities.text: Perfume creation workshop at one of Grasse's perfumeries: guided by a "nose," you learn to distinguish olfactory families and compose your custom fragrance among hundreds of essences. Sessions last 1h to 2h depending on workshops — book ahead as groups are limited.

The Provençal market at Cours Honoré Cresp perfumes the lanes: tapenades, olive oils from Cagnes and Nice, Marseille soaps, Provençal herbs and of course products perfumed with rose or jasmine. Every morning except Monday.

The MIP Gardens at Mouans-Sartoux (10 km from Grasse) constitute a living conservatory of perfume plants: 700 species including May roses, jasmine, tuberoses and violets that supply Grasse perfumeries. The "Remarkable Garden" label has recognized this space since 2022 — an ideal complement to the museum.

Head down to Vallauris, the potters' village where Picasso settled in 1947 to learn ceramics: artisan tradition continues in dozens of workshop-galleries. The chapel decorated by Picasso with his War and Peace in 1952 is open to visitors.

days_plan.itin_title: Provence, France: Lavender Fields, Hilltop Villages and Riviera Towns

days_plan.itin_title: Saône Valley, France: Medieval Abbeys, Wine and Comtois Heritage

places.name: Grenoble

visits.text: Stop in Chambéry in the morning. Capital of the Duchy of Savoy from 1295 to 1563, the town retains a discreet elegance. The Château des Ducs dominates the old town—you can tour the courtyards and exteriors, and glimpse the Sainte-Chapelle that once housed the Turin Shroud before it was transferred to Turin. On your way down, don't miss the Fontaine des Éléphants in the main square—four elephants without hindquarters, earning them the local nickname 'the four without arses.' The vaulted passages and lanes of the old center are a pleasure to wander, especially on Saturday mornings when the market is in full swing.

In Grenoble, the Musée de Grenoble is one of France's most important museums for ancient and contemporary art—Matisse, Chagall, and Picasso are all represented. The light-filled modern building is pleasant to explore even if you're not a dedicated museum enthusiast. Allow 1h30 for a proper visit.

Take a stroll through Grenoble's old center along the banks of the Isère. The city is wedged between three massifs—Chartreuse, Vercors, and Belledonne—giving it a spectacular mountain backdrop visible from every street. The area around Place Saint-André and Rue Très-Cloîtres retains a medieval character.

activities.text: Ride up to the Bastille in Grenoble's famous 'bubbles'—those spherical plexiglass cabins that have been a city symbol since 1976, designed by engineer Denis Creissels and manufactured by local company Poma. The cable car, inaugurated in 1934, is one of the world's first urban cable cars, following those in Rio and Cape Town. In 4 minutes and 266 meters of elevation gain, you reach the 19th-century fort at 476 m altitude. The panorama over Grenoble and the three surrounding massifs is spectacular—on clear days, you can see Mont Blanc. Walk back down the trails if you have time (45 min).

days_plan.itin_title: From the Alps to the Ardèche Gorges: Lakes, Peaks and Mediterranean Hills

map_keywords: Château des Ducs de Savoie Chambéry

visits.text: Visit the largest Renaissance château in southeastern France, perched on a 33-meter rocky promontory. Mentioned as early as the 11th century as a fortress, it was transformed during the Renaissance into a sumptuous residence. The Marquise de Sévigné stayed here during three long visits and died here in 1696. Admire the Adhémar Gallery, the Tinel hall, the Prelates' wing, and Madame de Grignan's chamber in the former medieval keep.

Stroll the famous terraces built atop the Saint-Sauveur collegiate church that offer unforgettable 360° panoramas of Provence. The view extends to La Lance mountain, Mont Ventoux, and the Dentelles de Montmirail. Madame de Sévigné wrote: 'It is even pleasant not to be tempted to leave your beautiful terraces.'

Explore this charming medieval village ranked among France's Most Beautiful Villages that hugs the contours of the rocky promontory. Reach the village via steep alleys where you can admire the 14th-century belfry and superb 15th-century houses. The village cultivates both its literary heritage and its terroir: PDO vineyards, truffle grounds, lavender fields.

Visit this collegiate church built in the 16th century, one of the first buildings listed as a Historical Monument in 1840. The Renaissance façade features two symmetrical square towers. Madame de Sévigné was interred in this collegiate church on April 18, 1696. She still rests in this history-laden place.

activities.text: Discover Madame de Sévigné's apartments, the painting collection from the 16th to 20th centuries, and the interpretation space tracing the château's architectural evolution. Guided tours illuminate daily life of Provençal nobility in the 17th century. Possibly attend one of the many concerts held in the Adhémar Gallery.

Walk through the surrounding landscapes where vineyards, lavender, and truffle grounds create a typically Provençal mosaic of colors. In June-July, blooming lavender fields offer an unforgettable spectacle with the château as backdrop.

Visit producers directly and the village's fine restaurants to discover local gastronomy. Taste Grignan-les-Adhémar PDO wines, Côtes-du-Rhône, Tricastin black truffles, Nyons olive oil, and goat cheeses.

days_plan.itin_title: Provence Drôme Road Trip: Medieval Villages & Local Terroir

visits.text: Grimaud Castle, built in the 11th century, preserves its three enclosures, two massive round towers, and a medieval keep. Pillaged during the Revolution, its ruins still offer a panorama over the Gulf of Saint-Tropez and the Maures massif. In summer, the ramparts host the Grimaldines festival.

Saint-Michel church, a 12th-century Romanesque structure, is a classified historic monument. Its typically Provençal architecture contrasts with the Penitents' chapels and Saint-Roch, whose restored mural paintings illuminate medieval devotion.

Get lost in the village's cobbled lanes: Rue des Arcades houses the Templars' house, a fine 16th-century Renaissance building adorned with three Gothic arches. Mullioned windows and doorways framed with serpentinite punctuate the stroll.

The Saint-Roch mill, a 17th-century windmill restored in 1990 by the Companions of the Tour de France, crowns the village on its hill. On the ground, a large slab was used for threshing wheat; beside it, an old press produced olive oil.

Reach the Pont des Fées via a 3 km loop from the mill. This 15th-century arched bridge spans the Garde and served as an aqueduct to supply the village with water. The trail follows the river through a cool, shaded valley.

activities.text: Port-Grimaud, a lakeside town created in the 1960s by architect François Spoerry, can be explored by boat on its canals. Each house has direct water access and a mooring ring. The 'Provençal Venice' can also be discovered on foot from the village.
The Provençal markets of Grimaud and Port-Grimaud alternate throughout the week. Local producers and artisans offer fruits, cheeses, olive oil, and art objects in a lively atmosphere.
Several wine estates around the village offer tastings of AOC Côtes de Provence wines. The Grimaud wine cooperative allows you to sample local vintages straight from the source.

days_plan.itin_title: Var Road Trip: French Riviera & Green Provence

visits.text: The old village of Gruissan is built in a "circulade" — its lanes spiral like a snail shell around a large limestone rock crowned by the Barberousse Tower, remnant of a 10th-century medieval castle. Climb up: the 360-degree view over the lagoons, pink salt marshes, sea and Massif de la Clape is spectacular, especially late afternoon.

The Plage des Chalets is Gruissan's most famous image. Its 1,330 colorful chalets arranged in 11 rows facing the sea are the invention of Narbonne families in the mid-19th century, who came seeking sea bathing and gradually raised their huts on stilts to protect them from storm surges. Destroyed in 1943 by Germans fearing a Mediterranean landing, they were rebuilt in the 1950s — this time in a staggered pattern so everyone would have a sea view. Jean-Jacques Beineix's 1986 film "Betty Blue" ("37°2 le matin") made it an iconic location: Betty and Zorg's chalet no longer exists, but cinephiles still make the pilgrimage.

The Salt Marshes of Île Saint-Martin cover 400 hectares exploited since Antiquity. In summer, the salt tables turn bright pink due to a micro-algae, dunaliella salina. Guided tours allow you to see salt workers at work and encounter pink flamingos, stilts and avocets. Bring good shoes — the path runs along the basin edges.

activities.text: The Plage des Chalets and the Massif de la Clape offer 15 km of sand. If you have energy after swimming, climb to Pech Redon (214 m) via the trail from the beach: plunging view over the lagoons and sea, with AOC Clape vineyards in between.
Oyster farms around the Gruissan lagoon offer tastings directly from the producer, waterside. A simple, flavorful moment, typical of the Aude coast.

days_plan.itin_title: Aude Road Trip: Carcassonne, Cathar Country & the Mediterranean

visits.text: Guebwiller is nicknamed 'the town of three churches' — and each represents a different style. The Romanesque Church of Saint-Léger with its two 12th-century towers is the oldest. Its sculpted portal and Gothic choir make it a remarkable example of Rhenish art: take time to examine the historiated capitals before entering.

The Dominican Convent, a listed monument, is another surprise: built from 1294, its ribbed-vault choir dates to 1322. The building was transformed into a hospice then a market hall after the Revolution. In 1838, an industrialist patron had a concert hall built in the choir — Clara Schumann performed there as early as 1862. The acoustics of this nave are reputed among the finest in Europe. Since 2013, the site has hosted exhibitions, concerts, and digital arts.

The Church of Notre-Dame, built between 1765 and 1785 in neoclassical style, completes the trio of three churches. Its Callinet organ resonates beneath the central dome — it became the parish church in 1792 after Murbach Abbey, to which it belonged, transferred its canons there. Continue to the vestiges of Murbach Abbey 4 kilometers away, a masterpiece of Alsatian Romanesque art of which survive the 12th-century choir and transept.

The Théodore Deck Museum traces the work of this ceramicist from Guebwiller born in 1823 who became director of the Sèvres National Manufactory. His enameled earthenware and Art Nouveau creations are displayed in this small municipal museum that preserves the memory of a craftsman of genius.

activities.text: Guebwiller's vineyard is unique in Alsace: it's the only commune in the region to produce four Grands Crus — Kessler, Kitterlé, Saering, and Spiegel. The slopes are vertiginous, some reaching 50 degrees, supported by 50 kilometers of dry-stone walls. Operators offer guided tours in electric 4x4s across these terraces for those who don't want to climb on foot. The tasting that follows helps you understand why these steep terroirs produce wines of exceptional minerality.

The marked vineyard trail climbs among the Grands Crus from lower Guebwiller to the overlook dominating the four terroirs. Count on two hours for the complete loop — solid shoes essential on these steep slopes. The view from the top over the Alsace plain and Rhine is worth the effort.

days_plan.itin_title: Upper Rhine Road Trip: Colmar and the Southern Wine Route

visits.text: Guérande's medieval walls stretch for 1,400 meters in an unbroken ring. Walk the rampart path from the Porte Saint-Michel for panoramic views over the salt marshes and the slate-roofed old town clustered within.

The Collégiale Saint-Aubin dominates the skyline with its bell tower-porch. Romanesque carved capitals and Gothic vaulting reflect five centuries of construction, each era leaving its architectural mark.
Step into the Terre de Sel visitor center amid the working marshes. Modern exhibits and live demonstrations reveal how white gold is still harvested entirely by hand.
Wander cobbled lanes lined with half-timbered houses. Saturday's market fills the Place du Marché-au-Bois beneath 15th-century timber halls, just as it has for centuries.
The Château de Careil preserves Renaissance furniture and 15th-century timber framing. Its water-filled moat mirrors the fortified manor's storybook silhouette.

activities.text: Join the paludiers in the crystallizing ponds to watch salt harvesting in action. From June through September, they rake coarse salt and hand-gather delicate fleur de sel with traditional wooden tools.
Cycle the paths between basins and dikes. Five thousand acres of salt marshes form a silver checkerboard that reflects the sky, crisscrossed by paths perfect for exploring by bike.
Sample oysters and pink shrimp at the oyster shacks in Trescalan. The clay-rich tidal flats give the oysters their distinctive briny intensity.

days_plan.itin_title: Loire-Atlantique Road Trip: Nantes and the Jade Coast

visits.text: The Museum of Art and Archaeology of Guéret, housed in the Hôtel des Moneyroux, a 15th-century mansion, shelters eclectic collections: paintings of the Creuse valley by Guillaumin, Madeline and Alluaud, Limousin enamels, regional archaeology, Asian arts. The collection of Chinese and Japanese porcelains is among the finest in the provinces.

The Hôtel des Moneyroux itself deserves attention: this Renaissance residence with mullioned windows and sculpted staircase tower testifies to the prosperity of the Creuse bourgeoisie in the 15th century. The inner courtyard and rooms with French-style ceilings compose a refined setting for the collections.

The town center of Guéret, prefecture of Creuse, preserves some mansions and Place Bonnyaud bordered by old houses. The Saint-Pierre church, remodeled over the centuries, presents a Romanesque portal. The town remains human-scale, conducive to strolling between shops and café terraces.

activities.text: The Animal Park of the Monts de Guéret, in the heart of Chabrières forest, offers an immersion in wolf territory. Created in 2001, it welcomes about fifty wolves in semi-freedom across six enclosures, the largest reaching 19,000 m². European gray wolves, Canadian white wolves, black wolves: from observation points and the watchtower, observe their natural pack behaviors.

The Giant Labyrinth of the Monts de Guéret, near the wolf park, unfolds its hedges over more than 6 hectares. Thematic questionnaires guide visitors through the plant maze. Mini-farm, skill course, educational space on sustainable development, and aquagliss complete this fun site ideal for families.

The Chabrières forest envelops the tourist sites of the Monts de Guéret. Hiking and mountain biking trails crisscross this forest massif where oaks, beeches, and conifers alternate. The sylvan atmosphere contributes to the spirit of the animal park, placing wolves in an almost natural environment.

days_plan.itin_title: Creuse Circuit: Valley of the Painters and Aubusson Tapestries

visits.text: Pont de la Mariée (Daluis) — 1927 tragedy.

days_plan.itin_title: Hautes-Alpes Majesty: High Mountain Passes, Ski Resorts & Alpine Valleys

visits.text: The Basilica of Notre-Dame-de-Bon-Secours, built from the 12th to 16th centuries, blends three styles across its south facade: Romanesque, Gothic, and Renaissance. It houses a rare Black Virgin, one of the few in western France, a paved labyrinth, and a magnificent 1654 organ case. Every year, the Saturday before the first Sunday of July, the Pardon animates the town—nocturnal procession of the Black Virgin, then lighting of three bonfires on Place du Centre. A tradition documented since the 16th century.

La Plomée, a Renaissance fountain on Place du Centre, is adorned with allegorical figures and dolphins. Vestiges of the medieval castle, residence of the Dukes of Brittany, can still be glimpsed in the urban fabric. Guingamp was the duchy's third fortress after Rennes and Nantes.
The banks of the Trieux, below the old town, reveal themselves via an 80-meter footbridge. Stone bridges, old washhouses, and terraced gardens border the river. A haven of calm steps from the center.

Guingamp's former prison, an unusual and rare heritage site, can be visited during guided tours. Period cells, prisoner graffiti, and 19th-century prison architecture—a little-known facet of the town's history.

activities.text: Guided tour of Guingamp, a Small Town of Character. From the town's origins to today, among cobbled streets, half-timbered houses, former convents, and 18th-century mansions. The guide leads you from castle ruins to the basilica.

La Vapeur du Trieux, steam tourist train between Paimpol and Pontrieux. The century-old locomotive descends the Trieux valley at 30 km/h through preserved natural heritage. Castles, manor houses, and grey herons pass along the estuary.

days_plan.itin_title: Côtes-d'Armor Road Trip: Dinan, Pink Granite Coast, Bréhat Island & Guerlédan Lake

places.name: Haguenau

visits.text: Haguenau, nicknamed the 'City of Barbarossa' (Emperor Frederick I Barbarossa had his imperial palace here in the 12th century), is the only town in northern Alsace to offer a Christmas market open daily for five weeks — from November 21 to December 30, without interruption. About sixty chalets spread across five downtown squares, in a pleasant and well-designed pedestrian area. This family-friendly, accessible market, 20 minutes from Strasbourg, is perfect for those wanting to experience Alsatian Christmas away from weekend hordes.

Haguenau is also the birthplace of nativity scenes in Alsace: a register dated 1420 preserved in the Municipal Archives mentions creating a nativity scene at the Église Saint-Georges — the oldest known mention in Alsace. The clay nativity scene created by potter Vincent Remmy of Betschdorf, fashioned with clay from Haguenau forest, is exhibited with free access throughout the market at Place de Neubourg. Exhibitions of nativity scenes from around the world are also visible at several town locations during Advent, notably at the Basilica of Marienthal.

activities.text: The entertainment that delights everyone in Haguenau? The magnificent 1900 carousel — period wooden animals (horses, donkeys, lions, and an Alsatian stork) whose original mechanism still turns. Children clamor for more, adults smile watching it. The bike-tree is another homemade innovation: by pedaling this stationary bike, you power the large tree on the square with electricity. A symbolic and playful gesture at once.

The Haguenau Exception Forest — France's largest lowland state forest, a stone's throw from town — offers marked Christmas trails for families. In December, snow-covered paths beneath pines, with their riddles and explanatory panels, provide welcome fresh air between two markets. Inquire at the tourist office for December programs.

days_plan.itin_title: Alsace's Most Beautiful Christmas Markets

map_keywords: Marché de Noël Cours de la Décapole Haguenau

visits.text: Château d'Haroué displays 365 windows, 52 chimneys, 12 towers, and 4 bridges. Between 1720 and 1729, Germain Boffrand integrated the medieval castle's towers and moats into his baroque masterpiece.
Marc de Beauvau-Craon commissioned this residence from the same architect who designed Lunéville château. The princely family still lives here after eight generations.
The grand staircase features ironwork by Jean Lamour, who also created the gates of Place Stanislas. The Beauvau family tree covers the wall.
The Pillement salon reveals Chinese paintings in a round tower. This boudoir also served as a music room.

activities.text: The French-style gardens spread before the château. Emilio Terry designed them in 1957 with perspectives over the Saintois region.
The château moats are still fed by the Madon river. The walk around the moats offers different perspectives on the baroque architecture.

days_plan.itin_title: Meurthe-et-Moselle Road Trip: Art Nouveau Nancy & Fortified Cities

visits.text: Hartmannswillerkopf — nicknamed Vieil Armand by poilus who couldn't pronounce the Alsatian name — dominates the Alsace plain at 956 meters. In 1915, it became the theater of combat of extreme violence: the summit changed hands repeatedly over a few weeks, both armies digging kilometers of trenches into the Vosges sandstone. An estimated 25,000 French and German soldiers fell here, for virtually no tactical result. The soldiers called it 'the man-eater' or 'the mountain of death.'

The National Monument, built between 1924 and 1932 and inaugurated by President Albert Lebrun, is one of four Great War national monuments along with Douaumont, Notre-Dame-de-Lorette, and Dormans. You access it through an 80-meter honor trench that immediately immerses you in the site's atmosphere. The crypt holds the bones of 12,000 soldiers whose identities were lost. At the entrance, two winged bronze statues are the work of sculptor Antoine Bourdelle. The Altar of the Fatherland, outside, bears the coats of arms of twelve donor cities.

The Franco-German Historial, inaugurated on November 10, 2017 by Presidents Macron and Steinmeier, is the first Great War museum to present events from both countries' perspectives. The scenography follows the evolution of Franco-German relations from the trenches to contemporary reconciliation. Testimonies from soldiers on both sides, archival photographs, and objects from military daily life constitute the bulk of the exhibition.

The battlefield preserves kilometers of trenches carved into the Vosges sandstone, underground shelters, barbed-wire networks, and shell craters. A 4.5-kilometer interpreted route winds through the combat zone. To pay your respects, the Silberloch necropolis, inaugurated in 1922, gathers 1,264 individual graves of French soldiers and six ossuaries.

activities.text: Follow the marked historical trail through the French and German trenches. Explanatory panels at each crossroads situate positions and recount the 1915 offensives. The complete circuit covers about 4.5 kilometers and takes two hours. Bring good shoes — the terrain is irregular and slopes can be slippery in wet weather.

From the Panorama Rock (Aussichtsfelsen), at the ridge's extremity, the view opens over the southern Alsace plain to Mulhouse, across the Belfort Gap, and on clear days to the Bernese Alps. Here you understand why this summit was so fiercely contested — it constituted an exceptional strategic observation point over the entire border.

days_plan.itin_title: Upper Rhine Road Trip: Colmar and the Southern Wine Route

visits.text: Perched on a rocky spur of the Meuse Heights, Hattonchâtel village overlooks the Woëvre plain by 80 meters. Fortified in the 9th century by Hatton, Bishop of Verdun, it preserves the 12th-14th century vaulted house, the 14th-century arcaded house, and a 1921 washhouse inspired by medieval and Japanese design.

The Saint-Maur collegiate church houses the Hattonchâtel altarpiece (1523), attributed to sculptor Ligier Richier. This polychrome stone triptych depicts three Passion of Christ scenes in a setting borrowed from the Italian Renaissance: scroll pilasters, interlaced motifs, and basket-handle arches.

The 14th-century cloister, of which only one gallery remains, adjoins the church. Stained glass windows by Jacques Grüber, Nancy master glassmaker, illuminate the nave with colored reflections. The 16th-century calvary outside completes this ensemble classified as Historic Monuments.

The castle, rebuilt in the 1920s thanks to American patron Belle Skinner, blends medieval fantasy with Norman checkerboard stonework. A private property housing a hotel, it can be visited by appointment. From the esplanade, the panorama extends over the Meuse Heights, mirabelle orchards, and Lake Madine.

Climb to the Montsec Butte (375 m) to discover the American memorial erected in honor of troops who reduced the Saint-Mihiel salient in September 1918. The bronze orientation table and circular colonnade offer a 360° panorama over the Woëvre plain and Lake Madine.

activities.text: Cycle around Lake Madine: a 20-kilometer bike path circles this large body of water ideal for water sports, swimming, and observing migratory birds.
Wander through Hattonchâtel's lanes following the arcaded street. Bucolic gardens border village houses and viewpoints offer views over the vineyards and orchards of the northern Meuse Heights.
Want to taste the terroir? Producers of the Meuse Heights offer gray, white, and red wines from Lorraine grape varieties with tangy and fruity aromas.

days_plan.itin_title: Meuse Circuit: Verdun and the Citadels

visits.text: Haut-Koenigsbourg castle is history within history. First mentioned in 1147, razed by Swedish troops after a 52-day siege in 1633, it lay in ruins for two and a half centuries before the city of Sélestat offered it in 1899 to Emperor Wilhelm II of Hohenzollern. He saw in it a symbol of Alsace's Germanic identity and entrusted the restoration to architect Bodo Ebhardt—a project from 1900 to 1908 that resurrected the castle as it was around 1500, in the Thierstein era. Pass through the high gate: two-thirds of the walls are medieval, the rest reconstructed using documented methods. Nearly 550,000 visitors per year.

The castle's lower court houses a forge, water mill, and reconstructed inn—a village within the village, which functioned self-sufficiently during sieges. The ramparts extend 270 meters. Two artillery towers flank the great bastion. Climb to the bastion terrace for the panorama: the Alsace plain, the Black Forest to the east, the Vosges to the west, and on exceptional days the Swiss Alps. Bring warm clothing—even in summer, it's often 5°C cooler than in the plain.

The banquet hall—called the 'Kaiser's Hall'—is the castle's most overtly political room. Wilhelm II had it enlarged beyond its original dimensions (a gallery indicates the initial height) and covered with heraldic frescoes by Léo Schnug illustrating Alsace's Germanic character. The imperial eagle dominates the coats of arms of Alsatian cities. Propaganda in stone worth seeing for what it reveals about the restoration's political ambition.

The medieval garden arranged in the lower enceinte cultivates aromatic and medicinal plants according to medieval methods—squares bordered by woven branches called 'plessis,' which protected against wild animals. Jean Renoir filmed a scene from 'La Grande Illusion' (1937) here, and illustrator John Howe, who worked on 'The Lord of the Rings,' says he was inspired by the castle.

activities.text: Theatrical tours animate the castle in season, with costumed actors reenacting scenes from medieval life. Check the castle's website program before your visit—time slots are often limited and animations vary by day and season.

days_plan.itin_title: Alsace Wine Route: Strasbourg to the Vosges

visits.text: Hauteville-Lompnes unfolds on a plateau at 800 meters altitude, benefiting from an exceptional climate that earned it the nickname 'Little Switzerland.' The town developed in the 19th century thanks to tuberculosis sanatoria that attracted thousands of patients. Period architecture, large parks, and tree-lined avenues testify to this past. Today, medical institutions have been converted while preserving their heritage character.

The Church of Saint-Pierre, rebuilt in the 19th century, stands out with its imposing neo-Gothic facade. The interior houses remarkable stained glass windows and period religious furniture. The bell tower serves as a landmark visible from the entire plateau. The peaceful square invites contemplation while enjoying pure mountain air.

Walk through the Vallon park, a green oasis in the heart of town. Century-old trees, flowerbeds, and winding paths create a romantic atmosphere inherited from the sanatorium era. The arboretum presents rare species adapted to mountain climate. Children's play areas and picnic tables attract families. The park hosts outdoor concerts in summer.

The Plateau de Retord, accessible by small mountain roads, offers spectacular wild landscapes. Vast expanses of meadows alternate with dense forests. The plateau, at 1200 meters altitude, serves as a Nordic skiing area in winter. In summer, hikers discover intact nature and exceptional panoramas extending to Mont Blanc. Marmots and chamois can be observed with binoculars.

activities.text: Cross-country ski on the Plateau de Retord, one of the finest Nordic domains in the region. Fifty kilometers of groomed trails wind through forests and clearings. All difficulty levels are represented, from gentle beginner loops to demanding courses for experts. Equipment rental and ski lessons are offered on site. The snowy landscapes and pure air create a magical atmosphere.

Hike the marked trails crossing Bugey mountains. The Crêt du Nu trail climbs to a belvedere offering 360-degree views of the Alps and Jura. The route passes through spruce forests, mountain pastures, and rocky passages. Diverse flora includes gentians, edelweiss, and rare orchids. The approximately 3-hour round trip is accessible to regular hikers.

Discover local gastronomy featuring mountain products. Restaurants serve comté cheese, smoked meats, blueberry tart, and Bugey wines. Some establishments perpetuate recipes from the sanatorium era when refined cuisine aided patient recovery. The warm atmosphere and hearty portions embody mountain conviviality.

Visit the Wednesday morning market that brings together local producers. Mountain cheese makers, vegetable gardeners, beekeepers offer quality products. Direct sales and advice from producers make shopping a moment of authentic exchange. Regional specialties - honey, jams, liqueurs - reflect mountain terroir wealth.

days_plan.itin_title: Ain Road Trip: Bresse Plains and Bugey Mountains

visits.text: Inside the Église Saint-Sindulphe lies Dom Pérignon's tomb. The Benedictine monk perfected the art of champagne-making here from 1668 to 1715.
Look up to discover nearly 140 wrought-iron signs adorning the village facades. Each one tells a story of local crafts and village life.
In the Parc Pierre Cheval stands a bronze statue of Dom Pérignon. A striking mural traces the village's winemaking heritage across the centuries.

activities.text: Follow marked trails through the vineyard slopes. The panoramas across the Marne Valley reveal the same landscape that inspired Dom Pérignon's genius.
Visit the village's independent winemakers. Taste champagnes produced from these historic UNESCO-listed slopes.

days_plan.itin_title: Marne Road Trip: Reims, Épernay and the Champagne Route

visits.text: The Broërec'h Gate frames two massive towers with pepper-pot roofs. Restored after the 1944 bombardments, it houses the museum of art and traditions of Lower Brittany.
Walk along the restored rampart walkway. The view plunges down over the town and the Blavet River. The fortifications bear witness to the walled town's medieval past.
The Basilica of Notre-Dame-du-Paradis soars in flamboyant Gothic style. Its spire reaches 213 feet. The Max Ingrand stained glass from the 1950s retraces Hennebont's history.
The National Stud has occupied the former Cistercian abbey of La Joie since 1857. Inaugurated by Napoleon III, it's the birthplace of the Breton draft horse breed. Classified as a historic monument.
Visit the Napoleonic stables of the Haras. The museum space recounts horse-related trades: farrier, saddler, blacksmith. The ceremonial tack room preserves its original tools.
The port of Hennebont extends at the foot of the railway viaduct. Once a commercial route on the Blavet, it now offers a peaceful stroll away from the bustle.

activities.text: Thursday's market is the largest in the Lorient area. Breton products, crêpes, and kouign-amann on the stalls.
Watch an equestrian show under the Haras big top. Mounted performers present Breton draft horses and acrobatic acts.
The Kerbihan Botanical Park covers 25 acres. 400 tree and flower species from around the world. The refreshing bamboo grove evokes Asia.

days_plan.itin_title: Morbihan Road Trip: Gulf, Carnac & Breton Coast

visits.text: Henrichemont is a 17th-century utopian city. Founded in 1609 by the Duke of Sully, Henri IV's minister, it was meant to be the capital of the Principality of Boisbelle — an independent micro-state where no taxes were paid. The planned layout: a perfect 500-meter square, eight streets radiating from a central square to the city gates. Inspired by Italian ideal cities and Charleville, its contemporary.

Henri IV's assassination in 1610 and Sully's disgrace froze the project. Only the pavilions around the central square were completed. You can recognize Salomon de Brosse's style — the architect of the Luxembourg Palace in Paris — in the pediment facades and ornate dormers. The Hôtel du Bœuf, Hôtel des Monnaies and Maison du Procureur fiscal testify to what should have been.

The Principality of Boisbelle, of which Henrichemont was the capital, remained independent until 1779. No royal taxes, its own justice system, currency minted on site. Louis XV eventually bought back these privileges, ending four centuries of autonomy. A small museum tells this unique story.

activities.text: The "Balade de Sully" is a 4h50 circuit around Henrichemont. You cross oak and beech forests, sleepy hamlets, fish ponds. Cher Pas-à-Pas waymarking. Bring water and a picnic.

From Henrichemont, the Menetou-Salon and Sancerre vineyards are less than 20 minutes away. Menetou-Salon produces whites (Sauvignon) and reds (Pinot noir) in the same style as its neighbor, often at gentler prices. The Château de Menetou-Salon, property of Prince d'Arenberg, can be visited in season.

days_plan.itin_title: Cher Circuit: Romanesque Berry and Jacques-Cœur Route

visits.text: Convent of the Visitation.
Cluny Abbey (largest medieval church).
Ursulines Museum.

days_plan.itin_title: Saône Valley, France: Medieval Abbeys, Wine and Comtois Heritage

visits.text: Hérisson, medieval city labeled Petite Cité de Caractère, sits perched above Aumance gorges. The picturesque site reveals rampart remains, fortified gates and old houses (15th-17th centuries). The Porte de Varenne and Porte de la Bade mark former medieval enclosure entrances. Views over valley and surrounding forests are spectacular.

The fortress ruins dominate the village from their rocky promontory. Towers, curtain walls and keep vestiges testify to this strategic stronghold's medieval power. The Bourbon coat of arms carved in stone recalls lords who controlled this border territory. Climb the ramparts for panoramic views over Combrailles.

The Saint-Pierre church (12th-15th centuries) mixes Romanesque and Gothic styles. The carved portal and interior capitals display medieval stonemason art. Stained glass windows from various periods illuminate the modest nave testifying to village religious life through the ages.

Walk along Aumance discovering old wash houses and mills lining the river. The green banks invite peaceful strolls in nature. Footbridges and footpaths allow exploring gorges where water carved a picturesque path through rock.

activities.text: Hiking trails depart from Hérisson village exploring surrounding forests and valleys. Marked paths wind through woods, meadows and along Aumance. Several loops allow 2 to 4 hour walks discovering preserved natural and built heritage.

The artisan market in summer brings village to life with regional product stalls. Local producers offer cheeses, cured meats, jams and crafts. The friendly atmosphere invites tasting and conversation with artisans.

days_plan.itin_title: Allier Road Trip: Vichy, Thermal Baths and Bourbonnais Bocage

visits.text: Hesdin: medieval town of the 7 Valleys, belfry & Notre-Dame.

activities.text: Nature loop in the forest of the 7 Valleys.

activities.text: Twilight walk around the port + sunset.
