days_plan.itin_title: Normandy's Classic Coast: Étretat Cliffs and Bocage Countryside Villages
Normandy's Classic Coast: Étretat Cliffs and Bocage Countryside Villages
Grand Tour of Normandy: From D-Day Landing Beaches to Étretat's White Cliffs
Calvados Circuit: D-Day Beaches and Normandy
Paris to Bordeaux Road Trip: Loire Valley Châteaux and Vineyards
Complete Normandy Road Trip: Cliffs, D-Day Beaches and Mont-Saint-Michel

visits.text: La Gravière and La Nord surf spots: Hossegor's legendary waves are influenced by the Gouf de Capbreton, an underwater canyon extending to nearly 4,000m depth offshore. The canyon begins at shallow depth near the coast and deepens progressively. These beach breaks, among the most powerful on the planet, attract the world's best surfers during World Surf League competitions each autumn.

Basque-Landes villas: Explore the seafront and its interwar architecture designed by renowned architects (Godbarge, Lagrange, Pomade, Prunetti). The Sporting Casino, a classified historic monument, dominates the beach. These elegant residences with characteristic half-timbering and steep roofs embody the Basque-Landes style of the 1920s-1930s.

Lake Hossegor marine lagoon: This reshaped natural lagoon offers 6km of promenade lined with traditional oyster cabins and elegant villas. The lake's calm waters contrast with the power of the nearby ocean. The complete loop on foot or by bike showcases this unique landscape between fresh and salt water.

Place des Landais: Hossegor's beating heart with its cafes, restaurants and designer boutiques. The laid-back atmosphere mixes surfers, families and vacationers. The mural of La Vague celebrates the town's surf identity. Evening markets in summer with local products and crafts.

activities.text: Surf lessons: Schools offer beginner courses and advanced coaching on spots suited to each level. South Beach suits beginners with more accessible waves. Equipment provided, maximum groups of 8. Booking advised in high season.

Lake loop by bike: 6km of flat bike path around the marine lake. The route offers beautiful views of oyster cabins and allows for tasting stops. Bike rental downtown and near the lake. Allow 45min to 1h depending on stops.

Oyster tasting on the lake: Oyster cabins offer tastings facing the water. Marennes-Oléron oysters accompanied by local white wine. Authentic atmosphere lakeside, feet in the sand. Open year-round, arrive before 12:30pm in season.

Bike paths through the forest: 40km of greenways connecting Hossegor to neighboring towns through the Landes pine forest. Shaded and flat routes, perfect for exploring the region. Connection to the Vélodyssée, the European Atlantic bike route. Electric bike rental available.

days_plan.itin_title: Landes Road Trip: Atlantic Coast, Pine Forests & Gascon Heritage

visits.text: Houdan's keep, a 25-meter cylindrical tower flanked by four corner turrets, was erected between 1125 and 1132 by Amaury III de Montfort. Restored between 2014 and 2016, its 250 steps lead to panoramic views over the Yvelines countryside.

The Church of Saint-Jacques-Saint-Christophe blends Flamboyant Gothic and Renaissance styles. It preserves a 1734 Clicquot organ, a rare classical instrument kept intact, and a 1582 fresco depicting the Montserrat pilgrimage.
Houdan's market hall recalls the commercial past of this rural town. 17th- and 18th-century half-timbered houses line neighboring streets, forming a preserved setting.

activities.text: The traditional Houdan market brings the town center to life each week. Free-range poultry, cheeses, and seasonal vegetables: local producers hold their stalls in the shadow of the keep.
Walk through the old town to spot half-timbered façades, interior courtyards, and carved details on door lintels.
Take country paths around Houdan for a pastoral walk between grain fields and groves. The rolling landscape evokes nearby Normandy.

days_plan.itin_title: Yvelines Road Trip: Versailles and Impressionist Villages

visits.text: The fortified Church of Saint James the Greater is one of the iconic images of Alsatian wine country. Perched on a knoll above the village and surrounded by a hexagonal enclosure with six bastions pierced by arrow slits, it served as a fortified refuge for residents in case of attack — a keep dressed as a church. Built in the 15th and 16th centuries on the remains of an 11th-century sanctuary, it's been classified as a Historical Monument since 1972. The interior reveals a curiosity unique in Alsace: the 16th-century pink sandstone pulpit is only accessible via a staircase passing through a church pillar. Since 1687, Catholics and Protestants have shared it in simultaneum — a cohabitation forced by Louis XIV that continues today.

Below the church sits the Fontaine Sainte-Hune, from which the village takes its name: according to legend, the 7th-century holy washerwoman transformed water into wine during a bad harvest. Beside the washhouse, the 1517 town hall still bears on its facade the coats of arms of Hunawihr's overlords: the antlers of Württemberg, the lozenges of the Duchy of Teck, the double-headed eagle of the Holy Roman Empire, and the two fish of the County of Montbéliard.

activities.text: Walk the circuit through the vines around the church to enjoy panoramas of Ribeauvillé, Zellenberg, and Riquewihr — a trio of villages you'll glimpse all three from the vineyard path. The Grand Cru Rosacker, whose name comes from the wild roses planted at the vineyard edges, produces high-class Rieslings and Gewurztraminers here. Easy loop of 3 to 4 km, 1 hour.

The NaturOparC in Hunawihr is the reintroduction center for white storks in Alsace — the regional symbol that nearly disappeared in the 1970s. Fishing shows feature otters and cormorants. The Exotic Butterfly Garden, located right next door, lets hundreds of tropical butterflies live freely in a greenhouse and botanical park. Both sites are worth the visit, especially for families.

days_plan.itin_title: Grand Est Road Trip: Vineyards, Ramparts and Forests
The 15 Most Beautiful Villages of Alsace

places.name: Hunspach

visits.text: In Hunspach, Alsace changes its palette. Forget the multicolored facades of the south — here, everything is white. The half-timbered houses, built between the 18th and 19th centuries with local wood and lime, form a sober and striking chromatic harmony that exists nowhere else in Alsace. Voted France's Favorite Village in 2020, the commune draws its fame from two unique architectural details: the convex windowpanes on the facades — baroque glazing that allowed residents to see into the street without being seen — and the balance-beam wells punctuating the courtyards open to the street. A strict building code still requires white facades and green or blue shutters.

Push on to Fort Schoenenbourg, 5 kilometers north of Hunspach — it's one of the most important installations of the Maginot Line in Alsace, built between 1931 and 1935. Its underground galleries stretch for 3 kilometers, carved 30 meters beneath the earth, and the fort could house 600 men for three months in complete autonomy. It was the most heavily attacked installation of World War II and withstood nine days of intense bombardment in 1940. The guided tour lasts about 1h30 — bring a jacket, it's cool even in summer.

activities.text: Follow the village's marked historical walk — about 45 minutes to spot remarkable facades, balance-beam wells, and hidden symbols on lintels. The tourist office also offers guided tours with anecdotes about each house. If you want to learn more about old rural life, the Petite Ferme d'Antan (Small Farm of Yesteryear) in the heart of the village displays the tools and utensils.

Head to Seebach, 5 km northeast, another white half-timbered village of the Outre-Forêt, organized in a 'ladder' pattern with two parallel streets connected by three cross streets. Its Festival of Flowered Houses in July is a rare spectacle. Cycling enthusiasts can link both villages on the 49 km circuit that then follows the Rhine.

days_plan.itin_title: The 15 Most Beautiful Villages of Alsace

map_keywords: Fort de Schoenenbourg Hunspach Ligne Maginot

visits.text: Built in the 1920s by architect Robert Mallet-Stevens, Villa Noailles is a classified modernist masterpiece. Gabriel Guévrékian's cubist garden offers panoramic views of the Golden Islands. The villa hosts year-round exhibitions and festivals celebrating fashion, design, and avant-garde art.

Below the villa, Parc Saint-Bernard unfolds its narrow terraces linked by stone staircases. The botanical garden gathers Mediterranean and exotic species: rosemary, wisteria, and rare plants that thrive in this microclimate.

Jardins Olbius-Riquier spreads across seven hectares of greenery. An exotic greenhouse shelters palms, bamboo, banana trees, and cacti. The pond and its cascade offer refreshing coolness in summer. Free admission year-round.

Climb up to the Collégiale Saint-Paul, adorned with ex-votos, then wind back down through the steep alleys of the old town. The Templars' Tower, a medieval vestige of the order, can be visited with a treasure hunt for children.

The ruins of the medieval castle crown a rocky peak at the town's summit. Dating from the 11th century, it offers a panorama over Hyères Bay, the Golden Islands, and the Maures massif. Free access via the ruin trail from Villa Noailles.

Visit the archaeological site of Olbia, a Greek trading post founded in the 4th century BCE. Excavations reveal ramparts, dwellings, and Roman baths by the sea—a tangible link to ancient Mediterranean commerce.

activities.text: On Saturday mornings, the Îles d'Or market takes over the old town. Seasonal fruits, tapenade, soaps, and local crafts fill the stalls under the plane trees in a scene of timeless Provençal charm.

Follow the coastal trail around the Giens peninsula. Between salt marshes, pine groves, and coves, the route traces the salt road and its marshes where flamingos and grey herons pause—a walker's paradise of biodiversity.
Craving the beach? L'Almanarre beach, a long ribbon of sand between sea and salt pans, is renowned for windsurfing and kitesurfing thanks to steady winds that make it a mecca for wave riders.

days_plan.itin_title: Var Road Trip: French Riviera & Green Provence
Southeast France Road Trip: Perched Villages and Dramatic Gorges

visits.text: Place Paoli spreads its century-old plane trees through the heart of L'Île-Rousse. Founded in 1758 by Pasquale Paoli to compete with Genoese Calvi, the town retains its orthogonal layout. The covered market with its 21 columns in antique style, built between 1844 and 1846 and classified as a Historic Monument, hosts stalls of charcuterie, cheese, and honey every morning.

The island of La Pietra is reached by a causeway from the marina. Red porphyry rocks give the town its name (Isola Rossa in Italian). The white lighthouse built in 1875, fully automated and solar-powered, offers panoramic views of the coast and the islets of Roccio, Roccetto, and Piano. The 15th-century Genoese tower stands watch over the waves.

The Church of the Immaculate Conception overlooks Place Paoli. Inaugurated in 1893, partially destroyed by fire in 1914, then rebuilt in 1935. Architecture blending baroque and neoclassical styles. Two 17th-century paintings from the Fesch collection adorn the interior.

activities.text: The Marinella promenade runs along Napoléon beach on the waterfront. A pedestrian path beneath palms with views of the island of La Pietra. The bronze Siren statue (A Sirenella di l'Isula Rossa) has perched on a granite rock since 2016. Best at sunset when the rocks turn crimson.

The beach train connects L'Île-Rousse to Calvi along the coast. U trinichellu stops at all the Balagne beaches: Bodri, Ghjunchitu, Algajola. A scenic journey at leisurely pace discovering coves and maquis. A relaxing alternative to driving for exploring the coastline.

The beaches of Bodri and Ghjunchitu stretch to the southwest 3-5 kilometers away. The finest sand in Corsica, translucent waters. Naturist zone at the northern end of Bodri. Access via coastal path from L'Île-Rousse or paid parking in season.

days_plan.itin_title: Upper Corsica Road Trip: Cap Corse and Balagne
Complete Tour of Corsica

visits.text: Tante Léonie's House - Marcel Proust Museum, a world literary pilgrimage site. In this 19th-century bourgeois home, young Marcel spent his holidays between 1877 and 1880. The quaint kitchen, Marcel's and Tante Léonie's bedrooms inspired the immortal pages of 'Swann's Way.'

Pré Catelan of Illiers-Combray, exotic garden created by Proust's uncle that became 'Swann's park' in In Search of Lost Time. From this haven of greenery, the Beauce plains stretch as far as the eye can see, inspiring the writer's contemplative descriptions. A place for literary meditation.

Church of Saint-Jacques in Illiers-Combray, whose painted framework in bright colors and boat-shaped vault captivated young Marcel. This is where he would place Combray's church in his novel, with its stained glass windows filtering light like 'a historical sun.'

Proustian circuit through Illiers-Combray, renamed in 1971 for the writer's birth centenary. The streets, squares, and houses correspond to locations in In Search of Lost Time: discover the 'Méséglise way' and the 'Guermantes way' that the narrator walked as a child.

activities.text: Tasting of artisanal madeleines at La Madeleine de Proust, those little scallop-shell-shaped cakes that made Illiers world-famous. The flavor of this madeleine dipped in tea triggered in Proust the flood of memories that became the most celebrated in French literature.

Hike along the paths of the surrounding Beauce countryside, where wheat fields undulate to the horizon as in Proust's time. The landscape has hardly changed in a century, offering total immersion in the world of In Search of Lost Time.

days_plan.itin_title: Eure-et-Loir Circuit: Chartres, Châteaux & Proust

visits.text: Saint-Austremoine Abbey Church in Issoire, classified as a Historic Monument since 1840, is Lower Auvergne's largest Romanesque church at 213 feet long and 56 feet high under the vault. Its apse is a masterpiece of tiered volumes, adorned with small columns, capitals, sculpted modillions, geometric mosaics, and medallions depicting zodiac signs. Inside, polychromy restored by Anatole Dauvergne between 1857 and 1860 bursts in vivid hues that transform the edifice into a jewel box of unexpected color within the blonde arkose's austerity.

The choir's historiated capitals develop a complete cycle of Christ's Passion and Resurrection with exceptional narrative quality. The celebrated Last Supper, carved with remarkable expressiveness, ranks among Auvergne's finest Romanesque capitals. In the crypt, Saint-Austremoine's 12th-century reliquary and a 15th-century Last Judgment painting complete this major heritage ensemble.

Issoire's historic center, established in the Val d'Allier, reveals itself through colorful facades and red roofs contrasting with monuments' volcanic stone. The Georges-Duby Romanesque Art Center, housed in a classified chapter house, exhibits collections on Auvergne Romanesque art and hosts temporary exhibitions.

activities.text: Join guided abbey church tours organized in July-August and by reservation for groups year-round. A free activity booklet for 6-12 year olds, available at the Tourist Office, transforms church discovery into an architectural treasure hunt. The abbey opens daily 8:30 AM to 6:30 PM.

Experience the Issoire Romanesque Art Festival, a major cultural event running late July to early August. Concerts, lectures, performances, and themed visits celebrate the town and region's Romanesque heritage in programming that attracts specialists and curious visitors alike.
Walk along the Allier River near Issoire. The Val d'Allier offers wild river landscapes with pebble beaches, riparian forests, and volcanic cliffs—ideal for walking, cycling, or simply contemplation.

days_plan.itin_title: VOLCANOES, ROMANESQUE STONE AND AUVERGNE TRADITIONS

visits.text: White Tower.
Saint-Roch Hospice Museum.
Basilica.

activities.text: Market (Saturday).
Festivals.
Gastronomy.

days_plan.itin_title: Indre, France: Secret Berry Region and Vallée Noire Rural Villages

visits.text: The Courvoisier house stands in its château on the banks of the Charente. Tour of the cellars including the famous 'Paradis' where the oldest eaux-de-vie age. Tasting overlooking the river.
The church of Saint-Pierre blends eras: 11th-century Romanesque foundations, a 12th-century crypt where the former Counts of Jarnac rest, later alterations. Tombs of the local lords in the crypt.

The Musée Donation François-Mitterrand occupies a former warehouse. State gifts received during his terms: paintings, sculptures, ceramics from around the world. The former president was born here and rests here.
The Jarnac quays line up trading houses and bourgeois residences. The Charente flows peacefully, white façades reflected in the water. Starting point for river cruises.

activities.text: Tour of the Courvoisier house. More low-key than the Cognac giants, with a family feel. The 'Paradis' cellar holds century-old eaux-de-vie. Tasting included.
River cruise Jarnac–Cognac. Licence-free barge for the day or guided gabare. The river winds between vineyards and villages. Manual locks, contemplative pace.
Visit to François Mitterrand's birthplace. The house where the former president was born in 1916. Guided tour by appointment, intimate atmosphere.

days_plan.itin_title: Charente Circuit: Cognac and Romanesque Heritage in 9 Days

places.name: Joigny

visits.text: Saint-Thibault Church, built between 1490 and 1529, blends flamboyant Gothic with early Renaissance influences. Its choir leans curiously to the left. Inside, admire the hanging vault keystones and Renaissance pulpit.
Château des Gondi, a Renaissance edifice, overlooks Joigny from the heights of the Saint-Jean quarter. The panorama embraces the entire town and Yonne valley.
At Place du Pilori, the Tree of Jesse House displays half-timbered carvings of Christ's genealogical tree, enhanced with polychrome ceramics. It's one of Burgundy's finest half-timbered houses.
The Bailli House, at 36 rue Montant-du-Palais, is a 16th-century tower house. Its façades bear Latin and Greek inscriptions dated 1573; its half-timbers are carved with scales and lozenges.
Saint-Jean Church, a castle chapel turned parish church in the 11th century, contains the tomb of Countess Aélis de Joigny (early 13th century) and a Renaissance Entombment of great refinement.
Cross the Pont Saint-Nicolas, long the only passage on the Paris-Lyon route. From the Baltard market halls, the view of half-timbered houses tiered in an amphitheater is striking.

activities.text: Climb into the vineyards of Côte Saint-Jacques, an AOC Bourgogne appellation. The trail offers an open view over the Yonne valley, between forests and grain fields.
Wander through Joigny's three historic quarters—Saint-Thibault, Saint-Jean, and Saint-André—linked by steep lanes, stairs, and narrow passages. Each quarter has its own personality and church.
The river port of Les Maillotins, at the foot of the old town, welcomes boaters between the Seine, the Canal de Bourgogne, and the Yonne. Landscaped banks invite strolling.
The Museum of the Resistance traces the action of Groupe Bayard, created in Joigny in 1940. Documents, photos, and period objects testify to the fight against the occupier.

days_plan.itin_title: Yonne Circuit: Northern Burgundy & Chablis Wine Country

map_keywords: Maison de l'Arbre de Jessé Joigny

visits.text: The Château du Grand Jardin was built between 1533 and 1546 by Claude de Lorraine, first Duke of Guise. This pleasure pavilion showcases Renaissance architecture imported from Italy.
The Saint-Claude chapel features a coffered vault adorned with high-relief motifs. Two caryatids representing Temperance and Justice survive from Claude de Lorraine's tomb.

The Renaissance garden extends across 4.5 hectares with compartmented parterres, boxwood topiaries, a verdant labyrinth, and orchards. The collection includes 139 boxwood and 56 yew topiaries—a National Collection.
The 19th-century romantic park unfolds its winding paths, rare plant groves, and centuries-old trees in picturesque arrangements.
The Renaissance Auditoire de Justice displays architectural style influenced by Italian campaigns. Notre-Dame church's portal also dates from this transformative period.

activities.text: Wander through this Petite Cité de Caractère's lanes. Follow the medieval town circuit from the upper château down to the Quai des Peceaux along the Marne.
Visit the Mon Moulin gardens in nearby Thonnance-lès-Joinville. Three National Collections of peonies flourish across 6,000 square meters.

days_plan.itin_title: Grand Est Road Trip: Vineyards, Ramparts and Forests
Haute-Marne Discovery: Langres & Historic Heritage

visits.text: Joinville was nicknamed "Hollywood-sur-Marne" — and it wasn't a metaphor. The Pathé studios, born in 1910 from a furniture rental warehouse on Avenue du Général Gallieni, produced between 1910 and 1987 up to 40% of French cinema: Les Enfants du Paradis, Marius, Les Misérables were shot there. The buildings were demolished in 1991 for a residential development, but the GTC laboratories chimney still stands on the quay — look for it. The La Belle Équipe footbridge (1933, used in the 1936 film of the same name) still spans the petit bras de Polangis.

Île Fanac is accessible by elevator from the Joinville bridge. Go down: 17 hectares, the Municipal Arts School with its turrets and chimera weather vane, and the Boat House of the Société Nautique de la Marne founded in 1876. This is also where Zola set the scene of Jullien's tavern for Au Bonheur des Dames.

Walk along Quai Gabriel Péri from the bridge: Art Deco and Art Nouveau villas line the banks, remnants of when wealthy Parisians had weekend houses built here. The former Pathé factory, converted into post-production workshops, is still active on this quay.

activities.text: Chez Gégène has existed since 1918 — Eugène Favreux, called Gégène, built the building himself on the site of a burned barge. He simultaneously ran the Pathé studios canteen, which brought him Jean Gabin, Édith Piaf, and pretty much the entire profession. Red checkered tablecloths, moules-frites, popular dance hall on weekends from May to September. Book if you're going on a Saturday.

Vendors offer electric boats requiring no license from the marina. If you want something fairly unusual, navigate through the Saint-Maur canal tunnel: 1,070 meters under the town, completed in 1822 on Napoleon's order to short-circuit the Marne loop.

days_plan.itin_title: Discovering Val-de-Marne in 10 Days
Val-de-Marne Road Trip: Castles and River Marne Banks

visits.text: Josselin Château overlooks the Oust River. Three medieval towers on the river side. On the courtyard side, the Renaissance façade in carved granite unfolds like stone lacework. Home to the Rohan family since the 15th century.
The Doll and Toy Museum occupies the former stables. The Rohan collection includes over 3,000 dolls from the 19th century. Temporary exhibitions complement the visit.

The château gardens are arranged on three levels. The formal French garden designed by Achille Duchêne, who also worked at Vaux-le-Vicomte. The rose garden contains over 150 rose bushes. English-style park at the foot of the ramparts.

The Basilica of Notre-Dame-du-Roncier blends Romanesque and Flamboyant Gothic styles. The recumbent effigies of Olivier de Clisson and Marguerite de Rohan rest in the nave. The wrought-iron pulpit is the work of Eustache Roussin.
Climb the 138 steps of the basilica's bell tower. Panorama over the Oust, the château, and the medieval town. Legend tells that in 808 a peasant discovered a statue of the Virgin in a thornbush.

The Sainte-Croix quarter climbs the hillside. Restored in 2006, it's among Josselin's oldest neighborhoods. The 11th-century Sainte-Croix chapel overlooks the valley. Half-timbered houses and cobbled lanes.
The House of the Caryatids dates from 1538. Its carved half-timbered façades are classified as historic monuments. One of Josselin's oldest dwellings.

activities.text: From the riverside quay at the château's foot, embark by paddleboard, canoe, or electric boat on the Nantes-Brest Canal.
Cycle along the canal toward Rohan. 15 miles through the countryside. Possible stop at Timadeuc Abbey, which makes Timanoix cheese aged in walnut liqueur.
Every two years on July 14th, the medieval festival takes over the streets. Knights in armor, jugglers, musicians, and a medieval market recreate the era of the Dukes of Brittany.

days_plan.itin_title: Morbihan Road Trip: Gulf, Carnac & Breton Coast

visits.text: Joyeuse spreads its medieval lanes around the shaded central square. The historic village preserves private mansions and ancient facades testifying to its commercial past.

activities.text: The weekly market brings Joyeuse to life every Thursday morning. Local producers and artisans offer Cévennes specialties, cheeses, and regional products.

days_plan.itin_title: Ardèche Road Trip: Wild Gorges and Heritage Villages

visits.text: Ancient Noviodunum, now the commune of Jublains, is western France's most important Gallo-Roman site. Capital of the Gallic Diablintes people and founded in the 1st century AD, the city counted 3,000 to 4,000 inhabitants at its peak and served as a road junction connected to a dozen cities.
The Gallo-Roman temple on the road to Mayenne marks the town's origin. Rebuilt in stone after 65 AD, it testifies to the acceleration of Romanization in the mid-1st century.

The fortress is the site's most spectacular element and a monument unique in the Roman Empire. This fortified trapezoid measuring 117.50 × 104.25 meters wasn't a military camp as long believed, but probably a warehouse where taxes in kind and precious metals transited to Rome.

The theater, built around 80 AD and gifted to the city by merchant Orgétorix, sits on the plateau slope facing the Coëvrons hills. Initially fully circular in the 1st century, it was rebuilt as a semicircle in the 2nd. It still hosts cultural events today.

Beneath the current church hide the Roman baths, transformed into a place of worship as early as the 5th century. They occupied a square urban block 60 meters on each side with porticos, bathing rooms and shops—the heart of Roman social life.

The departmental archaeological museum, labeled Tourism and Disability, presents collections from the Bronze Age to the early Middle Ages: pottery, jewelry, coins, game pieces, terracotta Venus statuettes and fragments of the 'pillar with masks' evoking a theater scene.

activities.text: Follow the archaeological trail by spotting the 12 bronze discs set into the ground. They mark a pedestrian circuit between the temple, baths, theater and fortress, crossing the ancient Roman town's layout.
Continue to the Crafts Forum, a venue dedicated to artisanship built after the discovery of an ancient artisan quarter whose activity continues today.

Excavations have been ongoing since 2010 at Jublains: archaeologists and student volunteers are uncovering a vast Gallo-Roman complex—domus or macellum—along the Grazay road. A temporary exhibition accompanies each year's excavation season closing.

days_plan.itin_title: Mayenne Circuit: Hedgerows and Medieval Towns

visits.text: Jujurieux defines itself as the "village of thirteen castles" — an exceptional concentration of medieval defensive residences and 19th-century industrial châteaux in a Bugey village. The coexistence of these two types of architecture, the castle and the industrial, reflects the complex economic history of this Albarine valley, alternately a stronghold and production center. Stroll between the façades and let local guides tell you about the rivalries between noble families that shaped this built landscape.

The urban fabric between the Ain valley and the Bugey hillsides deserves slow exploration. The historic 19th-century workshops testify to the passage from agriculture to textile industry in this part of Ain.

activities.text: Loop "castles and viewpoints" (3–4 km, ~90 minutes) allowing passage from one castle to another while gaining elevation over the Ain valley.
Heritage tour of the historic center: façades, old workshops, and architecture of 19th-century master houses. Allow 45 minutes taking your time.

days_plan.itin_title: Loop Around Lyon: Villages and Golden Stones

visits.text: Juliénas, the northernmost cru of Beaujolais, unfolds its vineyards on granite hillsides that give character to its powerful and structured wines. Legend attributes the village's name to Julius Caesar, who supposedly planted the first vines here. The Château du Bois de la Salle, a former commandery, dominates the appellation and houses a renowned tasting cellar.

The Church of Saint-Just-et-Saint-Pasteur stands atop the hill, offering a panoramic view of the vineyards stretching to the horizon. Its Romanesque bell tower watches over the vines that have made the village's reputation for centuries. The stained glass windows depict wine-related scenes, celebrating the inseparable bond between faith and viticulture.

The former church of Juliénas, now a wine cooperative, represents a unique architectural conversion. The sacred building, deconsecrated in the 1950s, was transformed into a cathedral dedicated to wine. Frescoes by artists Victor Charreton and Paul Borel adorn the walls, celebrating wine and work in the vineyards.

Walk through the old village where golden stone houses bear witness to the prosperity brought by wine. The vaulted cellars, dug directly into granite, maintain constant temperature ideal for wine aging. Some cellars open their doors to reveal bottles dating back several decades.

activities.text: Taste Juliénas wines in one of the many estate cellars. This cru, produced on only 600 hectares, reveals rich aromas of red fruits and spices. Independent winegrowers passionately explain the influence of granite terroir on character. The convivial tasting includes several vintages to understand the evolution over the years.

Hike the Cru Trail that winds through vineyards and offers spectacular views of Mont Blanc on clear days. The marked path passes through the different climats that make up the appellation: Juliénas, Les Mouilles, Les Capitans. Information panels explain viticultural practices and the influence of terroir.

Visit the Cellier de la Vieille Église, a former cooperative transformed into a tasting temple. The frescoes depicting vineyard work and winemaking techniques create a unique atmosphere. Tastings are accompanied by explanations of specific characteristics of Juliénas wines compared to other Beaujolais crus.

Participate in a traditional cooking workshop pairing Juliénas wines with Beaujolais terroir dishes. Local chefs teach how to prepare coq au vin, beef à la Juliénas, and Beaujolais tart. Each dish is carefully paired with an appropriate vintage to enhance flavors.

days_plan.itin_title: Ain Road Trip: Bresse Plains and Bugey Mountains

visits.text: Jumièges Abbey — Victor Hugo called these ruins "the most beautiful in France." Founded in 654, this Benedictine abbey preserves its twin Romanesque towers 46 meters high dating from 1067. The roofless nave open to the sky and the cloister evoke the grandeur of medieval monasticism.

Saint-Pierre Church of Jumièges — This 10th-century Carolingian church next to the abbey displays pre-Romanesque architecture with its irregular pillars and rough stone walls. One of the rare testimonies to Norman architecture before the year 1000.

Seine Viewpoint from Jumièges — The abbey's elevated position offers panoramic views of the Seine loop and surrounding forests. The sunset illuminating the ruins creates a romantic atmosphere that inspired 19th-century writers.

Saint-Martin-de-Boscherville Abbey — This 12th-century Romanesque abbey, remarkably preserved, displays a purity of lines characteristic of Norman architecture. Its chapter house and 17th-century French-style gardens with their yew topiaries create a meditative setting.

Ruins of Château Robert le Diable — This fortress perched on a rocky outcrop overlooking the Seine dates from the 12th century. Legend attributes its construction to Robert the Magnificent, father of William the Conqueror. The panorama spans the valley.

activities.text: Abbey Gardens — These 15-hectare landscaped gardens surrounding the ruins include century-old yew trees, rose gardens and medicinal plant beds evoking monastic gardens. The paths offer constantly changing perspectives on the ruins.

Fruit Route — The Seine loop between Jumièges and Duclair crosses orchards producing Norman apples. Cider producers welcome visitors for tastings. The blossoming in April transforms the landscape into a white and pink sea.
Seine Loop Hike — A 15-kilometer circuit around the Jumièges peninsula offers panoramas of the abbey, forests and the river. The GR2 follows the banks through orchards and Norman villages.

Brotonne Forest — This 7,000-hectare state forest covering the Seine loop offers 250 kilometers of hiking and mountain biking trails. Centuries-old beeches and oaks create a canopy sheltering fallow deer and roe deer.

Brotonne Bridge — This cable-stayed bridge inaugurated in 1977 spans the Seine with a central span of 320 meters. Its height allows the passage of cargo ships sailing upriver to Rouen. An emblematic structure of Norman engineering.

Vieux-Port Viewpoint — This high point in the Brotonne Forest offers a panorama of the Seine meandering through forests and meadows. Picnic tables allow you to appreciate the peacefulness of the river valley.

days_plan.itin_title: Seine-Maritime Road Trip: Cliffs of Étretat, Rouen and Jumièges Abbey
Seine-Maritime Road Trip: Cliffs of Étretat, Rouen and Jumièges Abbey

visits.text: Castle (Museum of Popular Arts and Traditions).
Castle (Baron Martin museum).
Notre-Dame Basilica.

activities.text: Bocage hikes.
Burgundian heritage.

days_plan.itin_title: Saône Valley, France: Medieval Abbeys, Wine and Comtois Heritage

visits.text: Kaysersberg's fortified bridge is unique in Alsace. Built in 1514 of pink sandstone, it spans the Weiss and is equipped with parapets and arrow slits to prevent any attack via the river. At the bridge's center, a shrine houses an 18th-century polychrome Virgin — residents once came to pray here before leaving the town. It also served as an 'open prison' for petty offenders exposed to public ridicule. Crossing this bridge means entering the oldest part of Kaysersberg, whose 15th and 16th-century half-timbered houses number among the finest examples of Rhine Renaissance in Alsace.

Climb to the imperial castle ruins, erected shortly before 1227 by a bailiff of Emperor Frederick II of Hohenstaufen to control the passage between Lorraine and Alsace. Its circular keep — walls 4.42 meters thick — dominates the town by 50 meters. The castle was ruined in 1632 by Swedish mercenaries during the Thirty Years' War. From the summit platform, the view embraces terraced vineyards, the Weiss valley, the Alsace plain, and on clear days, the Black Forest.

Kaysersberg was the birthplace of Albert Schweitzer, born here in 1875. Physician, theologian, philosopher, and musician — and Nobel Peace Prize laureate in 1952 — he founded the hospital of Lambaréné in Gabon and developed his philosophy of 'reverence for life.' The museum dedicated to him in his birth house traces his exceptional journey; his personal objects, organ scores, and correspondence are displayed in a contemplative atmosphere.

activities.text: Walk Rue du Général-de-Gaulle from the fortified bridge to the end of the historic center — 800 meters of Renaissance houses where every facade holds a detail to notice: carved oriels, wrought-iron signs, twisted posts. The 1521 town hall, the Renaissance well decorated with dolphins in the inner courtyard, and the 1594 Maison Brève Faller, which belonged to a cooper, are the masterpieces of this open-air museum.

Join the terraced vineyards above Kaysersberg via the ramparts trail for panoramas of the Vosges and Black Forest. Local cellars offer tastings of Grand Cru Schlossberg — the name evokes the castle and mountain, and the wines produced combine finesse and power. If you're there in December, Kaysersberg's Christmas market is renowned for its authenticity.

days_plan.itin_title: Grand Est Road Trip: Vineyards, Ramparts and Forests
The 15 Most Beautiful Villages of Alsace
Alsace Wine Route: Strasbourg to the Vosges
Alsace's Most Beautiful Christmas Markets

visits.text: The Château de L'Aigle, completed in 1730 to plans by Hardouin-Mansart, stands where an 11th-century fortress once rose—reputedly named for an eagle's nest. Its rubble and brick walls now house the town hall.

In the château's outbuildings, the June 1944 Museum, inaugurated in 1953, was France's first 'talking museum.' Life-size reconstructions, authentic BBC broadcasts, and a 390-square-foot animated strategic map recount the Battle of Normandy.
The Church of Saint-Martin, Gothic in style, survived the 1944 bombings that destroyed 80% of the town. Its ancient stained glass filters soft light.

On April 26, 1803, a shower of meteorites fell on L'Aigle. The permanent exhibition in the town hall displays fragments and recounts scientist Jean-Baptiste Biot's investigation, which proved the stones' extraterrestrial origin.
In the Square Eugène Pasquis, two 300-year-old plane trees—designated 'remarkable trees'—take root between two arms of the Risle. Botanists believe they could become thousand-year-old specimens.

activities.text: L'Aigle's market is one of France's largest. Miles of stalls spread Norman regional products, cheeses, poultry, and crafts every Tuesday.
In Saint-Sulpice-sur-Risle, an active factory produces needles and pins using techniques refined over 190 years. The tour reveals period machinery still in operation.
Trails along the Risle reveal the bocage landscape of Pays d'Ouche, the former domain of Countess de Ségur, who wrote many of her novels at the nearby Château des Nouettes.

days_plan.itin_title: Orne Road Trip: Norman Bocage and Prestigious Stud Farms

places.name: L Arbresle

visits.text: Three kilometers from the center of L'Arbresle, on the heights of Éveux, stands one of the most important architectural works of the 20th century: the Convent of Sainte-Marie de la Tourette, designed by Le Corbusier between 1953 and 1960 for the Dominican friars. It's Le Corbusier's last great work in France, inscribed on the UNESCO World Heritage list in 2016 along with 16 other Corbusian sites. The patron, Father Couturier, had chosen Le Corbusier — not a Catholic — because he had "sufficient sense of mystery." The result: a block of raw concrete set on pilotis in a sloping valley, where light is a material in its own right — light cannons, horizontal loopholes, undulatory glass panels designed by composer Iannis Xenakis. You can stay overnight on site, attend services, and visit the convent on a guided tour. Book in advance.

The old center of L'Arbresle, built at the confluence of the Brévenne and Turdine, preserves some lanes and 19th-century façades from the era of silk merchants. Less spectacular than the convent, but pleasant for a late-afternoon stroll along the quays.

activities.text: Urban route of L'Arbresle's bridges and quays (2 km, flat, ~45 min). The town was once an important textile trading center between Lyon and the Beaujolais.

Hillside trail above Éveux for views over the Lyonnais Mountains. The Tourette estate, acquired by the Dominicans in 1943, spans more than 70 hectares — a forest and agricultural setting that provides striking contrast with the convent's raw concrete.

days_plan.itin_title: Loop Around Lyon: Villages and Golden Stones

map_keywords: Couvent Sainte-Marie de la Tourette Éveux L'Arbresle

visits.text: The Basilique Notre-Dame rises dramatically from the Champagne plain. This flamboyant Gothic masterpiece took 121 years to complete (1406-1527), built to house a miraculous statue.
Carved gargoyles adorn the east end. Each fantastical creature tells a medieval tale frozen in stone.
A 15th-century rood screen closes off the choir. Behind its right arch stands the Virgin statue discovered, according to legend, in a burning thorn bush.

activities.text: Look up at the two mismatched spires. The left spire was leveled in 1798, then rebuilt in 1868.
Drive across the chalky plain. For centuries, the basilica has guided pilgrims walking the route to Santiago de Compostela.

days_plan.itin_title: Marne Road Trip: Reims, Épernay and the Champagne Route

visits.text: The Val-de-Marne Rose Garden was created in 1894 by Jules Gravereaux. The world's first modern rose garden, by 1910 it gathered all known forms of the Rosa genus.
Landscape designer Édouard André drew this revolutionary garden in 1899 entirely devoted to a single flower variety. Thirteen collections organized in chronological evolution.
Nearly 3,000 varieties and 11,000 rose bushes compose the collections. From wild roses to contemporary creations, the fascinating evolution of roses through the centuries.
The French formal rose garden displays its beds around the reflecting pool. Terraced gardens and classical perspectives showcase roses in an elegant setting.
The Norman pavilion now houses dining and a shop. This picturesque building completes the 13-hectare departmental park classified as a historic monument since 2005.

activities.text: June offers optimal flowering to admire all 3,000 varieties in full glory. Intoxicating fragrances and shimmering colors transform the garden into an olfactory paradise.
Jules Gravereaux created the Roseraie de L'Haÿ and Rose à parfum de L'Haÿ roses rich in essences. His work earned him the Legion of Honor and Commander of Agricultural Merit title.
The mobile audio guide offers an autonomous visit via download or QR codes in the garden. Audio description available developed with the Créteil Association for the Blind.
The departmental park features playgrounds, picnic areas, and walks in a 13-hectare landscaped setting. Ideal family relaxation after visiting the collections.

days_plan.itin_title: Discovering Val-de-Marne in 10 Days
Val-de-Marne Road Trip: Castles and River Marne Banks

visits.text: Ecomuseum.

activities.text: GR34 hiking trail.

Boat graveyard.

days_plan.itin_title: Finistère, Brittany Road Trip: Breton Headlands, Ponant Islands & Coastal Heritage

places.name: L Ile D Yeu

visits.text: Port-Joinville welcomes ferries and fishing trawlers. Colorfully shuttered façades line the quays where sea bass, monkfish, and shellfish are unloaded each morning.

The Vieux Château (Old Castle), listed as a Historic Monument in 1890, rises on a rocky spur surrounded by sea. Built in the 14th century at the outset of the Hundred Years' War, it evokes the castle from Hergé's The Black Island.

Climb the steps of the Grand Phare (Great Lighthouse), rebuilt after its destruction in 1944. From its 56-meter height, the panorama embraces the entire island and extends 30 nautical miles out to sea.
Port de la Meule, a tiny fishermen's haven on the wild coast, is lined with colorful cabins. The chapel of Notre-Dame-de-Bonne-Nouvelle overlooks the cove from the coastal path.
Saint-Sauveur Church, listed as a Historic Monument in 1906, is one of the island's oldest buildings. Twelfth-century frescoes were uncovered during restoration.
The Pierre Levée Citadel, built between 1858 and 1866 on the heights above Port-Joinville, served in turn as a state prison and barracks. A permanent exhibition traces the history of island defense.

activities.text: Grab a bike for the island circuit: 28 km of paths and roads traverse wild coastline, heathland, bocage, and golden sand coves.
Explore the island's prehistoric dolmens: the Dolmen des Petits Fradets and Dolmen de la Planche à Puare, both listed monuments, date from around 4,000 BCE.
Lay your towel on Plage des Vieilles, the island's most celebrated beach. Bordered by typical island houses, this fine-sand cove is a pristine corner of nature.

days_plan.itin_title: Vendée Tour: Noirmoutier Island, Puy du Fou & Coast

map_keywords: Port de la Meule Île d'Yeu

places.name: L Isle Adam

visits.text: The L'Isle-Adam river beach is France's largest in a river setting: created from 1895 onward, it extends over 3.5 hectares between the Oise and the forest. Its 44 wooden cabins from the 1920s, large semicircular basin opening onto the river, and music kiosk give it a Belle Époque resort atmosphere that attracted Mistinguett and Vlaminck in the 1930s. Labeled Regional Heritage of Interest, it opens during the summer season. Even off-season, the quays and promenade are worth exploring.

The Parc de Cassan, steps from the beach, shelters a late 18th-century Chinese pavilion listed as a Historic Monument - one of the rare examples of this style in Île-de-France. The estate with its pond and foliage is a quiet, little-visited spot.

The L'Isle-Adam state forest stretches over more than 1,500 hectares of oaks and beeches at the immediate edge of town - a former hunting reserve of the Princes of Conti that became state property at the Revolution. Crossed by the GR1 and large avenues laid out by Le Nôtre, it's easily explored in 2 hours from the center. If you have time, search for the Provost Oak, dean of the forest at about 350 years old - forest rangers can point you to its location.

activities.text: Hiking on the GR1 in L'Isle-Adam forest: from the beach, the trail climbs into the woods northward and offers viewpoints over the Marais de Stors, a riverside nature reserve. Easy loop of about 8 km, well marked. Avoid after heavy rain: the clay paths become sticky.

Rental outlets offer canoes and paddleboats on the Oise from the beach in season (generally May-September, 10 AM-5 PM). The backwaters between the Île du Prieuré and Île de la Cohue are the calmest - ideal in the morning before pleasure boats pass through.

days_plan.itin_title: Val-d'Oise Circuit: Impressionist Villages and Heritage

map_keywords: Parc de Cassan Pavillon chinois L'Isle-Adam
Musée Art et Histoire Louis-Senlecq L'Isle-Adam

visits.text: L'Isle-Jourdain developed around the Save River. The church of Saint-Martin dates from the 14th century with its characteristic octagonal bell tower. The town center preserves some half-timbered houses.
Lac de L'Isle-Jourdain is a leisure base with a developed beach. In summer, it's a swimming and water sports spot popular with families.

activities.text: Walk along the Save River that flows through town. The banks are landscaped and offer a pleasant setting for walking.
Saturday morning market offers local products from the Toulouse region. You'll find vegetables, cheeses, and charcuterie.

days_plan.itin_title: Haute-Garonne Road Trip: From Toulouse to the Pyrenees

visits.text: Nicknamed the "Venetian Comtat," L'Isle-sur-la-Sorgue is crossed by willow-lined canals. About fifteen paddle wheels still turn, remnants of the textile and paper industries that once enriched the town.

The Collégiale Notre-Dame des Anges surprises with its 17th-century baroque interior: 222 sculpted and gilded angels populate the nave, chapels, and choir. The ensemble is a classified Historic Monument.
Over 300 permanent antique dealers occupy seven antique villages, making L'Isle Europe's third-largest art and antiques marketplace.

Visit the Partage des Eaux, a natural site where the Sorgue divides into two branches according to historic distribution: five-twelfths toward Velleron, seven-twelfths toward Entraigues. A small shaded path follows the river.
The medieval Tour d'Argent and Renaissance mansions on Rue Ledru-Rollin testify to the city's past prosperity, enriched by silk and paper trade.

activities.text: Sunday and Thursday markets animate the Sorgue quays. In summer, the floating market parades boats loaded with goods along the canals, following the nego-chin ("wet bottom") tradition.
Rent a canoe or kayak to descend the Sorgue from Fontaine-de-Vaucluse to L'Isle. Transparent water flows between white cliffs and foliage.
Sunday browsers or serious collectors: move from stall to stall during the major antique fairs held at Easter and around August 15, attracting hundreds of vendors.

days_plan.itin_title: Vaucluse: Avignon, Luberon Villages & Ventoux Routes

visits.text: The Grande Plage unfurls nine kilometers of fine golden sand along the bay. An upscale resort since the Belle Époque, La Baule lines the shore with turn-of-the-century villas and Art Deco grand hotels.
Stroll the beachfront promenade shaded by umbrella pines. Luxury boutiques and ice cream parlors line up beside white-fronted palace hotels facing the eternal Atlantic.

The covered market brings together local market gardeners and producers under its metal-frame roof. Pénestin oysters, fleur de sel, and regional specialties change hands in the buzz of morning commerce.

activities.text: Rent a sand yacht or try kitesurfing on the vast beach. Westerly winds blow steadily across the bay, making La Baule a prime spot for wind-powered thrills on sand and water.
The Escoublac quarter preserves the Romanesque church of Saint-Guénolé. This old village core stands in quiet contrast to the seaside resort's Belle Époque splendor.

days_plan.itin_title: Loire-Atlantique Road Trip: Nantes and the Jade Coast

visits.text: La Borne is a unique village: 100 potters for 300 inhabitants. Pottery has been practiced here for 600 years, thanks to clay and wood from surrounding forests. In the 1950s, artist-potters arrived, attracted by cheap workshops and creative freedom. Today La Borne is one of France's main contemporary ceramics centers.

La Borne's studios are scattered throughout the village. Most display the "Open Studio" sign; you can enter, chat with the potter, see works in progress. Styles vary: utilitarian pottery, contemporary sculpture, installation art. Every piece is unique. Prices range from €10 (small bowl) to several thousand euros (large sculpture).

The Centre Céramique Contemporaine occupies old barns. Permanent exhibition on La Borne's history and techniques, temporary exhibitions of French and international ceramists. Workshop, kiln, archival photographs. Free entry. The shop offers a good selection if you haven't found anything in the studios.

activities.text: La Borne en Fête, the first weekend of June, is the major event. All studios open, demonstrations, wood-fired kiln, concerts, outdoor pottery market. Over 10,000 visitors. If you're in the area in early June, it's worth the trip.

The forest surrounding La Borne is crisscrossed with marked trails. The "Sentier des Potiers" (3h, 12 km) passes kilns, clay quarries, old workshops. Explanatory panels on the history of local pottery. Moderate, forest shade. Start from the village parking lot.

days_plan.itin_title: Cher Circuit: Romanesque Berry and Jacques-Cœur Route

visits.text: La Bourboule preserves its Belle Époque elegance along the upper Dordogne River at 2,790 feet in the Massif du Sancy. The spa town owes its renown to 136°F waters rich in bicarbonate, sodium chloride, and arsenic—celebrated for treating respiratory, dermatological, and mucous membrane conditions. The Grand Thermes, palace hotels, former casino, and Parc Fenestre compose a coherent architectural ensemble from the turn of the 20th century. Illustrious guests have included the Queen of Romania, Sacha Guitry, and Buster Keaton.

The Funiculaire du Capucin at Mont-Dore is France's oldest electric funicular, built in 1898 and classified as a Historic Monument. Its Art Deco departure station, expanded in 1930, accesses a cabin that climbs at one meter per second to the Salon du Capucin at 4,085 feet. From there, trails lead to hiking paths, a Sylvatorium for forest bathing, a treetop adventure park, and a via ferrata.

Parc Fenestre offers greenery and freshness in La Bourboule's heart. This spa garden bordering the Dordogne gathers rare species and Belle Époque features in a mountain resort atmosphere. The pure mid-mountain air, naturally low in allergens, makes the resort popular with families—La Bourboule holds the Famille Plus label.

activities.text: Hike to Puy de Sancy, the Massif Central's highest point at 6,188 feet. From Le Mont-Dore, the resort cable car provides summit access after a short ridge walk. Panoramas embrace the entire volcanic massif, the Limagne plain, and on clear days, the Alps.

Discover the Grande Cascade du Mont-Dore, one of Auvergne's highest waterfalls. Accessible by a shaded forest trail from Mont-Dore, the cascade offers refreshing spectacle in a green volcanic setting.

Stroll the spa districts of Mont-Dore and La Bourboule to admire Belle Époque villa and hotel architecture. Ornate facades, wrought-iron canopies, and sculpted pediments testify to Auvergne spa culture's golden age.

days_plan.itin_title: VOLCANOES, ROMANESQUE STONE AND AUVERGNE TRADITIONS

days_plan.itin_title: Vosges Mountains, France: Alpine Lakes, Spa Towns and Forest Heritage

visits.text: The Abbey of La Chaise-Dieu (Casa Dei, "House of God") was founded in 1043 by Robert de Turlande. The 14th-century Gothic abbey church shelters the white marble tomb of Pope Clement VI, surrounded by 55 sculpted figures.
The famous Danse Macabre, a 15th-century fresco painted on the north aisle, is one of the few surviving in France. Three dead and three living dance together: "Death spares neither small nor great."
Admire the 14 Flemish tapestries from the early 16th century, totaling 213 feet in length. Commissioned by Abbot Jacques de Saint-Nectaire, they illustrate biblical scenes from the Poor Man's Bible.
The 144 carved oak choir stalls surround the choir. Chimeras, monks in grotesque poses, grimacing faces: Flemish sculptors let their imaginations run wild here.
Visit the Echo Room, a genuine acoustic curiosity. Stand in one corner and whisper: your companion, in the opposite corner, hears you perfectly.
The flamboyant Gothic rood screen separates the nave from the monks' choir. Above the main entrance, the 17th-century Baroque organ case adorns the interior façade with sculpted musical angels.
The Clémentine Tower, an imposing 14th-century fortified keep at the church's chevet, once housed the abbey's treasures. The neighboring stables have been converted into an auditorium.

activities.text: Wander through the medieval town built below the abbey. The rue des Casernes, narrow and paved with pebbles, is the oldest. Some façades preserve bartizans, mascarons, and coats of arms.
Each August, the La Chaise-Dieu Music Festival, founded in 1966, hosts classical music concerts beneath the abbey's vaults. Check the program.
Step inside the Chapelle des Pénitents, the former monks' refectory transformed into a chapel after the Revolution. Free access year-round.

days_plan.itin_title: Haute-Loire Road Trip: Le Puy-en-Velay & Volcanic Peaks

visits.text: La Chapelle-en-Vercors is the central town of the Drôme Vercors, at the bottom of a basin surrounded by meadows and forests. During summer 1944, it too was struck by German occupation: several memorial sites in the village recall civilian massacres. A memorial site in the village recounts these events. If you visited Vassieux the day before, La Chapelle offers a complementary perspective on the same events—here, the civilian aspect predominates.

The Combe Laval road departs La Chapelle westward: it's one of the Vercors' most spectacular roads, carved into the cliff face 300 meters above the Lyonne valley. The view from developed viewpoints plunges onto the forest below and extends to Royans. The road itself is the attraction: don't leave it too quickly.

activities.text: The village-meadows-forest edge circuit (4-5 km) exits the village via farm roads and follows the basin's wooded rim. It's typical Drôme Vercors landscape: meadows in the basin floor, spruces on slopes, gray cliffs on ridges.

The climb to a high point for basin views from Trois Châteaux (south slope, accessible via forest path) offers the overall geography of La Chapelle-en-Vercors nestled in its plateau. Allow 1h round trip from the village.

days_plan.itin_title: Royans-Vercors Road Trip: Hilltop Villages and Gorges

days_plan.itin_title: Indre, France: Secret Berry Region and Vallée Noire Rural Villages

visits.text: First, step through the doors of Eden-Théâtre at 25 boulevard Clemenceau. Built in 1889, it's the world's oldest cinema still in operation. The Lumière brothers organized a private screening here as early as September 21, 1895—weeks before Paris—and the first paid public screening took place here on March 21, 1899. The Lumière family spent vacations in La Ciotat at their Clos des Plages château, and the town's streets were where they filmed their first movies, including the famous "Arrival of a Train at La Ciotat Station." The building, listed as a historic monument, was saved and renovated by passionate volunteers. Guided tours on Wednesdays and Saturdays afternoons.

Next, head down to La Ciotat's Old Port. The quays are lined with 17th-century houses in colorful façades. At the quay's end, the old Town Hall (1864) houses the Ciotat museum with maritime history and Lumière brothers memorabilia. Did you know pétanque was invented here in 1907 or 1910, depending on sources? A monument commemorates it near the port.

Continue on foot to Parc du Mugel, 20 minutes from the port. This 12-hectare botanical park nestles in a red-rock calanque, sheltered by the Bec de l'Aigle. Climb to the belvedere at 82 meters elevation: the view of ochre cliffs plunging into turquoise Mediterranean is probably La Ciotat's finest. The vegetation is lush—pines, eucalyptus, palms. Bring proper shoes for the trails.

Right next to Mugel, stop at Figuerolles calanque. Smaller and more intimate than those near Cassis, it's distinguished by strangely shaped rocks sculpted by erosion and crystalline waters. If you're there late afternoon, the grazing light on the red rocks is superb.

activities.text: To extend the day seaside, take a shuttle from the port to Île Verte, the only wooded island on the coast between Marseille and Toulon. The crossing takes 10 minutes. On site: hiking trails, small beach, and restaurant—a peaceful escape facing the mainland. Allow half a day.

Stroll the waterfront in the evening. In summer, the port comes alive with nighttime artisan markets and open-air concerts. Try the local bouillabaisse—each restaurant has its own recipe, and locals claim theirs is better than Marseille's.

days_plan.itin_title: From Marseille to Aix-en-Provence: Calanques, Hilltop Villages and Provençal Light

visits.text: The Fier Gorges form a spectacular canyon carved by the river of the same name. A walkway suspended 25 meters above the torrent follows walls sculpted by erosion.
Faces in the rock naturally appear in the limestone walls. Visitors' imagination discerns human profiles and animal silhouettes.
Montrottier Castle dominates the valley from its rocky promontory. This medieval fortress houses collections of antique weapons, faiences, and art objects from around the world.
The castle's circular keep offers a panoramic view of the gorges and surrounding mountains. Vaulted rooms preserve monumental fireplaces.
Menthon-Saint-Bernard Castle stands on a spur overlooking the lake. This fairy-tale residence allegedly inspired Walt Disney's Sleeping Beauty Castle.
Still inhabited by the same family for a thousand years, the castle opens its furnished rooms to visitors. The library holds over 12,000 antique volumes.
La Clusaz displays its authentic Savoyard village at the foot of the Aravis range. Wooden chalets and a pointed church steeple compose a postcard setting.
The Aravis Pass peaks at 1,486 meters offering a grandiose panorama of Mont Blanc on clear days. The winding road crosses pastures and fir forests.
The Balme, Torchère, and Fernuy cirques carve into the massif with their hanging valleys. These natural amphitheaters shelter remarkable alpine flora.
The Burgeat Waterfall tumbles down rocks in the Manigod Valley. A short forest trail leads to this refreshing curtain of water.
The Sulens chalet perched in the alpine meadow welcomes hikers with its farmhouse cheeses. Cows graze the rich grass that gives Reblochon its incomparable taste.

activities.text: Packrafting allows descending the Fier Gorges by water in May and June. This inflatable craft weaves between rocks and rapids.
The 430 steps leading to the gorges' ice cave offer an athletic challenge rewarded by underground coolness.
Visit Menthon Castle during summer evening tours when candles and torches recreate the medieval atmosphere.
Menthon Castle's Christmas market transforms the courtyard into an enchanted village with chalets, ice rink, and mulled wine.
Taste PDO farmhouse Reblochon made in the Aravis alpine pastures. This raw-milk cheese is enjoyed aged or melted in tartiflette.
The Thônes via ferrata offers an aerial route on the cliffs. Ladders, monkey bridges, and footbridges provide vertiginous sensations.
Hiking trails crisscross alpine meadows where herds graze. The climb to the Aravis Pass rewards walkers with an unforgettable panorama.
In summer, ski lifts transport mountain bikers to the summits for descents through flowering alpine meadows.

days_plan.itin_title: Haute-Savoie Road Trip: Alpine Lakes & Mont Blanc
Grand Tour of the French Alps: From the Thousand Ponds to Mont Blanc
Grand Tour of the French Alps: From the Thousand Ponds to Mont Blanc

places.name: La Colle Sur Loup

visits.text: La Colle-sur-Loup was once the capital of the May rose, the main flower in Chanel N°5 perfume. Rose fields covered the hills until the 1960s.
The 12th-century Saint-Jacques priory, founded by Lérins monks, preserves a classified Romanesque chapel with sober stone décor.
The 16th-century Saint-Jacques-le-Majeur parish church houses a baroque altarpiece and a polychrome wooden Virgin and Child.
The old village spirals around the hill, its golden stone houses connected by vaulted passages and staircases.
The fountain at Place de la Libération, shaded by plane trees, was the heart of the village's social life.
Terraces covered with olive trees and vines testify to traditional Mediterranean agriculture.
The medieval bridge over the Loup, below, once led to Saint-Paul-de-Vence via the old road.

activities.text: Visit the rose fields still cultivated to discover the May rose, harvested at dawn in May-June.
The priory's Romanesque chapel can be visited during heritage days or by appointment with the town hall.
The Terraces Trail offers a walk among olive trees and vines with views over the Loup gorges.
The neighboring village of Saint-Paul-de-Vence, 2 km away, deserves a detour for its art galleries and Fondation Maeght.
Sample local olive oil at Moulin de la Brague, cold-pressed using traditional methods.
The GR51 trail crosses the commune, allowing you to reach Vence or Tourrettes-sur-Loup through the hills.

days_plan.itin_title: Road Trip Through the Nice & Grasse Backcountry

map_keywords: Place de la Libération La Colle-sur-Loup

places.name: La Courneuve

visits.text: Parc Georges-Valbon spans 417 hectares: it's the Paris region's third-largest park after the Bois de Vincennes and Bois de Boulogne. Over 310 hectares are classified Natura 2000, harboring more than 140 bird species.
The park's rose garden gathers 15,000 rose bushes. In spring, the paths are fragrant and colors burst between carefully tended beds.
The Victoria Montou park house, by the Grand Lake, hosts free exhibitions and events year-round. Nearby, artist Rachid Koraïchi's sculpture Le Vigilant overlooks the water.
The Terrain des Essences, 13 hectares renaturalized after the Paris Games, has joined the park. An observatory lets you contemplate this still-young ecological reserve, a refuge for local wildlife.

activities.text: Take the 10 km hiking loop offered by the Hiking Federation. The trail winds between seven lakes, woodlands, clearings, and viewpoints.
In fine weather, pedal boats and rowboats are available on the Grand Lake. Families enjoy playgrounds, the carousel, and orienteering courses.
The park's equestrian center offers pony introductions for children. Horse-drawn carriage rides are organized from April to September.

days_plan.itin_title: Seine-Saint-Denis Circuit: Royal Basilica and Urban Culture

map_keywords: Maison du parc Victoria Montou La Courneuve
Lac du parc Valbon La Courneuve

places.name: La Couvertoirade

visits.text: La Couvertoirade rises from the Larzac plateau like a mirage. Its intact ramparts, built by the Hospitallers in the 15th century, encircle a village of 25 permanent residents. Two monumental gates topped by square towers and round towers at corners form the defensive system.

The Templar castle, erected in the late 12th century, is the only castle built by the Templars in France. Classified as a Historic Monument, it houses a space dedicated to the history of the Order of the Temple: armor, iconographic documents, plans. The keep is carved into rock.

The church of Saint-Christol, built by the Hospitallers in the late 13th century in flamboyant Gothic style, replaced a Templar chapel that had become too small. Its position on a rocky spur dominates the entire village.

From the rampart walk, the view embraces lauze roofs, the castle, paved lanes, and beyond the walls, the Larzac causse expanses to the Cévennes. Arrow slits and loopholes testify to the medieval defensive system.

The lavogne, an artificial pond lined with slabs, served as a watering trough for flocks on this limestone plateau where water is scarce. Along with houses' roof cisterns, it illustrates the ingenuity of causse architecture for capturing every drop.

The Maison de la Scipione, starting point of the visit, recounts the village's history through films and exhibitions. The communal oven, Redounel mill (17th century), and 16th-17th century mansion houses complete the architectural panorama.

activities.text: The hiking loop around La Couvertoirade crosses Larzac's agropastoral landscape: dry-stone walls, lavognes, sheepfolds, and expanses of limestone grasslands. Ideal for understanding inhabitants' daily life in the Templars' era.

The network of five Templar and Hospitaller sites of Larzac (La Couvertoirade, Sainte-Eulalie de Cernon, La Cavalerie, Viala-du-Pas-de-Jaux, and Saint-Jean-d'Alcas) can be visited with a combined pass. The commandery of Sainte-Eulalie, the network's mother house, particularly deserves a detour.

The Larzac plateau, inscribed as World Heritage for the agropastoral landscapes of the Causses and Cévennes, is crossed by marked trails. Shepherds still lead flocks of sheep whose milk produces Roquefort cheese.

days_plan.itin_title: Occitanie's 13 Most Beautiful Villages

map_keywords: Maison de la Scipione La Couvertoirade

visits.text: The Grande Arche, inaugurated in 1989, anchors Paris's historic axis. Danish architect Johan Otto von Spreckelsen conceived this 110-meter hollowed cube as a modern echo to the Arc de Triomphe.
The CNIT, built between 1955 and 1958, displays a triangular concrete vault spanning 220 meters per side. This spectacular shell rests on just three support points.
La Défense towers embody glass-and-steel architecture at its boldest. Tour First, Tour Total, and Tour EDF compose Europe's premier business district.

activities.text: The La Défense esplanade spreads across 3 hectares punctuated by modern sculptures: César's enormous Thumb, Barrias's La Défense statue, and Takis's kinetic fountains.
The Quatre Temps shopping center features 220 boutiques. When it opened in 1981, it was Europe's largest shopping mall.

days_plan.itin_title: Hauts-de-Seine Road Trip: Royal Heritage & Gardens

visits.text: The Prytanée National Militaire is an institution of uncommon historical density. In 1603, Henry IV donated his château of La Flèche to the Jesuits to found a college there—his declared objective: "to instruct youth and make them lovers of learning, honor, and virtue." René Descartes was one of its first students, from 1607 to 1615, and traced in the Discourse on Method the intellectual formation he received there. In 1808, Napoleon transferred the military Prytanée from Saint-Cyr here. The Henri IV quarter, 16 hectares classified as historic monuments in the heart of the city, can be visited in summer: three courtyards in succession, the Jesuit library, the halls of honor. Above all, don't miss the Church of Saint-Louis.

The Church of Saint-Louis, built between 1607 and 1621, is La Flèche's Baroque jewel. Its interior decoration was completed in 1693 and revolves around three major pieces: Pierre Corbineau's great altarpiece (1633), the organ and its gallery (1640), and the royal cenotaphs. Henry IV had promised, in his lifetime, to bequeath his heart to this church. His promise was kept—but during the Revolution, in 1793, representative Thirion had the two royal hearts thrown onto the pyre in the public square. A local surgeon collected some ashes in a glass vial, returned to the Prytanée in 1814. Today, a small gilded heart-shaped urn in the north transept arm preserves these mingled ashes of Henry IV and Marie de Medici.

The Zoo de La Flèche truly deserves a visit, and not just with children. Founded in 1946 by naturalist Jacques Bouillault—France's first private zoo—it extends over 18 hectares and gathers 1,500 animals representing 160 species. It regularly features in the top 5 most visited zoos in France and was the subject of the documentary television series broadcast on France 4. The gibbons, lemurs, and okapis alone are worth the trip. Allow at least two hours.

activities.text: Walk along the Loir riverbanks from the Parc des Carmes, where picturesque ruins of the old château remain. The landscaped banks follow the river for several kilometers—a flat, shaded walk, perfect midday. In season, you can continue to the Monnerie lakes which offer a beach and cycling path along the water.

The Cré-sur-Loir marsh, a regional nature reserve about ten kilometers from La Flèche, offers several marked trails to discover the Loir valley's wetland fauna. Herons, spoonbills, and sometimes otters are regularly observed here—bring binoculars and boots if you go after rain.

days_plan.itin_title: Sarthe Road Trip: Le Mans and the Loir Valley

places.name: La Flotte

visits.text: Cross the Île de Ré bridge (2.9 km) and head to La Flotte, classified as one of France's Most Beautiful Villages. You'll discover this small harbor with flowered lanes and white houses with green shutters is one of the island's most charming. The morning medieval market (daily in summer) is excellent—fish, goat cheeses, island salt.

Continue to Saint-Martin-de-Ré, whose Vauban citadel (17th century) is a UNESCO World Heritage site. The harbor is protected by star-shaped fortifications. The shopping lanes are lively yet remain human-scale—you'll find the area's most authentic atmosphere.

Climb the Phare des Baleines at the island's northern tip—257 steps to reach the top (57 meters). Built in 1854, it takes its name from the whales that once beached on this coast. The view sweeps across the ocean and straits. The 1682 lighthouse stands right beside it.

Detour through Ars-en-Ré—its black-and-white bell tower painted like a seamark has served as a landmark for sailors for centuries. You'll find a tiny pedestrianized village with art galleries and peaceful terraces.

activities.text: Get on a bike to explore the salt marshes—Île de Ré has over 100 kilometers of flat, marked cycling paths. You'll pass salt harvesters collecting fleur de sel, donkeys in trousers (a Ré tradition), and migratory birds. Allow 2 hours for a medium loop.
Settle in at Plage de la Conche des Baleines, one of the island's wildest beaches—long, bordered by dunes and pine forest. Few visitors outside July-August. Bring a picnic; there's nothing on-site.

days_plan.itin_title: Nouvelle-Aquitaine Loop: Medieval Villages, Vineyards & Dordogne Valley
Nouvelle-Aquitaine Loop: Medieval Villages, Vineyards & Dordogne Valley

map_keywords: Port de La Flotte Île de Ré
Phare des Baleines Île de Ré
Plage de la Conche Île de Ré

visits.text: 1970s architecture of La Grande-Motte, visionary seaside resort created from scratch by Jean Balladur. Residential pyramids with staggered white facades draw inspiration from pre-Columbian and modern architecture. The Grande Pyramide, iconic 14-story building, dominates the seafront. Seaside residences with bold geometric forms—Point Zéro, Grande Motte, Acapulco—illustrate the seaside utopia of the post-war boom years. The 20th-century architectural heritage classified with Heritage of the 20th Century label attracts modern architecture enthusiasts. Green spaces integrated into buildings create a unique garden city on the coast.

Port de plaisance of La Grande-Motte, modern marina with 1,600 berths nestled between dunes and pyramids. Pontoons welcome yachts, sailboats, and pleasure boats in a protected basin. Lively quays border restaurants, ice cream shops, and nautical boutiques in relaxed seaside atmosphere. Sailing school offers introductions and courses for all levels. Developed promenades invite strolling between boats and brutalist architecture. Sunsets set ablaze white pyramid facades reflecting in calm waters.

Beaches of La Grande-Motte, three miles of supervised fine sand bordered by protected dunes. Grande Plage facing the pyramids offers lifeguard stations, nautical clubs, and deckchair rental. Themed beaches—Plage du Couchant, de l'Esplanade—adapt to different practices: family swimming, water sports, sunbathing. Breakwaters create calm swimming zones for children. Handicapped access with tiralos allows swimming for all. Beaches with Blue Flag label guarantee water quality and preserved environment.

activities.text: Water sports at La Grande-Motte, premier destination for aquatic activities. Schools offer lessons in optimist sailing, catamaran, dinghy, or windsurfing. Kitesurfing practiced on windy spots with professional supervision. Paddleboarding and kayaking allow discovering coast and lagoons at peaceful pace. Scuba diving reveals Mediterranean seabeds, posidonia meadows, and local fauna. Nautical centers rent equipment and organize supervised outings. The tramontane wind guarantees optimal conditions for gliding sports.

Thalassotherapy and wellness at the resort's specialized centers. Marine cures use seawater, algae, and mud for restorative treatments. Heated seawater pools, massaging jets, and whirlpool baths relax after an active day. Facial and body treatments with marine products regenerate skin. Relaxing massages, wraps, and scrubs complete wellness offerings. Half-day or full-day packages include access to facilities and personalized treatments. The zen atmosphere invites a relaxing break facing the Mediterranean.

Evening at La Grande-Motte casino, gaming and entertainment establishment by the sea. Air-conditioned rooms offer slot machines, roulette, blackjack, and Texas Hold'em poker. Weekly tournaments gather skilled players in competitive atmosphere. The panoramic restaurant offers gourmet cuisine with sea views. Shows, concerts, and stand-up comedy animate cultural programming. The lounge terrace allows cocktails facing the waves. The building's brutalist architecture integrates into the resort's pyramidal style.

days_plan.itin_title: Gard Road Trip: Roman Nîmes, Pont du Gard & Camargue Wilderness

places.name: La Malene

visits.text: La Malène occupies a Tarn meander at the gorge floor. The Montesquieu barons' château, rebuilt in the 16th century, dominates the slate rooftops. Narrow lanes descend to the boatmen's landing.
Drive up to Roc des Hourtous or Roc du Serre. These two viewpoints, accessible by causse road, deliver a 600-meter vertical drop into the gorges. Griffon vultures often soar below.
Aven Armand, on the Méjean causse, shelters a forest of over 400 stalagmites. The tallest reaches 30 meters—a world record. A funicular descent leads to an underground chamber of 60,000 m².

The Vulture Observatory, above the Jonte gorges, is a museum space dedicated to raptors reintroduced since the 1970s. Telescopes and live cameras let you track griffon vultures, monk vultures, and Egyptian vultures on the cliffs.

The Méjean causse stretches between the Tarn and Jonte gorges. An almost lunar landscape of sparse grasslands, dolines, and dry-stone walls. Isolated farms perpetuate a UNESCO-recognized agropastoral tradition.

activities.text: Embark from La Malène for the flat-bottomed boat descent to the Baumes cirque. Boatmen navigate through Les Détroits, where cliffs plunge vertically into the Tarn.
Hike down the Jonte gorges from the Méjean causse. The trail winds between cliffs where vultures nest. Simply look up to spot raptors with nearly 3-meter wingspans.
Swim in the Tarn at pebble beaches upstream or downstream from La Malène. The turquoise water is brisk but invigorating. Cliffs frame natural spots sheltered from wind.

days_plan.itin_title: Lozère Circuit: Tarn Gorges and Cévennes

map_keywords: La Malène village Gorges du Tarn
Roc des Hourtous Gorges du Tarn
Roc du Serre Gorges du Tarn
Belvédère des Vautours Gorges de la Jonte

places.name: La Palud Sur Verdon

visits.text: Drive the Route des Crêtes (D23), a 23 km loop starting from La Palud-sur-Verdon. The road winds along the rim of the canyon's north side with about ten vertiginous viewpoints—some drop 700 meters straight down. The Belvédère de la Dent d'Aire and the Belvédère de l'Escalès (facing one of Europe's highest rock climbing walls, 300 meters of vertical face) are the most spectacular. Allow 2 hours with stops, longer if you're a photographer.

Stop at Point Sublime, on the right bank upstream. This is the gorges' most famous viewpoint—the view plunges into the canyon 180 meters deep, with the Verdon drawing a turquoise ribbon far below. The car park fills quickly in summer; arrive before 9 a.m.

activities.text: For hikers, the Sentier Martel is the classic route—14 km one-way, 6 to 7 hours' walking through the canyon floor. Named after speleologist Édouard-Alfred Martel who explored the gorges in 1905, it passes through tunnels carved in the rock, metal ladders, and passages cut into the cliff face above the Verdon. Elevation change is 300 meters. Bring good shoes, water, a headlamp for the tunnels, and don't start after 9 a.m. in summer. It's a one-way trail: arrange shuttle return or a second vehicle.

If the Sentier Martel seems too long, descend to the Couloir Samson from Point Sublime—a short hike (1.5 hours return) that gives you a taste of the canyon floor. You'll pass through a 670-meter tunnel (headlamp essential) before emerging in the gorges beside the water.

days_plan.itin_title: Alpes-De-Haute-Provence Circuit: Lavender and Perched Villages
Alpine Hinterland and Verdon Gorges: From Thorenc to Riez

map_keywords: Belvédère de la Dent d'Aire Verdon

places.name: La Petite Pierre

visits.text: La Petite-Pierre occupies a pink sandstone outcrop that overlooks in one sweep the Northern Vosges forests. Its castle—first traces dating to the 13th century—was considerably remodeled by Vauban in 1684 for Louis XIV, who made it a strategic control point between Alsace and Lorraine.
