Marked trails wind through pine forests, groves, and meadows between 874 and 1,139 meters elevation. The village's natural lake provides summer swimming and water sports. The 18-hole golf course spreads across a beautifully wooded, rolling site.

Memory Trail through the Village. Guided tours offered year-round for groups and on Tuesday evenings in summer for individuals. The circuit begins at the station with historical context, continues through the 'Welcome and Words from our Villages' exhibition, then winds on foot through the village discovering Aunt Soly's shelter, the presbytery, the old village, and the school. The tour concludes with a reflective moment in the temple. Reservations at the Tourist Office.

days_plan.itin_title: Loire Discovery: Wild Gorges and Industrial Heritage

map_keywords: Place de la Fontaine Le Chambon
Jardin de la Mémoire Le Chambon

visits.text: Le Croisic's harbor springs to life at dawn when the trawlers return. The modernized fish auction hall sells sea bass, sole, and langoustines while the catch is still glistening.

The Océarium showcases 4,000 marine creatures in tanks recreating tropical and Atlantic ecosystems. Sharks, rays, and clownfish glide through immersive tunnels that put you at the heart of underwater worlds.
Climb Mont Esprit for a 360-degree view of the peninsula. The panorama sweeps from salt marshes to open ocean, tracing the coastline all the way to La Baule's famous beach.
The Côte Sauvage lives up to its name with wave-battered rocks and dramatic cliffs. A coastal path runs between windswept heathland and the Atlantic, reaching the tip of Pen Bron peninsula.

activities.text: Cycle around the Guérande peninsula between ocean and marshland. Bike paths connecting Le Croisic, La Baule, and Guérande weave through some of Brittany's most distinctive landscapes.

days_plan.itin_title: Loire-Atlantique Road Trip: Nantes and the Jade Coast

visits.text: Le Crotoy: south-facing fishing port in the heart of the Somme Bay.

activities.text: Bay Train / Polynesian pirogue according to tides.

Visit to the Boissellerie du Hérisson, traditional Jura craft workshop

days_plan.itin_title: Jura Lakes & Mountains: Turquoise Waters, Waterfalls & Alpine Cheese Routes

visits.text: Start with the Falaise d'Aval—the star of Étretat. The natural arch towers over the sea at more than 80 meters high, and right beside it stands the famous Needle, rising 51 meters. Maupassant compared the Porte d'Amont to an elephant dipping its trunk into the sea. Climb the staircase from the beach—allow 20 minutes for the ascent—and pause at the viewing platform above the arch for the best photo. Caution: stay at least 5 meters from the edge, as rockfalls are frequent.

Continue along the path toward the Manneporte, 500 meters southwest. This arch is even more massive than the Porte d'Aval: 50 meters high, 40 wide. Maupassant claimed a ship could sail through it under full canvas. If the tide is low, you can descend to Jambourg beach between the two arches—but check tide times beforehand, as the climb back up via the iron ladder at the "trou à l'homme" is forbidden.

Head back down to Étretat beach and cross toward the Falaise d'Amont via the 263-step staircase behind the Hôtel des Roches Blanches. At the summit, you'll find the Chapelle Notre-Dame-de-la-Garde, protector of fishermen. The current building replaces a chapel destroyed by the occupying forces during World War II. Right beside it, the bird-shaped monument honors Nungesser and Coli—it was from here that their aircraft L'Oiseau Blanc was last sighted in 1927 before vanishing over the Atlantic.

The Jardins d'Étretat occupy the top of the Falaise d'Amont. Originally created in 1905 by Madame Thébault, an actress friend of Monet, they were reimagined between 2015 and 2017 by landscape architect Alexander Grivko. Seven themed gardens cascade down the cliff—the Émotions garden with its giant resin faces, the Zen garden with suspended sculptures, the Impressions garden with its statue of Monet before his easel. Allow a good hour. The panorama over the Needle and the Channel from the Amont terrace is the finest at the site.

Return to town and step into the Clos Lupin, the home of writer Maurice Leblanc. He lived here from 1919 onward and it was here that he imagined the adventures of Arsène Lupin. The museum tour traces the story of L'Aiguille creuse, published in 1909, where the gentleman-thief discovers a royal treasure hidden inside the Needle. If you've seen the Netflix series, you'll recognize the locations.

Head to Honfleur this morning—45 minutes' drive from Le Havre via the Pont de Normandie (toll bridge). Start with the Vieux-Bassin, the historic harbor ringed by tall, narrow half-timbered houses dating from the 16th-17th centuries. Arrive before 9:30 if you want the reflection of the façades in the water without the crowds—Honfleur welcomes 5 million visitors a year. Wander through the Rue des Petites Boucheries and Rue de la Prison, the oldest streets in the quarter.

A stone's throw from the harbor, you can't miss the Église Sainte-Catherine. It's the largest wooden church in France. Built in the second half of the 15th century after the Hundred Years' War, it was constructed by shipwrights who used oak from the Touques forest and their boat-building techniques. Result: the vault resembles an inverted ship's hull. The bell tower stands separate from the rest of the church—a precaution against lightning. Entry is free.

Next, climb up to the Chapelle Notre-Dame-de-Grâce, a 15-minute walk up the Côte de Grâce. Since Pentecost 1861, every year Honfleur's children have carried model boats in procession from the Église Sainte-Catherine to here. The chapel is lined with sailors' ex-votos. The view from the esplanade over the Seine estuary and the Pont de Normandie is worth the climb.

After lunch in Honfleur, take the coastal road to Deauville (30 min). Head straight for Les Planches, the famous wooden boardwalk along the beach. Each cabin bears the name of a director or star who participated in the American Film Festival. Stroll to the end—the view down the coast is unobstructed and the chic seaside atmosphere is unique in Normandy.

Cross the bridge spanning the Touques to reach Trouville-sur-Mer, Deauville's more down-to-earth neighbor. The Quai Fernand-Moureaux lines up its half-timbered houses facing the fishing port. Go to the fish market at the port—even if you don't buy anything, the display is a spectacle. The Église Notre-Dame-des-Victoires, in the heart of town, dates from the 11th century.

A day devoted to Le Havre. Begin with the Église Saint-Joseph—impossible to miss, its 107-meter lantern tower dominates the entire city. Built between 1951 and 1957 by Auguste Perret after the destruction of Le Havre by the September 1944 bombings, it's as much a memorial as a church. From outside, the raw concrete is startling. But push open the door: inside, 12,768 pieces of mouth-blown colored glass created by master glassworker Marguerite Huré line the octagonal tower. The light shifts with every hour of the day. The city center reconstructed by Perret has been UNESCO-listed since 2005.

Walk next to Le Volcan, the national theater designed by Brazilian architect Oscar Niemeyer and inaugurated in 1982. You'll immediately recognize its white curved forms set beside the Bassin du Commerce—locals long dubbed it the "yogurt pot." The Petit Volcan now houses the municipal library. Cross the Passerelle François-Le-Chevalier spanning the basin—the perspective over Le Volcan and the water is Le Havre's modern postcard.

The MuMa (Musée d'art moderne André Malraux) sits by the sea at the port entrance. This glass-and-steel building houses one of the finest Impressionist collections outside Paris—Boudin, Monet, Renoir, Sisley. The view over the sea through the bay windows is part of the visit. Allow 1h to 1h30.

After the MuMa, head to the Porte Océane, the monumental gateway between city and sea designed by Perret. Two 11-story towers frame a 600-meter boulevard bordered by low-rise buildings. Continue to the seafront—Le Havre's pebble beach stretches 2 km. You'll see the colorful cabanas and the sculpture UP#3 by Sabina Lang & Daniel Baumann.

Climb to the Jardins Suspendus, laid out in a former 19th-century military fort on the heights of Le Havre. The tropical greenhouses and themed gardens are pleasant, but it's mainly the 360° panorama over the city, the port and the Channel that's worth the trip. Free entry.

activities.text: In late afternoon, if the tide is low, descend onto the rocks at the foot of the Falaise d'Aval. You'll find the remains of the oyster park built in 1777 by Monsieur de Belvert—oysters from Cancale were refined here then shipped to Versailles for Marie-Antoinette, who adored them. Children will love lifting pebbles to uncover small crabs and shrimp.

If the late-day light is beautiful, return to Honfleur's Vieux-Bassin for the golden hour. The façades glow on the western side and it's photographers' favorite time—Monet, Boudin and Jongkind had already discovered the spot in the 19th century.

In Deauville, if the tide allows, walk barefoot on the wet sand. The beach is immense at low tide—you can venture far out toward the open sea. Bring a windbreaker, even in summer: the offshore breeze is fresh.

In the evening, go down to the pebble beach for sunset. The Le Havre sky is famous for its light—it's no accident that Monet painted "Impression, Sunrise" in the harbor in 1872, giving its name to the Impressionist movement.

places.name: Le Mans

visits.text: The Gallo-Roman ramparts of Le Mans are one of the most astonishing things you'll see anywhere in France. Built in the early 4th century to encircle ancient Vindinum—the red city, as the Romans called it, referring to its blood-colored bricks—it originally ran 1,300 meters and today preserves about 500 meters, with 12 towers and 3 posterns still standing. On certain sections, polychrome geometric friezes run more than 8 meters high—a decorative richness unique in the entire former Roman Empire. Le Mans is, in fact, a candidate for UNESCO listing for these ramparts. For the best view, descend the Pans-de-Gorron staircase to the left of the bishop's palace and look up.

The Cité Plantagenêt—old Le Mans, renamed in 2003—spreads across 20 hectares atop the promontory. This is where the Plantagenet dynasty shaped medieval England's history: in 1128, Geoffrey Plantagenet married Matilda, heiress to England's throne, in the cathedral here, and their son Henry II, future King of England, was baptized in the same place. Since then, little has really changed—15th-century half-timbered houses, Renaissance mansions, and cobbled lanes so picturesque that scenes from the film Cyrano de Bergerac were shot here. Walk along rue de Vaux with its climbing roses and look for the houses called des Deux-Amis and du Drapier: their carved signs alone tell the quarter's story.

The Cathedral of Saint-Julien has dominated the city since the 11th century—in reality far longer, its foundation dating according to archives to the 4th or 5th century. Its Romanesque nave and Gothic choir completed in 1254, signed notably by Jehan de Chelles who also worked on Notre-Dame de Paris, make it one of France's most complex buildings, at 134 meters long and 5,000 m² in area. Don't miss two curiosities: the Ascension window in a side chapel, dated late 11th century and considered the oldest stained glass still in place in any cathedral in the world; and the pink sandstone menhir attached to the west facade, nicknamed Saint Julien's Stone, which legend says touching its navel promotes fertility.

The Queen Bérengère Museum, housed in three late-15th-century half-timbered houses, owes its name to Richard the Lionheart's widow, who supposedly lived in one of them until her death. Labeled Musée de France, it houses on the ground floor Sarthe bonnets and 19th-century furniture, upstairs a fine collection of medical and civil pewter, and higher up paintings depicting Le Mans life—including a large canvas by Jean Sorieul of the 1793 Battle of Le Mans.

Three kilometers from the center, the Royal Abbey of Épau is one of the finest Cistercian foundations in northwestern France. Founded in 1229 by Queen Bérengère of Navarre—who died a year after construction began and never saw it completed—the abbey houses her white marble effigy in the abbatial church. Sculpted in the style of Eleanor of Aquitaine at Fontevraud, she is depicted crowned, with a purse at her belt and a lion at her feet. The effigy had been discovered under bales of straw in 1816 then transferred to Le Mans cathedral, before returning here in 1970. The site extends through a 13-hectare park.

The 24 Hours of Le Mans Museum traces the world's greatest endurance race, run without interruption since 1923 on the same 13.6 km circuit using departmental roads south of Le Mans. The permanent collection brings together more than 120 authentic vehicles: the 1924 Bentley 3-litre Sport that inaugurated the podium of the second edition, the Bugatti Type 57G that won in 1937, the Jaguar D-Type, the Ford GT40 victorious in 1969, the Porsche 917, the Audi R18... A showcase of 4,236 1:43 scale models represents every car entered since 1923. Note: the museum is currently closed for expansion work (reopening planned for 2026)—a temporary exhibition of 18 emblematic cars is accessible at the circuit's South Gate.

The guided tour of the 24 Hours of Le Mans circuit lets you access behind the scenes: the paddocks, starting line, the Hunaudières straight where cars reach over 350 km/h, the legendary corners of Tertre Rouge, Mulsanne, and Arnage. The permanent Bugatti circuit—4.2 km—is an integral part of the 24 Hours layout. Local operators also offer sports car tours or karting sessions within the complex.

The Tessé Museum, in Le Mans city center, holds a fine collection of paintings from the 13th to 20th centuries. Don't miss the enameled copper copy of Geoffrey Plantagenet—one of the oldest figurative representations in champlevé enamel in France—and the Egyptian archaeology collection that stands out in this context.

activities.text: In summer, don't miss the Nuit des Chimères: every evening from July to early September, the Gallo-Roman ramparts and cathedral become giant projection screens with sound and light. Wander freely through the Cité's lanes to discover the eight visual spectacles animating the medieval facades—Le Mans heritage seen as you never imagined it.

Stroll the Cité's lanes searching for underground passages that once connected the cathedral to other strategic points in the city. Gallo-Roman baths from the 1st century are partially preserved beneath the Beaux-Arts school—fragments of mosaics following Italian rules of the period—and the tourist office offers audioguides to explore these superimposed layers of history.

Operators offer karting or track driving sessions within the 24 Hours complex—karting on the Maison Blanche circuit, driving simulators, or sports car baptisms on the Bugatti Circuit. For serious enthusiasts, it's even possible to drive a homologated sports car on the legendary track. Book online before arrival to guarantee your slot.

The official 24 Hours of Le Mans shop, at the circuit entrance, is one of France's largest automotive merchandising stores: jerseys, caps, scale models, posters, car accessories. Even if you don't drive, it's a temple of motorsport that no enthusiast can miss.

days_plan.itin_title: Sarthe Road Trip: Le Mans and the Loir Valley
Sarthe Road Trip: Le Mans and the Loir Valley

map_keywords: Musée Jean-Claude Boulard Carré Plantagenêt Le Mans

places.name: Le Mont Dore

visits.text: Climb Puy de Sancy, the highest summit in the Massif Central at 1,885 meters—and the highest volcano in mainland France. This stratovolcano began forming 5 million years ago and went dormant about 250,000 years ago. The cable car, in service since 1936 (one of France's first), deposits you at 1,790 meters in 4 minutes from the resort base. Then 864 wooden steps lead you to the summit in 20 more minutes. From the top on clear days, the Chaîne des Puys, the Cantal mountains, Forez, and even the Alps spread before you.

Descending from the cable car, search out the sources of the Dordogne. The Dore and Dogne rivers rise on the flanks of Sancy at 1,680 and 1,640 meters altitude respectively—joining at 1,370 meters, they form the Dordogne, which then flows 483 km to the Gironde estuary. An interpretive panel on the trail guides you to the sources, which seem to spring from nowhere on a plateau covered in broom.

Back in Mont-Dore, visit the Art Nouveau thermal establishment, open since 1817. The town is a renowned spa resort for respiratory treatments—the waters rich in bicarbonate and arsenite gush forth at 44°C. Even without taking a cure, the interior architecture of the thermal building is worth the detour.

Mont-Dore and surroundings conceal a series of waterfalls worth a full day. The Grande Cascade, Auvergne's highest at 30 meters, is accessible on foot from downtown Mont-Dore via a well-marked trail (45 minutes round trip, easy). The Cascade du Saut du Loup, wilder, is reached after a short forest hike. Cascade de Queureuilh and Cascade du Rossignolet complete this circuit—bring waterproof shoes in wet periods.

Ride up to Salon du Capucin on the Mont-Dore funicular, built in 1898 and one of France's oldest still in service. The arrival station at 1,284 meters opens onto the Capucin plateau with panoramas over the Mont-Dore valley and hiking trails connecting to the Sancy ridges.

activities.text: For the more athletic, the complete ascent of Sancy on foot from the Mont-Dore parking offers a beautiful hike of 500 meters elevation gain over 7 km (about 3h30). Climb via the ridges for plunging views over the Chaudefour valley—a national nature reserve whose basalt cliffs are among Auvergne's most spectacular.

Late afternoon, watch for chamois and marmots on the high meadows above 1,500 meters. Chamois are present year-round on Sancy—spot them on rocky ridges. Marmots hibernate October through April but are very active in summer, especially mornings.

Set out on a loop hike from the Mont-Dore Thermes toward Grande Cascade, the Durbise plateau, and Roc de Cuzeau, with plunging views over the Chambon valley and Murol castle. This roughly 12 km loop (4h, intermediate level) lets you link several waterfalls and viewpoints without descending to the village.

End the day strolling through the lively thermal village of Mont-Dore and its animated lanes. The lower town concentrates small restaurants and shops offering Sancy products: Saint-Nectaire, Auvergne mountain charcuterie, mountain honey, and liqueurs based on volcanic plants.

days_plan.itin_title: Auvergne Volcanoes Road Trip: Puy de Dôme and Lakes
Auvergne Volcanoes Road Trip: Puy de Dôme and Lakes

map_keywords: Cascade du Saut du Loup Mont-Dore

visits.text: Carrouges Castle in the Perche Regional Natural Park is a national monument. Built from the 14th to 16th centuries, it's surrounded by moats and you access it via a brick-and-stone entrance gatehouse. The interior preserves period furniture and monumental fireplaces. Enjoy the 10-hectare park, freely accessible.

Château du Champ-de-Bataille, near Le Neubourg, is one of Normandy's most fascinating castles. Built in the 17th century, it was restored by a decorator who recreated monumental French formal gardens over 45 hectares. You'll see geometric parterres, ponds and linden alleys that rival Vaux-le-Vicomte. If the grand apartments are open, don't miss them - the painted ceilings and furniture are remarkable.

activities.text: Enjoy the Champ-de-Bataille gardens for a picnic in permitted areas. The estate's scale allows you to settle in peace, away from other visitors. Bring farmhouse cider and raw-milk Camembert - you're in Normandy, might as well make the most of it.

days_plan.itin_title: Grand Tour of Normandy: From D-Day Landing Beaches to Étretat's White Cliffs
Complete Normandy Road Trip: Cliffs, D-Day Beaches and Mont-Saint-Michel

visits.text: The Vauban Citadel has dominated Le Palais harbor since 1549. Expanded by Fouquet then fortified by Vauban in 1683, it served as a state prison - political detainees were imprisoned there until the 20th century. The rampart walk offers a complete panorama over the harbor.
The Aiguilles de Port-Coton, rocky pyramids battered by waves, fascinated a famous Impressionist who painted them 39 times in 1886. Colors change with each hour depending on light and tide.
Pointe des Poulains, at the island's northern tip, takes its name from the 'poulains' - rock debris extending it. A 19th-century military fort is accessible at low tide via a submersible causeway.
The Grand Phare, commissioned in 1836, rises 52 meters. Its 247 steps lead to a circular panorama embracing the Wild Coast, the mainland, and on clear days, Noirmoutier island.
Le Palais harbor, protected by two jetties, displays colorful houses facing the boats. Pastel facades - pink, yellow, blue - date from the 19th century when shipowners competed in elegance.
Donnant Beach: wedged between two black schist cliffs, this wild beach receives Atlantic swells head-on. Surfers frequent it; swimmers remain cautious of currents.

activities.text: Ride the Wild Coast by bike (rental at the port). The road follows 30 to 60-meter cliffs, interspersed with inaccessible coves and sea caves.
The coastal path from Port-Coton to Port-Goulphar winds along cliff edges between heather and gorse moors. Views of the needles and sea caves are spectacular.
Reach Pointe des Poulains at low tide to access the small fort. Absolutely check tide times: the causeway covers in 20 minutes at rising tide.

days_plan.itin_title: Brittany Road Trip: Historic Villages, Sea and Heritage

visits.text: Le Paradou is an endearing Provençal village that owes its gentleness to its network of irrigation canals — these "roubines" that wind at the foot of houses creating unexpected freshness in the heart of the Alpilles summer. A medieval village established on the Alpilles flank, it has preserved its stone wash houses, roadside oratories, and blonde limestone houses without transforming into a tourist attraction. An hour wandering its streets is enough to understand why artists and photographers prefer it to its more famous neighbors.

Le Paradou's canal network has fed olive groves and gardens on the Alpilles' south slope since the Middle Ages. A walk along these plane-tree-shaded canals, accompanied by the sound of water running over stones, is one of the most restful moments of the entire itinerary. Look particularly for the old open-air wash house where village women still came to wash clothes in the early 20th century.

activities.text: Shaded walk along the irrigation canals (about 4 km round trip, flat terrain). Marked rural paths follow the roubines between olive trees and market gardens. An authentic Provençal quality of life, without crowds.

Walking loop toward Maussane-les-Alpilles (3 km one way): a marked trail links the two villages through olive groves. The return route can take the heights for views over the Baux valley and limestone ridges. Allow 2 hours for the complete loop.

days_plan.itin_title: Alpilles, Aix-en-Provence & Camargue Circuit

places.name: Le Poet Laval

visits.text: The 12th-century keep towers over the village, its pale yellow stone walls glowing in the sunlight. The Knights Hospitaller of Saint John of Jerusalem established a commandery here—one of the most significant in all of Provence.

Step through the gates of the Château des Hospitaliers, meticulously restored by the French Historic Monuments Commission in 1996. The Renaissance wing adjoining the keep now hosts permanent and rotating exhibitions showcasing regional art and history.

The Chapelle Saint-Jean-des-Commandeurs dates from the early 13th century. From rue Neuve, the view of its standing nave and choir is striking—a testament to medieval craftsmanship weathering the centuries.

Wind through the covered rue de la Chalanque, then descend the lower rampart street. Vaulted passages, uneven stone steps, and sun-drenched terraces form a labyrinth blissfully free of cars and modern intrusions.

The former 17th-century Protestant temple—one of the rare survivors in France—now houses the Museum of Dauphiné Protestantism. Its exhibits trace the Reformation's rise, the bloody Wars of Religion, and the exile of Huguenot families who fled persecution.

The Yvon Morin Art Center occupies beautifully restored rooms built from materials salvaged throughout the village. Each summer it hosts major exhibitions alongside intimate evening concerts in the stone halls.

activities.text: Make your way to the Grand Portail, the village's ancient entrance framed by a vaulted chicane passage, then climb to the benches overlooking the Jabron Valley. The panorama sweeps across forested hills and lavender fields that shimmer purple in summer.

Le Poët-Laval marks the starting point of the "Huguenot Trail," a 1,800-km European footpath retracing the exile route of Dauphiné Protestants who fled to Germany. The first few kilometers wind through the surrounding hills and offer a taste of this historic journey.

Curious about local pottery? The nearby town of Dieulefit has kept alive an ancient ceramic tradition spanning centuries. Studios open their doors to visitors, offering a glimpse into the artisan's world.

days_plan.itin_title: Rhône-Alpes Circuit: Perched Villages and Mountain Peaks

map_keywords: Musée du Protestantisme Dauphinois Le Poët-Laval
Centre d'Art Yvon Morin Le Poët-Laval

places.name: Le Raincy

visits.text: The Church of Notre-Dame du Raincy, the world's first church built entirely in reinforced concrete, was erected in just thirteen months in 1922-1923. Its bell tower rises 43 meters. Le Corbusier called it "the Sainte-Chapelle of reinforced concrete."
Inside, 32 slender columns support barrel vaults. Load-bearing walls are replaced by openwork concrete claustra where stained glass is set, transforming the walls into walls of light.

The stained glass, designed by a Nabis painter and created by a pioneering female master glassmaker, adorns all the walls. Ten large figurative windows illustrate the life of the Virgin and the departure of the Marne taxis toward the Battle of the Ourcq in September 1914.
Le Raincy preserves beautiful turn-of-the-century villas along its tree-lined avenues. Walk down Allée des Tilleuls to admire millstone facades and enclosed gardens.
Le Raincy high school occupies a historic property surrounded by a vast park. Its gates and courtyard of honor deserve a glance in passing.

activities.text: Walk to the Allée de Montfermeil, a straight forest clearing that crosses the wooded heights between Le Raincy and Montfermeil. A verdant stroll far from urban tumult.
Look up at Le Raincy's modern architecture: the town served as an urban planning laboratory with its early-20th-century subdivisions conceived as garden cities.

days_plan.itin_title: Seine-Saint-Denis Circuit: Royal Basilica and Urban Culture

map_keywords: Parc du Plateau d'Avron Le Raincy

visits.text: Eu Castle — This 16th-century brick and stone castle was the summer residence of King Louis-Philippe from 1830 to 1848. Its apartments preserve Second Empire furniture and decor. The park of 22 hectares includes a French garden and an English garden.

Saint-Laurent Collegiate Church of Eu — This 12th-16th century Gothic church shelters the crypt where the counts of Eu rest, including the Guise princes. Its flamboyant portal and Renaissance statues testify to four centuries of construction.

Le Tréport Funicular — This 1908 funicular climbs the 100-meter cliff in 3 minutes. The upper station offers a breathtaking panorama of the Bresle estuary, Mers-les-Bains and its Belle Époque villas. An emblematic experience of the resort.

Port of Le Tréport — This fishing port welcomes a fleet of about thirty trawlers. The daily fish auction and the port restaurants offer freshly caught scallops, sole and herring. The quays provide a close view of fishing boats.

activities.text: Mers-les-Bains — Neighboring seaside resort on the Picardy coast, Mers-les-Bains displays 300 Belle Époque villas with polychrome facades classified as historical monuments. Their carved wooden balconies, bay windows and ceramics form a unique architectural ensemble.
Le Tréport Beach — This pebble beach bordered by cliffs has welcomed Parisians since the arrival of the train in 1873. Colorful beach cabins and seafood restaurants perpetuate the bathing tradition.

Saint-Jacques Church of Le Tréport — This 15th-16th century flamboyant Gothic church dominates the port with its massive silhouette. Its pendant keystones and Renaissance porch testify to the port's prosperity in the Middle Ages.

days_plan.itin_title: Seine-Maritime Road Trip: Cliffs of Étretat, Rouen and Jumièges Abbey

visits.text: Lectoure stands on a limestone promontory overlooking the Gers valley. Two miles of ramparts from the 13th to 17th centuries encircle the city, former capital of the Counts of Armagnac. On clear days, the Pyrenees chain cuts across the southern horizon. This part of Lomagne is nicknamed "French Tuscany."

Saint-Gervais-et-Saint-Protais Cathedral, built in the 12th century on the site of a Roman temple dedicated to Cybele, dominates the town with its 150-foot bell tower. The stone spire that once topped it, doubling the current height, was destroyed by a storm on the eve of the Revolution. The interior houses remarkable furnishings.

The Eugène Camoreyt Archaeological Museum, housed in the former bishop's palace—now the town hall—preserves a unique collection of twenty Gallo-Roman taurobolic altars dedicated to Cybele. The Hall of the Illustrious and the pharmacy room complete the visit to this building that also served as Marshal Lannes' residence.

Step through the doors of the Blue of Lectoure workshops. Woad, a blue pigment extracted from dyer's woad, made Occitanie's fortune in the 15th and 16th centuries. Here, the workshop perpetuates the tradition: dyeing scarves, dresses and cosmetics in that deep blue that once seduced kings and nobles.

The old castle of the Counts of Armagnac, now transformed into a hospital, closes off the western end of town. Opposite, the Gothic Fontaine de Diane supplied the city with water since the Middle Ages. The fountain trail leads from one to another, between stone stairways and hanging gardens.

activities.text: Climb to the cathedral bell tower. The 150 feet offer stunning views of the town, the Gers valley and, on clear days, the Pyrenees chain from Pic du Midi to Canigou. Guided tours available through the tourist office.
Lectoure market on Friday mornings. In the town center lanes, producers sell Lectoure melons, foie gras, local saffron and Lomagne garlic. A must for Gascon flavors and temperaments.

Lectoure thermal spa. Housed in an 18th-century mansion, the thermal center draws its water from more than 3,000 feet deep, at 108°F. Pool at 90°F, jacuzzi, hammam and sauna—accessible for half-day sessions without medical treatment.

days_plan.itin_title: Haute-Garonne Road Trip: From Toulouse to the Pyrenees
Gers Road Trip: Gascon Bastides, Armagnac & Musketeers

visits.text: The Louvre-Lens, inaugurated in 2012 on a former mining site, is all glass and light—a SANAA-designed symbol of the coalfield's UNESCO-listed rebirth.
The Time Gallery unfolds 200 Louvre masterpieces across 3,000 square meters of open space. A chronological journey from Mesopotamia to the 19th century flows uninterrupted, without walls or barriers.
Visit the miners' housing estate adjacent to the museum. These 19th-century corons (row houses)—UNESCO protected—reveal workers' life with their gardens, narrow alleys, and communal wells.
Base 11-19 at Loos-en-Gohelle preserves the pithead and its industrial buildings. This UNESCO site chronicles coal extraction from 1891 to 1960.
Stade Bollaert-Delelis, opened in 1932, is a cathedral of French football. Lens supporters, crowned best fans in France, create an electric atmosphere unmatched anywhere.

activities.text: Climb the twin slag heaps of Loos-en-Gohelle, Europe's highest at 186 meters. From the summit, 360° views sweep across the mining basin with glimpses of Vimy and Lorette.
Stroll through the Louvre-Lens landscaped park. The main axes follow former railway tracks, and grass mounds evoke the slag heaps that shaped this landscape.
Discover downtown Lens and its Art Deco treasures. Elegant facades and shops fill a neighborhood rebuilt after 1918 with distinctive style.

days_plan.itin_title: Pas-de-Calais Road Trip: Opal Coast & Historic Belfries

visits.text: Château Gaillard. Richard the Lionheart's proud fortress has dominated one of the Seine's most beautiful loops for 800 years. Built in just two years (1196–1198), this feat of military architecture prompted the King of England to exclaim: 'How beautiful she is, my one-year-old daughter!' The keep offers breathtaking panoramas over the valley and Petit-Andely below. Guided tours available April through November.

Petit-Andely. This medieval village born in the castle's shadow retains its picturesque charm. The 12th-century Saint-Sauveur church and narrow streets lined with old houses invite wandering. Facing the Seine, the quays offer the finest view of the perched fortress.

Notre-Dame Collegiate Church of Grand-Andely. Founded in the 6th century by Queen Clotilde, wife of Clovis, this church blends styles from the 13th to 17th centuries. Its 16th-century Renaissance stained glass windows rank among Normandy's finest. The Sainte-Clotilde fountain, a miraculous spring that appeared during construction, still draws pilgrims.

Nicolas Poussin Museum. Housed in a 17th-century mansion, this museum honors the classical painter born in Les Andelys in 1594. Local history collections, paintings, and art objects trace this strategic city's past.

activities.text: Richard the Lionheart Loop hike. This 8 km walking trail starts from the Seine riverbanks, crosses Petit-Andely, and climbs to the castle via limestone hillsides. The viewpoints over the river's meanders are spectacular, especially at sunset.
Seine river cruise. Cruise ships dock at Les Andelys to admire Château Gaillard from the river. Boat tours reveal this majestic loop from a unique perspective.

days_plan.itin_title: Grand Tour of Normandy: From D-Day Landing Beaches to Étretat's White Cliffs
Eure Road Trip: From Giverny to Normandy's Finest Medieval Villages
Complete Normandy Road Trip: Cliffs, D-Day Beaches and Mont-Saint-Michel

places.name: Les Baux De Provence

visits.text: Les Baux-de-Provence perches at 245 meters on a limestone spur — its name comes from the Provençal "bauç" meaning precipitous place. The first Lord of Les Baux appears in 10th-century chronicles, his family claiming descent from the wise king Balthazar. At their peak, they ruled over 79 towns and villages of Provence. Their fortress was dismantled by order of Richelieu in 1632 to punish their rebellion. Here in 1821 bauxite (aluminum ore) was discovered, named after the village by geologist Pierre Berthier. Arrive at opening (9 AM) in high season — after 10 AM tour buses arrive.

The Château des Baux-de-Provence spans 5 hectares of ruins. The audio-guided tour (in 10 languages, included in ticket) leads you through the keep, the Sarrazine and Paravelle towers, the castle chapel, and the former Quiqueran hospital. Reconstructed war machines — trebuchet, catapult — feature in summer demonstrations. From the summit, on clear days, the view reaches the sea and Mont Ventoux.

The Carrières de Lumières occupy former limestone quarries where the Alpilles' blonde stone was extracted — the same that built the village, the Arles arena, and Nîmes' Roman amphitheaters. This underground space of 6,000 m² hosts monumental digital projections on the walls (about 45 minutes of show). The temperature remains constant at 14-16°C year-round — bring an extra layer even in summer. Programs change annually.

The medieval streets of the pedestrian village merit slow wandering — restored Renaissance houses, mullioned windows, art galleries. The Saint-Vincent church (12th century) preserves contemporary stained glass offered by the Prince of Monaco in the 1980s. The Val d'Enfer, accessible on foot from the lower village, is a narrow valley with rocks sculpted by erosion — its bizarre shapes inspired medieval legends and served as film locations.

activities.text: Viewpoint over the Arles plain from the château ramparts: on clear days, the Camargue plain spreads to the horizon. It's the panorama that made the site photographically famous — arrive late afternoon for raking light on the Alpilles.

Hiking in the Alpilles from Les Baux: several marked trails start from the parking area (paid parking below the village). The GR6 follows limestone ridges toward Maussane, offering alternating views over the Baux valley and the Camargue plain. Access to the massifs is prohibited by order during high fire risk (particularly July-August).

days_plan.itin_title: Alpilles, Aix-en-Provence & Camargue Circuit
France's Most Beautiful Villages: Alps to Luberon in 7 Days

map_keywords: Église Saint-Vincent Les Baux XIIe siècle

places.name: Les Eyzies

visits.text: The National Museum of Prehistory in Les Eyzies houses over 18,000 artifacts spanning 400,000 years of human history. With nearly 7 million pieces in its collections, it's the world's largest repository of prehistoric materials. Paul Dardé's imposing statue of Primitive Man looms above the entrance—a fitting welcome to the birthplace of paleoanthropology.

Font-de-Gaume Cave is one of the last painted sanctuaries of the Ice Age still open to the public. Discovered in 1901, its galleries shelter approximately 230 polychrome images—bison, horses, mammoths, and reindeer—painted 14,000 years ago. To preserve these fragile masterpieces, visits are strictly limited to 13 people at a time. Book weeks in advance.

Combarelles Cave contains 800 engraved figures etched along 300 meters of narrow galleries. Discovered just three days before Font-de-Gaume in September 1901, it stands out for its 48 human representations—an unusual subject in Ice Age art. The engravings are subtle, rendered in fine lines that emerge slowly from the rock as your eyes adjust.

activities.text: The Vézère Valley concentrates about fifteen UNESCO-listed prehistoric sites within a few kilometers. Beyond the painted caves, you can explore the Pataud Shelter (14 distinct occupation levels), the Cro-Magnon Shelter (where the first anatomically modern humans in Europe were discovered in 1868), and the Cap-Blanc Shelter, famous for its sculpted frieze of life-sized horses.

days_plan.itin_title: Dordogne Road Trip: Cave Art, Medieval Fortresses & Périgord Delights

map_keywords: Musée National de Préhistoire Les Eyzies
Statue de l'Homme Primitif Les Eyzies

visits.text: Les Riceys holds the unique distinction in France of having three wine AOCs: Champagne, Coteaux Champenois and the legendary Rosé des Riceys. This pink wine, recognized AOC since 1947, was King Louis XIV's favorite. Produced only in exceptional years from 100% Pinot Noir, it develops aromas of red fruits and almonds. Only 50 to 100,000 bottles produced per year. Several estates offer tastings — book ahead as production is limited.

Largest wine commune in Champagne with 866 hectares of vines, Les Riceys actually groups three villages: Ricey-Bas, Ricey-Haut and Ricey-Haute-Rive, each with its own Renaissance church. The Saint-Pierre-ès-Liens churches in Ricey-Bas and Ricey-Haute-Rive showcase remarkable 16th-century stained glass. Walk through the three hamlets discovering half-timbered winegrowers' houses, wine cellars, communal washhouses. Authentic wine village atmosphere.

activities.text: The Riceys vineyard trail: 8 km marked circuit through vines. Interpretation panels explain viticulture, terroir, Rosé des Riceys specificity. Panoramic viewpoints over the three villages and surrounding hills. Moderate difficulty with elevation changes. Allow 2h30 walking. Download the map from the tourist office before departure.

Visit champagne estates: many Riceys producers welcome visitors to their cellars. Family estates explain their work from vine to bottle. Tasting of their three AOCs: champagne, Coteaux Champenois red and the famous rosé. Meet passionate winegrowers attached to their terroir. Direct purchase possible at estate prices.

days_plan.itin_title: Aube Circuit: Champagne, Medieval Heritage and Orient Forest Lakes

visits.text: Maillezais — Monks in the mist.
Prieuré St-Nicolas — Storm sounds.

activities.text: Access: public (Maillezais)
Access: public (Prieuré)

days_plan.itin_title: Loire Valley Phantoms: Tragic Histories and Haunted Castles of France

visits.text: Les Salles-sur-Verdon is not just any village. The original settlement was destroyed house by house starting in 1973 to make way for Lake Sainte-Croix — the church itself was dynamited on March 5, 1974 at 4:45 PM, and the last residents were forcibly evacuated by gendarmes just days before. The new village, rebuilt on the Bocouenne plateau from 1970 onwards, carries the memory of this sacrifice in every stone: the fountain and washhouse in the center came from the drowned village, the church bells were salvaged from the waters. Walk the 10-station historical trail — the interpretation panels created by the 'Mémoire des Salles sur Verdon' association evoke the vanished buildings with haunting precision.

The permanent exhibition at the Tourist Office recounts the dam's construction and the story of the submersion. Testimonies from former inhabitants are preserved here — if you're lucky, you might meet one of them still living in the village, since direct descendants of the expelled families still reside here today. The lake itself, France's fourth-largest reservoir at 2,200 hectares, flooded the valley over one year between 1973 and 1974, backed up behind the 95-meter-high double-arch dam.

Head down to the lakeshore to contemplate Costebelle Island, a small wild islet facing the village that you can reach by swimming or pedal boat. In summer, the lake welcomes up to 40,000 tourists per day along its shores — but in spring and September, you'll find rare tranquility here, with the Verdon's turquoise waters at their most spectacular.

activities.text: Take the Toine Nature Trail — an easy 14-station loop through the Provençal garrigue that follows the lakeshore for part of the route. Interpretive panels guide you through the local flora: kermes oaks, rock roses, rosemary, and lavender that perfume the air from spring onwards. Allow 1.5 to 2 hours depending on your pace.

Local providers offer pedal boats, paddleboards, and electric boats from the village's water sports center. Plan to book ahead in July-August — slots fill up fast. Since motorized boats with combustion engines are banned across the entire lake, the atmosphere remains remarkably peaceful.
Thursday mornings, the Provençal market sets up in the main square. Honey, cheeses, marinated olives, lavender from the nearby Valensole plateau — a delicious stop before hitting the road.

days_plan.itin_title: Lake Sainte-Croix and Verdon Circuit

visits.text: The Franciscan convent of Les Thons is a treasure that nobody knows about — and that's exactly what makes it so precious. Founded in 1452 by Guillaume de Saint-Loup and his wife Jeanne de Choiseul with the support of Duke Jean II of Lorraine, it is today the best-preserved Franciscan complex in all of northeastern France. The central part houses a country cuisine restaurant and a museum where you can see a reconstructed friar's cell, a prison arranged by order of King Louis XV, the cloister, the Saint-François fountain and the refectories. The convent church (consecrated in 1483), a masterpiece of late Gothic, is undergoing restoration thanks in part to the Loto du Patrimoine — work is progressing, check accessibility before arriving.

activities.text: The convent restaurant serves country cuisine in a large Louis XIII fireplace — if you arrive at lunchtime, it's a stop you won't regret. After the meal, stroll between Petit-Thon and Grand-Thon, the two hamlets that make up the commune. This bocage of the southwestern Vosges, silent and intact, has a rare charm.

days_plan.itin_title: Vosges Abbey Trail: Monastic Heritage Unveiled

visits.text: Les Vans layers its stone houses at the foot of the first Cévennes foothills. The Market Square vibrates with Provençal energy every Saturday morning. The authentic village retains its mountain character with cool lanes and singing fountains.

activities.text: The colorful market brings Les Vans to life every Saturday morning year-round. Local producers, Cévennes market gardeners, and artisans offer chestnuts, goat cheeses, lavender honey, and mountain specialties.

days_plan.itin_title: Ardèche Road Trip: Wild Gorges and Heritage Villages

places.name: Leucate

visits.text: The limestone cliffs of Cap Leucate plunge 40 to 51 meters straight into the Mediterranean — the town's name reportedly comes from the Greek leukos (white) for the rock's whiteness. The Sentier du Guetteur (4.3 km round trip) runs along the plateau edge between the lighthouse, semaphore and cliff to La Franqui. On clear days you'll glimpse the Canigou at 2,784 meters and the entire Pyrenees range. Morning light is best for photographs.

The Plage des Coussoules at La Franqui stretches 8 km, classified Natura 2000, wild and battered by the tramontane — a wind that blows here about 300 days a year. It's one reason Leucate has become Europe's kitesurfing and windsurfing mecca. Every spring, the Mondial du Vent has gathered the international elite here since 1996. Outside competitions, it's one of the most unspoiled and spacious beaches on the Aude coast.

The Leucate lagoon spans 5,400 hectares between sea and maritime Corbières. Its 280 bird species (89 protected) make it a major ornithological site — pink flamingos, egrets and terns appear seasonally. On the Grau, colorful oyster farmers' huts offer their oysters waterside.

The village of Leucate just above its cliff has all the hallmarks of an ideal Mediterranean village: shaded square, colorful lanes, castle ruins. Climb to the ruins for sunset over the lagoon — the golden light on the water and salt marshes is one of the coast's finest spectacles.

activities.text: Leucate is the best kitesurfing and windsurfing spot on the French Mediterranean coast. Local operators offer beginner courses on the lagoon (flat water, ideal for learning) and more committed sessions on the sea side. Book ahead in high season.

days_plan.itin_title: Aude Road Trip: Carcassonne, Cathar Country & the Mediterranean

map_keywords: Cap Leucate falaise sentier du Guetteur
Plage des Coussoules La Franqui Leucate

visits.text: Levens, a fortified medieval village perched at 600 meters, dominates the confluence of the Var and Vésubie valleys. Its partially preserved ramparts testify to its defensive past.
Marshal Masséna's birthplace stands in the old village—a local son who became one of Napoleon's greatest generals. François Malausséna, Nice's first French mayor, was also born here.
The Saint-Antonin parish church houses a finely carved 17th-century baroque pulpit and gilded altarpieces typical of Niçoise baroque style.
Houses built from local white stone, characteristic of the village, rise in tiers along steep alleyways bordered by covered passages.
Place de la République, the village heart, preserves its ancient fountain and century-old plane trees offering welcome shade in summer.
The covered 19th-century washhouse, fed by a spring, bears witness to daily life of bygone days.
From the village heights, the panorama sweeps across Cap d'Antibes, the Baie des Anges, and on clear days, Corsica.

activities.text: Stroll through the old village's cobbled lanes following the marked heritage trail that passes the main historic monuments.
Le Grand Pré, a 13-hectare leisure park, features a swimming pool, playgrounds, and relaxation areas surrounded by nature.
The Férion trail climbs to this 1,412-meter summit, an exceptional vantage point over the coastline and Pre-Alps.
The Colmiane via ferrata, accessible from Levens, offers a secured aerial route with views plunging into the Vésubie valley.
The Sunday morning Provençal market animates the square with local products, olives, cheeses, and mountain honey.
Try ravioli à la daube, a local specialty served in the village's traditional restaurants.

days_plan.itin_title: Road Trip Through the Nice & Grasse Backcountry

places.name: Lille

visits.text: Lose yourself in the cobbled streets of Old Lille. The brick and white stone façades blend Flemish and French styles, with 17th-century crow-stepped gables lining rue de la Monnaie and rue Esquermoise.

The Grand'Place (Place du Général-de-Gaulle) opens onto the Column of the Goddess, erected to commemorate the Austrian siege of 1792. All around, the façades of La Voix du Nord and the New Exchange create a striking Flemish backdrop.

Push through one of the four wooden portals of the Old Exchange, crowned with the lions of Flanders. This Renaissance Flemish jewel (1652-1653) aligns 24 identical houses around a courtyard where booksellers and chess players gather beneath the arcades.

The Town Hall belfry soars 104 meters high—the tallest in the world. This UNESCO-listed tower offers sweeping views of the entire metropolis from its summit. At its base, two sculpted giants recount the legend of Lydéric and Phinaert, mythical founders of the city.

The Palais des Beaux-Arts on Place de la République is France's second-largest museum by number of works after the Louvre. Its galleries house canvases by Rubens, Van Dyck, Goya and Delacroix, 40 sheets by Raphael, and fifteen relief maps of Vauban's fortified towns.

Notre-Dame de la Treille Cathedral surprises with its contemporary translucent marble façade, completed in 1999, which contrasts with its 19th-century neo-Gothic nave. The crypt houses a collection of contemporary sacred art.

The Hospice Comtesse, founded in 1237 by Countess Jeanne de Flandre, houses Lille's museum of art and history. The patients' ward, covered with a vaulted timber ceiling, recalls the Hôtel-Dieu of Beaune. In the kitchen, walls are tiled with Lille faience inspired by Delft.

The Porte de Paris, a triumphal arch erected between 1685 and 1692, celebrates Louis XIV's conquest of Lille. Its allegorical sculptures represent Victory, Hercules and Mars. It marks the historic entrance to the city.

The Palais Rihour, last remnant of the Dukes of Burgundy's residence in Lille (15th century), preserves a Gothic guard room and a chapel with ribbed vaults. The monumental white stone staircase is worth the visit alone.
Head to rue de la Monnaie, one of Lille's oldest streets. Its 17th-century Flemish gabled houses, in brick and limestone, are among the best-preserved in the city.

Charles de Gaulle's birthplace on rue Princesse recreates the bourgeois Lille interior of the late 19th century. It was here, in his maternal grandparents' home, that the future president was born in 1890.

activities.text: Stroll the shaded paths of Citadel Park, designed by Vauban in 1667. This pentagonal fortress, nicknamed the "Queen of Citadels," is surrounded by moats and woods where locals come to jog and picnic.

Sample Flemish specialties in an Old Lille estaminet: Welsh rarebit (cheddar melted with beer on toast), carbonnade flamande, and potjevleesch (terrine of meats in aspic). Local beer accompanies each dish.
Browse along rue de la Monnaie and rue Esquermoise to admire the finest shop windows in Old Lille. Boutiques occupy Flemish townhouses with sculpted façades.
The Wazemmes market comes alive every Sunday morning. Spices, fresh vegetables, colorful fabrics and roasted poultry spread across Place de la Nouvelle Aventure in a warm, cosmopolitan atmosphere.
Ready for shopping? Euralille, designed by Rem Koolhaas, is one of northern France's largest shopping centers. Its futuristic architecture contrasts sharply with the Flemish heritage of downtown.
In September, the Grande Braderie transforms Lille into a gigantic flea market for an entire weekend. Mussels and fries flow freely and piles of empty shells accumulate outside the estaminets.

days_plan.itin_title: Northern France Circuit: Lille, Belfries and Flemish Heritage
Northern France Circuit: Lille, Belfries and Flemish Heritage

map_keywords: Cathédrale Notre-Dame de la Treille Lille
Maison natale Charles de Gaulle Lille

visits.text: The Musée national Adrien Dubouché showcases 5,000 ceramic pieces from a reserve collection of 18,000. The circular route spans antiquity to contemporary porcelain, centered around the world's richest Limoges collection.

Step through the doors of Gare des Bénédictins, a classified Historic Monument. Built between 1924 and 1929, it blends Art Nouveau, Art Deco, and Neoclassical styles. Its campanile rises 61 meters high, while the copper dome crowns a hall bathed in light from Francis Chigot's stained glass depicting Limousin chestnut trees and foliage.

The Four des Casseaux, a classified Historic Monument, is a former porcelain kiln built in 1900. It bears witness to the titanic work of porcelain workers during 19th-century firings and hosts temporary exhibitions of contemporary ceramics.

Walk down to Cathédrale Saint-Étienne, the only monument in Limousin built in pure Gothic style. Construction stretched from 1273 to 1888. Admire the Flamboyant Gothic Saint-Jean portal, the Renaissance rood screen commissioned in 1533, and three sculpted tombs in the ambulatory.

The Jardins de l'Évêché cascade in terraces below the cathedral, offering sweeping views over the Vienne River. The botanical garden assembles medicinal plants and toxic species. The Musée des Beaux-Arts, housed in the former 18th-century episcopal palace, holds a remarkable collection of medieval enamels.
Cross the Pont Saint-Étienne, built around 1210 over the Vienne. Its medieval arches link the Cité quarter to the left bank and offer one of the finest views of the cathedral.

Visit a porcelain manufacture for an immersive guided tour. From raw material to finished product, you'll follow every manufacturing stage: modeling, slip casting, glazing, and high-fire kiln firing. Artisans work before your eyes.

The Église Saint-Michel-des-Lions, built between the 14th and 16th centuries, takes its name from two granite lions flanking its portal. It preserves the relics of Saint Martial and Saint Valérie, displayed during the septennial Ostensions.
The Église Saint-Pierre-du-Queyroix features a typically Limousin bell tower and beautiful stained glass. Its late Gothic atmosphere contrasts with the dark granite facade.

Discover the Cour du Temple, an elegant 17th-century Renaissance courtyard tucked between Rue du Temple and Rue du Consulat. Its arcades and terraces form an unexpected jewel in the heart of downtown.
The Pavillon du Verdurier, an octagonal Art Deco building designed by the railway station's architect, is adorned with porcelain and enamel tiles. It regularly hosts exhibitions.

activities.text: Wander through the Quartier de la Boucherie, Limoges' most picturesque neighborhood: half-timbered houses, narrow lanes, and the 15th-century Chapelle Saint-Aurélien, owned by the butchers' guild. The Maison traditionnelle de la Boucherie traces the techniques and tools of this corporation active since the Middle Ages.

Beneath Place de la République, the Crypte Saint-Martial preserves vestiges of the Benedictine abbey founded in the 9th century. This medieval scriptorium spread the art of illumination and polyphonic music — the word 'troubadour' was born here, from the 'tropes' composed by monks.
Head up to Place de la Motte in late afternoon to enjoy the terraces and illuminated half-timbered facades. The atmosphere buzzes with life in the evenings.
The Halles centrales de Limoges are a food lover's rendezvous. Fine-grained Limousin beef, farmhouse cheeses, chestnuts, and clafoutis: taste the spirit of Limousin among the market stalls.
Try your hand at modeling or painting on porcelain in a workshop. Beginners welcome — you'll leave with your own creation.
Stroll along the Vienne riverbanks in late afternoon for a peaceful walk between medieval bridges. Limoges boasts 52 m² of green space per inhabitant: nature is never far away.

days_plan.itin_title: Haute-Vienne Circuit: Limoges, Porcelain & Historic Villages
Haute-Vienne Circuit: Limoges, Porcelain & Historic Villages
Haute-Vienne Road Trip: Medieval Castles & the Périgord-Limousin

visits.text: Place de la République in Limoux hosts the world's longest carnival: from January to March, every Saturday and Sunday, the costumed bands of "Fécos" parade three times daily (11am, 4:30pm, 10pm) under the medieval arcades. Each band represents a neighborhood of the town. The ritual concludes each year with the "Nuit de la Blanquette" and the burning of His Majesty Carnival on Place de la République. If you're not there in season, stroll under the arcades and seek out the medieval Pont Vieux over the Aude.

Blanquette de Limoux is the world's oldest sparkling wine: in 1531, a monk at the Benedictine abbey of Saint-Hilaire (7 km from Limoux) discovered by chance that wine bottled and corked with cork formed bubbles — a century before champagne was invented. The Abbey of Saint-Hilaire, whose origins date to the 9th century, also preserves a marble sarcophagus-reliquary attributed to the mysterious Master of Cabestany — one of the great anonymous Romanesque sculptors of the 12th century, whose works dot Languedoc and Catalonia. Cellars in Limoux and estates throughout the area offer visits and tastings.

Make the 3 km detour to the sanctuary of Notre-Dame de Marceille. This 14th-century Gothic church houses a Black Virgin venerated since the Middle Ages and a spring pilgrims consider miraculous. From the esplanade, the view of the Pyrenees in clear weather ranks among the region's finest.

activities.text: At a cellar in the Limoux area, a guided tour takes you down into underground galleries to see manual riddling, disgorgement and corking — the traditional method inherited from the monks of Saint-Hilaire. The final tasting includes Blanquette méthode ancestrale (100% Mauzac, under 7° alcohol) and Crémant de Limoux. Allow 1 hour.

If you're in Limoux between January and March, the carnival is free and open to all. Three daily outings on Saturdays and Sundays — the evening session (10pm) is the liveliest and most authentic. The masked "Goudils" dance from café to café offering wine to spectators.

days_plan.itin_title: Aude Road Trip: Carcassonne, Cathar Country & the Mediterranean

visits.text: Porte Haute, flanked by two towers, guards the medieval town entrance. It ranks among the finest examples of fortified gates in Meurthe-et-Moselle.
The 12th-century Collégiale Saint-Pierre features a Romanesque tower topped with a modern bell tower. The organ, restored in 1999, dates from 1847.
Château Corbin was built on the ramparts between two towers. Its park offers views over the Moselle loop and now houses a media library.
Place de la Fontaine reveals 16th-century arcades. The Renaissance facades testify to the architectural wealth of this summer residence of the Bishops of Toul.
The 18th-century presbytery houses the tourist office. From its windows, views extend over the Moselle loop.

activities.text: Head to the viewpoints over the Moselle loop from Porte Haute or Château Corbin park. The Moselle draws a spectacular meander around the promontory.
Explore the medieval town's cobbled lanes. Corner statuettes served as landmarks for visitors.

days_plan.itin_title: Meurthe-et-Moselle Road Trip: Art Nouveau Nancy & Fortified Cities

places.name: Livry Gargan

visits.text: The Forest Park of the Poudrerie spans 137 hectares, the last witness to the immense Bondy forest. Created by decree of Napoleon III in 1865, the national gunpowder factory produced civilian and military powders for a century. Some thirty brick and stone buildings survive among the century-old oaks.

The Technical Museum of Gunpowder and Armament traces the history of this pioneering factory. The first steam-powered gunpowder plant, it employed up to 3,300 workers during World War I, producing 28 tons of powder daily.
The entrance pavilions of the Cour d'Honneur, built in 1884, mark the threshold of the estate. Their brick architecture with stone corners is characteristic of 19th-century state factories.

The Ourcq canal crosses the park and divides it into two worlds: the forest side with the Bois des Sablons and Bois de la Tussion, and the leisure side with ponds, the educational farm, and playgrounds.

The forest ponds of the Bois de la Tussion harbor palmate newts, crested newts, and spotted salamanders. These protected amphibians find refuge in this network of old ponds, habitat extremely rare in urban environments.

activities.text: Take the park's marked trails for a hike among giant sequoias, sessile oaks, and century-old chestnuts. Clearings left by demolished buildings welcome rare butterflies and insects.
In fine weather, a water sports center offers canoe introductions on the Ourcq canal in a wooded, quiet setting. Paddle in the shade of century-old trees.
Join free events organized year-round by the park's eco-rangers: birdwatching, nocturnal outings in search of amphibians, nature workshops for families.

days_plan.itin_title: Seine-Saint-Denis Circuit: Royal Basilica and Urban Culture

map_keywords: Parc forestier de la Poudrerie Sevran Livry-Gargan

visits.text: The royal citadel of Loches, one of France's finest medieval fortresses. Built on a rocky spur overlooking the Indre valley, it unfurls over a mile of ramparts protecting both keep and royal lodgings. Five centuries of construction, from the 11th to 16th century, can be read in its stones.

The keep of Loches, 120 feet high, built by Foulques Nerra starting in 1013. A masterpiece of Romanesque military architecture, it's one of the best-preserved in Europe. Transformed into a state prison by Louis XI, it still bears graffiti and frescoes painted by prisoners—including Ludovico Sforza, Duke of Milan.

The royal lodgings welcomed Joan of Arc on May 23, 1429, after the victory at Orléans, when she convinced Charles VII to proceed to Reims for his coronation. The king installed his mistress Agnès Sorel here in the 1440s. Anne of Brittany's oratory, in Flamboyant Gothic style, is the jewel of the wing added by Charles VIII and Louis XII.

The collegiate church of Saint-Ours, between keep and lodgings, presents a profile unique in France: its nave is topped by two octagonal pyramids called "dubes." The polychrome portal is one of the finest Romanesque testimonies in the Loire Valley. Inside, the alabaster tomb of Agnès Sorel watches over the "lady of beauty" for eternity.

Descend into Loches' lower town through winding medieval lanes. Half-timbered houses, Renaissance mansions, and hanging gardens compose a historic town scenery. The Cordeliers Gate and Saint-Antoine Tower punctuate the promenade.

activities.text: Interactive scenographic tour of the royal citadel. Tactile model, projections, video animations and sound markers immerse you in the monument's great moments. At the keep, the Histopad offers an augmented reality visit.

Stroll in the royal citadel's medieval gardens. At the base of the thousand-year-old keep, a "hortus deliciarum" inspired by medieval illuminations offers a peaceful haven with aromatic, medicinal and heirloom plants.

Walk along the Indre from Loches. The verdant, peaceful valley offers waterside trails with views of the perched citadel's silhouette. The abbey church of Beaulieu-lès-Loches, visible from the lodgings terrace, merits a detour.

days_plan.itin_title: Indre-et-Loire Road Trip: Loire Valley Châteaux, Secret Valleys & Touraine Vineyards

visits.text: The Broken Great Menhir lies in four pieces on the ground. At 21 meters long and 280 tons, it was the largest monolith ever erected in Europe. Transported 10 km from a gneiss quarry, it was raised around 4500 BC.

The Table des Marchands, a 30-meter-long cairn, houses a dolmen whose capstone bears a spectacular carving: a crook or hafted axe symbol. This fragment fits with another found at Gavrinis, 4 km away by sea.

The Er Grah tumulus, 140 meters long, is one of the largest funeral monuments of European Neolithic. Built in several phases between 4600 and 3900 BC, it contained chest burials accompanied by polished axes.

The Dolmen des Pierres Plates stretches 24 meters along the seaside. Its L-shaped angled corridor is covered with Neolithic carvings: axes, crooks, shield-shaped idols. One wall bears a stylized face nicknamed the 'Mother Goddess.'
The port of Locmariaquer faces the entrance to the Gulf of Morbihan. The tidal current reaches 9 knots - one of the strongest in Europe. Oyster beds grid the foreshore as far as the eye can see.
The Notre-Dame-de-Kerdro church, from the 11th century, preserves Romanesque elements. On the square, a 16th-century cemetery cross bears sculptures of the Virgin and Christ.

activities.text: Visit the megalithic complex (Great Menhir, Table des Marchands, Er Grah) in one go. The interpretation center at the site entrance explains megalith transport and erection techniques with models and films.
Follow the coastal path (GR34) to Pointe de Kerpenhir, facing Port-Navalo. The view embraces the entrance to the Gulf of Morbihan, Gavrinis island, and the Rhuys peninsula.

Embark on the crossing to Gavrinis island (15 min). The Neolithic cairn houses the most beautiful known megalithic carvings: 23 of 29 slabs are entirely sculpted with spiral motifs, chevrons, and arches.

visits.text: Vauban's fortifications form a star on the plateau since 1679. Longwy ranks among the nine new towns built ex nihilo by King Louis XIV's engineer.
Porte de France resembles a triumphal arch. Vauban gave this monumental entrance the prestige worthy of the Sun King.
The Siege Well and cross-shaped casemate complete the stronghold's equipment. The military bakery and powder magazine served the garrison's needs.
The Musée des Émaux et Faïences occupies the former military bakery. Faience produced since 1798 and cloisonné enamels appearing in the 1870s illustrate this unique craft.
The enamels with their distinctive blue acquired worldwide renown. The collections present tableware, vases, and sculptures from the 19th and 20th centuries.

activities.text: Walk the UNESCO ramparts to discover Église Saint-Dagobert and the town hall. The promenade offers views over bastions and curtain walls.
Manufacturers perpetuate the craft of cloisonné enamels. Some workshops open by reservation to discover the production stages.

days_plan.itin_title: Meurthe-et-Moselle Road Trip: Art Nouveau Nancy & Fortified Cities

visits.text: The Keroman submarine base covers 64 acres. Three reinforced concrete blocks (K1, K2, K3) were built between 1941 and 1943. It could shelter up to 30 submarines.
The Flore-S645 submarine is moored at the quay on Lorient La Base. Step inside the passageways, engine room, and command post. The museum space recounts the Battle of the Atlantic.
La Cité de la Voile Éric Tabarly celebrates offshore racing. Navigation simulator, 4D cinema, interactive exhibits. Éric Tabarly's Pen Duick yachts are on display. Dynamic and engaging visit.
Stroll along the offshore racing pontoons. The 105-foot Ultim trimarans and IMOCA 60s from the Vendée Globe are moored here. Skippers like Franck Cammas and Armel Le Cléac'h prepare their races.

The Museum of the French East India Company in Port-Louis displays Oriental porcelain, spices, and objects brought back by ships in the 17th and 18th centuries. The collection testifies to Lorient's maritime influence.

activities.text: Take a guided tour of the K3 block of the submarine base. The pens, slipway, and military installations are revealed with a guide.
Settle onto a terrace at La Base. Views of racing boats, bunkers, and maritime activity. Crêpes, seafood, and cocktails overlooking the pontoons.
The Keroman fishing port is one of France's largest. Visit the fish auction in the morning to see the journey from sea to plate.

days_plan.itin_title: Morbihan Road Trip: Gulf, Carnac & Breton Coast

visits.text: The Square Tower dominates Loudun from its 156 steps. Built between 1162 and 1185 under Henry II Plantagenet, it's the sole remnant of the fortress dismantled by Louis XIII to suppress the Protestants. The panorama sweeps across the city and the crossroads of Anjou, Touraine, and Poitou.

Sainte-Croix Collegiate Church, an 11th-century Romanesque edifice, was the theater of exorcism sessions during the trial of Urbain Grandier in 1634. Accused of witchcraft by the Ursuline nuns, the priest was tortured and burned at the stake. The interior preserves painted decorations from the 12th and 14th centuries, restored in 1995.
The Porte du Martray, sole survivor of the four gates in the fortified enclosure designed by Philip Augustus in the 14th century, opens onto one of Loudun's oldest quarters.

The Renaudot Museum occupies the birthplace of Théophraste Renaudot, physician to Louis XIII and founder of La Gazette de France in 1631. You'll discover a reconstructed print shop, a period herbalist's, and the library with original issues of the Gazette.
The Charbonneau-Lassay Museum houses eclectic collections bequeathed by the local scholar: archaeological objects, antique books, swords, and artifacts from the Loudun area.

activities.text: At the foot of the Square Tower, the medieval-inspired garden features medicinal plants in woven chestnut beds, including those in Renaudot's "Polychreston" remedy from 1619.
Walk the Philippe-Auguste and Foulques Nerra promenades that follow the old ramparts. Views of slate rooftops and bell towers compose a well-preserved tableau.

Join the guided tour "The Affair of the Possessed" offered by the tourist office. Various stops in Loudun illuminate this famous 17th-century witchcraft case, orchestrated by Richelieu against the priest Urbain Grandier.

days_plan.itin_title: Vienne Road Trip: Futuroscope and Romanesque Heritage

days_plan.itin_title: Saône Valley, France: Medieval Abbeys, Wine and Comtois Heritage

visits.text: The Grotte de Massabielle, on the banks of the Gave de Pau, is the site where Bernadette Soubirous reported eighteen apparitions of the Virgin Mary in 1858. This rock cavity (3.80 m high, 9.50 m deep) houses a Carrara marble statue sculpted by Fabisch, placed in the niche of the apparitions. The spring discovered by Bernadette still flows, captured and channeled to fountains accessible to visitors. The site is protected as a Historic Monument.

The Basilica of the Immaculate Conception, built from 1866 to 1871 atop the Massabielle rock, is in neo-Gothic style. Its single nave of five bays is adorned with ex-votos, and the arches bear the words that the Virgin addressed to Bernadette. The bell tower rises to 70 meters.

The Basilica of Our Lady of the Rosary, constructed between 1883 and 1889 below the previous one, adopts a Greek cross plan and Romano-Byzantine style. Its interior walls are covered with mosaics over more than 2,000 m² depicting the mysteries of the Rosary. It can accommodate 1,500 worshippers.

The Underground Basilica of Saint Pius X, inaugurated in 1958 for the centenary of the apparitions, is one of the world's largest churches with a capacity of 25,000 people. Its gemmail stained-glass windows diffuse a particular light in this elliptical concrete space buried beneath the esplanade.

The Château Fort, erected in the 11th century on a rocky outcrop, dominates the town and surrounding valleys. Classified as a Historic Monument, it houses the Pyrenean Museum (labeled Museum of France) dedicated to Pyrenean cultures, traditions, and crafts from the 18th to the 20th century. A botanical garden enhances the visit.

The Pic du Jer, accessible by a funicular inaugurated in 1900, rises to 1,000 meters altitude. At the summit, a 360° panorama embraces the Pyrenees chain, the Tarbes plain, and the town of Lourdes below. Developed caves can be visited on site.

activities.text: Cross the sanctuary esplanade to the grotto, then climb back toward the superimposed basilicas using the ramps that follow the Gave de Pau.
Climb to the Château Fort by the steep paths and enjoy the plunging view over the sanctuaries and rooftops of Lourdes before touring the Pyrenean Museum collections.
Take the Pic du Jer funicular for a fifteen-minute ascent, then explore the caves at the summit before descending on foot via the wooded trail.
In the evening, from Easter to late October, attend the torchlight procession that takes place every evening within the sanctuary grounds - a moment that touches believers and visitors alike.

Devote an excursion to the Bétharram Caves, located between Pau and Lourdes. The approximately 1h20 visit combines walking through five levels of underground galleries, a descent of 250 steps, a boat crossing on the underground river, and a return by small train over 700 meters.

days_plan.itin_title: Hautes-Pyrénées Circuit: From Tarbes to Cirques and Valleys

visits.text: Lourmarin is the southern gateway to the Luberon—and one of the region's most vibrant villages. Its cobbled lanes, plane-tree-shaded squares, and numerous art galleries make it both a heritage destination and a living community. This is where Albert Camus chose to settle in 1958 with his Nobel Prize money, purchasing an old silkworm farm that he furnished entirely as a gift to his family. He died on January 4, 1960, in a car accident while leaving the village; Lourmarin's footballers carried his coffin to the cemetery. You can reach it on foot in five minutes from the center: the grave, simple and covered with laurels, is one of the most visited in Provence.

The Château de Lourmarin is often described as Provence's first Renaissance castle. Its construction began in the 15th century under the d'Agoult family and extended until 1560, featuring an elegant Italianate loggia. The Renaissance section—the only part open to the public—houses furnished apartments and stairways of surprising geometry. Each summer it also serves as the venue for the Summer Music Festival, whose starlit evening concerts are worth the trip in themselves.

The village contains two symbolic religious buildings standing side by side: the Church of Saint-André and the Protestant Temple, a direct reflection of the Wars of Religion that marked the region. In 1545, a tragic episode unfolded here: the massacre of the Vaudois of the Luberon, during which the village was almost entirely destroyed before being repopulated by families from Piedmont and Briançon.

activities.text: Lourmarin's market takes place Friday mornings—one of the region's most renowned. Browse among stalls of cheeses, olives, lavender, and local products. Arrive before 9 AM to have space and avoid the high-season crowds.

Walk toward the orchards and dry-stone walls south of the village (30 to 45 min). The trail climbing toward the Lourmarin valley offers beautiful views of the village and château, particularly in the golden late-afternoon light.

days_plan.itin_title: The 15 Most Beautiful Villages of the Luberon

visits.text: Sant'Antonino is one of France's most beautiful villages—and one of Corsica's oldest, perched at 500 meters altitude on a granite outcrop since the 9th century. The lanes are so narrow you can barely pass. The view from the summit over the Balagne beaches and sea is breathtaking. Count on 45 minutes to walk around—cars stay below in the parking lot.

Occi is a ghost village: abandoned in the 1950s by its inhabitants who left for the coast, it sleeps on its spur facing the sea with its roofless houses overgrown by maquis. The trail from Lumio takes 45 minutes each way. It's a silent and slightly melancholy place that neatly sums up 20th-century Corsican inland depopulation.

Speloncato, at 600 meters altitude in the heart of Balagne, is a belvedere village overlooking the plain on one side and the ridges on the other. Cardinal Pietro Casini hailed from here. Twice a year, at sunset on the spring and autumn equinoxes, the sun's rays thread precisely through a church window portal—a spectacle announced in advance and much anticipated by locals.

activities.text: The Balagne village loop by car is a day in itself: Lumio, Lavatoggio, Zilia, Montemaggiore, Cateri follow one another on the hillsides. Stop in Zilia for its olives and reputed spring water, in Montemaggiore for the church perched above the vineyards. Local producers sell olive oil, honey, and figatellu directly.

days_plan.itin_title: Complete Tour of Corsica

visits.text: Germain Boffrand built Lunéville château between 1703 and 1723 for Duke Leopold I. Mansart's student created this 'Lorraine Versailles' after Louis XIV occupied Nancy.
Stanislas Leszczynski settled in Lunéville in 1737. He embellished the gardens with fabriques—exotic pavilions designed by Emmanuel Héré.
The ducal chapel, restored after the 2003 fire, now welcomes visitors. The Livery Room and Guards Room pay tribute to the reconstruction workers.
The château museum displays 18th-century paintings and decorative arts. Three rooms recount the château's history from the year 1000.
Église Saint-Jacques was built between 1730 and 1747 under Francis III and Stanislas. Its organ, designed by Emmanuel Héré, is entirely concealed by magnificent decoration.

activities.text: Parc des Bosquets spreads 21 hectares of French-style gardens laid out by Yves des Hours. Visitors roam freely between classical perspectives and wild groves.
The Lunéville Embroidery Conservatory perpetuates this recognized and transmitted Lorraine craft. Introductory workshops are offered regularly.

days_plan.itin_title: Meurthe-et-Moselle Road Trip: Art Nouveau Nancy & Fortified Cities
