days_plan.itin_title: Saône Valley, France: Medieval Abbeys, Wine and Comtois Heritage

visits.text: Lurs perches at 612 meters on a rocky spur above the Durance. Consecrated as Episcopal Land by Charlemagne in 812, the town was the summer residence of the bishops of Sisteron until the Revolution — this past is visible everywhere: castle of the prince-bishops, Clock Tower (whose bell dates from 1599, the oldest in the department), seminary built between 1738 and 1742. Enter the village via the Path of Scripts, inaugurated in 2010: 42 steles trace the evolution of Latin writing and typography, an idea born right here, as Jean Giono and typographer Maximilien Vox founded the International Encounters of Lure in 1952 — they still take place every last week of August.

Don't leave without taking the Bishops' Promenade: from the castle, an avenue lined with 15 oratories dedicated to the Virgin (built in 1864) leads to the Chapel of Notre-Dame de Vie (1552, with columned entrance). From the terrace, the view is 360° over the Durance valley, Valensole plateau, Montagne de Lure, and the Luberon — in clear weather, the Alps chain on the horizon. It's one of the finest views in Haute-Provence.

The Lurs olive grove is one of Provence's five most beautiful — often multi-century-old olive trees cascade down toward the Durance. The local Aglandau variety produces PDO oil. Descend for a walk among the trees via the calade path.

activities.text: The Roman Bridge loop (3h15, 270 m elevation gain) crosses the village, the Bishops' Promenade, a forest, and reaches a bridge built between 121 and 145 CE on the Via Domitia — the Roman road connecting Italy to Spain passed at the foot of Lurs. On a corner block in large stonework, a phallus deeply carved into the stone has intrigued archaeologists for a long time. The climb returns via a trail bordered by dry-stone walls and olive groves.

days_plan.itin_title: Alpes-De-Haute-Provence Circuit: Lavender and Perched Villages
Forcalquier & Verdon Gorges Road Trip

visits.text: Lusigny-sur-Barse sits at the gates of Orient Forest Regional Natural Park. This protected area covers 70,000 hectares around three major lakes: Orient, Temple and Amance. The park headquarters houses an information center: interactive exhibitions on forest ecosystems, wetlands, migratory birds. Maps and brochures for planning your nature explorations. Free admission.

Lusigny beaches hold Blue Flag certification: fine sand, clear water, supervised swimming. Water sports base offers sailing lessons, catamaran rentals, windsurfing, stand-up paddle. Shallow calm waters perfect for families with young children. Picnic areas, children's playgrounds, snack bars. Lively atmosphere in summer, peaceful in low season.

activities.text: Discover the Voie Verte trail: 42 km of paved path around the Orient Forest lakes reserved for cyclists and pedestrians. Flat easy route suitable for all. Rent bikes in Lusigny or Mesnil-Saint-Père. Stops at observation points, picnic areas, beaches. Allow a full day for the complete loop with breaks. Electric bikes available for less athletic riders.

Bird observation in Orient Forest: 265 species recorded including cranes, herons, osprey, harriers. Spring and fall migrations bring thousands of birds. The park offers guided walks with ornithologists. Bring binoculars. Observation hides placed along shores. Respectful quiet observation of nature. Magical moments guaranteed for bird lovers.

days_plan.itin_title: Saône Valley, France: Medieval Abbeys, Wine and Comtois Heritage

visits.text: The Church of Saint-André, called "of the Templars," is actually the work of the Hospitallers of Saint John of Jerusalem, who fortified it in the 14th century to protect pilgrims on the Way of Saint James from miquelets, Aragonese bandits. Its battlements, arrow slits, and defense towers give it a fortress-like appearance. Classified as a Historic Monument in 1840 - one of the very first Pyrenean monuments to receive this distinction - it contains a painted 16th-century vault depicting Christ surrounded by the four evangelists in tetramorphic form, and an 18th-century Baroque high altar.

The Pont Napoléon spans the Gave de Gavarnie at 63 meters height with an arch of 68 meters span. Constructed between 1859 and 1861 under Napoleon III, it was erected in gratitude to the valley inhabitants after Empress Eugénie stayed at the Saint-Sauveur thermal baths. The site has become a bungee jumping spot.

The Solférino Chapel, built in 1859 at Napoleon III's request to commemorate the victory at the Battle of Solferino in Italy, stands on a promontory overlooking the valley. Its Byzantine-inspired tower and surrounding benches make it a peaceful viewpoint over Luz and the surrounding mountains.

The Gorges de Luz carve a narrow passage through rock walls downstream from the village. The road that crosses them offers a striking view of the erosive power of the gave, with geological strata visible on the cliff faces.
The Château Sainte-Marie, in ruins on the heights at 630 meters altitude, preserves two medieval defense towers. The remains offer a viewpoint over the confluence of the Barèges and Gavarnie valleys.

Luz-Saint-Sauveur itself comprises two united towns: Luz, a commercial center animated by its Monday morning market at the foot of the fortified church, and Saint-Sauveur, a thermal spa where Second Empire elites stayed. Victor Hugo described the light of this valley by comparing its name - Luz - to the word "light."

activities.text: Visit the fortified church, paying attention to the sculpted Romanesque tympanum on the north facade, the polychrome painted vault, and the sacred art treasury displayed in the Notre-Dame-de-la-Pitié chapel.

Cross the Pont Napoléon on foot, then descend the small trail on the right that leads to the gave's edge, at the foot of the bridge - a cool spot for a break, with a view looking up at the monumental arch.
Climb to the Solférino Chapel for a peaceful panorama over the valley. On market days (Monday morning), first stock up on local products below before climbing to picnic.
Explore the town of Saint-Sauveur, its thermal facades and alleys, climbing toward the Château Sainte-Marie ruins for an overview of the Pays Toy.

days_plan.itin_title: The Pyrenees Spa Trail: Thermal Baths from Ax-les-Thermes to Bagnères-de-Luchon
Hautes-Pyrénées Circuit: From Tarbes to Cirques and Valleys

visits.text: Saint-Jean-Baptiste Cathedral, built between 1175 and 1480, blends Romanesque and Gothic styles across 260 feet of stone. Its 14th-century astronomical clock—one of Europe's oldest—calculates the positions of the sun, moon, and stars above Lyon. Henry IV and Marie de Medici were married here in 1600.

Old Lyon's traboules form a network of over 300 covered passages connecting streets through interior courtyards. The earliest date to the 4th century, others to the Renaissance. During World War II, the Resistance used them to escape the occupiers. A gilded lion's head marks their entrances.

Rue du Boeuf and Rue Saint-Jean concentrate the finest Renaissance townhouses in the quarter. Italian merchants, drawn by the four great trade fairs granted by Charles VII, built mansions with interior courtyards and Italian-style galleries. The Gadagne Museum, in the Hôtel de Pierrevive, traces Lyon's history and houses the world puppetry museum.

Place du Change, the 15th-century bankers' square, preserves the Exchange Lodge built by Soufflot in 1748. The half-timbered houses surrounding it recall when Lyon was Europe's leading financial center, before Paris seized that title.

Saint-Georges quarter, south of Old Lyon, was the craftsmen's domain. More working-class than Saint-Jean, it retains a village atmosphere with narrow lanes and its 19th-century church on the Saône's banks. Stunning views of the footbridge and Fourvière hill.

Notre-Dame de Fourvière Basilica, completed in 1884, towers over Lyon from the 'hill that prays.' Its neo-Byzantine interior astonishes with exuberant golden mosaics, stained glass, and sculptures. Guided visits access the rooftop and Observatory Tower for 360° panoramas of the city, the Alps, and the Pilat mountains.

The great Roman theater, built around 15 BC under Augustus, is the oldest in Roman Gaul. Expanded in the 1st century, it could seat 10,000 spectators. Its orchestra preserves polychrome marble paving and a stage curtain pit whose mechanism is reconstructed in the neighboring museum.

The odeon, smaller than the theater, was reserved for musical performances and poetry recitals. Its pavement of colored marbles, sourced from across the Empire, is among the finest in the Roman world. Together, the two structures formed a complex unique in ancient Gaul.

The Lugdunum Museum, embedded in the hillside by architect Bernard Zehrfuss (1975), houses one of France's richest archaeological collections. The Claudian Tablet—Emperor Claudius's speech before the Senate—and the Vaise Treasure rank among the masterpieces. Virtual reality tours available to relive the Roman city.

Fourvière esplanade offers Lyon's most celebrated panorama: the peninsula, both rivers, the Part-Dieu tower, Croix-Rousse, and on clear days, the Alpine chain from Mont Blanc to Vercors. Locals gather here for the Festival of Lights on December 8.

Place Bellecour, one of Europe's largest pedestrian squares, opens between the Rhône and Saône. Louis XIV's equestrian statue has stood at its center since 1825. The name likely derives from 'Bella Curtis'—beautiful garden in Latin. At no. 29, lion statues guard the pedestrian street entrance. The Little Prince and Antoine de Saint-Exupéry, Lyon-born, have had their statue on the square since 2000.

Place des Terreaux, at the foot of Croix-Rousse, aligns the Bartholdi fountain (originally intended for Bordeaux), the Fine Arts Museum in the former Saint-Pierre Convent, and the City Hall renovated after an 1674 fire. At night, Daniel Buren's 69 water jets illuminate the ground.

Lyon's Museum of Fine Arts, installed in a 17th-century former convent, is France's second most important after the Louvre. Its collections span from antiquity to contemporary art. The cloister garden, free and open to all, is a peaceful haven in the city center.

Lyon Opera House, renovated by Jean Nouvel in 1993, preserves its neoclassical façade crowned by a contemporary glass dome—a striking contrast between 18th-century elegance and modernity. Eight muses on the pediment contemplate the Place de la Comédie.

Les Halles de Lyon Paul Bocuse, 'Lyon's belly,' gathers over 60 merchants across 145,000 square feet. The first covered market opened in 1859 at Place des Cordeliers. Moved to Part-Dieu in 1971, it took the multi-starred chef's name in 2006. Aged Saint-Marcellin cheese, quenelles, charcuterie, praline brioches, and seafood—Lyon's gastronomy concentrated in one place.

The Mur des Canuts, on Boulevard des Canuts, is Europe's largest trompe-l'oeil: 13,000 square feet of fresco depicting neighborhood life. Created in 1987 by CitéCréation, it's updated every ten years—characters age, shops change. Spot Guignol, the Vélo'v bikes, and the loom between painted windows.

La Maison des Canuts, on Rue d'Ivry, is the living conservatory of Lyon's silk-making heritage. Weaving demonstrations on hand looms, explanations of 19th-century techniques. The canuts—silk-weaver workers—lived in workshop apartments with 13-foot ceilings, necessary to accommodate Jacquard looms.

Croix-Rousse's traboules, built in the 18th century, served to protect silk bales transported from street to street. During the 1831 Canut Revolt—one of history's first workers' uprisings—they allowed insurgents to move under cover. Their motto: 'Live working or die fighting.'

The Fresque des Lyonnais, on Quai Saint-Vincent, features 30 local personalities across 8,600 square feet of façade: Paul Bocuse, the Lumière brothers, Abbé Pierre, Antoine de Saint-Exupéry, Louise Labé. Recognizing them is a game for visitors—painted balconies and windows blend with real ones.

The 'Silk Roads' fresco, at Clos Jouve, traces 3,000 years of silk history across 1,600 square feet: from Lei Tsou to Genghis Khan, from Charlemagne to Francis I. The Croix-Rousse quarter is the birthplace of this industry that made Lyon's fortune from the 16th to 19th centuries.

activities.text: Guided tour of Old Lyon's traboules. Guides unlock Renaissance courtyard doors normally closed to the public. Spiral staircases, carved wells, and Italian-style galleries reveal themselves behind golden stone façades.

Dinner at a traditional Lyonnais bouchon in Old Lyon. Tablier de sapeur, pike quenelle, cervelle de canut, and pink pralines compose the traditional menu. The mâchon—a sort of workers' brunch inherited from silk weavers—is savored in the morning.
Sunset stroll along the Saône quays. Warm light illuminates the cathedral façade and Renaissance houses. The buildings' reflections in the water give the quarter its nickname: 'little Florence.'

Funicular ride from Old Lyon to Fourvière. Two lines: one to the basilica, another to the Roman theaters (Minimes station). Descend on foot through the Rosary Gardens—flowered staircases with views of the Saône and Old Lyon.

Les Nuits de Fourvière transforms the ancient theaters into a summer festival stage each year. Concerts, plays, circus acts, and dance under the stars—the antique setting gives performances magical dimension.

Visit Lugdunum Museum with virtual reality journey. Six immersive stations plunge you into the Roman city of Lugdunum, capital of the Three Gauls. Mosaics, sculptures, and a 3D model of 80 km² of the 2nd-century metropolis.

Gourmet stroll through Les Halles Paul Bocuse. Sample quenelles, aged cheeses, and oysters paired with a glass of Condrieu at the counter. Early morning brings a producers' market atmosphere; late morning, starred chefs do their shopping.

Visit the Museum of Fine Arts. The collection spans five millennia: Egyptian antiquities, paintings by Rubens, Rembrandt, Monet, Picasso, and a fine contemporary art ensemble. The cloister garden is free and open to everyone.

Shopping and wandering on Rue de la République, carved out in the 19th century by Prefect Vaïsse (Lyon's Haussmann). Covered passages—Passage de l'Argue, Passage Thiaffait—preserve the charm of 19th-century galleries.
Tour of Croix-Rousse's painted walls. From the Mur des Canuts, descend the slopes passing by iconic frescoes. Allow 90 minutes for the loop to the Fresque des Lyonnais, trabouling through courtyards.

Croix-Rousse market, one of Lyon's largest and liveliest. Boulevard de la Croix-Rousse, a UNESCO site, fills with producers' stalls on Tuesday, Thursday, Friday, and Saturday mornings. Cheeses, charcuterie, fruits, and vegetables from the Lyon region.

Visit La Maison des Canuts. Weaving demonstration on period Jacquard looms. Soierie Vivante workshop, in the quarter's last remaining workshops, extends the experience with guided tours in authentic facilities.

days_plan.itin_title: Ain Road Trip: Bresse Plains and Bugey Mountains
Rhône Road Trip: Lyon, Golden Stone Villages of Beaujolais & Condrieu Vineyards
Rhône Road Trip: Lyon, Golden Stone Villages of Beaujolais & Condrieu Vineyards
Rhône Road Trip: Lyon, Golden Stone Villages of Beaujolais & Condrieu Vineyards
Rhône Road Trip: Lyon, Golden Stone Villages of Beaujolais & Condrieu Vineyards
Lyon to Annecy Road Trip: Heritage and Alpine Lakes
Lyon to Annecy Road Trip: Heritage and Alpine Lakes
From the Alps to the Ardèche Gorges: Lakes, Peaks and Mediterranean Hills
From the Alps to the Ardèche Gorges: Lakes, Peaks and Mediterranean Hills

visits.text: Village of Lyons-la-Forêt. Ranked among France's Most Beautiful Villages and a Green Resort, this Norman jewel served as the backdrop for both adaptations of Madame Bovary (Jean Renoir 1933, Claude Chabrol 1991). Its 17th and 18th-century half-timbered houses, flower-filled streets, and 18th-century wooden market hall classified as a Historic Monument compose a portrait of preserved authenticity.

Lyons-la-Forêt Market Hall. Beneath their oak framework, these 18th-century halls have punctuated village life for three centuries. They still host the market (Thursday, Saturday, and Sunday mornings) and cultural events.

Mortemer Abbey. Normandy's first Cistercian abbey, founded in 1134 by Henry Beauclerc, William the Conqueror's son. Nicknamed 'France's most haunted abbey,' it houses the Museum of Legends and Ghosts in its 12th-century cellars. The White Lady of Matilda the Empress, Saint Catherine's fountain for singles, and the 18th-century dovecote fuel fantastic tales.
Saint-Denis Church of Lyons-la-Forêt. This 12th-century parish church houses an eagle-shaped lectern from Mortemer Abbey. Its peaceful cemetery invites meditation.

activities.text: Hiking in Lyons Forest. At 10,695 hectares, this beech cathedral is Normandy's largest forest and one of Europe's most beautiful. Century-old beeches soar up to 45 meters high. Over 200 km of marked trails traverse this massif dotted with clearings and valleys.

Les Bordins Arboretum. In the forest's heart, this woodland garden presents a hundred tree species from around the world. A playful orienteering course allows families to discover botanical diversity while having fun.

Saint Catherine's Fountain. This Fouillebroc spring, nestled in the forest, is linked to Mortemer Abbey. Young women came here to pray for a husband within the year. A place steeped in mystery and romance.

days_plan.itin_title: Normandy's Classic Coast: Étretat Cliffs and Bocage Countryside Villages
Eure Road Trip: From Giverny to Normandy's Finest Medieval Villages

places.name: Macinaggio

visits.text: Macinaggio is the port at Cap Corse's northern tip—the end of the end. Tour operators offer narrated cruises to the Giraglia islets and the cape's limestone cliffs, accessible only from the sea. The crossing follows a coast almost virgin of construction, with cliffs plunging directly into the water.

Nonza, on Cap Corse's west coast, is one of the island's most photographed villages: a 17th-century Genoese tower perched on a black cliff, and below a beach of black pebbles—colored by the mountain's schist. It's at Nonza that a lone woman, Catarina Gentili, defended the tower against the French in 1768 by making them believe an entire garrison was entrenched inside. Come in late afternoon for the light on the cliff.

If you still have time, push on to Erbalunga, 10 km north of Bastia—a small Genoese fishing village with houses clustered around a tower collapsed into the sea. It's one of the east coast's most picturesque spots on the cape, often photographed as a backdrop to the seafood terraces, among the region's finest.

activities.text: The sentier des douaniers (customs officers' path) between Macinaggio and Barcaggio is a 12 km one-way hike along Cap Corse's northern corniche, between maquis and sea cliffs. It's Corsica's wildest coastal trail—count on 4 hours round-trip or do it one way with a return shuttle. Bring water: no supply points along the route.

The Patrimonio wine route crosses Corsica's first AOC vineyards (the appellation dates from 1968) at the cape's foot. Cap Corse muscat and red nielluccio are the two pillars of this black schist terroir. Estates offer tastings without appointment, particularly around Patrimonio village.

days_plan.itin_title: Complete Tour of Corsica

map_keywords: Nonza plage galets noirs Cap Corse

visits.text: Old town.
Ursulines Museum.
Saint-Laurent Bridge.

The Maison de Bois, on Place aux Herbes, is Mâcon's oldest dwelling (circa 1500). Its half-timbered façade is adorned with risqué and fantastical sculptures: monkeys, burlesque characters, and half-human, half-animal figures. Locals also call it the "House of Love" for the saucy subjects of its ornaments.

The Saône quays, elegantly landscaped, stretch for nearly 1.2 miles facing the Beaujolais hills. The Pont Saint-Laurent, an 11-arch Romanesque bridge built in the 11th century, links Burgundy to Beaujolais. The Esplanade Lamartine, with its statue of the local-born poet, offers memorable sunsets.

The Vieux Saint-Vincent, vestiges of the former 11th-century cathedral, preserves a Romanesque narthex and sculpted tympanum. The two octagonal towers dominate the old town's rooftops. Below, Place aux Herbes and the pedestrian streets form the lively heart of the city.

The Musée des Ursulines, in the former 17th-century convent, unfolds its collections across three levels: Gallo-Roman archaeology on the ground floor, painting and decorative arts above. Regional collections include works by Greuze and an ethnographic section on rural life in the Mâconnais.

The Château de Lamartine at Saint-Point, 12 miles west of Mâcon, was the residence of poet Alphonse de Lamartine. The neo-Gothic dwelling, surrounded by a romantic park, preserves the study, library, and personal mementos of the author of Méditations poétiques. The neighboring church houses his tomb.

activities.text: Wooden House.
Solutré Rock.
Mâconnais Vineyards.
Saône river cruise departing from Mâcon. Boats sail upstream toward Tournus or downstream toward Lyon, between vineyards and stone villages. Commentary on river history and canal locks.
Bike ride on the ViaRhôna from Mâcon. The bike route follows the Saône southward, between vines and bocage. Possibility of looping through Mâconnais villages.
Mâcon market, Saturday mornings on the Esplanade Lamartine. Mâconnais goat cheeses, Burgundian charcuterie, seasonal fruits and vegetables. Mâcon's crème de cassis is renowned.

days_plan.itin_title: Saône Valley, France: Medieval Abbeys, Wine and Comtois Heritage
Saône-et-Loire Road Trip: Bibracte, Cluny, Solutré & the Mâconnais Vineyards

visits.text: Maillane is inseparable from Frédéric Mistral (1830-1914), Provençal poet, co-founder of the Félibrige in 1854, and 1904 Nobel Prize in Literature winner. Here he grew up, built his house in 1876 (the year of his marriage), and died. The house-museum, classified as a Historic Monument since 1930, has retained its original appearance with its 2,120 objects, 60,000 letters received by the poet, and intact furnishings. Its facade is sculpted with figures of Mireille and Estérelle, heroines of his two Provençal epics. Book ahead: visits are by appointment.

Mistral's tomb in Maillane cemetery deserves a discreet visit: he himself had this mausoleum built in 1906 modeled on the Love Pavilion of Queen Jeanne of Les Baux-de-Provence. A gesture consistent with his works that celebrated medieval Provence. Mistral also used his Nobel Prize funds to finance the development of Arles' Museon Arlaten, the Provençal ethnographic museum.

The Maison du Lézard is the small family house where Mistral lived with his mother from 1855 to 1875 before moving into his purpose-built house. He wrote part of Mirèio and Calendau here. Now the municipal library and tourist office, it still features a sundial adorned with a lizard that he had installed in 1903 — a discreet symbol of his permanent presence in the village.

activities.text: "Discovery of communal heritage" trail: a marked route with 12 panels starts from the house-museum to guide visitors on the trail of Antonin Moulin (Jean Moulin's father, schoolteacher in Saint-Andiol), as well as to Mistral sites in the village — fountains, oratories, old wells. Allow 1h30.

Walk toward Costières landscapes: agricultural paths heading west from Maillane cross a transition between the Rhône plain and the first hillsides of the Costières du Gard — an open landscape of vines and almond trees characteristic of this border between Provence and Languedoc.

days_plan.itin_title: Alpilles, Aix-en-Provence & Camargue Circuit

visits.text: The ruins of Maillezais Abbey dominate the marsh from a former island. It was a fortress, Romanesque abbey, then Gothic cathedral from the 11th to 17th centuries.
The cathedral measured 105 meters long and reached 60 meters high. Only 25 meters of the north wall's height and the two bell towers remain.
Climb the tower to contemplate the surrounding marsh excavated by monks as early as the 11th century.
The refectory and salt cellar testify to monastic life. François Rabelais stayed here for five years during the Renaissance.
An immersive film on three walls traces the history of the abbey in the former refectory.

activities.text: Picnic in the shaded area with a view of the majestic ruins.
Board from the abbey's embarkation point to navigate the canals of the wet marsh.

days_plan.itin_title: Deux-Sèvres Circuit: Marais Poitevin and Romanesque Heritage

visits.text: Château de Maintenon, home of Françoise d'Aubigné, Louis XIV's secret wife. This architectural jewel blends medieval remains with Renaissance and classical elements. The 17th-century apartments reveal Madame de Maintenon's intimate world with period portraits, furniture, and the Noailles gallery adorned with master paintings.

Vauban's unfinished aqueduct, a monumental testament to Louis XIV's ambition. Commissioned in 1685 to channel Eure waters to Versailles' fountains, this colossal project was abandoned in 1688 for lack of funds. Its romantic ruins majestically cross the park for nearly 4 kilometers.

French gardens designed by Le Nôtre, restored in 2013 according to the original 1686 plans. The flower parterres of blue sage, roses, and impatiens surround a canal bordered by two paths named after Racine and Le Nôtre. Facing the aqueduct, deck chairs invite contemplation of this grandiose setting.

activities.text: Walk along the Eure through the château park, where the river has been transformed into a canal passing beneath the aqueduct's majestic arches. White swans glide on the water while century-old lime trees create an atmosphere of aristocratic serenity.

Explore the village of Maintenon with its old houses and Saint-Nicolas church, connected to the château by a wing built under Louis XIV. A peaceful village that has preserved its old-world charm in the shadow of the royal estate.

days_plan.itin_title: Eure-et-Loir Circuit: Chartres, Châteaux & Proust

visits.text: The Fragonard Museum was founded in 1766 — older than the Louvre. It's housed in the grounds of the École nationale vétérinaire d'Alfort, itself created the same year by Louis XV in the Château d'Alfort. The collection is dizzying: 4,200 pieces, hundreds of animal skeletons, natural "monsters" (two-headed calves, five-legged sheep, cyclops), and especially Honoré Fragonard's 21 Écorchés — human and animal bodies preserved for 250 years thanks to a larch resin varnish. His painter cousin used the same resin for his paintings. The most famous depicts a rider on a galloping horse, muscle by muscle. Allow 1h30. Forbidden to children under 8, for good reasons.

The Sainte-Agnès church, built between 1928 and 1936 by architects Teisseire and Pelletier, is Val-de-Marne's finest Art Deco example and is listed as a historic monument. Stained glass, frescoes, sculptures, wrought iron — everything was conceived as "total art." The founder of Suze, a gentian-based aperitif, financed much of the construction: his factory facade is still visible right next door.

End on the Marne banks at the Saint-Maurice dam. This is where the Promenade Paul Cézanne from Saint-Maur terminates. The Maisons-Alfort bridge, with its stone pier topped by a ship's prow and RF letters, dates from 1911 — built by the same engineers as the Pont du Petit-Parc in Joinville.

activities.text: The dam creates eddies that local kayakers train to paddle upstream against. Vendors offer license-free boat rentals between May and September from the Maisons-Alfort banks.

The Maisons-Alfort neighborhood is home to a large Eastern European community — locals call the local market "the Russian market." Slavic groceries, Polish and Russian bakeries: an unexpected change of scenery 10 km from Paris.

days_plan.itin_title: Discovering Val-de-Marne in 10 Days
Val-de-Marne Road Trip: Castles and River Marne Banks

visits.text: Lake Saint-Point is the 3rd largest natural lake in France at 7.2 km long, 950 m wide, and 40 m deep at an altitude of 849 m. Crossed by the Doubs, it is separated from Lake Remoray by a protected wetland. It frequently freezes in winter and becomes a natural ice rink. Five villages border its shores: Saint-Point-Lac, Les Grangettes, Labergement-Sainte-Marie, Montperreux, and Malbuisson.

Malbuisson has been a Station Verte since 2009, recognized for its hotel and gastronomic tradition. The village overlooks the lake and serves as the starting point for the lake loop trail (23 km, pedestrians only). The Aqua2lacs water complex offers pools overlooking with panoramic lake views, jacuzzi, and relaxation area.

Mont d'Or rises to 1,463 meters, the highest point in the Doubs department. From its summit, accessible by the Morond chairlift or on foot via the GR5, you discover an exceptional panorama of more than 300 Alpine peaks including Mont Blanc, as well as the Haut-Doubs, Haut-Jura, and neighboring Switzerland. The site is classified as a Sensitive Natural Area for its remarkable flora including some protected species.

activities.text: Four water sports centers offer sailing, catamaran, stand-up paddle, canoe-kayak, and pedal boats on Lake Saint-Point. The beaches at Les Grangettes and Oye-et-Pallet are supervised in July-August. The water remains cool even in summer (18-22°C in August).

The Mont d'Or ridge hike (approximately 2h round trip, 6 km) connects the Morond summit to the Mont d'Or summit via a panoramic trail. A museum space in the snow factory presents the history and biodiversity of the massif. Free trail guides are available at the station ticket offices.

days_plan.itin_title: Doubs Circuit: Watchmaking and Swiss Border

visits.text: Malicorne-sur-Sarthe became France's capital of artistic faience thanks to a fortunate combination: quality clay deposits at Ligron, abundant wood for kilns, and a port on the Sarthe to distribute production. In 1747, Jean Loiseau, a faience maker from Touraine, opened the first manufactory here in a former inn called the Plat d'Étain. Push open the door of Faïencerie Bourg-Joly Malicorne—the direct heir of that foundation, still in operation—and watch the artisans work. Their openwork baskets are Malicorne's signature: the technique involves cutting the raw clay piece with a pointed blade to create fine openings reminiscent of lace. In 2011, during Monaco's princely wedding, Bourg-Joly delivered 342 of these baskets to decorate the Opera reception tables.

The Museum of Faience and Ceramics, labeled Musée de France, is housed in a former 19th-century stoneware manufactory with its kilns and chimney. The tour traces the history of Malicorne faience from the 18th century to contemporary creations—decorations inspired by Rouen, Quimper, or Moustiers, and especially the famous openwork invented in 1924 by Émile Tessier. He had the idea of piercing the still-raw piece with a pointed blade before firing. Slip-casting workshops are offered for young and old—a good way to leave with a piece made by your own hands.

The Malicorne color mill houses the Arts and Crafts shop on the first floor. This is where faience makers once ground their stones under millstones to extract colored pigments. The building is worth seeing even if you don't buy anything.

activities.text: Embark on an electric boat ride on the Sarthe. Operators offer these outings from Malicorne port—an original perspective on the village from the water, with its tuffeau facades and poplar-lined banks. Allow an hour for the short version, two hours to reach Sablé-sur-Sarthe downstream.

Operators offer Sarthe descents by canoe-kayak from Malicorne. Successive locks rhythm the navigation and allow stops on wild banks. Bring spare clothes—even in calm weather, lock crossings can hold surprises.

days_plan.itin_title: Sarthe Road Trip: Le Mans and the Loir Valley

places.name: Mamers

visits.text: The Halles aux Grains of Mamers, built in 1818 replacing an older building, still host Monday, Friday, and Saturday markets today. Countess de Ségur mentioned them in Memoirs of a Donkey, published in 1860. These halls fit into a monumental triptych around Place Carnot: facing them, the Italian-style theater—one of the region's rare examples—occupies the ground floor of the festival hall building; behind, Saint-Nicolas church built between the 13th and 14th centuries raises its remarkable tower-belfry and Renaissance portal classified as a historic monument.

On Place de la République, look up to spot Mamers' Eiffel Tower: a 1:100 replica, built in 1889—the same year as the Paris Tower—by a local tinsmith who had worked on the Parisian construction site. Restored in 1997, it sits on a roof and remains probably the oldest known copy of the Eiffel Tower in the world. The story says it was commissioned to attract officers from the local garrison.

The Convent of the Visitation, founded in 1630 by Marie-Augustine Davoust, was confiscated at the Revolution to install the town hall and state prisons. Graffiti from royalist prisoners are still visible on the walls of the former cells. Also stroll through the coulées mamertines—these paved passages so narrow they can't be distinguished from the square—and along rue de Cinq-Ans with its hemp weavers' houses.

activities.text: Tasting Sarthe rillettes is an obligation in Mamers. The Brotherhood of Knights of Sarthe Rillettes, founded here in 1968, organizes the annual National Contest of Best Rillettes each year. Maine rillettes are slowly cooked in their own fat—melting texture, concentrated and slightly caramelized pork flavor. Market butchers will offer you several producers to compare.

The Maine Saosnois Greenway, a former railway line converted to a gentle path, extends 12 kilometers from Mamers through the Sarthe Perche countryside. Accessible on foot, by bike, or stroller—flat, natural, and with views over bocage. Operators rent bikes at the start.

days_plan.itin_title: Sarthe Road Trip: Le Mans and the Loir Valley

map_keywords: Halle aux Grains Mamers place Carnot

visits.text: Mane is a village of blonde stone perched on a safre hill (local ochre rock). Its name may come from Mana Genita, Roman goddess of funerals — the site has been inhabited since prehistory and the Via Domitia passed below. The 12th-century citadel, built on its peak, is the only feudal fortification remaining intact in Haute-Provence. Wander the cobbled lanes and observe the pointed cabanons (bories) scattered across the territory: dry-stone constructions without mortar, built between 1850 and 1950, witnesses to a vanished peasant know-how.

The Salagon Priory is the key visit of the stop. Classified as a Historic Monument, this vast 50 x 30 m complex groups a 12th-century Romanesque church (barrel vaults, carved capitals, rare 15th-century mural fresco), a 15th-century priory lodging, and 6 hectares of ethnobotanical gardens. The interior surprise: a series of six contemporary stained-glass windows by Aurélie Nemours (installed in 1998) bathe the nave in intense red — a striking contrast with Romanesque austerity. The gardens present more than 1,700 plants, including a medieval garden and a poisonous plant garden. The museum traces 2,000 years of site occupation, from the Neolithic to monastic life.

Less than a kilometer from Salagon, the Château de Sauvan (18th century) is a replica of Versailles's Petit Trianon — an architectural curiosity amid Provençal scrubland. Period furniture and classical gardens, guided tours in season.

activities.text: Walk the route of calades and fountains through the town, then reach the medieval Roman bridge over the Laye that once led pilgrims to Salagon Priory with dry feet. The round-trip walk from the village takes about 45 minutes and offers lovely views of Mane's peak.

days_plan.itin_title: Forcalquier & Verdon Gorges Road Trip

visits.text: The historic center displays its winding streets between two medieval gates. Porte Saunerie and Porte Soubeyran guard the fortified old town entrance. Renaissance mansions reveal their inner courtyards and spiral staircases behind heavy wooden doors.

The Jean Giono Center celebrates the local-born writer. The birthplace and urban trail trace the life and work of the author. Manuscripts, original editions and personal objects evoke the universe of the one who sang of Provence and its inhabitants.

Saint-Sauveur church blends Provençal Romanesque and southern Gothic. The 14th-century sculpted portal precedes a nave with soaring vaults. Side chapels house Baroque altarpieces and Renaissance baptismal fonts.
The Présentation convent occupies a vast monastic complex. Terraced gardens offer a green break in the city center. The Baroque chapel displays its gilded stuccoes and restored frescoes.

Discover shaded squares where fountains and washhouses murmur. Place de l'Hôtel de Ville brings Manosque's heart to life. Café terraces invite observing the daily animation of this authentic Provençal town.

activities.text: The Route Napoléon crosses Manosque on the Emperor's historic route. The itinerary follows Bonaparte's footsteps during his return from the island of Elba in 1815. Commemorative markers and panels mark this mythical route between Golfe-Juan and Grenoble.

The Valensole plateau extends at Manosque's gates. Lavender fields draw their fragrant geometry from June to July. Perched villages and distilleries punctuate country roads through this iconic landscape.
Hiking trails crisscross wooded hills around Manosque. Mont d'Or offers a moderate ascent rewarded by a panorama over the Durance and Luberon. Aleppo pines and kermes oaks punctuate the routes.

days_plan.itin_title: Alpes-De-Haute-Provence Circuit: Lavender and Perched Villages

visits.text: The Collégiale Notre-Dame de Mantes-la-Jolie, built between 1140 and 1240, bears striking resemblance to Notre-Dame de Paris. Its Last Judgment rose window, dated 1210-1220, and Burgundian glazed tile roof set it apart.
The Saint-Maclou Tower, remnant of a 14th-century Gothic parish church, stands sentinel above the old town rooftops. It serves as a landmark visible from the Seine quays.
The Hôtel-Dieu Museum holds the largest collection of works by Maximilien Luce, a neo-Impressionist painter native to the town. A lapidary deposit displays carved fragments from the collégiale.

activities.text: Stroll along the Seine quays to Île aux Dames, accessible via a pedestrian footbridge. The island offers lawns, playgrounds, and views of the collégiale from the opposite bank.
Wander through Mantes-la-Jolie's old town: medieval alleyways, fountains, and half-timbered façades surround the collégiale.
Listen to the 1897 Merklin organ during concerts regularly held beneath the collégiale's vaults.

days_plan.itin_title: Yvelines Road Trip: Versailles and Impressionist Villages

places.name: Marche Enfants Rouges

visits.text: The Marché des Enfants Rouges, opened in 1615 on Rue de Bretagne in the Marais, is Paris's oldest covered market—its name supposedly comes from children at the neighboring hospice who wore red clothes, the uniform of charity orphans at the time. Closed and fallen into disrepair in the 20th century, it was classified as a historic monument in 1982 and reopened in 2000 after restoration. Today a dozen stalls offer Moroccan, Japanese, Lebanese, Creole, and French cuisine under the original metal and glass canopies. It's a place to eat on-site, standing or sitting on benches, like Parisians from the neighborhood.

activities.text: Lunch at the Marché des Enfants Rouges—choose your dish at one of the counters, settle on the communal benches under the arcades. The neighborhood market atmosphere, between onlookers and regulars, nicely sums up what's lively and informal about Paris far from the museums. It's a fitting way to end this cultural tour.

days_plan.itin_title: The Great Museums of Paris

map_keywords: Marché des Enfants Rouges Paris Marais

visits.text: Lupiac, a castelnau perched at 800 feet on a Gascon hillside, is the birthplace of the most famous musketeer. Charles de Batz de Castelmore was born here around 1613 at the family castle, before setting off to conquer Paris around 1630. He became captain-lieutenant of King Louis XIV's Musketeers and died at the siege of Maastricht in 1673.

On the central square, the first equestrian statue of d'Artagnan rears skyward—an 11-foot monumental bronze inaugurated in 2015. Sword brandished, the rider seems ready to resume his journey to Paris. The village preserves Saint-Barthélemy Church, whose four bells date from the musketeer's era.

The d'Artagnan Museum, housed in the Saint-Jacques Chapel classified as a Historic Monument, traces the life of the real musketeer. Wax figures in Grand Siècle costume, period documents, editions of Dumas's Three Musketeers and movie posters. A twenty-minute film draws parallels between legend and reality.

Nine miles southwest, the Tower of Termes-d'Armagnac rises 120 feet above the Adour valley. Classified as a Historic Monument, it's the last vestige of the palace of the lords of Termes, vassals of the Counts of Armagnac (late 13th century). Its most famous resident, Thibault de Termes, accompanied Joan of Arc at the siege of Orléans in 1429 and remained faithful to her until the end. From the summit, spectacular views of the Pyrenees.

Inside the Termes keep, climb the 150 steps of the spiral staircase. Reconstructed armory, seigneurial chamber, reception hall—each floor plunges into the life of a 13th-century Gascon palace. Children can duel with foam swords in a mini-arena above the weapons room.

Marciac, a royal bastide founded in 1298 through a paréage between the Abbot of Case-Dieu and the Seneschal of Toulouse, has preserved its grid plan intact. Its impressively vast central square is bordered by elegant arcades with imposing wooden pillars. This is where jazzy Gascony's heart beats.

Notre-Dame Church raises its bell tower above the terracotta rooftops. Its painted portal exemplifies southern Gothic. Nearby, the spire of the former Augustinian convent chapel recalls that Marciac also lies on the Arles route leading to Santiago de Compostela.

Territoires du Jazz, a scenographic space unique in Europe, offers a journey into the heart of jazz history. Infrared headset on, explore twelve settings: from Africa's origins to New Orleans, from feverish blues to New York clubs, to Europe as the welcoming land for jazz musicians.

L'Astrada, a modern performance hall inaugurated to extend the festival spirit year-round. Dance, circus, theater, classical music and of course jazz take turns here. The Augustins cultural center, arranged in the former convent, houses the tourist office and exhibitions.

activities.text: Visit the d'Artagnan Museum with activity booklet for children. Pencil in hand, young musketeers embark on an adventure through the rooms. Written guide available in six languages for adults.

Walk around Lake Lupiac, a few hundred yards from the village. Shaded trail, swimming possible in summer, fishing and picnic areas. The bucolic setting of Gascon hillsides lends itself to a peaceful break.

Medieval events at the Tower of Termes in summer: jousting tournaments, theatrical tours, medieval banquets. It's the only medieval site in Gers open year-round. In August, the "d'Artagnan chez d'Artagnan" festival transforms Lupiac into a 17th-century village.

Stroll beneath the medieval arcades of the central square. Terraces, shops and art galleries animate this bastide that has vibrated to the rhythm of jazz since 1978. In summer, the free off-festival takes over the square with impromptu concerts on every corner.

Walk around Lake Marciac, half a mile from the bastide. Created in 1963, this body of water offers jogging, fishing, paddle boats and swimming in summer. The landscape setting, ringed by hills, invites a pause between visits.

Saint-Mont wine and armagnac tasting. The AOC Saint-Mont vineyard, a stone's throw from Marciac, holds a curiosity: the Sarragachies vine, dating from the early 19th century and classified as a Historic Monument—one of the oldest pre-phylloxera vines in France, with 21 grape varieties including 7 unknowns.

Visit in summer for Jazz in Marciac (late July through mid-August). Created in 1978 by a handful of friends, the festival now attracts 250,000 spectators and the biggest names in world jazz. A 6,000-seat big top in the evenings, free concerts on the square during the day.

days_plan.itin_title: Gers Road Trip: Gascon Bastides, Armagnac & Musketeers
Gers Road Trip: Gascon Bastides, Armagnac & Musketeers

visits.text: Brouage, a citadel asleep in the marshes. Founded in 1555, it was Europe's leading salt port before silting up. Richelieu made it an impregnable stronghold between 1628 and 1633 under Pierre d'Argencourt, then Vauban reinforced the fortifications in 1685. Samuel de Champlain, founder of Quebec, was born here around 1570. Today, 500 inhabitants and an uncanny silence.

The Brouage rampart walk (2 km) offers views over former salt marshes turned bird reserve. Black-winged stilts, egrets and spoonbills nest here. The powder magazines, forge and restored church can be visited.

Marennes, oyster capital. The Marennes-Oléron basin produces 45% of French oysters. The Saint-Pierre church bell tower (85 m) is the tallest in Charente-Maritime. The Oyster City explains aging in 'claires', those brackish water pools that green and refine the oysters.

activities.text: Visit an oyster shack. Producers open their establishments to explain farming and aging. Tasting on site with views of the claires – a glass of local white accompanies the oysters.
Walk in the Brouage marshes. Marked trails through former salt marshes. Binoculars recommended for watching migratory birds. The sunset over the pools is memorable.

days_plan.itin_title: Charente-Maritime Road Trip: La Rochelle, Île de Ré, Fort Boyard and Roman Saintonge

visits.text: The Margaux appellation is one of six communal Médoc appellations, renowned for elegant and aromatic wines. The vineyard extends over 1,500 hectares across four communes: Margaux-Cantenac, Soussans, Arsac, and Labarde. The appellation counts 21 grands crus classés from 1855, including the celebrated Château Margaux. The gravelly terroir (pebbles) brings finesse and complexity to the wines. Margaux village itself is small but charming.

The Margaux Wine and Tourism Center welcomes you to discover the appellation. The museum space explains the terroir, vineyard history, and the 1855 classification. A wine selection is available for tasting and purchase. Staff can advise you on châteaux to visit and help with reservations. It's the ideal starting point for organizing your vineyard discovery.

The Margaux-Cantenac Châteaux loop is a 15 km route to walk or cycle. This marked itinerary crosses the vineyard passing 18 of the 21 classified grands crus in the appellation. You'll pass prestigious properties like Château Margaux, Château Palmer, Château Lascombes. The path offers beautiful estuary and Île Margaux views. Allow 2 hours by bike, 4 hours on foot without visit stops.

Saint-Michel Church in Margaux, built in the 19th century in neo-Romanesque style, stands in the village center. Its square bell tower is visible from afar across the flat Médoc landscape. The sober interior houses some interesting stained glass. The forecourt opens onto the main village square where the market is held. It's a useful landmark for orientation in Margaux.

activities.text: Visit and tasting at a Margaux château. The appellation offers a wide diversity of wine tourism experiences, from classified grands crus to more accessible family properties. Guided tours help you understand vineyard work, vinification, and barrel aging. Tastings reveal the elegance characteristic of Margaux wines. Reservations essential, sometimes several weeks in advance for the most prestigious estates.

Cycling through Margaux vineyards. The Médoc's flat topography and quiet small roads make cycling very pleasant. Several rental shops offer classic or electric bikes. You can follow the château loop or create your own itinerary. Landscapes of vines aligned as far as the eye can see are magnificent. Plan breaks to admire châteaux and take photos.

Margaux Golf offers an 18-hole, 6,000-meter course spread over 45 hectares. Designed in a natural setting amid vineyards, it's renowned for its beauty and impeccable maintenance. Greens are bordered by century-old elms and ash trees. The clubhouse offers views over the course and surrounding vineyards. On-site restaurant. Green fees accessible with reservations recommended.

Margaux Saveurs is an annual event held the third weekend of November. Many appellation châteaux open their doors for tastings, entertainment, concerts, and gourmet meals. It's a festive and convivial opportunity to discover the Margaux appellation differently. The program blends wine discoveries with musical ambiance. Tickets and reservations on the event website.

days_plan.itin_title: Gironde Road Trip: Bordeaux, Vineyards & Arcachon Bay

visits.text: Discovery of Lake Chalain, largest natural lake in Jura, and walk around the turquoise waters
Ascent to the 4 Lakes viewpoint for a panorama over Ilay, Narlay, Petit and Grand Maclu

activities.text: Swimming and relaxation by Lake Chalain
Sunset walk around Lake Ilay

days_plan.itin_title: Jura Lakes & Mountains: Turquoise Waters, Waterfalls & Alpine Cheese Routes

visits.text: The 13th-century Église Notre-Dame blends Gothic architecture with 19th-century restoration. Inside, admire the Baroque altarpiece and the Entombment sculpture.
The Renaissance Cloister built in 1540 preserves two elegant galleries. Its varied decoration showcases the refinement of the era.
The Jardin du Cloître landscaped in 1950 follows French formal garden principles. Boxwood parterres, sculpted yews, and a central basin create perfect harmony.
Stroll along Rue Labat lined with 16th-century half-timbered houses. These dwellings testify to Marmande's medieval prosperity.
The statue of Ferline with her pomme d'amour on Place Clemenceau reminds visitors that Marmande is the capital of heirloom tomatoes.

activities.text: Browse the market to taste the famous Marmande tomatoes. Growers and market gardeners display their bountiful harvests.
Walk the Garonne riverbanks at the Plaine de la Filhole. This green meander offers a peaceful stroll along the river.
Explore the Chemin de Ronde du Caillou behind the church. These medieval fortification remnants include the Tour du Passeur.

days_plan.itin_title: Lot-et-Garonne Road Trip: Medieval Bastides & Villages

visits.text: Maroilles: abbey site, Saint-Humbert church & Trail of the Senses.

activities.text: Cheese shop & AOP Maroilles tasting (for epicureans).

days_plan.itin_title: Hauts-de-France: Opal Coast Cliffs, Medieval History & Great War Sites

visits.text: Start with La Bonne Mère—that's what locals call Notre-Dame-de-la-Garde, and it's the first thing you should do upon arrival. Perched at 154 meters atop a limestone outcrop, this Romano-Byzantine basilica consecrated in 1864 dominates everything. The gilded statue of the Virgin at the summit measures 11.20 meters and weighs nearly 10 tons—it was crafted by the Christofle house and requires regilding every 25 years. Inside, the walls are covered with 1,200 m² of gold-backed mosaics and sailors' ex-votos: suspended ship models, thank-you plaques, and naïve paintings. Go up in the morning for the light and avoid weekends.

Head back down to the Old Port and climb up into Le Panier district, Marseille's oldest neighborhood—this is where Greek settlers from Phocaea landed 2,600 years ago. Today, narrow alleys are covered in street art, hidden squares shelter artists' workshops and improvised terraces. Don't look for a precise route: get lost, that's the whole point. Place des Moulins, the district's highest point, makes a good landmark if you lose your bearings.

Leaving Le Panier toward the sea, you'll encounter La Major cathedral. Built between 1852 and 1893 in neo-Byzantine style, it's massive—142 meters long, the largest religious building constructed in France during the 19th century. The contrast between white Carrara marble and green Florence stone gives it a striking striped appearance. Glance inside at the choir mosaics, then exit on the sea side for views over the port of La Joliette.

End the day with a stroll along the Old Port. It's been Marseille's beating heart for 26 centuries. In the morning, the fish market on quai des Belges is a spectacle—fishermen sell their catch directly from boats, shouting in Provençal. By late afternoon, the terraces take over. The Vieux-Port ombrière, Norman Foster's immense polished steel structure, acts as a mirror reflecting the crowds—it's become one of modern Marseille's symbols.

Devote the morning to MuCEM—this concrete cube wrapped in an openwork lattice facing the sea was designed by Rudy Ricciotti. Opened in 2013 when Marseille was European Capital of Culture, it entered the world's 50 most visited museums the following year. Collections cover anthropology, archaeology, and contemporary art around the Mediterranean. Even if exhibitions don't appeal, head to the rooftop terrace: the view over the Old Port and the open sea is free and spectacular.

Cross the 130-meter footbridge linking MuCEM to Fort Saint-Jean—suspended above the harbor basin, it offers a vertiginous view of the sea. The fort, built on Louis XIV's orders from 1668 onward, dominates the Old Port entrance from its 20-meter height. It served as much to protect Marseille as to watch over it—cannons were aimed at the city in case of revolt. Free entry, it houses the Garden of Migrations, a 12,000 m² Mediterranean botanical space with lavender, rosemary, and orange trees. Look for the Fanal tower (17th century) that served as a lighthouse and King René's tower (15th century), whose terrace offers plunging views over the port.

Return landward via the second footbridge leading directly into Le Panier district. Take rue du Petit-Puits to La Vieille Charité, a former 17th-century hospice built by Pierre Puget around an arcaded courtyard and oval chapel. Today, the building houses the Museum of Mediterranean Archaeology and the Museum of African, Oceanic, and American Indian Arts. Even without entering the museums, the courtyard is magnificent and free.

activities.text: Walk up to Notre-Dame-de-la-Garde on foot from the Old Port via Saint-Victor Abbey—one of France's oldest, founded in the 5th century, with crypts and early Christian sarcophagi. Allow 30 minutes uphill. Alternatively, bus 60 departs from the Old Port and drops you at the basilica's foot.

Late afternoon, walk along Corniche Kennedy heading south. It's a 25-minute walk from the Old Port to reach Vallon des Auffes, a tiny fishing harbor wedged between two concrete cliffs. This is postcard Marseille, with colorful pointus (traditional boats) and little bouillabaisse restaurants. The spot is perfect for an aperitif facing the sunset.

If you enjoy contemporary art and alternative spaces, make a detour to Friche Belle de Mai—a former tobacco factory converted into a 45,000 m² cultural center. Galleries, artists' studios, concert hall, rooftop skatepark, restaurants. This is creative Marseille, far from tourist circuits. Take bus 49 from the Old Port.

Late afternoon, head down to Prado beaches or Escale Borély for a seaside walk. Parc Borély is Marseille's largest green space, with its 18th-century château, botanical garden, and lake. The atmosphere is relaxed—joggers, families, pétanque players. Grab a drink on a terrace facing the sea before heading to Cassis the next day.

days_plan.itin_title: From Marseille to Aix-en-Provence: Calanques, Hilltop Villages and Provençal Light
From Marseille to Aix-en-Provence: Calanques, Hilltop Villages and Provençal Light

visits.text: Grotte Dechaux — Inscriptions (1865–1868).
Rochers des Deux Frères — Double legend.

activities.text: Access: public (Dechaux)
Access: public (Deux Frères)

days_plan.itin_title: Mystical Provence: Haunted Villages and Paranormal History of Southern France

visits.text: Martel, the "Town of Seven Towers," is listed among France's Most Beautiful Villages. A merchant city founded in the 11th–12th centuries by the viscounts of Turenne, it has preserved its intact medieval layout encircled by 12th–14th-century ramparts.
Place des Consuls forms Martel's beating heart. An 18th-century market hall shelters stalls beneath stone pillars. All around, half-timbered houses and arcaded dwellings compose a preserved setting.

The Palais de la Raymondie, built between 1280 and 1330, raises its square tower above the square. The former seigneurial residence houses the Uxellodunum Museum—Gallo-Roman collections and Quercy history. Designated a Museum of France.

The fortified Church of Saint-Maur impresses with its 40-meter bell tower-keep built for defense. A carved 12th-century tympanum adorns the portal. Inside, entirely painted decoration, surprisingly colorful, contrasts with the facade's austerity.

Spot the seven towers that gave the town its nickname. The 13th-century Tour Tournemire, a former prison, offers panoramic views from its summit. The Tour des Cordeliers is the sole remnant of a Franciscan monastery founded in 1230. The Maison Fabri recalls a dark episode: here a young Anglo-Norman king allegedly breathed his last after pillaging Rocamadour.

activities.text: The Truffadou tourist train follows the former Bordeaux-Aurillac line carved into the cliff 80 meters above the Dordogne. The 13-kilometer route between Martel and Saint-Denis offers plunging views of the valley from open wagons.
Browse Martel's market on Wednesday and Saturday mornings beneath the 18th-century hall. Seasonal truffles, walnuts, Quercy terroir products, and local specialties.
Visit a traditional walnut oil mill near Martel. Périgord walnuts, with AOC designation, are pressed using artisanal methods. Tastings and on-site sales.

days_plan.itin_title: Lot Itinerary: Cahors, Rocamadour & the Lot Valley

visits.text: Mountain village.
Col de Port.

activities.text: Hiking.
Cascades d'Ars.
Nature observation.

days_plan.itin_title: Ariège Pyrenees Road Trip, France: Cathar Castles & Prehistoric Cave Art

visits.text: Vauban's fortifications still encircle Maubeuge along two-thirds of their original layout. Erected in record time from 1679 to 1685 on Louis XIV's orders, they transformed the city into a sentinel of the Pré Carré, that line of strongholds protecting the kingdom's northern frontier.

The Porte de Mons, classified as a Historic Monument since 1924, is the rampart's last intact gate. Built in 1682, this monumental gate preserves its complete defensive complex: drawbridge, guardhouse and ravelin.

The Corps de Garde Museum occupies an 1683 building erected following Vauban's directives. Beneath its ancient timber frame, it traces Maubeuge's military history. On the ground floor, a 19th-century relief map clarifies the fortifications' geometry.

Saint-Pierre-Saint-Paul Church, erected between 1955 and 1958, is built entirely of reinforced concrete. Its 43-meter bell tower, pierced with glass bricks, houses a 28-bell carillon. The tympanum is adorned with a mosaic designed by Jean Lurçat, brother of architect André Lurçat.

The Sthrau Hall, listed as a Historic Monument since 1958, is a unique Art Deco ensemble inserted into a former baroque chapel of the Jesuit college. Rebuilt between 1923 and 1927, it comprises a two-level music hall with characteristic geometric decoration.

Maubeuge was 80% destroyed in 1940. André Lurçat, charged with reconstruction, designed a downtown of remarkable modernist homogeneity: concrete and brick buildings, airy perspectives, green spaces integrated into ramparts. Architecture that still divides opinion but leaves no one indifferent.

activities.text: Maubeuge Zoo occupies 7 hectares within Vauban's very ramparts. Tour the bastions while observing over 300 animals of 60 species, in a setting where the fortifications' moats and embankments serve as natural enclosures.

Walk along the Monier ponds southeast of the fortifications. Backed against the rampart, these former defensive basins bordered by willows now form a peaceful haven popular with strollers and fishermen, steps from downtown.

Follow the fortifications' covered way from Porte de Mons to the Oratoire bastion. Artillery access ramps, embrasures and casemates punctuate the route—you can imagine horses pulling cannons to firing platforms.

days_plan.itin_title: Northern France Circuit: Lille, Belfries and Flemish Heritage

visits.text: The Basilique Notre-Dame-des-Miracles rises at the heart of Mauriac, a sanctuary founded in the 6th century by Théodechilde, daughter of Clovis. The sculpted Romanesque portal of the 12th century announces the majesty of the building. Inside, the Gothic choir and the relics of Saint Marius (an evangelizing disciple whose name is said to have inspired "Puy Mary") bear witness to the age-old religious influence of this town between the Dordogne and the Cantal mountains.

The old town retains the charm of Auvergne market towns with its bourgeois houses, shaded small squares and volcanic stone façades. Stroll through the lanes climbing towards the basilica, discover the local shops and the peaceful atmosphere of this Cantal sub-prefecture with its preserved character.

activities.text: The weekly market brings the central square to life in a bustle of colours and flavours. Farm producers, market gardeners and artisans offer aged cheeses, local cured meats, sourdough breads and seasonal vegetables. The perfect opportunity to meet passionate farmers and taste Cantal's authenticity.

The walk to the Château d'Anjony reveals one of the finest castles in Cantal, a 15th-century medieval fortress overlooking the Doire valley. Its four corner towers frame a remarkable square keep. The Renaissance frescoes in the chapel and the wall paintings in the apartments are exceptional artistic treasures within this austere military setting.

days_plan.itin_title: Cantal Road Trip: Auvergne Volcanoes and Mountain Traditions

visits.text: Maussane-les-Alpilles is the olive-growing heart of the Baux Valley — its cooperative mill processes olives from several hundred members to produce a PDO oil recognized among France's finest. Several local varieties enter the blend: Aglandau (also called Beruguette), Grossane, Salonenque, and Verdale des Bouches-du-Rhône. Providers offer visits and tastings — a gustatory experience differentiating the two typicities (fruity green and fruity black) that the valley's millers passionately defend.

Maussane's central square with its fountain and southern facades is the quintessential Provençal village image: café terraces under plane trees, local merchants, tractors crossing the town. It's also one of the rare Alpilles squares not overrun by tourist shops. Settle on a terrace and observe.

activities.text: Olive grove-vineyard walk on agricultural tracks: from Maussane center, unpaved rural paths wind between olive groves and plots of PDO Les Baux-de-Provence vines. Morning light on century-old olive trees is remarkable. Allow 2 hours for a 7 km loop.

Oil mill visit (according to opening): several artisanal and cooperative mills in the valley welcome visitors, especially during extraction season (November-January). Outside the season, guided tours explain the process from olive to oil, from manual harvest (November) to cold pressing.

days_plan.itin_title: Alpilles, Aix-en-Provence & Camargue Circuit

visits.text: Mayenne Castle overlooks the river of the same name. Over 1,100 years old, it houses Europe's best-preserved Carolingian palace, classified as an archaeological site of national interest. The reception hall, cellar and residential tower are still legible in the masonry.

The museum, labeled Museum of France and starred in the Michelin Green Guide, traces 2,000 years of history through an interactive journey. The collections from excavations are exceptional: chess and backgammon pieces from the 10th-12th centuries, ceramics, coins, arrowheads and funerary furnishings testifying to the life of Mayenne's lords.

In the upper courtyard, a medieval-inspired garden has been recreated from period documents. Plants and shrubs are classified as in the Middle Ages: medicinal herbs, aromatics, edibles, medicinals or 'magical' plants.

The Basilica of Notre-Dame-des-Miracles, on the right bank, is a composite edifice from the 12th, 16th and 19th centuries. The west façade is original Romanesque (circa 1110). The 19th-century choir draws inspiration from Le Mans Cathedral's chevet. A chapel houses the 15th-century polychrome wooden statuette of Our Lady of Miracles.

The basilica's current stained glass windows, designed by Maurice Rocher and installed between 1952 and 1962, replaced those destroyed during the June 1944 bombings that devastated the town. The basilica remained closed for thirteen years for restoration.

Stroll through Place de Hercé with its octagonal fountain, then reach Place Cheverus surrounded by 17th-18th century townhouses. On the left bank, the 11th-century Saint-Martin church stands out with its russet granite.
The Calvary Sisters Chapel, last vestige of a vast 17th-century convent, now houses an art center devoted to contemporary creation.

activities.text: Walk along the Mayenne quays for a riverside stroll. The castle park, right in the town center, offers a bandstand, towering trees and a stunning view from the ramparts.

Drive 5 km to Fontaine-Daniel, a village of character nestled in a clearing in the Salair woods. Founded around a Cistercian abbey in 1205, it became the birthplace of Toiles de Mayenne in 1806 when the abandoned abbey was transformed into a spinning mill. The factory, labeled Living Heritage Company, still weaves on site.
In season, board the pleasure boat departing from the river stop for a narrated cruise on the Mayenne River. Wildlife, flora, local history and manual lock operation on the program.

days_plan.itin_title: Mayenne Circuit: Hedgerows and Medieval Towns

places.name: Mazamet

visits.text: Hautpoul clings to its rocky outcrop, 300 meters above Mazamet. According to legend, this village was founded in 413 by a Visigoth king. A Cathar refuge then Protestant stronghold, its steep streets preserve remnants of medieval fortifications.

Climb to the Rocher de la Vierge and the old castle terraces for an exceptional panorama over the Arnette valley and Mazamet below. The neighboring medieval garden presents medicinal and dyeing plants from the period.
The Wood and Toy House unfolds over 1,500 m² of exhibitions on the journey from forest to tree, tree to wood, and wood to toy. Children and adults alike linger here.
Descending toward Mazamet, the mid-19th-century Cormouls Houlès Gardens offer a spectacular botanical trail overlooking the valley, punctuated by viewpoints over Montagne Noire.

Follow Mazamet's self-guided heritage circuits, labeled '100 Most Beautiful Detours of France.' The architecture tells of the era when the city was the world center for processing wool imported from Australia and Argentina.

activities.text: Cross the Mazamet footbridge, suspended 70 meters above the Arnette valley. 140 meters long, it sways gently underfoot and connects the town to Hautpoul village. Free and open daily.
Take the Jamarié path, the old salt road and Roman way, to reach the footbridge via a history-laden route. The return can follow a different trail in a loop.
In the evening, return to admire the illuminated footbridge: each night it lights up in changing colors until 2 a.m., creating a luminous spectacle above the gorges.

days_plan.itin_title: Tarn Road Trip: Albi & Hilltop Villages

map_keywords: Maison du Bois et du Jouet Mazamet

places.name: Meaudre

visits.text: Méaudre is one of the "Four Villages" of the northern Vercors plateau, with its aligned chalets, gallery farms, and grassy squares. Autrans and Méaudre have now merged into a new commune. The village is distinguished in winter by its Narces Nordic domain, 300 meters above the village, and its biathlon stadium. In summer, the flowering meadows surrounding the village are the typical Vercors image between hay and firs.

The church and gallery farms in Méaudre's center constitute the essential heritage elements. The covered wooden galleries served to store hay and shelter animals—functional architecture adapted to 1,000 meters altitude.

activities.text: The gentle valley-floor loop between Méaudre and Autrans (5-7 km, flat) follows the plain connecting the two villages. In summer, it's a walk through meadows with views of wooded ridges on both sides. In winter, it's the Nordic traverse route, one of the classics of the 180 km trail network.

The short forest-edge climb to the Gève plateau from Autrans (30-minute drive from Méaudre, then hike) offers a panorama of the entire Alps chain in clear weather. This is the site that hosted the Olympic cross-country skiing events in 1968, on the domain's legendary yellow trail.

days_plan.itin_title: Royans-Vercors Road Trip: Hilltop Villages and Gorges

map_keywords: Foyer de ski de fond Méaudre

visits.text: The Saint-Étienne Cathedral of Meaux, begun around 1175, displays four centuries of Gothic styles layered: classical, rayonnant, and flamboyant. Five richly sculpted portals frame a central tympanum depicting the Last Judgment. The vaults reach 33 meters.

Inside, look for the 14th-century Crucifixion stained glass window above the choir, the great 15th-century rose window, and Bossuet's tomb—the famous 17th-century bishop and orator nicknamed "the Eagle of Meaux".

The Bossuet Museum occupies the former episcopal palace, built from the 12th to 17th centuries. Its vaulted rooms house a collection of 17th and 18th-century paintings. Wood paneling, paved ramp, and garden views make the site remarkable.

The Bossuet Garden, attributed to Le Nôtre, extends over 8,500 square meters in the shape of an episcopal mitre. Boxwood parterres, a pool, centuries-old linden trees, and views of the Gallo-Roman ramparts make it one of the finest formal gardens in Île-de-France.

The Museum of the Great War of the Pays de Meaux, inaugurated in 2011, is one of the largest museums dedicated to World War I in Europe. It stands on the very spot where the German advance was halted in September 1914.

The Gallo-Roman ramparts, erected in the late 3rd century, line the Boulevard Jean Rose for 250 meters. Their brick chains alternating with small stone courses are characteristic of late Roman architecture.

activities.text: Visit the Maison du Brie de Meaux for a discovery tour: from milk collection to a recreated 19th-century aging cellar. The visit concludes with a tasting of the "king of cheeses".
Walk along the Marne and Ourcq Canal. The Parc naturel du Pâtis, one of the largest urban parks in Île-de-France, offers hiking trails, waterways, and birdwatching.
In summer, the Grand Spectacle historique de Meaux brings together over 500 costumed actors to retrace the town's history outdoors.

days_plan.itin_title: Seine-et-Marne Circuit: Fontainebleau and Royal Châteaux

visits.text: Accessible by gondola, this calvary stands at 1,840 meters on Mont d'Arbois. It offers an exceptional 360° viewpoint over the Mont Blanc massif, where you can clearly distinguish the summit soaring to 4,809 meters—Western Europe's rooftop spread before your eyes.

The chicest resort in the French Alps, Megève has preserved its authentic Savoyard village character. The center clusters with superb wooden chalets sporting flower-laden balconies, while excellent gastronomic restaurants serve Alpine cuisine elevated to an art form.

activities.text: Explore the flower-studded Alpine pastures surrounding Megève. Gentle trails wind through meadows and forests up to working mountain farms. Stop at the farmsteads to taste Beaufort, Tomme de Savoie, and Reblochon where they're made—cheese with a view.

Stroll the cobbled lanes lined with elegant boutiques and discover Savoyard specialties in the gourmet food shops: mountain charcuterie, aged Alpine cheeses, and artisan honey that captures the flavor of wildflower meadows.

days_plan.itin_title: Haute-Savoie Road Trip: Alpine Lakes & Mont Blanc

visits.text: The Château de Mehun-sur-Yèvre was Charles VII's favorite residence — the king who owed his crown to Joan of Arc and died here in 1461, perhaps from starvation following an abscess. Only the north tower and part of the curtain wall remain standing. The tower houses a museum on Charles VII, the Hundred Years' War and the château's construction site, illustrated by the Très Riches Heures du Duc de Berry, where the castle appears in all its splendor.

The château once rivaled Chambord in magnificence. Three wings, watchtowers, chapel, gardens descending to the Yèvre. Charles VII brought his court, artists, scholars here. Agnes Sorel, his favorite, lived here. The Duke of Berry's miniaturists painted the castle around 1410: blue roofs, gilded weathervanes, sculpted galleries. Comparison with the ruins shows the extent of revolutionary destruction.

Mehun is known for its porcelain since 1854. Several workshops still operate, some open for visits. Pillivuyt, the most famous, supplied the Élysée Palace. The Pôle de la Porcelaine (closed Mondays) traces two centuries of manufacturing, displays historic pieces and contemporary creations. You can see potters at work and purchase in the shop.

activities.text: The Saint-Martin Collegiate Church (12th-13th centuries) towers over the Yèvre. Romanesque portal, Gothic nave, 16th-century stained glass. Inside, a beautiful polychrome Entombment and the tomb of Charles VII (the king's remains were transferred to Saint-Denis in the 19th century).

Walk along the Yèvre to see old tanneries, mills, washhouses. The river formed the town's economy: tanneries, fulling mills, paper mills. Mehun once housed up to 500 workers. The banks are pleasant, shaded, with views of the castle ruins.

days_plan.itin_title: Cher Circuit: Romanesque Berry and Jacques-Cœur Route

visits.text: Mende's Notre-Dame-et-Saint-Privat Cathedral dominates the skyline with its mismatched twin towers. Built in 1368 at the request of Pope Urban V—a native son of Gévaudan—it once housed the Non-Pareille, the largest bell in Christendom when cast in 1516. Only its clapper survives, now displayed at the organ's base.

Step into the Gévaudan Museum, spread across 1,200 m² and three floors. Twelve galleries showcase over 500 artifacts spanning prehistory to the present day, organized around two themes: Nature and Culture. The legend of the Beast of Gévaudan is documented here with scholarly rigor.
The medieval Pont Notre-Dame spans the Lot River upstream from the center. Riverside houses sport distinctive upturned boat-hull roofs, a hallmark of Mende's architectural heritage.
The Tour des Pénitents stands as the last remnant of the medieval ramparts. From its summit, views sweep across the old town's slate-topped roofs and frame both cathedral spires.

Visit the old hospital pharmacy, constructed between the 17th and 19th centuries. Its walnut woodwork and apothecary instruments rank it among France's ten finest historic pharmacies. Accessible by guided tour only.

The old town's cobbled lanes reveal themselves along a heritage trail marked by 28 posts. Ancient doorways, fountains on small squares, an 18th-century town hall with a slate roof—pick up a free map at the Tourist Office.

activities.text: Climb Mont Mimat to the summit cross. The orientation table offers a complete panorama: Mende at your feet, the Margeride mountains to the north, the Mende causse plateau to the south. Best at sunset.
Craving thrills? Mende's via ferrata features routes for all levels on cliffs above town. Suspension bridges, ladders, and a zipline punctuate the course.

days_plan.itin_title: Lozère Circuit: Tarn Gorges and Cévennes

places.name: Menerbes

visits.text: Ménerbes is one of the Luberon's most evocative villages: a long rocky spur resembling a stone ship perched on the garrigue, it remains in global imagination thanks to Peter Mayle, who wrote A Year in Provence here in 1989. But before Mayle, there was Picasso—who owned a house here—and Dora Maar, his photographer muse. Wandering the lanes, you'll still find the Maison Dora Maar, a handsome 18th-century dwelling that's now an artists' residence, open for visits on Tuesdays and Saturdays. Don't miss the panorama from the old Roman cemetery: the view over the Luberon to the south and the Vaucluse Mountains to the north is among the village's finest.

The Maison de la Truffe et du Vin du Luberon has occupied the Hôtel d'Astier de Montfaucon since 2004, a 17th-century building classified as a Historic Monument on the Place de l'Horloge. You'll discover over 170 wine references from the Luberon appellation, sold at estate prices, and a shop specializing in truffle products. If you're here in winter, the small black truffle market (tuber melanosporum) on the last Sunday of December is an event in itself.

Ménerbes' Clock Tower and belfry date from the 15th century. From the vaulted passage adjoining it, a view opens onto the plain and neighboring villages. Below the village on the Bonnieux road, the Dolmen de la Pitchoune is one of only two well-preserved dolmens in the Vaucluse, testimony to human occupation since the Neolithic era.

activities.text: Loop trail along the northern slope viewpoints—views over the Petit Luberon and the Calavon plain. The Portalas trail (3.2 km, 1h15) starts from the Bonnieux Cedar Forest and returns to Ménerbes along the ridges.

Descent toward the wine terraces at the foot of the village. The Côtes du Luberon appellation produces typical red and rosé wines here, and some estates welcome visitors for tastings. Call ahead before stopping by.

days_plan.itin_title: The 15 Most Beautiful Villages of the Luberon

map_keywords: Maison de la Truffe et du Vin Ménerbes

visits.text: The Basilique Saint-Michel Archange has dominated the old town since its 17th-century construction: its ochre and yellow facade with two asymmetrical bell towers is Menton's most photographed image. Inside, glittering gold and polychrome marble illustrate Niçoise Baroque in full power. The pebble mosaic pavement is itself a work of art.

Lose yourself in Old Menton: cobbled lanes climbing toward the basilica, pastel facades pressed against each other, turquoise and pale yellow shutters, staircases transforming into shaded squares. The architecture recalls Ligurian villages across the border — Italy is just 2 km from here.

The Musée Jean Cocteau — Séverin Wunderman Collection is one of the French Riviera's most surprising museums: 1,800 works assembled by an American entrepreneur and Cocteau fan in a luminous contemporary building inaugurated in 2011. Cocteau had a strong connection with Menton — he decorated the town hall's marriage room in 1957-1958, just after Villefranche.

Le Bastion, a 17th-century fortress perched directly on the water at the old port entrance, was the first place dedicated to Cocteau's work during his lifetime. He decorated it himself in 1957. The terrace offers a view of the sea and the town — an intimate spot that crowds overlook.
