days_plan.itin_title: Hérault, France: Mediterranean Beaches and Languedoc Wine Country

visits.text: Montpeyroux is one of this circuit's surprises. Classified among France's Most Beautiful Villages, this medieval village is built almost exclusively of arkose—a golden feldspathic sandstone quarried locally, lending it a surprisingly southern air at this latitude. Its name, from Latin mons petrosus, means 'stony mount'—it has been since antiquity. Enter through the 14th-century fortified gate, remnant of the old ramparts, and lose yourself in the calades (lanes paved with cobblestones) winding between winegrowers' houses.

Climb to the 13th-century medieval keep—33 meters of circular tower dominating the village and the Allier valley. It's the last vestige of the fortress that once guarded the seigneury, which passed notably to Madeleine de La Tour d'Auvergne, whose daughter married Lorenzo de Medici—Montpeyroux was thus, via her granddaughter Catherine de Medici and then Queen Margot, a possession linked to French history until 1610. From the summit platform, your gaze carries to the Chaîne des Puys westward and Puy de Dôme, Sancy to the southwest, and the Allier valley below.

15 km from Montpeyroux, make the detour to Issoire and its Saint-Austremoine abbey church—one of Auvergne's five great Romanesque churches and one of the best preserved. Its polychrome interior decoration, restored in the 19th century, blends ochres, earth tones, and lapis in a striking result. The apse capitals illustrate John's Apocalypse with rare narrative force.

activities.text: Local outfitters offer tastings of PDO Côtes d'Auvergne Montpeyroux wines. Vines planted on the sunny slopes of the hill produce mainly gamay for reds and chardonnay for whites. This vineyard, which nearly disappeared after the phylloxera crisis, has since the 2000s seen a renaissance driven by several passionate winemakers.

Wear good shoes for the 'On the arkose path' circuit (8.5 km, 2h30, easy) departing the village and skirting the old arkose quarries, passing near the Allier and Château de Chadieu. Along the way, you'll see outcrops of the blonde rock that 19th-century quarrymen exploited to supply Clermont-Ferrand, Issoire, and even the streets of Paris.

days_plan.itin_title: Auvergne Volcanoes Road Trip: Puy de Dôme and Lakes

visits.text: Saint-Martin Collegiate Church has dominated the village since the 14th century. Its 16th-century Flemish tapestries, classified as Historic Monuments, depict the life of Saint Martin in vivid colors and intricate detail.
Explore the bastide center with its white stone houses. Arcades and covered passages testify to Quercy's medieval architecture.
Discover the collegiate church's side chapels adorned with murals. Liturgical furnishings include carved choir stalls and gilded altarpieces.

activities.text: Stroll cobbled lanes lined with half-timbered houses and dovecotes. The tranquil atmosphere reflects rural Quercy life.
From November to March, visit the truffle market animating the bastide. The Quercy black diamond changes hands beneath the market hall.

days_plan.itin_title: Tarn-et-Garonne Road Trip: Medieval Bastides and Orchard Country

visits.text: Montréal-en-Ardèche is a medieval village perched on the foothills of the Tanargue massif, overlooking the valley below. Its square keep and church are the village's two architectural landmarks, visible from approach roads. Cobbled lanes link the upper village to agricultural terraces below—dry-stone terraces testifying to considerable clearing and development work over several centuries.

From the village's high point, the view over southern ridgelines—Tanargue, Cévennes plateaus, first Cévennes heights—is particularly striking. On clear days, you can make out the silhouettes of Gerbier-de-Jonc and Mont Mézenc to the north. Climb to the keep for the best panorama.

Viewpoints over southern ridgelines are reached by signposted trails from the village. Plan to pick up the hiking map at the tourist office before leaving, as routes can be wet or cluttered depending on season.

activities.text: Take the trail climbing toward the high point behind the keep for a 3 to 4 km round trip offering an extended panorama over southern ridges and the network of old terraces. Figure 1h30 to 2h. The path crosses chestnut groves and follows dry-stone walls characteristic of southern Ardèche.

The short heritage loop within the enclosure and outskirts (2 km) can be done in under an hour. It lets you read the village's medieval layout and spot defensive elements still visible in the built fabric. Access is entirely free.

days_plan.itin_title: The 18 Most Beautiful Villages of Ardèche

visits.text: Montréal-du-Gers is a 13th-century hilltop bastide, listed among France's Most Beautiful Villages. Its central square is bordered by Gothic arcades. The fortified church dominates the village with its square bell tower that served as a keep.

The Gallo-Roman villa of Séviac lies a few kilometers away. This archaeological site presents exceptional mosaics dating from the 4th century. The private baths with their polychrome mosaics are particularly well preserved.

activities.text: Stroll through the cobbled lanes descending from the central square. Houses of honey-colored stone and half-timbering, vaulted passages, and small squares give the village tremendous charm.
The panorama from the church terrace embraces the Gers countryside and Armagnac hills. On clear days, you can glimpse the Pyrenees to the south.

days_plan.itin_title: Haute-Garonne Road Trip: From Toulouse to the Pyrenees

visits.text: Montréjeau is a hilltop bastide dominating the confluence of the Garonne and Neste rivers. Its 19th-century market hall marks the town center. The architecture follows the grid pattern typical of bastides.
The panorama from the terrace embraces the entire Pyrenees chain. On clear days, the view extends from Pic du Midi to the peaks of Spain. It's one of the finest viewpoints over the central Pyrenees.

activities.text: Saturday morning market draws people from across the region. You'll find mountain products and Pyrenean specialties.
From Montréjeau, Saint-Bertrand-de-Comminges is visible in the distance on its rocky outcrop. The road leading there crosses landscapes of hills with the Pyrenees as backdrop.

days_plan.itin_title: Haute-Garonne Road Trip: From Toulouse to the Pyrenees

visits.text: Three kilometers of perfectly preserved ramparts encircle the upper town. This tree-lined promenade offers magnificent views across the Canche Valley.
The star-shaped citadel, built in the 16th century atop a Philippe Auguste castle, commands the valley from 40 meters up. It houses the Roger-Rodière Museum: sacred art and medieval sculptures.
Saint-Saulve Abbey Church dates from the 12th century and holds one of northern France's finest sacred art treasures. Europe's oldest crosier, from the 7th century, is displayed here.
Wander cobbled medieval lanes: Rue du Clape-en-Bas, Cavée Saint-Firmin. Half-timbered houses, blonde stone, and red brick tell a thousand-year story.
The Boulogne Gate, rebuilt in 1828, is a majestic vestige of military architecture. It marks the northern entrance to the fortified town.

activities.text: Descend via the Poterne Tower for an 8-kilometer rampart-base walk. Cross countryside and discover the lower town.
Victor Hugo stayed in Montreuil in 1837 and set part of Les Misérables here. The town still inspires with its timeless medieval atmosphere.
Explore 18th-century townhouses. Nearly 40 exceptional residences testify to the economic prosperity that transformed the urban landscape.

days_plan.itin_title: Hauts-de-France: Opal Coast Cliffs, Medieval History & Great War Sites
Maine-et-Loire Road Trip: Anjou and Loire Valley Châteaux
Seine-Saint-Denis Circuit: Royal Basilica and Urban Culture
Pas-de-Calais Road Trip: Opal Coast & Historic Belfries

Bishops' Palace.
