Cathar history.

days_plan.itin_title: Ariège Pyrenees Road Trip, France: Cathar Castles & Prehistoric Cave Art

visits.text: Pass through the Porte de Bourgogne, remnant of the 1,400 meters of fortifications erected by Philippe Auguste in the 12th century. Its defensive systems—portcullis, murder hole, and machicolations—remain visible.

The bridge over the Loing, immortalized by the Impressionist painter who lived here his last twenty years, offers one of the most photographed views in town. Enameled lava plaques mark the exact spots where he set up his easel.
Notre-Dame Church, Gothic from the 13th to 15th centuries, was painted fourteen times by the same artist. Its two-tower facade frames a richly sculpted portal.
The keep, remnant of the royal castle, has dominated the town since the 12th century. The ramparts still preserve several turrets and the Porte de Samois, the town's second fortified entrance.
Step into the Maison du Sucre d'Orge (Barley Sugar House). This confection, created in 1638 by Benedictine nuns, has been made here for nearly four centuries using a closely guarded secret recipe.
The so-called François I Gallery, dating from 1527, features a Renaissance facade adorned with detailed sculptures. Ancient facades line the town's cobbled streets.

activities.text: Follow the "In Sisley's Footsteps" trail: a route marked by enameled plaques at the locations where the artist set up his easel. Reproductions of his paintings are visible on-site.
Cycle along the banks of the Loing. A bike path connects Moret-sur-Loing to Souppes-sur-Loing following the Loing Canal towpath.
Visit the Halle de Moret-sur-Loing on market days to taste local Brie specialties: valley cheeses, forest honey, and barley sugar drops.

days_plan.itin_title: Seine-et-Marne Circuit: Fontainebleau and Royal Châteaux

places.name: Mortagne Au Perche

visits.text: The Saint-André Crypt is all that remains of the old Toussaint collegiate church, founded by Countess Mahaut of Bavaria around 1200. Its low vaults and carved capitals evoke the medieval Perche.
The Church of Notre-Dame, in Flamboyant Gothic style, was built with support from Marguerite of Lorraine in the late 15th century. Its 18th-century woodwork frames a luminous interior.
Climb to the Porte Saint-Denis, remnant of the old fortifications. It houses local history collections: Percheron costumes, Gallo-Roman mosaics, and commemorative medals.
The Saint-François Cloister and Chapel, built by Marguerite of Lorraine in the 16th century, form a well-preserved monastic ensemble at the heart of the town.

The Alain Museum, housed in the former residence of the Counts of Perche, pays tribute to philosopher Émile-Auguste Chartier, known as Alain, a native of Mortagne. Manuscripts, photographs, and personal items trace his life.
Stroll the formal gardens of the town hall, planted in the French style. The surrounding cobbled lanes reveal handsome limestone houses in shades of rose and ochre.

activities.text: Mortagne-au-Perche is the capital of black pudding. Visit an artisan butcher to discover variations: farm-style, with apples, with walnuts, with chili. The Brotherhood of Knights of the Black Pudding has upheld the tradition since 1963.
The Saturday morning market sets up in the square. Percheron regional products, cheeses, and farmhouse cider fill the stalls.
Want fresh air? Trails around Mortagne wind through the Percheron bocage. Gentle hills, hedgerows, and stone manors punctuate the route.
A few miles away, stud farms offer encounters with the Percheron, the region's iconic draft horse breed. Visits, rides, or introductory sessions are available.

days_plan.itin_title: Orne Road Trip: Norman Bocage and Prestigious Stud Farms

map_keywords: Jardin de l'Hôtel de Ville Mortagne-au-Perche

visits.text: The Saut du Doubs is a 27-meter waterfall on the Franco-Swiss border, classified as a Grand National Site. Created 12,000 to 14,000 years ago by a landslide, it marks the end of Lake Brenets (or Lake Chaillexon). The Basins of the Doubs that precede it form a 3.5 km long canyon, 100-200 m wide and 30-40 m deep, bordered by high limestone cliffs with sometimes strange shapes. The level varies by nearly 20 meters between floods and low waters. The site attracts 200,000 visitors per year.

Morteau is the birthplace of the eponymous sausage, protected by PGI since 2010 and Label Rouge since 2006. Its cylindrical shape with wooden peg is recognizable among all. Smoking with coniferous wood (fir, spruce, juniper) in traditional tuyés is inscribed on UNESCO's intangible heritage. Production reaches approximately 5,000 tons per year. The town, labeled Cité de Caractère, preserves beautiful monuments including the Town Hall (1590) and the Renaissance-facade Château Pertusier.

The Pays Horloger saw the birth of the French watchmaking industry in the mid-18th century. This know-how is inscribed on UNESCO's intangible cultural heritage. The watchmaking museum, installed in Château Pertusier, is currently closed for renovation and will reopen in 2026 under the name Cité des Horlogers with new exhibition spaces.

activities.text: Two boat companies offer commented one-hour cruises to the Saut du Doubs from Villers-le-Lac. You can also access it on foot (2.5 km from Vions parking), by carriage from La Courpée, or by car to the viewpoint. The sporty hike to the Châtelot dam offers superb views of the gorges.

Visit a tuyé farm to discover traditional Comtoise architecture and the meat smoking process. These buildings with large roofs house a central room with a chimney that can reach 15 meters where coniferous smoke slowly perfumes sausages, bresi, and hams for several weeks. The Ferme-musée du Beugnon in Grand'Combe-Châteleu presents this heritage.

days_plan.itin_title: Doubs Circuit: Watchmaking and Swiss Border

visits.text: The old village of Mougins sits perched at 260 meters above sea level between Cannes and Grasse, its narrow lanes spiraling around the bell tower—a defensive design inherited from medieval times. Explore on foot in the morning before restaurants open and terraces fill: the flower-covered facades, fountains, and art galleries belong entirely to you. At the village entrance, a monumental 500-kg sculpture of Picasso's head greets visitors—a reminder that the cubist master lived here from 1961 to 1973 at Mas Notre-Dame-de-Vie, until his death on April 8, 1973.

The Mougins Photography Centre occupies the former presbytery since 2020, continuing the legacy of the André Villers Photography Museum and preserving its photographic collection. André Villers was Picasso's friend and portraitist—he photographed the master throughout his Mougins years and received as a gift Picasso's famous Rolleiflex camera, which is displayed here. Temporary exhibitions rotate regularly—check the program before visiting.

If you're passionate about art, head to FAMM (Women Artists Museum Mougins), Europe's first private museum entirely dedicated to female artists: works span from the 17th century to present day. The building is recent, the collection ambitious. Meanwhile, the more than 30 galleries and art studios scattered through the lanes make this a true gallery-village.

From April to October, climb to the top of the bell tower for a 360° view from over 300 meters altitude: Cannes Bay, the Lérins Islands, the Grasse hills, and the snow-capped Pre-Alps in the background. It's a panorama that Mougins locals simply call "the view"—which says everything. In summer, nighttime tower visits are organized.

activities.text: The "Étoiles de Mougins" festival, created in 2006 to honor chef Roger Vergé who opened his Moulin de Mougins in 1969 and brought the village to 7 Michelin stars by 1992, takes place every September. Demonstrations, workshops, and culinary competitions animate the lanes for an entire weekend. If you're here during this period, book several months ahead—the event sells out very early.

Stroll around the Fontmerle pond in Valmasque park (427 hectares of preserved nature between Mougins and Sophia Antipolis): in summer, blooming lotus flowers create an unexpected spectacle that few visitors know about. A marked trail circles the pond in an easy hour's walk.

Mougins gastronomy is no myth—even the village's more accessible establishments work with quality local produce. Take time for lunch on a terrace in the lanes before leaving: the sunshine cuisine dear to Roger Vergé, blending Provençal and Mediterranean influences, has left a lasting imprint on every restaurant in the village.

days_plan.itin_title: Nice Hinterland Circuit: Perched Villages and Mountain Trails

visits.text: The CNCS (National Center for Stage Costume and Scenography) displays over 10,000 costumes in an exceptional collection illustrating three centuries of performing arts. Former 18th-century barracks transformed into a modern museum present costumes worn by Nureyev, Pavlova or Caruso. Temporary exhibitions unveil backstage secrets and creations by major designers like Christian Lacroix and Yves Saint Laurent.

The Notre-Dame Cathedral, built between 15th and 19th centuries, shelters a masterpiece: the Moulins Master Triptych (circa 1498-1499). This flamboyant Gothic painting depicting the Virgin in Glory with donors Pierre II of Bourbon and Anne of France ranks among French Gothic art masterpieces. The central panel dazzles with colors and refined details characteristic of Franco-Flemish style.

The Anne de Beaujeu Museum occupies a ducal pavilion (late 15th century), testimony to the Dukes of Bourbon's power. Archaeological collections trace the region from prehistory to Middle Ages. The fine arts section displays paintings from 15th to 20th centuries including works by the Moulins Masters. Decorative arts reveal ceramic, sculpture and furniture treasures.

The Jacquemart belfry (15th century) stands out with its 46-meter-high square tower topped by a wrought-iron campanile. Since 1651, three jacks strike the hours: Jacquemart the father, his wife Jacquette added in 1655, and their children in 1774. Climb the 106 steps to enjoy a panorama over the city and Allier valley.

The old Moulins unfolds its cobblestone streets between half-timbered houses and Renaissance mansions. Rue d'Allier and Place d'Allier concentrate bourgeois facades from the 15th to 18th centuries. The Mal Coiffée tower, remnant of the ducal castle razed by Richelieu, overlooks the city from its 38 meters.

activities.text: Stroll along Allier quays converted into a promenade with playgrounds and green spaces. Pedestrian paths invite riverside walks with views of the river and surrounding nature. The proximity to city center allows combining urban visits and outdoor relaxation.

The Saturday morning market fills Place d'Allier with stalls offering regional products, Bourbonnais cheeses, farm poultry and seasonal vegetables. Local producers present their specialties while craftsmen exhibit traditional know-how. The atmosphere is lively and authentic.

days_plan.itin_title: Allier Road Trip: Vichy, Thermal Baths and Bourbonnais Bocage

visits.text: Mouriès is France's premier olive-growing commune by number of trees: 91,000 trees spread across the Alpilles' south slopes. The Mouriès cooperative mill, whose origins go back to the 17th century (the primitive mill called Mas-Neuf dates from this period), produces a PDO Vallée des Baux-de-Provence oil regularly award-winning. In November, it's a family celebration for 450 members bringing their harvests: weighing, de-leafing, washing, then pressing within 24 hours.

The village center — a few small squares linked by limestone alleys, a fountain, the 17th-century church — looks unassuming but conceals flowery back courtyards and carefully restored manor houses. It's also a bull-running village: its arena hosts Camargue-style races in summer, a discipline where men armed with hooks face bulls (without killing).

activities.text: Eastern Alpilles ridge trails: from Mouriès' north slope, marked trails climb toward limestone ridges for magnificent views toward the Crau and Camargue. Fire risk may close these trails from mid-July to mid-September. Inquire before departing.

Olive oil tasting and purchase directly at the mill: the Moulin Coopératif de Mouriès offers visits and direct sales. It's an opportunity to understand differences between typicities and varieties — and leave with a PDO oil that Parisian restaurants snap up.

days_plan.itin_title: Alpilles, Aix-en-Provence & Camargue Circuit

visits.text: Look up as soon as you arrive in Moustiers: between the two limestone cliffs that squeeze the village, a golden star gleams at the end of a 135-meter chain. Popular legend — recounted by Frédéric Mistral in 1885 — attributes this ex-voto to the knight of Blacas, taken prisoner during the Crusades in 1210 and freed after promising to suspend a star for the Virgin if he returned alive. The mystery remains unsolved. In any case, the star you see dates from 1957, after the previous one fell. The village, classified among France's Most Beautiful Villages since 1983, also takes its name from its founding monastery: monks from Lérins settled here in the 5th century.

The church of Notre-Dame-de-l'Assomption is an often-underestimated Romanesque gem. Its 12th-century Lombard bell tower, classified as a Historic Monument since 1913, dominates the village with its sturdy silhouette. Inside, a five-bay nave with pointed barrel vaults and an altar carved from a 5th-century sarcophagus — one of the rare examples of reusing an early Christian piece in a medieval Provençal building.

The Musée de la Faïence presents more than 300 pieces and traces five centuries of an art that made Moustiers famous throughout Europe. It all began in 1668 when Pierre Clérissy obtained the secret of white enamel from a monk at Lérins Abbey — legend has it that Louis XIV's financial crisis, forcing him to melt down his gold tableware, opened the market for luxury faience. The Moustiers workshops subsequently graced the tables of the kingdom's elite. Today, workshops perpetuate the craft in the village center — push open doors when they're open to watch the artisans at work.

activities.text: Climb the 262 steps of the calade (paved path) leading to the Notre-Dame-de-Beauvoir chapel. This chapel blends 12th-century Romanesque with 16th-century Gothic — it was formerly called Notre-Dame-d'Entreroches, 'between the rocks'. Along the way of the cross, the stations are decorated with faience tiles. From the esplanade, the panorama embraces all of Moustiers' rooftops, Lake Sainte-Croix below, and the first buttresses of the Verdon. Avoid the climb at midday in summer — the slope is exposed. Coming back down, take the alternate path passing by the Sainte-Madeleine grotto.

Friday mornings, the market sets up on Place Montelupo, opposite the faience museum. It's a chance to leave with Haute-Provence honey, herb-marinated olives, fresh goat cheese, or a Provençal kougelhopf. Then spend the morning exploring the faience workshops open as boutiques — some offer demonstrations of wheel-throwing.

Providers based 5 km away on the shores of Lake Sainte-Croix offer canoe rentals to paddle up the water corridor at the entrance to the Verdon gorges — a unique experience where the water transitions from the blue-green of the lake to the emerald tint of the canyon. Allow half a day for the round trip.

days_plan.itin_title: Alpes-De-Haute-Provence Circuit: Lavender and Perched Villages
The 10 Most Beautiful Villages of the Haut-Var
Forcalquier & Verdon Gorges Road Trip
Lake Sainte-Croix and Verdon Circuit
France's Most Beautiful Villages: Alps to Luberon in 7 Days
Alpine Hinterland and Verdon Gorges: From Thorenc to Riez

places.name: Mouzon

visits.text: Abbey Church of Notre-Dame de Mouzon. This 13th-century Gothic jewel stands proudly in the village center, testament to medieval religious importance. The harmonious architecture combines purity of line with elegance of proportion in the tradition of great Champagne cathedrals. Ancient stained glass bathes the nave in colored light while the 17th-century Baroque organ resonates during concerts.

Spanish House. This 16th-century half-timbered dwelling evokes the Hispanic presence in the region when Mouzon belonged to the Spanish Netherlands. Ornate corbels and carved beams testify to the skill of period builders. The picturesque facade illustrates preserved Ardennes civil architecture.

Felt and Wool Museum. The museum traces local textile industry history through antique machinery and demonstrations of artisan techniques. Collections present the evolution of felting from its origins to modern applications. Workshops offer hands-on introduction to working with wool and felt.

activities.text: Hike through the fortifications. Follow trails that traverse remnants of the old Vauban ramparts that once encircled the town. Bastions, fortified gates, and glacis offer tangible testimony to the military architecture of the Grand Siècle. Educational panels explain the defensive system of this strategic frontier stronghold.

Cycle tour of the fortifications. A cycling route reveals the entire defensive system by following the trace of former walls. Dried moats, bridges, and monumental gates punctuate a pleasant green circuit. The star-shaped configuration of bastions appears particularly well from the heights.

Discover military urbanism. Rectilinear streets and the central parade ground illustrate the rigorous planning of fortified towns. Observe how civil architecture adapts to military constraints with aligned row houses. The built heritage reveals the methodical organization of the garrison-city.

days_plan.itin_title: Ardennes Road Trip: Forests & Fortresses (9 Days)

map_keywords: Musée du Feutre et de la Laine Mouzon

visits.text: Welcome to the geographical center of the Monastic Cross of the Vosges. Founded in 671 by Saint Hydulphe, chorepiscopus of Trier who came to live as a hermit in this wooded valley, the abbey took the name Medianum Monasterium — the monastery in the middle — because it stands exactly at the crossroads of the four other abbeys founded at the same time. The current buildings are a baroque masterpiece built in ten years by the Benedictine architect Ambroise Pierson, inaugurated in 1776. Inside, the church has cathedral dimensions but total sobriety — the contrast with the furnishings is striking. Don't miss the choir stalls from 1698: 36 seats whose carved misericords show faces, and the backs are decorated with cloister perspectives painted in trompe-l'œil. The church is open from 10 am to 5 pm.

At the old abbey cemetery, the Saint-Grégoire Oratory preserves the 8th-century Vosges pink sandstone sarcophagus believed to have been the first tomb of Saint Hydulphe — founder of the abbey who died in the early 8th century. From the hill, you have a view of the entire abbey complex and the Rabodeau. Allow 20 minutes, it's short but frames the day well.

activities.text: Stroll through the 4 hectares of gardens surrounding the abbey — for two centuries, industrial buildings had hidden the facade and no one could see them. Their recent clearance has changed everything. Then walk along the Rabodeau to the east: you'll see remnants of the abbey's enclosure wall, an old gate and a corner tower half collapsed. Guided tours are offered on the third Saturday of each month from April to October, by reservation at the Senones Tourist Office.

days_plan.itin_title: Vosges Abbey Trail: Monastic Heritage Unveiled

places.name: Mulhouse

visits.text: Welcome to the city of Bollwerk — and to Alsace's most magical season. Mulhouse's Christmas market, now in its 34th year, spans four historic squares in the city center: Place de la Réunion (its beating heart with 91 wooden chalets), Place des Victoires, Place des Cordiers, and Place de la Concorde. What sets Mulhouse apart from all of Alsace? Its Christmas fabric — a unique textile created specially each year for the event, draping façades, chalets, and garlands in homage to the city's glorious textile heritage. Wander around the Hallebardier fountain, warm yourself with spiced mulled wine, and treat yourself to bredeles (little Alsatian Christmas cookies) and manalas (brioche shaped like little men).

Before or after the market, step inside the Musée de l'Impression sur Étoffes, housed in a 19th-century building. Its collections delve into Mulhouse's industrial soul: 6 million textile samples and 50,000 documents assembled since 1833 by industrialists of the Société Industrielle de Mulhouse. If you visit during the Christmas market, the museum hosts its own indoor market featuring fabric gift ideas — a tradition within the tradition. Compare notes at the Christmas tree on Place des Victoires, decorated with fabric spheres created by Marie-Jo Gebel, before catching the sound-and-light show projected every evening from 5:30 to 8:30 p.m. on the façade of the Société Industrielle de Mulhouse.

For automobile enthusiasts (and everyone else), the Cité de l'Automobile — National Museum, installed in a former textile mill, houses quite simply the world's largest car collection: 450 vehicles displayed across 25,000 m², including the legendary Schlumpf collection with its Bugatti Royales. Allow at least 2 hours. In December, the museum occasionally organizes concerts in its halls in partnership with the conservatory — inquire at the entrance.

activities.text: Board the little Christmas train that winds through Mulhouse's new pedestrian district — a €4 ride that delights children and offers views of the illuminations from a different angle. In the evening, linger at Place de la Réunion to admire the Hallebardier fountain transformed into a recreation of medieval Mulhouse and the illuminated gear wheels — symbol of the city — that light up at nightfall.

Stop by the Boutique aux Étoffes on the ground floor of the Hôtel de Ville at Place de la Réunion to take home a piece of this year's Christmas fabric — each collection is unique and becomes a collector's item. Handcrafted products made with the year's fabric by some twenty local artisans. It's one of the most distinctive souvenirs along the entire Christmas Market Route.

days_plan.itin_title: Alsace's Most Beautiful Christmas Markets
Upper Rhine Road Trip: Colmar and the Southern Wine Route

map_keywords: Marché de Noël Place de la Réunion Mulhouse
Musée de l'Impression sur Étoffes Mulhouse

visits.text: The Munster Cheese House traces the birth and making of eastern France's only AOP cheese. The story begins in the 7th century when Benedictine monks founded an abbey in the valley — the Monasterium Confluentis — which gave its name to the town, the valley, and the cheese. This soft-paste, washed-rind cheese, rubbed by hand every two days during a 14 to 21-day aging period, has a pronounced nose but remains mild on the palate. Don't miss the guided tasting with a glass of Gewurztraminer — the classic pairing.

Grand Ballon rises to 1,424 meters — the highest point in the Vosges massif. On clear days, the view from the summit extends across the Alsace plain, the Rhine, Germany's Black Forest, and sometimes the Swiss Alps. Don't miss the Blue Devils Monument at the summit, honoring the chasseurs alpins who fell during the Great War on these ridges.

Descending via the Route des Crêtes, pause at the Col de la Schlucht at 1,139 meters. This road was built in 1915 for military purposes to supply troops while staying out of range of German lines — it runs for 73 kilometers from Sainte-Marie-aux-Mines to Cernay, mostly above 1,000 meters elevation. The panoramas of the Alsace plain to the east and Lorraine valleys to the west change with every bend.

Eguisheim, which you'll glimpse from the ridges, will be your next stop tomorrow. But if you have time this evening, take a first stroll through its concentric lanes in late daylight — the golden light on the half-timbered façades is particularly beautiful in early evening.

activities.text: The marcaire meal at a high-altitude farm inn is one of the Upper Rhine's most emblematic culinary experiences. The traditional menu includes soup, valley tourte, sautéed potatoes (röigebrageldi in local dialect), hot Munster, and blueberry tart. Farmhouses in the upland pastures offer these meals typically between 11 a.m. and 3 p.m. Reservations required on weekends.

Hike to the ruins of the Three Castles that dominate Eguisheim from their ridge. The castles — Wahlenbourg, Dagsbourg, and Weckmund — were built in the 11th and 12th centuries by the Counts of Eguisheim. The climb from the village takes 30 to 45 minutes depending on your chosen path and offers a plunging view over the vineyards below.

days_plan.itin_title: The 15 Most Beautiful Villages of Alsace
Alsace's Most Beautiful Christmas Markets
Upper Rhine Road Trip: Colmar and the Southern Wine Route

visits.text: As you arrive in Murat, look up: three basalt rocks aligned along a single fault rise above the houses. These are three ancient Cantal volcanic plugs from eruptions 3.6 million years ago — the Bonnevie, Bredons, and Chastel rocks. Bonnevie towers 140 meters over the town with some of Europe's finest basalt organ pipes, now crowned by an 8-meter cast-iron Virgin erected in 1878. This is where the imposing fortress of the Viscounts of Murat once stood — in the 12th century, the viscounty included 25 fortified castles, 29 châteaux, 59 villages, and 110 fiefs. In 1633, Cardinal Richelieu spent six months razing it to the ground. Wander the medieval lanes between basalt and phonolite houses, following the circuit of 26 heritage interpretation panels.

Don't miss Saint-Pierre de Bredons church, perched on its rock 1.5 km from the town centre: a Benedictine priory founded around 1050 by monks from Moissac, a jewel of Cantal Romanesque architecture with its gilded wooden altarpieces and billet-moulding portal. The view over the Alagnon valley from the esplanade is spectacular. The climb from below takes 20 minutes on foot via a paved path.

The Collégiale Notre-Dame des Oliviers in the heart of Murat dates from 1380 — its construction was authorized by the Bishop of Saint-Flour after a lightning-caused fire in 1493 destroyed and rebuilt it. Its 19th-century bulb tower stands out in the Auvergnat landscape. Inside, look for the Virgin and Child that Saint Louis supposedly brought back from the Holy Land. The Maison de la Faune, a 16th-century townhouse, is also open for visits in season.

activities.text: Climb to the top of Rocher de Bonnevie via the trail from the tourist office: allow 30 minutes one way to reach the Virgin and the basalt organ pipes. From the summit, the view of Murat terraced in an amphitheatre and the Cantal valleys is one of the region's most spectacular. The path passes the site of the former razed castle — from up here, you understand better why it took Richelieu six months to demolish this fortress.

Murat is served by the Aurillac-Clermont-Ferrand railway line. In the evening, a walk through the old town's doorways, mascarons, and former shops reveals Renaissance architectural details often overlooked by hurried visitors. The Des Danses et Musiques du Monde festival, held every year in early August, transforms the town into an open-air stage for several days.

days_plan.itin_title: Cantal Road Trip: Auvergne Volcanoes and Mountain Traditions
Auvergne's Volcanic Villages Trail

visits.text: Muret's old town preserves half-timbered houses and some medieval buildings. The 15th-century church of Saint-Jacques has a bell-gable typical of the Southwest.
The Battle of Muret in 1213 was decisive in the Albigensian Crusade. A memorial commemorates this event that marked the region's history. The site lies near the Garonne.

activities.text: The banks of the Garonne in Muret are landscaped for walking. It's a peaceful place to stroll along the river.
Tuesday and Saturday morning markets take place in the town center. You'll find products from the Toulouse region.

days_plan.itin_title: Haute-Garonne Road Trip: From Toulouse to the Pyrenees

visits.text: Murol Castle rises on a basalt pinnacle at nearly 1,000 meters altitude—it commands a single sweeping view of Lake Chambon, the valley, and Sancy. Built from the 12th century, enlarged and modified until the 15th century, it was classified a Historic Monument in 1889, which halted the systematic pillaging of its stones by surrounding villagers. Guy de Maupassant left a striking description in his story Humble Drame (1883). Today, costumed tours bring to life the world of Lord Guillaume de Murol in the 15th century, whose account book, preserved for 600 years, reveals with precision the daily life of the fortress.

Head down to Saint-Nectaire, a thermal village nestled in the valley a few kilometers away. Its 12th-century Romanesque church, perched on its volcanic knoll, is one of Auvergne's five great Romanesque churches. Its historiated capitals inside are among the region's finest. And don't leave without visiting a cheese dairy: Saint-Nectaire is one of the rare PDO cheeses still largely farm-produced, aged on regional rye straw in natural caves.

At Saint-Nectaire, the Grottes de Jonas constitute a unique curiosity: a medieval troglodyte village carved into a volcanic phonolite cliff face, inhabited from the 11th to 19th centuries. You'll visit a chapel hewn from rock with painted frescoes, a guardroom, and monks' cells. The atmosphere is strange and fascinating.

activities.text: In summer, Murol castle's torchlight evening shows—July and August, Sunday through Thursday from 8:30 PM—let you tour the fortress by candlelight in the company of court characters. Book ahead: places are limited and shows often sell out.

Climb to the summit of Tartaret volcano (957 m), the most recent volcano in the Chaîne des Puys—it erupted just 5,000 to 8,000 years ago, blocking the Couze river and thus creating Lake Chambon. From its summit, you embrace the lake in one sweeping glance, Murol castle, and the Sancy ridges.

days_plan.itin_title: Auvergne Volcanoes Road Trip: Puy de Dôme and Lakes

visits.text: At 500 meters altitude in the Vaucluse Mountains, Murs is a welcome antidote to the tourist bustle of Gordes, just 8 km away. Its château has dominated the village since the 12th century—originally built by the d'Agoult family, expanded between the 15th and 18th centuries. It's private and not open to visitors, but the facade with its round towers, weathered walls, and wrought-iron gate bearing the Astouaud coat of arms merits a stop. The village is also the birthplace of Le Brave Crillon, Henri IV's companion in arms, born in 1543 during his mother's visit to the lord of Murs. His 13th-century house is still visible in the lanes.

Stroll through the village center lanes to spot the fountains with unusual names (fox fountain, sheep fountain) and the numerous bories catalogued by the Luberon Park. On the northern edge of the territory, the Mur de la Peste is a 25-km dry-stone sanitary line built in four months in 1721 to stop the epidemic coming from Marseille. A marked hiking trail of 7.5 km follows it.

activities.text: Ridge loop toward the valleys and isolated farms of the karst plateau. The Gorges de Véroncle, a few kilometers away, are an exceptional natural site with ruined watermills and waterfalls—a hike off the usual tourist paths (2h round trip).
Circuit of oratories on the edge of the garrigue (approximately 1h). Murs has several old oratories and wayside crosses scattered in the countryside, witnesses to preserved rural heritage.

days_plan.itin_title: The 15 Most Beautiful Villages of the Luberon

places.name: Musee Art Moderne Paris

visits.text: The Musée d'Art Moderne de Paris—free permanent admission—occupies the east wing of the Palais de Tokyo, built for the 1937 International Exposition. Its collections cover the major movements of the 20th century: Fauvism, Cubism, abstract art, Nouveau Réalisme, through to contemporary creations. The highlight of the visit: Raoul Dufy's La Fée Électricité (The Electricity Fairy) from 1937, a 600 m² fresco installed in a large ground-floor room—the world's largest painting, commissioned by the Parisian Electricity Distribution Company for the 1937 Expo. Allow 90 minutes to 2 hours.

activities.text: Steps away, the Palais de Tokyo hosts Paris's most experimental contemporary art exhibitions—regularly renewed programs, often open until midnight. The terrace of its restaurant with views of the Eiffel Tower is one of the best vantage points from the Trocadéro.

days_plan.itin_title: The Great Museums of Paris

map_keywords: Fée Électricité Musée d'Art Moderne Paris

visits.text: The Musée des Arts Décoratifs occupies the Rohan wing of the Louvre palace, on the Rue de Rivoli side. Its collection spans seven centuries of French decorative arts: from medieval furniture to Hector Guimard's Art Nouveau interiors, passing through 18th-century table arts and Belle Époque posters. An entire section is devoted to fashion and textiles—more than 100,000 pieces of clothing. Less crowded than its neighbors, it offers a more peaceful visit just meters from the Louvre. Allow 90 minutes.

activities.text: After the museum, the arcaded Rue de Rivoli leads you directly to the Palais Royal—its interior gardens, open to the public, are one of the best-kept secrets of the 1st arrondissement: a tree-lined courtyard surrounded by shopping galleries, sheltered from the noise of the city.

days_plan.itin_title: The Great Museums of Paris

places.name: Musee Arts Metiers Paris

visits.text: The Musée des Arts et Métiers, installed since 1794 in the former Saint-Martin-des-Champs priory, is one of Paris's strangest and most fascinating museums. At the center of the large choir hall, hanging from the vault: Foucault's pendulum—the original pendulum that demonstrated the Earth's rotation at the Panthéon in 1851, transferred here after physicist Léon Foucault's death. On the nave walls, Clément Ader's Avion III—the first airplane to take off in 1897, three years before the Wright brothers. The collection traces the history of technical and scientific inventions since the 17th century: automata, steam engines, measuring instruments. A museum for the curious of all ages. Allow 2 hours.

activities.text: The museum is two minutes' walk from the Marché des Enfants Rouges—visiting both in the same half-day is natural. The Marais neighborhood around Rue de Bretagne deserves a stroll: art galleries, designer boutiques, and cafés have succeeded one another here since the neighborhood's rehabilitation in the 1980s.

days_plan.itin_title: The Great Museums of Paris

map_keywords: Musée des Arts et Métiers Paris
Pendule de Foucault Arts et Métiers Paris
Avion Clément Ader Arts et Métiers Paris

places.name: Musee Cluny

visits.text: Start with the Musée de Cluny, nestled in the heart of the Latin Quarter within a Gothic mansion built in 1510 by Burgundian monks from Cluny Abbey—on the ruins of Gallo-Roman baths whose remnants you'll still see on the façade. The highlight of your visit: the six Lady and the Unicorn tapestries, woven around 1500 and rediscovered by chance by Prosper Mérimée and George Sand in 1841 in a château in Creuse. Five tapestries illustrate the five senses; the sixth, titled "À mon seul désir" (To my only desire), remains the most debated enigma in French medieval art. Allow 90 minutes for a leisurely visit—avoid Wednesday mornings, which are packed with school groups.

activities.text: After Cluny, wander through the narrow streets of the Latin Quarter around Place de la Sorbonne—the museum sits just steps from the main entrance of the university founded in 1257. The bouquinistes (used-book sellers) along Boulevard Saint-Michel and the nearby Fontaine Saint-Michel are worth a detour before heading to your next museum.

days_plan.itin_title: The Great Museums of Paris

map_keywords: Dame à la Licorne Musée de Cluny

places.name: Musee Guimet

visits.text: The Musée Guimet, created by Lyon industrialist Émile Guimet in 1879 and donated to the state in 1888, is one of the world's largest Asian art museums outside Asia. Its 45,000 works cover India, China, Japan, Southeast Asia, Central Asia, and Afghanistan, from prehistory to the 20th century. Don't miss the Buddhist rotunda at the museum's heart and the Khmer collections from Angkor—among the most complete outside Cambodia. The Hôtel d'Heidelbach annex, 200 meters away, houses Japanese arts in reconstructed salons. Allow 2 hours.

activities.text: You're steps from the Trocadéro—the terrace of Palais de Chaillot offers the most frontal view possible of the Eiffel Tower, and it's photographers' favorite spot at sunrise and sunset. Descend the stairs to the Champ-de-Mars if you want to get closer.

days_plan.itin_title: The Great Museums of Paris

map_keywords: Musée national arts asiatiques Guimet Paris

visits.text: The Louvre is the world's largest art museum: 72,735 m² of rooms and galleries, 35,000 works on display out of 460,000 in total—spanning from prehistory to 1848, from Europe to Iran via Egypt. The building itself is a 12th-century fortress transformed into a royal palace over 800 years before opening as a museum on August 10, 1793, with 537 paintings. I.M. Pei's glass pyramid, inaugurated in 1989, sparked as much scandal as the Eiffel Tower did in its day. Come with a strategy: in 2 hours, you'll see the Mona Lisa, Venus de Milo, and Winged Victory of Samothrace. In a full day, you'll traverse the Egyptian antiquities and the painting department. Arrive at opening time (9 a.m.) for the Mona Lisa—an average of 15,000 people visit the museum each day.

activities.text: After the Louvre, stroll through the Jardin des Tuileries, which Napoleon III had landscape architect Édouard André design—the garden connects the museum to Place de la Concorde over 1 km of perspective. Modern sculptures scattered throughout the pathways serve as an open-air museum.

days_plan.itin_title: The Great Museums of Paris

visits.text: The Musée de Montmartre occupies the oldest house in the neighborhood: a 17th-century building where Renoir, Suzanne Valadon, Maurice Utrillo, and Raoul Dufy all lived in turn. The museum traces the artistic history of the Butte since the 19th century—posters by Toulouse-Lautrec, paintings by the Nabis, photos of Pigalle's nightlife. The Renoir Gardens, recently restored according to period documents, recreate the garden that Renoir painted in several of his canvases between 1875 and 1877. In summer, the view from the terrace over the Montmartre vineyards is one of the gentlest in Paris.

activities.text: Montmartre is best explored on foot: Place du Tertre with its street painters, the Sacré-Coeur whose dome marks the horizon from almost everywhere in Paris, Rue Lepic and its food shops. If you're there on Saturday morning, the market on Rue Lepic is one of the liveliest in northern Paris.

days_plan.itin_title: The Great Museums of Paris

visits.text: The Orangerie is one of Paris's smallest museums—and one of its most mesmerizing. On the ground floor, two oval rooms built according to Monet's own plans house the eight Water Lilies panels, given to France in the aftermath of the November 11, 1918 armistice. Each panel measures 1.97 m tall; the total length of the canvases installed here reaches 91 meters. Natural zenithal light, filtered through a fabric veil according to the painter's instructions, makes the colors vibrate with the rhythm of the sky. André Masson called this ensemble the "Sistine Chapel of Impressionism." Come on an overcast day if possible—diffuse light works better than direct sun. In the basement, the Walter-Guillaume collection brings together 144 paintings by Renoir, Cézanne, Matisse, Picasso, and Modigliani.

activities.text: Leaving the museum, cross the Jardin des Tuileries to Place de la Concorde—the 23-meter Luxor Obelisk, gifted by Egypt in 1830, marks the geometric center of the Parisian historical axis. From there, the Champs-Élysées climbs toward the Arc de Triomphe over 1.9 km.

days_plan.itin_title: The Great Museums of Paris

visits.text: The Musée d'Orsay has occupied the former train station built by Victor Laloux between 1898 and 1900 for the Universal Exposition since 1986—the painter Édouard Detaille said as early as 1900: "The station is superb and looks like a fine arts palace." It took 86 years for the prophecy to come true. Here you'll find the world's most important collection of Impressionist and Post-Impressionist paintings: more than 1,100 canvases, including 72 Monets, 64 Renoirs, plus Degas, Van Gogh, Cézanne, and Gauguin. You absolutely must climb to the 5th level for the Impressionist Gallery, which runs along the Seine quays—the light streaming through the great gilded clocks is incomparable. Book your ticket online: lines at the ticket office can exceed 45 minutes.

activities.text: Leaving Orsay, cross the Pont Royal to reach the Tuileries—you can grab coffee on a terrace facing the garden before continuing. If you have the energy, the Musée de l'Orangerie is a 10-minute walk from the Left Bank: the two can be combined in the same half-day.

days_plan.itin_title: The Great Museums of Paris

visits.text: The Musée Picasso, installed in the Hôtel Salé in the Marais—a mansion from 1659 nicknamed for its owner who made his fortune from the salt tax—brings together the world's largest collection of Pablo Picasso's works: 5,000 pieces bequeathed by the family in payment of inheritance taxes in 1973. Paintings, sculptures, ceramics, collages, sketchbooks, correspondence: the museum traces 80 years of creation through all his periods, from the Rose to Les Demoiselles d'Avignon, from Cubism to Guernica. If you've never seen this much Picasso assembled, the impact is real. Allow 2 hours.

activities.text: The Marais is the ideal neighborhood to end the afternoon: Place des Vosges, Paris's first royal square built by Henri IV in 1612, is a 5-minute walk. The arcades surrounding it house art galleries and a few cafés—sit under the vaults for a final Parisian drink.

days_plan.itin_title: The Great Museums of Paris

places.name: Musee Quai Branly

visits.text: The Musée du Quai Branly - Jacques Chirac, designed by Jean Nouvel and inaugurated in 2006, is dedicated to the arts and civilizations of Africa, Asia, Oceania, and the Americas. Its façade is greened by Patrick Blanc—an 8,000 m² wall covered with 15,000 plants of 150 different species. Inside, the scenography in semi-darkness highlights ritual masks, African statues, musical instruments, and textiles from around the world: 370,000 objects in total, of which 3,500 are in permanent exhibition. The approach is not encyclopedic but sensory—Chirac wanted these objects treated as artworks, not ethnographic curiosities. Allow 2 hours.

activities.text: The Quai Branly borders the Seine with a direct view of the Eiffel Tower—a stroll along the banks between the two is ideal in late afternoon. If you visit in the evening (Thursday through Saturday until 9 p.m.), you'll see the light show on the tower from the museum garden.

days_plan.itin_title: The Great Museums of Paris

map_keywords: Musée du Quai Branly Jacques Chirac Paris
Collections arts premiers Quai Branly Paris

places.name: Musee Rodin Paris

visits.text: The Musée Rodin occupies the Hôtel Biron, an 18th-century mansion that Rodin inhabited from 1908 until his death in 1917. He bequeathed his works to the state on the condition that the house become a museum—which happened in 1919. In the 3-hectare garden, you'll find The Thinker, The Burghers of Calais, and The Gates of Hell—an ensemble of 180 bronze figures that Rodin worked on for 37 years without ever considering it finished. Inside, the plasters, marbles, and work notebooks reveal the sculptor's creative process. The collections also include 300 paintings by Eugène Carrière, Van Gogh, and Monet bequeathed by Rodin. Allow 90 minutes to 2 hours.

activities.text: Lunch or picnic in the Musée Rodin garden—one of the most pleasant addresses in the 7th arrondissement, sheltered from the city. The museum café in the garden's orangery is open to visitors without an entrance ticket for the terrace.

days_plan.itin_title: The Great Museums of Paris

map_keywords: La Porte de l'Enfer Rodin Paris

visits.text: The National Museum of Natural History, founded in 1635 under Louis XIII as the Royal Garden of Medicinal Plants, sprawls across the Jardin des Plantes in the 5th arrondissement. Don't miss the Grande Galerie de l'Évolution: inaugurated in 1994 in an 1889 building, it stages a procession of thousands of taxidermied animals—the giraffe, African elephants, a suspended whale—in a scenography that takes your breath away. The basement houses the hall of threatened and extinct species, sober and haunting. Allow 2 hours.

activities.text: While you're in the Jardin des Plantes, take a tour of the tropical greenhouses built between 1834 and 1936—one of the oldest collections of living plants in Europe. On your way out, the menagerie founded in 1794 is the world's oldest zoo still in operation, just a few steps farther.

days_plan.itin_title: The Great Museums of Paris

visits.text: Cordes-sur-Ciel - Enter through the Porte des Ormeaux, the gateway housing the Charles-Portal museum, and climb the steep rue Droite without rushing. Founded in 1222 by Raymond VII of Toulouse to resist the northern crusaders, this bastide perched on the Puech de Mordagne is one of the oldest in Occitanie. It's nicknamed the 'City of a Hundred Arches' for its concentration of Gothic civil façades - look up at the Maison du Grand Fauconnier with its carvings of fantastic creatures, then at the Maison du Grand Veneur with its hunting scenes in bas-relief. Albert Camus, who loved strolling here, said: 'In Cordes, everything is beautiful, even regret.' Arrive early in the morning - the village receives 600,000 visitors a year and the narrow streets get crowded quickly in summer.

Church of Saint-Michel in Cordes - Push open the door of this 13th-century southern Gothic church, modified in the 15th century. The single nave is typical of the Southwest, wide and without side aisles. If you have time, detour by the covered market hall, built in 1350 for the cloth and leather trade: beneath its 24 stone pillars lies a well 113 meters deep, one of the deepest in France.

Najac - The village stretches along an unusually long rocky ridge, above a meander of the Aveyron River. A single street connects the town to the fortress, lined with medieval houses from the 13th-15th centuries. Stop at the 14th-century fountain, still in place. The Sunday morning market on the Place du Faubourg is worth catching if you're there on the right day.

Church of Saint-Jean-l'Évangéliste in Najac - Step inside this 13th-14th century church in southern Gothic style. Its history is peculiar: the inhabitants, suspected of Cathar sympathies, were condemned by the Inquisition to finance it themselves. Look at the vaults - the single nave is typical of the region.

Royal Fortress of Najac - Climb up to the keep, it's steep (wear good shoes). Built between 1253 and 1266 by Alphonse de Poitiers, brother of Saint Louis, this fortress was never captured. The keep towers 40 meters above the gorges and houses arrow slits 6.80 meters tall - the tallest in France. Look for the secret passage in the thickness of the wall, between the chapel of Saint-Julien (with its remains of 13th-century frescoes) and the Governor's chamber. The dungeon served as a prison for the last Templars of Rouergue after their arrest in 1307. Take the guided tour to miss nothing.

activities.text: Walk along the Aveyron River - Descend from the village toward the river for a stroll along the water. In summer, it's also a popular swimming spot. If you want to extend your time here, outfitters offer canoe trips down the gorges - a different way to see the village from below.

days_plan.itin_title: Midi-Pyrénées Circuit: Bastides, Cliffs and Abbeys

visits.text: Place Stanislas spans 106 by 124 meters, its UNESCO-listed ensemble framed by Jean Lamour's gilded wrought-iron gates and crowned by Neptune and Amphitrite fountains. Built in 1755, this architectural masterpiece connects the medieval old town to the planned new city.
Arc Héré bridges Place Stanislas and Place de la Carrière, its bas-reliefs celebrating Louis XV beneath the inscription 'Terror of enemies, architect of treaties.'
Place de la Carrière stretches 124 meters between two rows of trees. Emmanuel Héré unified its 18th-century facades to complete the UNESCO architectural ensemble.
The Musée des Beaux-Arts occupies one of the pavilions on Place Stanislas. Its collections showcase Old Masters, Rodin sculptures, and Lorraine artists from the 14th century to today.
The neo-Gothic spire of Basilique Saint-Epvre soars to 87 meters. Prosper Morey built it between 1864 and 1871 using Euville stone, with financial support from Emperor Franz Joseph I of Austria.
Saint-Epvre's interior glows with over 70 stained glass windows created by Vienna's Geyling workshops. The windows total approximately 2,300 square meters of richly colored glass.

The Musée de l'École de Nancy occupies patron Eugène Corbin's former property. Its rooms display over 400 glass pieces by Émile Gallé, furniture by Louis Majorelle, and stained glass by Jacques Gruber.
The Masson dining room unites Eugène Vallin's woodwork, Victor Prouvé's painted ceiling, and Daum lighting fixtures in a harmonious Art Nouveau ensemble.
The museum garden cultivates peonies, anemones, and hydrangeas. An Art Nouveau aquarium pavilion houses Gruber's stained glass depicting underwater worlds.
Henri Sauvage designed Villa Majorelle in 1901 for furniture maker Louis Majorelle. Jean Lamour created the ironwork, Jacques Gruber the stained glass, and Alexandre Bigot the stoneware facade.
Villa Majorelle's interior displays nearly 100 pieces of furniture and decorative art. The woodwork, ironwork, and furniture are Louis Majorelle's own creations.

activities.text: Parc de la Pépinière spreads 21 hectares of greenery in Nancy's heart. Shaded walkways wind past a rose garden and a small zoo.
Walk Avenue Foch and Rue de la Commanderie to discover Art Nouveau facades with their organic curves and Majorelle ironwork flowing across the buildings.
Rue Saint-Jean brings together boutiques and cafés in the old town, where Renaissance buildings stand beside contemporary storefronts.
Wander through the Saurupt quarter, a garden city where Art Nouveau and Art Deco residences nestle among greenery.
Nancy's covered market has offered Lorraine products and regional specialties since 1851. Its metal halls host vendors Tuesday through Sunday mornings.

days_plan.itin_title: Meurthe-et-Moselle Road Trip: Art Nouveau Nancy & Fortified Cities
Meurthe-et-Moselle Road Trip: Art Nouveau Nancy & Fortified Cities

visits.text: The Frasne peatland is one of the best-preserved in the Jura massif—a high-altitude natural environment formed over thousands of years of peat accumulation. Wooden walkways allow you to cross this fragile environment above ground, with educational panels on carnivorous plants and nesting birds that live there. Bring binoculars.

The source of the Lison, at Nans-sous-Sainte-Anne, is one of the most powerful natural sites in the Doubs—a karst resurgence where the river gushes at the foot of a limestone cliff in a waterfall that never runs dry. Free access from a parking lot 200 meters away. Following the path for a few minutes, you reach the Grotte Sarrazine: an immense natural arch 100 meters high and 30 meters wide, overflow of the underground network during flood periods. This site gave rise to the first French law protecting natural sites, voted in 1906 after a citizen mobilization against a water capture project.

200 meters from the source, the Taillanderie of Nans-sous-Sainte-Anne is a historic monument classified since 1984. Founded in 1828 and active until 1969, this farm-workshop made scythes, billhooks and cutting tools thanks to the hydraulic power of the Arcange stream. Its trip hammers, bucket wheels and the entirely oak bellows system—a unique piece in Europe—still function during guided tours. It produced up to 20,000 scythes and 10,000 tools per year, sold in 27 departments and exported to Switzerland and North Africa.

activities.text: The limestone cliffs around Nans-sous-Sainte-Anne are hunting grounds for peregrine falcons, and the rocky slopes shelter chamois. A hike to the Vieux-Château viewpoint (former medieval stronghold at the mountain top) offers a 360° panoramic view over the Lison valley. Allow 1.5 hours round trip from the village.

days_plan.itin_title: Doubs Road Trip: Nature and Heritage of Franche-Comté

visits.text: Fort Mont-Valérien, built in 1841, dominates Paris from 161 meters above sea level. Between 1941 and 1944, over 1,000 resistance fighters and hostages were executed here by Nazi firing squads.
The Mémorial de la France combattante, inaugurated by General de Gaulle on June 18, 1960, honors World War II victims. Sixteen bodies symbolize the different forms of French Resistance.
The Parcours du Souvenir retraces the condemned prisoners' final path. In the chapel, scratched graffiti bears witness to their last hours. The clearing preserves the execution posts.

activities.text: The Jacques Baumel departmental park-promenade circles the fort, offering panoramas of Nanterre, Rueil-Malmaison, Suresnes, Paris, and La Défense.
Suresnes American Cemetery gathers the graves of soldiers fallen during both World Wars. A wall of the missing inscribes their names.
Parc des Chenevreux, labeled an eco-garden, offers walking paths and community gardens with predominantly native flora.

days_plan.itin_title: Hauts-de-Seine Road Trip: Royal Heritage & Gardens

places.name: Nantes

visits.text: The Château des ducs de Bretagne houses 32 museum rooms chronicling the city's layered past. Walk the 15th-century granite ramparts, then step into the inner courtyard where white tuffeau stone facades glow against the weathered battlements.

The Grand Elephant towers 12 meters high, a mechanical giant that trumpets and sprays water from its articulated trunk. Climb aboard this walking marvel at Les Machines de l'Île and feel the deck sway as it lumbers along the quays.

Inside the Galerie des Machines, machinists bring giant mechanical spiders and caterpillars to life. Ride the Carrousel des Mondes Marins on mechanical jellyfish and octopuses that rise and dive through three spinning levels.

Built in 1843, the Passage Pommeraye cascades down three levels across a 9-meter drop. Monumental stairways, statue-candelabras, and sculpted glass canopies make this covered arcade a neoclassical masterpiece.
Lose yourself in the medieval maze of the Bouffay quarter. Half-timbered houses from the 1400s, cobbled lanes, and Renaissance facades line the Place du Pilori, where Nantes' oldest streets converge.

The Cathedral of Saint Peter and Saint Paul dominates the historic heart with its soaring Gothic nave. Don't miss the flamboyant portal and the intricate tomb of Duke François II carved in white marble.

activities.text: Follow the green line painted on the ground for Le Voyage à Nantes art trail. Contemporary installations, surprising viewpoints, and historic monuments unfold along this urban treasure hunt through the reimagined city.

Wander the Île de Nantes where old shipyards have been reborn as cultural spaces. The Hangar à Bananes stretches along the Loire, its industrial shell now home to galleries, restaurants, and riverside terraces.
Head up the Erdre by boat or bike. Called France's most beautiful river, it winds between châteaux and tree-shaded parks, offering a green escape just minutes from downtown.

days_plan.itin_title: Loire-Atlantique Road Trip: Nantes and the Jade Coast

map_keywords: Château des ducs de Bretagne Nantes

visits.text: Nantua nestles along the shores of a glacial lake enclosed in a mountain cirque. The town developed around its Benedictine abbey founded in the 8th century. The lake, 2.7 kilometers long and 650 meters wide, offers enchanting landscapes with mountains reflected in calm waters. Nantua also played a crucial role during the Resistance, serving as a refuge for many maquis fighters.

The Abbey Church of Saint-Michel, rebuilt in the 19th century in neo-Romanesque style, stands out with its two asymmetrical bell towers. The interior houses an exceptional collection of religious art including a monumental wooden altarpiece and remarkable stained glass windows. The crypt preserves vestiges of the primitive abbey dating from the 8th century. The building's history intertwines with that of the town over twelve centuries.

The Resistance and Deportation Museum pays tribute to Bugey maquis fighters who fought Nazi occupation. The collections present period documents, weapons, personal objects, and moving testimonies. Reconstituted scenes recreate daily life of clandestine fighters in the mountains. A section is dedicated to local heroes deported to concentration camps. The visit, both educational and emotional, keeps alive the memory of these dark years.

Walk along the lake on the promenade offering spectacular views of surrounding mountains. The tree-lined path connects beaches, picnic areas, and belvederes. Lakeside cafés invite you to enjoy quenelles de Nantua, local specialty with crayfish sauce, while admiring the landscape. Children's play areas and pétanque courts attract families. The peaceful atmosphere and natural beauty make this walk a privileged moment.

activities.text: Taste the famous quenelles de Nantua, this pike dumpling topped with crayfish sauce that made the town's culinary reputation. Local restaurants all offer their version of this emblematic dish. The original recipe, jealously guarded, dates back several centuries. Some establishments open their kitchens to show preparation of this technical dish. The delicate texture and rich sauce delight fine palates.

Swim or practice water sports on the lake. The supervised beaches offer safe swimming in summer. Canoe, kayak, and paddleboard rentals allow exploration of hidden coves. Fishing enthusiasts can catch pike, perch, and trout. Pedalos invite families to gentle cruises while admiring mountain landscapes. The clear, fresh water delights swimmers.

Hike the Tour du Lac trail, an 8 km loop offering varied perspectives on water and mountains. The marked path alternates between lakeside sections and wooded passages. Belvederes reveal panoramic views of the cirque and town. The gentle slopes make this walk accessible to everyone. Picnic areas invite pauses to fully enjoy the landscape.

Visit during Fête du Lac in August, when the town celebrates summer with fireworks reflected in calm waters. The entire lakefront comes alive with stands, entertainment, and concerts. Local restaurants offer outdoor tasting menus. The festive atmosphere and spectacular fireworks attract thousands of visitors. The tradition, perpetuated for decades, marks the high point of the summer season.

days_plan.itin_title: Ain Road Trip: Bresse Plains and Bugey Mountains
Grand Tour of the French Alps: From the Thousand Ponds to Mont Blanc

places.name: Narbonne

visits.text: The Cathedral of Saint-Just-et-Saint-Pasteur is the city's most soaring monument. Begun in 1272 in the Rayonnant Gothic style, it was never completed — Narbonne's consuls refused to demolish the ramparts to allow the nave to extend. Result: only the choir exists, but what a choir — 41 meters under vault, making it France's fourth tallest cathedral, after Beauvais, Amiens and Metz. Climb the Gilles-Aycelin tower (1295-1306, 42 meters) for the best view over the city and the Canal de la Robine.

Descend beneath Place de l'Hôtel-de-Ville to the Roman Horreum. These underground galleries from the 1st century BC — probably a public market or warehouse — are the only ones of their kind still visible in France. Narbonne, the first Roman colony outside Italy founded in 118 BC, was then the capital of Gallia Narbonensis and the region's primary port. Allow 45 minutes for the circuit.

The Archbishops' Palace, built and expanded over eight centuries, occupies an entire side of Place de l'Hôtel-de-Ville. Its art and archaeology museums house Flemish paintings, Orientalist halls and the Via Domitia — the Roman road's paving stones are visible directly in the floor of the Passage de l'Ancre. Take time for the Passage de l'Ancre, a covered lane linking the palace to the cathedral.

The Canal de la Robine is a UNESCO-listed branch of the Canal du Midi. Its Pont des Marchands — one of France's rare inhabited bridges with its colorful houses perched on seven arches (only one is visible from the canal side) — links the Roman quarter on the right bank to the medieval quarter on the left. The Promenade des Barques, running along the quays, is Narbonne's loveliest stroll in fine weather.

The Narbonne Covered Market, built in the early 20th century in a Baltard-style of metal and glass, opens every morning. Fishmongers, butchers, cheese shops, olives, oysters from the lagoon — it's the liveliest spot in town to lunch standing at the counter or to pick up a picnic.

activities.text: In the evening, the Robine quays come alive with terraces and street musicians in summer. From the Pont des Marchands walk up to the port-canal de la Clape for a drink facing the barges. The sunset over the canal is worth the trip.

days_plan.itin_title: Aude Road Trip: Carcassonne, Cathar Country & the Mediterranean

map_keywords: Canal de la Robine Pont des Marchands Narbonne

visits.text: The Navacelles cirque is a massive abandoned meander carved 300 meters deep into the limestone causses by the Vis River. From the northern belvedere (Causse de Blandas side), you overlook this verdant chasm—it's one of Europe's largest natural cirques. The tiny village of Navacelles nestles at the bottom with its waterfall and medieval bridge.

Descend to the bottom of the cirque via the winding road (watch for tight turns). The Vis waterfall, in the village heart, flows over moss-covered rocks. You're standing in the "Cirque de Navacelles" Grand Site of France. The climb up the southern side (Causse du Larzac) offers equally spectacular views.

activities.text: Hike the causse around the cirque—several marked trails depart from the belvederes. The Mediterranean vegetation (boxwood, juniper, wild lavender) contrasts with the austere limestone. Allow 1.5 hours for the loop from the northern belvedere.

days_plan.itin_title: Southeast France Road Trip: Perched Villages and Dramatic Gorges

visits.text: Navarrenx's ramparts form France's first purpose-built bastioned fortification, erected from 1538 onward. Their 1.5-kilometer circuit still protects the historic center in an intact Renaissance star pattern.
The Porte Saint-Antoine, inscribed on UNESCO's heritage list, marks the Compostela pilgrims' entrance. Its fortified bridge has spanned the Gave d'Oloron since the 16th century.
The Église Saint-Germain blends Romanesque and Gothic styles. Its 19th-century stained glass windows illuminate the restored nave with jewel-like colors.
Walk the chemin de ronde that crowns the ramparts. The corner bastions reveal Renaissance military architecture designed to withstand cannon fire.

activities.text: The Gave d'Oloron flows at the ramparts' foot. Salmon fishing and canoeing are practiced from April to October in these clear mountain waters.
Follow the Camino de Santiago that crosses through the bastide town. Pilgrims have rested at Navarrenx since the Middle Ages before tackling the mountains ahead.

days_plan.itin_title: Pyrénées-Atlantiques Road Trip: Basque Country & Béarn

visits.text: Naves sits at the gates of the Parc Naturel Régional des Monts d'Ardèche, a preserved territory where settlement has maintained its traditional form. The village with crowded houses and paved cobbled lanes gives directly onto the Chassezac valleys, a river tumbling from high Cévennes plateaus. The church and shaded squares form the village heart—a quiet place off the usual tourist circuits.

Remnants of the enclosure and arched passages are visible in old village lanes. Look for faïsses—these small terraces in restanques developed on slopes for cultivation—from village heights: it's one of the most typical landscapes of Cévennes Ardèche, shaped over centuries by peasants to gain land on hillsides.

Viewpoints near the village offer views over the Chassezac valleys and its tributaries. The watercourse below is renowned for its clear waters and pebble beaches. In summer, some unsupervised swimming spots are accessible via trails from the village.

activities.text: The old village and nearby viewpoints discovery loop (2 to 3 km, 1h) is signposted from the church square. It passes the main viewpoints over the valleys and returns through medieval lanes. An accessible circuit for all, without significant elevation change.

The walk toward faïsses and traditional terraced cultivation (3 km round trip) starts from village heights and descends toward restanques developed on slopes. It's an opportunity to understand concretely how these dry-stone structures retained earth on steep hillsides. Allow 1h15.

days_plan.itin_title: The 18 Most Beautiful Villages of Ardèche

visits.text: The Château-Musée de Nemours rises above the Loing River. Built in the late 12th century by Gauthier I de Villebéon, it's one of the rare medieval urban castles still standing in Île-de-France. Its rectangular keep is flanked by four corner turrets.
Climb the turret to discover the oratory, a Gothic architectural gem on two levels. The château houses 25,000 objects, from 19th-century paintings to antique prints.

The Saint-Jean-Baptiste Church, founded in 1170, was rebuilt after the 1358 fire during the Hundred Years' War. The choir dates from the 15th century. The stained glass windows and high altar were added in the 19th century.

The Prehistory Museum of Île-de-France occupies a building designed by Roland Simounet, classified as 20th-century heritage. The collections span 500,000 years of human history, from early hunters to Gallic metalworkers. A 9th-century Carolingian boat completes the tour.
Head to the banks of the Loing from the château. The picturesque streets of Nemours preserve the charm of an ancient town, with stone bridges and medieval facades.

activities.text: Extend your museum visit with the Nemours communal forest loop. This marked trail crosses a sea of sand, old quarries, and strangely shaped boulders.
Canoe down the Loing from Grez-sur-Loing to Moret-sur-Loing. The river winds between willows, meadows, and artists' villages.
The nearby village of Larchant deserves a detour for its spectacular Saint-Mathurin church ruins and the Dame Jouanne rock, one of the highest bouldering spots in the massif.

days_plan.itin_title: Seine-et-Marne Circuit: Fontainebleau and Royal Châteaux

visits.text: The Château-musée Henri IV preserves the north wing of this Renaissance palace. Its gallery with twisted columns evokes the golden age of the d'Albret court in the 16th century.
Follow the Allée des 3000 Pas through the Parc de la Garenne. This 2.5-kilometer promenade created by Queen Margot winds beneath ancient oaks along the Baïse River.
Discover the park's Renaissance fountains: the Fontaine Saint-Jean, Fleurette's grotto, and the Fontaine du Dauphin erected in 1601.
In the Petit Nérac quarter, stroll Rue Séderie lined with half-timbered houses. The Vieux Pont offers lovely views of the old port and quays.
At Place du Général Leclerc, Henri IV's statue commemorates the future king of France's years here with Marguerite de Valois between 1577 and 1582.

activities.text: Follow in the footsteps of literary figures who frequented the court—Marguerite d'Angoulême welcomed Clément Marot and possibly Calvin himself.
Browse the market along the Allées d'Albret, where farmhouse cheeses, melons, and seasonal porcini mushrooms tempt food lovers.
Walk down to the navigable Baïse where pleasure boats moor in poetic tranquility.

days_plan.itin_title: Lot-et-Garonne Road Trip: Medieval Bastides & Villages

visits.text: The thermal baths of Néris perpetuate 2000-year-old Gallo-Roman tradition. Ancient thermal springs called "Aquae Nerii" attracted Romans seeking benefits of mineral waters. Current modern facilities treat rheumatism, osteoarthritis and anxiety disorders. The Belle Époque architecture preserves spa resort charm from early 20th century.

The archaeological site reveals Gallo-Roman thermal baths and ancient sanctuary remains. Educational panels explain Roman bathing practices and daily life in ancient spa resort. Excavated vestiges testify to thermal installation importance in antiquity. Free access allows discovering this historical heritage.

The thermal park extends 50 hectares of English gardens around spa facilities. Paths wind among century-old trees, flowerbeds and ponds. The landscaped setting invites strolling and relaxation in green environment. Bandstand and kiosks host summer concerts and events.

The thermal quarter reveals Belle Époque architecture with hotels and villas from early 20th century. Bourgeois facades, balconies and ornate ironwork testify to spa resort splendor. Rue de la République and surrounding streets concentrate period buildings creating nostalgic atmosphere.

activities.text: The Néris casino offers games, slot machines and evening shows in Belle Époque setting. This leisure establishment perpetuates spa resort festive tradition attracting visitors seeking entertainment. Restaurant and bar complete the offer for convivial evening.

Walk around Néris lake discovering beach, nautical base and picnic areas. Water activities include pedal boats, canoes and swimming in summer. Paths around lake offer family strolls in nature close to town center.

days_plan.itin_title: Allier Road Trip: Vichy, Thermal Baths and Bourbonnais Bocage

places.name: Neuf Brisach

visits.text: Neuf-Brisach is an absolute curiosity: a town created from scratch in 1698 on the Alsace plain after France lost the fortress of Alt-Breisach following the Treaty of Ryswick. Louis XIV himself chose, among three projects submitted by Vauban, this perfect octagonal plan — which cost him 4 million livres. To transport the pink sandstone from the Vosges, a special canal was dug. The fortifications were completed in 1702, the Church of Saint-Louis in 1736. The ensemble has been listed as a UNESCO World Heritage site since July 2008, as the sole example of Vauban's 'third system.' The best way to grasp it is from the air — operators offer microlight flights that reveal the geometric perfection of the sixteen-pointed star.

The place d'Armes is a large square 120 meters on each side occupying the geometric center of the octagon. Around it, 48 rectangular blocks are organized in a grid of implacable logic: barracks against the ramparts to protect civilians, buildings of power around the central square. Walk to the royal fountain and observe the symmetry of streets converging toward you from all cardinal points.

The Vauban Museum, installed in the Porte de Belfort, traces through models and original plans the construction of this ideal fortress-town. Here you'll understand the genius of the double-wall system: the enceinte de sûreté surrounding the town directly, then the outer works — demi-lunes, counter-guards, bastions — which multiply firing angles to make any enemy approach suicidal.

The Royal Church of Saint-Louis, whose first stone was laid on October 11, 1731 and work completed in 1736, expresses the sober classicism of 18th-century Alsace. Its clean lines survived the American bombing of 1945 that devastated much of the town. The façade of the military hospital built in 1765 to plans by architect Gabriel-Ignace Ritter is also worth a look.

activities.text: Walk the sentry path that makes a complete circuit of the enceinte over 2.4 kilometers. A marked historical route starts from the place d'Armes and guides you through the different military works. Contemporary artworks have also been installed in the ramparts, which since 2013 have housed France's second street art museum (MAUSA).

Operators offer microlight flights from a nearby airfield. From the air, the geometric perfection of Neuf-Brisach's sixteen-pointed star makes complete sense — it's the only angle from which you truly understand Vauban's military genius.

days_plan.itin_title: The 15 Most Beautiful Villages of Alsace
Upper Rhine Road Trip: Colmar and the Southern Wine Route

map_keywords: Musée Vauban Porte de Belfort Neuf-Brisach

visits.text: The church of Saint-Nicolas is a fascinating architectural puzzle — and that's what makes it so endearing. Founded at the end of the 11th century by Duke of Lorraine Thierry II, it has two superimposed levels: an 11th-century Romanesque crypt below, a 12th–13th-century Gothic upper church above, each with its own stacked chapels. You've never seen this anywhere else in Lorraine — even specialists say so. In the upper church, don't miss the 15th-century polychrome stone Entombment: nine life-size figures of striking expressiveness. Outside July-August, visits are by guided tour only — book at the Tourist Office.

The church of Saint-Christophe, first mentioned in 1094, dates in its main structure from the 13th century with a rare Burgundian influence in Lorraine. Don't leave without seeing the Wiriot chapel (1505): it's the only pendant vault chapel in France to have a vault entirely without voûtains — a technical feat that intrigued architects for centuries.

The historic center of Neufchâteau is the first Site Patrimonial Remarquable of the Vosges — labeled Petite Cité de Caractère and ranked among the 100 Plus Beaux Détours de France. The Hôtel de Houdreville hides a 16th-century Renaissance staircase and medieval cellars that can be visited. On Place Jeanne d'Arc, look for the Maison des Goncourt, which belonged to the grandfather of writers Jules and Edmond — an unexpected literary connection in this Vosges town.

activities.text: Push open the door of the old Scala theater, installed in the outbuildings of an Augustinian Ladies convent sold as national property in 1791. Its interior decor in plasterwork and painting dates from renovations in 1891-1893. The Tourist Office offers guided tours of the town throughout the year by reservation — the VIP package includes access to normally closed sites.

days_plan.itin_title: Vosges Mountains, France: Alpine Lakes, Spa Towns and Forest Heritage

places.name: Neuilly Sur Seine

visits.text: The Île de la Jatte stretches two kilometers between Neuilly and Levallois, once home to the Château de Neuilly where King Louis-Philippe resided. Square Jatte preserves a charming Temple of Love pavilion originally from Parc Monceau.

Georges Seurat immortalized this island in his masterpiece A Sunday Afternoon on the Island of La Grande Jatte. Monet, Sisley, and Van Gogh also painted these bucolic riverbanks dotted with riverside cafés.
The 19th-century Saint-Pierre church towers over the residential quarter, its spire rising above tree-lined avenues bordered by elegant Haussmannian buildings.

activities.text: Parc de l'Île de la Jatte occupies the island's northern tip. Discover the educational apiary, peaceful gardens, and Seine embankments where barges and rowing sculls glide past.
The Bois de Boulogne unfolds across 850 hectares south of Neuilly. Once the royal hunting grounds, it shelters the Parc de Bagatelle and Jardin d'Acclimatation.
The Fondation Louis Vuitton, designed by Frank Gehry, showcases breathtaking architecture. Its glass sails evoke ships catching wind and light.
