places.name: Nice

visits.text: The Promenade des Anglais deserves its backstory: this two-meter-wide seafront path was financed in 1822 by English reverend Lewis Way, who wanted to provide work for Niçois day laborers ruined by an orange tree freeze. Nice belonged to the Kingdom of Piedmont-Sardinia at the time. Two years later, in 1824, the promenade was born. Seven kilometers of palms lining the Baie des Anges, pebble beaches, and swimmers even in February—it's one of Europe's longest seafront boulevards. Skip summer weekends if you're rollerblading or cycling: it's chaos in every direction.

Place Masséna is Nice's beating heart: a checkerboard plaza in black and white inaugurated in 1844, flanked by Italian-style red and ochre facades. Seven luminous statues by artist Jaume Plensa have perched on columns since 2011. From here, Avenue Jean-Médecin shoots north and tram lines converge. Perfect for grasping the city's layout before diving into the alleyways of Old Nice.

Lose yourself in Vieux-Nice, where baroque ochre and terracotta facades recall centuries of Italian rule. Sainte-Réparate Cathedral was built between 1650 and 1699 in honor of a young martyr whose decapitated body supposedly drifted to Nice on a boat guided by angels. Its baroque interior overflows with stucco, marble, and 17th-century paintings. Just beside it, Place Rossetti with its gelato vendors is the best spot for a break.

Don't miss Palais Lascaris on Rue Droite—a mid-17th-century baroque palace built by the powerful Lascaris-Vintimille family. Classified as a historic monument since 1946, it houses the municipal museum of musical instruments: 65 classified pieces including 18 harps. The monumental staircase and trompe-l'œil painted ceilings alone justify the visit. Free admission, open daily except Tuesday.

Place Garibaldi, east of Old Nice, dates to 1780—it's Nice's oldest urban square. Its arcades and trompe-l'œil facades draw direct inspiration from Turin's Royal Square. Here, General Giuseppe Garibaldi was born in 1807, and you'll find the tram station for reaching Cimiez.

Climb the Colline du Château for panoramic views unmatched anywhere in Nice. The castle that gives it its name was demolished in 1706 on Louis XIV's orders, after withstanding sieges in 1543 and 1691. In 1822, King Charles-Félix of Sardinia turned it into a public park. Today, 320 trees planted in that era shade viewpoints over the Baie des Anges and the port. Access on foot (stairs from Quai des États-Unis) or by free elevator.

The Promenade du Paillon (Coulée Verte) is the city center's green lung—a 12-hectare linear garden traced over the old covered waterway, inaugurated in 2013. Its 3,000 m² water mirror with programmed jets is the evening gathering spot for children. Connects Jardin Albert 1er to Place Masséna in 10 minutes.

Head up to Cimiez for the Musée Matisse, set in a 17th-century Genoese villa amid an olive grove. Matisse settled in Nice in 1917, drawn by "the luminosity of the day." He stayed until his death in 1954 and rests in the nearby monastery cemetery. The collection spans 600 works from his early Impressionist portraits to the grand blue cut-paper pieces of his final years. Closed Tuesdays.

Steps from the Musée Matisse, the Musée Marc Chagall is one of the rare national museums created during an artist's lifetime—inaugurated in 1973 with Chagall himself present. His 17-canvas cycle on the Biblical Message forms the collection's core: large-format paintings in blue and green tones depicting Genesis, Exodus, and the Song of Songs. Don't miss the three stained-glass windows in the concert hall.

The Monastère de Cimiez is a classified historic monument. Founded in the 9th century, it shelters a Franciscan church whose interior preserves three altarpieces by Louis Bréa, the principal 15th-century Niçois primitive painter. Its garden—the former monks' vegetable patch—is an oasis of climbing roses, citrus trees, and cypresses with an open view over the Paillon valley.

The Roman arenas of Cimiez are the vestiges of ancient Cemenelum, capital of the Roman province of Alpes-Maritimes. Built in the 2nd century, they could hold 5,000 spectators. The adjacent baths (north, east, and west) are among the best-preserved in Roman Gaul. Entry is free; the site sits directly next to the Musée Matisse.

Villefranche-sur-Mer deserves a detour for its bay—one of the deepest in the Mediterranean (90 m at its center), which is why it regularly hosts American aircraft carriers. The old town tumbles down in colorful alleyways to Quai Courbet, where the Chapelle Saint-Pierre was entirely decorated by Jean Cocteau in 1957 as a sign of friendship for the port's fishermen. Charlie Chaplin attended the opening. The Citadelle Saint-Elme (1554) dominates from its bastioned ramparts.

Èze village perches at 429 meters between Nice and Monaco—100% pedestrian, 100% vertiginous. Its medieval lanes shelter artisan workshops, two perfumeries (free guided tours), and the Jardin Exotique created in 1949 on the castle ruins. Feminine terracotta sculptures by artist Jean-Philippe Richard populate the cacti since the 2000s. On clear days, Corsica stands out 170 km away. Arrive early—tour buses roll in by 10 a.m.

Roquebrune-Cap-Martin possesses one of the rare Carolingian castles still standing in France, with foundations dating to the 10th century. The medieval village remained property of Monaco's Grimaldis from 1350 to 1848, before voting to join France in the 1860 plebiscite. Climb to the esplanade for panoramas over Monaco and the Italian Riviera.

The Tête de Chien (445 m) above La Turbie is one of the French Riviera's most spectacular viewpoints: Monaco and its harbor at your feet, the sea stretching to Italy and, on clear days, Corsica. The Trophy of Augustus in La Turbie, visible from the road, is a Roman monument erected in 6 BC to commemorate Augustus's victory over the 45 Alpine tribes.

activities.text: Cours Saleya Market: Since 1816, this pedestrian corso in Old Nice has hosted morning flower and Provençal produce markets (except Mondays)—freshly grilled socca, pissaladière, olives, cheeses. Arrive before 9 a.m. to watch restaurateurs stock up. On Mondays, the antiques and flea market takes over.

Swimming on the pebble beaches of the Promenade des Anglais: public beaches are free, private beaches rent loungers. Avoid the central stretch in July-August—too crowded. The Carras beaches on the west end are far quieter.
Sunrise workout on the Promenade des Anglais: hundreds of Niçois gather every morning to run, cycle, or simply walk. The sight of morning light on the Baie des Anges is unparalleled.

Haut-de-Cagnes and the high backcountry: excursion from Nice to the medieval hilltop village to the west. The Grimaldi Castle, erected in 1309, houses an Olive Museum and a collection of 40 portraits of cabaret singer Suzy Solidor painted by Van Dongen, Lempicka, and Foujita. Count 30 minutes' drive from Nice.

Saint-Paul-de-Vence in the evening: the 16th-century ramparts (saved from demolition in 1872 by Mayor Layet) form a 20-minute walk around the village. In summer, galleries stay open until 8 p.m. Chagall, who lived here from 1966 until his death in 1985, rests in the village cemetery.

Gorges du Loup and Tourrettes-sur-Loup: descend the gorge from Gourdon to the Saut du Loup—an impressive waterfall where the river has carved a natural Grande Marmite into the limestone cliffs. Tourrettes-sur-Loup, "City of Violets," is the only place in France where Victoria violets have been cultivated as a primary crop since the 19th century.
Plage des Marinières (Villefranche): the water clarity in this sheltered bay is remarkable for snorkeling. Arrive before 10 a.m. in season to secure a spot. The beach is fine pebbles, well exposed.

Nietzsche Path (descent Èze village → Èze-sur-Mer): the philosopher took this steep 400 m descent during his stays on the coast in 1883—here he supposedly conceived the final parts of "Thus Spoke Zarathustra." Count 1 hour descending (1h15 ascending). Bring closed-toe shoes and water.

Coastal trail at Cap d'Ail to Plage de la Mala: one of the wildest coastal paths steps from Monaco. Mala Beach, wedged between cliffs and accessible only on foot (20 min from parking), is one of the Riviera's most beautiful pebble beaches. Arrive at opening in summer—the trail gets busy.

visits.text: Arènes de Nîmes, one of the world's best-preserved Roman amphitheaters at 433 feet long and 69 feet high. Built in the 1st century AD, it held 24,000 spectators who came to watch gladiatorial combat. The two-tiered exterior arcade in honey-colored Vers-Pont-du-Gard stone demonstrates Roman engineering mastery. The free audioguide in seven languages recreates the atmosphere of ancient games. Today the monument hosts bullfights, concerts, and historical reenactments including the Roman Days of Nîmes each late April.

Maison Carrée, a 1st-century BC Roman temple inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 2023. The Corinthian facade with 30 fluted columns illustrates the harmony of classical proportions. It's the only completely preserved ancient temple from the entire Roman world. Innovative museography with immersive projection "Nemausus, the Birth of Nîmes" traces 2,000 years of history on the interior walls. The building successively served as a consular house, church, prefecture, and departmental archives.

Tour Magne, remnant of Roman ramparts dominating Nîmes from atop Mont Cavalier at 105 feet high. Built under Augustus, it marked the northwestern corner of fortified walls that stretched nearly four miles. Climbing the 140 steps leads to a panoramic platform offering 360° views over the city, garrigue scrubland, and on clear days, Mont Ventoux and the Cévennes mountains. The octagonal tower integrated into the most imposing defensive system in Roman Gaul.

Jardins de la Fontaine, an 18th-century public park designed around the sacred spring that gave birth to Nemausus. Majestic tree-lined pathways bordered by balustrades and neoclassical statues follow a monumental perspective inspired by Versailles. The Temple of Diana, a mysterious Roman sanctuary with collapsed arcades, stands at the garden's heart. Terraced pools capture spring waters while staircases climb toward Mont Cavalier and Tour Magne. In summer, the garden becomes a cool refuge beneath centuries-old plane trees.

Musée de la Romanité, contemporary building opened in 2018 facing the Arena. The pleated glass facade reflects the amphitheater, creating dialogue between ancient heritage and modern architecture. The 5,000 pieces displayed across 37,500 square feet trace 25 centuries of history from Nemausus to Nîmes: exceptional mosaics, statues, everyday objects, augmented reality reconstructions. The multimedia immersive experience uses interactive technologies and monumental projections. The panoramic rooftop overlooks the city's roofs and Roman arena.

Nîmes Old Town, medieval historic center with winding streets lined by Renaissance facades and private mansions. Notre-Dame-et-Saint-Castor Cathedral combines Romanesque and Gothic styles with its sculpted frieze depicting the Old Testament. Shaded small squares shelter café terraces and artisan shops. The 19th-century central market halls offer Provençal products: olives, tapenades, Costières de Nîmes wines, brandade. The southern atmosphere comes alive at daily markets in a colorful, fragrant ambiance.

activities.text: Experience the Nîmes Férias, major bullfighting events in May (Pentecost) and September. For five days, corridas in the arena, lively bodegas, musical peñas, free concerts, and marching bands invade downtown. Aficionados in white outfits stroll from bar to bar in a festive, convivial atmosphere. Abrivados and bandidos see Camargue bulls running through the streets. Goyesque corridas feature bullfighters in period costumes. The electric atmosphere celebrates Camargue and Spanish bullfighting culture.

Taste brandade de Nîmes at a traditional restaurant in the historic center. This specialty made from desalted cod, milk, olive oil, and garlic is served hot with croutons. Family establishments perpetuate ancestral recipes passed down since the 18th century. Brandade houses also offer versions with truffles or gratinéed. This emblematic Nîmes dish pairs with white Costières wine or chilled rosé.

Wander through Nîmes' Provençal markets, particularly the one on Boulevard Jean-Jaurès Monday and Friday mornings. Stalls overflow with Gard fruits and vegetables, Pélardon AOC goat cheese, artisanal charcuterie, honey, seasonal fruits. Local producers sell garrigue honey, homemade jams, aromatic plants. The lively atmosphere resonates with southern accents and spirited haggling. Aromas of rotisseries, socca, and paella float between the aisles.

days_plan.itin_title: Gard Road Trip: Roman Nîmes, Pont du Gard & Camargue Wilderness
Cèze Valley and Ardèche Gorges Circuit
Roman Provence Road Trip: Ancient Monuments and Timeless Villages
Southeast France Road Trip: Perched Villages and Dramatic Gorges

visits.text: The twin towers of the Keep rise 28 meters above the Sèvre Niortaise River. Listed as a historic monument since 1840, it's one of the finest Romanesque keeps in France.
Climb the spiral staircase to the terrace to contemplate the green expanses of the Marais Poitevin.
The refectory hall displays sculpted capitals and traditional Poitevin furniture with its elevated beds.
The museum presents the ethnographic collection and graffiti of English sailors imprisoned in the 18th century.
Walk along the banks of the Sèvre Niortaise to the Épona space where remains of the ancient sanctuary of Port Boinot are exhibited.

activities.text: Stroll through the historic quarter between market halls and sculpted dragons that animate the city center.
The Bernard d'Agesci Museum brings together fine arts, decorative arts, and natural history collections in a grand cabinet of curiosities.

days_plan.itin_title: Deux-Sèvres Circuit: Marais Poitevin and Romanesque Heritage

visits.text: The Cutlery Museum occupies a former blade-making workshop founded in 1725. Over 8,000 pieces chronicle this craft's evolution from the 18th century to present day.

Collections showcase folding knives with intricate mechanisms, Nicolas Pierre Pelletier's embroidery scissors, the Garnier brothers' zeppelin knife inspired by the airship that crashed here in 1917, and surgical instruments of remarkable precision.

The Nogent cutlery basin extends across 20 kilometers. Seven major categories divide production: folding knives, table cutlery, professional tools, shears, specialized implements, surgical instruments, and grooming accessories.
The old town preserves several historic houses and cobbled lanes. The Marne riverbanks invite leisurely strolls along the water's edge.

activities.text: Follow the Route des Vallées Coutelières (Cutlery Valley Route). This circuit introduces you to workshops and artisans perpetuating Nogent's blade-making traditions.
Browse the museum shop's offerings. Handcrafted knives, scissors, and cutlery items manufactured locally make distinctive souvenirs.
Visit the Friday morning market. Haute-Marne regional produce and local specialties bring the town square to life.

days_plan.itin_title: Haute-Marne Discovery: Langres & Historic Heritage

visits.text: Nogent-sur-Marne — Carné filmed it in 1929 in Nogent Eldorado du dimanche: Parisians arriving by train from Bastille for Sunday boating, riverside taverns packed, the Marne as a substitute sea. But before the taverns, the town was aristocratic: from the 17th century, nobles and bourgeois had country houses built there. The Hôtel des Coignard, an 18th-century mansion in the town center, is the most visible witness to this heritage.

The Maison nationale des artistes occupies two 17th and 18th-century mansions bequeathed to the state by the Smith sisters — Madeleine, a painter, and Jeanne, a photographer. The 10-hectare park has been classified as a picturesque site since 1909. It now houses both a retirement home for artists and MABA, a contemporary art center. Free entry, daily 9am to 6pm.

The Nogent-sur-Marne Intercommunal Museum is France's only museum dedicated to the history of the Marne loops. Founded in 1962, over 6,000 items: glass photographic plates from the early 20th century, paintings by Ferdinand Gueldry, Belle Époque postcards. This is where the golden age of riverside taverns truly comes to life.

activities.text: The Nogent viaduct spans 800 meters with 34 arches — it was built to bring the train from Mulhouse, which transformed the town into a fashionable residential suburb. At its feet, Art Nouveau villas designed by Nachbaur father and son, Nogent architects who built much of this neighborhood between 1900 and 1914. The Marne Valley tourist office organizes guided walks on this heritage in season.

Nogent's marina carries the France Station Nautique label. Clubs offer kayaking, rowing, and paddleboarding on the Marne — this is where Belle Époque nautical festivals and regattas were held. On spring weekends, the place regains some of that animation.

days_plan.itin_title: Discovering Val-de-Marne in 10 Days
Val-de-Marne Road Trip: Castles and River Marne Banks

visits.text: The Passage du Gois, a 4.2-km tidal causeway unique in the world, connects the mainland to Noirmoutier. Cross it by car or on foot at low tide—check tide schedules before you set out.
Noirmoutier Castle, a 12th-century fortress built by the lords of La Garnache, towers over the town center. Climb to the top of the keep for breathtaking island views.
Saint-Philbert Church rests on the foundations of a Benedictine abbey founded around 674. The crypt preserves the empty tomb of the saint, moved to Tournus during Norman invasions.

Wander the flower-lined lanes of the Banzeau quarter, one of the island's oldest neighborhoods. Whitewashed houses with colorful shutters, hollyhocks, and climbing vines create a quintessentially Noirmoutier scene.
Stroll through the Bois de la Chaise: maritime pines, holm oaks, and strawberry trees lead to Plage des Dames and its iconic white beach huts.
Walk along Jetée Jacobsen at the heritage harbor. To the left, the Müllembourg reserve with its salt marshes; to the right, sailboats and the small boat graveyard.

activities.text: Join a salt worker (saunier) to learn about salt harvesting. Salt marshes cover a third of the island, and ancestral techniques transform seawater into coarse salt and fleur de sel.
Set off by bike on the island's cycle paths linking all four communes: Barbâtre, La Guérinière, L'Épine, and Noirmoutier-en-l'Île.
Pull on your boots for a shellfish-gathering session at spring tides. Periwinkles, cockles, and clams can be found on the Gois and Mardi Gras beaches.
Discover the village of Le Vieil, a former fishing hamlet with whitewashed houses standing at the water's edge. Its narrow lanes and peaceful coves offer total escape.

days_plan.itin_title: Vendée Tour: Noirmoutier Island, Puy du Fou & Coast

visits.text: Pass through the Porte de Tonnerre and plunge into the Middle Ages: Noyers-sur-Serein boasts 78 buildings classified or registered as Historic Monuments, most dating from the 15th century and little altered since. Half-timbered houses stand alongside stone mansions, arcades, and squares with evocative names: Place du Marché-au-Blé, Place de la Petite-Étape-aux-Vins. Voted 7th Favorite Village of France in 2014.

Walk along the ramparts with their 19 defense towers still partially visible. The fortifications, originally flanked by 24 towers, protected a prosperous town built on wine and timber trade. Film buffs will recognize the Hôtel du Globe: this is where Bourvil and Louis de Funès shot the inn scene in La Grande Vadrouille in 1966. More than twenty films have been made in this village.

Visit the Museum of Naïve and Popular Arts, housed in a beautiful 15th-century mansion. Three floors and 1,500 square meters of exhibition space showcase works by craftsmen and folk artists. If you come in autumn, book your Saturday for the Burgundy truffle market—an event that draws gourmets from across France.

activities.text: Walk around the village along the riverside path beside the Serein. Noyers is built within a meander of the river that almost completely encircles the town—a natural defense that gives it its island-like character. From the small bridge downstream, you'll have the finest overall view of the rooftops, towers, and reflections in the water.
The trails around the village traverse the meurgers—those typical Burgundian dry stone walls—and old vine huts. Allow 1.5 hours for the hillside loop offering views over the Serein valley.

days_plan.itin_title: Yonne Circuit: Northern Burgundy & Chablis Wine Country
Burgundy's 15 Most Beautiful Villages
Burgundy Road Trip: Cathedrals, Vineyards & Medieval Villages

visits.text: Notre-Dame Cathedral began rising in 1145 as a milestone of early Gothic architecture. Its double transept with radiating chapels remains unique in French Gothic architecture, shared only with Soissons.
The Chapter Library stands on wooden pillars dating from 1506. As one of Europe's rare half-timbered library buildings, it preserves 3,000 volumes including editions by Calvin, Pascal, and Montaigne.
The Morienval Gospel Book dates from the 9th-century Carolingian period. Its wooden binding inlaid with ivory and illuminations illustrating Aesop's fables make it a major bibliophilic treasure.
The Jean Calvin Museum occupies a 1927-1930 building erected on the reformer's birthplace in 1509. Protestant history and rare 16th-century engravings trace the Reformation movement.
The Noyonnais Museum occupies the former episcopal palace. Two thousand years of local history unfold from Gallo-Roman vestiges through Charlemagne's coronation here in 768.

activities.text: Wander through the cathedral quarter among canons' houses from the 17th and 18th centuries. The chapter house and episcopal dependencies line the cobbled streets around Notre-Dame.

days_plan.itin_title: Oise Circuit: Imperial Châteaux, Ancient Forests & Living History

visits.text: Admire this elegant medieval bridge built in the 14th century that spans the Eygues with a single daring arch 43 meters long and 18 meters high. Construction began in 1340 but wasn't completed until 1409. Listed as a Historical Monument in 1925, it's a masterpiece of medieval engineering and Nyons' iconic symbol.

Wander the picturesque alleys of the old village with its ancient houses bathed in the warm atmosphere of Drôme Provençale. The Randonne Tower emerges from the rooftops, crowned with a curious pyramid of arcades topped by a Virgin statue. Nyons has earned the France's Most Beautiful Detours and Remarkable Taste Site labels.

Visit one of the historic olive oil mills at the foot of the Roman Bridge. The Dozol-Autrand Mill, established since 1750, has seen 14 generations of millers pass through. A video traces an olive's journey to its transformation into oil. The Vieux Moulins Mill offers unique heritage in France with two old mills from the 18th and 19th centuries and a soap factory.

Contemplate the olive groves stretching across the surrounding hills, often terraced. The Tanche olive, a unique local variety grown only in the Baronnies Provençales territory, can withstand temperatures down to -10°C. The Nyons black olive stands out with its monk's robe color and gentle taste with red fruit aromas. Nyons olive oil, France's first PDO oil in 1994, is characterized by its golden color and buttery taste.

activities.text: Explore the traditional mills and discover the complete olive oil production process. Possibly attend a demonstration of scourtins weaving at La Scourtinerie, France's only such workshop. Taste different Nyons PDO olive oils and preserved black olives. The ideal period to observe production is December through January during harvest and pressing.

Don't miss Thursday morning's colorful market, a true Nyons institution. Browse stalls overflowing with local products: Nyons PDO olives and oils, Picodon goat cheese, lavender honey, aromatic herbs. The market comes alive beneath century-old plane trees.

Discover local craft shops, art galleries, and creator workshops. Visit the World of Olives Institute and the Olive & Sens exhibition that engages all five senses to explore the olive universe. Culinary and cosmetic workshops centered on olives are regularly offered.

days_plan.itin_title: Provence Drôme Road Trip: Medieval Villages & Local Terroir
From the Alps to the Ardèche Gorges: Lakes, Peaks and Mediterranean Hills

visits.text: Obernai concentrates the best of medieval Alsace on a single square. At Place du Marché—where the weekly market has been documented since 1301—the Renaissance Town Hall (1523, remodeled in 1610), the 1554 Corn Exchange (originally a butcher shop and salt store), the 1579 Six-Bucket Well, and the Kappellturm belfry form a remarkably cohesive ensemble. Arrive in the morning for the light and before the tour buses descend.

The Kappellturm belfry dominates the square at 60 meters tall. It's what remains of a chapel built in the late 13th century—the chapel was destroyed in 1873 but the bell tower survived, with its six bells, the two oldest dating from 1429 and 1474. The gallery with its watchtowers was added in 1596-97. On the northeast buttress, you'll find the official measurements of the ell and dagger carved into the stone—medieval measurement standards.

Obernai is the legendary birthplace of Saint Odile, patroness of Alsace, born blind in the 7th century and said to have regained her sight on the day of her baptism. The medieval ramparts that still encircle the center—1,400 meters of walls with towers and moats—testify to the town's strategic importance. In 1354, Obernai joined nine other cities to found the Decapolis, the league of free imperial cities of Alsace. A walk along the rampart path gives you a good sense of the scale of the work.

Saints Peter and Paul Church, built between 1867 and 1872 in monumental neo-Gothic style, preserves two classified treasures: the 1882 Merklin organ and the 1504 Holy Sepulchre altar, which depicts the risen Christ surrounded by the Holy Women with striking realism for its time.

activities.text: Climb to Mont Sainte-Odile, 15 km from Obernai, Alsace's foremost pilgrimage site since the 7th century. The Abbey of Hohenbourg perches at 764 meters and offers views on clear days as far as the Bernese Alps and the Black Forest. Around the abbey runs the Pagan Wall—a 10 km enceinte of cyclopean stones whose origin remains debated, probably Celtic. An 11 km hiking circuit allows you to walk its full perimeter.

In the evening, linger in Obernai's lanes to taste Alsatian specialties in a winstub: tarte flambée, baeckeoffe, choucroute garnie, and sweet kouglof for dessert. The town is the Bas-Rhin's second tourist destination after Strasbourg—restaurants are numerous and quality is generally good.

days_plan.itin_title: Grand Est Road Trip: Vineyards, Ramparts and Forests
The 15 Most Beautiful Villages of Alsace
Alsace Wine Route: Strasbourg to the Vosges
Alsace's Most Beautiful Christmas Markets

visits.text: Oingt, classified among the Most Beautiful Villages of France, unfolds its golden stone houses on a rocky outcrop overlooking the Azergues valley. The medieval village, fortified in the 13th century, has preserved its authentic character with ramparts, fortified gates, and cobbled streets called 'calades.' The golden color of buildings, due to local limestone, creates a unique luminosity particularly magical at sunset. Oingt embodies the quintessence of Golden Stone Country.

The Church of Saint-Mathieu, perched at the village's highest point, offers a panoramic view extending from Mont Blanc to the Pilat massif on clear days. Built in the 12th century in Romanesque style, it houses remarkable religious furniture and frescoes recently restored. The square bell tower serves as a landmark visible from the entire valley. The interior, soberly decorated, invites contemplation.

The medieval castle, though partially ruined, testifies to Oingt's strategic importance during the Middle Ages. The square tower, still standing, dominates the village and offers exceptional views of surrounding landscapes. Guided tours reveal the secrets of medieval defense architecture and daily life of lords. The castle gardens, redesigned according to period principles, showcase plants cultivated in the Middle Ages.

Walk the covered walkways and narrow calades that climb between golden stone houses. Each corner reveals new perspectives on surrounding landscapes: vineyards, forests, and hills unfolding to the horizon. Artisan workshops occupy vaulted cellars: pottery, painting, sculpture. The charming village has attracted many artists seeking authentic beauty.

activities.text: Discover local wines at winegrowers cultivating hillsides around the village. The terroir of Golden Stones produces fruity and elegant wines, less known than great crus but of remarkable quality. Independent producers welcome visitors to explain their work and passion. Tastings take place in vaulted cellars dug into rock, creating an intimate and authentic atmosphere.

Visit artisan workshops perpetuating traditional know-how: pottery, weaving, painting on stone. Artists draw inspiration from surrounding landscapes and special light of Golden Stones. Some open their workshops for demonstrations and explanations of techniques used. Unique creations reflect the village's soul and beauty of local materials.

Hike the Sentier des Pierres Dorées circuit that crosses vineyards and hamlets. The 8 km marked trail offers spectacular views of the Azergues valley and Mont Blanc in the distance. The route passes through several golden stone villages, each with its own character. Information panels explain geological particularity of local limestone and its influence on architecture.

Attend an outdoor concert during the Music Festival that takes place in July. Church square transforms into an open-air stage where classical, jazz, and world music echo off golden stone walls. The unique acoustics and magical setting make each performance an unforgettable moment. Spectators settle on steps and walls for an immersive musical experience.

days_plan.itin_title: Ain Road Trip: Bresse Plains and Bugey Mountains
Rhône Road Trip: Lyon, Golden Stone Villages of Beaujolais & Condrieu Vineyards
Loop Around Lyon: Villages and Golden Stones

visits.text: The Château d'Oiron has housed the Curios & Mirabilia collection since 1993, contemporary artworks inspired by Renaissance cabinets of curiosities.
The 55-meter-long Renaissance Gallery illustrates the history of Troy. Its 16th-century frescoes are among the most important after Fontainebleau.
Christian Boltanski photographed Oiron's schoolchildren between 1993 and 2000, subverting the tradition of portrait galleries.
The painted and sculpted 17th-century woodwork dialogues with creations by 63 contemporary artists.
The adjoining collegiate church completes the visit to the estate of the Gouffier family, Grand Equerry of Henri II.

activities.text: Stroll through the park designed by Pascal Cribier between 2005 and 2008 where contemporary art inhabits the green spaces.
Discover the village of Oiron where contemporary creation has taken over public squares and alleyways.

days_plan.itin_title: Deux-Sèvres Circuit: Marais Poitevin and Romanesque Heritage

visits.text: The UNESCO-listed Cathédrale Sainte-Marie features a carved Romanesque portal from the 12th century. Its 73 medallions depict the Apocalypse and medieval daily life in intricate stone storytelling.
The Sainte-Croix quarter preserves cobbled lanes and half-timbered medieval houses. The Romanesque church has crowned the hill since the 11th century, watching over the valley.
Reach the confluence where the Gave d'Aspe and Gave d'Ossau merge to form the Gave d'Oloron. Stone bridges offer panoramic views toward the Pyrenees rising majestically.

activities.text: Oloron berets are still crafted using traditional methods. Workshops perpetuate this Béarnais know-how dating from the 19th century when local factories supplied all of France.
The Aspe Valley opens toward Spain. The road and Camino de Santiago cross the Col du Somport pass at 1,632 meters, a historic route through the mountains.

days_plan.itin_title: Pyrénées-Atlantiques Road Trip: Basque Country & Béarn

visits.text: Oppède-le-Vieux is perhaps the most moving of the Luberon villages: half-ruined, half-restored, it feels frozen in the Middle Ages. The original village was gradually abandoned from the 20th century onward in favor of the lower village in the plain. By 1940, only six inhabitants remained. It wasn't until the 1960s that artists and heritage enthusiasts began restoring houses and stone lanes. Wear good shoes: the path from the parking lot climbs for 15 to 20 minutes along steep cobbled lanes before emerging onto the church esplanade.

The Collégiale Notre-Dame-d'Alydon crowns the village. Its origins likely date to the 10th or 11th century, with significant alterations in the 16th and 17th centuries: the hexagonal bell tower dates from 1500-1501. The interior preserves an 18th-century gilded wooden altarpiece and a painting by Provençal artist Reynaud-Levieux (The Presentation of the Rosary). The building was saved from ruin through the efforts of a network of artists united by an annual festival. Free entry, irregular hours outside summer.

The feudal castle ruins at the summit are inaccessible for safety reasons, but the silhouette is spectacular. A dark anecdote haunts this site: in 1545, Jean Maynier d'Oppède, lord of the château and First President of the Parliament of Aix, ordered the destruction of neighboring Waldensian villages, causing nearly 3,000 deaths. This massacre of the Vaudois of the Luberon remains one of the darkest episodes in Provençal history.

activities.text: Climb via the stone lanes to the summit esplanade—360° view over the Calavon valley, Ventoux, and the Alps. The light is fantastic at midday on the honey-colored stones.
Below the parking lot, the Jardins de Sainte-Cécile offer fifteen terraces of Mediterranean plants with over 80 hardy species. An unexpected natural respite at the foot of a medieval village.

days_plan.itin_title: The 15 Most Beautiful Villages of the Luberon

visits.text: The Centre de la Mémoire d'Oradour, inaugurated in 1999, is the sole entrance to the martyred village ruins. Its permanent exhibition traces, through archival documents and filmed testimonies, the context of World War II and the events of June 10, 1944.

The martyred village preserves intact the ruins of the Limousin town destroyed on June 10, 1944, by the SS Das Reich division. 643 victims — men, women, and children. The cleared streets, reinforced walls, and bullet marks left untouched allow you to measure the massacre's full extent. Classified as a Historic Monument since 1946, the site receives 300,000 visitors annually.
The martyred village church is the most emotionally charged place: here 450 women and children were locked inside and killed. Fire and impact traces are carefully preserved during each restoration.
The memorial crypt, designed in the 1950s, houses in its display cases personal objects of victims found in the rubble — stopped watches, toys, keys — relics of shattered daily life.

activities.text: Take time to walk the esplanade linking the Centre de la Mémoire to the new town, rebuilt after the war. The new Oradour church faces the ruins — two eras, one village.
At the communal cemetery, the Tombeau des martyrs gathers the victims' ashes. Ex-votos placed over decades bear witness to enduring remembrance.

days_plan.itin_title: Haute-Vienne Circuit: Limoges, Porcelain & Historic Villages

visits.text: Orange's ancient theater awaits you with its stage wall measuring 103 meters long and 37 meters high — Louis XIV called it "the finest wall in my kingdom." Built during the Augustan era, it's one of the best-preserved Roman theaters in the world and the only one still standing with its facade wall intact. The cavea could accommodate 9,000 spectators across 34 tiers arranged according to social rank. In the central niche of the stage wall, a colossal 3.5-meter statue presides over performances — probably Augustus, though some historians contest this attribution. UNESCO-listed since 1981. Since the 19th century, the theater has hosted the Chorégies d'Orange each summer, one of France's oldest outdoor opera festivals — book ahead if you're there in July-August.

At the northern entrance to town, Orange's triumphal arch rises 19 meters long and 20 meters high in the middle of a roundabout. Erected between 10 and 25 AD under Tiberius to honor the veterans of Caesar's Second Gallic Legion, founders of the colony of Arausio, its three bays bear exceptional decoration: shields, helmets, military standards and — even rarer — maritime attributes evoking the Battle of Actium. Its bas-reliefs of chained Celtic captives recreate the atmosphere of Roman conquest in Gaul. Since spring 2023, a 12-station sound trail surrounds the monument.

activities.text: The Orange Museum of Art and History, facing the ancient theater, preserves the world's most complete collection of Roman land registers — engraved marble slabs that precisely mapped the colony's properties. These unique documents reveal how Rome organized land distribution to veterans. An ideal complementary visit to contextualize what you've seen in the theater.

days_plan.itin_title: Vaucluse: Avignon, Luberon Villages & Ventoux Routes
Roman Provence Road Trip: Ancient Monuments and Timeless Villages

visits.text: Lake Serre-Ponçon.
Viewpoint.

activities.text: Sailing.
Paddle boarding.
Beach.

days_plan.itin_title: Hautes-Alpes Majesty: High Mountain Passes, Ski Resorts & Alpine Valleys

visits.text: Notre-Dame d'Orcival Basilica stands as one of Auvergne's five major Romanesque churches and the only one to preserve its original bell tower intact. Built in the 12th century, it displays remarkably homogeneous architecture with its tiered pyramidal apse and broad transept. In the choir sits the celebrated Majesty Virgin, a silver and vermeil statuette that has drawn pilgrims since the Middle Ages. Painted capitals adorn the nave and choir, while the crypt opens from the side aisles.

The Porte Saint-Jean, the basilica's side entrance, deserves careful attention. Its cedar panels predating the 13th century bear extraordinary ironwork adorned with animal heads and human faces—rare testimony to Romanesque ironcraft. Nicknamed Notre-Dame des Fers, the church preserves chains and cannonballs offered as ex-votos by former prisoners grateful for their liberation. A 16th-century sundial carved with a chained prisoner recalls this singular devotion.

Château de Cordès rises at 2,950 feet elevation, two kilometers north of Orcival. This 15th-18th century estate is most notable for its French formal gardens designed by André Le Nôtre's workshop in 1695. The hornbeam and beech hedges—unique at this altitude—compose a geometric green theater classified as a Historic Monument and labeled a Remarkable Garden. Visits run during summer season.

activities.text: Join a free guided tour of Orcival Basilica offered in summer by parish volunteers. These passionate guides unveil the capitals' iconographic subtleties, the Majesty Virgin's eventful history, and the architectural secrets of this exceptionally coherent Romanesque edifice.

Play The Prisoner of Orcival tablet game, available at the Tourist Office. This playful interactive trail invites families to solve puzzles tied to the village and basilica's history while exploring streets and church surroundings.

Hike the volcanic landscapes around Orcival in the Parc Naturel Régional des Volcans d'Auvergne. Several marked trails wind through summer pastures dotted with stone shepherds' huts and beech forests surrounding the village, offering viewpoints over the Chaîne des Puys peaks.

days_plan.itin_title: VOLCANOES, ROMANESQUE STONE AND AUVERGNE TRADITIONS

visits.text: The Sainte-Croix Cathedral towers over Orléans with its soaring Gothic spires. Ten magnificent 19th-century stained glass windows chronicle the epic tale of Joan of Arc, who liberated the city from English siege in 1429.

In the Place du Martroi, Joan of Arc's bronze equestrian statue raises her eyes skyward in gratitude. This powerful monument, unveiled in 1855, was cast from captured English cannons—a fitting symbol of victory.

The Maison de Jeanne d'Arc faithfully recreates the house where the Maid of Orléans lodged in April 1429. Now the world's leading Joan of Arc research center, it houses 37,000 documents, plus films and interactive exhibits that bring her story to life.

The Hôtel Groslot unfurls its striking red-brick Renaissance façade like a page from history. This 16th-century palace once hosted King Francis II and Catherine de Medici. Explore the grand salons and jewel-toned stained glass windows—admission is free.
The Musée des Beaux-Arts showcases Italian primitives, French masters, and extensive Joan of Arc collections. La Tour's luminous pastels and 19th-century paintings fill the elegant galleries.

activities.text: Stroll the redesigned Loire riverbanks, where tree-lined paths, riverside guinguette cafés, and sweeping views of the royal river—a UNESCO World Heritage site—await.

Every year in early May, the Fêtes de Jeanne d'Arc bring the city to life with historical processions in medieval costume, a bustling period market, and spectacular sound-and-light shows on the cathedral façade.
Linger in the Place du Châtelet, where café terraces spill onto the square. On Saturday mornings, a vibrant market sets up beneath the shade of plane trees.

days_plan.itin_title: Loiret Road Trip: Orléans & Loire Valley Châteaux

visits.text: On the way to Ornans, make a detour through the Museum of Comtois Houses in Nancray, 16 km from Besançon. This open-air museum, unique in France, brings together about thirty rural buildings dismantled from their original villages in the 17th, 18th and 19th centuries and reassembled on site—Comté cheese dairy, farmhouse with smoke chamber, pink sandstone house, half-timbered barn. Thematic gardens (witch's kitchen garden, heritage orchard) complete the visit.

At Étalans, just before Ornans, the Poudrey chasm is worth the detour. Discovered in 1899, it's the largest developed chasm in France with its main chamber of about 1 million m³. A sound and light show highlights its stalagmites and stalactites. Constant temperature of 10°C—bring a jacket.

Ornans is nicknamed the "little Venetian Comté" for its houses built overhanging the Loue—a protected site. The Courbet Museum, reinstalled in the painter's birthplace then expanded in 2011 to reach over 2,000 m², presents more than 70 original works across 21 rooms. Gustave Courbet (1819-1877), leader of Realism, painted these Loue landscapes more than 13 times. The museum's large bay windows frame directly onto the river and cliffs—a remarkable architectural effect.

15 km from Ornans, the source of the Loue at Ouhans absolutely deserves the detour. It's not a true source: it's a resurgence of the Doubs, spectacularly proven in 1901 when a fire in a distillery in Pontarlier colored the Loue waters with the smell of absinthe. The waterfall gushes at the foot of a 100-meter rocky recess. A protected site since 1912—among the first protected sites in the history of environmental conservation in France.

activities.text: Operators offer canoe rentals on the Loue from Ornans. The descent through the valley between limestone cliffs and forests is one of the trip's finest natural moments. Allow 2 hours for a leisurely route.

days_plan.itin_title: Doubs Circuit: Watchmaking and Swiss Border
Doubs Road Trip: Nature and Heritage of Franche-Comté

places.name: Ospedale

visits.text: Climb to the Ospedale forest, at 900 meters altitude above Porto-Vecchio: an oasis of coolness among laricio pines and holm oaks, with an artificial lake built in the 1960s shimmering at the basin's bottom—a surprising image at this altitude. From the forest road you'll have some of the finest views over the Bay of Porto-Vecchio. Stop at the viewpoints on the village road.

The Piscia di Gallo waterfall—"rooster piss" in Corsican, which gives an idea of local poetry—is Corsica's highest waterfall at 60 meters. It's 30 minutes on foot from the forest parking lot. Only catch: it's spectacular in spring and after autumn rains, but can reduce to a trickle in August.

activities.text: Two-hour round-trip hike to Piscia di Gallo waterfall from the forest parking lot: well-marked trail in the pines, moderate 200 m elevation gain. Hiking shoes required—the path is rocky.
From the Ospedale forest belvedere, the Bay of Porto-Vecchio spreads below with its islets and green waters. Stop there in late afternoon: the declining light turns the gulf to gold.

days_plan.itin_title: Complete Tour of Corsica

map_keywords: Point de vue baie Porto-Vecchio Ospedale

visits.text: Paimboeuf served as Nantes' deep-water port in the 17th and 18th centuries. Shipowners enriched by maritime trade built elegant mansions along the Loire quays, their fortunes written in stone facades.
The Church of Saint-Louis crowns the town with its neoclassical bell tower. Built in the 18th century, it stands as testimony to the port's vanished prosperity.
Stroll the redeveloped quays facing the wide Loire. The estuary broadens here before flowing to the Atlantic at Saint-Nazaire, its waters still rising and falling with the distant tide.

activities.text: Explore the Nantes-Saint-Nazaire estuary and its Estuaire art trail. Monumental sculptures dot the riverbanks and islands, creating an open-air museum where contemporary art converses with the working river.

days_plan.itin_title: Loire-Atlantique Road Trip: Nantes and the Jade Coast

visits.text: Paimpol port, labeled Port of Heritage Interest, was the departure point for the schooners of 'Iceland Fishermen' immortalized by Pierre Loti. Colorful houses of shipowners and privateers line the quays. The memorial to those lost at sea recalls the toll paid by Paimpol families.

Beauport Abbey, founded in 1202 by Premonstratensian canons, stands facing the bay and Bréhat archipelago. A stop on the Way of Saint James—scallop shells carved on the walls bear witness. The 120-hectare estate, owned by the Coastal Conservatory, shelters a heritage orchard of antique apple varieties.

Paimpol's historic center features cobbled streets, art galleries, and handsome granite townhouses. Place du Martray hosts the Tuesday market—Paimpol AOC white beans, scallops, blue lobsters, and farmhouse cider fill the stalls.

Bréhat Island, 3.5 km long and 1.5 km wide, was France's first classified natural site in 1907. No cars circulate here. The lush southern island resembles the Mediterranean with its agapanthus, mimosas, and fig trees. The wilder northern island spreads with heather and moorland facing the open sea.

Pont ar Prad, built by Vauban in the 18th century, links the two islands that form Bréhat. From this stone bridge, views embrace pink granite rocks, flowering gardens, and turquoise sea. The passage between the two islands is one of Brittany's most photographed spots.

Paon lighthouse, at the island's northern tip, dominates a spectacular granite chaos. Jagged rocks plunge into seas churned by currents. In heavy weather, waves can reach the cliff top. The lighthouse was rebuilt after its 1944 destruction.

Bréhat's citadel, built in the 17th century, now houses the Verreries de Bréhat glassworks. Glassblowers create unique pieces inspired by the island's colors—sea blue, granite pink, garden green. You can watch glassblowing demonstrations.

Port de la Corderie was the departure point for Newfoundland fishermen. In 1508, the Bonaventure was the first known European vessel to return from Newfoundland with holds full of cod—16 years after the 'discovery' of the Americas. Small pink granite houses still line the quays.

activities.text: Visit Beauport Abbey. The restored buildings host contemporary art exhibitions and artist residencies. The natural estate, freely accessible, unfolds between meadows, salt marshes, reed beds, and foreshore. Stunning views over Bréhat Island.

Hike the GR34 along the Plouézec and Plouha cliffs. Plouha's cliffs rise to 104 meters—Brittany's highest. The path passes the Shelburn monument, memorial to the escape of 135 Allied airmen during World War II.
Oyster tasting at an oyster farm on Pointe de Kérarzic, feet in the sand facing the bay. Paimpol oysters, award-winning several times, have a briny, meaty flavor.

Walk around Bréhat Island. Paths cross gardens where exotic plants grow—agapanthus, hydrangeas, eucalyptus—in a mild microclimate. Allow 3 to 4 hours for the complete circuit, with contemplative pauses.

Boat or sailing tour of the archipelago. The 86 islets and reefs of the Bréhat archipelago reveal themselves from the water. Cormorants to the east, sea bass and sand eels to the west, seals on isolated rocks. Navigation between buoys and currents is a spectacle in itself.

days_plan.itin_title: Brittany Road Trip: Historic Villages, Sea and Heritage
Côtes-d'Armor Road Trip: Dinan, Pink Granite Coast, Bréhat Island & Guerlédan Lake
Côtes-d'Armor Road Trip: Dinan, Pink Granite Coast, Bréhat Island & Guerlédan Lake

visits.text: The Abbey of Paimpont, founded in the 7th century and rebuilt in the 13th, is mirrored in the pond that borders the village. Its Romanesque portal, 17th-century woodwork, and ivory altarpiece classified as National Treasure deserve an extended stop.

Merlin's Tomb - two red slate slabs leaning against each other - hides in a beech undergrowth. Destroyed in 1894 by a treasure hunter, only these remains survive, still covered with offerings and little notes.

The Fountain of Barenton springs from a natural source in the middle of the forest. Legend says that if you pour its water on Merlin's stone - a neighboring granite slab - a storm immediately rises. Bubbles regularly rise from the bottom, a real geological phenomenon.

At the Val sans Retour, purple schist cliffs overlook a sunken valley where, according to legend, the fairy Morgana imprisoned unfaithful lovers. A water mirror stagnates at the bottom - the 'Fairies' Mirror.'
The Golden Tree, a gold-leaf sculpture planted in 1991 after a devastating fire, stands among five charred trunks. This memorial recalls the 400 hectares of forest destroyed that year.

In Tréhorenteuc, the Sainte-Onenne church blends Christianity and Arthurian legend. The Holy Grail stained glass window, the Val sans Retour paintings, and the white stag mosaic make this small church a unique case in France.

activities.text: Walk the Val sans Retour loop from the Tréhorenteuc parking lot. The trail climbs to 'Merlin's Seat' (viewpoint over the valley), descends to the Fairies' Mirror, and reaches the Golden Tree.
Reach the Fountain of Barenton via the forest trail from the hamlet of Folle Pensée. The path crosses a stand of centuries-old beeches bathed in a mystical atmosphere.
Circle the Paimpont pond by bike or on foot. Abbey reflections on the water, water lilies, and gray herons create a peaceful tableau opposite to the forest's dark legends.

days_plan.itin_title: Brittany Road Trip: Historic Villages, Sea and Heritage

visits.text: The Palais de la Découverte, installed in the west wing of the Grand Palais since its creation in 1937 by physicist Jean Perrin, is an interactive, participatory science museum. Planetarium, static electricity demonstrations, atomic reconstruction: here science is touchable, hands-on, explained by live demonstrators. If you're visiting Paris with children or teenagers, this is the museum not to miss in the 8th arrondissement. Allow 2 hours to enjoy the demonstrations.

activities.text: After the Palais de la Découverte, head down Avenue Montaigne to glimpse the façades of the great fashion houses—Dior at no. 30, boutiques of Parisian labels. A terrace café on the avenue remains one of the most classic spots for people-watching in the 8th.

days_plan.itin_title: The Great Museums of Paris

places.name: Pantin

visits.text: The Magasins Généraux, former customs warehouses built in 1929, rise in concrete silhouette along the Ourcq canal. Nicknamed the "cathedral of graffiti" before their conversion, they now host exhibitions and cultural events.
The Grands Moulins de Pantin, a former industrial flour mill, bears witness to the town's manufacturing past. Its brick facade towers over the canal banks.
The National Dance Center occupies Pantin's former administrative headquarters. Its rehearsal studios regularly open to the public for work-in-progress presentations.
The Church of Saint-Germain, in the heart of old Pantin, preserves a 13th-century bell tower and 19th-century stained glass. The square overlooks the quiet lanes of the old village.
Head to Place de la Pointe, a public space designed between the harbor basin and the Magasins Généraux. Engravings on the ground recall the site's port history.

activities.text: Follow the Ourcq canal by bike or on foot from La Villette to Pantin. The towpath alternates between street art murals, cultural barges, and floating terraces.
The Bas-Pantin neighborhood teems with artists' studios installed in former warehouses. Push through open doors during dedicated events to discover ceramicists, painters, and designers.
The canal banks host guinguettes and barges in summer. Settle on a waterside terrace to watch the ballet of joggers, cyclists, and pleasure boats.

days_plan.itin_title: Seine-Saint-Denis Circuit: Royal Basilica and Urban Culture

map_keywords: Centre National de la Danse Pantin

visits.text: The Basilica of the Sacred Heart of Paray-le-Monial, built between the 11th and 12th centuries, is considered the "scale model" of Cluny Abbey before its destruction. The three-story elevation and play of light in the choir reproduce Cluniac aesthetics. Its stone seems gilded by sunlight. The recently restored north portal is a jewel of Romanesque ornamentation with Moorish-inspired motifs. It's Saône-et-Loire's most-visited site, drawing 450,000 visitors annually.

The Chapel of the Apparitions, at the Visitation Convent, preserves the relics of Marguerite-Marie Alacoque. Between 1673 and 1675, the young mystic received three "Great Apparitions" of Christ showing his flaming heart. The cult of the Sacred Heart, born here, spread throughout the Catholic Church.

The Cloister Garden, adjoining the basilica, is medieval-inspired. The mosaic sculpture "Lames de pluie" (Rain Blades) plays with light and reflections. The former town hall, in an elegant Renaissance building, houses the Musée du Hiéron devoted to sacred art.

activities.text: Guided tour of the basilica and sanctuary. Guides explain the Cluniac architecture, capitals, and history of the Sacred Heart. Audio guides available in multiple languages.

Walk or cycle along the Canal du Centre. The towpaths offer a peaceful route on foot or by bike. This 70-mile canal links the Saône to the Loire—barges still ply its waters. Narrated cruises available.

Circuit of Romanesque churches in the Brionnais and Charolais. In half a day, you can visit three to five Romanesque chapels in the surrounding hills. Golden stone, sculpted capitals, and bocage landscapes—a concentrate of Romanesque Burgundy.

days_plan.itin_title: Saône Valley, France: Medieval Abbeys, Wine and Comtois Heritage
Saône-et-Loire Road Trip: Bibracte, Cluny, Solutré & the Mâconnais Vineyards
Burgundy's 15 Most Beautiful Villages

visits.text: Oil heritage: Discovery of Western Europe's largest oil field discovered on March 25, 1954. The deposit extends between 2,000 and 2,500m depth beneath Lake Parentis-Biscarrosse. View from the shores of drilling installations, wellheads and derricks operated by Canadian company Vermilion producing a few thousand barrels per day. This unique activity in the Landes financed the village's development.

Roland Portalier Arena: Built in 1927 and completely renovated in 2021, the only such structure in the northern Landes. Typical Landes arena architecture with wooden stands. The Feria Sen Bertomiu in August gathers thousands of festayres for course landaise, concerts and bandas over 5 days. Free exterior visits outside events.

Lake Parentis-Biscarrosse: 3,540 hectares shared with Biscarrosse, Gastes and Sainte-Eulalie. Lake beaches Moutéou Pipiou and Calède with fine sand and fresh waters. Picnic tables and children's games. Spectacular sunsets over the oil installations creating a unique atmosphere. 15th-century Saint-Pierre church in the village center.

activities.text: Lake fishing: Paradise for predator anglers (pike, zander, perch) and carp. Numerous developed spots along the banks. Boats and float tubes authorized. Fishing permit required, sold at tobacco shop. Catch-and-release recommended to preserve stock. Best months: April-May and September-October.

Lake beach swimming: Moutéou Pipiou beach (tables, children's games) and Calède (wilder). Fresh warm waters in summer, pleasant temperature higher than ocean. Lifeguards in July-August. Sandy gently sloping bottom. Ideal for young children and swimming beginners.

Water sports: Paddleboard, canoe-kayak, pedal boat rental. The lake's calm waters perfect for beginners. Water sports centers on main beaches. Lessons and courses in July-August. Life jackets provided. Exploration of quiet coves and lake wildlife observation.

Bike paths around the lake: Loops connecting Biscarrosse and neighboring villages through the forest. Flat shaded routes. Viewpoints over the lake and oil installations. Bike rental in the village. Bring water and picnic for longer outings.

days_plan.itin_title: Landes Road Trip: Atlantic Coast, Pine Forests & Gascon Heritage

visits.text: Start at Trocadéro—it's the finest vantage point for the Eiffel Tower, especially in the morning when light strikes the iron lattice head-on. The esplanade offers an unobstructed panorama over the tower, the Champ-de-Mars, and the École Militaire. Walk down the terraced gardens to the Trocadéro fountains for that classic postcard shot.

Cross the Pont d'Iéna to reach the Eiffel Tower. Built by Gustave Eiffel for the 1889 World's Fair, it stands 330 meters tall with its antenna. Book your tickets online—queues regularly exceed 2 hours without a reservation. If you're going up, the second level offers the best balance between view and vertigo. The summit (276 m) is breathtaking but often windy.

Walk up the Champs-Élysées from Place de la Concorde. This 1.9 km avenue connects the Luxor Obelisk (a gift from Egypt in 1829) to the Arc de Triomphe. Climb to the top of the Arc—284 steps—for the view over the twelve radiating avenues. Napoleon commissioned its construction in 1806 after Austerlitz, but never saw the monument completed (1836).

Morning at the Louvre—the world's largest museum with 380,000 works of which 35,000 are on display. Arrive at opening (9 a.m.) via the Carrousel entrance (less crowded than the Pyramid). Priorities: the Mona Lisa (Denon wing, 1st floor), the Venus de Milo, and the Winged Victory of Samothrace. Allow at least 3 hours, even if skimming.

After the Louvre, stroll through the Tuileries Garden to Place de la Concorde. This 25-hectare garden, created in 1564 for Catherine de' Medici, is the oldest in Paris. The green chairs around the central basin are the perfect spot to catch your breath.

Late afternoon, dive into the Marais—the best-preserved quarter of old Paris. Place des Vosges, inaugurated in 1612 by Henri IV, is the capital's oldest royal square. Pass by the Marché des Enfants Rouges on rue de Bretagne—Paris's oldest covered market (1615). You'll find international food stalls and excellent caterers.

Start at Île de la Cité. Notre-Dame Cathedral, devastated by the fire of April 15, 2019, reopened to the public in December 2024 after 5 years of restoration. The new spire reproduces Viollet-le-Duc's design. If you visit, book a time slot—entry is free but regulated.

Cross over to the Latin Quarter—Paris's oldest student district, centered on the Sorbonne founded in 1257. Stop by Shakespeare & Company bookshop (opposite Notre-Dame), opened in 1951 by George Whitman. Walk up to the Panthéon, a former church turned national mausoleum—Voltaire, Rousseau, Hugo, Curie, and Joséphine Baker rest here.

Late in the day, climb to Montmartre. The Sacré-Coeur basilica, completed in 1914, dominates Paris from atop the butte (130 m). The view from the forecourt is among the city's finest. Then descend toward Place du Tertre (the portrait artists), rue Lepic, and the Moulin de la Galette—the neighborhood where Toulouse-Lautrec, Renoir, and Picasso lived.

Day trip to Versailles—40 minutes on RER C from Paris. Book your tickets online with a time slot. The Hall of Mirrors, 73 meters long, features 357 mirrors facing 17arched windows. This is where the Treaty of Versailles was signed on June 28, 1919. The King's and Queen's Grand Apartments are just as impressive as the Hall—don't rush through them.

The Versailles gardens cover 800 hectares—you'll only see a fraction. The Grand Canal (1.5 km long), groves, and fountains are the work of André Le Nôtre. If you're there between April and October, aim for the Musical Fountain Shows days (Tuesday and weekends): the fountains operate to music.

If you have the energy, push on to the Grand Trianon and Petit Trianon. The Petit Trianon was Marie Antoinette's refuge—she had a hamlet built there with a farm, dairy, and mill to play at being a shepherdess. It's a 20-minute walk from the château, or take the little train.

activities.text: In the evening, return to the foot of the Eiffel Tower for the light show—every hour after nightfall, 20,000 bulbs sparkle for 5 minutes. Settle on the Champ-de-Mars with a picnic—it's a Parisian ritual.

Foodie walk through the Marais—try a falafel on rue des Rosiers (the queues speak for themselves), a pastry at Jacques Genin, or a coffee in one of the quarter's hidden courtyards. The Marais is also the district for art galleries and concept stores.

In the evening, walk down the Montmartre steps toward Pigalle and soak up the district's nightlife—cocktail bars, concert halls, bohemian vibes. Or head back to the Seine embankments for a stroll along the pedestrianized quays, illuminated after dark.
