Golden-stone medieval dwellings tier in concentric arcs from the seigneurial tower at the summit down to the old port below. From the bridge across the Lot, the overall view of the terraced citadel and its reflection in the water forms the village's iconic image.

The 13th-century episcopal tower rises 23 meters—the last vestige of the bishops' palace of Cahors, who gave the town its name in 1228. Its four levels, connected by a staircase within the wall thickness, served as a watchtower. The top floor preserves a fireplace and latrines carved into the stone.

The Church of Saint-Sauveur, a Historic Monument from the 14th–15th centuries, occupies an unusual position: outside the castrum, on the heights. Its Gothic nave with ribbed vaults and its bell tower—a former watchtower—dominate the valley. A remarkable feature: the church possesses the Lot department's only historic carillon, composed of 15 bells, the oldest dating from 1632.

Descend through the hanging gardens, created in 2016 on the site of ruined medieval houses. This terraced pedestrian pathway connects the upper town to the river, punctuated by rest stops with open views across the Lot Valley and the citadel rooftops.

At the village base, Rue de la Cale leads to the old port where gabarres moored, loaded with wine barrels bound for Bordeaux. Street names preserve the memory of former trades: boatmen, nailsmiths, dyers, washerwomen. On Place Guillaume de Cardaillac, admire medieval houses from the 13th–16th centuries and a beautiful half-timbered dwelling.

activities.text: Board an electric gabare at the foot of the village. This replica of 19th-century boats glides silently on the Lot. The guide recounts river navigation history while vineyards, cliffs, and mills slip past.

Puy-l'Évêque is the capital of the Cahors wine region. Malbec, planted here since Roman times, produces deep-colored, structured AOC wines. Several estates open their cellars to visitors for tastings led by the winemaker.
The Lot Valley cycle route winds between vineyards and river. From Puy-l'Évêque, ride toward Albas and its steep lanes, or toward Luzech and its peninsula, for panoramas of the Lot's meanders.

days_plan.itin_title: Lot Itinerary: Cahors, Rocamadour & the Lot Valley

visits.text: The Wild Coast stretches for 5 miles between Pointe du Percho and Château Turpault. The Atlantic sculpts cliffs and coves beneath pounding waves. The GR 34 coastal path follows the shoreline.
At Pointe du Percho, the guardhouse ruins overlook the cliffs. This military building was once connected to an artillery battery. Spectacular views of Atlantic rollers.
The Port Blanc arch pierces through the coastal rock. This natural formation has become one of the peninsula's most photographed landmarks. At low tide, walk right up to the arch's base.

Château Turpault marks the entrance to the Wild Coast. This Anglo-medieval manor was built in 1904 by a textile manufacturer from Cholet. It's private property but makes a striking photograph from the road.

Pointe du Conguel extends out for more than half a mile. The trail circles this entire tongue of land. Views of the Teignouse lighthouse, Belle-Île, Houat and Hoëdic islands. Ruins of seaweed ovens dot the slabs.
Port Haliguen combines a fishing harbor with a pleasure marina. The 36-foot lighthouse was inaugurated in 1856.

activities.text: Ride the Tire-Bouchon, a tourist train that links Auray to Quiberon along the coast. The journey travels the entire peninsula with sea views.
Grande Plage stretches out facing Belle-Île-en-Mer. This south-facing beach offers supervised swimming in summer. Tiralo accessibility chairs available for people with reduced mobility.
From Port-Maria, catch ferries to Belle-Île-en-Mer, Houat, or Hoëdic. Boats make several daily crossings in season.

days_plan.itin_title: Morbihan Road Trip: Gulf, Carnac & Breton Coast

visits.text: Rennes-le-Château, population 89 on a rocky spur at 450 meters altitude, is France's most mysterious village. It all begins with Abbé Bérenger Saunière, appointed parish priest of this poor parish in 1885: from 1891 onward, he spent considerable sums renovating the church, building a luxurious villa (Villa Béthanie), a crenellated neo-Gothic tower (Tour Magdala), gardens and an orangery — all in the name of his housekeeper Marie Dénarnaud. Where did the money come from? Visigoth treasure, Templar gold, Cathar riches, Church secrets? Nobody knows. Saunière died in 1917 without revealing anything. Enter the Church of Sainte-Marie-Madeleine: right at the porch, a Latin inscription warns — "This place is terrible" — and on the left a devil named Asmodeus, guardian of treasures, supports the holy water font. The church is free.

Then climb to Tour Magdala, built from 1900 at the hill's edge. Saunière used it as a library and meditation retreat. From the crenellated platform, you can see some twenty villages of the Corbières and Pyrenees. The estate (villa, gardens, presbytery converted to museum, rampart walk) charges admission — allow 1.5 hours to see everything.

On the return route toward Quillan, don't miss the Gorges de l'Aude — the canyon between Quillan and Axat, including the Gorges de Pierre-Lys (road carved into the cliff) and the Gorges de Saint-Georges, two spectacular passages that take you along the river between limestone walls rising tens of meters high.

activities.text: Operators in Axat and Quillan offer rafting descents on the Aude ranging from 7 to 17 km. Class III-IV rapids, passages named "triple chute" and "flipper". Accessible from age 6 or 12 depending on routes. Book the day before or morning — the activity depends on river flow.

days_plan.itin_title: Aude Road Trip: Carcassonne, Cathar Country & the Mediterranean

visits.text: Saint-Corentin Cathedral dominates Quimper's skyline with its twin spires soaring 75-80 meters high, built between 1854 and 1856 through a public subscription — the people of Quimper themselves funded the completion after centuries of waiting. Construction began in 1239 under Bishop Raynaud, but the building stretched across centuries until the 15th. The nave measures 92 meters long and rises 20 meters to the vaulted ceiling. Look for the equestrian statue of King Gradlon, Quimper's legendary founder, perched between the two spires — an architectural quirk unique in France. Step inside to see the 15th-century stained glass depicting the lords of Cornouaille, miraculously preserved through wars and revolutions. The south spire opens for visits on the last Sunday of each month off-season and every Sunday in summer — book ahead at the Maison du Patrimoine.

The Fine Arts Museum, housed in the town hall adjacent to the cathedral, holds one of Brittany's richest collections: paintings from the Pont-Aven School (Gauguin, Émile Bernard), drawings by Quimper native Max Jacob, and an entire section devoted to faïence pottery. Budget at least 90 minutes if you have any appreciation for Breton painting.

Stroll down Rue Kéréon, Quimper's main medieval artery, lined with 15th and 16th-century half-timbered houses whose corbeled facades and hydrangeas lean inward as if sharing secrets. Cross through the old bishop's ramparts to reach the bishop's gardens with views of the cathedral's apse. Quimper has been designated a City of Art and History — its street layout traces directly back to the intersection of two Roman roads. End at the covered market halls for a break with local charcuterie or a Breton crêpe.

Twenty kilometers from Quimper, Concarneau's walled town (ville close) ranks among Brittany's best-preserved fortified settlements. Perched on an islet 350 meters long and 100 wide in the middle of the harbor, it's linked to the mainland by two small bridges. The granite ramparts — 2.5 to 3 meters thick, punctuated by 9 towers — earned historic monument status as early as 1899, thanks to a petition from residents. Vauban came to modernize the fortifications in 1694; his work was completed that same year according to the archives. Walk the rampart path for sweeping views across the fishing harbor and the bay. Avoid July-August if you can't stand crowds — but arriving by ferry from the Lanriec quarter, a short 200-meter crossing, is a delight in any season.

The Fishing Museum, in the heart of the walled town, occupies a former arsenal and chronicles the history of Concarneau, France's third-largest fishing port. Ship models, antique nets, and a section aboard a real lobster boat moored along the ramparts complete the visit. Allow 75 to 90 minutes.

About twenty kilometers away, the thatched cottages of Kerascoët in Névez are among Finistère's most photographed architectural curiosities: a handful of houses with thick thatched caps, rough granite walls, and hydrangeas spilling over the edges form a hamlet that looks as though it stepped from a Breton fairy tale. It's one of the department's best-preserved thatched hamlets — allow 30 minutes for a stroll, brief but unforgettable.

Port Manec'h, at the mouth of the Aven River, is one of southern Finistère's most photogenic corners. White bathing cabins line the beach facing the bay; from the point, the view sweeps across the Aven estuary and maritime pine forests. If you're drawn to Gauguin and the Pont-Aven School, the village of Pont-Aven itself is 10 km away — the museum dedicated to this 19th-century painters' colony is worth the detour.

From Quimper, set out for the Crozon Peninsula — 80 km of road that plunges you into territory where cliffs, heathland, and turquoise waters rival any Atlantic coast. Begin at Cap de la Chèvre, the peninsula's southernmost point, more than 40 meters above the sea. The memorial atop the cliffs honors naval aviation sailors. On clear days, the panorama sweeps across Douarnenez Bay to the south and Pointe du Raz on the horizon.

Morgat is the peninsula's marina, framed by a long fine-sand beach. Local operators offer boat excursions to the sea caves — six caves carved into the granite cliff face, some colored with ochre and lilac. Arrive early: departures are in the morning.

Camaret-sur-Mer is the Crozon Peninsula's capital — and the boat graveyard lining the Sillon is one of Brittany's most photographed sites. The hulks of lobster boats beached on the shore, some dating from the 1950s-1960s, tell the story of the end of the peninsula's deep-sea fishing ports. Beside it, the 17th-century chapel of Notre-Dame-de-Rocamadour houses boat models suspended as ex-votos — gifts from sailors saved from shipwreck. The ensemble sits on the Sillon, a natural dike protecting the harbor.

Camaret's Tour Vauban, called the 'golden tower' for the ochre color of its coating, was built to protect the entrance to the Brest roadstead against invasion. Constructed in the 17th century, it's part of Vauban's fortifications inscribed on the UNESCO World Heritage list in 2008. Inside, an interpretation center traces the peninsula's maritime life. Don't bother climbing in fog — the view over the harbor is far better from the rampart walk.

A 10-minute walk from the harbor, the Lagatjar alignments gather 143 Breton standing stones on a heath, one of Finistère's best-preserved megalithic ensembles. Re-erected in 1928, some menhirs reach over 3 meters tall. A surprise in this very coastal corner — few visitors venture here.

Pointe de Pen Hir is the peninsula's showpiece. At 63 meters above the Iroise Sea, the cliffs plunge vertically toward the Tas de Pois — six rocky islets emerging from the waves in a line. A 15-meter-tall Cross of Lorraine stands at the summit, erected in homage to the Bretons who joined de Gaulle in London, refusing the 1940 capitulation. Settle on the rocks at sunset — in clear weather, the raking light on the cliffs and sea foam is spectacular.

activities.text: The coastal walk from Port Manec'h toward neighboring coves follows a section of the GR34, the customs officers' trail, heading toward Rospico cove — translucent water and white pebbles, accessible at mid-tide. The trail is marked in red and white; allow 2 hours round-trip for a pleasant loop.

Walking the rampart path of Concarneau's walled town: the promenade atop the ramparts, freely accessible, circles the entire citadel with successive views of the fishing port, the marina, and the bay. Allow 45 minutes for the complete circuit — more if you stop at each of the 9 towers.

The GR34 trail between Saint-Hernot and the tip of Cap de la Chèvre offers a succession of viewpoints over Douarnenez Bay and Cornouaille. The complete loop of Cap de la Chèvre (from Morgat to Château de Dinan) makes a full day's hike; if you only have a few hours, a round trip from Saint-Hernot to the point is thoroughly satisfying. Wear closed shoes — the ground is often damp.

Operators offer kayak and canoe rentals from Morgat to explore sheltered coves and approach the sea caves from the water. The activity depends on weather — ask for forecasts at the Crozon tourist office before setting out. Outings typically last 2 to 3 hours.

days_plan.itin_title: Finistère, Brittany Road Trip: Breton Headlands, Ponant Islands & Coastal Heritage
Brittany Road Trip: Historic Villages, Sea and Heritage
Brittany Road Trip: Pink Granite Coast, Legendary Lighthouses & Wild Beaches
Brittany Road Trip: Pink Granite Coast, Legendary Lighthouses & Wild Beaches

visits.text: The Château de Rambouillet, a 14th-century fortress turned royal then presidential residence, preserves exceptional rocaille woodwork and the chamber where François I died in 1547. The first G6 summit was held here in 1975.

The Queen's Dairy, commissioned by Louis XVI for Marie-Antoinette between 1785 and 1787, houses Pierre Julien's sculptural group "Amalthea and the Nymph" in a neoclassical setting of marble and stucco.
The Shell Cottage conceals beneath its thatched roof one of Europe's finest mother-of-pearl and seashell decorations, created between 1779 and 1780 for the Princess de Lamballe.

The National Sheepfold, created by Louis XVI in 1786, houses France's only flock of Rambouillet merinos, direct descendants of sheep imported from Spain. Cows, goats, horses, and poultry complete the educational farm.

activities.text: Hike in Rambouillet forest, 14,550 hectares of oaks and pines classified as a "protected forest." The Étang d'Or trail or the Coupe-Gorge loop are among the most pleasant routes.
Visit Espace Rambouillet to observe deer, does, roe deer, and wild boar in semi-freedom across 250 hectares of forest. The Forest of Eagles features a birds of prey free-flight show.
Stroll through the château gardens, 150 hectares labeled "Remarkable Garden" with canals, six islands, a French garden, and an 18th-century English garden. Park access is free.

days_plan.itin_title: Yvelines Road Trip: Versailles and Impressionist Villages

visits.text: Enter the heart of Réalmont to discover this royal bastide founded in 1272 on Philip the Bold's orders. The arcaded square, bordered by a double row of covered galleries, preserves the geometric rigor typical of medieval bastides.

Notre-Dame-du-Taur church occupies a unique place: born from a 17th-century Protestant temple, converted to a Catholic church in 1628. Its quadrangular bell tower dates from 1712, and the nave shelters four richly decorated chapels on each side.

Spot the Fontaine de la Fréjaire, a traditional 17th-century spring, and the 1880 wash-house. These small heritage elements mark a signposted historical circuit passing corbelled houses and traces of former fortified gates.

Walk the perpendicular streets, signature of southwestern bastides. Here, every façade tells of builders past: half-timbered houses, upper floors wider than ground level, details carved in brick and stone.
The small Protestant temple, discreet but historically charged, recalls that Réalmont was the scene of conflicts between Catholics and Huguenots during the Wars of Religion.

activities.text: Wednesday mornings, Réalmont hosts the Tarn's oldest market. Local producers spread cured meats, cheeses, fruits, and vegetables beneath the arcades. Friendly atmosphere guaranteed.
Reach the waterfalls of Arifat, a few kilometers southeast of Réalmont. A wooded trail leads to these forest cascades, ideal for a refreshing break.
Go cycling or mountain biking on the rolling trails of central Tarn. Lake Bancalié, 15 minutes from Réalmont, offers canoeing, pedal boats, and fishing in a preserved natural setting.

days_plan.itin_title: Tarn Road Trip: Albi & Hilltop Villages

visits.text: Notre-Dame Cathedral soars skyward with 2,303 statues crowning its facade. Built in the 13th century, this Gothic masterpiece witnessed the coronation of 29 French kings.
The Palais du Tau houses the cathedral's glittering treasury. Once the archbishop's residence, it transformed into a royal palace on coronation days.
Step inside the Basilique Saint-Remi, where Romanesque meets Gothic. Its 11th-century nave leads to a soaring Gothic choir, where Saint Remi's tomb takes center stage.

activities.text: Descend into the Taittinger cellars, carved from ancient Gallo-Roman chalk quarries 20 meters beneath the city streets.
Wander through 18 kilometers of Pommery cellars, where contemporary art installations emerge from the chalk alongside aging champagne.
Explore the Veuve Clicquot caves, where ventilation chimneys pierce through the chalk right up to street level above.

days_plan.itin_title: Marne Road Trip: Reims, Épernay and the Champagne Route

visits.text: At Relanges, the church of Notre-Dame is an extremely rare vestige of 11th-century Romanesque art in southern Lorraine. A Cluniac priory was founded here around 1030 by Ricuin de Darney and his wife Lancède — in the pure Cluniac tradition of intercession for the souls of the deceased. The priory disappeared without a trace, but the church's facade gable is one of the rare 11th-century remnants still standing in the region. The nave and side aisles were rebuilt in the 16th century. Don't miss the lavoir d'en-haut in the village, and the Château de Lichecourt if you like discreet ruins at the bottom of meadows.

7 km north, the ruins of the priory of Bonneval at Saint-Baslemont deserve a stop for lovers of abandoned sites. Only walls and the outlines of a former church remain, but the chapel of Notre-Dame-des-Neiges (1861) still stands as guardian of the place. In Saint-Baslemont itself, the 16th-century church of Saint-Jean-Baptiste and the remains of the 11th-century Tour Séchelles complete the visit.

activities.text: This part of the southern Vosges — bocage, agricultural plateaus, hamlets in blond stone — is one of the least frequented in the entire department. Take the opportunity to simply walk from Relanges on the marked trails around the village. The light in late afternoon over these meadows has something special, especially in May-June. The calm is complete.

days_plan.itin_title: Vosges Abbey Trail: Monastic Heritage Unveiled

visits.text: Remiremont has a particularity that few French towns can claim: its 18th-century canonical quarter has remained practically intact. The noble canonesses who populated this chapter — reserved for the aristocracy, it wasn't within everyone's reach — lived in these elegant residences around the abbey church of Saint-Pierre. The Romanesque crypt of the abbey is one of the last witnesses to the monastery founded in the 7th century on the neighboring Saint-Mont by the monks Amé and Romaric. Audio guides are available at the Tourist Office to explore the quarter at your own pace.

The Charles Friry Museum is going to surprise you. Installed in a canonical residence built in 1750 for the last dean of the chapter, it preserves the collection assembled by Charles Friry, prosecutor to King Louis-Philippe who bought it in 1833 and for fifty years accumulated paintings and art objects there. The centerpiece: The Hurdy-Gurdy Player with a Bag by Georges de La Tour — one of forty known works by this 17th-century Lorraine painter, long obscure before being rediscovered in the 20th century. Added are prints by Rembrandt, Callot and Goya. The garden preserves two 18th-century rocaille fountains classified as Historic Monuments. Open Wednesday to Sunday.

activities.text: Stroll along Rue de la Xavée, lined with porticoed arcades, one of the most picturesque in Lorraine. End on the quays of the Moselle at the end of the day. For a panoramic view of the town and valley, climb to the chapel of Notre-Dame du Calvaire — the climb takes about twenty minutes from the center, and the view over the rooftops and wooded hills is well worth the effort to close this itinerary in style.

days_plan.itin_title: Vosges Mountains, France: Alpine Lakes, Spa Towns and Forest Heritage
Vosges Abbey Trail: Monastic Heritage Unveiled

visits.text: Rencurel sits at the outlet of the Bourne gorges, where the valley opens onto meadows and hamlets scattered through forests. The village preserves a chapel and rural architecture typical of the Vercors—steep roofs, gallery farms, and stone walls. It's a transition village between the limestone cliff Royans and the gentler plateau that heralds Villard-de-Lans.

From the viewpoint above the village, you have a plunging view of the Bourne gorges you arrived through. The wooded ridges of the Quatre Montagnes extend to the east. It's a different framing from the cave: here, it's the overall geography of the Vercors that imposes itself.

activities.text: The hamlets and clearings loop around Rencurel (4-6 km, minimal elevation gain) crosses meadows and forest edges between the commune's scattered hamlets. In May-June, meadows blooming with gentians and wild orchids are spectacular. This kind of pastoral mid-mountain landscape is exactly what justifies the designation "Vercors Regional Natural Park."

The round trip to the gorges viewpoint from the village heights (about 45 minutes) follows a marked forest path. The view of the Bourne gorge cut between cliffs gives the measure of the relief you've just traversed from Pont-en-Royans.

days_plan.itin_title: Royans-Vercors Road Trip: Hilltop Villages and Gorges

visits.text: Parliament of Brittany.
Mordelaise Gates.
Medieval castle (one of the largest in Europe).
Old upper and lower town.

activities.text: Les Lices Market (Saturday morning).
Champs Libres (museum).
Thabor (garden).

days_plan.itin_title: Ille-et-Vilaine, Brittany: Saint-Malo and Medieval Walled Towns

visits.text: Rennes-le-Château — Treasure & esotericism.
Abbey of Saint-Papoul — Nocturnal chants.

activities.text: Access: public (RLC)
Access: public (Saint-Papoul)

days_plan.itin_title: Occitanie Mysteries Road Trip: Cathar Legends & Hidden Secrets of Southern France

visits.text: Church of Saint-Nicolas. This 16th-century Flamboyant Gothic church dominates the town with its high square tower. The interior shelters beautiful Renaissance stained glass windows and remarkable liturgical furnishings. Ribbed vaults and side chapels testify to the prosperity of this merchant town on the route to Flanders.

Museum of the Rethel Region. Housed in the former town hall, the museum presents local history from Gallo-Roman times. Archaeological collections, sacred art objects, and testimonies from both World Wars tell the turbulent past of this frontier town. The section dedicated to boudin blanc evokes Rethel's gastronomic renown.

Old town and built heritage. Pedestrian streets reveal ancient stone and brick dwellings typical of Ardennes architecture. The central square bordered by arcades once hosted the three annual fairs that enriched the town. Restored half-timbered houses stand alongside 18th-century private mansions.

activities.text: Tasting boudin blanc de Rethel. World capital of this specialty benefiting from PGI certification, Rethel has perpetuated since the 17th century the recipe for this delicate dish. Composed solely of pork, milk, eggs, and shallots without breadcrumbs or starch, it's enjoyed pan-fried in butter accompanied by apple compote. Artisan charcuteries also offer truffle or forest mushroom versions.

Traditional market. The weekly market unfolds its stalls of farm products, cheeses, cured meats, and Ardennes specialties. Local producers offer seasonal vegetables, honey, craft beers, and homemade pastries. The friendly atmosphere and authentic flavors invite discovery of the terroir.

Stroll along the Ardennes Canal. Follow the shaded banks of this waterway connecting the Meuse to the Aisne over 88 km. The restored towpath offers a flat route ideal for walking or cycling between locks and old bridges. Observe pleasure barges, fishermen, and aquatic birds in a peaceful atmosphere.

days_plan.itin_title: Ardennes Road Trip: Forests & Fortresses (9 Days)

places.name: Revin

visits.text: Viewpoint of the Dames de Meuse. Perched at 393 meters altitude, this belvedere offers vertiginous panoramas over the loop the Meuse draws 200 meters below. The river almost completely encircles the Dames peninsula in a spectacular meander. Legend tells that three sisters transformed into rocks eternally watch over the valley.

Museum of Old Revin. Housed in an ancient dwelling, the museum traces the industrial history of this working-class town marked by metallurgy. Collections of tools, period photographs, and testimonies evoke work in forges and rolling mills. The section devoted to slate recalls the importance of this extractive industry in the massif.

activities.text: Hike in the Ardennes forest. Steep trails climb toward ridges dominating the Meuse through century-old beech and oak groves. Outcropping rocks, deep ravines, and fern undergrowth compose a wild and mysterious setting. Viewpoints reward effort with grandiose panoramas over the narrow valley.

Canoeing on the Meuse. Descend the river with the current between steep cliffs and forests plunging into the water. The tranquil route allows observation of riparian wildlife and access to viewpoints unreachable by road. Rental companies offer various packages from a few hours to a full day.

Climbing on schistose rocks. Schist and quartzite cliffs attract climbers to equipped routes of all levels. Walls overhanging the Meuse offer spectacular setting and quality rock. Climbing sites are distributed throughout the valley with free access for autonomous practitioners.

days_plan.itin_title: Ardennes Road Trip: Forests & Fortresses (9 Days)

map_keywords: Point de Vue des Dames de Meuse Revin

visits.text: Ribeauvillé plays in a category of its own: its Christmas market isn't 'Alsatian,' it's medieval. Just two weekends in December (the 6th-7th and 13th-14th), the town known as the City of Minstrels travels back several centuries. Beggars, peasants, jugglers, troubadours, and fire-breathers in period costumes wander the cobbled lanes, you eat wild boar roasted on a spit and drink cervoise (ale) brewed using ancient methods — all imported products are strictly banned. The town has even prohibited tour buses to preserve the atmosphere: here, you take your time. Park at the Cordon company's park-and-ride lot (exit 20 from the A35) and take the free shuttle to the center.

Ribeauvillé takes its name from the Ribeaupierre family, 13th-century lords who built three castles on hills overlooking the town: Saint-Ulrich, Girsberg, and Haut-Ribeaupierre. The Three Castles circuit (GR5 trail, about 2.5 hours round-trip) is possible even in December for equipped hikers — the view over the village and snow-covered vineyard from the ruins is worth the effort. At the town's foot, the church of Notre-Dame de Dusenbach, 2 km away, is a medieval haven of peace few visitors know.

activities.text: Immerse yourself in the medieval market atmosphere without frantically photographing everything — chat with artisans who weave, forge, or carve before you, taste cervoise by brazier light, let the fire-breathers dazzle you. It's the Alsatian market that requires the most effort — and leaves the most singular memory. If you're there on a Sunday evening, the nocturnal parade with fire-breathers concludes with fireworks.

Ribeauvillé's Grand-rue houses Renaissance fountains and the Tour des Bouchers — a former medieval tower — recalling the history of the Ribeaupierre Seigneury. The Pfifferdaj, the Minstrels' Festival held each first Sunday in September, is Alsace's oldest festival. Even in December, you'll find Pfiffer (fife player) statues scattered through the lanes recalling the founding legend.

days_plan.itin_title: The 15 Most Beautiful Villages of Alsace
Alsace's Most Beautiful Christmas Markets

visits.text: Richelieu is a unique masterpiece of 17th-century urban planning. Founded in 1631 by the Cardinal and designed by Jacques Lemercier (architect of the Sorbonne), this "ideal city" follows a grid plan enclosed by ramparts and moats, accessible through three monumental gates.

Walk down the Grande Rue, 12 meters wide, lined with 28 identical townhouses—all listed as Historic Monuments. Regularity, symmetry, and perfect alignment: the Cardinal's spirit is readable in every façade.

The market halls, completed in 1638, are a fine example of functional architecture from the Grand Siècle. They still host a market on Friday mornings, opposite the Notre-Dame-de-l'Assomption Church in classical style.

Step into the Espace Richelieu, housed in a restored townhouse, for an interactive exhibit on the Cardinal, his city, and new towns. 3D glasses let you immerse yourself in the vanished splendor of the château.

The Richelieu Museum displays art collections that belonged to the Cardinal, a great patron and collector: portraits by Philippe de Champaigne, engravings by Jean Marot showing the château as it was, and a model of the town.

Stroll through the château park, 475 hectares enclosed by walls. Of the palace that inspired Versailles, what remains is the Dome Pavilion, the orangery, the cellars, and the entrance bridge. Nearly 2,000 workers labored on this titanic construction project.

activities.text: Hop on a bike along the Richelieu-Chinon Greenway, 20 km on a former railway line through unspoiled nature in the Loire-Anjou-Touraine Regional Nature Park.
Walk around the ramparts and moats. The fourth gate, a fake, was created solely to respect the ensemble's symmetry—a detail that sums up the Cardinal's spirit.
In summer, browse the craft shops and antique dealers installed in some fifteen boutiques around the city.

days_plan.itin_title: Vienne Road Trip: Futuroscope and Romanesque Heritage

visits.text: The Cathedral of Sainte-Marie in Rieux, despite its small size, was the seat of a bishopric. Built in the 14th century, it presents a rare octagonal plan. Its tower-belfry dominates the Arize valley.
The medieval bridge spans the Arize River. Rebuilt several times over the centuries, it links the two banks of Rieux.

activities.text: The Volvestre is known for foie gras and ceramics. Pottery workshops continue an ancient tradition. You can visit some workshops by appointment.
The Arize River is renowned for trout fishing. The shaded banks offer lovely spots to fish or simply walk.

days_plan.itin_title: Haute-Garonne Road Trip: From Toulouse to the Pyrenees

visits.text: At the entrance to Riez, in a field on the left of the road, you'll come upon four monolithic columns in gray granite from Estérel, 5.90 meters high, topped with white marble Corinthian capitals. This is all that remains of a tetrastyle temple dedicated to Apollo, erected in the 1st century CE — classified on the first Historic Monuments list of 1840. Because Riez is no ordinary village: under the name Colonia Julia Augusta Reiorum Apollinaris, it was one of the 13 Latin law cities of Narbonensis. At its peak in the 2nd century, the town spread over 20 hectares, with two thermal baths, a theater, and several necropolises.

The early Christian baptistery (5th century) is one of the rare buildings of this type preserved in Provence. Behind its austere square facade hides an octagonal room with eight granite columns, Corinthian marble capitals, and an immersion basin — baptismal, used one night per year during the Easter ceremony. Comparable to the Fréjus baptistery, it has been classified as a Historic Monument since 1840.

In the old town, enter through the Saint-Sols gate to reach the Grande-Rue and its Renaissance facades. Look for the Hôtel de Mazan (1523): its staircase with four twisted columns is adorned with gypseries — these molded and sculpted plaster decorations that make Riez's specialty. There was no ashlar in the vicinity, so Riez residents covered everything with sculpted plaster, on facades, chimneys, ceilings, and staircases. Then climb to the Chapel of Saint-Maxime (17th century, on the hill) for a panoramic view over three valleys.

activities.text: The Wednesday and Saturday morning market is one of the largest in the region — held for centuries under plane trees on the central square. In winter (December-February), a truffle market takes place Wednesday morning. The transhumance festival (third or fourth Sunday in June) is a major event: sheep cross the village heading up to alpine pastures.

Riez sits at the heart of Verdon Regional Natural Park: Lake Sainte-Croix (2,200 hectares, the largest of the Verdon lakes) is 14 km away. If the weather is favorable, an afternoon on or beside the water is a fine option before heading to Moustiers the next day.

days_plan.itin_title: Alpes-De-Haute-Provence Circuit: Lavender and Perched Villages
Forcalquier & Verdon Gorges Road Trip
Alpine Hinterland and Verdon Gorges: From Thorenc to Riez

visits.text: The old town of Riom-ès-Montagnes spreads its historic houses around the parish church in the atmosphere of an authentic mountain town. The nerve centre of the Gentian country, this small town retains the character of Cantal villages where lava stone bears witness to the territory's volcanic origins.

The Maison de la Gentiane et des Plantes d'Auvergne reveals the secrets of this iconic plant with its yellow root that grows on high-altitude pastures. Interactive museography, a botanical garden and an educational distillery trace the history of the artisanal harvest and transformation into liqueur. The yellow gentian, hand-picked on the summer pastures, remains a powerful symbol of mountain Auvergne.

activities.text: A visit to a gentian distillery reveals the ancestral techniques for producing the famous liqueur. The roots macerate for several years before distillation to release their characteristic bitter aromas. A comparative tasting between different expressions (Avèze, Salers Gentiane) allows you to appreciate the nuances of this quintessentially Auvergnat spirit.

Hiking on the plateau offers beautiful escapes through summer pasture landscapes where Salers cattle graze. These vast green expanses dotted with abandoned burons tell the pastoral story of Cantal. Trails wind between heather moors and flowering meadows with views of the surrounding mountains.

days_plan.itin_title: Cantal Road Trip: Auvergne Volcanoes and Mountain Traditions

visits.text: Riquewihr had the fortune to pass through both world wars without a single bombing—its relative geographic isolation earned it this miraculous preservation. The result: 300 half-timbered houses from the 13th to 18th centuries, intact, in a village of 1,200 inhabitants surrounded by 368 hectares of vineyards. Listed among France's Most Beautiful Villages since 1982, it attracts over 1.5 million visitors per year. Arrive at opening time or late afternoon—during the day, the main street is packed.

The Dolder, a 25-meter bell tower erected in 1291 along with the first fortified enceinte, is Riquewihr's symbol. Its Alsatian name means 'the highest point.' On the exterior side, it's austere and defensive; on the town side, it presents a half-timbered façade. Climb to the museum at the top for views over the rooftops and vineyard—from up there you understand better why the two grand crus Schoenenbourg and Sporen surround the village.

The 13th-century Thieves' Tower—former prison of the Wurtemberg seigneury—preserves its medieval torture instruments and the interior of a 16th-century winemaker's house. The atmosphere is dark and authentic, very different from the picturesque main street. Not to be confused with the castle of the counts of Wurtemberg-Montbéliard, a 16th-century Renaissance building that today houses a museum of communication in Alsace.

activities.text: Push through the doorways into interior courtyards—behind the main street façades lie winemakers' cellars that welcome visitors for tastings. About fifteen family estates still produce the village's two grand crus: Schoenenbourg, renowned since the 16th century for its rieslings of remarkable minerality and longevity, and Sporen, more clay-and-marl, the preferred terrain of gewürztraminer.

The vineyard trail of the Grands Crus starts from rue de la Piscine and winds through the vines of Schoenenbourg and Sporen. In summer, winemakers lead guided tours followed by free tastings. Allow 90 minutes for the complete circuit. It's the best way to understand the soils and topography that make Riquewihr wines distinctive.

Make the 5 km detour to Ribeauvillé, at the foot of the Vosges. A marked 7 km circuit (2h30) connects its three ruined medieval castles perched on the ridges: Saint-Ulrich castle, Girsberg, and Haut-Ribeaupierre. The panorama from each embraces the Alsace plain and the vineyards below.

days_plan.itin_title: Grand Est Road Trip: Vineyards, Ramparts and Forests
The 15 Most Beautiful Villages of Alsace
Alsace Wine Route: Strasbourg to the Vosges
Alsace's Most Beautiful Christmas Markets

visits.text: Riverie is a small high-altitude village ringed with vestiges of medieval ramparts, with its cobbled lanes and keep. The village is little frequented, which is precisely its charm: you have the impression of discovering a place everyone has forgotten. The view from the viewpoint over the Rhône valley and the Pilat foothills is one of the finest in the Lyonnais Mountains sequence.

The viewpoint facing the Pilat and the Rhône valley deserves a long pause. On the horizon, on clear days, you can make out the reliefs of the Massif Central to the west and the Pilat to the south — a panorama that gives the full measure of the circuit you're making around Lyon.

activities.text: Rampart tour and climb to the village's high point (~1 km, 30 min). The walkway follows the vestiges of the medieval walls.
Ridge walk toward neighboring terraces with views over the Lyonnais valleys (2–3 km, ~1 hour). The landscape of meadows and bocage is typical of this part of the Lyonnais Mountains.

days_plan.itin_title: Loop Around Lyon: Villages and Golden Stones

visits.text: Joseph-Déchelette Museum. This art and archaeology museum occupies a beautiful 18th-century townhouse. Collections trace Roannais history from prehistory onward. Gallo-Roman ceramics, archaeological artifacts from the Joeuvres site, paintings, and sculptures fill the galleries. Joseph Déchelette, an eminent early 20th-century archaeologist, contributed enormously to local heritage knowledge. The museum notably preserves bronze statuettes from the 1st century BCE discovered at the Celtic oppidum of Joeuvres.

Marina and Roanne-Digoin Canal. The Loire riverbanks, redesigned in 2021, create a pleasant promenade just steps from downtown. Water features, slides, and sports fields enliven the embankments. The marina welcomes barges and river cruise boats. The Roanne-Digoin canal connects the Loire to the Saône—a route beloved by cyclists on the Véloire path. Contemporary architecture seamlessly integrated into the landscape completes this urban development.

Roanne Old Town. Shopping streets, Halles Diderot, and Place du Marché form the historic heart. The covered market hosts local producers, charcutiers, cheese-makers, and market gardeners. On Saturdays, the grand weekly market spills into adjacent streets. Historic quarters with bourgeois houses and restored façades testify to the past prosperity of this 35,000-inhabitant city positioned at the crossroads of Burgundy, Beaujolais, and Forez.

activities.text: Roanne Gastronomy. Roanne enjoys worldwide renown for Maison Troisgros, a three-Michelin-star restaurant since 1968, now located in Ouches at the Domaine du Bois sans Feuilles estate. César Troisgros perpetuates the family tradition. Le Central, the Troisgros family's downtown brasserie opposite the train station, serves inventive home-style cuisine in a convivial atmosphere. The Praluline—a pink praline brioche created in 1955 by Auguste Pralus—is still sold warm at Halles Diderot every morning. Numerous quality restaurants throughout downtown.

Roanne Market. On Saturdays, the market takes over downtown. Producers from the upper Beaujolais, Forez, and Côte Roannaise offer charcuterie, goat cheeses, organic vegetables, and AOC wines. A lively atmosphere where residents and visitors mingle. Halles Diderot operates every morning with permanent shops.

days_plan.itin_title: Loire Discovery: Wild Gorges and Industrial Heritage

visits.text: Rocamadour unfolds on three levels clinging to a cliff face: the commercial village below, the sanctuaries midway, the castle at the summit. France's second most-visited religious site draws over one million visitors annually. The Grand Staircase of 216 steps, which pilgrims climb on their knees, connects the main street to the chapels above.

The sanctuary comprises seven chapels gripping the rock wall. The Chapel of Notre-Dame houses the Black Virgin, a 12th-century walnut statue darkened by time and candle smoke. Above the entrance, the miraculous 8th-century bell reportedly rang to announce sailors' rescues at sea. Ex-voto model ships hang from the vault.

The Romanesque-Gothic Basilica of Saint-Sauveur preserves the relics of Saint Amadour in its crypt. Legend says his body was found intact beneath the threshold of the Virgin's chapel. In the rock beside Notre-Dame Chapel, a sword embedded in the cliff is said to be Durandal, the legendary weapon of Roland.

Take the Way of the Cross from the sanctuaries: fourteen stations climb through the trees to the castle ramparts. From the crenellations, your view plunges into the Alzou canyon and sweeps across the entire citadel below.

Descend through the medieval city. Rue de la Couronnerie, the sole commercial thoroughfare, passes beneath the fortified gateways of Figuier and Viguier. Honey-stone facades have sheltered shops and workshops since the Middle Ages.

The Gouffre de Padirac opens 103 meters deep into the limestone plateau. An elevator or staircase descends to the underground river. The visit alternates one kilometer walking through galleries and one kilometer by guided boat. The Grand Dome chamber soars 94 meters high; the Lake of Rain glitters beneath stalactites. Discovered in 1889, the site welcomes over 500,000 visitors yearly.

activities.text: At the Rocher des Aigles, over 400 birds representing 60 species live on the cliffs facing Rocamadour. Free-flight demonstrations launch eagles, vultures, condors, and falcons above the canyon, followed by macaws and cockatoos.

Walk the cliff-top promenade along the rock face. Several viewpoints offer successive perspectives of the citadel, valley, and plateaus. The most famous, at L'Hospitalet, frames the postcard view of Rocamadour.
Themed guided tours explore the citadel from different angles: general discovery, in pilgrims' footsteps, or tales of miracles. Town of Art and History guides lead these excursions.

days_plan.itin_title: Occitanie Mysteries Road Trip: Cathar Legends & Hidden Secrets of Southern France
Lot Itinerary: Cahors, Rocamadour & the Lot Valley
Midi-Pyrénées Circuit: Bastides, Cliffs and Abbeys

visits.text: Le Chalard is one of the most discreet and surprising villages in the Limousin. The settlement grew around an abbey founded in 1088 by Saint Geoffroy, and the Romanesque church built starting in 1096—consecrated in 1100—remains its heart. It houses the tomb of Saint Geoffroy, protected by a 15th-century screen, and a chapel adorned with frescoes. The English occupied the site from 1419 to 1421 during the Hundred Years' War and fortified its walls—the supports for the wall-walk are still visible. Then push open the small door at the church's apse: you'll discover the monks' cemetery, some forty tombs from the 12th to 15th centuries whose carved stones represent symbols of trades (weaver, blacksmith, cleric). It's one of the most intact medieval funerary ensembles in France.

Right next to the church, don't walk past the Maison de l'Or without entering. The Limousin is one of France's most gold-rich regions, and Le Chalard has preserved the most vibrant memory of it—gold mines were worked here from Gallic times onward, and the permanent exhibition traces this entire history with models, films, and artifacts. Budget 45 minutes. If you're traveling with children, the gold-panner workshop works brilliantly.

The Château de Châlus-Chabrol is an 11th-century fortress perched on a rocky spur above the town of Châlus. Its history hinges on a single date: on March 26, 1199, Richard the Lionheart, King of England and Duke of Aquitaine, inspected the defenses of the castle he was besieging when Pierre Basile struck him with a crossbow bolt fired from the keep. Gangrene did the rest: Richard died in this castle on April 6, 1199, at age 42, in the arms of his mother Eleanor of Aquitaine. Before dying, he requested that his entrails be deposited in the castle chapel. You can still see the chapel and the keep from which the fatal bolt was fired. Access is free year-round with explanatory panels. The view from the ramparts over the Limousin countryside is worth the trip.

activities.text: After Le Chalard, make a stop at the medieval bridge of La Tour, a few minutes below the village. This pointed-arch bridge with cutwaters marks the historic boundary between the Limousin and the Périgord. It's a good spot for a photo pause.

The route between Châlus and Rochechouart crosses through the heart of the Périgord-Limousin Regional Natural Park. Take the small roads—the D87 or secondary routes through Dournazac—rather than the main highway: granite hamlets, ponds, and chestnut forests roll past at a pace that makes you want to stop. These roads are lightly trafficked and pleasant to drive any season.

days_plan.itin_title: Haute-Vienne Circuit: Limoges, Porcelain & Historic Villages
Haute-Vienne Road Trip: Medieval Castles & the Périgord-Limousin

visits.text: Rochecolombe is one of Ardèche's most discreet villages, but its rocky peak and medieval lanes deserve the detour. The Château de Rochecolombe, mentioned as early as the 11th century, was long the residence of the lords of Vogüé before they moved to Vogüé in the 17th century. What remains of the castle is the Chapelle Saint-Barthélémy, several of whose architectural elements were transferred to Vogüé castle during reconstruction: the 16th-century bas-relief of the twelve Apostles, a monstrance and a 13th-century coat of arms. Climb to the ruins for views over the surrounding hills and valleys.

The lower village lanes cling to the limestone peak's flank: tightly packed medieval houses, remnants of the enclosure surfacing between buildings, small covered passages. Take time to wander between upper and lower village to read how the village developed on the rocky ledge over the centuries. Near the village, the hamlet of Sauveplantade shelters a small Romanesque church dating to the Early Middle Ages.

The Cascade de Rochecolombe, accessible via a short trail (20 min one-way), is particularly spectacular after Cévennes rains. The stream tumbles through a setting of cliffs and wild vegetation. In dry weather, the trickle is modest, but the setting remains pleasant for a cooling break.

activities.text: The short loop around the peak (2 to 3 km) starts from the parking lot and circles the castle rock with interpretive points about the site. Ideal in late afternoon when low light enhances the limestone. Figure 1h to 1h30 with stops.

From Rochecolombe, the small road circuit that reaches the viewpoint above the village lets you appreciate the site's layout from outside. You'll have an overview of the peak, village and surrounding wooded hills—useful perspective for understanding the defensive choice of this site.

days_plan.itin_title: The 18 Most Beautiful Villages of Ardèche

visits.text: The Royal Rope Factory of Rochefort, built between 1666 and 1669, measures 374 meters long – more than the height of the Eiffel Tower. This manufactory supplied ropes to the Royal Navy for two centuries. The International Sea Center presents the arsenal's history and seamanship techniques.

The Hermione, replica of the frigate that took La Fayette to America in 1780, was rebuilt over 17 years in the Louis XV dry dock. The project brought together traditional ship carpentry skills. Visit the ship with explanations about life for the 300-man crew.

The National Naval Museum occupies Hôtel de Cheusses, Rochefort's oldest civilian building. Ship models built at the arsenal, navigation instruments, figureheads. The arsenal produced 550 ships between 1666 and 1927, including France's first submarine (Le Plongeur, 1863).

activities.text: Accro-mâts, in the Louis XV dry dock, recreates the Hermione's masts. A treetop adventure course at 30 meters height to experience the sensations of 18th-century topmen. Spectacular final zip line.

Stroll through the Arsenal gardens: the Garden of Returns, the Navy Garden and the King's vegetable garden. Palm trees, Virginia tulip trees, begonias – plants brought back from maritime expeditions. Peace and shade facing the Charente.

The Rochefort transporter bridge (1900), the last of its kind in France, connected both banks of the Charente by a suspended gondola. Listed as a Historic Monument in 1976, restored between 2016 and 2020. Gondola crossings available.

days_plan.itin_title: Charente-Maritime Road Trip: La Rochelle, Île de Ré, Fort Boyard and Roman Saintonge

visits.text: Rochefort-en-Terre stands on a rocky spur overlooking the Gueuzon valley. Voted France's Favorite Village in 2016. Classified among The Most Beautiful Villages of France and designated a Small Town of Character.
The château was rebuilt in the early 20th century by American painter Alfred Klots. Built on the ruins of a 12th-century fortress with salvaged materials. Neo-Gothic style.

The château park offers panoramic views over the village rooftops. Gardens arranged on terraces. The Naïa Museum occupies one wing of the château and presents over 300 works of visionary and fantastic art.

Place du Puits is surrounded by overhanging houses from the 16th and 17th centuries. Restored half-timbered façades. Geraniums and ivy overflow from window boxes. Pedestrian historic center in summer.

The Church of Notre-Dame-de-la-Tronchaye blends Romanesque and Flamboyant Gothic styles. Former chapel enlarged in the 16th and 17th centuries for a college of canons. Façade and choir stalls are classified.
Cobbled lanes climb between flowering granite houses. Rue Saint-Michel and Place des Halles concentrate artisan shops and art galleries. Stylized wrought-iron signs.
Café Breton is a historic café housed in a medieval building. Authentic atmosphere.

activities.text: Since 1945, the flowering tradition has continued. Alfred Klots created the flowering window contest in 1911. Residents compete with creativity to adorn balconies and façades.
Artisan workshops line the lanes. Potters, embroiderers, glassmakers, and sculptors share their expertise. La Maison des Artisanes brings together local creators.
The Étang du Moulin Neuf lies a 15-minute walk away. Recreation area offering canoe-kayaking, paddleboarding, pedal boats, and a zip line in 37 wooded acres.
In December, the Christmas market transforms the village. Illuminations, decorated trees, and artisan stalls create a magical atmosphere.

days_plan.itin_title: Brittany Road Trip: Historic Villages, Sea and Heritage
Morbihan Road Trip: Gulf, Carnac & Breton Coast

places.name: Rocroi

visits.text: Fortress of Rocroi. Unique in France for its perfectly preserved star-shaped plan, this fortified town was created in 1555 by Henri II. Two concentric enclosures—the 16th-century pentagon and Vauban's decagon—encircle the town over nearly 4 km. Bastions, demi-lunes, and counterguards compose an exemplary defensive system of bastioned military architecture.

Museum of the Battle of Rocroi. In the former 17th-century guardhouse, relive the celebrated victory of young Duke d'Enghien over Spanish tercios on May 19, 1643. An impressive model recreates the battle with 2,000 lead soldiers, marking the end of Spanish invincibility. The relief map details the town's fortified architecture in the 18th century.

Place d'Armes. The nerve center of the town, this pentagonal square sees ten radio-concentric streets converging toward the ramparts. The central fountain and ancient well recall urban organization designed for military life. Gray stone facades shelter cafés and boutiques in a timeless atmosphere.

activities.text: Walk the ramparts. The complete 4 km circuit on the covered way reveals the ingenuity of Vauban's system. Cross bastions, powder magazines, and casemates while enjoying panoramic views of the Ardennes plateau. Explanatory panels detail the evolution of fortifications from the 16th to 19th centuries.

Panoramic observation. From the heights of the bastions, contemplate the Ardennes landscape stretching to the horizon. The marshes that once protected the town have given way to undulating bocage and forests. On clear days, the Belgian Ardennes massif appears on the northern horizon.

days_plan.itin_title: Ardennes Road Trip: Forests & Fortresses (9 Days)

map_keywords: Musée de la Bataille de Rocroi

visits.text: The Soulages Museum opened in 2014 with nearly 500 works by the Rodez-born painter. The architecture by Catalan firm RCR Arquitectes features Corten steel that develops a patina over time. The galleries alternate between varying heights and half-light to showcase Soulages' creative periods.

Notre-Dame Cathedral required 300 years of construction, from the 13th to the 16th century. Its bell tower reaches 87 metres and offers a 360° view after climbing 400 steps. The interior houses ancient and contemporary stained glass windows, a monumental organ, and richly carved choir stalls.

The Fenaille Museum holds the largest collection of statue-menhirs in Europe. These sculptures from the 3rd millennium BC remain enigmatic. The building occupies the Hôtel de Jouéry, saved from demolition in the early 20th century by Maurice Fenaille.

activities.text: Follow the historic centre trail available from the tourist office on Place de la Cité. The numbered route takes you past the Maison d'Armagnac, the bishop's palace, and Renaissance façades.

The market takes place on Wednesday and Saturday mornings. You'll find Aveyron specialities there: farçous, aligot, and Aubrac beef. The brasseries in the centre serve these dishes throughout the week.

days_plan.itin_title: Aveyron Road Trip: Causses, Tarn Gorges and Villages

visits.text: Rogliano gathers seven hamlets scattered across the mountains between 110 and 500 meters elevation. Bettolacce, Magna, Vignale, and Olivo reveal medieval chapels and square towers. The Church of San Michele in Bettolacce features a bell tower separated from the main building following the Pisan tradition.

Macinaggio centers around its marina, the largest in Cap Corse. This is where Pasquale Paoli landed in 1790 after 20 years in exile. The marina hosts shops and artisans in a peaceful atmosphere. Offshore, the Finocchiarola islands are classified as a nature reserve.

Barcaggio spreads its blonde sand beach at the tip of Cap Corse. The isolated hamlet faces the Giraglia islet crowned by its lighthouse. Corsican cows sometimes graze on the sand. The end-of-the-world atmosphere contrasts sharply with the bustle of the east coast.

activities.text: The customs officers' trail runs from Macinaggio toward Centuri for 35 kilometers of wild coastline. The first section to Tamarone beach crosses maquis and secret coves. Count on 3 hours to Barcaggio with spectacular views of the sea and islands.

The beaches of Macinaggio and Tamarone attract swimmers with their crystalline waters. Fine sand bordered by pines and eucalyptus. Popular snorkeling spot for observing fish and rocky seabeds in the marine reserve.

days_plan.itin_title: Upper Corsica Road Trip: Cap Corse and Balagne

visits.text: Discover this imposing collegiate church built from 837 onwards. Listed as a Historical Monument since 1840, it blends Romanesque and Gothic architecture. Admire the 14th-century murals in the choir and the embroidered tapestry of the Mystery of the Passion from the 16th century.

Immerse yourself in 4,000 years of footwear history in this former 17th-century convent. Romans was France's luxury shoe capital. The museum displays 16,000 pairs from around the world, including creations by Charles Jourdan and André Pérugia.

Wander the cobbled streets of the historic center, designated a City and Land of Art and History. Admire the 47-meter-high Jacquemart Tower built in 1370, and visit the Old Bridge spanning the Isère since the 11th century. The Sunday morning market was voted France's favorite market.

Explore this medieval village ranked among France's Most Beautiful Villages and voted the French People's Favorite Village 2025. Founded in 1070 around the abbey that became a major pilgrimage site. Navigate the maze of picturesque cobbled alleys and discover artisan workshops.

Visit this remarkable abbey church built between the 12th and 15th centuries, considered one of Dauphiné's most impressive Gothic achievements. Listed as a Historical Monument since 1840, it houses a magnificent sculpted portal and the main altar containing the reliquary of Saint Anthony of Egypt.

activities.text: Discover shoe manufacturing workshops still in operation. Observe every step of shoemaking. Workers share Romans' long tradition and the current revival of small local businesses.

Follow the playful trail of 12 giant shoes scattered throughout Romans' city center. These monumental sculptures pay tribute to the local shoe industry's history. The route covers approximately 3.4 kilometers and is accessible to all.
Housed in the former novitiate and Great Stables, the museum presents two permanent exhibitions on the abbey's medieval history and cloister gardens. Discover the abbey's medieval garden.

days_plan.itin_title: Provence Drôme Road Trip: Medieval Villages & Local Terroir

visits.text: Museum of Sologne in Romorantin-Lanthenay housed in the Chapter Mill beside the Sauldre. This ethnographic museum unveils the secrets of a fascinating territory through an immersive sensory journey. Collections trace Sologne's history, its natural environment of heaths and ponds, its pink-brick architecture, and ancestral customs. Old trades, hunting, and poaching are evoked through authentic objects and detailed reconstructions. Temporary exhibitions regularly enrich the visit. The connection to François I reveals Romorantin's prestigious past when it nearly became the kingdom's capital.

Renaissance heritage of Romorantin testifying to the era when François I envisioned establishing his capital here. The Saint-Pol hotel preserves a remarkable spiral staircase and Renaissance galleries. The chancellery presents a sculpted façade with medallions and friezes. Half-timbered houses line the old town's narrow streets. Canals and bridges spanning the Sauldre lend Venetian charm to the city. Saint-Étienne church dominates the town with its high tower. The remnants evoke Leonardo da Vinci's mad project to divert the Loire and create a grand navigable canal.

Matra Automobile Space in Romorantin celebrates the history of this innovative brand that marked French automotive racing history. Displayed models trace Matra's technical and stylistic evolution since the 1960s. Competition cars victorious at the 24 Hours of Le Mans stand alongside production models like the Bagheera and Murena. Prototypes reveal pioneering engineers' audacity. Period documents and photographs recount this French industrial adventure born in Sologne.

activities.text: Romorantin market on Friday mornings in Place de la Paix animating the entire town center. Sologne producers offer game, poultry, goat cheeses, and locally grown vegetables from family farms. Specialties like Easter pies and fruit tarts delight food lovers. Flowers and vegetable seedlings attract amateur gardeners. The market's convivial atmosphere enables exchanges with locals and discovery of regional products. Cafés open their terraces to welcome regulars for Sunday aperitifs.

Walk through the Sauldre valley winding through wild Sologne. Marked trails follow the river lined with alders and weeping willows. Shimmering ponds attract herons, egrets, and kingfishers in an aquatic ballet. Forests of maritime pines and oaks shelter roe deer and wild boar. Heath landscapes evoke Maurice Genevoix's descriptions in Raboliot. The preserved silence invites contemplation and observation of intact nature.

Discovering Sologne architecture characterized by red-brick houses and flat-tile roofs. Traditional farms present enclosed courtyards with dwellings, barns, and stables arranged around them. Bull's-eye dormers illuminate grain lofts. Monumental chimneys testify to heating's importance in this humid region. Villages preserve Romanesque churches, communal washhouses, and restored bread ovens. This preserved vernacular architecture constitutes authentic rural Sologne's visual identity.

days_plan.itin_title: Loir-et-Cher Itinerary: Royal Châteaux and the Loir Valley

visits.text: Round Hall.
Saint-Lazare Cathedral.

activities.text: Malsaucy Pond.
Saint-Laurent Island.

days_plan.itin_title: Saône Valley, France: Medieval Abbeys, Wine and Comtois Heritage

visits.text: The medieval castle of Roquebrune is one of the rare Carolingian castles still standing in France—its first foundations date back to the 10th century. Perched above the old village lanes, it belonged to the Grimaldis from 1350 to 1848 before Menton and Roquebrune voted for their annexation to France in the 1860 plebiscite. Climb to the esplanade for a panorama over Monaco and Cap Martin.

The Cap Moderne site (Villa E-1027 + Le Corbusier's Cabanon) is one of Europe's most remarkable modern architectural ensembles, inscribed on the UNESCO World Heritage list. Villa E-1027 was designed between 1926 and 1929 by Eileen Gray and Jean Badovici—its name is a code: E for Eileen, 10 for J in Jean (tenth letter), 2 for B, and 7 for G. Alongside, Le Corbusier built his 3.66-meter-square Cabanon in 1952, inhabited 3 to 4 weeks per year. He drowned there in 1965 in the nearby sea. Booking required, open April to November.

activities.text: Le Corbusier Trail (former customs officers' path): five kilometers between sea and cliffs at the foot of Cap Martin, lined with Mediterranean vegetation and a few wild pebble beaches like Plage du Buse. Jacques Brel loved to compose there in the early morning. Bring good shoes and water.

Swimming in the sheltered coves along the trail, especially at Plage du Buse: warm water, pebbles, underwater freshwater springs creating pockets of coolness—this was Coco Chanel's spot in the 1920s. Accessible only on foot.

days_plan.itin_title: The 15 Jewels of the French Riviera and Its Hinterland

visits.text: Start the day at Cordes-sur-Ciel, a bastide founded in 1222 by the Count of Toulouse. Perched on a limestone peak, it seems to float above the morning mist—hence its nickname "sur Ciel" (in the sky). Climb the cobbled lanes bordered by Gothic houses with sculpted facades (houses of the Grand Falconer, the Grand Huntsman). Artisan workshops (leather, ceramics, jewelry) bring life to the streets of this bastide ranked as a Grand Site of Occitanie.

In the afternoon, head to Roquefort-sur-Soulzon, in the heart of the Aveyron causses. Several aging cellars open their doors to reveal how Roquefort is made: Lacaune sheep's milk is aged in fleurines (natural cracks in the limestone rock of Combalou) at 11°C and 95% humidity for at least 3 months. Penicillium roqueforti, a natural cave fungus, gives the cheese its blue-green veins. Bring a sweater—it's cool year-round.

activities.text: End the day with a cheese tasting in the village. Beyond Roquefort, try Pérail (mild sheep's cheese) and Aveyron Tomme. Pair them with a Marcillac wine, a light and fruity red from the Aveyron hillsides.

days_plan.itin_title: Aveyron Road Trip: Causses, Tarn Gorges and Villages
Southeast France Road Trip: Perched Villages and Dramatic Gorges

visits.text: The Notre-Dame-de-Croas-Batz church (16th century) is one of Brittany's most richly decorated. Its Renaissance bell tower, with two superimposed galleries and lantern, bears ship sculptures - ex-votos from shipowners who financed construction.
Shipowners' and privateers' houses line the harbor and Rue Amiral Réveillère. Their sculpted granite facades display maritime details: anchors, rigging, ship prows. Some date from the 16th century.

The Exotic Garden, planted on a rock facing Île de Batz, houses 3,500 plant species from the southern hemisphere. The Gulf Stream warms the coast: cacti, palms, and proteas grow here outdoors, at London's latitude.

The Johnnies House traces the epic of Roscoff pink onion merchants who, from 1828, crossed the Channel by oar to sell their harvest in England. They traveled by cart, onion braids over their shoulders - the quintessential 'Frenchman' image.

The old port, protected by a long jetty, offers views of Île de Batz and the lighthouse. Sainte-Barbe chapel, from the 16th century, stands at the jetty's end - sailors came to pray before putting to sea.

activities.text: Cross to Île de Batz (15 min by boat). The island circuit on foot (10 km) passes the colonial garden, lighthouse (198 steps, 360° view), white sand beaches, and ruins of Romanesque Sainte-Anne chapel.
Follow Roscoff's coastal path eastward to Pointe de Bloscon. Rocks sculpted by erosion, multicolored seaweed, and low-tide pools form a natural aquarium.
Visit the seaweed discovery center. The Léon coast produces 70% of French edible seaweed. Cooking workshops allow you to taste them: seaweed tartare, kombu butter, sea lettuce salad.

days_plan.itin_title: Brittany Road Trip: Historic Villages, Sea and Heritage

visits.text: Saints-Pierre-et-Paul church (12th century) is one of the finest Romanesque edifices in Alsace. Its frieze of human and animal figures runs along the external cornice. Inside, sculpted capitals blend foliage, masks and fantastical motifs.
The Maison romane (circa 1152), on rue du Général-de-Gaulle, is considered one of the oldest stone civic constructions in Alsace. Its façade preserves Romanesque arcading and twin windows.
Four medieval gates survive in the ramparts: Porte du Lion, Porte de l'École, Porte Basse and Porte de la Vierge. Each retains sculpted door crowns, a unique vestige of Rosheim's medieval décor.
The six-bucket well on Place de la Mairie, and the 16th and 17th-century half-timbered houses complete a dense and homogeneous intra-muros heritage.
Follow the marked heritage route: panels explain the history of this former free imperial city, fortified as early as the 13th century.

activities.text: The Alsace Vineyard Green Way leaves from Rosheim on a disused railway line. By bike or on foot, it crosses vineyards and orchards to Saint-Nabor.
Drive up to Mont Sainte-Odile (13 km): the Pagan Wall, a 10-km megalithic enclosure, rings the summit. The monastery offers a panorama over the Alsace plain.
Rosheim winegrowers cultivate mainly Riesling and Pinot Gris. Cellars open along the main street offer tastings.

days_plan.itin_title: Grand Est Road Trip: Vineyards, Ramparts and Forests

visits.text: La Piscine in Roubaix is a former 1932 Art Deco Olympic pool transformed into a museum of art and industry. A sheet of water remains from the pool, reflecting a colored glass ceiling depicting the sun. The old shower cabins now house a vast ceramics collection, and rooms display paintings, sculptures and textiles.

In Croix, Villa Cavrois is a manifesto of modernist architecture. Built between 1929 and 1932 for a textile industrialist, its 60-meter yellow brick façade overlooks a reflecting pool. The interior, entirely designed by the architect, features central heating, telephones in every room, and indirect lighting—visionary luxuries for the era.

Cross through Parc Barbieux, a 34-hectare landscaped garden designed in the 19th century. Its paths wind between rolling lawns, flower beds and century-old trees. A canal runs through the entire park.
The Condition Publique, a former 19th-century warehouse where wool was weighed and processed, has become a cultural and arts venue. Its vast red brick halls host exhibitions and performances.

activities.text: Follow the blue line painted on the ground from Roubaix station: it leads straight to La Piscine, crossing downtown and its converted industrial façades.
Roubaix was nicknamed the "city of a thousand chimneys" for its textile mills. Walk among the old red brick factories, some converted into lofts or cultural spaces.
The gardens of Villa Cavrois, a few hundred meters from the villa, extend the walk through a geometric landscape dotted with contemporary structures.

days_plan.itin_title: Northern France Circuit: Lille, Belfries and Flemish Heritage

visits.text: Gros-Horloge — This 14th-century Gothic belfry houses one of Europe's oldest astronomical clocks, with a mechanism dating from 1389. The Renaissance arch built between 1527 and 1529 spans the pedestrian street with its 2.50-meter golden dial depicting a 24-ray sun on a star-studded blue background. A single hand points to the hour while a globe indicates the phases of the moon.

Place du Vieux-Marché — It was in this square that Joan of Arc was burned on May 30, 1431. The Sainte-Jeanne-d'Arc church, inaugurated in 1979, features bold architecture shaped like an inverted flame and houses the Renaissance stained glass windows from the former Saint-Vincent church destroyed during the war.

Saint-Ouen Abbey Church — A masterpiece of Rayonnant Gothic built between the 14th and 16th centuries, this former Benedictine abbey impresses with its 137 meters length and its 14th-century stained glass windows among the most complete in France. Its grand Cavaillé-Coll organ from 1890 has 64 stops.

Musée Le Secq des Tournelles — Housed in the former 15th-century Saint-Laurent church, this museum unique in the world presents 12,000 pieces of ornamental ironwork from the 3rd to 19th century: signs, locks, grilles, everyday objects testifying to the craftsmanship of master blacksmiths.

Panorama Côte Sainte-Catherine — From this 150-meter hill overlooking the left bank, embrace the entire view of Rouen: the cathedral painted by Monet, the spires of Gothic churches, the meanders of the Seine. The viewpoint immortalized by the Impressionists in the 19th century.

Rouen Cathedral — Monet painted this Gothic masterpiece more than 30 times to capture its changing light. Built between the 12th and 16th centuries, its 151-meter cast-iron spire makes it France's tallest. The Lady Chapel and the tombs of Norman dukes bear witness to eight centuries of history.

Palais de Justice de Rouen — This flamboyant Gothic building from the late 15th century was the seat of the Exchequer of Normandy. Its western facade, ornate with sculpted gables and pinnacles, survived the 1944 bombings. The Salle des Pas Perdus reveals medieval vaults.

Musée des Beaux-Arts de Rouen — This museum founded in 1801 houses a major collection of paintings from the 15th to 21st century. Works by Caravaggio, Velázquez, Monet, Sisley and a room dedicated to Géricault born in Rouen illustrate five centuries of European art.

activities.text: Historial Jeanne d'Arc — Housed in the archbishop's palace where Joan was condemned then rehabilitated, this immersive scenographic journey retraces the destiny of the Maid through testimonies, projections and reconstructions of the 1431 trial.

Rue Eau-de-Robec — This picturesque cobbled street runs along a canal where dyers once washed their fabrics. Its half-timbered houses from the 15th and 16th centuries, restored after the 1944 bombings, plunge the stroller into medieval Rouen.

Jardin des Plantes de Rouen — This 8.5-hectare botanical garden created in 1840 houses more than 5,600 plant species, tropical greenhouses and a rose garden. A green lung in the heart of the city, conducive to strolling among botanical collections and century-old trees.

Aître Saint-Maclou — This unique ossuary from the 16th century is a rare testimony to the plague epidemics. Its half-timbered galleries surrounding a courtyard display macabre sculptures: skulls, bones, gravedigger's tools carved into the beams.

Panorama XXL — This 32-meter-high rotunda hosts giant 360° panoramas. Past exhibitions have transported visitors to ancient Rome, Monet's Rouen or the Amazon. A unique immersive artistic experience in France.

Seine Riverbanks — The redeveloped left bank offers 3 kilometers of walks from the Pierre-Corneille bridge to the Île Lacroix. Guinguettes, floating gardens and barges transformed into cultural venues bring life to the quays.

days_plan.itin_title: Grand Tour of Normandy: From D-Day Landing Beaches to Étretat's White Cliffs
Seine-Maritime Road Trip: Cliffs of Étretat, Rouen and Jumièges Abbey
Seine-Maritime Road Trip: Cliffs of Étretat, Rouen and Jumièges Abbey
Complete Normandy Road Trip: Cliffs, D-Day Beaches and Mont-Saint-Michel

visits.text: The Church of Notre-Dame de l'Assomption is a singular edifice: its construction began in the 11th century in Romanesque style, continued in the 13th and 14th centuries in Gothic, and never really finished — the south tower remained incomplete until 1875 when it was raised in neo-Gothic style. This mixture produces a strange and fascinating result. On your way out, look for vestiges of an old practice: in the chevet, deep notches in the stone — winegrowers once sharpened their pruning knives on the church stone, which was supposed to ensure good harvests. And don't miss the 'Smile of Rouffach': two delicate carved faces — a young woman and a young man — flanking the door of the old sacristy.

The Witches' Tower, last tower surviving from Rouffach's medieval fortifications, is attached to the old town hall. Its round base dates from the 13th century, its square upper part from the 14th and 15th centuries. It served as a prison for centuries, notably for women accused of witchcraft during the Inquisition era — hence its name. A stork's nest tops it today, giving it a more peaceful air.

The Récollets Church, built between 1280 and 1300 by the Franciscans, is one of the very rare mendicant order churches preserved in Alsace with its exterior pulpit. These outdoor pulpits allowed popular preachers to address the crowd directly on the square. Inside, the bareness required by the Franciscan order contrasts with the richness of the neighboring Notre-Dame church.

On place de la République, take time to observe the monumental ensemble: the old 16th-century Halle aux Blés with its characteristic gables, the old town hall with double Renaissance pediment, the houses with stepped gables testifying to the town's golden age under the prince-bishops of Strasbourg. Rouffach was long the capital of the Haut-Mundat, their possession in Upper Alsace.

activities.text: The Vorbourg Grand Cru vineyard trail winds across the limestone slopes above Rouffach, facing south. As you climb, views progressively open over Rouffach, its medieval monuments, the Alsace plain, and on clear days the Black Forest. A marked 4-kilometer trail loops through rows of Gewurztraminer and Riesling.

Climb to the Notre-Dame de Schauenberg sanctuary via the forest path from Pfaffenheim. This pilgrimage site perched among beech trees dominates the valley and vineyards. The path starts in the vineyard, crosses meadows, then enters the woods — the round trip takes about 2h30.

days_plan.itin_title: Upper Rhine Road Trip: Colmar and the Southern Wine Route

visits.text: The ruins of Château de Rougemont crown the Montagne des Boules at 736 meters altitude. Built in the late 12th century by Count Frédéric II of Ferrette, it guarded the southern gateway to Alsace and the Roman road from Langres to Basel. Besieged in 1375 by Enguerrand de Coucy's mercenaries, it was abandoned and later demolished on Louis XIV's orders in 1673. Restored between 1977 and 1980, the ruins have been a classified historic monument since 1996.

The Chapelle Sainte-Catherine, rebuilt in 1876, stands beside a spring once believed to cure eye ailments. In the 18th and 19th centuries, pilgrimages here were so popular that offerings collected were 75 times greater than those at the parish church.
From the summit, the panorama stretches from the Vosges to the Jura with views of the Swiss Alps on clear days. Rougemont nestles against the mountain at an average elevation of 450 meters.

activities.text: Climb to the castle through forest trails from the Plainot picnic area. This 3-hour circuit traverses 1,249 hectares of communal forest integrated into the Natura 2000 site and the Ballons des Vosges Regional Natural Park.
Follow the botanical trail at Mont Marty. Discover Vosges flora with valley views. This 247-hectare Natura 2000 site preserves forest habitats.

days_plan.itin_title: Territoire de Belfort Road Trip: The Citadel and the Lion

visits.text: Roussillon ranks among France's Most Beautiful Villages. Its ochre-rendered facades display every shade from pale yellow to blood red, drawn from pigments extracted in nearby quarries.

The Sentier des Ocres, nicknamed the "Giants' Causeway," winds between ochre cliffs sculpted by erosion and former mining in red, orange, and yellow. Two marked routes of 30 and 50 minutes cross this mineral landscape shaded by pines.
The Conservatoire des Ocres et Pigments Appliqués, housed in the old Mathieu factory, traces the history of Provence's ochre industry. Demonstrations of pigment production and hands-on workshops.
The Église Saint-Michel dominates Place de la Mairie with its square bell tower. From the nearby orientation table, the gaze sweeps across the Vaucluse plateau, Luberon mountains, and Ventoux.

Six miles away, the Colorado Provençal of Rustrel unfolds 75 acres of wilder ochre formations. Fairy chimneys, the Cirque du Sahara, and the White Desert compose a landscape worthy of the American West.

activities.text: Follow the Belvédère circuit at Rustrel's Colorado Provençal. This two-hour trail climbs through forest to a panorama overlooking orange dunes and fairy chimneys.
Stroll through art galleries and painters' studios in the village. Ochre has inspired artists for centuries: natural pigments, watercolors, and colored earth appear in canvases.
Late in the day, head to the Castrum overlook to watch the setting sun ignite the ochre cliffs. The colors shift in intensity minute by minute.

days_plan.itin_title: Vaucluse: Avignon, Luberon Villages & Ventoux Routes
The 15 Most Beautiful Villages of the Luberon
France's Most Beautiful Villages: Alps to Luberon in 7 Days

visits.text: Royan, a seaside resort rebuilt after the 1945 bombings. The 1950s architecture, called 'Les Cinquante Glorieuses', now holds the Ville d'Art et d'Histoire label. Modernist villas, curved seafronts, luminous concrete.
