Notre-Dame Church of Royan (1958), a brutalist concrete masterpiece by Guillaume Gillet. A 45-meter elliptical nave, inverted V-shaped vault, abstract stained glass. Controversial at its construction, it is now a listed Historic Monument.

Royan central market, a concrete and glass structure by Louis Simon and André Morisseau (1956). Undulating dome 52 meters in diameter, spectacular natural light. Seafood, orchard fruits, Charentes specialties. One of the finest covered markets in France.

Royan's seafront stretches across 5 beaches: Grande Conche, Foncillon, Chay, Pigeonnier, Pontaillac. Promenade along modernist villas, casino, congress center. Elegant seaside atmosphere facing the Gironde estuary.

activities.text: Guided tour 'Royan 1950s'. The tourist office offers architectural tours with commentary: the Parc district villas, congress center, Protestant temple, bus station. A fresh look at 20th-century heritage.

Grande Conche beach, 2 km of fine sand in an arc. Lifeguarded in summer, surf and sailing schools, terraces facing the ocean. The Cordouan lighthouse, 'Versailles of the sea', stands on the estuary horizon.
Royan-Le Verdon ferry. The 20-minute Gironde estuary crossing offers a unique view of both the Charente and Médoc coasts. Option to continue to Cordouan lighthouse at low tide (guided excursion).

days_plan.itin_title: Charente-Maritime Road Trip: La Rochelle, Île de Ré, Fort Boyard and Roman Saintonge

visits.text: Chapel of the Holy Spirit (treasure).
Belfry.
Old town.

activities.text: Bay of Somme.
Marquenterre Nature Reserve.

days_plan.itin_title: A Road Trip Through the Somme Valley: Cathedrals, History & River Landscapes

visits.text: Joséphine de Beauharnais purchased Château de Malmaison in 1799. Between 1800 and 1802, alongside the Tuileries, it became the seat of French government where Bonaparte received his ministers.
Architects Percier and Fontaine redecorated the château in antique style. The Council Room shaped like a military tent, the library, and Joséphine's oval bedroom exemplify the Consulate style.
The second floor presents objects from Napoleon's exile in Saint Helena: the campaign bed on which his body was displayed, his furniture, and death mask.

activities.text: Joséphine designed the park in English landscape style with help from Scottish gardeners. She cultivated every rose variety known at the time. The restored rose garden now features 150 antique varieties.
Parc des Impressionnistes evokes Giverny with its water garden. Monet, Renoir, and Sisley painted along Rueil's riverbanks.

days_plan.itin_title: Hauts-de-Seine Road Trip: Royal Heritage & Gardens

visits.text: Ruoms conceals behind its lively shopping street one of Ardèche's best-preserved medieval defense systems. The old village was founded in the 10th century around a Benedictine priory of Cluny Abbey—only the Chapelle Notre-Dame-des-Pommiers survives, which you'll find in the historic center. The rectangular ramparts flanked by seven round towers were built in the 14th century during the Hundred Years' War, in local limestone and Ardèche river pebbles. Look for the Maison des Gardes, whose facade preserves a Renaissance fresco depicting Saint Christopher carrying the Christ Child, and the Maison de Justice with its lintel naively carved with a cross, scales and key. The self-guided circuit takes about an hour.

From the eastern exit of the village, the scenic Route des Défilés de Ruoms offers a striking geological spectacle: carved into rock in the 19th century (completed 1866), it plunges through a limestone corridor via a succession of tunnels and vaults, then follows a deep canyon formed by the Ardèche and Ligne rivers. Drive the route but stop at the viewpoints—the exposed rock strata are worth the pause.

Leaving the village northward, don't miss the Renaissance Château de Chaussy: visible from the road, it makes a lovely composition with its corner turrets and mullioned facade. It's not open to the public, but its silhouette amid the greenery deserves a photo. Ruoms is also known for hosting Ardèche's largest breweries, which closed in 1967—wine has since largely replaced them, and the department's largest wine cooperative is located in the commune.

activities.text: The signposted pedestrian loop inside the walls lasts about an hour and is entirely free to access. Panels along the route explain the defensive elements. If you want a guided commentary tour, ask at the tourist office—guided visits are organized in season by reservation.

Local operators offer canoe or kayak descents on the Ardèche from Ruoms. The pebble beaches beside the river, accessible from the municipal campground, are also ideal for a peaceful swim after visiting the village. The river is supervised July-August at this location.

days_plan.itin_title: The 18 Most Beautiful Villages of Ardèche

visits.text: The Château de Sablé-sur-Sarthe, rebuilt in the 18th century, dominates the confluence of the Sarthe and Vègre from its promontory. It replaced the old medieval fortress of the lords of Sablé—including Geoffroy the Old, who founded in 1010 the priory that would become Solesmes Abbey. From the terrace, the view over the two rivers and city rooftops is particularly beautiful in late afternoon. Black marble extracted from Sablé quarries was used for certain exterior facings of the Palace of Versailles.

The Marble Ecomuseum traces the marble industry that made Sablé and its region wealthy from the 17th to 20th centuries, employing at its peak over 2,000 workers. Discover quarrymen's tools, extraction and polishing techniques, and some remarkable pieces from royal commissions. It's a fascinating and little-known local economic history.

The Saint-Nicolas suburb preserves some half-timbered medieval houses adorned with ironwork and ridge finials. Their winding lanes open onto the Sarthe pleasure port—the busiest in the West—where boaters travel up and down the river between May and October.

activities.text: Operators offer river cruises from Sablé: the navigable Sarthe reveals its wooded meanders and tuffeau villages from the water. Allow two hours for a round trip to Solesmes—the view of the abbey from the river is one of the circuit's great moments.

The weekly market of Sablé-sur-Sarthe is one of the department's liveliest. Taste Sarthe rillettes—slowly cooked for hours in their own fat, they have a melting texture very different from industrial rillettes—accompanied by goat cheeses from local producers and shortbread cookies made according to the original recipe of the eponymous village.

days_plan.itin_title: Sarthe Road Trip: Le Mans and the Loir Valley

visits.text: Écomusée de Marquèze: Exceptional immersion in an authentic 19th-century Landes hamlet, accessible only by historic train from 1903-1910 (Monument Historique carriages) from Sabres. After a 10min journey through the forest, discover the 25-hectare airial where 30 to 40 people once lived. Half-timbered manor houses and Landes farmhouses dating from the 18th and 19th centuries, sheepfolds, water mill on stilts relocated from Geloux, bread ovens under centuries-old oaks.

Traditional craft demonstrations: Costumed interpreters explain pine resin tapping, spinning, weaving, bread making, pastoral life with stilt shepherds. Traditional Gascon games (palet, rampeau, nine-pins). The ecomuseum testifies to the evolution of the Landes landscape, from wet moorland to industrial forest imposed by Napoleon III in 1857. Tasting of pastis landais baked in wood-fired oven.

Le Pavillon: Contemporary building presenting permanent exhibition on the history of Landes de Gascogne and annual temporary exhibition. Modern architecture contrasting with the airial's traditional buildings. Interactive and wheelchair-accessible museum route. Video projections and sound testimonies complete the visit.

activities.text: Historic train journey: Boarding at Sabres station in 1903-1910 carriages classified as Historic Monuments. 10min forest crossing on the former Sabres-Labouheyre-Mimizan line. Rear platform recommended on return for panoramic view. Regular departures throughout the day. Single ticket includes train and airial access.

Participatory workshops: Introduction to Landes stilts, Gascon games, straw weaving, artisan soap making according to schedule. Activities for all ages. Activity booklets for children transforming the visit into a treasure hunt. Theatrical scenes on certain days with costumed actors. Digital tour with tablets available at reception.

Full day at the ecomuseum: On-site dining with Landes specialties. Shaded picnic area. Craft product shop (pastis landais, soaps, honey). Allow 4 to 6h to fully enjoy the site. Themed guided tours on certain days. Hours 10am-7pm in high season (April 1 - September 28). Booking advised for groups.

days_plan.itin_title: Landes Road Trip: Atlantic Coast, Pine Forests & Gascon Heritage

visits.text: Saignon magnificently concludes this tour of the Luberon. This eagle's nest above Apt takes its name from the Latin "signum"—the rock dominating the village served as a light signal for Gallic populations before the Romans arrived. From the Place de la Fontaine, with its centuries-old hackberry tree and cool fountains, wander through narrow, steep lanes where every doorway deserves a glance. The village counted up to forty different lords in the 14th century—which is to say, a tumultuous history.

The Church of Notre-Dame-de-Pitié dates back to the 11th and 12th centuries. Classified as a Historic Monument, this Romanesque church houses Carolingian baptismal fonts, red marble columns, and a 16th-century reliquary. In the Middle Ages, it was a stop on the Via Domitia for pilgrims en route to Santiago de Compostela. The portal with three doors and its large Gothic arch is particularly beautiful.

The Rocher de Bellevue is the village's highest point—about thirty meters above the houses. It served as the base for three medieval fortress-châteaux whose ramparts and sentry paths are still visible. An orientation table at the summit allows you to identify Mont Ventoux, the Montagne de Lure, and the Alps.

activities.text: Climb to the Rocher de Bellevue via the fitted-out stairs (10 min from the village). 360° view over the Apt valley, the Luberon, Ventoux, and on clear days the Alps. The most beautiful panoramic ending imaginable for an itinerary.

Circuit of the two wash-houses fed by the Valsorgues springs, and of the bories and clapas that mark the paths around the village. The Claparèdes Plateau trail connects Saignon to Sivergues through a landscape of garrigue and dry stone.

days_plan.itin_title: The 15 Most Beautiful Villages of the Luberon

visits.text: Saint-Affrique stretches along the banks of the Sorgues in the heart of the Grands Causses Regional Natural Park. The Pont-Vieux, listed as a Historic Monument, connects the two banks. The centre retains stone houses, small squares, and vaulted passageways.

The Rocher de Caylus offers a panoramic view over the town and surrounding hills. A cross tops this landmark site. The Notre-Dame de la Miséricorde Church features a tall Gothic spire visible from afar.

The Maison de la Mémoire du Pays Saint-Affricain displays objects, stories, and photographs of daily life in times past. The Tribunal des Arts occupies the former courthouse and exhibits works by local artists.

activities.text: The Saturday morning market brings the town centre to life. Producers sell local cheeses (Pérail, Roquefort, Recuite), charcuterie, and regional specialities. The atmosphere is friendly and Aveyron delicacies are plentiful.

The landscapes around Saint-Affrique range from blazing red rougiers to wild causses and green valleys. The Rougier de Camarès to the south offers spectacular red-earth terrain. Hiking, mountain biking, and rock climbing are all available in the area.

days_plan.itin_title: Aveyron Road Trip: Causses, Tarn Gorges and Villages

visits.text: Saint-Amand-les-Eaux: baroque abbey tower & thermal park.

activities.text: State forest walk + thermal relaxation.

days_plan.itin_title: Hauts-de-France: Opal Coast Cliffs, Medieval History & Great War Sites

visits.text: The Château de Montrond dominates the Cher from its rocky spur. Built in the 11th century, rebuilt in the 15th, it belonged to the Princes of Condé. The keep, curtain walls and towers offer a good overview of military fortifications from the Middle Ages to the Renaissance. The interior houses the Musée Saint-Vic (archaeology, regional history). Guided tours in summer.

The fortress witnessed major moments in French history. In 1569, during the Wars of Religion, it held against Protestant troops. In 1651, the Great Condé took refuge there after the Fronde. In 1652, the young Louis XIV came to besiege it. The curtain wall still bears traces of cannonballs.

Saint-Amand-Montrond calls itself the "City of Gold." The town has 300 goldsmiths and jewelers, a quarter of French national production. The craft has been present since the 19th century; today it mixes tradition and contemporary creation. Several workshops welcome visitors by appointment. The Cité de l'Or, opened in 2019, explains working techniques, history and showcases creations.

activities.text: The Cher and Berry Canal crosses Saint-Amand. Towpath, locks, canal bridges: a pleasant 5 km walk follows the waterway. You can spot herons, grebes, sometimes a kingfisher. The canal, dug between 1830 and 1840, connected the Loire to the Allier; barges transported coal, wood, wine.

Saint-Amand's Saturday morning market is one of Berry's liveliest. Local producers, cheeses, charcuterie, vegetables, artisan breads. In season, asparagus, strawberries, melons from nearby farms. Also clothing, household goods. Covered market + outdoor stands.

days_plan.itin_title: Cher Circuit: Romanesque Berry and Jacques-Cœur Route

visits.text: Saint-Andiol was founded in the 10th century by monks from Saint-Victor Abbey in Marseille, who drained the marshes enabling agricultural development of the plain. The Saint-Vincent parish church is a fortress in itself: built in Romanesque style in the 11th-12th centuries, fortified in the 14th with machicolations and crenellations, its massive bell tower and thick walls give it the appearance of a keep. Sundials are visible on the south-facing wall. Free admission.

The Château de Saint-Andiol belonged successively to the d'Albe, Varadier, and d'Estourmel families — the latter had it elevated to a marquisate in 1656. Its beautiful double-revolution staircase from the 16th-17th century, Renaissance style, merits a visit to the 5-hectare wooded park planted with century-old plane trees and chestnuts. The château now houses a community socio-cultural center.

Saint-Andiol has a direct link to Resistance history: Jean Moulin, future founder of the National Resistance Council, grew up here at 35 Route Nationale. On January 2, 1942, after being parachuted near Fontvieille, he walked to the Saint-Andiol family home to coordinate the REX mission. The Jean Moulin Museum, inaugurated in 2018, traces his life from Béziers to the Panthéon, where his father Antonin was a schoolteacher.

activities.text: "In the footsteps of Antonin Moulin" trail: a marked route with explanatory panels starts from the Jean Moulin museum to tour the key sites in the village and Moulin family history. Allow 1h30 to complete the communal heritage tour.

Rural route through orchard-olive country: the commune dedicates 66% of its territory to permanent crops (orchards, olive groves). Agricultural paths cross cherry orchards, apricot trees, and olive groves along the banks of the Durance and irrigation canal. The Sainte-Croix chapel (before the year 1000) on the road to Mollégès is one of the oldest monuments of pure Romanesque style in the region — listed as a historic monument.

days_plan.itin_title: Alpilles, Aix-en-Provence & Camargue Circuit

visits.text: Saint-Antoine-l'Abbaye was voted France's Favorite Village in July 2025, and arriving from the plain makes it clear why: the Gothic abbey church rises at the end of an avenue of centuries-old linden trees like a vessel of stone. Its construction spanned from 1130 to the late 15th century—nearly four centuries of building. The structure measures 32 meters wide by 22 meters high, an unusually wider-than-tall format. Inside, don't miss the walnut choir stalls, Aubusson tapestries, and the 17th-18th century organ. Classified as a Historic Monument by Prosper Mérimée as early as 1840.

The village's history began in 1070 when Geilin, a local lord, brought back the relics of Saint Anthony the Great from Constantinople—believed to cure "St. Anthony's fire," ergot poisoning that caused gangrene and hallucinations. The Antonine Order, these monk-physicians, eventually established a network of 300 commanderies across Europe. You'll find their treasury in the church sacristies: liturgical vestments, medieval surgical instruments, and an ivory Christ. Plan for a guided tour to access these pieces—schedules vary by season.

The museum housed in the former novitiate offers two permanent exhibitions—"Chronicles of a Medieval Abbey" and "Healing Soul and Body"—and provides access to the medieval garden in the former stables. Medicinal plants, herbs, and fruit trees are cultivated according to Antonine pharmacopoeia. The garden is free; allow an hour for the complete visit.

Wander through the goulets—narrow cobblestone alleys—between half-timbered houses and facades adorned with mullioned windows from the 14th and 15th centuries. The village is also a stop on the Way of St. James. Climb to the viewpoint above the abbey courtyard to take in the entire site with the Chambaran hills as backdrop.

activities.text: The 2-3 km heritage loop within the village walls connects the abbey church, medieval alleys, market hall, and rampart remnants. Interpretive panels mark the route. Start from the abbey courtyard square in late afternoon when the light gilds the molasse facades—the local golden, slightly rosy stone.

The trail to Saint-Jean-le-Fromental chapel follows the old Antonine pilgrim paths for about 7 km (2h45 round trip). Along the way, you'll pass walnut groves overlooked by the Vercors rim on the horizon. A map is available at the tourist office.

days_plan.itin_title: Royans-Vercors Road Trip: Hilltop Villages and Gorges

visits.text: The Romanesque House, built around 1150, is France's oldest surviving civilian building. Restored by Viollet-le-Duc in the 19th century, it houses a museum dedicated to prehistory and folk traditions.
Explore medieval lanes where half-timbered houses and carved facades follow one another. The Maison de l'Amour displays two lovers carved in stone since the 15th century.
Descend to the Bessarel quarter where old tanneries survive. Canals once channeled water for treating hides that made the town's fortune.
The 19th-century market hall rises at the square's center. The Sunday market has attracted pilgrims and residents for centuries.
Reach the belvedere at the foot of Roc d'Anglars. This 90-meter limestone cliff dominates the confluence of the Aveyron and Bonnette rivers.

activities.text: Paddle the Aveyron Gorges by canoe. Sheer cliffs, ancient bridges, and natural beaches line the emerald river.
Climb routes on the limestone cliffs. Roc d'Anglars and Pech Dax offer climbs for all levels.
Browse the Sunday morning market renowned for local products. Goat cheese, honey, and charcuterie spread beneath the hall and across the square.

days_plan.itin_title: Tarn-et-Garonne Road Trip: Medieval Bastides and Orchard Country

visits.text: The Abbey Church of Saint-Nabor in Saint-Avold is one of the most important religious monuments in Moselle. The abbey was founded in 722 by Sigisbaud, Bishop of Metz, and in 765, Bishop Chrodegang deposited the relics of Saint Nabor here, a Roman soldier martyred for his faith—making the site a regional pilgrimage center. The current building was reconstructed from 1754 to 1769 according to plans by Dom Léopold Durand, monk-architect, in multicolored sandstone from Saint-Avold quarries. Its bell tower rises to 45 meters. Listed as a Historical Monument since 1930, it houses an Entombment of Burgundian craftsmanship dating from 1510, a statue of Saint Nicholas carved in oak in 1773, and Baroque woodwork executed between 1755 and 1762. The traditional Nativity scene is visible from December 4 through the end of January, daily from 8 AM to 4 PM.

Make the detour to Boulay-Moselle, 30 km to the north. This former medieval fortified town erected as a County in 1615 has preserved the two "Dada" towers, the last remnants of its fortified enclosure destroyed in 1635. Saint-Étienne Church from 1782, topped with a bulbous bell tower from 1863, was formerly renowned for its monumental organs—Boulay housed France's largest organ factory, founded in 1863 by Dalstein-Haerpfer. The nativity occupies the altar of Mary and depicts Bethlehem and Jerusalem with plaster figures from the mid-20th century.

In Longeville-lès-Saint-Avold, don't miss Saint-Magne Church. It dates from 1740 and was transformed and enlarged in 1889 in neo-Gothic style. The organ, built in 1905-1906 by the Lorraine Factory of Great Organs, was completely restored in 2008 by the Bernard Aubertin Organ Factory. Its stained glass windows illustrate evangelical subjects and parables. The carved Stations of the Cross from 1896 is one of the finest in the region.

In Saint-Avold, the Church of Sainte-Jeanne-d'Arc houses a nativity 4 meters by 2 meters, representing the Nativity with landscapes and pastoral scene—one of the most generous in detail of the entire circuit. Visible from mid-December to mid-January during services, every other Sunday.

In Folschviller, the Résidence Sainte-Barbe installs each year a traditional nativity created by volunteers, visible from December 15 to January 14 from 2 PM to 5 PM. The one in Altviller is also worth the detour: santons dressed by hand by the village patchwork club compose an artisanal village nativity.

activities.text: A few minutes from the abbey church stands the American military cemetery of Saint-Avold, the largest in Europe—it holds the graves of American soldiers who fell during the battles of 1944-1945. A sober and striking visit that places Lorraine Christmas in its turbulent history. Free admission.

Saint-Avold organizes each year an extensive Christmas festivities program from the market square, with Santa's village and musical entertainment. Saint Nicholas's arrival by carriage with the Municipal Harmony and a torchlight retreat animate the town in early December.

days_plan.itin_title: Moselle Circuit 10 Days: Metz, Bitche Citadel and Chagall Stained Glass
Christmas Nativity Trail through Moselle

visits.text: Saint-Béat is the marble village. Quarried since Roman times, this white marble was used for numerous monuments including the Paris Opera Garnier. The village church is entirely in marble. The old quarries on the mountainside can be visited.
The 11th-12th century château dominates the town. Its ruins testify to Saint-Béat's strategic importance controlling the passage to Spain.

activities.text: The Spanish border is very close. The valley narrows between mountains. The landscapes become truly mountainous with peaks exceeding 2,000 meters.
Hikes depart from Saint-Béat toward surrounding peaks. The trails often follow ancient Roman roads. The Garonne here is just a mountain torrent.

days_plan.itin_title: Haute-Garonne Road Trip: From Toulouse to the Pyrenees

visits.text: Saint-Jacques Basilica (unique Romanesque rotunda in France).
George Sand and Vallée Noire Museum.
White Tower.
Saint-Roch Hospice Museum.

activities.text: Painter artists.
Creuse Gorges.
George Sand's House in Nohant (nearby).

days_plan.itin_title: Indre, France: Secret Berry Region and Vallée Noire Rural Villages

visits.text: Saint-Bertrand-de-Comminges, labeled "Most Beautiful Villages of France," "Great Occitanie Site," and "Remarkable Heritage Site," occupies a promontory at 515 meters altitude overlooking the Comminges plain. The medieval city unfolds in winding, steep streets around the cathedral, with a fortified enclosure called "scepte" that gathered the episcopal palace, canonical houses, cemetery, and cloister. The city walls incorporate numerous stones and sculptures reused from ancient Lugdunum Convenarum.

The Cathedral of Sainte-Marie, classified as a Historic Monument in 1840 and inscribed on the UNESCO World Heritage List in 1998 as part of the Ways of Saint James, is a Romano-Gothic edifice whose construction dates to around 1100. Bishop Bertrand de l'Isle (1083-1123), canonized in 1218, had the Romanesque cathedral and first cloister built. In 1307, Bertrand de Got, who became Pope Clement V, launched reconstruction of the Gothic apse and choir, and had Saint-Bertrand's relics transported in 1309.

The cathedral interior holds exceptional treasures. The carved Renaissance choir stalls, commissioned by Bishop Jean de Mauléon (1523-1551), are among France's finest. The organ, built in the 16th century at an angle on five Corinthian oak columns, rises to sixteen meters high and has three keyboards, a thirty-note pedal board, forty stops, and 2,621 pipes - it is considered one of Europe's most beautiful classical organs. The treasury preserves embroidered copes offered by Clement V, including the Cope of the Passion depicting scenes from Christ's passion, and Saint-Bertrand's pastoral staff, carved from a narwhal tusk.

The Romano-Gothic cloister from the 12th to 14th centuries, adjoining the cathedral, consists of four galleries from distinct periods: the western Romanesque gallery is the oldest, the southern and eastern galleries date from the first half of the 13th century, and the northern gallery, the only one vaulted, dates from the 14th century. Its openings onto nature, pierced in the early 20th century, offer an unexpected panorama over the Pyrenees. A stuffed crocodile, an ex-voto brought from the Holy Land in the 14th century, hangs in one of the galleries.

The remains of Lugdunum Convenarum, a Gallo-Roman city among the most important in Roman Aquitaine, extend at the hill's foot. Founded in the Augustan era, the city had up to ten thousand inhabitants at its peak before being destroyed in 585 during Frankish succession wars. Excavations, conducted from 1913 to 1970 then resumed from 1985 to 2006, have uncovered a theater of five thousand spectators from the 1st century, a temple of imperial cult in Pyrenean marble, a forum, baths, and a Christian basilica. The Augustan trophy, in white Saint-Béat marble, is one of the most complete found in France. The upper town ramparts, from the 5th century, are listed as a Historic Monument.

The Basilica of Saint-Just de Valcabrère, classified as a Historic Monument and inscribed on the UNESCO World Heritage List like the cathedral, is an isolated Romanesque edifice in the middle of fields, a few hundred meters from Saint-Bertrand. Built on the site of a Roman necropolis whose stones it reuses, it is mentioned as early as Bishop Bertrand's time (1083-1123). Remains of a cloister and annex buildings testify to a former regular canonical house.

activities.text: Devote the morning to the cathedral, taking time to admire the carved choir stalls, climb to observe the monumental organ, and tour the cloister to the openings onto the Pyrenees - morning light is particularly beautiful there.

Descend to the ancient site of Lugdunum Convenarum to walk among the remains of the theater, forum, and baths. A single ticket sold at the Musée Les Olivétains gives access to the cathedral, basilica, and archaeological site.
Walk to the Basilica of Saint-Just de Valcabrère via the path through fields - the gradual approach to this solitary Romanesque edifice in its green setting is one of the visit's highlights.
Visit the Musée Les Olivétains, installed in a former convent, which offers a common ticket office, bookstore, exhibitions, and local products. Guided tours can be reserved on site.
If the schedule permits, attend a Festival du Comminges concert in the cathedral: the acoustic qualities of the organ and nave give works performed here exceptional resonance.

days_plan.itin_title: Haute-Garonne Road Trip: From Toulouse to the Pyrenees
Hautes-Pyrénées Circuit: From Tarbes to Cirques and Valleys
Occitanie's 13 Most Beautiful Villages

visits.text: Notre-Dame Collegiate Church. For over 600 years, this Gothic masterpiece has crowned Saint-Bonnet-le-Château from its hilltop perch. Built between the early 15th and 16th centuries, it impresses through austere grandeur and sheer scale. From the parvis, sweeping views embrace the Forez plain, the Massif Central, the Pilat mountains, and on clear days, the Alps themselves. The lower chapel preserves early 15th-century murals among the finest in France. The crypt holds mysteriously preserved natural mummies discovered in a 17th-century vault—bodies that refuse to decay.

Medieval Fortified Town. 14th-century ramparts still encircle portions of the medieval core. Cobbled lanes lined with 15th- and 16th-century burgher houses testify to past prosperity. The Porte Louis Mandrin and Porte de la Châtelaine mark the old fortified gateways. The village retains its medieval character through aristocratic residences, vaulted passages, and hidden squares. The ensemble speaks of tanners, weavers, armorers, and locksmiths who once made this town thrive.

International Pétanque and Boules Museum. Set in the town center, this museum celebrates Saint-Bonnet as the birthplace of OBUT boules, created in 1955. That year, locksmith Frédéric Bayet partnered with mechanic Antoine Dupuy to create the marque de la 'boule à jouer'—the playing ball that would conquer the world. Exhibitions trace manufacturing techniques, different types of boules, and the game's history. Saint-Bonnet has mastered metalworking for centuries; OBUT simply channeled that craft into spheres.

activities.text: Rail Adventure Greenway. A family-friendly voie verte follows a former railway line between Estivareilles and Saint-Marcellin-en-Forez. This multi-kilometer path winds through the Forez countryside. A cheerful railway worker mascot accompanies visitors along the trail. A vintage 1950s tourist train also runs for families—children especially love it.

Medieval and Renaissance Heritage Walk. Self-guided walk with map available at the Tourist Office. Questionnaire-game for children. Ramparts promenade offering surrounding views. Weekly Friday market where local producers sell regional specialties.

days_plan.itin_title: Loire Discovery: Wild Gorges and Industrial Heritage

visits.text: Saint-Brieuc's Saint-Étienne Cathedral, built from the 13th to 15th centuries, is one of the rare fortified churches in Brittany. Its two massive towers, pierced with arrow slits, resemble a fortress entrance more than a religious edifice—it served as a citadel during several 14th-century sieges. Enter when the sun reaches its zenith: the south transept's rose window projects brilliant colors. A baroque altarpiece from 1745 and a 1540 organ case rank among the interior treasures.

Saint-Brieuc's old town centers on the cathedral. Pedestrian streets, half-timbered houses from the 15th to 17th centuries, and granite mansions tell the story of an episcopal city. The Hôtel des Ducs de Bretagne (1572), with carved lion heads and Renaissance floral motifs, welcomed Mary Stuart in 1548.

The Saint-Brieuc fountain, below the cathedral, marks the site of the monastery founded in 580 by Welsh monk Brieuc. This is where everything began: an oratory near a spring, a land grant from Breton chief Riwal, and a monastery that gave birth to the city.

The Cesson Tower, on a rocky spur overlooking the bay, once guarded the Gouët estuary entrance. This strategic site has been occupied since Viking and Roman times. The ruins offer an unobstructed panorama over the entire Saint-Brieuc bay.

activities.text: Guided tour of the historic center. Between sacred monuments and half-timbered houses, decipher carved facades, shop signs, and ornaments that showcase Breton craftsmanship from the Middle Ages to the Renaissance.

Walk in the Saint-Brieuc Bay nature reserve, Brittany's largest. At low tide, mudflats attract thousands of migratory birds. Binoculars recommended for observing brent geese, sandpipers, and oystercatchers.
Saint-Brieuc market, Wednesday and Saturday mornings around the historic metal halls classified as a Historic Monument. Fish from Légué, local cider, galettes, and kouign-amann.

days_plan.itin_title: Côtes-d'Armor Road Trip: Dinan, Pink Granite Coast, Bréhat Island & Guerlédan Lake

visits.text: Saint-Céneri-le-Gérei has been classified as one of France's Most Beautiful Villages since 1983—and seeing the ensemble from the stone bridge spanning the Sarthe, you immediately understand why. The 11th-century Romanesque church perched on its granite spur reflects in the river's peaceful water. Arrive via the RD56 to enjoy this emblematic viewpoint before entering the village. Inside the church, frescoes from the 12th and 14th centuries were covered with whitewash in 1650 then rediscovered in the late 19th century: a Virgin extending her blue mantle above a crowd, a Christ in Majesty, and the hermit Saint Céneri himself. Also look for the bee's nest carved in stone—present according to tradition since the 9th century, they would be guardians of the holy place since they attacked soldiers who threw themselves into the Sarthe to escape them.

The Chapel of Petit Saint-Céneri, dating from the 15th century, stands in a vast meadow on the other bank of the Sarthe—facing the village. Its chestnut framework forms an inverted boat hull. This is where, according to legend, founder Céneri's hermitage was located, an Italian monk who came to evangelize the Alpes Mancelles in the 7th century. Tradition holds that young women wishing to marry within the year come to plant a needle in the saint's statue's feet.

The Auberge des Sœurs Moisy, transformed into a tourist information office from April to September, was until 1908 the haunt of Impressionist and Naturalist painters who came for inspiration in the Alpes Mancelles. Corot, Courbet, Harpignies, Boudin, Mary Renard all stayed here. Go up to the first floor to see the Decapitated Room: by candlelight, visiting painters amused themselves by tracing on the whitewashed walls, in charcoal, the shadow puppet profile of their guests—sixty decapitated silhouettes still watching you today.

activities.text: Walk the Saint-Céneri on the motif pedestrian trail, set up by the Normandie-Maine Regional Natural Park. This route leads to places appreciated by 19th-century painters for the particular quality of light on the valley—panels indicate the exact position from which such and such a painting was made. A playful booklet is available at the tourist office.

Hikes in the Alpes Mancelles from Saint-Céneri offer landscapes of cliffs, rocks, and wild river that surprise in this corner of France. The Haut-Fourché and Toyères cliff sectors offer marked circuits of 2 to 4 hours in the Regional Natural Park. Local operators also offer Sarthe descents by canoe from the village.

days_plan.itin_title: Normandy's Classic Coast: Étretat Cliffs and Bocage Countryside Villages
Sarthe Road Trip: Le Mans and the Loir Valley

visits.text: Saint-Cirq-Lapopie clings to a limestone cliff 100 meters above the Lot River. France's Favorite Village, it boasts thirteen listed historical monuments and fewer than 200 residents. Houses from the 12th to 15th centuries, with brown-tile roofs, cascade toward the river below.

Climb to the Rocher de Lapopie, the village's highest point. Ruins of a 10th–12th-century fortress crown the summit. The 360° panorama embraces the Lot Valley, limestone cliffs, and the village rooftops spread beneath your feet.

The 12th-century fortified church dominates the village from its rocky promontory. Its bell tower doubled as a defensive structure during troubled medieval times. Inside, simple furnishings and understated decor invite quiet contemplation.

Descend Rue de la Couronnerie, the main medieval thoroughfare lined with artisan workshops and stone-arched houses. Place du Sombral, the village heart, offers a shady rest stop. At the far end, the Porte de la Pélissaria opens onto the path leading down to the Lot.

The former Mariniers Inn, perched on the cliff edge, once enchanted a Surrealist poet who settled here and famously declared he never wanted to be anywhere else. His house-studio, now an artists' residency, is recognizable by its facade hanging over the void.
Pass through the Porte de Rocamadour, a fortified gateway in the medieval ramparts. Remnants of the defensive wall are still visible along the path that circles the upper village.

activities.text: Walk the towpath between Bouziès and Saint-Cirq-Lapopie, carved into the cliff face 80 meters above the Lot between 1843 and 1847 for horses pulling barges. Along the way, a 30-meter bas-relief sculpted into the rock depicts the valley's fauna, flora, and fossils. The route passes through the manual lock at Ganil.

Board a traditional flat-bottomed gabare from Bouziès. These historic boats navigate the Lot, passing through locks with commentary on river navigation history and views of the cliffs and perched village.
Rent a canoe, kayak, or stand-up paddleboard at the river's edge below the village. Outfitters offer routes of varying lengths, with shuttle service or loop options.

days_plan.itin_title: Occitanie's 13 Most Beautiful Villages
Lot Itinerary: Cahors, Rocamadour & the Lot Valley
Southeast France Road Trip: Perched Villages and Dramatic Gorges
Midi-Pyrénées Circuit: Bastides, Cliffs and Abbeys

visits.text: Saint-Clar, a bastide founded in 1274 through a paréage between the King of England and the Bishop of Lectoure, possesses a rarity: two arcaded squares. On Place de la Garlande, the hall classified as a Historic Monument raises its 20 weathered wooden pillars supporting a 13th-century timber frame. The rear, rebuilt in stone in the 18th century, is topped with a neoclassical bell tower.

The second arcaded square, the Plaçote (Place de la République), preserves its "cornières"—covered galleries beneath golden stone houses. The old-fashioned storefronts hint at interior courtyards and a prosperous commercial past. Remarkably rare, these two squares lie just dozens of yards apart.

Head up to the Château-Vieux, Saint-Clar's original core. The old 12th-century Romanesque church, with its handsome bell tower, overlooks half-timbered houses packed into medieval lanes. The Garden of Four Seasons, created on the site of collapsed houses, pays tribute to Gascon poet Dastros (1594-1648).

Saint-Clar is the capital of Lomagne white garlic, cultivated here since the 13th century and protected by a PGI since 2009. The House of Garlic offers visits to understand the cultivation, drying and uses of this Gascon specialty. In summer, garlic braids adorn the market stalls.

activities.text: Saint-Clar market on Thursday mornings. Under the wooden-pillared hall, tradition dates back to the Middle Ages. White garlic, fattened poultry, seasonal fruits and vegetables—a concentrate of Lomagne terroir. In summer, the garlic market runs from July through September.

Guided tour of Saint-Clar bastide. The tourist office offers commentary-led circuits to understand the singular architecture of this two-square bastide. Medieval lanes, ramparts, wash houses and Gascon pigeon houses complete the picture.

Hike the hillsides around Saint-Clar. Marked trails offer views of the Arrats valley and Lomagne countryside—fields of sunflowers, garlic, wheat and vines compose a patchwork of colors depending on the season.

visits.text: Catherine de' Medici acquired the estate in 1577. Louis XIV gifted it to his brother Philippe d'Orléans in 1658. The château, set ablaze in 1870 during the Franco-Prussian War, was razed in 1891.
The Grande Cascade, a 17th-century hydraulic masterpiece, spans 92 meters. Jets, bubbling fountains, and basins compose a water spectacle. The Grand Jet basin shoots water over 40 meters high.
Pavillon de Breteuil houses the International Bureau of Weights and Measures. Two exemplars of the meter and kilogram standards are preserved here.

activities.text: The 460-hectare national park unfolds with formal French gardens designed by Le Nôtre, groves, and terraced allées. The summit at 161 meters offers sweeping views over Paris.
The Rock en Seine festival takes over the estate each summer.

days_plan.itin_title: Hauts-de-Seine Road Trip: Royal Heritage & Gardens

visits.text: The marina features 1,885 berths, Europe's third-largest port by capacity. Developed since 1967 with three protected basins, modern harbor master's office, and services for boaters.
The Saint-Cyprien Collections display 19th- and 20th-century paintings. Eugène Boudin, Barbizon School, Utrillo, Marquet, Modigliani, and François Desnoyer enrich the galleries.
The Botanical Garden spans five hectares in the Villa des Capellans park. Over 800 plant species, bamboo grove, antique roses, and contemporary sculptures.
The Saint-Cyprien Resort Golf offers an 18-hole and a 9-hole course. Links-style atmosphere with views of the Pyrenees and Mediterranean.

activities.text: Practice sailing or motorboating. The Yacht Club offers lessons and boat rentals for cruising along the coast.
Enjoy the water sports center on the golf lake. Water skiing, wakeboarding, towable tubes, paddleboarding, and windsurfing available.
Relax on the long fine sandy beach. Six kilometers of Blue Flag coastline with beach clubs and entertainment.
Discover the port fountain that cleans the waters. Built in 1991, it draws seawater and injects it into the harbor to eliminate odors.

days_plan.itin_title: Pyrénées-Orientales Road Trip: French Catalonia and the Vermilion Coast

visits.text: Ternand, a Small Town of Character, nestles its old village on a rocky spur partly composed of white marble, overlooking the Azergues valley. Former summer residence of Lyon's archbishops, the village preserves a rampart walk along the old fortifications with views over vineyards and the Beaujolais mountains.

The Church of the Nativity of Saint John the Baptist, Romanesque in style, shelters beneath its choir an 11th-century crypt decorated with Carolingian frescoes—rare and precious, they likely date to the 8th century. Access to the crypt is restricted (heritage days or by request), but reproductions are visible.

The ruins of the 12th-century castle, destroyed in 1562 by Huguenot troops under Baron des Adrets, are laid out as a public garden. The keep's remains and lanes with golden stone houses, mullioned windows, and medieval vaults compose a village frozen in time.

Mont Thou (2,005 feet), the accessible high point of the Monts d'Or, offers exceptional panoramas over greater Lyon, the Saône plain, Beaujolais, and the Alpine chain—from Mont Blanc to Vercors. The orientation table at the summit helps identify peaks on clear days.

The Mont Cindre Hermitage (1,542 feet) directly overlooks Lyon. Its esplanade is a natural belvedere over the city and its two rivers. The Monts d'Or, a small limestone massif at the metropolis's gates, shelters golden stone quarries and cabornes—dry-stone huts built by winegrowers.

activities.text: Morning walk through old Ternand. The rampart walk, medieval lanes, and views over the Azergues valley can be explored in a good hour. Sample gamay with a village winemaker before hitting the road.

Afternoon hike from Mont Thou to Mont Cindre. The 6-mile loop connects both summits through forests, meadows, and heathland. Wild orchids in spring, dry-stone cabornes, and the Birds of Prey trail marked with panels about local species.

Mont d'Or villages—Saint-Cyr, Saint-Romain, Curis, Poleymieux—preserve surprising rural charm at the metropolis's doorstep. Golden stone houses, washhouses, and fountains compose a preserved Lyon countryside landscape.

days_plan.itin_title: Rhône Road Trip: Lyon, Golden Stone Villages of Beaujolais & Condrieu Vineyards

places.name: Saint Denis

visits.text: The first masterpiece of Gothic architecture, the cathedral basilica houses over 70 sculpted recumbent effigies from the 12th to 16th centuries. Forty-two kings and thirty-two queens rest beneath its vaults, from Dagobert to Louis XVI.

Descend into the crypt to discover the burial site of Saint Denis, martyred around 250 AD. Eight effigies commissioned by Saint Louis stand alongside the monumental tomb of François I and Catherine de Medici.

The Paul Éluard Museum of Art and History occupies a former Carmelite monastery founded in 1625. Its collections trace the history of the 1871 Paris Commune, the Hôtel-Dieu apothecary, and the life of the surrealist poet.
Step into the Fabrique de la Ville, an educational center dedicated to archaeology and excavations that reveal two thousand years of Saint-Denis history.
The Saint-Denis market brings together nearly 220 permanent merchants. Stalls overflow with spices, fabrics, and products from around the world.

activities.text: Follow Saint-Denis's historic trail: 20 markers chart the route between the Stade de France and the basilica, tracing 2,000 years of history along the canal.
The Street Art Avenue runs along the Saint-Denis canal for 5 km between the Stade de France and Parc de la Villette. Some forty monumental murals bring color to the banks.
Stroll through Parc de la Légion d'Honneur in the heart of downtown. In spring, the Tulip Festival transforms the lawns into a botanical rainbow.

days_plan.itin_title: Seine-Saint-Denis Circuit: Royal Basilica and Urban Culture

map_keywords: Musée d'art et d'histoire Paul Eluard Saint-Denis
Parc de la Légion d'Honneur Saint-Denis

visits.text: The cathedral complex at Saint-Dié is going to throw you off balance — in a good way. Three pink sandstone buildings connected together: the cathedral, the small church of Notre-Dame de Galilée, and their Gothic cloister. The cathedral dates back to the 7th century, founded by Saint Déodat, an Irish monk who gave his name to the town. Its current walls date from the 12th–13th centuries, but the 18th-century baroque facade (signed by Giovanni Betto) clashes with everything else. The surprising detail: the cathedral was dynamited by the Germans in November 1944, then rebuilt identically with new stained glass windows designed by Jean Bazaine. If you look carefully in the cloister, you'll find the gargoyle nicknamed the Iroquoise — because of its feathers, not because of any link to America. It had been torn off by the 2003 earthquake; it returned to its place in 2017.

Don't walk past the cloister without entering — it's one of the largest in eastern France, rebuilt from 1444 thanks to indulgences granted by Pope Eugene IV (Charles VII and Duke René I had put their weight behind it). The galleries blend flamboyant Gothic and early Renaissance accents. On the cloister floor, look for the stylized map of the New World embedded in the pavement: this is where, in 1507, a small printing press published the first map to print the name America.

The Pierre-Noël Museum is right next to the cathedral and worth 45 minutes: it preserves the documents and works of the master glaziers who worked on the reconstruction of the building after 1944. A good complement to understanding what you just saw in the cathedral.

activities.text: At the end of the day, climb up to the Roche Saint-Martin via the trail at the edge of town. Allow 1 hour round trip. The plunging view over the Meurthe valley and the pink sandstone spires of the cathedral is well worth the effort, especially in late afternoon when the light warms the stone.

days_plan.itin_title: Vosges Abbey Trail: Monastic Heritage Unveiled

places.name: Saint Dizier

visits.text: Lac du Der, Europe's largest artificial lake, spans 4,800 hectares. Created between 1964 and 1974, it regulates Marne River floods upstream from Paris.
The Village-Musée du Der chronicles the story of three villages submerged beneath the lake. The half-timbered church from Nuisement-aux-Bois was dismantled and reassembled piece by piece.
Half-timbered churches dot the region. Built from wood and cob, these 15th-, 16th-, and 17th-century structures—like the one in Châtillon-sur-Broué—represent unique architectural heritage.
Saint-Dizier's château, now the sub-prefecture, dates from the 13th century. Three towers connected by curtain walls survive from the medieval fortifications.
Notre-Dame church preserves an Entombment sculpture. 16th-century half-timbered houses surround it.
The Petit Paris façade at 478 avenue de la République displays Art Brut style. Created with pottery shards and seashells, it exemplifies 20th-century folk art.

activities.text: Observe common cranes from October through March. Between 200,000 and 350,000 birds migrate through annually, with 20,000 to 30,000 wintering on the lake—one of Europe's great wildlife spectacles.
Cycle the 38-kilometer lake circuit. Bike paths and greenways traverse the Champagne bocage and follow the feeder canal from Saint-Dizier.
Relax on one of six sandy beaches. Sailing, windsurfing, and waterskiing complement swimming and sunbathing.
Follow the Route des Églises à Pans de Bois. Eleven timber-framed churches comprise this architectural heritage unique in France.

days_plan.itin_title: Haute-Marne Discovery: Langres & Historic Heritage

map_keywords: Église à Pans de Bois Nuisement-aux-Bois

visits.text: The medieval village of Saint-Émilion has been a UNESCO World Heritage site since 1999. Perched on a rocky promontory, it preserves cobbled streets, ramparts, and honey-colored stone houses. The village welcomes over one million visitors annually. Take time to wander the lanes discovering artisan shops, wine cellars, and ancient buildings.

Saint-Émilion's Monolithic Church is Europe's largest underground church, carved from limestone in the 11th-12th centuries. The Gothic bell tower, standing 68 meters high, can be climbed for panoramic views of the village and vineyards (118 steps). The church interior can only be visited on guided tours organized by the tourist office. The dimensions are impressive: 38 meters long, 20 meters wide, 11 meters high.

The King's Tower, a 13th-century Romanesque keep, is the only remaining vestige of Saint-Émilion's château. Standing 32 meters tall, it's open for self-guided visits. The 118 steps lead to the top where you'll discover 360° views over the village, tiled roofs, surrounding vineyards, and the Dordogne Valley. It's Saint-Émilion's best viewpoint.

The Cordeliers Cloister is a 14th-century former Franciscan convent transformed into a sparkling wine cellar. The underground galleries, carved from limestone, now house Crémant production. The visit combines historical heritage with discovery of traditional sparkling winemaking methods. Tasting included in the visit. The cloister garden is highly photogenic.

Saint-Émilion's catacombs form a network of underground galleries developed during the Romanesque period. These former limestone quarries served as burial sites and ossuaries. The guided tour reveals the fascinating history of these mysterious places. The atmosphere is special with constant coolness (14°C) and plays of light and shadow.

activities.text: Visit and tasting at a Saint-Émilion wine château. The appellation counts 82 classified grands crus in a small area, creating an exceptional concentration. Family estates and grand domains alike offer guided tours of cellars and vineyards. Tastings help you understand the appellation's typical blends (predominantly Merlot, with Cabernet Franc and Cabernet Sauvignon). Reservations essential.

Saint-Émilion Wine Route circuit. Several marked routes cross the vineyard between prestigious châteaux and family properties. The UNESCO-listed vineyard landscape remains intact. You can follow the circuit by car, bicycle (rentals available), or tourist train. Views over the hillsides are magnificent. Plan stops for tastings at châteaux that interest you.

Tasting Saint-Émilion macarons, a local specialty since the 17th century. These small almond cakes, different from Parisian macarons, have a soft texture and pronounced almond flavor. Several village pastry shops offer them. It's a pleasant sweet break after visiting the village. They keep well and make good souvenirs to bring home.

Cycling through Saint-Émilion's vineyards. The Roger Lapébie bike path, built on a former railway line, connects Sauveterre-de-Guyenne to Bordeaux passing through the vineyard. The route is flat and easy, perfect for family outings. Bike rentals available in Saint-Émilion or neighboring villages. Allow 2-3 hours for a leisurely loop with photo stops.

days_plan.itin_title: Gironde Road Trip: Bordeaux, Vineyards & Arcachon Bay
Nouvelle-Aquitaine Loop: Medieval Villages, Vineyards & Dordogne Valley

visits.text: Puits Couriot Mining Museum. This remarkable museum occupies an authentic mining site that operated until 1973. Deep underground, reconstructed galleries bring the miners' world to life through machinery sounds and atmospheric lighting. The iconic 'hanging room,' where workers suspended their gear between shifts, captures the daily rhythm of a workforce that once numbered 1,500 at this single pit. Through artifacts, photographs, and firsthand accounts, the exhibitions chronicle Saint-Étienne's coal mining saga—a legacy that shaped the soul of this industrial city.

Museum of Art and Industry. Three thematic galleries showcase the region's industrial genius. The cycle exhibition traces bicycle evolution from primitive wooden draisines to cutting-edge composite racing machines—remarkably, France's first bicycle was built right here in 1886. The arms collection presents centuries of weaponry from the royal manufacture, displaying swords, rifles, and muskets as both historical artifacts and works of art. The textile section celebrates Saint-Étienne's renown for ribbon-making and passementerie, crafts that once employed thousands.

Museum of Modern and Contemporary Art. France's second-largest contemporary art collection lives here, with nearly 20,000 works spanning movements from 1960 onward. Geometric abstraction, Nouveaux Réalistes, Pop Art, and Art Brut dialogue across Didier Guichard's striking black ceramic-tiled building. Rotating exhibitions draw over 60,000 visitors annually to this beacon of international contemporary art in the heart of industrial France.

Saint-Étienne Historic Center. Place Marengo forms the beating heart of urban life, its Belle Époque bandstand and tree-lined esplanade hosting markets and gatherings. Along Rue des Martyrs de Vingré, 18th-century townhouses preserve their exposed timber frames and carved stone facades. The Grand'Église, the city's sole Gothic survivor built in 15th-century Forez sandstone, shelters a Renaissance Entombment sculpture and a soaring flamboyant Gothic choir—a spiritual anchor in this city of industry.

activities.text: Cité du Design. Born from the spectacular conversion of the old arms factory, the Cité du Design hosts the International Design Biennial every two years. Permanent and rotating exhibitions explore how design intersects with innovation and manufacturing. The site embodies Saint-Étienne's remarkable industrial transformation—now recognized as a UNESCO Creative City of Design, proof that old factories can become crucibles of creativity.

Renovated District Walk. The Saint-Charles quarter brilliantly reimagines former industrial wastelands as vibrant urban spaces. Throughout the city, playful 'test bench' installations—sculptural benches designed by contemporary artists—invite unexpected pauses. Around Châteaucreux station, modern architecture and redesigned public squares create a vision of post-industrial urban renewal done right.

days_plan.itin_title: Loire Discovery: Wild Gorges and Industrial Heritage

visits.text: Saint-Étienne-du-Grès is a peaceful gateway to the Alpilles between the Crau and the massif's piedmont. Its old center, spared by tourist flows, preserves side streets and small squares where little has changed since the 1970s. A few minutes north of the village, the Romanesque chapel of Saint-Gabriel (11th-12th centuries, classified historic monument) stands alone amid olive trees. Its sculpted facade represents the Annunciation — one of the finest examples of primitive Romanesque sculpture in the region.

Domaine Dalmeran, at the village's north exit, is one of the last large olive and wine estates in this part of the Alpilles, certified PDO Les Baux-de-Provence. Providers offer visits to vineyards and olive groves, plus tastings. The "Les Baux-de-Provence" designation covers only 8 communes — including Saint-Étienne-du-Grès — and produces 85% organic, an exceptional proportion.

activities.text: Agricultural paths toward the Alpilles foothills: from the village, agricultural tracks gradually climb toward the first limestone ridges. Views open toward the Crau plain south and the Alpilles garrigue north. Allow 2 hours for an 8 km loop.

Heritage trail (wash houses, oratories, fountains) through Saint-Étienne center: an hour of quiet walking in the old town reveals a dozen small dry-stone structures — the communal wash house fed by a spring capture, roadside oratories with their flower-filled niches, and some sculpted 17th-century doorways.

days_plan.itin_title: Alpilles, Aix-en-Provence & Camargue Circuit

visits.text: Château de Saint-Fargeau, a 'giant of pink brick,' unfolds its five corner towers crowned with bell turrets around a pentagonal courtyard. Its origins date to 980, when the Bishop of Auxerre built a fortified hunting lodge here.

Tour the furnished apartments of La Grande Mademoiselle, cousin of Louis XIV, exiled here in 1652. The wooden library, trophy room, and reconstruction of her bedroom transport you to the Grand Siècle.
The château's timberwork, open to the public, forms a spectacular labyrinth of beams. An attic full of old-fashioned toys is arranged under the rafters—children can handle the toys from yesteryear.
The château chapel preserves wall paintings and a grand stone staircase. The 120-hectare English-style park extends behind the ramparts.

Twenty-five kilometers away, the Guédelon construction site builds a fortified castle using only 13th-century techniques. Stonemasons, carpenters, and blacksmiths have worked before visitors' eyes since the late 1990s.

activities.text: In summer, the château's historical pageant brings together 600 volunteers and 50 horsemen to retrace 1,000 years of history in 90 minutes. On Friday and Saturday evenings in July-August, the château blazes at nightfall.
Lake Bourdon, the Yonne's largest lake, spreads at the village's foot. Supervised swimming in summer, hiking trails, and fishing enliven its wooded shores.
On Thursday summer evenings, candlelit night tours of the château reveal private apartments. Costumed characters guide visitors between legend and reality.

days_plan.itin_title: Yonne Circuit: Northern Burgundy & Chablis Wine Country

places.name: Saint Florent

visits.text: Saint-Florent is nicknamed the Corsican Saint-Tropez—an upscale marina on the Gulf of Saint-Florent, with its white yachts, waterfront restaurant terraces, and evening buzz. The 15th-century Genoese citadel dominates the harbor from the hill. It's well preserved and you can walk its ramparts. Below the harbor, wander the old town's lanes before joining the evening promenade.

The Cathedral of Santa Maria Assunta del Nebbio, 1 km from Saint-Florent on the Bastia road, is one of Corsica's finest examples of Pisan Romanesque architecture: built in the 12th century in white limestone with an apse in cul-de-four and sculptures of serpents and animals on the capitals. The sober, cool interior contrasts with the heat outside. Take 20 minutes to walk around.

The beaches of Saleccia and Lotu, in the Désert des Agriates, rank among the Mediterranean's most beautiful wild beaches—white sand, turquoise water, and not a building on the horizon. Accessible only by shuttle boat from Saint-Florent harbor (30-minute trip) or by a 2-hour walking trail from Chjera. Shuttles run several times daily from May to October. Don't miss Saleccia: a kilometer of white sand surrounded by umbrella pines.

activities.text: Stroll through Saint-Florent's lanes and enjoy an aperitif on the harbor in late afternoon: the waterfront terraces come alive around 6 PM. It's a chance to taste Cap Corse, the local aperitif made from macerated Corsican wines that the Italians copied to make their Martini.

Tour operators offer day sea kayak rentals to reach the Désert des Agriates beaches from Saint-Florent. The sea route follows a coast virgin of any construction—count on 2 hours of paddling each way, with swimming stops along the way. Ideal for travelers who like getting away from crowds.

days_plan.itin_title: Upper Corsica Road Trip: Cap Corse and Balagne
Complete Tour of Corsica

map_keywords: Plage du Lotu Désert des Agriates

visits.text: Saint-Floret nestles against the rock at the entrance to a narrow gorge the Couze Pavin has carved through granite — Stendhal wrote there was 'nothing more picturesque.' The village developed around a seigneurial castle built from the 13th century by the Dauphins of Auvergne. Dare to push open the door of the castle's lower hall: you'll discover 13 exceptional frescoes painted around 1370 recounting the Romance of Tristan and Iseult — the only French depiction of this version of the medieval romance. The colours are vivid, the figures life-size. Visits are guided only, April to September (check times with the Issoire tourist office). In June 1944, the village was bombed by the Luftwaffe in reprisal — one section of town remains in ruins, deliberately preserved as a witness.

Cross the old Pont de la Pède from the 15th century: a single arch topped by an oratory housing a polychrome Romanesque Virgin in Majesty from the 13th century. This bridge is said to be miraculous since, during the 1944 bombing, the only bomb that failed to explode fell precisely here. Then climb to the Chastel site on the granite mound: a 12th-century Romanesque church surrounded by a necropolis with anthropomorphic tombs carved in rock, a pyramidal ossuary, and 14th-century frescoes inside — an archaeological site of rare density.

A few kilometres upstream from the village, don't miss the Tête de Lion spring: an impressive limestone concretion with a feline profile formed by deposits of calcium carbonate carried by the water (travertine). It's one of the most spectacular mineral springs among the 600 catalogued in Auvergne. The path follows the Couze Pavin — allow 20 minutes from the spring car park.

activities.text: The Couze Pavin gorge trail (3–5 km, moderate elevation gain, ~2h) follows the river upstream through its canyon between granite walls. Rock climbing sites line the river for enthusiasts — local operators offer introductions in the gorges. The Couze flow can be strong during rainy periods: check locally on conditions.

days_plan.itin_title: Auvergne's Volcanic Villages Trail

visits.text: The Cathédrale Saint-Pierre dominates the Place d'Armes with its two square towers rising over 80 metres. Built between the 14th and 15th centuries in dark lava stone, it holds the record for the highest Gothic cathedral in Europe by altitude (892 m at the forecourt). The massive austerity of its façade contrasts with its interior treasures: the "Beau Dieu noir", a 12th-century walnut-wood Christ, and an organ that resonates during regular concerts testify to its spiritual grandeur.

The upper town stands like a stone vessel on its basaltic promontory at 900 metres altitude. Labelled City of Art and History, it reveals cobbled lanes, Renaissance townhouses and medieval ramparts in a setting of volcanic stone. The ancient fortified gates recall its motto: "None took me by force but the wind". From the heights, the view plunges down to the Ander valley and the Cantal mountains.

The Musée de la Haute-Auvergne, housed in the former episcopal palace, presents ethnographic and archaeological collections tracing the territory's history. Discover pastoral traditions, local craftsmanship and testimonies of daily life in this historic capital of Haute-Auvergne.

activities.text: The weekly market fills the upper town in a convivial atmosphere. Auvergne cheeses, artisanal cured meats, honeys and regional products are displayed on the stalls of local producers. Cantal gastronomy reveals all its mountain generosity here.

The ramparts circuit follows the natural and medieval fortifications that once protected the city. This walk reveals the vertiginous basaltic escarpments, preserved fortified gates and panoramas over the surrounding valleys. The defensive dimension of Saint-Flour appears in all its strategic force.

days_plan.itin_title: Cantal Road Trip: Auvergne Volcanoes and Mountain Traditions

visits.text: The Collegiate Church of Saint-Pierre-et-Saint-Gaudens dates from the 11th century. Its Romanesque bell tower and sculpted portal are remarkable. Inside, historiated capitals narrate biblical scenes.
The municipal museum presents local archaeology and sacred art collections. You'll also see works on the history of Saint-Gaudens.

activities.text: Thursday morning market is one of the most important in Comminges. Mountain and plain producers gather to sell poultry, cheeses, and vegetables.
Walk in the Garonne valley which begins to narrow here. The Pyrenees draw closer and the landscape changes with the first mountain reliefs.

days_plan.itin_title: Haute-Garonne Road Trip: From Toulouse to the Pyrenees

places.name: Saint Germain En Laye

visits.text: En route, stop at the Museum of the Royal Estate of Marly: scale models and engravings trace the history of Louis XIV's vanished château and the workings of the famous Marly Machine.

Visit the remains of the Marly Machine, dubbed the "eighth wonder of the world" when built between 1681 and 1684. Its 14 hydraulic wheels, each 12 meters in diameter, pumped water from the Seine to feed Versailles' fountains.

The Château of Saint-Germain-en-Laye houses the National Museum of Archaeology and its 29,000 objects spanning Paleolithic to Merovingian times. The Lady of Brassempouy, a tiny ivory face carved 21,000 years ago, is the centerpiece.
The Sainte-Louis Chapel, built between 1230 and 1238, is a jewel of Rayonnant Gothic that inspired Paris's Sainte-Chapelle. Its tall stained-glass windows flood the nave with light.

Walk along the Grande Terrasse designed by Le Nôtre: 2.4 kilometers in a straight line with panoramic views over the Seine Valley and western Paris. On clear days, you can spot the La Défense skyline on the horizon.

activities.text: Follow the Impressionists' Path from Bougival to Chatou along the Seine. Enameled plaques reproduce paintings created at each spot, including Monet and Renoir's La Grenouillère.
Browse the Saint-Germain-en-Laye market, one of the finest in Yvelines, to taste regional produce from the Île-de-France.

days_plan.itin_title: Yvelines Road Trip: Versailles and Impressionist Villages

map_keywords: Musée du Domaine royal de Marly Port-Marly

visits.text: Saint-Gilles fishing port is the leading sardine port on the Atlantic coast. Trawlers unload sardines, anchovies, and hake onto the quays each morning.

Lose yourself in the narrow streets of Vieux Saint-Gilles, the town's oldest quarter. Rue Torterue, the main thoroughfare since the Middle Ages, preserves stone houses built from ballast unloaded by merchant ships.
Climb to the top of Tour Joséphine, the town's first lighthouse built in 1851 at the request of sixteen harbor captains. From its base, boats depart for Île d'Yeu.
Cross the Pont de la Concorde, built in 1952 to unite two formerly rival communes. A sailor statue displays the tidal scale.
Saint-Gilles Church, restored in 1971, houses a Stations of the Cross by Henry Simon and remarkable stained glass. Free guided tours take place in summer.
The Remblai promenade lines the Grande Plage for 3.6 km. Head to the Villa Notre-Dame viewpoint for sweeping vistas over both harbors, the beach, and Boisvinet beach.

activities.text: Visit the Sardine Workshop (Atelier de la Sardine), classified as a Remarkable Taste Site. The museum traces the stages of artisanal canning: fishing, selection, hand-packing into tins.
Follow the Corniche Vendéenne on foot or by bike between Boisvinet and Saint-Hilaire-de-Riez. Belle Époque seaside villas overlook the rocky coastline facing the open sea.
Discover the salt marshes of Saint-Hilaire-de-Riez. Salt workers harvest white gold from the oeillets (crystallizing ponds) and explain the difference between coarse salt and fleur de sel.
Climb aboard a draisine on the disused railway line. This pedal-powered rail bike crosses the Vendée countryside for several kilometers, between marshland and bocage.

days_plan.itin_title: Vendée Tour: Noirmoutier Island, Puy du Fou & Coast

visits.text: City center.
Traditional market (Saturday).

activities.text: Fishing.
Hiking.
Discovery of Couserans.

days_plan.itin_title: Hérault, France: Mediterranean Beaches and Languedoc Wine Country
Occitanie's 13 Most Beautiful Villages
Southeast France Road Trip: Perched Villages and Dramatic Gorges

places.name: Saint Haon Le Chatel

visits.text: Medieval Fortified Village. Perched on a porphyry rocky outcrop, Saint-Haon-le-Châtel preserves its 14th-century ramparts—a configuration unique in the Loire. At just 87 hectares, it's the department's smallest commune. Seventeen towers punctuate the medieval walls. Cobbled lanes lined with timber-framed houses, Renaissance manors like the Fleur de Lys with its original turrets, and 17th- and 18th-century townhouses compose this Village of Character. The Pelletier townhouse, a beautiful Renaissance dwelling, leans against one of the rampart towers.

Saint-Eustache Romanesque Church. This massive 12th-century Romanesque edifice anticipates the Gothic style with its pointed arches and lancet windows. The bell tower rises over the nave's first bay, dominating the village. 15th-century chapels were added on the north side. A 17th-century five-sided chevet with tall round-arched windows replaced the original. Statuary and murals adorn the interior. A porch adjoining the west façade provides access to the historic cemetery, which offers exceptional panoramas from the hilltop.

Petit Chateaumorand and Gardens. This seigneurial residence opens during guided tours. The medieval garden, reconstructed in the 1990s, presents medicinal and aromatic plants cultivated in the Middle Ages. Didactic panels explain plant uses. Garden access is free. The Sundial House, Maret House, and Murette House complete the village's remarkable architectural heritage.

activities.text: Village Guided Tour. Historical walks offered year-round, even at night for special atmosphere, by reservation. Guides lead visitors through streets, alleys, interior courtyards, ramparts, and the medieval garden. Anecdotes and historical explanations enrich the visit. Jean-Jules Jusserand, French ambassador to the United States from 1903 to 1925, vacationed in Saint-Haon-le-Châtel. A street and tower bear his name.

Medieval Treasure Hunt. A scavenger hunt for families with children. 'The Lost Cannonball of Charles VII' adventure plunges participants into the village's history in 1440, when Charles VII confronted his son Louis XI. Capucine and Colline guide treasure hunters through medieval lanes to recover the king's favorite cannonball. Booklet sold at the Tourist Office.

days_plan.itin_title: Loire Discovery: Wild Gorges and Industrial Heritage

map_keywords: Manoir de la Fleur de Lys Saint-Haon

visits.text: The Cirque de Consolation is a wild 350-meter-deep reculée with magnificent limestone cliffs, in the heart of the Doubs Horloger regional natural park. This is where the Dessoubre is born, fed by the springs of Lançot, Tabourot, and Source Noire. The Minimes monastery, built in 1670 with stones from the castle of Châtelneuf-en-Vennes destroyed during the Ten Years' War, forms a 40-meter square. The 1682 chapel preserves art objects and the mausoleum of Ferdinand-François de Rye.

The Lançot waterfall plunges 47 meters into the Cirque de Consolation. It is fed during heavy rains or snowmelt. The path leading there passes through a magical atmosphere of streams, tufa formations, and small caves. The lush vegetation creates an atmosphere conducive to contemplation.

Saint-Hippolyte, a character town of Bourgogne-Franche-Comté, nestles at the confluence of the Doubs and Dessoubre (the 'Revirot'). Freed by Jean II de la Roche in 1298, it preserves its 14th-century collegiate church of Notre-Dame where the Shroud of Turin was kept for 35 years (1418-1452), the 18th-century Ursuline convent, and 16th and 17th-century mullioned houses. It is the historic capital of the Franche-Montagne.

activities.text: The Roche du Prêtre viewpoint offers a vertiginous panorama over the Cirque de Consolation and the Dessoubre valley, 350 meters below. Accessible by car or on foot via a sporty hike. According to legend, a priest was thrown from the top of the cliff by the friends of a giant he had locked in a cave. Picnic tables provided.

Descend the Dessoubre valley to Saint-Hippolyte. This picturesque 33 km valley, where many water mills once sang, offers exceptional environmental richness. It is paradise for fishermen (trout, grayling) and walkers who appreciate the ambient calm. In summer, the cool water invites you to dip your feet.

days_plan.itin_title: Doubs Circuit: Watchmaking and Swiss Border

visits.text: Thermal spa.
Church.
Belle Époque heritage.

activities.text: Thermal baths.
Hiking in the Morvan forest.

days_plan.itin_title: Nièvre, France: Morvan Hills and Ducal Castle Heritage Routes

visits.text: Villa Ephrussi de Rothschild is a pink palace built between 1905 and 1912 by Baroness Béatrice de Rothschild on 7 hectares at the summit of the peninsula. The baroness competed for this land with King Leopold II of Belgium, who wanted to expand his neighboring property. She required her gardeners to wear sailor berets to imagine herself surrounded by a crew on a crossing. The palace is surrounded by nine themed gardens (French, Spanish, Florentine, Japanese, exotic, Provençal…). Classified as a Historic Monument, managed since 2023 by the Académie des Beaux-Arts.

Cap Ferrat coastal path: marked 10-km trail that makes a complete circuit of the peninsula. Between cliffs, secret coves, and maritime pines, you'll pass the Saint-Hospice Tower (11th century) and contemplate alternating views over Beaulieu on one side, Villefranche on the other. Count 3 hours for the complete circuit, or take a section according to your stamina.

activities.text: Coastal kayaking or paddleboarding: several operators offer rentals from Plage de Passable. The waters around the cape are remarkably clear and pleasure boat traffic is moderate outside July-August. The kayak circuit around the cape takes about 2.5 hours at your own pace.

Swimming at Plage Paloma (fine pebbles, eastern exposure, clear water) or at Plage de Passable (west side, well sheltered). Paloma is the most crowded; if you're seeking more tranquility, head down to the small coves along the coastal path.

days_plan.itin_title: The 15 Jewels of the French Riviera and Its Hinterland

visits.text: Saint-Jean-de-Côle is one of Périgord Vert's most charming villages, classified as one of France's Most Beautiful. The central square, bordered by the Château de la Marthonie (15th-17th century), the market hall, and the Romanesque church, is among Dordogne's most harmonious. From the old humpback bridge, you'll capture the village's postcard view.

activities.text: Walk the village heritage loop—a 3-kilometer marked trail takes you through the lanes, along the river, and back via the old bridge. Allow 45 minutes. In May, the village hosts the Floralies, one of southwestern France's finest flower markets.

days_plan.itin_title: Nouvelle-Aquitaine Loop: Medieval Villages, Vineyards & Dordogne Valley

visits.text: The Église Saint-Jean-Baptiste houses a stunning baroque altarpiece in gilded wood crafted between 1663 and 1669. Its three tiers of carved oak galleries are quintessentially Labourd, creating an intimate ship-like interior.

Maison Louis XIV, built in 1643, hosted the Sun King in May 1660 before his wedding. Its pink sandstone facades and corner towers exemplify 17th-century shipowner architecture when Basque corsairs grew wealthy.
Step inside the Maison de l'Infante where Marie-Thérèse of Austria stayed before becoming Queen of France. Its two galleries with five arcades each draw inspiration from Italian Renaissance palazzos.
Wander along Rue de la République where pink brick shipowners' mansions line this pedestrian artery that once connected Bayonne to Spain in the days of maritime trade.

activities.text: The Port des Pêcheurs comes alive at dawn when trawlers unload anchovies, tuna, and hake from the Bay of Saint-Jean, continuing centuries of fishing tradition.
Ride the petit train de la Rhune that climbs to 905 meters in 35 minutes. At the summit, wild pottok ponies and griffon vultures roam freely across the mountain grasslands.
Watch a pelota basque match at the port's fronton where players wield the chistera and bare hands in this traditional game passed down through generations.

days_plan.itin_title: Pyrénées-Atlantiques Road Trip: Basque Country & Béarn

visits.text: The Cathédrale Saint-Jean-Baptiste, founded in the 6th century, rebuilt in the 11th, and enlarged in the 15th, possesses one of France's oldest timber frames (wood dated to 1075). In the choir, 82 walnut Gothic stalls carved in 1498 depict saints, apostles, fauna, and flora—each one different from its neighbor.

The cloister, built around 1450 between the cathedral and the canons' refectory, unfolds its tufa vaults reinforced with local gypsum ribs. From the south bay, you access the Romanesque crypt, rediscovered in 1958 beneath the Gothic choir.

The neoclassical portico (1771) shelters the cenotaphs of three of the first princes of the House of Savoy: Humbert the White-Handed, founder of the dynasty, Amadeus I, and Boniface. The bell tower, a former 11th-century capitular keep, lost its Gothic spire during the Revolution.

The Musée Opinel occupies nearly 700 m² dedicated to the famous Savoyard knife. From the old forge to modern workshops, the exhibition traces the adventure of a tool created in 1890 in the nearby hamlet of Gévoudaz, today distributed in over 70 countries. Free entry, personalized engraving in the boutique.

Opposite the cathedral, the former bishop's palace (listed Historic Monument) houses the Musée des Costumes, Arts et Traditions Populaires. The collection of traditional Maurienne costumes, among the Alps' most original, displays embroidered headdresses, festive aprons, and dresses from the Arvan valleys.

Stroll through the historic center: the cathedral square hosts the market, lanes reveal the Tabellion block (medieval tribunal), the Art Deco festival hall (1933) that became the Théâtre Gérard Philipe, and at the town entrance, two giant Opinel knife sculptures mark local pride.
