activities.text: Excursion to the Lacets de Montvernier. This spectacular road chains together eighteen tight hairpins on a vertical wall above the Maurienne valley. Plunging view guaranteed, a legendary Tour de France passage since 2015.
Cycling on the Maurienne greenway. The bike path follows the Arc through the valley, linking villages between orchards and valley-floor meadows. E-bike rental available on-site.

Tasting of Mauriennais specialties: Saint-Jean local cheese, diots (smoked sausages cooked in white wine), corn polenta, and crozets (small square buckwheat pasta). Restaurants around the cathedral square offer these traditional dishes.

days_plan.itin_title: Savoie from Lakes to Glaciers: Yenne to Haute-Maurienne

visits.text: Village of Saint-Jean-du-Gard, typical Cévennes town in the heart of Cévennes National Park. Schist houses with slate roofs tier along the Gardon. The 12th-century Romanesque church stands beside a Protestant temple testifying to Cévennes Huguenot history. Paved streets lined with artisan workshops wind between fountains and shaded squares. Tuesday morning market animates the center with local products and friendly atmosphere. The multi-arched medieval bridge spans the Gardon offering views of surrounding mountains.

Musée des Vallées Cévenoles – Maison Rouge, ethnographic collection tracing traditional lifestyles. Reconstructions present authentic Cévennes interiors: rustic furniture, daily utensils, regional costumes. The silk-farming department evokes the epic of magnaneries and flourishing silk industry until 1900. Thematic rooms document terraced agriculture, sheep farming, century-old chestnut groves. The Camisard space relates Wars of Religion and Protestant resistance in the 18th century. Temporary exhibitions deepen intangible Cévennes heritage.

Atlantide Parc, family water park and leisure center on 12 acres. Play pools, water slides, and lazy river refresh during hot summer days. The wellness area offers spa, hammam, and jacuzzis in green surroundings. Land activities include mini-golf, trampolines, inflatable structures for children. Shaded picnic areas under plane trees allow family lunches. The park blends aquatic relaxation and entertainment in preserved natural environment.

activities.text: Hike the Stevenson Trail (GR70) crossing the Cévennes. The Scottish writer covered 137 miles in 1878 with his donkey Modestine, recounted in "Travels with a Donkey in the Cévennes." The mythical trail winds through chestnut groves, schist ridges, deep valleys, and isolated hamlets. Stages around Saint-Jean-du-Gard discover typical Cévennes landscapes: cultivated terraces, pastoral drailles, mountain panoramas. Gîtes d'étape dot the route welcoming hikers. Donkey rental perpetuates the Stevensonian tradition.

Discover Cévennes chestnut groves, cultivated forests shaping terraced landscapes. The chestnut tree, the "bread tree," ensured Cévennes subsistence providing flour, building wood, and heating. Century-old orchards with gnarled trunks tier on slopes arranged in terraces supported by dry-stone walls. Autumn sees fallen chestnuts gathered and transformed into cream, jam, flour. Clèdes, vaulted buildings for drying, dot the chestnut groves. Interpretive trails explain this heritage silviculture inscribed by UNESCO.

Visit a traditional magnanerie, silkworm breeding building. Restored magnaneries testify to prosperous silk-farming industry in the 18th-19th centuries. Vaulted rooms sheltered racks supporting millions of worms devouring mulberry leaves. The complete process—from egg to cocoon, silk reeling, weaving—unfolds through exhibitions and demonstrations. Mulberry trees planted on terraces fed seasonal breeding operations. The Cévennes silk epic exported to Lyon enriched the region before declining against Asian competition.

days_plan.itin_title: Gard Road Trip: Roman Nîmes, Pont du Gard & Camargue Wilderness

visits.text: The Porte Saint-Jacques, inscribed on UNESCO's World Heritage list, marks the historic entrance for pilgrims. Since the Middle Ages, it has opened onto the route through the Ports de Cize passes toward Roncevaux.
Climb to the Citadel fortified by Vauban in the 17th century. From its ramparts rising 70 meters above the town, views plunge onto pink sandstone roofs and the Nive River winding below.
The Église Notre-Dame-du-Bout-du-Pont displays Gothic architecture beside the Nive. Its ogival vaults have sheltered pilgrims since the 14th century on their journey to Compostela.
The Rue de la Citadelle winds between half-timbered houses with overhanging upper floors. Its worn cobblestones have felt the tread of thousands of pilgrims heading to Compostela.
Visit the Prison des Évêques which presents an exhibition on medieval pilgrimage to Compostela. The 13th-14th century building tells the Camino's story through artifacts and displays.

activities.text: The pilgrim welcome office on Rue de la Citadelle provides information about crossing the Pyrenees. Over 60,000 walkers pass through annually on their way to Roncevaux.
Walk the aerial wooden gallery built for Louis XIV's wedding procession. It follows the Nive from the Porte Notre-Dame, suspended above the rushing waters.

days_plan.itin_title: Pyrénées-Atlantiques Road Trip: Basque Country & Béarn

places.name: Saint Jean Saint Maurice Sur Loire

visits.text: Château de la Roche. Anchored to its rocky outcrop in the middle of the Loire since the 13th century, this iconic gorge château appears to float on water when the river runs high, or to sit on a Scottish crag when autumn brings low waters. Theatrical guided tours feature costumed actor-guides portraying Monsieur and Madame Roustan, who owned the estate from 1900 to 1930. Period room settings transport visitors into the Belle Époque and the château's history. The four-leaf clover, a good-luck symbol, adorns façades and interior decoration. Two escape games unlock the château's secrets.

Medieval Village of Saint-Maurice. Perched on a promontory overlooking the Loire, the medieval town preserves its 12th-century donjon rising 17 meters. A spiral staircase climbs to the summit offering 360-degree panoramas of the Villerest dam waters and Loire meanders. Castle walls, the 17th-century Manoir de la Mure with its sculpted lintel doorway and finely decorated stair tower, and the Sundial House compose this Village of Character. Rue Guy de la Mure, lined with ivy-covered houses, offers a peaceful atmosphere.

Saint-Maurice Church. The choir entirely covered with classified 13th-century frescoes constitutes an exceptional pictorial ensemble. In a window embrasure on the south wall, a painting depicting Saint James attests that Saint-Maurice stood on the Compostela pilgrimage route. The Cluny/Le-Puy-en-Velay Jacquaire route still crosses the village. Saint-Jean's church, rebuilt around 1870, preserves its 12th-century bell tower-porch, sole witness to the Benedictine priory dismantled during the Revolution.

Joeuvres Archaeological Site. On the Loire's right bank, this hamlet shelters a Celtic oppidum inhabited since prehistoric times. Excavations led notably by Joseph Déchelette in 1911 revealed two 1st-century BCE bronze statuettes representing a horse and a wild boar, visible at Roanne's Joseph-Déchelette Museum. Ionian and Phocaean amphora shards prove Mediterranean trade via the Rhône and Ardèche. A bronze workshop operated on this strategic site.

activities.text: Pêt d'Âne Viewpoint. In the commune of Vézelin-sur-Loire, this panoramic site offers the finest view of the Loire's meanders. A contemplation bench faces the river's grandest bend. Below, kayaks and paddleboards glide on the wild river. The medieval silhouettes of Saint-Jean-Saint-Maurice-sur-Loire create a living postcard. Parking and easy access from the road.

Ridge Trail. A short walk offering the best views of the Loire meanders and Saint-Jean-Saint-Maurice tower. A high-altitude route overlooking the river. Trails through woods and meadows. Several artisan artists with studios in the village open by appointment.

days_plan.itin_title: Loire Discovery: Wild Gorges and Industrial Heritage

visits.text: Saint-Jeannet is a perched village nestled at the foot of the Baou—a Provençal word designating a steep rock with a flat summit—about twenty kilometers from Nice. The Queirard quarter, the village's oldest, unfolds its typical "calades": sloping lanes interrupted by steps to combat erosion, a characteristic inherited from Roman roads. The village was long a land of vines and wine; the Rasse family perpetuates this tradition on the route des Sausses toward Gattières.

From the village Panorama, the view extends from Col de Braüs on the Italian border to the Estérel mountains in the west—on clear days, Corsica can be distinguished on the horizon. Artists began settling here in the 1950s, drawn by this tranquility and these views. Georges Ribemont-Dessaignes, Dada poet, lived here from 1944 to 1974 and brought his friends—Tristan Tzara and Jacques Prévert stayed in Saint-Jeannet.

activities.text: The hike to the Baou de Saint-Jeannet is the area's essential excursion: 6 km round trip from the village parking lot for 400 meters positive elevation gain. The summit, at 802 meters altitude, offers a 360° panorama over the Côte d'Azur, Mercantour, and Italy—one of the region's finest panoramic views. The Baou is also a renowned climbing site: dozens of routes have been opened in its limestone cliffs. Leave early in the morning, parking fills quickly in summer.

If you prefer a longer circuit, the Castellet trail passes by the ruins of a 14th-century fortified sheepfold and the Baou de la Gaude, with its remains of a Ligurian enclosure and a centuries-old oak measuring 6.7 meters in circumference. Allow 5 hours for the complete loop.

Descending back to the village after your hike, refresh yourself at the village fountains—several shade the lanes. Artisans offer herb ravioli to take away: a local specialty not to be missed if you have access to a kitchen tonight.

days_plan.itin_title: Road Trip Through the Nice & Grasse Backcountry
The 15 Jewels of the French Riviera and Its Hinterland
Nice Hinterland Circuit: Perched Villages and Mountain Trails

places.name: Saint Junien

visits.text: The Collégiale de Saint-Junien, one of the largest and oldest Romanesque churches in Limousin, was built from the 11th to 13th centuries in local granite. Its tomb of Saint Junien, carved around 1100 in La Rochefoucauld limestone, bears on three faces the twenty-four elders of the Apocalypse and Christ in a mandorla.

In the nave, look up: the fresco of the twenty-four elders of the Apocalypse, from the 12th century, echoes the tomb's iconography. The Saint-Martial chapel preserves Romanesque paintings showing the display of relics followed by pilgrims seeking healing.

The Chapelle Notre-Dame-du-Pont, in Flamboyant Gothic style, has guarded the Vienne bridge entrance since 1451. Enlarged thanks to the generosity of King Louis XI, who came on pilgrimage in 1463, it remains a place of popular devotion.

The Cité du Cuir, opened in late 2025 on the Vienne riverbank at a former tannery site, traces the complete luxury glove manufacturing cycle. The immersive museum route runs from raw hide to finished product, passing through reconstructed workshops.

The Maison des Consuls, built in the 14th century, testifies to the medieval town's prosperity. A few steps away, the Bourse du Travail on Place Lénine recalls Saint-Junien's working-class social history, a glove-making town since the 11th century.

activities.text: Every seven years, Saint-Junien celebrates the Ostensions septennales, inscribed on UNESCO's list: 1,500 costumed participants parade through town in procession around the relics. Four Swiss guards watch over the tomb year-round — a unique feature in Limousin.

Follow the Glane riverbanks to the Corot site, classified as a Natural Area of Ecological Interest. The 2 km trail crosses one of the Vienne's largest islands, where painter Corot once set up his easel.
Visit a glove-making workshop to observe artisan craftsmanship, from cutting leather to assembling gloves. Saint-Junien has supplied the greatest fashion houses since the 19th century.

days_plan.itin_title: Haute-Vienne Circuit: Limoges, Porcelain & Historic Villages

map_keywords: Site Corot Bords de Glane Saint-Junien

visits.text: Saint-Lary-Soulan was born from the 1963 merger of the villages of Saint-Lary and Soulan to enable the ski resort's expansion. Located in the Aure valley and classified as a Land of Art and History, the village is a historic stop on the Way of Saint James. The main street bears the name of Vincent Mir, founder of the resort. The Pyrenean architecture of its stone houses and enhanced facades, the Pic Lumière cable car and the Vignec gondola linking the village to the slopes compose an authentic mountain setting.

The Maison du Patrimoine, located at 41 rue Vincent Mir on the Tourist Office square, houses several museum spaces dedicated to Pyrenean heritage: traditions, fauna, architecture, old-time schools and livestock farming. Upstairs, an interactive 3D map allows immersion in the valley's trails, passes, and lakes. Temporary exhibitions of painting, photography, and sculpture are presented throughout the year. Guided village tours are organized by the Aure-Louron Land of Art and History association.

The Church of Saint-Bertrand, built between 1950 and 1952 and consecrated in October 1952, replaced the old Saint-Hilaire church. Nearby, the Sainte-Marie Chapel and the Romanesque chapels from the 11th and 12th centuries in surrounding villages testify to the valley's rich religious heritage. The restored Debat Mill can be visited, as can the Moulin de la Mousquère in Soulan village, renovated and dating from the Middle Ages.

Soulan, a preserved and sunny pastoral village, dominates Saint-Lary from the heights toward the ski resort. Three kilometers away, Vielle-Aure offers a typical village with cobbled streets, a 12th-century Romanesque church, and the Château de Moulor with its keep and chapel, also from the 12th century. Further on, Ancizan (six kilometers), a former village of clothiers and weavers, preserves the Saint-Blaise-et-Saint-Martin church. Gouaux and Grailhen offer a remarkable exhibition and breathtaking view of the peaks, with the Gouaux goat farm to visit.

The Rioumajou Valley, accessible from the Frédancon parking lot at 1,380 meters altitude (twelve kilometers from Saint-Lary), is an easy two-hour round-trip family hike on a forest track following the Neste du Rioumajou. The pastoral valley unfolds with vast meadows crossed by a stream winding to the Hospice du Rioumajou, a refuge-restaurant at 1,560 meters altitude with 27 sleeping spaces. Labeled "Esprit Parc national," it is open from June to September. For more experienced walkers, the Balcon de l'Hospice (1,969 m) offers a viewpoint overlooking the site, accessible in two and a half hours round trip. Vehicle access is regulated from July to August between 12 pm and 4 pm.

The Néouvielle Nature Reserve, created in 1936 and one of France's first nature reserves, covers 2,313 hectares between 1,800 and 3,091 meters altitude. It shelters seventy lakes (Aubert, Aumar, Orédon, Cap-de-Long, l'Oule), record-breaking black pines for longevity and altitude, 1,250 vascular plants, 370 animal species, and 570 algae species. Access is via the lakes road from Saint-Lary, with shuttles between Orédon and Aubert in season. Bivouacking is authorized from 7 pm to 9 am in specific zones, dogs and swimming are prohibited.

activities.text: Stroll through Saint-Lary's streets starting from the Maison du Patrimoine, then climb toward Soulan village for a sunny panorama over the Aure valley and its peaks.
Hike in the Rioumajou Valley to the Hospice, an easy and bucolic two-hour round-trip walk along the stream, ideal for families - the refuge restaurant serves cuisine labeled "Esprit Parc national."
Take the lakes road toward the Néouvielle Reserve to discover Lakes Aubert and Orédon in their setting of thousand-year-old black pines - shuttles facilitate access in summer.
Visit the villages of Vielle-Aure and Ancizan to discover traditional Pyrenean architecture, cobbled streets, and Romanesque churches, before stopping at the Gouaux goat farm.
Thrill-seekers will find a wide choice of activities in Saint-Lary: canyoning, rafting on the Neste, mountain biking in the bike park, paragliding, via ferrata, and downhill electric scooters.

days_plan.itin_title: The Pyrenees Spa Trail: Thermal Baths from Ax-les-Thermes to Bagnères-de-Luchon
Hautes-Pyrénées Circuit: From Tarbes to Cirques and Valleys
Occitanie's 13 Most Beautiful Villages

visits.text: Saint-Laurent-sous-Coiron places you at the foot of the Coiron massif, an imposing basalt plateau that visually contrasts with lower Ardèche's white limestone. The village occupies a plateau edge with exceptional circular views. Remains of the old castrum read in the village fabric: partly preserved enclosure, houses crowded around the church square, defensive layout typical of perched villages in Bas-Vivarais.

The Coiron's geological interest is spectacular. The basaltic flows forming the plateau—of volcanic origin, like virtually all of northern Ardèche—created landscapes of columns and dykes visible on the ridges. From the village heights, on clear days, views embrace the Cévennes westward and the first ridge lines leading toward Aubenas.

The church square atop the village is the quietest spot for a break: houses shelter the square's corners and an old fountain sometimes runs. The view south over valleys descending toward the Ardèche is particularly beautiful late in the day.

activities.text: The ridge trail on the Coiron rim (3 to 5 km from the village) is the area's signature walk. It follows the break in slope between the basalt plateau and limestone hills, with views north over the plateau and south over valleys. Allow 1h30 to 2h at your pace.

Stop on the road reaching the plateau to observe basalt columns on the cliff face—these hexagonal columns formed by cooling lava flows are visible from the road without requiring a walk. A good moment of geological reading to understand where the black stones omnipresent in local construction come from.

days_plan.itin_title: The 18 Most Beautiful Villages of Ardèche

visits.text: Limeuil, at the confluence of the Dordogne and Vézère rivers, is classified as one of France's Most Beautiful Villages. Climb to the Jardins Panoramiques for views across both rivers—you'll look down over the entire confluence from the former château site. In summer, the pebble beaches are perfect for a swim.

Saint-Amand-de-Coly shelters a fortified 12th-century abbey—one of Dordogne's most beautiful. You'll see the fortress-church, with its 2-meter-thick walls and wall-walk, served as a refuge during wartime. The honey-stone village is tiny and silent.

Saint-Léon-sur-Vézère is a small town nestled in a meander of the Vézère. Its 12th-century Romanesque church, right at the water's edge, is one of Périgord's most harmonious—you'll see it reflected in the river from the bridge.

activities.text: Canoe down the Vézère from Saint-Léon—the section toward Les Eyzies is easy, flat, and passes through landscapes of limestone cliffs. Allow 2 to 3 hours depending on the distance you choose—you'll make the most of it.

days_plan.itin_title: Nouvelle-Aquitaine Loop: Medieval Villages, Vineyards & Dordogne Valley

visits.text: The Collégiale de Saint-Léonard, inscribed on UNESCO's World Heritage List as part of the Routes of Santiago de Compostela, dominates the town from its seven-story bell tower rising 52 meters. Pass through the Limousin portal to discover the barrel-vaulted nave, the octagonal lantern tower, and the vast choir with ambulatory encircled by seven radiating chapels.

At the heart of the collegiate church, Saint Léonard's tomb is covered with chains of 'freed prisoners.' Tradition holds that women touch the saint's bolt to have their prayers answered. Small booties are still placed on the tomb in thanks.

The baptistery rotunda, vaulted with a cupola resting on eight columns, was erected around 1100, during the time of the First Crusade. It illustrates Limousin Romanesque architectural mastery at its finest.

The Moulin du Got, Limousin's last working paper mill, brings to life handmade papermaking and printing techniques from the 15th to 20th centuries. Take home a souvenir made from paper crafted on-site.

The Musée Gay-Lussac, labeled Maison des Illustres, honors the chemist born here in 1778. Personal clothing, laboratory instruments, and publications trace the life and work of this scientist who gave his name to several fundamental laws of gas physics.

activities.text: Roam the lanes of the medieval town between the round tower and square tower. Gothic facades, secret passages, and remnants of fortified gates mark the route. Saint-Léonard is classified among France's 'Plus Beaux Détours.'
Walk down to the old 13th-century stone bridge over the Vienne. The small 4 km loop offers views of the viaduct and surrounding countryside — ideal for a post-meal stroll.

Taste the massepain de Saint-Léonard, a biscuit made from ground almonds, sugar, and egg white. Legend says this Mediterranean-flavored delicacy was brought back by pilgrims returning from Compostela.

days_plan.itin_title: Haute-Vienne Circuit: Limoges, Porcelain & Historic Villages

visits.text: Cathedral (UNESCO).
Cloister.
Bishops' Palace.

activities.text: Medieval village.
Stroll through the narrow streets.

days_plan.itin_title: Ariège Pyrenees Road Trip, France: Cathar Castles & Prehistoric Cave Art

visits.text: The abbey church founded in the 5th century was rebuilt in the 17th century in Gothic style by the Benedictines of Saint-Maur after the Wars of Religion.
The 17th-century rood screen, sculpted woodwork, and sixteen-lobed monolithic baptismal font testify to the richness of the building.
The 7th-century crypt houses the relics of Saint Léger. Its barrel vaults and Romanesque capitals are listed as Historic Monuments.
The cloister and chapter house now host exhibitions and cultural events.

activities.text: Visit the National School for Active Non-Commissioned Officers created in 1963, a unique training ground for the French Army.
Stroll along the banks of the Sèvre Niortaise between half-timbered houses and 16th-century mansions.

days_plan.itin_title: Deux-Sèvres Circuit: Marais Poitevin and Romanesque Heritage

visits.text: Mont-Dol (panorama).
Champ-Dolent Menhir.
Chateaubriand Castle.
Tranquil Lake.

activities.text: Oyster farms.
Guided bay crossing.
Spring tides.
La Mère Poulard Inn.

days_plan.itin_title: Ille-et-Vilaine, Brittany: Saint-Malo and Medieval Walled Towns
Brittany Road Trip: Historic Villages, Sea and Heritage

visits.text: In the 15th century, Saint-Marcellin was the stronghold hosting the region's largest market, held three times weekly. This market gave its name to the town's emblematic cheese—an 80-gram disc with soft paste and bloomy rind, first documented in King Louis XI's household accounts from 1461. Saved from a bear in 1447, Louis XI allegedly received bread and cheese—Saint-Marcellin—from two local woodcutters. According to legend, he promised them fortune and nobility. The cheese earned its PGI designation in 2013, covering 274 communes across Isère, Drôme, and Savoie.

Saint-Marcellin's old center preserves a medieval layout around its church with arcades, narrow streets, and remnants of ramparts. Then descend to the Isère riverbanks: the tree-lined promenades along the river offer open views of the Royans hillsides.

activities.text: The historic gates and suburbs circuit of Saint-Marcellin covers about 2 km on flat ground and reveals the different layers of occupation from the medieval town onward. If you're there Wednesday or Saturday morning, the weekly market is a perfect opportunity to taste Saint-Marcellin PGI in its two states: dry with blue rind, Dauphiné style; or soft and creamy, Lyon style.

The riverside walk along the Isère, downstream from the town center, leads to the confluence with the Bourne. It's a bucolic route with no elevation gain, perfect for evening strolls with reflections on the water and Royans hillsides in the background.

days_plan.itin_title: Royans-Vercors Road Trip: Hilltop Villages and Gorges

visits.text: Saint-Martin-d'Ardèche marks the spectacular end of the Gorges at their confluence with the Rhône. This green resort village offers equipped beaches and family activities. The Famille Plus label guarantees infrastructure and entertainment suited to all ages.

The scenic road winds for 29 km between Vallon and Saint-Martin, offering eleven equipped belvederes. The Cathedral belvedere, Cathedral Rock, and La Madeleine reveal vertiginous views over the river's meanders. The limestone cliffs plunge up to 300 meters high.

The beaches of Saint-Martin line the Ardèche with tranquil coves. Pont Cassé beach offers a peaceful setting for swimming and picnics. The calm waters downstream from the gorges are perfectly suited for families with young children.

The medieval village of Saint-Marcel-d'Ardèche layers its cobbled lanes paved with Rhône pebbles. Ochre facades and vaulted passages compose an authentic Provençal scene. The Saint-Marcel cave reveals a spectacular underground network nearby.

activities.text: The Bois de Païolive spreads its white oak forest among tortured limestone rocks. Rock formations sculpted by erosion create a unique mineral labyrinth. The Chassezac River winds through this geological chaos classified as a Sensitive Natural Area, offering swimming in natural pools.

The weekly market brings Saint-Martin to life every Wednesday morning. Colorful stalls offer local products, olives, goat cheeses, and Ardèche specialties. The vibrant Provençal atmosphere celebrates local flavors beneath the plane trees.

days_plan.itin_title: Ardèche Road Trip: Wild Gorges and Heritage Villages

visits.text: Saint-Martin-de-Brômes is a village of endearing simplicity: Jean Giono nicknamed it 'the curled-up fox'. It's built in an amphitheater on the hillside of Castelleras, at the confluence of the Colostre and Pontet rivers. The village emblem is the Clock Tower — former keep built by Boniface de Castellane in the 14th century, 24 meters high with machicolations and arrow slits. Classified as a Historic Monument since 1921, it has housed since 1972 one of France's smallest museums (10 m²): on the ground floor, a 4th-century Gallo-Roman sarcophagus with a 300 kg lead lid and intact skeleton, discovered in 1972 during road works, and a milestone of Emperor Carus (282-283 CE) that marked the Roman road between Aix-en-Provence and Riez.

The Romanesque Church of Saint-Martin (12th-14th centuries), classified as a Historic Monument, is known for its 19th-century murals by Esprit Michel Gibelin — student of Cézanne's workshop — depicting the lives of Saint Martin and the Virgin in the nave's blind arcades. Also look for the statuette of Saint Anthony the Hermit in gilded and polychrome wood, patron of truffle hunters, the oldest in the church.

activities.text: 7 km away, Lake Esparron-de-Verdon opens onto turquoise water — an artificial reservoir created in 1967, but set in a landscape of limestone gorges that gives it a wild appearance. Operators offer pedal boats, canoes, and electric boats for rent. It's one of the best swimming spots in the region, ideal for an afternoon.

The GR4 crosses the commune and offers beautiful ridge trails above the Colostre — views of the Valensole plateau on one side and the emerging Verdon gorges on the other. A 2-hour loop suffices for the most interesting viewpoints.

days_plan.itin_title: Forcalquier & Verdon Gorges Road Trip

visits.text: Welcome to the Crau plain — a landscape unique in France: 55,000 hectares of pebbles rolled by the ancient Durance three million years ago, forming Western Europe's only dry grassland steppe. The Crau sèche, an area classified as a national nature reserve covering 7,400 hectares, harbors species existing nowhere else in France — little bustard, pin-tailed sandgrouse, ocellated lizard. The Crau ecomuseum offers a complete introduction to this singular ecosystem.

The irrigated Crau's canal irrigation system is a hydraulic work dating back to Adam de Craponne (16th century) — the engineer who created the canal bearing his name to bring Durance water to the Crau. This network transformed part of the dry steppe into Crau hay meadows (PDO since 1997, the only PDO for hay in France). Irrigated meadows are easily recognized by their grass's intense green in midsummer.

activities.text: Wildlife observation on Crau sèche tracks: according to regulations (inquire at the ecomuseum), marked circuits allow observation of steppe fauna — little bustards (during display period, April-May), pin-tailed sandgrouse, short-toed snake eagles, and lesser kestrels. The Crau is an ornithologically exceptional area for open Mediterranean habitat species.

Cycling loop in the irrigated plain (completely flat terrain): agricultural paths crossing the irrigated Crau among hay meadows, shepherds' huts, and market gardens offer a ride completely off ordinary marked trails. A few 17th-18th century farmhouses dot the route.

days_plan.itin_title: Alpilles, Aix-en-Provence & Camargue Circuit

visits.text: The Île de Ré bridge, 2.9 km long, has connected the mainland to the island since 1988. Toll on entry (free on exit). The crossing offers a first view of the salt marshes and the black and white bell tower of Ars-en-Ré.

The fortifications of Saint-Martin-de-Ré, built by Vauban between 1681 and 1691, form a 14 km star-shaped rampart. UNESCO World Heritage since 2008, they could shelter the entire island population during an English invasion. Walking or cycling the ramparts is spectacular.

The port of Saint-Martin-de-Ré, nestled within the fortified walls, has quays lined with terraces. Convicts bound for Cayenne departed from here – the citadel served as a depot. Climb the church tower for a panoramic view of the island.

The Baleines Lighthouse, at the western tip of the island, stands 60 meters tall. 257 steps lead to the top – 360° view over the ocean and island. The name comes from whales that once stranded on this coast. The old Vauban tower (1682) and a lighthouse museum complete the site.

activities.text: Île de Ré by bike: 110 km of cycle paths connect the island's ten villages. Rentals available in every village. The Saint-Martin → Baleines Lighthouse route (15 km) crosses salt marshes and pine forests.
Visit the salt marshes of Loix or Ars-en-Ré. Salt workers harvest fleur de sel by hand using age-old techniques. Guided walks in the marshes, explanations of the work and tastings.
Île de Ré beaches: fine sand on the south coast (Bois-Plage, La Couarde), wilder to the north. Fish traps, semi-circular stone walls, catch fish at low tide – an ancestral technique still practiced.

days_plan.itin_title: Charente-Maritime Road Trip: La Rochelle, Île de Ré, Fort Boyard and Roman Saintonge

visits.text: Saint-Martin-en-Vercors is a valley village nestled at the foot of the Drôme Vercors ridges. The Romanesque church and surrounding hamlets compose a tableau of peaceful mountain countryside, crossed by combe streams. Wet meadows and beech forests descend to the first houses—an atmosphere that changes from Vassieux's open plateau.

The Combe de la Balme, downstream from the village, is a wooded corridor between two series of cliffs. Looking east from the village bottom, you see ridges culminating around 1,500 meters—a continuous wall marking the boundary between plateau and Die plain. The gray-orange cliffs change hue according to time of day.

activities.text: The hamlets and stream banks loop (3-4 km) connects the various hamlets composing the commune via farm roads. Combe streams are cool even in summer. It's a gentle hike, ideal before the final stage to Corrençon.

The Herbouilly Nordic area, 8 km from Saint-Martin-en-Vercors, is one of three gateways to the High Vercors Nordic domain. In summer, paths winding between beech forests and pastures with Scandinavian accents allow car-free walks. Pack a picnic to enjoy views of the High Plateaus at 1,000 meters altitude.

days_plan.itin_title: Royans-Vercors Road Trip: Hilltop Villages and Gorges

visits.text: Saint-Martin-Vésubie, nicknamed "the little Switzerland of Nice," is the tourist capital of Mercantour: at 1,000 m altitude, surrounded by peaks exceeding 3,000 meters, it offers access to 3,600 hectares of larch forests and alpine pastures. Stroll the alpine village lanes with wooden chalets, murmuring fountains and the canal running down the middle of the main street.

Centre Alpha "Le Temps du Loup" is the only structure of its kind in France: three natural habitats allow observation of wolves in semi-freedom at feeding time. Wolves were naturally reintroduced to Mercantour in the 1990s from Italy — the center helps understand the biology and behavior of these predators now at the center of debate. Book ahead in high season.

Continue to the Sanctuaire de la Madone de Fenestre at 1,903 meters altitude: this pilgrimage site accessible by winding road from Saint-Martin has been frequented since the Middle Ages. The view of the Gélas, culminating at 3,143 meters (highest point in the Alpes-Maritimes), is exceptional in good weather. The road is generally open from June to October.

activities.text: Hike to Lac du Boréon from the hamlet of the same name at 1,473 m: pale blue waters reflecting larches, marmots whistling on rocks, ibex surveying the horizon from ridges. The lake is accessible in 2 hours of easy walking from the parking lot. Don't leave without checking the weather — afternoon storms are frequent in summer.

The Lacs de Prals trail links several alpine lakes in a landscape of flowered pastures and scree: a moderate-level hike of about 4 hours that rewards walkers with views of Mercantour's rocky needles. Bring a warm layer — temperature drops quickly in shade.

Lac des Trécolpas, 9.5 km via the Boréon valley, is one of Mercantour's most beautiful lakes: picturesque forests of Swiss stone pines and larches, rolling meadows and mountain streams mark the route. Guaranteed sightings of alpine fauna — brown trout, chamois and golden eagles.

Operators offer canyoning or aqua-hiking outings in the crystalline gorges around Saint-Martin: jumps, natural slides, rappel descents into turquoise pools at 8-10°C. Family formulas are accessible from age 8, "expert" outings descend technical gorges with 20-meter rappels.

days_plan.itin_title: Alpes-Maritimes Road Trip: From the French Riviera to Perched Villages
Nice Hinterland Circuit: Perched Villages and Mountain Trails

places.name: Saint Maur Des Fosses

visits.text: Saint-Maur-des-Fossés forms a peninsula with 14 km of banks surrounded by the Marne's last major loop. When the Marie-Thérèse canal was cut through in 1825, barges stopped navigating these meanders — and the now-useless towpaths were colonized by riverside taverns, boaters, and painters. Cézanne came to set up his easel here. Follow the Promenade Paul Cézanne for 4 km from Quai Fernand Saguet to the Maisons-Alfort bridge. You'll pass islands classified as a departmental nature reserve — some were never developed.

Walking the banks, spot the vacation villas that sprang up from the late 19th century: flamboyant neo-Gothic, Anglo-Norman chalets, Art Nouveau on millstone grit. When the Bastille line opened in 1859, Parisians poured in by the thousands on Sundays, and architects from Nogent like the Nachbaurs had work for thirty years. Some of these houses carry Regional Heritage of Interest labels.

The Abbey of Saint-Maur was founded in the 9th century and received the relics of Saint Maur — disciple of Saint Benedict — on November 13, 868. It gave the town its name and dominated the commune's development for centuries before being dismantled during the Revolution.

activities.text: The Guinguette de l'Île du Martin-Pêcheur in Saint-Maur, accessible by footbridge, is one of the rare survivors from the golden age — there were over 200 between Joinville and Chelles during the Belle Époque. If you're there on a weekend between May and September, book a table for the evening. The accordion, paper lanterns, and checkered tablecloths haven't disappeared.

Vendors rent electric boats requiring no license from May to September. Take one to make the Marne loop — it's from the water that you'll understand why Cézanne and Lebasque kept coming back to paint the reflections and islands. The Saint-Maur canal tunnel (1,070 m, completed in 1822) lets you short-circuit the loop if you want to reach Joinville directly.

days_plan.itin_title: Discovering Val-de-Marne in 10 Days
Val-de-Marne Road Trip: Castles and River Marne Banks

map_keywords: Guinguette de l'Île du Martin-Pêcheur Saint-Maur
Réserve naturelle îles de la Marne

visits.text: Saint-Michel-l'Observatoire is a Provençal village of pale houses — it owes its name and worldwide fame to the Haute-Provence Observatory (OHP), founded in 1937 by CNRS after measurements taken as early as 1925 revealed 260 to 280 nights per year of excellent sky above the plateau. The OHP's coup: in 1995, astronomers Michel Mayor and Didier Queloz discovered the first confirmed exoplanet (51 Pegasi b) using the T193 telescope (1.93 m mirror) and its Élodie spectrograph — a discovery rewarded with the 2019 Nobel Prize in Physics. Today 13 domes are scattered through the oak forest. Guided tours of the main dome are organized regularly; tickets are sold online or at the Astronomy Center, not on site.

The village itself merits a tour: the Church of Saint-Michel dates from the 12th century, ramparts and a Romanesque tower (Tour de Porchères) recall its medieval past. Seven educational displays mark the 'Path of Stars' on the hill overlooking the town — a discovery trail about astronomy accessible to all and permanently open.

activities.text: The Astronomy Center (at the Moulin plateau, a few minutes from the village) hosts year-round activities for the public: telescope observation evenings, planetarium sessions (64 seats), solar observation. In summer, the Été Astro festival brings together more than 10,000 visitors. Book your seats in advance — observation evenings fill quickly in July-August.

The Observatory walk (6 km loop, 2h, easy) follows the oak forest around the scientific site and offers beautiful views of Montagne de Lure and the Valensole plateau. A country stroll between terraces, ruined mills, and country chapels.

days_plan.itin_title: Forcalquier & Verdon Gorges Road Trip

places.name: Saint Mihiel

visits.text: Saint-Étienne church preserves Ligier Richier's absolute masterpiece: the Sepulcher, composed of thirteen figures slightly larger than life, carved from Euville stone between 1554 and 1564. The dead Christ, carried by Nicodemus and Joseph of Arimathea, is surrounded by Mary Magdalene, the Virgin, and the Holy Women. Classified as a historic monument as early as 1840.

The Benedictine Library, on the second floor of the former abbatial palace, is considered one of France's five most beautiful libraries. Its two architecturally remarkable rooms contain 8,780 books, including 74 manuscripts from the 9th to 16th centuries and 86 incunabula. Some volumes still bear the scars of a 1915 shell.

Saint-Michel abbey church, founded in the 8th century, preserves a Romanesque tower-porch. Inside, Ligier Richier's Swoon of the Virgin - two life-size walnut wood figures - shows Mary fainting in Saint John's arms after Christ's death.

The Museum of Sacred Art, in the monks' former refectory, gathers religious objects, paintings, and sculptures from the 13th to 19th centuries deposited by departmental communes. The Saint Elisabeth attributed to Ligier Richier is an exceptionally refined piece.

Walk through Saint-Mihiel's Renaissance streets: the Gondrecourt mansion with its gargoyles representing a ram, dog, and lion; the Bousmard mansion (1520); Ligier Richier's house; the 16th-century Café des Arcades. An open-air museum of Lorraine architecture.

activities.text: Hike to the Seven Rocks, limestone cliffs over twenty meters high overlooking the Meuse valley. The 7-kilometer trail crosses forests and rock formations with panoramic viewpoints.
The Meuse Greenway follows the river: a bike and pedestrian path crossing several picturesque villages. Bike rental shops offer equipment suitable for the whole family.
Taste local specialties: croquets and rochers de Saint-Mihiel, traditional dry cookies that accompany Lorraine coffee.

days_plan.itin_title: Meuse Circuit: Verdun and the Citadels

map_keywords: Église Saint-Étienne Saint-Mihiel Sépulcre Ligier Richier

visits.text: Saint-Montan welcomes you with one of Ardèche's most remarkable stories: in the 1970s, the old medieval village was a field of ruins. Volunteers rebuilt it stone by stone over forty years—an estimated 20,000 people contributed to the effort, including scouts, students and volunteers from around the world. Wander the cobbled lanes climbing toward the castle: ochre facades catch the southern sun, and every corner reveals an ornate doorway or medieval vault. The free exhibition at the village entrance tells this extraordinary restoration story.

The Château de Saint-Montan sits atop Rocher de l'Agache, a limestone spur above the village. The original keep dates to the 12th century—written mention of a "De Saint-Montan" family appears as early as 1110. The castle expanded until the 14th century, reaching a total area of two hectares by the 15th century with an enclosure and three main gates. It was sacked in 1570 when Coligny's army of 3,000 men and 400 horses passed through, then abandoned until its modern resurrection. From the ramparts, views stretch across the Rhône valley and on clear days to Mont Ventoux. During high season, costumed medieval animations fill the courtyard—check dates before you go.

Head down to the church of San Samonta at the village base: it comprises a 10th-century chapel and a 12th-century Romanesque nave, joined into one discreet building. Local legend holds that the village owes its name to a 5th-century hermit, Montanus, who supposedly lived thirty years in a cave in the neighboring valley. A bit further along, the Curé Garden—formerly the scented garden—is freely accessible year-round, planted with aromatic herbs beside the stream.

activities.text: Take the trail to the Ermitage de la Sainte-Baume from the village, a loop of roughly 3 hours that crosses the gorges before rejoining Larnas. Warning: the trail is fairly steep and not recommended for inexperienced hikers. Along the route, you'll pass the Grotte de l'Ermite Montanus, carved into the cliff face, with an exceptional viewpoint over the entire village from above.

For a gentler approach, walk down to the valley floor and climb the opposite side to read the castle site as a whole—the silhouette of the castle perched on its rock and the village's layout on the southern slopes are particularly legible from this vantage point. Figure 2 km round trip.

days_plan.itin_title: The 18 Most Beautiful Villages of Ardèche

visits.text: Descend into the submarine Espadon moored in the fortified lock. This 77-meter vessel became the first French submarine to reach the North Pole in 1964, surfacing through the Arctic ice cap.

Inside the submarine base, Escal'Atlantic recreates the world of transatlantic ocean liners. Luxury cabins, engine rooms, and artifacts from the legendary Normandie bring the golden age of ocean travel back to life.
The Chantiers de l'Atlantique shipyards sprawl across 250 acres. A giant gantry crane lifts 1,400-ton blocks to assemble the world's largest cruise ships in a modern marvel of engineering.
Climb to the rooftop terrace of the fortified lock. The 360-degree view sweeps across the harbor, the Loire estuary, and the elegant bridge that spans the wide river.

activities.text: Head to the waterfront and its fine sandy beaches. A redeveloped promenade, colorful beach huts, and open views to the Atlantic horizon make for pleasant seaside wandering.
The Écomusée chronicles Saint-Nazaire's industrial and maritime history. Post-war reconstruction, shipbuilding heritage, and working-class life unfold through photographs and artifacts.

days_plan.itin_title: Loire-Atlantique Road Trip: Nantes and the Jade Coast

visits.text: Upon entering Saint-Nazaire-en-Royans, the aqueduct greets you—without warning. This structure, 235 meters long and 35 meters high with 17 arches, was built between 1873 and 1876 to irrigate the Valence plain. It was presented at the 1878 Paris Universal Exposition as a masterpiece of hydraulic engineering. A panoramic elevator beside the artificial lake formed in 1958 by the EDF dam lifts you to the pedestrian walkway above the Bourne. From up there, the view extends to the entrances of the Bourne gorges on the right and Combe Laval on the left. Allow 1 hour.

The medieval village below the aqueduct deserves a stroll: steep streets, old houses, and remnants of the 12th-century Dauphine tower recall that Saint-Nazaire was a Dauphin stronghold. During World War II, on June 29, 1944, the village was bombed by German aviation—it was a strategic crossroads between Valence, Grenoble, and the Vercors maquis. Some forty resistance fighters were executed in the castle park.

The Thaïs cave at the village entrance is a prehistoric cavity inhabited during the Magdalenian period. An hour-long guided tour reveals galleries rich in archaeology. Don't confuse it with Choranche cave: Thaïs is smaller but older in terms of human occupation.

activities.text: Local operators offer paddlewheel boat cruises on the Isère from the artificial lake's shores. The 1.5-hour outing circles the limestone cliffs of the Vercors foothills and travels upstream to Pont-en-Royans. A guide narrates the history of villages crossed and the birds nesting in the cliffs.

The hike to the Vanille viewpoint (3-hour round trip) climbs the western hillside and offers a 360° panorama of the aqueduct, village, and three breaches in the Vercors cliff face. Yellow and red trail markers from the lakeside.

days_plan.itin_title: Royans-Vercors Road Trip: Hilltop Villages and Gorges

visits.text: Perched on Mont Cornadore, Saint-Nectaire's Romanesque church ranks among Auvergne's five major examples. Its lava stone nave, topped by a dome and flanked by two facade towers, shelters 103 polychrome sculpted capitals of striking expressiveness. The treasury constitutes one of France's most remarkable Romanesque metalwork ensembles: the gilt copper and enameled reliquary bust of Saint Baudime, the Notre-Dame-du-Mont-Cornadore Virgin, and several reliquaries testify to medieval Auvergne's artistic radiance.

The Grottes du Cornadore reveal 1st-century Gallo-Roman baths carved into rock. The route recreates the Roman bathing ritual: from the tepidarium (warm room) to the caldarium (hot room), passing two perfectly preserved bathtubs from the original twenty. Millennial stalactites, an ancient pool, and hot mineralized CO2-rich water basins punctuate the guided visit. Six daily tour times from 10:30 AM to 5:30 PM, open year-round except December 25 and January 1.

The Maison du Fromage dedicates discovery space to Saint-Nectaire PDO, one of France's oldest cheeses. Traditional production, terroir history, and tasting explain why this unpasteurized pressed cheese, aged on rye straw, became the region's gastronomic emblem.

Saint-Nectaire-le-Bas spreads its villas and parks along more than forty thermal springs. The 19th-century spa development left architectural heritage still visible in former bathhouse facades and shaded walkways connecting the springs.

activities.text: Attend music concerts in Saint-Nectaire's Romanesque church, programmed year-round. The nave's exceptional acoustics magnify sonorities and offer a musical experience in an extraordinary heritage setting.

Join a guided tour of Saint-Nectaire's Romanesque church offered in summer. Guides detail the 103 capitals' iconography, decode biblical and secular carved scenes, and present the metalwork treasury's masterpieces.

Hike around Mont Cornadore with views over the Monts du Sancy and Couze Chambon valley. Several marked trails leave from the village and reveal volcanic landscapes, thermal springs, and high pastures.

days_plan.itin_title: VOLCANOES, ROMANESQUE STONE AND AUVERGNE TRADITIONS

visits.text: Notre-Dame Cathedral, built from the 12th to 16th centuries, is France's last Gothic masterpiece. Its 1558 astrolabe clock still tracks the heavens with precision.
The Sandelin Museum occupies an 18th-century mansion. Its fine arts collections and 750 ceramic pieces form one of France's most impressive assemblages.
The 20-hectare public garden spreads across former ramparts. French formal gardens, English landscape gardens, arboretum, cascades, and an open-air theater.
Saint-Bertin Abbey ruins testify to the town's medieval religious importance. Founded in the 7th century, it was dismantled after the Revolution.
The 18th-century town hall, nicknamed "the coffee grinder" for its cubic shape topped by a cupola, now houses an Italian-style theater.

activities.text: Board a bacôve for a journey through the Audomarois marshes. This flat-bottomed boat navigates 160 kilometers of UNESCO-listed watergangs (canals).
Visit the Marsh House to understand the exceptional biodiversity: 200 bird species including white storks, grey herons, and kingfishers.
Wander the medieval lanes of the historic center. Yellow brick and stone facades, elegant townhouses, and lively squares reveal Flemish architecture.

days_plan.itin_title: Pas-de-Calais Road Trip: Opal Coast & Historic Belfries

places.name: Saint Ours Les Roches

visits.text: Vulcania is Europe's volcanism theme park, inaugurated on February 20, 2002—a symbolic date deliberately chosen: 20-02-2002. Set on 57 hectares in the heart of the Chaîne des Puys, 15 km northwest of Clermont-Ferrand, it has welcomed over 8 million visitors since opening. The main building, largely buried beneath an earth mound, reveals only a monumental cone evoking the neighboring volcanoes—architecture by Hans Hollein. The science center covers three themes: volcanoes, natural phenomena (earthquakes, tsunamis), and Earth in Space. Plan a full day: visits easily exceed 6 hours.

A few minutes from Vulcania, the Lemptégy volcano offers a radically different experience: you literally descend into the guts of a volcano laid bare by an old pozzolana quarry. The guide leads you on foot or by small train among volcanic chimneys, lava flows, and volcanic bombs. It's geology at full scale—nothing like a theme park.

activities.text: At Vulcania, don't miss the Awakening of the Auvergne Giants—a dynamic experience projecting you into a volcanic eruption—and the Namazu roller coaster that lets you experience an earthquake. The planetarium, France's largest, offers shows on a hemispheric dome. In summer, nighttime animations and the light show on the park's façades are worth the evening.

days_plan.itin_title: Auvergne Volcanoes Road Trip: Puy de Dôme and Lakes

map_keywords: Maison de Site Puy de Dôme Orcines

places.name: Saint Paul De Vence

visits.text: Pass through the 14th-century Porte de Vence to enter the fortified village: one of the rampart cannons is the one captured during resistance against Charles V in 1536 — the Niçois came to help defend the village, and François I rewarded Saint-Paul with numerous privileges. Allow a morning to explore the village before tour buses invade Rue Grande.

Rue Grande traverses the fortified village from end to end over 300 meters: art galleries, artists' studios, beige limestone medieval facades. Picasso, Chagall and Prévert strolled here — Chagall lived his final years here and rests in the village cemetery, grave n°152 aisle C.

The 17th-century Chapelle des Pénitents Blancs was entirely decorated by Belgian artist Jean-Michel Folon between 1997 and 2008: floor mosaics, stained glass, murals and a monumental sculpture transform this space into a poetic world of winged figures and deep colors. A surprise in this medieval setting.

Climb to the 16th-century bastion for the panorama: the view of the Baous chain, Mont Chauve and the backcountry hills is one of the village's finest. Below, the cemetery where Chagall rests — a sober and touching visit.

activities.text: The Fondation Maeght is France's first private art foundation — inaugurated on July 28, 1964, by André Malraux — and one of Europe's most important collections with 13,000 works. Miró, Giacometti, Chagall, Braque and Calder participated in designing the building with Catalan architect Josep Lluís Sert: the Giacometti courtyard, Miró labyrinth and Chagall's wall mosaics are part of the architecture itself. Allow at least 2 hours.

Walk the Chemin de Ronde on the ramparts: the plunging view over village rooftops and the Niçoise countryside is what photographers seek. The path makes a complete circuit of the fortifications in 20 minutes.

Place du Général de Gaulle comes alive beneath century-old plane trees: pétanque games here are legendary — Montand, Ventura and Lino regularly faced off in the 1960s-70s. The tradition continues with local players from morning to evening.

Continue to Tourrettes-sur-Loup, the "violet village" 15 minutes away: cobbled lanes wind between medieval stone houses, potter and weaver artisans have taken up residence in the town's arcades. The Fête des Violettes has been held every March since 1952 — off-season, the village is peaceful and authentic.

Art galleries line every Saint-Paul lane: paintings, sculptures, ceramics in spaces that were sometimes the masters' studios. The spirit of 20th-century artists still hovers — even if commerce has taken over, some galleries present high-quality works.

days_plan.itin_title: Alpes-Maritimes Road Trip: From the French Riviera to Perched Villages
The 15 Jewels of the French Riviera and Its Hinterland
Around Nice Circuit: Coast and Hilltop Villages
Nice Hinterland Circuit: Perched Villages and Mountain Trails

map_keywords: Chapelle des Pénitents Blancs Folon Saint-Paul-de-Vence

visits.text: The Chartreuse de Valbonne is one of this circuit's most unusual curiosities — nestled at the bottom of a wooded valley, you reach it via a winding road through 1,500 hectares of state forest sheltering rare species for the Mediterranean region. Founded in 1204 when Guillem de Vénéjean, Bishop of Uzès, ceded to the Carthusian order a then-marshy domain, the monks cleared and drained the site, giving it the name 'vallis bona' — the good valley, origin of Valbonne. Pillaged in 1585 during the Wars of Religion, rebuilt from 1593, expropriated during the Revolution, bought back by the Carthusians in 1836, then transformed into a leprosarium from 1929 by Pastor Philadelphe Delord — who welcomed up to 400 patients and died on site in 1947 — its history spans the entire modern era.

The Chartreuse's architecture is immediately recognizable by its Burgundian-style glazed tiles covering the towers and bell tower of the conventual church — a surprising detail for a Provençal charterhouse. The great cloister measures 355 meters in perimeter; around it, the monastic cells bear letters from A to Y (Q is not used). The visit provides access to the conventual church with its vaults of complex stereotomy, work of the Franque brothers from Avignon, as well as the small cloister dating from the early 13th century. The Chartreuse has been classified as a Historic Monument since 1959. Check visiting hours before departure — the site is open from April to early October.

The Chartreuse vineyard produces organic Côtes du Rhône — a tasting is offered on site. Around the buildings, the Valbonne forest offers shaded trails with pubescent oaks, Scots pines and strawberry trees mixed with Mediterranean species.

activities.text: The Chartreuse de Valbonne forest trail (5 to 7 km loop, moderate level, ~2h) crosses the state forest around the monastery and offers a striking contrast between the silence of the woods and monastic architecture. You encounter old dry stone walls, capitelles (shepherds' shelters) and more open garrigue areas with views over the reliefs.

days_plan.itin_title: Cèze Valley and Ardèche Gorges Circuit

visits.text: Saint-Pierre-de-Mézoargues is a discreet Rhône-side commune — a few hundred inhabitants, orchards as far as the eye can see, and the Rhône dike as horizon. It's one of those villages existing only in regional literature and local memories. Its 12th-century church (remodeled) and 17th-18th century farms witness an ancient settlement on this fertile alluvial land that Saint-Victor monks began draining in the 10th century. The spirit of "deep Provence" is total here.

The village's rural fabric deserves an hour's walk: 17th-18th century closed-courtyard farms, stone irrigation canals, old wells, and oratories at crossroads. Careful observation reveals architectural and agricultural details typical of Rhône-side Provence — the same sober architecture as in Camargue villages.

activities.text: Rural walking loop among hedgerows and orchards: rural roads and paths surrounding Saint-Pierre-de-Mézoargues cross a mosaic of cherry, apricot, plum trees, and market garden plots. The terrain is completely flat, ideal for a 2-hour walk without difficulty.

Observation from the Rhône dike: the several-kilometer-long dike offers direct views of the Grand Rhône, its pebble banks, and riparian vegetation (black poplars, white willows, ash trees). A spot of absolute calm for observing river birds — grey herons, great cormorants, kingfishers, and in autumn, migrating waders.

days_plan.itin_title: Alpilles, Aix-en-Provence & Camargue Circuit

visits.text: The Saint-Pourçain-sur-Sioule vineyard extends over 640 hectares producing AOC wines since 2009. The terroir cultivates unique Tressallier grape, rare white variety found almost exclusively here. Winegrowers perpetuate ancestral tradition dating back to Romans and Middle Ages when Saint-Pourçain wines supplied royal court and popes.

The Vineyard and Wine Museum occupies a renovated old building presenting viticulture history and winemaking techniques. Collections display traditional tools, historical documents and bottles tracing wine evolution over centuries. Educational tours explain Tressallier grape specificity and local terroir characteristics.

The Sainte-Croix church (12th-13th centuries) dominates city with its massive bell tower. Romanesque architecture displays carved capitals and sculpted portal characteristic of period. Interior shelters remarkable stained glass windows and liturgical objects testifying to religious heritage richness.

Old Saint-Pourçain reveals paved streets lined with stone houses (15th-18th centuries) and winegrower residences. The historic center preserves medieval character with narrow streets, small squares and ancient fountains. Stroll discovering facades and details testifying to wine town past prosperity.

activities.text: Wine cellar visits and tastings allow discovering Saint-Pourçain AOC in authentic setting. Winegrowers present their cuvées (whites, rosés, reds) including rare Tressallier expressing limestone terroir. Direct sales and explanations on vineyard work enrich the oenological experience.

Walk along Sioule discovering old mills and wash houses lining the river. Green banks invite peaceful strolls in nature. Footpaths allow exploring valley where water carved picturesque path through limestone.

days_plan.itin_title: Allier Road Trip: Vichy, Thermal Baths and Bourbonnais Bocage

visits.text: The Saint-Quentin Basilica soars with its 83-meter spire. This Gothic masterpiece from the 12th-15th centuries shelters relics of Saint Quentin. The interior dazzles with its luminosity and extraordinary dimensions with a 34-meter high nave. Stained glass windows tell the saint's martyrdom.

Explore the largest Art Deco heritage in France along the architectural circuit. Facades rebuilt after World War I display geometric motifs, bow windows and sculptural decorations characteristic of the 1920s-1930s. The former Manufacture building, town hall and theater illustrate this artistic wealth.

The Antoine-Lécuyer Museum preserves the world's largest collection of pastels by Maurice-Quentin de La Tour. The 18th-century portraitist immortalized personalities of his time in intimate works of stunning realism. The museum also displays Flemish and French paintings from the 15th to 18th centuries.

The Marais d'Isle natural reserve extends over 120 hectares in the heart of the city. Peat bogs, ponds and humid meadows shelter remarkable fauna and flora. A nature trail with interpretation panels runs through this preserved wetland.

activities.text: Walk along the canal and Somme banks. The towpath converted into a promenade invites peaceful strolls through greenery. Pleasure boats and fishing spots enliven this waterfront.

Discover the underground city sheltering 45 kilometers of medieval quarries and wartime refuges. Guided tours reveal this mysterious underground world beneath Saint-Quentin streets. Chalk walls bear witness to centuries of extraction and wartime hiding.

Visit the butterfly conservatory sheltering over a thousand tropical butterflies flying freely in a greenhouse recreating their natural habitat. Exotic plants and warm climate transport visitors to distant lands.

days_plan.itin_title: Aisne Circuit: Gothic Cathedrals and Picardy Nature

visits.text: The priory church (1722) surprises with its three onion-domed bell towers that draw a baroque silhouette in the hollow of the Vosges hills. Inside, the Silbermann organ of 1746, a listed Historic Monument, is the only authentic survivor of Jean-André Silbermann's work.

The Haute Chapelle, a 12th-century Romanesque construction, overlooks the village from a wooded relief. According to legend, the mule transporting Saint Quirin's relics from Rome stopped here in 1049 and refused to go further.
Walk around the priory to find the miraculous fountain below. For centuries, it has been attributed with healing powers for skin diseases.
The archaeological site of Croix Guillaume, accessible by a marked forest trail, reveals a Gallo-Roman hamlet: traces of dwellings, necropolis, sandstone quarries and cult remains.

In Lettenbach, the Verriers chapel (1756), rococo style, sports an onion-domed bell tower and folk art stained glass. The monks had established a glassworks here in the 15th century that became a Royal Manufacture under Louis XV.

activities.text: Take the Club Vosgien marked trails around ponds and deciduous forests. The village counts 75 listed or registered buildings, from Lorraine farms to wayside crosses.
Seven churches and chapels scattered around the village form the "7 Roses" circuit. Each tells its story, between Christianized Celtic sanctuaries and forest hermitages.
In summer, the Silbermann Musical Days offer organ recitals in the priory church: a rare moment in an exceptional setting.

days_plan.itin_title: Grand Est Road Trip: Vineyards, Ramparts and Forests

visits.text: Begin at Château de la Napoule in Mandelieu, on the seafront facing the Bay of Cannes. The original medieval fortress dates to 1387. In 1917, an eccentric American sculptor—heir to a Wall Street baron—bought the ruins with his wife. Together they rebuilt the castle in their own image: porphyry columns, chimeras on the capitals, exotic animals roaming freely in the garden. The inscription at the gate sums it all up: "Once upon a time." The 4-hectare gardens created by Marie Clews are labeled "Remarkable Garden."

The Corniche d'Or between Saint-Raphaël and Mandelieu is one of France's most spectacular coastal roads. It was built between 1898 and 1903 at the initiative of the Touring Club de France—27 kilometers carved into red porphyry for 500,000 francs of the era. Its red rocks owe their color to rhyolite, a volcanic stone born 250 million years ago when Esterel was nothing but an immense volcano. At each turn, a different cove opens onto turquoise sea. Essential stops: the Agay roadstead, Anthéor, Pointe du Cap Roux.

Île d'Or at Le Dramont, visible from the beach 300 meters offshore, is a porphyry rock topped by a medieval-looking square tower. It supposedly inspired the skull in Tintin's "The Black Island." Across from it, the Sémaphore du Dramont offers panoramas over the "Lion de Mer" and "Lion de Terre," two porphyry rocks guarding the entrance to the Gulf of Fréjus.

Saint-Raphaël became a seaside resort in 1864 thanks to the railroad. The town has hosted Picasso, Apollinaire, and Saint-Exupéry. Its old port and Notre-Dame de la Victoire basilica (11th century, remodeled in the 12th) merit a late-afternoon tour, before following the coastal path from Santa Lucia to Calanque du Fournas.

activities.text: Hike to Cap Roux (453 m) from Anthéor: the summit offers 360° views over the entire massif and coastline, from the Maures to the Alps. Halfway up, the Grotte de Saint-Honorat (or Sainte-Baume) supposedly served as the saint's refuge in the 4th century, before he founded the monastery of the Lérins Islands. Ascent about 1h30 from Col de l'Évêque. Hiking shoes essential.

Sea kayaking from Agay Bay: the Agay roadstead is the most sheltered in Esterel, dominated by the red Rastel (288 m). Outfitters offer rentals and guided outings along the coves between Agay and Anthéor—clear waters, coves inaccessible otherwise. Available April through October.

Snorkeling at Calanque du Fournas (Saint-Raphaël): a discreet cove accessible from the coastal trail, whose depths teem with posidonia and fish. The Cap Roux area is classified as a natural marine reserve—biodiversity is remarkable. Avoid the anti-pollution buoys in summer marking protected zones.

days_plan.itin_title: Var Road Trip: French Riviera & Green Provence
French Riviera & Esterel Circuit: Hidden Coves and Hilltop Villages

places.name: Saint Remy De Provence

visits.text: At Saint-Rémy's southern edge, before even entering Glanum's ticketed site, you come face to face with Les Antiques: the triumphal arch (8 m high, dated between 20 and 10 BC) and the Mausoleum of the Julii (17 m high, circa 30-20 BC) rise beside the road, visible free at any hour. These were the first monuments classified in France, on Prosper Mérimée's 1840 list. The mausoleum — actually a cenotaph erected by the Julii family to commemorate their father and grandfather — is unique in all known Roman architecture: a sculpted base bearing a double-bay arch, surmounted by a domed circular temple.

Glanum's archaeological site occupies a narrow valley between the white ridges of the Alpilles. Founded in the 3rd century BC by a Celto-Ligurian tribe around a sacred spring dedicated to the healing god Glanis, Glanum was successively Gallic, Hellenistic then Roman before being sacked by Germanic invaders around 270 AD and permanently abandoned. Its inhabitants moved a kilometer north, founding what would become Saint-Rémy. Excavations began in 1921 and have revealed twin temples, baths, forum, basilica and dwellings. Viewing platforms set on the heights let you grasp the city's organization from the ridges.

A few hundred meters from Glanum, the Saint-Paul-de-Mausole monastery deserves a visit. Vincent Van Gogh stayed here for a year of his own volition, from May 1889 to May 1890, after the episode when he cut off his earlobe in Arles. He painted over 150 canvases here, including The Starry Night, Irises and several versions of Wheatfield with Crows. His room has been recreated and a wing of the Romanesque cloister houses a museum tracing his stay and the evolution of psychiatry.

Finish in Saint-Rémy's historic center, with its Provençal alleyways and fountains against colorful facades. Nostradamus was born here in 1503 — a fountain bearing his name marks the approximate location of his birthplace. Wednesday morning's market is one of the liveliest in the region: flowers, Provençal herbs, olives and local cheeses.

activities.text: For an overview of the Alpilles and Glanum site, take the marked trail climbing from Les Antiques parking toward the ridges. On clear days, the Alpilles chain and the Crau plain unfold 360 degrees. Allow 1h30 round trip, with good walking shoes.

days_plan.itin_title: Provence, France: Lavender Fields, Hilltop Villages and Riviera Towns
Provence, France: Lavender Fields, Hilltop Villages and Riviera Towns
Alpilles, Aix-en-Provence & Camargue Circuit
Roman Provence Road Trip: Ancient Monuments and Timeless Villages

map_keywords: Les Antiques mausolée arc de triomphe Saint-Rémy
Saint-Paul de Mausole Saint-Rémy Van Gogh

visits.text: Saint-Saturnin perches on a basalt flow overlooking the Monne gorges, 15 km south of Clermont-Ferrand. Its monumental duo seen from the road — Romanesque church and medieval castle in golden arkose and Volvic lava — is one of Auvergne's most photographed sights. Notre-Dame church is one of five so-called 'major' Romanesque churches in Auvergne, alongside those of Issoire, Orcival, Clermont, and Saint-Nectaire — listed as a Historic Monument in 1862. Built in the 12th century, its octagonal bell tower is one of the best preserved in Auvergne, and its capitals are carved from local volcanic lava. Allow 45 minutes for the interior visit in season (guided tours in July-August).

Right next to the church, the Royal Château de Saint-Saturnin was built in the 13th century by the La Tour d'Auvergne family, then embellished in the Renaissance by Catherine de' Medici — Countess of Auvergne — who added a formal French garden. In 1566, King Charles IX and his entire court stopped here. In 1586, Queen Margot passed through before her exile in Usson. The visit (about 50 minutes, guided) grants access to the keep, wall-walk, and furnished rooms.

Walk along Rue de la Boucherie with its medieval shop and houses from the 15th–17th centuries, then reach the Renaissance fountain from the 16th century between the church and castle — its decoration bears the La Tour d'Auvergne coat of arms. The 12th-century Romanesque Sainte-Madeleine chapel adjoins the former cemetery transformed into a garden. On the lintel of the door, read the 1668 inscription: 'We have been like you, one day you will be like us, Think well on it.' Rarely has an invitation to contemplation been so direct.

activities.text: The washhouses walk descends to the Monne from the Renaissance fountain, following the flank of the basalt flow. On the south-facing slopes (the Serre mountain), old vineyard terraces and dry-stone huts recall that Puy-de-Dôme was France's second-largest wine-producing department in the 19th century. If you search carefully in the walls, you'll find fossilized stromatolite impressions 25 million years old.

Saint-Saturnin is 15 km from Clermont-Ferrand and makes a fine last stop before the city. The Saint-Jacques de Compostela path Via Arvena meets the Via Podiensis here — several waymarked loops explore the Monne valley and its steep gorges, accessible on foot or mountain bike from the village.

days_plan.itin_title: Auvergne's Volcanic Villages Trail

visits.text: Saint-Saturnin-lès-Apt is a medieval town that underwent three successive expansions in the 13th, 15th, and 17th centuries, each leaving its traces in the urban fabric. As you climb the cobbled lanes toward the château, you'll cross the remains of several fortified enclosures. The Portail Eyguier, a 15th-century corner tower, is the sole survivor of the second enclosure. From the summit ramparts, the panorama embraces the Alpilles, the Vaucluse Mountains, and both Luberon massifs—a 180° view over all of Provence. In the village center lanes, look for the carved doors and window frames classified as Historic Monuments, as well as the 18th-century public fountain whose sculpted pediment was a gift from the last Marquis de Monclar—guillotined in 1794.

The Castle Chapel, consecrated in 1056, is perched atop the original rock on the remains of a 6th-century early Christian church. Accessible on request at the town hall, it's worth the detour. In 1850, a villager named Rosette Tamisier reportedly saw a painting bleed here—the affair caused quite a stir before she was imprisoned for fraud. Two 17th-century windmills dominate the hill, one of which still has its sails—a rarity in Provence.

activities.text: Climb to the castle plateau via the cobbled lanes (20 min round trip). View from the orientation table over the Apt basin and the ochres of Roussillon on the horizon.

Trail of the Aiguiers—a discovery loop around the cisterns carved into the limestone rock to collect rainwater. Allow 1h30 for a short loop. A rare hydraulic heritage, well explained by the Luberon Park panels.

days_plan.itin_title: The 15 Most Beautiful Villages of the Luberon

visits.text: Colette's birthplace, in the village heart, has been restored according to the writer's own descriptions. Wallpaper, parquet, woodwork, and vegetable garden recreate the setting of her childhood in Puisaye.

The Musée Colette occupies the château rebuilt in the 17th century. It offers a sensory discovery of the writer's life: manuscripts, photographs, personal objects, and immersive artistic installations.

The Tour Sarrasine, an ovoid 11th–12th century keep, is the last vestige of the old medieval fortress. Colette evokes it in 'Claudine at School': 'the Saracen tower, thick, low, sheathed entirely in ivy, crumbling at the top.'
The village church, recognizable by its absent bell tower, stands on the main square. Dating from the 13th century, it's part of the 'Following Gabrielle's Footsteps' trail through the village.

On the road to Moutiers, the Poterie de la Bâtisse perpetuates ten generations of know-how. The old 18th-century reclining kiln and 1930 workshop are still in operation. Colette's pencil pot is among the iconic creations.

activities.text: Take the 'Following Gabrielle's Footsteps' walk through the village. The one-hour route passes places evoked in Colette's work, from the Petit Saint-Jean washhouse to woods that 'ripple to infinity.'
Puisaye has been the land of potters since the Middle Ages. Visit the open workshops of Saint-Amand-en-Puisaye and discover the region's characteristic blue stoneware.

The Puisaye countryside—a bocage of ponds and forests—lends itself to peaceful hikes. Sunken lanes bordered by hedges lead from village to village through a landscape Colette described as 'the most beautiful country in the world.'

days_plan.itin_title: Yonne Circuit: Northern Burgundy & Chablis Wine Country

visits.text: Discovery of the medieval village of Saint-Savin and its Benedictine abbey overlooking the valley.
Walk in the Val d'Azun and observation of preserved landscapes.

activities.text: Sensory experience at the Jardins des Bains: Babylon hammams, Emerald Forest and Mesopotamian Ziggurat.

days_plan.itin_title: The Pyrenees Spa Trail: Thermal Baths from Ax-les-Thermes to Bagnères-de-Luchon
Vienne Road Trip: Futuroscope and Romanesque Heritage

places.name: Saint Suliac

visits.text: Saint-Suliac has been classified as one of France's Most Beautiful Villages since 1999. The narrow lanes, so tight that two people cannot pass side by side, wind between granite houses with arched doorways and thatched roofs. Fishing nets still hang from the facades.
The 6th-century church, founded by the monk Suliau, houses stained glass windows, wooden sculptures, and maritime ex-votos left by the families of Newfoundland fishermen.
Walk down the Ruette des Petits Puits - a 17th-century well gives the lane its name. Legend says it concealed an underground passage connecting the church to the harbor.
At the harbor, dories - flat-bottomed boats of the Newfoundland fishermen - sway facing the Rance River. Sailors from Saint-Suliac departed each year to fish for cod off the coast of Newfoundland.
The Beauchet tidal mill, listed as a Historic Monument, used tidal energy to grind grain. Rebuilt in stone after a fire, it operated until 1980.

At low tide, stone foundations emerge in the Vigneux cove. Listed as a Historic Monument since 1986, this site is believed to be an ancient fortified Viking camp where longships sheltered on the landward side.

activities.text: Climb to the summit of Mont Garrot (73 m) for a 360° panorama over the Rance estuary, the harbor, and the Viking remains below.

Reach the Grainfollet oratory via the coastal path. In 1874, fishermen promised to build this sanctuary if all returned safely from Newfoundland. Twenty years later, the vow was fulfilled. At sunset, the view over the Rance is sublime.
The Saint-Suliac peninsula loop follows the old salt marshes, Beauchet mill, and the Chablé menhir (5 m high, nicknamed 'Gargantua's tooth'). Warning: some passages are submerged at high tide.

days_plan.itin_title: Brittany Road Trip: Historic Villages, Sea and Heritage

map_keywords: Moulin à marée de Beauchet Saint-Suliac

visits.text: Saint-Symphorien-sur-Coise is a character town in the Lyonnais Mountains that has preserved a remarkably intact historic heart: Gothic church, half-timbered houses, old market halls, and sections of ramparts. Take time to get lost in the lanes: the façades tell several centuries of prosperity linked to trade between Lyon and the Massif Central. Don't miss the climb to the panorama over the Lyonnais Mountains — on clear days, the view extends far toward the Pilat.

The ramparts and their fortified gates remain partially visible. The town's position, on a viewpoint over the Coise valley, illustrates the medieval defensive logic characteristic of this passage region between the Rhône plains and the Massif Central plateaus.

activities.text: Intramural heritage loop (2–3 km, ~1 hour). The circuit passes by the main fortified gates, the market halls, and the lanes connecting the different levels of the town.
Ridge path to a high point for landscape reading of the Lyonnais Mountains. This massif, little known to tourists, offers preserved nature less than an hour from Lyon.

days_plan.itin_title: Loop Around Lyon: Villages and Golden Stones

visits.text: Île d'Oléron, the largest French Atlantic island (30 km long). The free viaduct (1966, 3 km) links it to the mainland. Varied landscapes: pine forests in the south, salt marshes in the north, fine sand beaches on the west coast.

The Chassiron lighthouse, at the northern tip of the island, has worn its black and white stripes since 1836. 224 steps to the lantern – views of Fort Boyard and Île d'Aix on clear days. The spiral garden at the lighthouse's base echoes oyster shell patterns.

Le Château d'Oléron, former stronghold at the island's entrance. The Vauban citadel guarded the estuary. Today, its ramparts house an artisan and local produce market (summer). The colorful port cabins have become artists' studios.

Fort Boyard, oval fortress built between 1801 and 1857 between Oléron and Île d'Aix. Never used militarily, it became famous through the TV game show. No visits inside, but cruises circle it – an iconic silhouette set on the ocean.

activities.text: Cruise around Fort Boyard. Departures from Boyardville or Saint-Denis-d'Oléron. Full circuit of the fort, passing near Île d'Aix. Commentary on construction history and filming anecdotes.
