Oyster tasting at La Cotinière port, the leading artisanal fishing port in Charente-Maritime and France's sixth largest. Auction in the morning, tasting shacks in the afternoon. Oléron's 'fines de claire' and 'spéciales' oysters have an incomparable briny flavor.

Île d'Oléron beaches: Grande Plage at Saint-Trojan, Les Saumonards at Boyardville, Vert-Bois in the south. Maritime pine forests stretch to the sand. The little Saint-Trojan train crosses the forest to Gatseau beach.

days_plan.itin_title: Charente-Maritime Road Trip: La Rochelle, Île de Ré, Fort Boyard and Roman Saintonge

visits.text: The Musée de l'Annonciade, housed in a 16th-century chapel, holds a world-class collection: Pointillist, Nabis, and Fauvist paintings. Signac, Matisse, Derain, and Marquet are all represented—a reminder that Saint-Tropez was a major center of avant-garde painting.

The Citadel, erected in 1602 under Henri IV, has dominated the harbor for four centuries. The hexagonal keep, a historic monument since 1921, houses the Museum of Maritime History. Models, artifacts, and immersive scenography recount the Tropezian sailors—from whaling to trade with the Ottoman Empire.

Place des Lices, shaded by century-old plane trees, has been the village's beating heart since the 19th century. On Tuesdays and Saturdays, the Provençal market takes over the square. The rest of the time, pétanque games animate the café terraces.

Notre-Dame de l'Assomption church, with its characteristic ochre and pink bell tower, has watched over the village since the 16th century. Inside, the bust of Saint Torpès recalls the founding legend of the town.

From the Citadel ramparts, about thirty peacocks roam freely. The plunging view over Tropezian rooftops, the naval cemetery, and the gulf composes one of the coast's most beautiful panoramas—peacocks included.

activities.text: Follow the coastal path from Graniers beach. This seaside trail hugs the coves and headlands, offering panoramas between pine groves and turquoise waters. Swimming is possible in the wild coves—nature's own infinity pools.
The old harbor invites aimless wandering: fishing boats mingle with yachts, and colorful facades reflect in the water. Head to Quai Jean-Jaurès for the permanent spectacle of maritime life.

Push on to Ramatuelle, a medieval village perched on Paillas hill overlooking Pampelonne Bay. Its spiral lanes and ramparts offer a tranquil counterpoint to Tropezian bustle—old Provence above the glitz.

days_plan.itin_title: Var Road Trip: French Riviera & Green Provence

visits.text: Saint-Vaast-la-Hougue, voted France's Favorite Village in 2019, juts into the sea like a ship's prow. Its Vauban quay runs alongside an active fishing port—third-largest in the Manche department—where trawlers unload their daily catch.

Walk the causeway from the Sailors' Chapel—the choir of the original 11th-century church dedicated to Those Lost at Sea—to La Hougue Fort. The 66-foot Vauban tower, a UNESCO World Heritage site since 2008, commands the bay from its granite promontory 130 feet above sea level.

Board the amphibious boat to reach Tatihou Island, 70 acres of protected wilderness. Its twin Vauban tower—69 feet tall, 66 feet in diameter—once crossed fire with La Hougue's to seal the bay after the naval defeat of 1692.

Tatihou's Maritime Museum houses archaeological collections from shipwrecks of the royal fleet sunk during the 1692 Battle of La Hougue. Everyday objects, cannons, and wreckage tell the story of this defeat at the hands of the Anglo-Dutch fleet.

Tatihou's gardens shelter remarkable flora protected within granite dikes and walls. Palm trees from the Val de Saire, exotic plants, and wildflowers share space with a bird sanctuary hosting over a hundred nesting species.

activities.text: Walk to Tatihou at low tide, following the submersible path through oyster beds. At high tide, the amphibious boat hoists itself onto wheels for the return journey on land—a uniquely Norman mode of transport.

Discover the oyster beds between Saint-Vaast and Tatihou in the Cul de Loup inlet. Saint-Vaast oysters, renowned for their hazelnut taste, can be sampled right at the source from local oyster farmers along the port.
In summer, the Traversées de Tatihou festival brings maritime music concerts to the island. Experience the unique atmosphere of an island festival accessible only by sea.

days_plan.itin_title: Manche Road Trip: Cotentin Peninsula & Mont-Saint-Michel

visits.text: Saint-Valery-sur-Somme: medieval city, sailors' quarter (Courgain), panorama over the bay.

activities.text: Seal watching with guide & walk on the salt marshes.

days_plan.itin_title: A Road Trip Through the Somme Valley: Cathedrals, History & River Landscapes
Hauts-de-France: Opal Coast Cliffs, Medieval History & Great War Sites

visits.text: Border station.
Fort of Janus.
Clarée Valley.

Welcome to 6,699 feet above sea level - the highest inhabited commune in Europe. Saint-Véran stretches for a kilometer along the mountainside, divided into five distinct quarters separated by firebreaks after the devastating blaze of 1526. The traditional chalets, built in fuste style (larch wood on stone foundations), served triple duty as dwelling, stable, and hayloft. Look up: some thirty painted sundials adorn the facades, several signed by Giovanni Francesco Zarbula, a renowned 19th-century sundial maker from Piedmont.

Step inside the 17th-century church, built atop the foundations of an earlier structure. Outside, notice the sculpted lions clutching children in their paws - a rare motif in Alpine religious architecture. On the wall, the sundial bears the village motto: 'Lou plus aouto coumunoutas inte se mangeu lou pan de Diòu' - the highest commune where one eats the bread of God.

The Musée Le Soum occupies the village's oldest house, dated 1641. You'll discover how Queyras families lived: box beds positioned near livestock for warmth, carved wooden tools, and the everyday objects of harsh mountain life at 6,500 feet. It's compact but richly detailed.

activities.text: Hiking.
Discovery of local crafts.

Wander from quarter to quarter through the village - each section had its own bread oven, larch fountain, and mission cross. Stop at the fountains: the round section served as a trough for animals, the rectangular one as a wash house. In summer, the artisan market offers sheep's cheese, mountain honey, and herbal teas.

If you're a confident hiker, climb toward the Clausis chapel (1.5 hours one way). The trail crosses alpine meadows where you'll likely spot marmots and possibly chamois. Near the chapel, remnants of the ancient copper mine remind us the site was worked as far back as the late Neolithic period.

days_plan.itin_title: Hautes-Alpes Majesty: High Mountain Passes, Ski Resorts & Alpine Valleys
France's Most Beautiful Villages: Alps to Luberon in 7 Days

visits.text: The Château de Bonneval in Coussac-Bonneval has stood in its present form since the 14th century—though a fortress already occupied this site in 930, as evidenced by a stone embedded in the masonry of the entrance tower. The Bonneval family has owned it continuously since 1055, making it one of the longest-held castles by the same lineage in France. The guided tour takes you through ten furnished rooms and two galleries decorated in Louis XIII and Louis XVI style, complete with Aubusson and Fontainebleau tapestries. Don't miss the chamber of Count Claude-Alexandre, nicknamed "the Three-Tailed Pasha" for his 18th-century adventures in the Orient. Important note: the castle only opens in the afternoon, from May to September, and guided tours are mandatory—plan to start your day with Coussac so you arrive right at opening time.

Stop at Place Attane: the collegiate church of Saint-Yrieix commands attention immediately with its 12th-century Romanesque façade, built by the canons thanks to the generosity of the Dukes of Aquitaine. Inside, the carved capitals are worth examining, but don't leave without asking to see the 12th-century Bible preserved in the municipal library—376 vellum sheets covered in illuminations, classified as a historic monument and considered the finest Romanesque manuscript in the Limousin. It weighs 23 kilograms. An extraordinary object that few visitors know about.

Right next to the collegiate church stands the Tour du Plô, the oldest civil monument in town. Standing 24 meters tall, constructed in schist slabs in the early 13th century by the Viscount of Limoges to assert his authority against the canons, it served successively as a prison (ground floor, windowless), a vicomtal residence on the third floor, and a lookout post at the summit. Restored and reopened to the public in 2021 after years of closure, its terrace offers an unobstructed view over the medieval rooftops and surrounding countryside. Make the climb—it's the best viewpoint in town.

activities.text: Wander through the village of Coussac-Bonneval before your castle tour: the Place aux Foires and the lanes around the castle give you a vivid sense of the medieval settlement. The Church of Saint-Saturnin, just steps away, houses the tombs of Bonneval family members—a natural extension of your castle visit.

Saint-Yrieix-la-Perche is classified as one of France's "Most Beautiful Detours" and a stroll through its medieval lanes earns that distinction. The town is also the birthplace of kaolin: it was here that Jean-Baptiste Darnet discovered this white clay in 1768, essential for manufacturing Limoges porcelain. The archaeological site at Marcognac, on the edge of town, helps you understand this industrial adventure. If you're there in the morning, the weekly market sells local specialties: Bijou madeleines, cul-noir pork, cheeses from the Limousin countryside.

days_plan.itin_title: Haute-Vienne Road Trip: Medieval Castles & the Périgord-Limousin

visits.text: Sainte-Agnès claims the title of Europe's highest coastal village at 800 meters altitude. Classified among France's 'Most Beautiful Villages,' its pebble-paved lanes climb up to the ruins of the feudal castle—erected by the Counts of Ventimiglia then demolished on Louis XIV's orders. From the summit, the view stretches from Cap Martin to the Italian Riviera. Legend attributes the village's foundation to a Roman princess named Agnes who, rescued from a storm, had a chapel built as a sign of gratitude.

Don't leave without visiting the Maginot Line fortification at Sainte-Agnès, the southernmost bastion of the Maginot Line. Built between 1932 and 1938—irony of history: by an Italian company—it could accommodate 350 men in total self-sufficiency for three months with electricity, central heating, a hospital, and an interior railway network. Its artillery fired 1,201 shells against Italian troops in June 1940, repelling the invader for five days. Book your guided tour by text message 48 hours in advance.

activities.text: Walk on the ridge trail from the Saint-Sébastien chapel (17th century): several marked circuits lead to Mont Ours (1,239 m) or Pic du Baudon (1,264 m). Moderate level, but the panoramas over the coast and the Mercantour reward the effort.
Village square: taste local produce (ravioli with daube, honey products) on the central square. The village's glassmakers and jewelers display their creations in the surrounding lanes.

days_plan.itin_title: The 15 Jewels of the French Riviera and Its Hinterland

visits.text: Sainte-Croix-du-Verdon perches on a rocky promontory facing the lake at the mouth of the gorges. The village nearly disappeared: the dam's original plans called for flooding it, before the water level was lowered. The stone Provençal houses, flower-filled squares, and tight alleyways retain the character of Haute-Provence's hilltop villages. From the main overlook, the 180° panorama across the lake, the dam to the west, and the Verdon ridges to the east is one of the broadest on the entire circuit.

The 16th-century parish church on the central square is open for visits. The museum and conservatory of Popular Arts and Traditions of Sainte-Croix-du-Verdon traces pre-dam Provençal peasant life — agricultural tools, costumes, everyday objects — and gives a sense of what the valley was like when it lived off lavender, truffles, and honey. It's a moving complement to the visit to Les Salles-sur-Verdon.

Head down to the dam to see up close the structure that changed everything. The double-arch vault, 95 meters high and built with only 55,000 cubic meters of concrete to hold back 760 million cubic meters of water, makes it one of Europe's most 'efficient' dams in terms of concrete-to-water ratio. Though closed to direct visits for security reasons, you can admire it from the D111 bridge spanning the lake just downstream.

activities.text: The Sainte-Croix-du-Verdon water sports center offers windsurfing, kayaking, pedal boats, and sailing on the turquoise waters of the lake. It's one of the best sailing spots on the lake thanks to the wind that regularly rises from the gorges in the afternoon. Providers offer introductory lessons and hourly or half-day rentals.

Hiking the hills overlooking the lake from Sainte-Croix-du-Verdon offers different views at every hour — in the morning, the lake reflects the cliffs in absolute calm; at sunset, the water takes on orange and pink hues. Allow 1.5 to 2 hours for the loop from the village.

Tuesday mornings, a local producers' market sets up on the church square. Honey from the Alpes de Haute-Provence, dried herbs, goat cheeses, local olive oils. A final chance to leave with the flavors of the Verdon before hitting the road.

days_plan.itin_title: Lake Sainte-Croix and Verdon Circuit

visits.text: Here is one of the most improbable sites in France: a medieval charterhouse entirely transformed into a village at the Revolution. Founded in 1281 by Béatrix de la Tour du Pin, the charterhouse of Sainte-Croix-en-Jarez welcomed Carthusian monks for five centuries — prayer, solitude, and silence — before being sold as national property in 1792. Since then, inhabitants have literally lived in the cells, cloisters, and monastic buildings. It's the only Carthusian monastery in the world to have become a commune. It's been classified among "France's Most Beautiful Villages" since 1982 and counts fewer than 500 inhabitants. Take the guided tour: it gives access to closed interior spaces (14th-century wall paintings, a restored Carthusian father's hermitage) that you won't see without a guide.

The small squares and houses built against the monastic structures tell the story of the unique coexistence between lay life and Carthusian heritage. A school has remained active in the brothers' courtyard since 1839. During a free stroll, observe how inhabitants have converted the former cells into ordinary homes — it's both strange and touching.

activities.text: Hike to the Roches de Marlin in the heart of the Pilat Regional Natural Park (7.3 km, ~3 hours, yellow blazing). This unusual megalithic site nestled in the Jarez hills offers views over the Gier and Rhône valleys. Hiking boots recommended.

Nighttime storytelling walks organized from late July to late August every Thursday evening: about 75 minutes by lantern light in the charterhouse with a local storyteller. A rare experience — check the program at the Pilat tourist office before arriving.

days_plan.itin_title: Loop Around Lyon: Villages and Golden Stones

visits.text: Sainte-Énimie clings to the flank of the Sauveterre causse, between 600-meter-high cliffs. According to legend, the Merovingian princess Énimie, sister of King Dagobert, was cured of leprosy by bathing in the Burle spring and founded a monastery in the 6th century.

The Burle spring gushes at the village heart in a turquoise-water basin. This karst resurgence, explored by divers through 400 meters of underground galleries, constitutes one of the major outlets of the Sauveterre causse.

The church of Notre-Dame-du-Gourg, backed against the causse, dates from the 12th century and was remodeled in the 14th. Its Romanesque portal with round arch and two voussoirs opens onto a three-bay nave. Inside, a chapel illustrates Saint Énimie's story in contemporary ceramics.
The chapter house of the former Benedictine monastery, restored in 951, housed the monks' refectory and passing pilgrims. Its walls preserve acanthus decorations and carved human faces.
The wheat hall preserves one of the last stone grain measures in Gévaudan. The old village around the hall reveals its half-timbered houses, vaulted passages, and broad stone steps worn by centuries.

Castelbouc, a troglodyte village one kilometer upstream, can be visited from the riverside path. Its houses are pressed against the cliff, dominated by ruins of a medieval castle destroyed in 1592 to prevent it serving as Protestant refuge.

activities.text: The hermitage trail climbs from the Tarn bridge to the cave where Énimie supposedly retreated. Short but steep (45 min round trip), it offers a plunging panorama of the village and river's bend.

Outfitters offer Tarn canoe descents on several routes (from 30 min to over 5h). The stretch between Sainte-Énimie and La Malène crosses the Défilé des Détroits, the gorges' narrowest and most spectacular passage.

Aven Armand, 30 minutes by car on the Méjean causse, shelters a forest of 400 giant stalagmites, the largest reaching 30 meters. The funicular descent accesses an underground hall of cathedral dimensions.

Saint-Chély-du-Tarn, one kilometer downstream, offers a spectacle of cascades plunging into the Tarn from the cliff. Its 11th-century Romanesque church and semi-troglodyte chapel of Cénaret complete the visit.

days_plan.itin_title: Occitanie's 13 Most Beautiful Villages
Lozère Circuit: Tarn Gorges and Cévennes

visits.text: Opened in 1927, the Russian Cemetery is the largest Orthodox necropolis in Western Europe. More than 15,000 Russian émigrés who fled the 1917 revolution rest here.
Rudolf Nureyev, Serge Lifar, Andrei Tarkovsky, and Princess Obolensky number among the 5,200 graves. Princes, artists, and resistance fighters lie alongside entire families.

The Church of Our Lady of the Dormition was inaugurated in 1939. Its green roof symbolizes the earth, its blue dome the heavens. The architecture draws inspiration from 15th and 16th-century Novgorod.
The portico features a fresco of the Assumption; the dome shows Christ bestowing blessings. The crypt houses the tombs of bishops and architect Albert Benois.
Orthodox crosses, carved steles, and frescoes testify to the spiritual richness of the Russian diaspora. Birches, spruces, and pines create an authentically Russian atmosphere.

activities.text: Stroll among the graves beneath the towering birch trees. The Russian-style landscaping with small benches and trees creates a pastoral setting unique in France.
The municipal park runs along the Orge on the edge of town. Walking paths and playgrounds invite a nature break after visiting the cemetery.

days_plan.itin_title: Essonne Journey: Castles and River Valleys

visits.text: L'Île-Bouchard, an unusual village composed of two settlements separated by an island in the Vienne. The Priory of Saint-Léonard, founded in 1067, preserves only its Romanesque choir and ambulatory with three apsidal chapels. Its capitals, carved around 1130-1140 depicting scenes from sacred history, form one of Touraine's most complete ensembles of Romanesque sculpture.

The church of Saint-Gilles in L'Île-Bouchard, remodeled in the 15th then 19th centuries, dominates the Vienne's right bank. The Franciscan convent, with 12th- and 17th-century remains classified as Historic Monuments, completes this discreet heritage. Joan of Arc, en route to Chinon in 1429, supposedly paused here before Saint-Gilles' Romanesque portal.

Sainte-Maure-de-Touraine, the small town that gave its name to the famous AOP goat cheese. The truncated cone-shaped log pierced by a labeled rye straw has been produced here since Carolingian times. Legend says it was Moorish women, who remained after the 732 defeat, who transmitted their cheesemaking knowledge to the people of Touraine.

The market hall of Sainte-Maure, built in the 17th century, houses a lively market. Nearby, a dolmen and remains of a Roman camp testify to ancient occupation of the plateau. The 11th-century church and 16th-century houses complete this Touraine market town's heritage.

activities.text: Visit to a goat farm and Sainte-Maure-de-Touraine AOP tasting. Farm producers open their doors to explain the raising of Alpine and Poitevine goats, lactic coagulation, ladle molding and aging. The rye straw, laser-engraved, guarantees the cheese's authenticity.

Walk in the Vienne valley around L'Île-Bouchard. The Loire-Anjou-Touraine Regional Natural Park offers marked trails between vineyard slopes, riverbanks and tufa villages. Bucolic landscapes and absolute peace.

Sainte-Maure-de-Touraine market. Under the 17th-century market hall, producers display goat cheeses, rillettes, honey and seasonal fruit. Sainte-Maure AOP pairs perfectly with a red Chinon or dry Vouvray.

days_plan.itin_title: Indre-et-Loire Road Trip: Loire Valley Châteaux, Secret Valleys & Touraine Vineyards

visits.text: Sainte-Suzanne crowns a rocky spur rising 70 meters above the Erve valley. Listed among France's Most Beautiful Villages and as a Small Town of Character, this fortress was the only stronghold to successfully resist the assaults of William the Conqueror in the 11th century.

Step into the castle courtyard to discover the Renaissance lodge, built between 1608 and 1613 by Guillaume Fouquet de la Varenne, Henri IV's Postmaster General. It now houses the CIAP (Centre for Architecture and Heritage Interpretation) with scale models, audiovisuals and tactile displays exploring Mayenne's heritage.

The 11th-century quadrangular keep, erected by the Beaumont family from around 1020, stands protected by round towers equipped with arrow slits—the flour tower and the powder tower. The entrance was on the first floor via a ladder, designed to protect the lord during attacks.

Walk the patrol path along the double rampart walls. The 360° view sweeps across the Coëvrons hills, the Mayenne hedgerow countryside and the Erve valley. Head to the Watch Tower and West Tower for the finest panoramas over the town's rooftops.

The church, remodeled in 1884, preserves a Renaissance portal and remarkable statues from the 14th to 17th centuries, including those of Saint John the Baptist and Saint Suzanne, patron saint of engaged couples.

Push open the door to the municipal museum, housed in the former 17th-century courthouse. Nine rooms trace 3,000 years of local history through models, weapons from the Hundred Years' War and elements of Celtic vitrified walls. The centerpiece: France's oldest suit of armor, dating from 1410-1430, restored by the Musée des Invalides.

Descend toward the Grand Moulin, the oldest mill in the lordship. Transformed from a grain mill into a paper mill, it's still run by a master papermaker who crafts pure rag paper by hand using 13th to 18th-century techniques. The town once boasted 16 paper mills from the 17th to 19th centuries.

activities.text: The Mills Walk follows the Erve for 3.6 km below the fortified town. This family-friendly path passes the vestiges of old water mills installed along the river.
Visit the Erves dolmen, Mayenne's oldest monument. This Neolithic megalith, approximately 6,500 years old and classified in 1889, once sheltered the burials of 14 individuals.
Climb to Tertre Ganne in the late afternoon. This former strategic post from the Hundred Years' War offers the finest panorama of the medieval town and castle—spectacular at sunset.

days_plan.itin_title: Mayenne Circuit: Hedgerows and Medieval Towns

visits.text: The Arch of Germanicus (19 AD), a two-bay Roman arch that marked the entrance to the bridge over the Charente. Moved stone by stone in 1843 to escape demolition. Dedicatory inscriptions to Tiberius, Germanicus and Drusus Caesar are readable on the attic.

The Gallo-Roman amphitheater of Saintes (1st century) could hold 15,000 spectators. Built into a hillside valley, it retains its tiers, vomitoria and elliptical arena. Summer concerts and shows in this ancient setting.

The Abbaye aux Dames (11th-12th century), the first women's monastery in Saintonge. Romanesque facade sculpted with grimacing heads and fantastic animals. Conical bell tower covered in stone scales. Now a cultural center and home to an early music festival.

Saintes old town stretches between the Charente and the hillsides. Saint-Pierre Cathedral (rebuilt after the Wars of Religion), 18th-century mansions, medieval lanes. Wrought iron halls for the market.

activities.text: Guided tour of Gallo-Roman Saintes. The tourist office offers a complete circuit: Arch of Germanicus, Saint-Saloine baths, amphitheater, archaeological museum. Mediolanum Santonum was capital of the Aquitaine province.

Cruise on the Charente departing from Saintes. Traditional gabares or electric boats head upriver toward Cognac. Meadow landscapes, weeping willows, grey herons. Commentary on the history of cognac river transport.
Pineau and cognac tasting. Regional producers open their cellars for guided visits. Pineau des Charentes, a blend of grape must and cognac, is enjoyed chilled as an aperitif.

days_plan.itin_title: Charente-Maritime Road Trip: La Rochelle, Île de Ré, Fort Boyard and Roman Saintonge

visits.text: Salernes is France's capital of the hexagonal tomette — that small red terracotta tile that has paved Provençal floors since the early 19th century. From 1830 onwards, local manufacturers specialized in this flooring prized for its thermal inertia: cool in summer, warm in winter. The Terra Rossa museum, housed in a former tile factory redesigned by architect Jean-Michel Wilmotte, traces 7,000 years of ceramic activity in Salernes. The visit passes through a paleontological room revealing local excavations, a complete reconstruction of early 20th-century workshops with period machinery, and an exceptional collection of over 2,000 decorated tiles from the Middle Ages to the 19th century — including pieces unique in Europe. Allow at least 90 minutes to fully appreciate it.

The rest of Salernes can be explored leisurely on foot: about fifteen pottery and ceramics workshops are established in and around the village, most open to the public. Step inside two or three to watch artisans at work and understand the techniques that transform the red clay from local hills into tomettes or decorated faience. The old town's lanes, fountains and the Bresque River below complete the stroll.

activities.text: On Sunday morning, the Salernes market is one of the liveliest in the Haut-Var. Local producers sell honey, fresh figs (the white Salernes fig is renowned), olive oil and goat cheeses. It's also an opportunity to find artisan ceramic pieces directly from exhibitors. Arrive between 8 AM and 10 AM for the best selection.

Salernes' old mill and oil press allow you to complete your discovery of traditional local industry. Guided tours combining all three sites — mill, oil press and Terra Rossa — are offered on certain Tuesdays in July and August. Check with the tourist office for dates.

days_plan.itin_title: The 10 Most Beautiful Villages of the Haut-Var

visits.text: Salers sits at 951 meters altitude and welcomes 400,000 visitors a year — for a population of just 312 — and with good reason: this village has remained virtually intact since the Renaissance. Ranked among France's Most Beautiful Villages since 1982, its silhouette in black volcanic stone is the iconic image of Cantal. In the 15th century, the village became the seat of the Bailliage des Hautes Montagnes d'Auvergne: wealthy magistrate families built the turreted townhouses with mullioned windows and slate roofs you admire today. Place Tyssandier d'Escous, at the heart of town, is lined with these mansions and dominated by a statue of the agronomist who improved the local cattle breed. In season, guided tours grant access to normally closed interior courtyards.

Saint-Mathieu church (13th century) houses five 17th-century Aubusson tapestries and a remarkable polychrome 15th-century Entombment — don't miss going inside. Afterwards, head to the Esplanade de Barrouze, a grassy terrace on the village heights: from here opens the finest plunging view over the Maronne valley and Cantal ridges. In autumn, the contrast between black stone and golden forests is striking.

Walk the circuit of the ramparts passing through Porte du Beffroi (15th-century clock tower with a bell dating from 1604) and Porte de la Martille with its two round towers. The so-called Maison des Templiers and Maison de Ronade with its vaulted gallery adorned with gargoyles are the two townhouses not to miss — ask at the tourist office about access.

activities.text: The Salers hill loop (3–5 km) lets you leave the fortified town and understand its position on the volcanic promontory above the Maronne valley. Allow 1h30–2h, easy level. Keen trail runners and hikers can head for Puy Violent, accessible from Col de Néronne a few km away (3h30 return, intermediate level, 360° views over the Cantal mountains).

Salers is also a gastronomic destination: the mahogany-coated, lyre-horned Salers cow produces one of the rare PDO cheeses made exclusively on farms, in summer pastures, from raw milk — Salers cheese. Local producers open their farms to visitors around the village. Don't leave Salers without trying truffade and, if you find it, Cantal gentian liqueur.

days_plan.itin_title: Cantal Road Trip: Auvergne Volcanoes and Mountain Traditions
Auvergne's Volcanic Villages Trail

visits.text: The Belle Époque thermal baths in Spanish-Moorish style, built in 1894, dominate the spa quarter. Their water contains 300 grams of salt per liter—ten times saltier than seawater.
Descend into the Bayaà crypt where the salt spring gushes, 200 million years old. This fountain has belonged to the Parts-Prenants shareholders since 1587, a unique medieval collective ownership.
The Musée du Sel traces 35 centuries of salt history in a 17th-century Salies house. The legend of the boar explains how hunters discovered the salt spring.
The Salines produce 2,000 tons of salt annually. This white PGI salt is essential for curing authentic Bayonne ham, giving it its distinctive flavor.
The Église Saint-Vincent blends 11th-century architecture with Flamboyant Gothic style. Its 18th-century altar dominates the nave, restored after a devastating fire.

activities.text: Indulge in thermal treatments recognized by the French Academy of Medicine in 1891. The water rich in 26 trace elements treats rheumatism and gynecological conditions.
Stroll along the Saleys River that winds between half-timbered houses on stilts. Salies is nicknamed the Béarn Venice for its stone bridges and waterside charm.

days_plan.itin_title: Pyrénées-Atlantiques Road Trip: Basque Country & Béarn

visits.text: Grande Saline (UNESCO).
Thermal baths.
Fort Saint-André.
Old town.

activities.text: Mountain biking in the forest.
Visit to diamond cutting workshops.
Hike to the Flumen waterfalls.

days_plan.itin_title: Jura Mountains Road Trip, France: Comtois Traditions & Emerald Lakes

visits.text: Did you know that Salles-Arbuissonnas is the 5th longest commune name in France? But that's not why we make the detour: it's for its cloister, the last Romanesque cloister in the Rhône department. Built in the 10th century by Cluniac monks, the priory of Saint-Martin-de-Salles houses a 12th-century gallery with ribbed vaults resting on double columns in golden stone. The site's history is equally savory: in the 18th century, noble families placed their daughters here as countess-canonesses — pious yet worldly young women who had individual houses built around the court of honor. One of them, Alix des Roys, was the mother of the poet Lamartine. The Priory Museum is open from March 1 to November 30 (closed Monday and Tuesday).

The wine-growing village with its golden stone façades deserves a slow stroll: the AOC Beaujolais-Villages classified vines hug the hillsides to the horizon, and the panoramas over Mont Brouilly (crowned by its Notre-Dame-des-Raisins chapel) and, on clear days, the Alps, are among the most beautiful in Beaujolais. Take advantage of the opportunity for a tasting in a local cellar.

activities.text: Walking loop "cloister–village–vineyards" (3 km, very easy, ~1 hour). The trail follows the wine-growing hillsides, offers plunging views over the village, and joins Mont Brouilly visible in the distance. In autumn, during harvest time, the atmosphere is particularly lively.
Short climb to a viewpoint over the Beaujolais hills. On clear days, the Alpine chain sketches itself to the east. It's one of those moments when you understand why we travel.

days_plan.itin_title: Loop Around Lyon: Villages and Golden Stones

visits.text: Reach the Montenvers site aboard the little red cog railway in service since 1909. At 1,913 meters altitude, discover the Mer de Glace—the largest glacier in the French Alps, stretching 7 kilometers down the valley. Visit the Ice Grotto accessible via more than 400 steps carved into the glacier itself.

Discover Samoëns, a charming village classified among France's most beautiful flowered villages. The heart centers around the Place du Gros Tilleul, where a massive linden tree planted in 1438 still provides shade. Admire the Church of Notre-Dame-de-l'Assomption, completed in 1555—a monument to centuries of village devotion.

Explore this exceptional 3.7-hectare Alpine botanical garden, classified as a Remarkable Garden of France. Gifted in 1906 to her native village by Marie-Louise Cognacq-Jaÿ, the garden showcases over 2,500 mountain plant species along 1.5 km of winding trails. The Château Chapel offers panoramic views over the Giffre Valley.

activities.text: Board the historic red train that climbs from Chamonix to the Montenvers site facing the Mer de Glace. The narrated journey reveals the dramatic history of mountaineering that transformed this valley—from 19th-century pioneers to modern alpinists.

Descend the metal staircase (more than 400 steps) to access the grotto carved fresh each year into the glacier. Marvel at the ice sculptures and translucent blue walls—a magical world deep within the living glacier.

Wander this unique Alpine garden where every sense awakens. The serpentine path leads through meticulously recreated Alpine ecosystems—from rocky outcrops to lush meadows—revealing how mountain plants adapt to harsh conditions with breathtaking beauty.

days_plan.itin_title: Haute-Savoie Road Trip: Alpine Lakes & Mont Blanc

visits.text: Sancerre sits on its rocky butte like a ship's prow overlooking the Loire. From the Tour des Fiefs (14th century), the panorama sweeps 360°: Loire Valley, vineyards terraced like amphitheaters, Pouilly-Fumé hills across the river, Morvan on the horizon. The climb is worth it. 183 steps, stunning view at every level.

The old town is lovely to walk through. Stone houses, cobbled streets, hidden passages. The Saint-Jean Gate (15th century) is the last medieval gate standing. The Saint-Père-la-None Church towers over the square; inside, beautiful 15th-century frescoes recently restored. Several viewpoint terraces offer stunning views of the vineyards.

The Maison des Sancerre is the essential stop to understand the appellation. Interactive museum, tasting workshop, vineyard explanation. You'll learn everything about terroirs (terre blanche, caillottes, silex), grape varieties (Sauvignon for whites, Pinot noir for reds and rosés), vinification. At the end, commented tasting of 3 wines. Booking recommended in high season.

Sancerre has over 300 winegrowers. Many cellars welcome visitors, often by appointment. Some favorites: Domaine Vacheron (organic, biodynamic), Domaine François Cotat (legendary whites), Pascal Jolivet (contemporary cellar, beautiful view). Direct sales, commented tastings. Allow 1h per cellar.

activities.text: Crottin de Chavignol is Sancerre's other star. This small goat cheese (AOC since 1976) comes in 5 stages of aging: fresh, semi-dry, dry, very dry, "stone." Dozens of producers around Sancerre. Visit a farm, watch milking, taste cheese at different stages. The Dubois-Boulay farm in Chavignol opens daily in summer.

The "Route du Crottin" is a 32 km circuit through the Sancerre vineyards. It connects Sancerre, Chavignol, Verdigny, Bué — the four main wine-producing villages. Signs, explanatory panels, viewpoints. Can be done by car or bike (hilly, allow 3-4 hours). Very beautiful in May-June (vines in flower) and September (harvest).

The Loire flows 8 km from Sancerre. Several river ports for walks: Saint-Satur, Saint-Thibault, Tracy-sur-Loire. The banks are wild, the river wide, migratory birds stop here. At Saint-Thibault, the 11th-century Romanesque church is a gem.

days_plan.itin_title: Cher Circuit: Romanesque Berry and Jacques-Cœur Route

visits.text: Grostenquin opens this final day with one of the most spectacular exhibitions on the circuit: 150 nativity scenes from five continents, gathered in one place. The centerpiece is a grand Provençal nativity of about 45 m², with about a hundred santons of 27 cm and around fifteen automata, recreating the life of a Provençal village at Christmas time. Admission is free. The village's neo-Gothic church, dedicated to Saint John the Baptist and rebuilt in 1867, deserves a look: during the demolition of the old one in 1863, a Swedish colonel's tomb was discovered there.

In Destry, the nativity is assembled each year by the village children. It brings together santons of 50 to 60 centimeters from the early 20th century, all carefully restored. About twenty figures in their original version, sometimes supplemented with snowmen depending on the year's mood. A living, popular, and touching nativity.

Saint-Barthélemy Church in Raville, built in 1758, holds a surprise: it displays nativities made in bobbin lace, Tiffany glass, and ceramics—uncommon artisanal techniques that give these representations of the Nativity an almost sculptural character. A detour worth the trip for craft enthusiasts.

In Créhange, a family nativity of 7 m² gathers 130 figures and automata in a meticulous setting. In Bambiderstroff, the Church of Saint-Félix de Nola displays a wooden nativity representing a Palestinian landscape on the side altar. Bionville-sur-Nied closes this overview with the historic nativity of the Kahl family, built in 1910 by the grandfather and brought back to light by descendants after twenty years in storage.

The FICI workshop in Sanry-sur-Nied is the final point of the route and one of its gems. The santon maker, originally from Poland and trained by a master from Aubagne, is one of the rare members of the Brotherhood of Santon Makers of Provence based outside Provence. She has been making hand-painted clay santons for over 30 years—some 7 cm, others much larger and dressed—including original Lorraine creations like Saint Nicholas, Father Fouettard, and the Lorraine miner. MOSL Quality certified. Workshop visits possible and on-site sales. Contact in advance for groups.

activities.text: Take time to wander through the Pays de Nied villages between Grostenquin and Sanry: Romanesque and neo-Gothic churches punctuate the country roads of the Lorraine plain. A photo circuit of these rural religious architectures, often open without supervision, captures the diversity of the region's construction know-how between the 18th and 20th centuries.

At FICI santonnier workshop, the exhibition-sale is open every day except Monday in December, from 2 PM to 6:30 PM. It's an opportunity to purchase unique artisanal santons—some incorporate Lorraine or mining costumes you won't find anywhere else—and to witness, if you're lucky, the work of modeling and hand-painting.

days_plan.itin_title: Christmas Nativity Trail through Moselle

visits.text: Meursault is nicknamed the 'white wine capital of Burgundy'—and the title is well deserved. Its 400-hectare vineyard devotes 95% of its surface to Chardonnay, on clay and limestone soils ideal for this grape variety. Tour the village starting with the 14th-century Gothic church, then walk past centuries-old cellars: the winegrowers' 'little châteaux' testify to ancient opulence. Thomas Jefferson, future President of the United States, wrote to his supplier on January 22, 1789: 'I am in urgent need of Meursault... it is the one I prefer!'

Puligny-Montrachet is the commune of the world's most sought-after white wines: its 4 grands crus—Montrachet, Chevalier-Montrachet, Bâtard-Montrachet and Bienvenues-Bâtard-Montrachet—are often designated as the planet's finest Chardonnays. The Montrachet grand cru itself covers only 7.99 hectares, straddling Puligny and Chassagne. Walking among the rows, look for the signs marking the parcels—sometimes just a few meters separate a village appellation from a premier cru, and a €50 bottle from a €500 one.

Chassagne-Montrachet, Puligny's neighbor, shares two of the same grands crus but is also known for its red wines—discreet but excellent. Stroll through the pale stone village to feel the atmosphere of a place where every house conceals a cellar. The commune added Montrachet to its name in 1879, to capture the renown of the grand cru it shares.

Santenay closes the Côte de Beaune—and with it the UNESCO-inscribed Climats of Burgundy, which stretch from Dijon to here. The commune bears a dual vocation: viticultural, with 12 premiers crus in Pinot Noir, and thermal—its waters are Europe's richest in lithium and have been known since Roman antiquity. The Château de Santenay dominates the vines; Philippe le Hardi, Duke of Burgundy, was one of its owners. From atop Mont de Sène (521 m), called the 'Mountain of the Three Crosses,' the view extends on clear days to the Jura and Mont Blanc.

activities.text: The Voie des Vignes cycling route follows small roads between Beaune and Santenay over a 35 km section without excessive elevation: it passes through Pommard, Volnay, Meursault, Puligny and Chassagne. Rental shops offer bikes—traditional or electric—in Beaune and the villages. It's the slowest and most pleasant way to read the wine landscape: you'll see the changes in soil, exposure and stone colors from wall to wall.

Hot air ballooning over the Côte de Beaune, departing from the Beaune or Pommard area, is the experience that gives this entire vineyard its geographic meaning. Seen from above, the Côte resembles a mosaic of puzzle pieces—each parcel has its own soil color, slope, exposure. Flights take place early morning or late afternoon, depending on winds. Operators offer regular departures from May to October; book several weeks ahead in summer.

days_plan.itin_title: Burgundy's Grand Crus Route: Dijon to Santenay

visits.text: The Franciscan monastery of Saorge is one of the region's architectural rarities: founded in 1633 by Recollect friars in the Roya valley, it presents a unique blend of sober Baroque typical of Franciscans — no marble or ostentatious gilding, but 17th-century frescoes of exceptional finesse. The cloister houses 23 frescoes illustrating the life of Saint Francis of Assisi, and a remarkable series of nine sundials painted around 1760. From the esplanade, the view of the amphitheater village and Roya gorges is striking.

Push open the door of Église Saint-Sauveur in the village: built in the 15th century then enriched in the 17th, it preserves an 1847 Italian organ transported by mule from Nice — a remarkable logistical feat for an instrument this size. The twisted-column Baroque decor and walnut woodwork testify to the care lavished on the valley's religious buildings.

Saorge's lanes constitute a visit in themselves: the "pountin" — vaulted passages typical of Ligurian architecture — wind between fountain squares and wash houses, tall houses stack up until they tumble into the valley. The entire village is classified as a historic monument. Three fortified castles once watched over this "Roya lock," a reputedly impregnable stronghold.

The violet flagstone roofs, extracted from local mountain quarries, give the village a unique color in the Niçoise backcountry. The tall houses — sometimes seven stories — pressed against the slope recall Ligurian perched villages. Take time to search for painted sundials on the facades: Saorge counts about twenty.

activities.text: Parc National du Mercantour is within hiking distance from Saorge: the trail toward the rock carvings of the Vallée des Merveilles begins a few kilometers away, with representations of animals and figures from the Bronze Age 3,000 to 4,000 years old. If you don't have time for the trip to the Vallée des Merveilles, inquire at the Musée des Merveilles in Tende (next stop).

Board the Train des Merveilles on the Nice-Tende line from Breil-sur-Roya: tunnels carved through rock, viaducts suspended above gorges, dizzying panoramas over the Roya. This section built between 1906 and 1928 is one of France's most beautiful — a contemplative alternative to the road.

Cool off at the Bain du Sémite, accessible on foot from the village: a crystalline water spring nestled in a verdant setting, a natural pool formed by rock where residents have bathed for generations. A secret spot tourist guides rarely mention.

Follow the Baroque route from village to village in the Roya valley: each church harbors its treasure — historic organs, Niçoise school retables, 15th-century frescoes. The Menton-Riviera & Merveilles tourist office offers themed guides to explore this heritage trail.

Sit down at one of the village's rare restaurants to savor Roya valley cuisine: wild herb ravioli and local goat cheese feature prominently on menus. The valley's cuisine blends Niçoise and Piedmontese influences — a specialty found nowhere else.

days_plan.itin_title: Alpes-Maritimes Road Trip: From the French Riviera to Perched Villages

visits.text: The Église Saint-Martin raises its five-tiered bell tower at the heart of the village. Its sculpted bell ranks among France's most ornately decorated, a masterpiece of Basque craftsmanship.
The Grottes de Sare burrow into the mountain for 900 meters. The son-et-lumière guided tour reveals stalactites, stalagmites, and traces of Paleolithic occupation dating back millennia.
Maison Ortillopitz, built in 1660, perfectly illustrates traditional Basque architecture. Its wooden galleries and period furnishings tell the story of rural Basque life across the centuries.
Follow the Galtzada, a medieval route that once linked Bayonne to Spain. Stone bridges cross mountain streams through a green valley haven.

activities.text: Hike the Camino de Santiago paths that have wound through Sare since the Middle Ages. The GR8 and GR10 trails offer panoramic loops around La Rhune mountain.
Explore Ainhoa and Ascain, authentic Basque villages classified among France's Most Beautiful Villages. Their half-timbered houses frame pelota courts where locals still gather.

days_plan.itin_title: Pyrénées-Atlantiques Road Trip: Basque Country & Béarn
Nouvelle-Aquitaine Loop: Medieval Villages, Vineyards & Dordogne Valley

visits.text: Sarlat holds the world record for density of protected monuments: 65 classified buildings packed into just 11 hectares. France's first historic preservation zone under the Malraux Act (1964), the town is a maze of honey-colored stone townhouses, medieval alleys, and slate-roofed buildings that seem frozen in amber. Walking these streets feels like stepping onto a film set—which, in fact, it often is.

The Maison de La Boétie, built in the 16th century, ranks among Sarlat's most beautiful Renaissance facades. Mullioned windows, pilasters, and sculpted medallions adorn the stonework. This was the birthplace of Étienne de la Boétie in 1530—Montaigne's closest friend and author of the revolutionary essay Discourse on Voluntary Servitude.

Sainte-Marie Church underwent a dramatic transformation by architect Jean Nouvel, who converted the medieval structure into a covered market. A panoramic glass elevator now rises through the bell tower, offering 360-degree views over Sarlat's slate rooftops. Nouvel's massive Corten steel doors sparked heated debate—but the view is undeniable.

activities.text: Sarlat's market is legendary throughout Périgord. Wednesday morning it fills Place de la Liberté; on Saturday it sprawls through the entire old town. Foie gras, winter truffles (December through March), walnuts, wild mushrooms, tangy goat cheeses, and charcuterie crowd the stalls. The truffle market alone draws connoisseurs from across France.

The Manoir de Gisson at Place des Oies is the only classified private mansion in Sarlat open for tours. Furnished with period pieces, it reveals how wealthy 17th-century merchant families lived. The spiral staircase tower connects three floors of elegant rooms—a rare glimpse into private life behind these stone facades.

days_plan.itin_title: Dordogne Road Trip: Cave Art, Medieval Fortresses & Périgord Delights
Nouvelle-Aquitaine Loop: Medieval Villages, Vineyards & Dordogne Valley

visits.text: The Peace stained glass window by Marc Chagall, created between 1974 and 1976 for the 13th-century Cordeliers chapel, rises 12 meters high and spans 7.5 meters wide. This is the largest stained glass window ever designed by the artist, a hymn to life and reconciliation in celestial blue tones, emerald green, and flamboyant red. Allegorical figures (tree of life, prophets, animals) intertwine in poetic composition. Natural light animated by 600 m² of glass transforms throughout the day.

The Sarrebourg museum, installed in the former Cordeliers convent, presents Gallo-Roman archaeology and regional collections. Mosaics from the 2nd-3rd centuries, funerary steles, and ceramics attest to Roman Sarrebourg's importance. The medieval section displays sculptures, liturgical furniture, and paintings from the 15th to 18th centuries. The lapidary garden brings together fragments of ancient buildings.

The Sarrebourg canal marina, on the Marne-Rhine canal, offers boat rides and canal cruises. The port hosts 120 pleasure boat berths and welcomes cruise boats. Waterside promenades, nautical activities (canoe, pedal boat), and cycle paths along the canal. The Boat Museum traces the history of canal navigation and traditional trades. Boat rentals available for exploring the canal.

activities.text: Walk in the forest of Sarrebourg, 3,500 hectares of oaks and beeches crisscrossed by 50 km of marked trails. The Sentier des Sources (8 km) discovers the springs feeding the Sarre, with footbridges over streams and forest clearings. The Lorraine sandstone peaks offer panoramic views. Medium difficulty, 2h30 walking, signposting and maps at the tourist office.

Explore the historic center where the Saint-Barthélémy church (18th century) displays baroque altarpieces and an 18th-century organ. The pedestrian streets preserve 18th and 19th-century bourgeois houses. The Place du Marché, surrounded by arcaded facades, hosts a market on Tuesday and Friday mornings. The town hall (1770) in classical style faces the park with centuries-old trees.

days_plan.itin_title: Moselle Circuit 10 Days: Metz, Bitche Citadel and Chagall Stained Glass

visits.text: The Faience Museum occupies the former Moulin de la Blies, reconstructed in 2007 around the listed 1880 Winter Garden. This 750 m² metal and glass structure shelters 2,500 pieces tracing 200 years of ceramic production: earthenware services, decorative panels, Art Nouveau vases, and Art Deco creations. The collections present the evolution of techniques and styles from 1790 to the 1990s, when the last Sarreguemines kilns shut down.

The Casino des Faïenceries, an Art Nouveau jewel from 1927, amazes with its ceramic decorations: mosaics, friezes, and monumental panels by master ceramist Alexandre Sandier. The grand hall, completely covered in earthenware, evokes Sarreguemines' golden age when 3,000 workers produced 25 million pieces per year. The building now houses temporary exhibitions on ceramic history and industrial heritage.

The Faience Garden (Jardin des Faïenciers) displays monumental ceramic panels in an 8,000 m² landscaped park. The route presents 150 decorative pieces: Art Nouveau friezes, company signs, stove tiles, and architectural elements salvaged from demolished buildings. Each panel illustrates a facet of earthenware art: advertising, architecture, technical virtuosity. Free access year-round.

activities.text: Stroll through the German district (Deutsches Viertel) built between 1870 and 1918 during the German annexation. Neo-Gothic buildings, half-timbered houses, and bourgeois villas with Art Nouveau facades line the streets. The Sainte-Croix church (1886) in red sandstone, the town hall (1895) with its 46-meter tower, and the Protestant temple (1897) illustrate Wilhelminian architecture. A unique cross-border architectural heritage.

Cross the Saar footbridge to Germany and walk along the riverbanks connecting France and Saarland. The promenade links Sarreguemines to Saarbrücken through 15 km of greenways. Medieval mills, locks, and old factories punctuate the route. Cycling possible, bike rentals available on the French side. A border walk illustrating the Franco-German reconciliation.

days_plan.itin_title: Moselle Circuit 10 Days: Metz, Bitche Citadel and Chagall Stained Glass

visits.text: Marina.
Ospédale Forest.
Bavella Needles.

activities.text: Santa Giulia).
Swimming in natural pools.

days_plan.itin_title: Southern Corsica Travel Guide: Corsican Mountains & Gulf Heritage Itinerary
Complete Tour of Corsica

visits.text: The Saulges cave valley is the only prehistoric site presenting decorated caves north of the Loire. Carved into 320-million-year-old limestone by the Erve river, this small canyon shelters 22 known cavities, classified Natura 2000, frequented by humans for at least 300,000 years.

Margot cave, classified Historic Monument since 1926, is the only decorated cave open to visitors in western France. In 2003, archaeologist Romain Pigeaud discovered over 200 prehistoric engravings from the Upper Paleolithic (~25,000 years ago): horses, bison, woolly rhinoceros and engraved birds—the latter extremely rare in cave art.

Rochefort cave, a human-sized chasm, reveals an archaeological excavation site, spectacular concretions (stalactites, stalagmites) and an underground lake. Excavations have uncovered a Solutrean habitat dated 25,000 to 20,000 years before present.

The Prehistory Museum, opened in 2017, traces the life of the Erve valley's first inhabitants through original archaeological collections: laurel leaves, spear points, reindeer antler harpoons, ibex plaquette. An immersive 3D dome allows virtual visits to Mayenne-Sciences cave, closed to the public, which contains drawings of bison and mammoths 27,000 years old.

Saint-Pierre church, in Saulges village, is a building constructed between the 8th and 10th centuries. A few minutes' walk away, the Céneré oratory perpetuates the memory of the hermit who evangelized the valley.

activities.text: Take hiking trail n°33 from the museum. It crosses the canyon passing Japanese stepping stones over the Erve, follows the limestone cliffs and offers plunging views of cave entrances nestled in the rock faces.
The canyon cliffs count a hundred climbing routes from 3a to 7c, on walls reaching 25 meters. A popular venue for initiation and recreational practice.

The Natura 2000 valley shelters wild orchids, dragonflies, rare butterflies and one of western France's most important bat colonies. Ouessant sheep maintain the site ecologically. Take time to observe wildlife at dusk.

days_plan.itin_title: Mayenne Circuit: Hedgerows and Medieval Towns

visits.text: The château-palace of the Dukes of Anjou has watched over the Loire since the 14th century. Its Gothic silhouette was immortalized in the Très Riches Heures du Duc de Berry, one of medieval Europe's most celebrated illuminated manuscripts.

The Cavalry School has perpetuated French equestrian tradition since 1763. Its 18th-century buildings, classified as historic monuments, represent one of Europe's finest examples of military equestrian architecture.
The Tank Museum assembles over 200 military vehicles—one of the world's most significant collections of armored machinery.
The 12th-century Church of Saint-Pierre preserves rich liturgical furnishings and medieval sculptures that speak to centuries of devotion.

activities.text: Saumur's sparkling wine cellars extend for kilometers through the tuffeau limestone. Maison Bouvet-Ladubay, founded in 1851, offers tours through its subterranean galleries where méthode traditionnelle wines age in darkness before their fine bubbles see daylight.
The Loire à Vélo cycling route passes through Saumur. Landscaped quays follow the royal river to neighboring châteaux through vine-covered countryside.

The Saumur-Champigny vineyards cascade down limestone slopes. Cabernet Franc, Chenin Blanc, and Chardonnay grapes produce the renowned red, white, and sparkling wines that have made this region famous.

days_plan.itin_title: Maine-et-Loire Road Trip: Anjou and Loire Valley Châteaux

visits.text: Saverne's Château des Rohan astonishes with its proportions. Nicknamed the 'Little Alsatian Versailles,' its neoclassical pink sandstone façade extends exactly 140 meters—no accident: it's the equivalent, horizontally, of the height of Strasbourg Cathedral. Built in the late 18th century by architect Nicolas Salins de Montfort for Cardinal Louis René de Rohan-Guéméné, it's the fourth building erected on this site since the Middle Ages. Its museums (Gallo-Roman collections, art and history, Louise Weiss archives) deserve 90 minutes.

A few kilometers from Saverne, Haut-Barr castle merits the detour. Nicknamed 'the Eye of Alsace,' this 12th-century medieval castle perched at 470 meters on three sandstone rocks offers a panorama over the entire Alsatian plain as far as Strasbourg Cathedral on clear days. The Devil's Bridge footbridge, suspended between two rocks, is a highlight of the visit.

Saverne's rose garden extends at the foot of the pass—France's second-oldest rose garden, it assembles nearly 5,000 rose bushes from 800 varieties. Open from late May through late August, it's particularly spectacular in June during the Rose Festival.

activities.text: The Saint-Louis-Arzviller inclined plane, about ten kilometers from Saverne, is a unique engineering feat in Europe. Opened in 1969 to replace 17 locks over 3.8 km, this transverse inclined plane allows barges to cross 44 meters of elevation on the Marne-Rhine Canal in a single 35-minute operation. Operators offer tourist boat rides on the inclined plane in season.

Stroll along the Marne-Rhine Canal facing the Château des Rohan. The 70-berth marina, installed in the large basin, gives the town particular animation. Operators offer bike rides along the canal toward Arzviller or eastward. The greenway is pleasant and easy.

days_plan.itin_title: Alsace Wine Route: Strasbourg to the Vosges

Discovery of local crafts.
Hiking to the forts.
Cross-border hike.

days_plan.itin_title: Hautes-Alpes Majesty: High Mountain Passes, Ski Resorts & Alpine Valleys

places.name: Sceaux

visits.text: Colbert acquired the Sceaux estate in 1670. The current château, rebuilt in 1856 by the Duke of Trévise, houses the departmental museum. Collections evoke French art from Louis XIV to Napoleon III.
The Pavillon de l'Aurore preserves a dome painted by Charles Le Brun. The Orangerie, built by Jules Hardouin-Mansart, measures 80 meters long.
Walk through the woods to Parc de Saint-Cloud. The national estate spreads across 460 hectares along the Seine.

activities.text: The 181-hectare Parc de Sceaux unfolds formal French gardens. André Le Nôtre exploited the terrain's relief to create two perspectives: the château axis and the Grande Cascade descending toward the Octagon Basin.
Montretout offers panoramic views over Paris and the Seine valley.

days_plan.itin_title: Hauts-de-Seine Road Trip: Royal Heritage & Gardens

map_keywords: Musée du Domaine départemental de Sceaux

places.name: Sedan

visits.text: Sedan Fortress. Europe's largest fortress spreads its 35,000 m² across seven levels, a legacy of the Princes of La Marck built from 1424 onward. Ramparts 26 meters thick tell six centuries of military history, from the Wars of Religion to the 20th-century commando training center. The scenographic tour plunges visitors into the epic saga of Sedan's princes through armories, casemates, and underground galleries.

Museum of the Battle of 1870. Housed within the fortress, the museum commemorates Napoleon III's defeat by the Prussians on September 1, 1870. An impressive model recreates the 19th-century town entirely surrounded by fortifications. Bavarian painter Ludwig Braun's panorama sketch and the collection of Prussian helmets evoke this dramatic turning point in French history.

Star-shaped stronghold. The bastioned plan drawn in the 16th century and reinforced by Vauban organized the defense of this independent principality until 1642. Four star-arranged bastions connected by imposing curtain walls surrounded the now-vanished urban enclosure. Discover remnants of the defensive system that made Sedan one of Europe's most formidable fortresses.

Felt Museum. This museum testifies to the major textile industry that thrived in Sedan from the 17th to 20th centuries. Collections showcase felt and fine cloth manufacturing techniques that brought international fame to the town. Period machinery and fabric samples tell the story of Sedan's industrial manufacturing adventure.

activities.text: Historical show at the fortress. During summer, attend chivalric tournaments that transport visitors to medieval times. Stunt fighters clash on horseback or in single combat, bringing to life the codes of honor and the art of the joust. This immersive family show delights young and old in the exceptional fortress setting.

Walk along the Meuse. Shaded banks invite leisurely strolls between gardens and natural areas. The river draws wide meanders at the foot of the castle reflected in calm waters. Landscaped paths connect to the Trans-Ardennes greenway following the old towpath for over 120 km.

Discover felt craftsmanship. Local workshops perpetuate Sedan's centuries-old textile expertise through contemporary creations and traditional pieces. Visit artisan boutiques to observe felting techniques and discover the history of this industry that employed thousands of workers. Craftspeople offer hats, accessories, and decorative objects in natural felt.

days_plan.itin_title: Ardennes Road Trip: Forests & Fortresses (9 Days)

map_keywords: Musée de la Bataille de 1870 Sedan

visits.text: Notre-Dame Cathedral, built between 1210 and 1310, is the jewel of Norman Gothic architecture in Orne. Its spires soar to 230 feet. The choir, a masterpiece of Île-de-France Gothic, is bathed in light from magnificent ancient windows. Two Michelin stars.
The Basilica of the Immaculate Conception, built in 1854 in neo-Gothic style, was the first in France dedicated to that title. Its interior decoration and distinctive lighting set it apart.

The Palais d'Argentré, built in 1778 for the bishop who tutored the future Louis XVIII, displays neoclassical facades and ordered gardens. It testifies to the clergy's splendor in the Age of Enlightenment.

The Departmental Museum of Religious Art, housed in a 14th-century listed building, displays metalwork, sculptures, paintings, and liturgical vestments from the Middle Ages to today, drawn from rural Orne churches.
Visit the Cours des Fontaines, a classified site with an 18th-century washhouse. This verdant haven follows the remnants of the old fortified wall.
Explore Sées to spot its 13 listed historic monuments, including the remains of the 11th-century Church of Saint-Pierre and the 17th-century chapel of the Hôtel-Dieu, adorned with paintings on wood.

activities.text: The Haras National du Pin—nicknamed the 'Versailles of Horses'—sprawls across 2,470 acres 12 miles from Sées. Founded in 1715 by order of Louis XIV, it houses red-brick stables, a ceremonial saddlery, and a collection of listed horse-drawn carriages.
From June to September, the 'Thursdays at Le Pin' present different horse breeds in the Aure riding hall. Equestrian shows, farrier demonstrations, and carriage rides complete the experience.
The Écouves forest, a few miles from Sées, peaks at Signal d'Écouves (1,355 feet), the highest point in western France. Marked trails wind through sessile oak and beech groves.

days_plan.itin_title: Orne Road Trip: Norman Bocage and Prestigious Stud Farms

visits.text: The Church of Sainte-Madeleine dominates Segré with its 30-meter-high neo-Byzantine dome. Classified stained glass windows flood the interior with colored light.
From the church esplanade, views sweep across the town and the confluence of the Oudon and Verzée rivers. These two waterways flow through this settlement founded in ancient Roman times.

The Château de la Lorie, built during Louis XIII's reign, stands as the northernmost of the Loire châteaux. Its grand salon, entirely composed of marble, evokes princely palaces with its elegant opulence.
The historic center preserves ancient houses and classified churches. Rue Pasteur features a half-timbered dwelling listed as a historic monument—a rare survival of medieval domestic architecture.

activities.text: The Mine Bleue in Noyant-la-Gravoyère descends 126 meters underground into an authentic slate quarry. Guided tours reveal the harsh working conditions of miners and the extraction techniques for the prized blue slate that roofed countless buildings.
The Oudon riverbanks offer shaded walks through rolling landscapes. The trail follows the peaceful river as it meanders through verdant countryside.
The local market showcases producers from the Angevin terroir. Artisan workshops and gastronomic events regularly animate the town with regional flavors and crafts.

days_plan.itin_title: Maine-et-Loire Road Trip: Anjou and Loire Valley Châteaux

visits.text: Séguret ranks among France's Most Beautiful Villages. Its cobbled calade lanes climb at the foot of the Dentelles de Montmirail, between honey-colored stone houses and vine trellises.
Pass through Porte Reynier, the fortified village's main entrance. The 15th-century belfry stands out with its unique wrought-iron spire, visible from far across the valley.
The 17th-century baroque Fontaine des Mascarons flows in the central square. Four sculpted faces spout water into a stone basin worn smooth by the centuries.
The Église Saint-Denis, built between the 10th and 13th centuries, houses a gilded wooden altarpiece and medieval frescoes. Its Romanesque bell-wall towers over the village rooftops.
Climb to the feudal castle ruins crowning the rock. The 360° panorama embraces the Rhône Valley, vineyards, and the jagged crests of the Dentelles de Montmirail.
The Place des Arceaux below surprises with its natural pergola formed by plane trees planted around 1860, whose branches have fused together over time.

activities.text: Hike into the Dentelles de Montmirail from Séguret. Trails wind between limestone cliffs and pine forests, with passages carved through rock.
Taste AOC Côtes-du-Rhône Villages Séguret wines directly in the village cellars or surrounding estates. The clay-limestone terroir yields powerful reds and fruity rosés.
In December, Séguret transforms into a living nativity scene: villagers reenact scenes from the Nativity in the lanes by torchlight. A unique Provençal spectacle.

days_plan.itin_title: Vaucluse: Avignon, Luberon Villages & Ventoux Routes
France's Most Beautiful Villages: Alps to Luberon in 7 Days

visits.text: The Humanist Library of Sélestat is one of the Bas-Rhin's finest surprises. Housed since 1889 in a former neo-Romanesque corn exchange built by Gustave Klotz (1843-1845), entirely restructured by Rudy Ricciotti and reopened in 2018, it preserves one of Europe's most precious collections. Its treasure: the 423 volumes of the personal library of Beatus Rhenanus (1485-1547), friend of Erasmus born in Sélestat, bequeathed to his native city at his death. The collection has been inscribed in UNESCO's Memory of the World Register since 2011. Notable items include a 7th-century Merovingian lectionary—the oldest book preserved in Alsace—and a copy of the 1507 Cosmographiae Introductio where the name 'America' appears for the first time.

Sélestat's Romanesque church of Sainte-Foy dates from 1094 and is part of the Alsatian Romanesque Route. Its three-aisle nave in pink Vosges sandstone and octagonal tower are perfect examples of Rhenish Romanesque art. Before it, visit the Gothic church of Saint-Georges—built between the 13th and 15th centuries—which once housed the parish library of Sélestat's Latin school, with some volumes chained to lecterns to protect them from theft.

Wander through Sélestat's historic center—its Renaissance houses, Vauban ramparts, and fountains tell of a time when it was one of the ten free cities of the Alsatian Decapolis. An amusing detail: the city archives preserve the world's first written mention of a Christmas tree, dated 1521, where the city notes the purchase of wood to 'make a Christmas tree'.

activities.text: From Sélestat, admire the silhouette of Haut-Koenigsbourg castle perched at 757 meters on its rocky outcrop—it dominates the entire Alsace plain and can be seen from many points in town. You'll visit it in detail tomorrow, but seeing it from the plain already gives you a good sense of its imposing character and the political dimension of its restoration.

In summer, Sélestat's Corso Fleuri transforms the town with giant flower-covered floats. The city is also known for the Fête du Feu festival in July, which transforms the castle park into a pyrotechnic spectacle. Check local programming according to your visit period.

days_plan.itin_title: Alsace Wine Route: Strasbourg to the Vosges
Alsace's Most Beautiful Christmas Markets

visits.text: Semur-en-Auxois sits on a pink granite spur encircled by the Armançon gorges. The four round 13th-century towers—including the Tour de l'Orle d'Or, standing 44 meters high—dominate the town and are visible from afar. Climb up to the walkway for a plunging view over the river 30 meters below.

Step through the door of the Collégiale Notre-Dame, built in the 13th and 14th centuries. It preserves remarkable stained glass windows, including one donated by the drapers' guild depicting the stages of cloth manufacture. Outside, linger over the sculpted portals. The Place du Terreau just in front is the heart of the town.

Walk down to the Pont Joly, below the town. From here you'll have the most photographed view of Semur: the towers and ramparts reflected in the Armançon, with medieval houses clinging to the cliff face. Come late afternoon for the best light.

activities.text: Walk around the ramparts then descend to the banks of the Armançon via the marked trail. The river carves deep gorges here—a striking natural setting for a Burgundian town. Allow 1.5 hours for the complete loop.
Every year in late May, Semur hosts France's oldest horse race—documented since 1639. If your dates coincide, don't miss this popular festival that takes over the town for three days.

days_plan.itin_title: Burgundy's 15 Most Beautiful Villages
Burgundy Road Trip: Cathedrals, Vineyards & Medieval Villages

visits.text: The Collégiale Saint-Hilaire is the jewel of Semur-en-Brionnais — and arguably one of the most beautiful Romanesque churches in Saône-et-Loire. Built in two campaigns during the 12th century (apse around 1125, west portal around 1180), it's the last great Romanesque church erected in the Brionnais region. Its octagonal bell tower, three apsidioles, and the interior corbelled gallery — nicknamed "the ice cream cone" by locals — draw direct inspiration from Cluny III. Look carefully at the west portal's tympanum: it depicts one of the rare scenes of the Council of Seleucia in Romanesque art, an almost unique subject in France. The church is open year-round from 8am to 8pm, free entry.

A few dozen meters from the collegiate church, the Château Saint-Hugues is the oldest castle still standing in Burgundy, built from the 10th century onwards on a motte. Its 22-meter Romanesque keep preserves three floors, including the lower floor with no windows, dating from the 10th century. This is where Hugues de Semur was born in 1024, future abbot of Cluny from 1049 to 1109: under his abbacy, the Cluniac order spread across all of Europe. His architects sent the plans for the collegiate church to Semur in homage to their patron. The castle is open from March 1 to November 15 — opt for a guided tour to fully grasp its historical richness.

Wander through the cobbled lanes of the old village among the sandstone houses, the gates of the former ramparts, and the still-legible layout of medieval fortifications. The village was sacked several times: by the English in 1364, burned in 1467 by Louis XI, then again in 1576 during the Wars of Religion. Yet it has retained remarkable architectural cohesion. In late afternoon, the raking light on the golden sandstone façades is worth the journey alone.

activities.text: Heritage loop "collegiate church–castle–old village" (2–3 km, moderate elevation). The path at the foot of the hill follows the old fortifications and offers a panorama over the Charolais and Brionnais countryside. Allow 90 minutes at a leisurely pace.

Walk to the Brionnais ridges for landscape reading from the heights. This region is considered one of the cradles of Burgundian Romanesque art, with more than twenty Romanesque buildings recorded within a 20 km radius. A marked circuit departs from the village.

days_plan.itin_title: Loop Around Lyon: Villages and Golden Stones
Burgundy's 15 Most Beautiful Villages

visits.text: Notre-Dame Cathedral soars above Senlis, built between 1153 and 1191 with its Virgin Portal—a masterpiece of 12th-century Gothic sculpture. Its 1240 Gothic spire pierces the sky at 78 meters, dominating the cityscape.
The Gallo-Roman ramparts from the 3rd century encircle the historic heart for a kilometer. Fifteen towers survive from this six-hectare enclosure built after the Germanic invasions of 275.
The Royal Château stands against the Gallo-Roman walls on the site where Hugh Capet was elected King of France in 987. Medieval vestiges bear witness to this founding royal residence.
The Gallo-Roman arena dates from the 1st century when the town was called Augustomagus. As the only preserved amphitheater in northern France, it testifies to the city's early Romanization.
The Museum of Art and Archaeology occupies the former episcopal palace. Gallo-Roman vestiges and votive offerings from the forest temple of Halatte trace two thousand years of local history.

activities.text: Stroll through medieval lanes past 15th and 16th-century half-timbered houses. Rue de la Tonnellerie, de la Tannerie, and du Chat Haret still bear the names of ancient crafts.

days_plan.itin_title: Oise Circuit: Imperial Châteaux, Ancient Forests & Living History

visits.text: Senones Abbey is like an adventure novel. Founded around 640 by Gondelbert, bishop of Sens, it came under the protection of Charlemagne — who appointed his own archichaplain as abbot around 768 — then was slowly captured by the Salm family, which created in 1751 the Principality of Salm-Salm, a small independent state of which Senones became the capital until 1793. You can still see today the abbatial palace and its monumental spiral staircase, decorated with a wrought iron railing signed by Jean Lamour — the same craftsman who created the gates of Place Stanislas in Nancy. Abbot Dom Calmet (died 1757) had assembled here 15,000 volumes, a library so renowned that Voltaire came to stay there for three weeks in 1753, attending all the services and searching through the archives. Guided tours during school holidays and on the 2nd and 4th Saturdays of each month from April to October, by reservation at the Tourist Office.

Continue to the two remaining wings of the pink sandstone cloister — they now house shops and the Tourist Office. The entire 18th-century building complex is well preserved: large library to the south, farm buildings, abbatial residence. Opposite the abbey, the castles and mansions of the former princely capital deserve a look — you can still sense the ambition of this tiny independent state.

activities.text: Senones is labeled Petite Cité de Caractère — stroll through the narrow streets to spot the 18th-century pink sandstone residences quarried from a site at the town gates. A local craft brewery offers tours in season: check with the Tourist Office for time slots. If you like character beers, it's a good way to end the day.

days_plan.itin_title: Vosges Abbey Trail: Monastic Heritage Unveiled

visits.text: Begin your road trip at the Cathedral of Saint-Étienne in Sens—quite simply the world's first Gothic cathedral. Construction began around 1135 under Archbishop Henri Sanglier, even before Notre-Dame de Paris. The exceptionally wide nave (50 feet across) is covered with sexpartite vaults similar to those in Paris. In 1234, Saint Louis married Marguerite of Provence here. Don't miss the Flamboyant transept designed by Martin Chambiges in the late 15th century, or the stained-glass window depicting the assassination of Thomas Becket—the Archbishop of Canterbury had taken refuge in Sens before being murdered in 1170.

Right next door, the Cathedral Treasury occupies the former chapel of the archbishop's palace. You'll see remarkable pieces: 15th-century Flemish tapestries, Thomas Becket's chasuble, and the Sainte Châsse, an ivory reliquary casket. This is one of France's richest cathedral treasuries—allow 30 to 45 minutes.

Wander through old Sens by following the "Circuit de Brennus" (named after the Gallic chieftain of the Senones who captured Rome in 390 BC). Arrows on the ground guide you through narrow streets, half-timbered houses, and the banks of the Yonne River. Pick up the guidebook at the Tourist Office before you set out.

Return to Sens to complete the loop. If you didn't do it on the first day, climb the south tower of the cathedral (open in summer, by reservation)—the view from the top embraces the town and the Sénonais countryside. Otherwise, revisit the nave with fresh eyes after seeing all the Romanesque and Gothic churches along the route—you'll appreciate its pioneering status all the more.

End at the Jardins de l'Orangerie, behind the cathedral—you'll find a formal garden that has recaptured its 19th-century appearance. Horticultural beds, shaded paths, view of the cathedral's rear. It's a peaceful, free conclusion to your journey.

activities.text: If you arrive on a Wednesday or Saturday morning, explore the Sens market—it's the best place to sample regional products from the Yonne: goat cheeses, Gâtinais honey, and Burgundy snails.

If you're leaving on a market day (Wednesday or Saturday), make your last Burgundy purchases—cheeses, mustard, gingerbread, Gâtinais honey. Sens has a TGV station with connections to Paris in one hour, making for an easy return.

days_plan.itin_title: Yonne Circuit: Northern Burgundy & Chablis Wine Country
Burgundy Road Trip: Cathedrals, Vineyards & Medieval Villages
Burgundy Road Trip: Cathedrals, Vineyards & Medieval Villages
