days_plan.itin_title: Hérault, France: Mediterranean Beaches and Languedoc Wine Country

visits.text: The Manufacture de Sèvres has produced porcelain since 1756. Founded under Louis XV with Madame de Pompadour's patronage, it supplied European royal courts and still serves the French Presidency.

The workshops reveal the secrets of Sèvres blue. 120 ceramists master about thirty trades: wheel throwing, molding, painting, gilding. Pastes and glazes are crafted on-site using time-honored techniques.
The National Ceramics Museum gathers 55,000 pieces from the Middle Ages to today. Sèvres porcelain, faience, stoneware, and pottery from around the world compose a reference collection.

activities.text: Join the Parc de Saint-Cloud through the gardens. The Manufacture occupies a site bordering the national estate.
The Seine embankments offer peaceful views of the river. Walk between Sèvres and Saint-Cloud.

days_plan.itin_title: Hauts-de-Seine Road Trip: Royal Heritage & Gardens

visits.text: Seyne-les-Alpes reveals itself from the Col de Maure pass: the Vauban citadel on its rocky spur of La Robine suddenly appears, crowning the village. It's a former Gallic oppidum that became a frontier town with Savoy under Louis XIV. The Grande Tour from the 12th century (Seyne was then called 'Seyne la Grande Tour') stands at the very heart of the fortress — restored and classified as a Historic Monument, it offers from its terrace a unique panorama over the Blanche mountain chain and the valley. Allow 1h30 for the complete visit, including the stalactite cistern, cannons, and old bread oven.

The citadel proper was built in 1693 by Niquet and Vauban — its narrow, elongated 'shark' shape follows the spur's relief. In addition to the permanent exhibition on military history, you'll find a contemporary art installation in free access: The donjon of the sleeping bear, by American artist Mark Dion, classified Viapac. An escape game in the fort's underground passages (180 m²) is offered depending on the season.

Descend into the old village for the pedestrian crossing from the Porte de Provence to the Place d'Armes: washhouses, half-timbered houses, flowered squares, and small museums of the forge, the bugade (traditional laundry), and the tailor mark the route. The Church of Notre-Dame-de-Nazareth, crowning its historic gardens, and the Penitents Chapel with its rare three-faced bell tower warrant a stop.

activities.text: A few kilometers from the village, the Nordic area of Ratery (1,700 m altitude) offers hiking and, in winter, cross-country skiing and snowshoeing. In summer, trails around Seyne in the Haute-Provence Geopark offer varied routes between larch forests and ridges. If you have time, the hamlet of Chardavon houses the Maison du Mulet — a small museum on the mule-breeding tradition of the Pays de Seyne, surprising and endearing.

days_plan.itin_title: Forcalquier & Verdon Gorges Road Trip

visits.text: Sillans-la-Cascade is a medieval village with 11th-century ramparts built with a unique purpose: not to protect a fortress, but to safeguard a spring. The Bresque River, which rises at Fox-Amphoux, feeds the highest waterfall in the department here. The village has a complex castle history: three castles have succeeded one another on its territory — the first medieval one disappeared, the second was erected in 1609 and destroyed during the Revolution, and the third was built in the 19th century and purchased by the commune in 1992 from the Count of Castellane.

The Sillans waterfall is the village's showpiece — don't miss it under any circumstances. From the parking area, an 800-meter marked trail winds through an understory of centuries-old plane trees before emerging at the viewpoint, facing the 42-meter cascade. The Bresque's waters plunge into a basin colored emerald and turquoise by micro-algae and the limestone bed. Swimming in the main pool is strictly forbidden for safety reasons — rockfalls are possible — but swimming areas are accessible further downstream. Allow 25 to 30 minutes for the round-trip walk, or 45 minutes if you descend to the river. Arrive early in summer: parking is paid from April through September and fills up fast after 10 AM.

activities.text: After the viewpoint, continue on a longer circuit along the Bresque to discover the small natural pools open for swimming downstream from the waterfall. The water is cool even in midsummer and the shaded banks are ideal for a picnic break. Marked trails let you climb back up to the heights overlooking the village with views across the entire valley.

days_plan.itin_title: The 10 Most Beautiful Villages of the Haut-Var

visits.text: Simiane-la-Rotonde perches at 650 meters on an advanced promontory of the Albion plateau, at the center of a Ventoux-Lure-Luberon triangle. The village owes its name to its exceptional keep: La Rotonde, a dodecagonal tower (12 sides) built at the end of the 12th century by the Simiane-Agoult family. Its interior is the real surprise — on the first floor, a 5-meter-high ribbed dome whose capitals adorned with strange and grotesque human masks fascinate historians. Some compare it to the Holy Sepulcher of Jerusalem brought back by crusaders. Classified as a Historic Monument as early as 1841, it's visited from February to November. In August, an international early music festival (Les Riches Heures Musicales de la Rotonde) holds its concerts there since 1982 — the acoustics are remarkable.

Then stroll the lanes to reach the 16th-century covered market: beneath its columns, a panoramic view of the lavender fields below opens like a painting. These halls were immortalized by Henri Cartier-Bresson in 1971. Nearby, the birthplace of writer Ponson du Terrail (1829-1871), author of the Rocambole saga, whose title gave rise to the French adjective 'rocambolesque' (far-fetched).
5 km from the village, Valsaintes Abbey houses a rose garden of 600 varieties and permaculture gardens around Cistercian ruins. The ideal period to visit is rose blooming, late May - early June.

activities.text: Simiane houses the SCA3P cooperative (founded in 1979), the largest lavender and lavandin cooperative in France: 330 members, 5,000 hectares of fine lavender and 15,000 of lavandin, representing 90% of world lavandin production. If you're there between mid-June and mid-July, the flowering Albion plateau is a photographic spectacle not to be missed. A visit to the cooperative or a local farm completes the day nicely.

days_plan.itin_title: Forcalquier & Verdon Gorges Road Trip

visits.text: The Sisteron citadel crowns the rocky spur blocking the Durance valley. The imposing fortress displays its bastioned enclosures and keep over eight centuries of military architecture. The underground staircase of 258 steps carved into rock in the 19th century links the town to the citadel in a mysterious ascent.

Notre-Dame-des-Pommiers cathedral blends Provençal Romanesque and Lombard influences. The sober 12th-century building displays a harmoniously proportioned nave. Side chapels house Baroque works and the 19th-century great organ resounds during summer concerts.

Walk through old town streets tiered between Durance and citadel. 14th-century medieval towers punctuate the former ramparts' layout. Renaissance fountains and fortified gates mark this labyrinth where Provençal town atmosphere reigns.

The Baume rock rises facing the citadel on the other side of the Durance. Horizontal limestone strata give the impression of a stack of slices. This remarkable geological site dominates the cluse where the valley narrows.

Medieval watchtowers punctuate the old town's perimeter. Five of them were preserved thanks to Prosper Mérimée. Round outward and open toward the town, they testify to Sisteron's medieval defensive system.

activities.text: The Sisteron via ferrata winds on the Baume rock walls. Cables, ladders and aerial footbridges allow a vertiginous ascent. The equipped route offers thrills safely with plunging views over the Durance and citadel.

Paragliding takes off from surrounding heights. The Durance valley's powerful thermals carry wings in an aerial ballet. The panorama embraces citadel, Durance and Alpine valleys in an unforgettable flight.

The Route Napoléon crosses Sisteron on the Emperor's return historic route. On March 5, 1815, Napoleon crossed the town without resistance during his march to Grenoble. A museum in the citadel traces this epic episode of French history.

Hiking trails radiate around Sisteron. The Baume rock ascent offers a sporty route rewarded by an exceptional panorama. The nearby Méouge gorges offer swimming and water hiking in a preserved setting.

days_plan.itin_title: Alpes-De-Haute-Provence Circuit: Lavender and Perched Villages

visits.text: Saint-Jean-des-Vignes Abbey displays its Gothic facade with two 75-meter spires. Founded in 1076, this Augustinian monastery experienced brilliant medieval prosperity before Revolutionary sale. The ruins preserve remarkable Gothic architecture with refectory, cloister and chapter house. A museum presents abbey life and heritage conservation.

The Saint-Gervais and Saint-Protais Cathedral mixes Romanesque and Gothic styles over four centuries of construction. The south transept displays pure Gothic while other parts retain Romanesque elements. The Merovingian crypt, among France's oldest, shelters sarcophagi and archaeological vestiges.

Explore the 12th-century underground Saint-Léger crypt beneath the old abbey. This Romanesque space preserves remarkable carved capitals. Vaulted galleries create a mysterious atmosphere charged with history.
Stroll along the Aisne River banks and canal. Promenades follow the water through greenery. Locks and boats enliven this fluvial landscape offering peaceful views.

activities.text: Visit the arsenal museum presenting French military heritage. Collections include weapons, uniforms and equipment from medieval times to present. Interactive displays explain French army evolution.
Walk the Clovis Path retracing Frankish king's history who was baptized in Reims but spent time in Soissons. Interpretation panels dot this heritage circuit through the historic city.
Cycle the Aisne greenway converted from an old railway. The flat route suitable for all levels winds through countryside and villages. Several loops allow half-day or full-day rides.

days_plan.itin_title: Aisne Circuit: Gothic Cathedrals and Picardy Nature

places.name: Solenzara

visits.text: Fautea beach, 15 km north of Solenzara, has a silhouette you won't see anywhere else: a round 16th-century Genoese tower stands directly on the beach, its feet in white sand. Built to watch for Barbary raids, it's now a listed historic monument. The beach in front is uncrowded—tourists rarely stop.

Make a detour to the Étang de Diane, accessible by track from the national road: a natural lagoon exploited for oyster farming since Antiquity—the Romans already appreciated its oysters. The site remains wild, bordered by tamarisks. Go early in the morning: the raking light on the water and mountains behind is one of the east coast's finest.

activities.text: The Côte des Nacres between Fautea and Solenzara is a string of undeveloped beaches with translucent waters and polished granite bottoms. Stop at Canella beach—white sand, no beach bar, no paid parking—for a no-frills afternoon.

Solenzara is the starting point for the Gorges du Solenzara, 10 km west: a mountain road follows the river between granite walls to a series of pools and waterfalls. Ideal for a late-afternoon swim before returning to the village.

days_plan.itin_title: Complete Tour of Corsica

map_keywords: Plage de Fautea tour génoise Solenzara

visits.text: The Abbey of Saint-Pierre de Solesmes has dominated the Sarthe for over a thousand years. Founded in 1010 by Geoffroy the Old, lord of Sablé, who donated a monastery to the monks of Marmoutier, it remained a simple priory until the Revolution. It was Dom Prosper Guéranger who in 1833 made it the pinnacle of Gregorian chant that it is today. Seen from the river, the abbey's silhouette is stunning—austere and massive like a sacred fortress, in the words of a 19th-century traveler. From the bridge spanning the Sarthe, take time to contemplate this emblematic panorama before entering.

Only the abbatial church is freely visited. Once through the medieval door, the Saints of Solesmes await you in the transept: two sculptural ensembles from the 15th and 16th centuries considered masterpieces of French sculpture. The north group represents the tomb of Our Lord in flamboyant Gothic style, the south group illustrates the Assumption of the Virgin. The abbey organ, 39 stops of North European inspiration, resonates under the vaults during services. The crypt, accessible at indicated hours, houses Dom Guéranger's tomb.

Attending a service sung by the community of Benedictine monks is an experience difficult to describe. The sung conventual mass takes place at 10 a.m. every day (about 1h15, 1h45 on Sundays and feast days). Gregorian chant resonates in the abbey's particular acoustics with a depth and purity unlike anything else. Arrive early—seats in the nave fill quickly on Sundays.

activities.text: The abbey shop, open 9 a.m. to 7 p.m. outside services, offers theology books, Gregorian chant recordings by the monks themselves, and monastic products. Solesmes recordings are an international reference in their field—buy one to extend the experience at home.

Continue on the pedestrian path that runs along the Sarthe beneath the abbey to Cri Cri beach. The walk is short and peaceful, perfect after the spiritual immersion of the service. You'll also pass nearby the building of Sainte-Cécile Abbey, founded in 1866, where 45 Benedictine nuns now reside.

days_plan.itin_title: Sarthe Road Trip: Le Mans and the Loir Valley

visits.text: The Abbatiale Saint-Pierre-Saint-Paul de Solignac is a unique case in Limousin: its three-bay nave is covered with hemispherical domes on pendentives, in the manner of Périgord churches. The Benedictine abbey, founded between 632 and 638 by Saint Éloi, goldsmith and advisor to King Dagobert, was Limousin's most powerful from the 10th to 13th centuries.

Look up at the choir capitals: griffins seized by men, figures battling serpents, and stacked palmettes stand out in limestone. The nave capitals, more archaic, show checkered patterns and animal heads.

The oak choir stalls, carved between 1457 and 1484, rank among Limousin's most remarkable. Flip the misericords: profane scenes appear — grimacing faces, mocking characters, and licentious figures hidden beneath the monks' seats.

In the right transept arm, a 15th-century fresco depicts Saint Christopher carrying the Christ Child, inspired by the Golden Legend. Donors' coats of arms and a detailed ship complete the composition.

The Château de la Borie, a 17th-century Renaissance residence, houses the Fondation La Borie dedicated to sound and music arts. Its garden of sounds, set in a 7-hectare park, blends sound installations and outdoor sculptures.

activities.text: Cross the 12th-century Romanesque bridge, built by monks for Compostela pilgrims. Its three ogival arches and two cutwater piers carved to break the Briance current span the river with timeless grace.

Wander the medieval village lanes, among half-timbered houses and stone foundations. The abbey's turbulent history — Saracen and Norman raids, Wars of Religion, Revolution — left scars that Benedictine monks, returned in 2021, work to heal.
Take a section of GR 654 toward the Pont-Rompu, a 13th-century vestige on the old Roman road. The 6 km loop follows the Briance through preserved bocage landscape.

days_plan.itin_title: Haute-Vienne Circuit: Limoges, Porcelain & Historic Villages

visits.text: The Rock of Solutré, a spectacular limestone escarpment formed from fossilized coral reefs 160 million years ago, rises to 1,617 feet amid the vineyards of the Mâconnais. A Grand Site of France, it owes its fame to its prehistoric site, rare limestone grasslands, and the ritual annual ascent by François Mitterrand.

The Museum of Prehistory, semi-buried beneath a planted dome at the Rock's foot, presents collections from one of the richest prehistoric sites in Europe. A hunting ground frequented for 55,000 years, the site gave its name to the Solutrean culture (21,000–18,000 BC). The "laurel leaves" of knapped flint represent the apex of prehistoric technique.

The archaeological and botanical garden complements the museum visit. A trail with eight stations traces the geology, hunting techniques, and 150 years of research. Wild orchids, boxwoods, and junipers populate the limestone grasslands at the summit—a unique microclimate protected by Natura 2000.

The Pouilly-Fuissé vineyards spread out at the Rock's foot. This clay-limestone terroir, planted with Chardonnay, produces internationally renowned white wines. The appellation, created in 1936, covers the villages of Solutré-Pouilly, Fuissé, Vergisson, and Chaintré. The Château de Pouilly, a fortified 15th-century manor, overlooks the vines.

activities.text: Ascent of the Rock of Solutré. The 2.5-mile trail from the museum parking lot climbs 330 feet through the vineyards then the heath. At the summit, a 360-degree panorama over the Mâconnais, the Saône valley, the Beaujolais, and the Alps on clear days.

Pouilly-Fuissé tasting at a winemaker's. The Maison du Grand Site offers an initial introduction, then cellars in surrounding villages let you compare terroirs. Mâcon-Villages and Saint-Véran complete the palette of local whites.

Hike to the Rock of Vergisson, Solutré's twin sister. Less frequented, it offers comparable panoramas and a wilder atmosphere. The loop linking the two rocks via the ridges (5 miles) is one of the finest in southern Burgundy.

days_plan.itin_title: Saône-et-Loire Road Trip: Bibracte, Cluny, Solutré & the Mâconnais Vineyards

visits.text: The Pont-Vieux has spanned the Bévéra since the 13th century: Sospel's emblem, it once housed a toll station on the Salt Road linking Nice to Turin via the Tende Pass. The central tower pierced by an arch served as a toll booth. If you arrive in late afternoon, the light on the trompe-l'œil facades of riverside houses is particularly beautiful.

The Cathédrale Saint-Michel raises its 11th-century Romanesque bell tower at the old town's heart: it's one of the region's oldest and contrasts with the Baroque nave added in the 17th century. The interior houses a retable from the 15th-century Niçoise school attributed to François Bréa — don't miss the Virgin and Child in the side chapel.

Fort Saint-Roch is one of the alpine Maginot Line's most impressive fortifications: built between 1931 and 1933, it mobilized 5,000 m³ of concrete and 385 tons of steel. Its 2,000 meters of galleries carved 50 meters underground constituted a true subterranean city for 250 soldiers autonomous for three months. A 149 mm cannon weighing 13 tons remains. Bring a sweater — it's 14°C inside year-round.

Fort de l'Agaisen, built from 1932 to 1936 on Mont Agaisen, completes the defensive system around Sospel. Equipped with powerful artillery protecting neighboring forts, it offers guided tours in season led by passionate volunteers. The orientation table at the summit lets you locate all the valley's fortified structures.

The trompe-l'œil facades of the old town display ochre and pink hues characteristic of neighboring Liguria: Renaissance doorways, wrought-iron balconies, squares shaded by century-old plane trees. Sospel has preserved an authentic alpine town atmosphere, far from the coast's bustle.

activities.text: Lace up your boots for the Bévéra valley: the GR 52 trail climbs toward the Col de Turini through chestnut forests and alpine pastures. In good weather, the Mediterranean and Mercantour peaks share the horizon from the viewpoints. The Col de Turini (1,607 m) is known to rally racing fans — it's a historic stage of the Monte-Carlo Rally.

Local operators offer electric mountain bike outings on Mercantour circuits: gentle routes for families in the valley or demanding single tracks for experienced riders. It's an excellent way to discover the backcountry without excessive effort.

The Gorges du Piaon are the area's canyoning paradise: rappel descents, natural slides, pools of fresh water in a setting of rock and forest. Supervised operators offer half-days accessible to beginners and families from age 8.

Thursday mornings, Place des Platanes comes alive for the weekly market: goat cheeses from alpine pastures, honey from Mercantour hives, olive oil from the hills, garden vegetables. An authentic village market, far from mass tourism.

days_plan.itin_title: Alpes-Maritimes Road Trip: From the French Riviera to Perched Villages

visits.text: The Abbey Church of Sainte-Marie, a masterpiece of Romanesque art, crowns the town with its three domes on pendentives. Founded in the 10th century by Benedictines following the Abbey of Saint-Géraud d'Aurillac, the current church was constructed between 1145 and 1150.

The Romanesque portal, relocated inside the abbey church for protection from the elements, represents a treasure of medieval sculpture. The tympanum recounts the legend of Theophilus; the trumeau interweaves Isaac's sacrifice and original sin. The pillar of the prophet Isaiah, all movement and flowing drapery, is a unique piece of Romanesque art.

Inside, a painting attributed to Chasseriau, "Christ in the Garden of Olives" (1844), and a monumental Stoltz organ (1855), listed as a Historic Monument, complete the visit. The acoustics beneath the domes naturally amplify sound.

Explore Souillac's historic center, crossed by seven viaducts spanning streams and ravines. Ancient alleyways lead to the 19th-century market hall, still active on market days, and to the Dordogne riverbanks where the old river port once stood.

activities.text: Walk along the Dordogne riverbanks downstream from Souillac. The path follows the river through woods and meadows, with views of the hillsides and town rooftops.
The Town of Art and History offers themed guided tours of Souillac and the abbey church, highlighting monastic history and Romanesque sculpture.
Souillac hosts a renowned summer jazz festival. Open-air concerts, performances in the abbey church, and events at various venues animate the summer season.

days_plan.itin_title: Lot Itinerary: Cahors, Rocamadour & the Lot Valley

visits.text: The Basilica of Notre-Dame-de-la-Fin-des-Terres is a 12th-century Romanesque art jewel, UNESCO World Heritage listed. Built to welcome pilgrims on the Way of Saint James, it was buried by dunes in the 18th century then completely excavated in the 19th. The Romanesque architecture is remarkably preserved with sculpted capitals. The sober luminous interior invites contemplation. Classical music concerts are regularly organized here.

Soulac's Old Village counts about 500 Belle Époque villas built between 1860 and 1930. These pink brick houses, with their sculpted eaves, canopies, arabesques, and colored stained glass, testify to the seaside resort's heyday. Two visitor circuits reveal the finest: the Soulac villas circuit (1.5 hours) and the architectural circuit (1 hour). Maps available at the tourist office. Some villas bear pretty feminine names.

Soulac's seafront stretches over 7 kilometers of fine sandy beaches. The landscaped promenade runs along supervised beaches with ocean views and distant Cordouan Lighthouse. Breakwaters—concrete slabs installed in the 1930s to combat erosion—create natural seawater pools at high tide. Several accesses descend to beaches from the promenade. Restaurants and ice cream shops line the seafront.

A 4-meter-tall replica of the Statue of Liberty stands on the seafront facing the ocean. Like the New York original, it was created in the Bartholdi workshops. She gazes toward America and the Côte d'Argent dunes. It's a friendly landmark and popular photo spot. A memorial to the northern Médoc fortress stands nearby, recalling German occupation.

activities.text: Excursion to Cordouan Lighthouse, nicknamed the Versailles of the Seas or King of Lighthouses. Built starting in 1584 under Henri IV, it's France's oldest lighthouse still in operation. Standing 68 meters tall, it's located at sea 7 km from shore. UNESCO World Heritage listed since 2021, it can be visited from April to September. The boat excursion lasts 3.5 hours with 1.5 hours on-site visit. Landing is done feet in the water. Impressive interior with royal apartments and chapel.

Swimming at Soulac beaches. The resort has several supervised summer beaches: central beach, Naïades beach, Amélie du Nord beach. Atlantic waves attract surfers and bodyboarders. Surf schools offer lessons on two beaches. Beaches are wide and clean, with fine sand. Beware of baïnes, dangerous currents typical of the Atlantic coast. Always swim in supervised areas.

Visit to Pointe de Grave, the northern tip of the Médoc peninsula where the Gironde meets the Atlantic. A monument commemorates the 1917 American troop landing. The site offers spectacular views over the estuary, ocean, and Cordouan Lighthouse. It's the starting point of the GR8 trail running up the entire Atlantic coast. A ferry connects Pointe de Grave to Royan in Charente-Maritime (20-minute crossing). Wild landscapes of dunes and forests.

Cycling through pine forest. Soulac has a network of bike paths winding through the Landes forest or leading toward the estuary. Routes are shaded and pleasant, away from beach heat. Several marked circuits reveal heritage and nature. Bikes, rosalies, and tandem rentals available in town. Paths are flat and accessible to all.

Visit the Soulac Archaeology Museum. This small museum presents the history of the Médoc Point from Antiquity to medieval times. Collections include objects discovered during local excavations: ceramics, tools, coins, architectural elements. Several rooms trace the basilica's history and the Saint James pilgrimage. Modest admission. Guided tours possible by reservation.

days_plan.itin_title: Gironde Road Trip: Bordeaux, Vineyards & Arcachon Bay

visits.text: The Souvigny priory, dependency of Cluny Abbey, ranks among the five most important Cluniac sites with Cluny, Paray-le-Monial, Vézelay and La Charité-sur-Loire. The priory church Saint-Pierre-et-Saint-Paul (11th-15th centuries) combines Romanesque and Gothic. Its two bell towers, one Romanesque and the other Gothic, symbolize architectural evolution over four centuries of construction.

The Bourbon Necropolis shelters tombs of Louis II de Bourbon (†1410) and wife Anne Dauphine d'Auvergne, and of Charles I de Bourbon (†1456) and wife Agnès de Bourgogne. These sculpted black marble gisants testify to ducal power and artistic refinement of the 15th century. The tombs constitute exceptional funeral art treasures classified as Historic Monuments.

The Zodiac Column (12th century), unique in France, rises 5.30m displaying sculpted capitals representing signs of the zodiac, months of the year and agricultural labors. This Romanesque masterpiece illustrates medieval cosmic vision linking celestial time and earthly rhythms. A rare and precious testimony of Romanesque symbolic art.

The Saint-Marc Museum occupies former prior's building (17th century) presenting Souvigny priory history and treasures. Excavated capitals, liturgical objects and historical documents trace spiritual and artistic life of this major Cluniac center. Temporary exhibitions highlight medieval heritage and sacred art.

activities.text: Walk the historic village discovering stone houses (17th-18th centuries) and Saint-Marc church (11th-12th centuries). Narrow streets reveal ancient facades and small squares where priory past resonates. The village preserves an authentic monastic atmosphere.

The Tronçais Forest, one of the most beautiful oak forests in Europe, extends over 10,600 hectares just 20 km from Souvigny. Marked trails wind through three-century-old trees including oaks exceeding 40 meters. Ponds, glades and forest roads invite hiking and nature discovery.

days_plan.itin_title: Allier Road Trip: Vichy, Thermal Baths and Bourbonnais Bocage

visits.text: Steene (Stennebourg) — Little girl of the moats.
Aire-sur-la-Lys — Priest in cassock.

activities.text: Access: private ext. (Steene)
Access: public (Aire)

days_plan.itin_title: Haunted Flanders Tour: Northern France's Castles, Legends & Dark History

visits.text: The European Museum of Beer occupies the former provisions store of Stenay citadel, transformed into a malthouse in the 19th century. Labeled "Museum of France" and listed as Historic Monuments, it displays 55,000 objects across 2,500 square meters. Six sequences trace brewing history from Mesopotamian origins (12,000 years ago) to the present day.

The museum's highlight: the advertising posters room reveals how graphic art served brewers. It notably features a lithograph by Czech artist Alfons Mucha (1897), created for Bières de la Meuse of Bar-le-Duc, in Art Nouveau style.

Stenay's Saint-Joseph church, rebuilt after Great War destruction, stands out for its sober architecture. The city's coat of arms, adorned with a devil, recalls this former stronghold of the Counts of Bar's rich past.

activities.text: The museum's botanical garden presents Lorraine's only hop garden, surrounded by aromatics and spices used in brewing. The visit stimulates sight, smell, and touch.
End at the museum tavern for a tasting of craft beers with top, bottom, or spontaneous fermentation. Local specialties accompany the glasses: beer sausage, cheese, and Stenay madeleines.
Hop on a bike along the Meuse Greenway, a cycle path part of EuroVelo 19. The section from Stenay follows the river through a Natura 2000 zone rich in birds and valley-floor landscapes.

days_plan.itin_title: Meuse Circuit: Verdun and the Citadels

places.name: Strasbourg

visits.text: You've saved the best for the penultimate day. Strasbourg, self-proclaimed 'Christmas Capital' since 1992, hosts France's oldest Christmas market — the Christkindelsmärik (in Alsatian: 'Christ Child's Market'), existing since 1570. Its history begins with the Protestant Reformation: in December 1570, Pastor Johannes Flinner preached against the Saint Nicholas tradition deemed 'papist,' and the Town Council decided to transfer gifts to the Christ Child and move the market to just before Christmas. The Christkindelsmärik was born. Today it's held at Place Broglie — where it's been anchored since 1871 — with hundreds of wooden chalets in a model imposed by the municipality to guarantee visual harmony.

Don't miss the Cathédrale Notre-Dame — one of the Gothic world's tallest, with its 142-meter pink sandstone spire, and whose sculpted façade is a picture book carved in stone. In December, its tapestries commissioned by Richelieu for Louis XIII (14 pieces on the Virgin's life) are exhibited in the choir — they're only visible between Advent and Epiphany. The Petite France quarter, with its 16th and 17th-century tanners' half-timbered houses reflected in the Ill river, is even more beautiful beneath December garlands.

Climb Place Kléber's grand Christmas tree — no, you can't literally, but stay to contemplate it from a winstub (traditional Alsatian bistro) terrace overlooking the square. This monumental tree, different each year, has been Strasbourg's Christmas symbol for decades. In the evening, when it lights up simultaneously with the Aubette façade, it's the iconic image of Christmas in Alsace.

This final day in Strasbourg deserves exploring the city from a different angle — fewer markets, more heritage, with the serenity of a winter Sunday. The Musée de l'Œuvre Notre-Dame, installed in medieval buildings behind the cathedral, houses original sculptures from the portals (copies are outside to protect them), 12th-century stained glass, and an extraordinary collection of Rhenish art. It's one of Strasbourg's most overlooked and most beautiful museums.

The Musée Alsacien, installed in an ensemble of 17th-century patrician houses in the Grande-Île, recreates authentic Alsatian interiors from the 18th to 20th centuries — kitchens, bedrooms, artisan workshops — with a unique collection of everyday objects. In December, the museum offers Christmas storytelling sessions in Alsatian for children (ages 3-7). The Palais Rohan, an 18th-century former episcopal palace that welcomed Napoleon and Marie-Antoinette, houses three museums including the Musée des Arts Décoratifs — its 'Cardinal's Apartment' is one of France's most refined rococo interiors.

activities.text: Strasbourg deploys over 300 chalets across a dozen UNESCO-listed Grande-Île squares and streets. Each has its theme: local crafts at Place des Meuniers, Alsatian gastronomy at Place du Marché aux Cochons de Lait, decorations at Place de la Cathédrale. Allow at least half a day for the complete circuit — and stay until nightfall for illuminations that transform medieval façades into fairy tale settings.

In the evening, enjoy free concerts resounding in all Strasbourg churches during Advent — Catholic, Protestant, Orthodox. The cathedral, the Église Saint-Thomas (which houses Marshal de Saxe's Mausoleum by Pigalle), the Église Saint-Pierre-le-Jeune: each December evening, a church lights up and fills with voices. It's the spiritual dimension of this Alsatian Christmas sometimes forgotten behind the chalets.

For a final view of illuminated Strasbourg, climb to the Barrage Vauban terrace — this 17th-century dam spans the Ill at the entrance to Petite France and offers a 360° panorama over the old town's rooftops, the cathedral, and the Vosges. Access is free. In December, with low afternoon light, it's the most beautiful postcard.

Finish this final afternoon strolling one last time through the Village de l'Avent at Place Broglie, and treat yourself to a final mulled wine in a Petite France winstub before heading to the TGV station or airport. Strasbourg is served by direct TGV from Paris (1h50) and by Strasbourg-Entzheim international airport. It's one of Europe's best rail hubs for departing to any destination.

Alsace Wine Route: Strasbourg to the Vosges
Alsace's Most Beautiful Christmas Markets
Alsace's Most Beautiful Christmas Markets

map_keywords: Quartier de la Petite France Strasbourg
Marché de Noël Place du Temple Neuf Strasbourg

visits.text: The Château de Sully rises in medieval splendor on the banks of the Loire. This 14th-century fortress, inscribed on the UNESCO World Heritage list, preserves a remarkable barrel-vaulted timber roof and Renaissance apartments where Voltaire once sought refuge.
The château park invites leisurely wandering. Water-filled moats, centuries-old trees, and Loire River views create an atmosphere of timeless romance.
The Église Saint-Ythier mingles Romanesque and Gothic styles. The 12th-century nave and Gothic choir house the burial vaults of the Lords of Sully.

activities.text: Each June, the Classical Music Festival brings the château to life with evening concerts. Music fills the great medieval hall beneath the historic chestnut timber framework.
Walk or cycle along the Loire riverbanks. The Loire à Vélo route passes through Sully between the levees and the river's wooded islands.

days_plan.itin_title: Loiret Road Trip: Orléans & Loire Valley Châteaux
The Essential Châteaux of the Loire Valley

visits.text: Suresnes American Cemetery, established in 1917, shelters 1,541 burials from World War I and 24 unknown soldiers from World War II. A chapel watches over the white Carrara marble crosses.

The crosses align in a V-formation on Mont-Valérien's slopes. A wall of the missing perpetuates the memory of 974 soldiers. This is the only American military cemetery in Europe commemorating both World Wars.

activities.text: 4,800 vines spread across Mont-Valérien's slopes, continuing a winemaking tradition dating back to Roman times. The climb rewards you with an unforgettable view of the Eiffel Tower.
Terrasse du Fécheray offers a 180° panorama over Paris. This verdant oasis is cherished for its natural beauty.
Walk along the Seine from the dam and lock. The 185-meter river structure controls the waterway. The embankments preserve a bucolic atmosphere.

days_plan.itin_title: Hauts-de-Seine Road Trip: Royal Heritage & Gardens

visits.text: Talloires-Montmin nestles in Lake Annecy's most beautiful bay. Cézanne, Napoleon III, and Churchill succumbed to the charm of this village nicknamed the Pearl of the Lake.
The former 11th-century Benedictine priory now houses an American university campus. Its terraced gardens descend to turquoise waters.
The 17th-century Abbey was built by Benedictine monks on the site of a 9th-century monastery. It overlooks the marina.
The Roc de Chère nature reserve preserves 68 hectares of forests and cliffs. Trails lead to viewpoints offering plunging views over the lake.
Saint-Germain's Hermitage and its cave nestle in the cliff. This pilgrimage site offers an exceptional panorama of Talloires and the lake.
The Angon Waterfall plunges 60 meters down a cliff in a green setting. The path follows the rock face to the base of this impressive cascade.

activities.text: Canyoning in Angon Canyon is the region's signature activity. Rappels under waterfalls, natural slides, and jumps spice up this aquatic descent.
Take off paragliding from the Forclaz Pass to soar over the lake and mountains. Tandem flights offer unforgettable sensations facing the Alpine panorama.
The Roc de Chère hike winds through a forest with varied atmospheres: Mediterranean pine groves and mountain beech forests coexist on this promontory.
Rent a kayak or paddleboard to explore Talloires Bay from the water and discover coves inaccessible by road.

days_plan.itin_title: Haute-Savoie Road Trip: Alpine Lakes & Mont Blanc
Grand Tour of the French Alps: From the Thousand Ponds to Mont Blanc

visits.text: Talmont-sur-Gironde, a village of 100 inhabitants perched on a rocky promontory. Listed as one of France's Most Beautiful Villages, it overlooks the Gironde estuary. Lanes lined with hollyhocks, white houses with blue shutters, artisan shops.

Sainte-Radegonde Church of Talmont (12th century), Romanesque Saintonge style, stands at the cliff edge. Its apse overlooks the estuary – the sea has eaten away the cemetery. Sculpted facade, single nave, golden light. One of the most photographed churches in France.

The carrelets of Talmont, fishing huts on stilts equipped with square nets. A traditional estuary technique for catching shrimp, eels and mullet. Some can be rented overnight – a unique sunset experience.

activities.text: Talmont market (summer). In the village lanes, local producers and artisans. Pineau, cognac, jams, soaps. Festive atmosphere on summer evenings with concerts on the square.
Talmont cliff trail. 3 km walk along the estuary, between carrelets and vineyards. View of the Meschers cliffs and their troglodyte caves on the other side of the Gironde.

days_plan.itin_title: Charente-Maritime Road Trip: La Rochelle, Île de Ré, Fort Boyard and Roman Saintonge
Nouvelle-Aquitaine Loop: Medieval Villages, Vineyards & Dordogne Valley

Calvary panorama.

days_plan.itin_title: Nièvre, France: Morvan Hills and Ducal Castle Heritage Routes

visits.text: The Château du Roi René is one of France's best-preserved medieval castles — and one of the least frequented for a monument of this quality. Built between 1400 and 1434 by the Dukes of Anjou on rock at the Rhône's edge, its 4-meter-thick walls rise 45 meters. René d'Anjou, heir to the County of Provence, furnished it as a pleasure residence between 1447 and 1449. Until 1481, it controlled the Rhône's political border — the opposite bank, Beaucaire, was in the Kingdom of France. The terrace offers exceptional panoramas over the river, Alpilles, and Montagnette.

The Tarasque is Tarascon's legendary monster — a half-ox, half-crocodile dragon that terrorized Rhône boatmen before Saint Martha, who came from Palestine after Christ's death, captured it with her rosary and holy water. The legend, recounted in the Golden Legend (circa 1260), explains the town's very name. The Tarasque festival, created by King René in 1469 and inscribed on UNESCO's Intangible Heritage list, is held annually in June. The crypt of the Sainte-Marthe collegiate church (11th century) preserves the saint's tomb.

Tarascon's old center deserves an hour of wandering: its streets compress 17th-18th century townhouses and medieval houses side by side. The town is also the birthplace of Tartarin, the boastful character invented by Alphonse Daudet — a house in his image has existed since 1985 at 55 Boulevard Itam.

activities.text: Walk along the Rhône banks and view of Beaucaire: from the quay along the château, the suspension bridge allows reaching Beaucaire (Gard department) on foot. From the opposite bank, the view of the Château du Roi René's facade perched on its rock at river's edge is striking. Return by the same bridge. Allow 1h30.

Circuit of Tarascon's ramparts and medieval gates: the town preserves several vestiges of its medieval fortifications — gates including the Porte du Rhône and sections of ramparts integrated into dwelling facades. A 45-minute walking circuit from the tourist office.

days_plan.itin_title: Alpilles, Aix-en-Provence & Camargue Circuit

visits.text: Count's castle.
Cathar castle (emblematic site).

activities.text: Cathar history.
Monday market.

days_plan.itin_title: Ariège Pyrenees Road Trip, France: Cathar Castles & Prehistoric Cave Art
Ariège Circuit: Cathar Castles, Prehistoric Caves & Mountain Lakes

visits.text: The Jardin Massey, classified as a "Remarkable Garden," spans eleven hectares in the city center. Designed in the 19th century by botanist Placide Massey, former director of the Trianon nurseries in Versailles, it features centuries-old trees, an orangery, free-roaming peacocks, and the remains of the cloister from the Saint-Sever-de-Rustan abbey, whose 15th-century capitals are classified as a Historic Monument.

The Musée Massey occupies the former residence of the botanist, recognizable by its Moorish tower offering a panorama over the Pyrenees mountain chain. On the first level, the International Hussars Collection brings together more than 15,000 objects tracing four centuries of history of this elite cavalry, from the 16th to the 20th century, across thirty countries.

The second level of the museum presents a fine arts collection assembled in the 19th century by deputy Achille Jubinal. It features works from the Italian, Flemish, Dutch, and French schools from the 15th to the 20th century, with paintings by Girolamo del Pacchia, Frans II Francken, Baron Gérard, and Jean-Léon Gérôme.

The Birthplace of Marshal Foch, at 2 rue de la Victoire, is an 18th-century Bigourdan house classified as a Historic Monument. Its exterior gallery with balusters, marble-framed windows, and carved wooden staircase exemplify local architecture. Ferdinand Foch spent his first twelve years here before becoming Marshal of France, Great Britain, and Poland.

The Cathedral of Notre-Dame de la Sède is the oldest monument in Tarbes. Largely remaining Romanesque, it blends Romanesque and Gothic styles. Inside, the Baroque high altar combines local green and red marble, modeled after that of Saint Peter's in Rome. You can also discover Louis XVI's will engraved on a black marble plaque.

The Haras de Tarbes, created by decree of Napoleon I in 1806, occupies eight hectares in the heart of the city. Its Empire-style stables, collection of horse-drawn carriages, ceremonial saddlery, and the Maison du Cheval trace the history of breeding the Anglo-Arab horse, the emblematic breed of the Hautes-Pyrénées born here from the crossbreeding of local mares and oriental stallions.

activities.text: Stroll through the shaded pathways of Jardin Massey to observe the peacocks, then climb to admire the Pyrenean panorama from the museum's Moorish tower.
Tour the rooms of the Musée Massey, taking time to examine the ornate Hussar uniforms, reconstructed on 130 mannequins representing 30 different nations.
Follow the heritage trail marked by studs in the ground through the historic quarter: from the Foch House to the cathedral, then toward the stud farm, each stop reveals a chapter of Tarbes history.
Attend a guided tour of the Haras to access the stables and see the horses at work - on certain days, the farrier still strikes iron in his forge.

days_plan.itin_title: Hautes-Pyrénées Circuit: From Tarbes to Cirques and Valleys

visits.text: The Musée des Merveilles is the essential gateway to the Vallée des Merveilles: designed by architect Jean-Michel Wilmotte, it presents archaeological collections across 1,200 m², with casts and photographs of rock carvings from Mont Bégo dating to the Bronze Age (2,000 to 1,500 BC). Over 50,000 carvings dot the massif's slopes — a unique open-air museum in Europe. If you plan to hike to the carvings, get information here on access conditions.

The medieval village of Tende climbs in an amphitheater up the hill: 4-to-6-story houses in local green schist, topped with flagstone roofs, squeeze into cobbled lanes. A former major stop on the Salt Road between Nice and Piedmont, Tende was only attached to France in 1947 — Italian identity remains very present in architecture and cuisine.

The Collégiale Notre-Dame de l'Assomption blends an 11th-century Romanesque bell tower with a 17th-century Baroque interior: this mix well illustrates Tende's history, a cultural crossroads between Ligurian, Piedmontese and French influences. The building's richness testifies to the town's past prosperity on the Salt Road.

Climb to the ruins of Château des Lascaris and the Tour de l'Horloge to overlook the village: the panorama embraces the Roya valley up to Mercantour peaks exceeding 3,000 meters. Ideal in late afternoon when golden light illuminates the flagstone roofs.

activities.text: The Vallée des Merveilles is the unmissable excursion from Tende: over 50,000 Bronze Age rock carvings cover glacier-polished rocks around Mont Bégo. Independent access is free but an approved guide is strongly recommended to spot the carvings. Allow a full day for the hike from Castérino. Snow can make the site inaccessible before July.

Reach the hamlet of Castérino by car from Tende: this small hamlet at 1,550 m altitude is the starting point for hikes toward the Vallée des Merveilles and Vallée de Fontanalba. Along the way, watch for chamois, marmots and ibex that frequent the alpine pastures.

The Vallée de Fontanalba offers an alternative to the Vallée des Merveilles with its own rock carvings and spectacular alpine lake landscape. The hike is more accessible and less crowded. Local guides offer themed outings focused on alpine botany or wildlife.

Board the Train des Merveilles from Tende station: vertiginous tunnels, viaducts suspended above gorges, one of France's most spectacular railway lines built in 1928. The Nice-Tende-Cuneo line crosses alpine landscapes otherwise inaccessible.

days_plan.itin_title: Alpes-Maritimes Road Trip: From the French Riviera to Perched Villages
Nice Hinterland Circuit: Perched Villages and Mountain Trails

visits.text: The Collegiate Church of Saint-Thiébaut is Thann's Gothic jewel and one of the most representative edifices of Rhenish Gothic in Alsace. Its construction lasted over two centuries, from the late 13th century until 1516. The people of Thann like to say: 'Strasbourg has the tallest, Freiburg the biggest, Thann the most beautiful!' — speaking of the bell tower. The stone-lace spire rises to 78 meters. The west portal is carved with three tympana featuring over 500 figures: the Life of the Virgin, the Nativity, and the Crucifixion. Inside, don't miss the 15th-century choir stalls or the stained glass from the same period.

The Witch's Eye watches over Thann from the ruins of Engelbourg castle. In 1673, Louis XIV ordered this fortress dismantled — miners from Giromagny set fire to explosive charges. After three attempts, the great tower broke and a section lifted vertically, forming this cyclopean fragment of stone that resembles an eye scrutinizing the plain. The climb from lower Thann takes twenty minutes and offers a 360° view over the Thur valley, the Rangen vineyard, and the Vosges ridges.

Stroll through Thann's old town among 16th-century houses, their oriels and fountains. The place Saint-Thiébaut at the foot of the collegiate church is the heart of local life. The Museum of the Friends of Thann, installed in a former grain hall from 1519, gathers objects and archives on the town's history and the vineyard.

activities.text: The Rangen vineyard is Alsace's southernmost and steepest, on volcanic slopes facing full south between 350 and 450 meters elevation. It's the only vineyard in Alsace entirely classified as Grand Cru — Riesling and Gewurztraminer acquire a unique mineral character from the basaltic soil. Operators offer guided tours of the vineyard. It's also the southern terminus of the Alsace Wine Route running 170 kilometers from Marlenheim.

Climb to Engelbourg castle via the trail starting from the Bungert parking lot in Thann. Cross the bridge over the Thur, take rue du Rangen for 150 meters then first right. The climb takes thirty minutes and the ruins site is freely accessible. The panoramic view from the Witch's Eye over Thann, the ridges, and the plain amply rewards the effort.

days_plan.itin_title: Alsace's Most Beautiful Christmas Markets
Upper Rhine Road Trip: Colmar and the Southern Wine Route

visits.text: Thiers, France's cutlery capital for over six centuries, clings its carved half-timbered houses and private mansions to a rocky spur facing the Chaîne des Puys. The protected sector runs from rue de la Coutellerie to the Saint-Jean quarter via covered passages called pedde, typical of this vertical city. Historic cutlers' shops and artisan workshops dot the route. Today, 2,000 jobs and 80% of French knife consumption still originate from Thiers.

The Cutlery Museum, the former Auvergne region's fourth most visited museum, unfolds across three sites: the Maison de l'Homme des Bois, a classified medieval building, and the Maison des Consuls downtown trace seven centuries of cutlery through objects, demonstrations, films, and sound-and-light shows. The third site, the Vallée des Rouets, is visited two miles upstream in the Durolle gorges.

The Vallée des Rouets, rated two stars in the Michelin Green Guide as 'worth a detour,' preserves the last functioning rouet: this grinding mill allowed blade polishers to sharpen knives lying belly-down over the wheel, earning them the nickname Yellow Bellies. The hiking trail along the Durolle gorges crosses wild natural landscapes punctuated by industrial ruins and the Chez Lyonnet museum.

Le Creux de l'Enfer, a contemporary art center of national interest opened in 1988, occupies a former cutlery factory closed in 1956 in the Durolle gorges. Recognizable by the red devil painted on its facade, this space marries industrial heritage and contemporary creation in a spectacular setting of cliffs and waterfalls. The surrounding Vallée des Usines preserves former cutlery ruins along the river, including the Maison de l'Aventure Industrielle in the May factory.

activities.text: Workshop: build your own knife at the Cutlery Museum. Participants assemble their own Le Thiers® knife, a protected brand recognizable by the T logo engraved into the blade. A unique artisanal souvenir from France's knife capital.

Hike From Hell to the Wheels, a 7.5-mile loop connecting the Vallée des Usines to the Vallée des Rouets along the Durolle gorges. The interpretive trail reveals secrets of papermakers, tanners, and cutlers who harnessed the river's hydraulic power for centuries.

Discover Le Thiers® knives in the town's workshops and boutiques. This protected brand encompasses pocket, table, and kitchen knives, each piece engraved with the maker's name. The Coutellia festival in May gathers over 230 art cutlers and manufacturers worldwide across 27,000 square feet of exhibition.

days_plan.itin_title: VOLCANOES, ROMANESQUE STONE AND AUVERGNE TRADITIONS

visits.text: Thines is this itinerary's most isolated village—and one of the most remarkable. Perched at 550 meters altitude on a rocky promontory above the Thines river, the hamlet is accessible only by an old narrow, winding track. The brown schist houses are covered with schist lauzes—stone slabs whose weight requires particularly robust framing. The ensemble is one with the rocky ground from which they're drawn. Thines was inscribed as a UNESCO Geosite in 2022.

The church of Notre-Dame de Thines is a Romanesque art masterpiece. Built by Benedictines between 1170 and 1190 in polychrome sandstone—a mix of schist, grey granite and red sandstone—it was classified as a Monument Historique by Prosper Mérimée himself in 1848. Its monumental portal, carved capitals and facade polychromy are exceptional for a building of this size. Climb the imposing staircase leading to the porch and observe the carved lintel: you read Christ's arrest with remarkable narrative freshness. The column statues were partially mutilated during 16th-century Wars of Religion, which paradoxically accentuates their presence.

The Maison du Gerboul, at the hamlet base, is the territory interpretation center—documentaries, regional books and local crafts are available. This is where the Rocher de Thines interpretation trail (2 km, 10 thematic panels) starts, guiding geological, architectural and historical reading of the site. The terrace with panoramic views over the Thines valley is ideal for a break.

activities.text: The balcony trail above the thalweg (3 to 5 km from the hamlet, 150 m elevation gain) follows cliffs and offers plunging viewpoints over the river and hamlets on the opposite bank. It's on these heights that the Trois Croix pilgrimage is held each year. The path is sometimes narrow and exposed—avoid in rainy weather; schist can be very slippery.

The hamlet circuit and old cultivation terraces around Thines (2 km, 1h) passes through accols—these small strips of land laboriously developed on slopes—and mule tracks that linked hamlets together. The guide "Voyage au Rocher de Thines" from 2024 is available at Maison du Gerboul to deepen the visit.

days_plan.itin_title: The 18 Most Beautiful Villages of Ardèche

visits.text: The Tour aux Puces, a massive 14th-century donjon 30 meters high, dominated the Moselle valley with its 4-meter-thick walls. This remnant of the medieval fortifications houses the Thionville municipal museum, with Gallo-Roman collections and exhibits on 1,000 years of border history. From the top, the panorama extends from Luxembourg to Germany. The tower owes its name to a flea market that was held at its feet.

Vauban's casemates, designed between 1679 and 1687, plunge into 500 meters of underground galleries dug into the rock. These defensive tunnels could accommodate 1,200 soldiers and controlled access to the Luxembourg border. The vaulted chambers, powder magazines, and countermine wells tell the story of 17th-century military engineering. The temperature stays constant at 12°C year-round.

activities.text: Walk along the Moselle riverbanks, redesigned between 2010 and 2015 into green promenades. The riverside path connects the medieval Cloister Park with contemporary gardens, passing by the 18th-century Château de Guentrange and its French garden. Plane trees, rose gardens, and playgrounds line the 3 km of walkways. In summer, outdoor cafes and river shuttle boats enliven the quays.

Explore the historic center where the Saint-Maximin church (12th-16th centuries) displays a flamboyant Gothic facade topped by a 54-meter bell tower. The pedestrian streets of the old town preserve 18th and 19th-century bourgeois houses with colored facades. The Place du Marché, bordered by 18th-century arcades, hosts a market on Wednesday and Saturday mornings, perpetuating a 700-year tradition.

days_plan.itin_title: Moselle Circuit 10 Days: Metz, Bitche Citadel and Chagall Stained Glass

visits.text: Discovery of Lake Coiselet, on the border of Ain and Jura
Final walk through the gorges of the Ain and arrival in Thoirette

activities.text: Observation of Jura landscapes and photo stop on the heights of the Ain valley
Free return or continuation of the journey to the sources of the Doubs

days_plan.itin_title: Jura Lakes & Mountains: Turquoise Waters, Waterfalls & Alpine Cheese Routes

visits.text: The Château de Thoiry, built in 1559, functions as an architectural sundial: at the solstices, sunlight passes straight through the entrance hall. Its period-furnished salons complement the wildlife park.
Thoiry's botanical gardens cover 95 hectares labeled "Remarkable Garden." Formal gardens attributed to Le Nôtre, English gardens, and a rose garden compose a varied landscape ensemble.

activities.text: Drive through the 8-kilometer safari amidst the African reserve: lions, bears, elephants, and giraffes roam in semi-freedom. Allow 45 minutes to an hour.
Explore the zoo on foot: over 1,300 animals of 150 species spread across themed enclosures. The tunnel beneath the lion enclosure offers a thrilling viewpoint.
In winter, the Thoiry Lumières Sauvages festival illuminates the park with thousands of giant lanterns depicting animals and landscapes.

days_plan.itin_title: Yvelines Road Trip: Versailles and Impressionist Villages

visits.text: This spectacular Alpine valley stretches between the Bornes massif and the Aravis chain. Limestone peaks soaring beyond 2,700 meters form a dramatic natural amphitheater—raw Alpine grandeur at its finest.

As the Reblochon capital, Thônes has preserved its traditional Savoyard market town character. Wander the historic center's narrow lanes and visit the 18th-century baroque Church of Saint-Hippolyte with its ornate interior.
This small neo-Gothic chapel from 1861 stands in magnificent Alpine surroundings at the hamlet of Les Confins. It marks the trailhead for numerous mountain hikes into pristine wilderness.

activities.text: Hike from Les Confins hamlet to this charming mountain lake nestled at 1,430 meters. The easy 2 km trail meanders through flower-studded meadows and spruce forests—quintessential Alpine scenery without the crowds.
The Lake Annecy Golf Club offers an exceptional 18-hole course with stunning views across the lake to the mountains—where the challenge of the game meets breathtaking Alpine beauty.

days_plan.itin_title: Haute-Savoie Road Trip: Alpine Lakes & Mont Blanc

places.name: Thorenc

visits.text: The Monts d'Azur Biological Reserve offers an experience unmatched anywhere in France: 700 hectares on a plateau at 1,200 meters altitude, where European bison, Przewalski's horses, elk, deer, and wild boar roam in complete freedom. This project was launched in 2006 by veterinarian Patrice Longour, inspired by African reserves—he wanted to recreate on this plateau the "guild" of large European herbivores as it existed several millennia ago. Over 500 species coexist here, including some twenty protected by international conventions. The reserve is 40 minutes from Grasse and 1 hour from Nice—a total surprise for anyone arriving from the coast.

The village of Thorenc itself is a quiet mountain hamlet at 1,250 meters altitude, typical of the Grasse backcountry. Take advantage of your visit to walk along the meadows and forests surrounding the plateau—the contrast with the Côte d'Azur an hour's drive away is striking. In autumn, the deer's roar echoes through the valley.

activities.text: The guided walking safari (1h45) or horse-drawn carriage tour (1h15) is mandatory to enter the reserve: booking is essential as visitor numbers are strictly limited to avoid disturbing the animals. In autumn, the rutting safari lasts 2.5 hours and takes place in the evening—males answer each other across the forest, ears pricked. A naturalist guide accompanies each group. Bring warm clothing even in summer—at 1,200 m, temperatures remain cool.

Eco-lodges are available within the reserve for total immersion nights: you observe animals at dusk and dawn from your accommodation, right in the middle of bison territory. It's one of the Alpes-Maritimes' most unusual experiences—book well in advance, spots are rare.

days_plan.itin_title: Nice Hinterland Circuit: Perched Villages and Mountain Trails
Alpine Hinterland and Verdon Gorges: From Thorenc to Riez

map_keywords: Réserve biologique des Monts d'Azur Thorenc

visits.text: The castle of the Dukes of La Trémoille has dominated the rocky promontory since the 8th century. Jacques Lemercier redesigned it in the 17th century for Marie de La Tour d'Auvergne.
The Collegiate Church of Notre-Dame was built by Gabrielle de Bourbon-Montpensier from 1503 to 1515 in Flamboyant Gothic style with Renaissance influences.
The Church of Saint-Médard preserves its 12th-century Romanesque façade and sculpted portals. The 56-meter Gothic bell tower dominates the town.
The half-timbered houses of Place Saint-Médard and Rue du Château include the 15th-century Hôtel des Trois Rois.

activities.text: Descend toward the collegiate chapel via Rue Imbert and Rue du Château where small houses and mansions stand side by side.
Contemplate the breathtaking panorama over the Thouet River and the Pont des Chouans from the heights.
Visit the Henri-Barré Museum housed in a mansion in the historic center.

days_plan.itin_title: Deux-Sèvres Circuit: Marais Poitevin and Romanesque Heritage

visits.text: The King's Ladder carves its vertiginous staircase into the basalt flow dominating the Ardèche. The 210 steps suspended above the void descend the black lava cliff.

activities.text: The geological hike reveals the wonders of Ardèche volcanism. The lava flow trail crosses basalt formations, lava columns, and rocky chaos.

days_plan.itin_title: Ardèche Road Trip: Wild Gorges and Heritage Villages

visits.text: Fosse Dionne has intrigued visitors since antiquity. This vauclusian spring forms a turquoise pool whose known gallery plunges 61 meters deep over 360 meters. A circular half-rotunda washhouse, listed as a Historic Monument, has surrounded it since 1758.

The Hôtel-Dieu, founded in 1293 by Marguerite de Bourgogne, is Europe's longest medieval hospital. Its patients' hall stretches 90 meters beneath original timberwork. The 15th-century Entombment sculpture is preserved here.

Climb to Saint-Pierre Church, perched on the hill overlooking Tonnerre. Its architecture blends flamboyant Gothic and Renaissance. The interior houses an antique organ, an 18th-century pulpit, and polychrome stained glass.
Notre-Dame Church, in the town center, presents a mix of Romanesque and Gothic styles. Its stones bear witness to Tonnerre's different construction eras—a town destroyed and rebuilt multiple times.
The medieval-inspired gardens, adjoining the Hôtel-Dieu, feature boxwood hedges, hornbeams, and yews. Medicinal plants are identified according to their period uses.
Ten kilometers away, Château de Tanlay rises from its moat amid a vast park. This Burgundian Renaissance residence houses a trompe-l'oeil room and the League Tower adorned with allegorical frescoes.

activities.text: The Canal de Bourgogne cuts through Tonnerre's heart. Take the towpath on foot or by bike for a waterside stroll past locks and barges.
Tonnerrois winemakers open their cellars for tastings. The AOC Bourgogne Tonnerre and Bourgogne Épineuil reveal a discreet but quality terroir.
On Wednesdays and Saturdays, Tonnerre's market offers snails, gougères, Chaourçois cheese, and Armançonnettes—local sweet specialties.

days_plan.itin_title: Yonne Circuit: Northern Burgundy & Chablis Wine Country
Burgundy Road Trip: Cathedrals, Vineyards & Medieval Villages

visits.text: Cathédrale Saint-Étienne was built between 1221 and 1496. Its flamboyant Gothic facade, erected between 1475 and 1496, ranks among the finest in eastern France.
The cathedral's Gothic cloister measures 54 meters long and 42 wide. It's one of the largest in France.
The cathedral towers rise to 62 meters. The spires planned to reach 90 meters were never built.
The 13th-century Collégiale Saint-Gengoult impresses with its bright interior. Its clear glass windows and 16th-century cloister invite contemplation.
Vauban's fortifications have encircled the town since 1678. Star-shaped bastions, casemates, and glacis testify to the marshal's military genius.

activities.text: Toul's cellars have produced vin gris for centuries. This typically Lorraine rosé can be tasted at estates in the surrounding area.
Walk along the Marne-Rhine Canal that runs through Toul. The banks offer a peaceful stroll at the foot of the ramparts.

days_plan.itin_title: Meurthe-et-Moselle Road Trip: Art Nouveau Nancy & Fortified Cities

places.name: Toulon

visits.text: Behind the monumental 18th-century gateway, the National Maritime Museum showcases ship models, galleys, and paintings tracing four centuries of naval tradition. The museum reopened with immersive scenography, including a 3D reconstruction of the Arroyo shipwreck that brings maritime archaeology to life.

Board the cable car for a six-minute ride and 378-meter ascent to the summit of Mont Faron. At the top, the Memorial Museum of the Provence Landing recounts the August 1944 operation through period artifacts and multimedia exhibits that honor the liberation forces.

Notre-Dame de la Seds Cathedral, founded in the 5th century, blends Romanesque and Gothic elements with a unified Gothic nave, a bell tower crowned by a campanile, and an 18th-century organ. The side chapels house the Chapel of the Virgin and the Corpus Domini chapel.

Built in 1524 on Louis XII's orders and classified as a historic monument since 1947, the Royal Tower rises with seven-meter-thick walls and moats—a rare example of Italian 'torrione' fortification. It hosts cultural events in summer against a backdrop of Mediterranean vistas.

The Toulon Opera House, erected over 150 years ago on Place Victor Hugo, seats up to 1,100 spectators. Its classical facade and renowned acoustics make it one of the jewels of the old town, hosting everything from opera to ballet.

Step into the Hôtel des Arts TPM, a 400 m² space dedicated to contemporary art. Rotating exhibitions alternate between paintings, sculptures, and installations from emerging and established artists. Admission is free.

activities.text: Wander through the stalls of Cours Lafayette market, open Tuesday through Sunday. Herbs de Provence, olives, soaps, and fresh produce line the stands in a quintessential Provençal market atmosphere buzzing with local color.

Embark on a narrated one-hour boat tour of Toulon's harbor. You'll discover military vessels at anchor, fortifications guarding the bay, mussel farms in Lazaret Bay, and the haunting naval cemetery—a floating journey through maritime history.

Head to the Mourillon beaches, bordered by landscaped gardens, lawns, and playgrounds. Beyond, the coastal path leads to the wild coves of Méjean and Magaud—secluded inlets at the foot of pine-studded cliffs where the Mediterranean feels gloriously untamed.

days_plan.itin_title: Var Road Trip: French Riviera & Green Provence

map_keywords: Musée national de la Marine Toulon
Cathédrale Notre-Dame de la Seds Toulon

visits.text: Place du Capitole has been Toulouse's beating heart since the 12th century. Its 135-meter neoclassical façade houses both city hall and the theater. The Occitan cross inlaid in marble on the ground celebrates the region's distinct identity. Step inside the Capitole for free to discover the Salle des Illustres, where monumental frescoes chronicle Toulouse's storied past across soaring walls.

Basilique Saint-Sernin stands as the largest intact Romanesque church in all of Europe. Built between the 11th and 13th centuries, this UNESCO World Heritage site served pilgrims walking the Way of Saint James. Its octagonal bell tower soars 64 meters above the city. Inside, carved capitals and a vast ambulatory still echo with the footsteps of medieval travelers.

The Couvent des Jacobins dates from the 13th century. Its southern Gothic architecture culminates in the Palm of Stone—a 28-meter column whose ribbed vaults fan out like a tree beneath the ceiling, one of medieval architecture's most stunning achievements. The peaceful cloister invites contemplation. Between 11am and 3pm, sunlight streams through stained glass, illuminating painted walls in shifting colors.

La Cité de l'Espace plunges you into the universe of space exploration. A full-scale Ariane 5 rocket commands attention at the entrance. Inside, the Planetarium and IMAX theater transport you to the heart of the cosmos. Interactive exhibits explain actual space missions and let you touch real meteorites brought back from beyond Earth.

Hôtel d'Assézat ranks among Toulouse's most magnificent Renaissance mansions. Built in 1555 for a merchant enriched by the woad trade, its interior courtyard showcases exquisite Renaissance decoration. The building now houses the Bemberg Foundation, displaying old master paintings alongside modern works.

activities.text: The quays along the Garonne make for a lovely riverside walk. The 17th-century Pont Neuf is Toulouse's oldest bridge. At night, it lights up beautifully, and the view from the quays becomes magical as pink brick buildings reflect in the water. Walk or cycle while watching the city glow.

Marché Victor Hugo opens every morning except Monday. This covered market is a temple of Toulouse gastronomy where the region's finest producers gather. You'll find foie gras, cassoulet, local wines, and artisan cheeses. Upstairs, restaurants let you taste everything on the spot.

Try cassoulet, THE dish of Toulouse. This slow-cooked ragout of white beans, duck confit, and Toulouse sausages simmers for hours. It's a hearty celebration of Gascon tradition. Restaurants throughout the center offer their own recipes, each slightly different. Pair it with a Fronton or Gaillac wine.

days_plan.itin_title: Haute-Garonne Road Trip: From Toulouse to the Pyrenees

visits.text: Tournemire stretches along a volcanic ridge above the Doire valley, one of thirteen valleys radiating from Puy Mary. The village — one of France's Most Beautiful Villages — centres on its 12th-century Romanesque church, which preserves a relic of the Holy Thorn brought back from the Crusades by a local lord. At the far end of the village, Château d'Anjony rises from its basalt mound with four slender towers reaching 40 meters high. Built around 1430 by Louis d'Anjony, companion-in-arms of Joan of Arc and charged by Charles VII with restoring order in Haute-Auvergne, it has remained in the same family for six centuries. To reach the entrance, you walk through the village from the car park (15 minutes) — factor this into your timing.

The château's interior is worth the visit: the Hall of the Nine Worthies frescoes (around 1575) depict nine legendary knights — Charlemagne, King Arthur, Alexander the Great — life-size on their horses. They were rediscovered in the early 20th century behind 18th-century panelling. The chapel, meanwhile, is decorated with 16th-century Passion scenes. This castle also has a vendetta story worthy of a novel: the Anjony and Tournemire families, dangerously close neighbours, feuded bitterly for two centuries. In 1523, Claude d'Anjony was assassinated by the Tournemires; in 1623, a duel in front of the church left three dead on each side. The visit is guided, approximately 60 minutes.

activities.text: The viewpoint loop above the Doire (3–4 km, ~1h30) offers sweeping views of the Cantal mountains and pastoral valleys. From the ridges, you can see the basalt organ pipes of neighbouring rocks and, on clear days, Puy Mary. Proper walking shoes essential — paths can be slippery after rain.

Study the houses of Tournemire in detail: slate roofs, dark andesite window frames, carved lintels. This perfectly preserved traditional Cantal architecture represents an open-air manual of volcanic construction. The village is small, but every façade tells a story of craft, century, and family name.

days_plan.itin_title: Auvergne's Volcanic Villages Trail

visits.text: Saint-Philibert Abbey (Romanesque).
Greuze Museum.
Hôtel-Dieu.

The Abbey of Saint-Philibert in Tournus, founded in the 9th century, is one of the oldest Romanesque structures in France. Its nave, vaulted with transverse barrel vaults—a unique arrangement in Burgundy—bathes in rosy light filtering through high windows. Monks brought the relics of Saint Philibert here while fleeing Vikings from Noirmoutier.

The crypt of Saint-Philibert, beneath the choir, preserves pillars and capitals from the 10th century among the oldest in Burgundy. The atmosphere is contemplative, among the squat columns and vestiges of the Carolingian edifice.

The historic center of Tournus stretches between the abbey and the Saône. Medieval streets, townhouses, and half-timbered shops compose a preserved urban ensemble. The Musée Greuze, dedicated to the painter born here in 1725, displays his canvases alongside archaeological collections.

The quays along the Saône offer a peaceful riverside walk. Barges ascend the current toward Chalon and Lyon. The bridge spans the Saône with views of the abbey and its bell tower mirrored in the water.

activities.text: Saône quays.
Old town.
Markets.

Visit to the Abbey of Saint-Philibert. The nave with transverse barrel vaults, the two-story narthex, the Chapel of Saint-Michel, and the crypt compose a unique architectural journey. Self-guided or audio-guided visit.
Tasting at a Tournusian bouchon. The town is renowned for its Burgundian gastronomy: Bresse chicken, snails, gougères, and crème de cassis accompany wines from the Côte Chalonnaise.
Bike path along the Saône. The greenway follows the river between Tournus and Mâcon on a flat, shaded path. Bocage landscapes, stone villages, and barges punctuate the route.

days_plan.itin_title: Saône Valley, France: Medieval Abbeys, Wine and Comtois Heritage
Saône-et-Loire Road Trip: Bibracte, Cluny, Solutré & the Mâconnais Vineyards

visits.text: Tourrette-Levens crowns a rocky spur just 12 km from Nice, commanding the Paillon valley below. This fortified medieval village served as a strategic lookout post since the Middle Ages.

The 12th-century castle, remodeled in the 17th century, towers over the village. Its vaulted halls house the butterfly and natural history museum, one of France's most important collections with 5,000 specimens.
The triangular bell tower of Saint-Rosalie church is a rare architectural curiosity in Provence. This unusual shape dates from the 17th century.
The prehistory museum displays excavations from the Merle cave, revealing human occupation since the Paleolithic era with flint tools and prehistoric animal bones.
The traditional crafts museum recreates the workshops of blacksmiths, cobblers, and coopers in the castle's lower halls.
Cobblestone alleyways wind between medieval houses with carved lintels and vaulted passages. The Clock Gate marks the entrance to the old fortified town.

activities.text: Climb to the castle belvedere for sweeping views between sea and mountains, from Cap Ferrat to the snow-capped peaks of Mercantour.
The Mont Chauve botanical trail leads from the village to this 854-meter summit offering 360° views over Nice, the Mediterranean, and the Alps.
Visit the three free museums within the castle walls: butterflies, prehistory, and traditional crafts, all housed in the medieval fortress.
The rural chapels circuit takes you to discover the oratories and country chapels scattered across the communal territory.
Sample the sweet Swiss chard pie, a Niçoise specialty, at one of the village restaurants with panoramic terraces.

days_plan.itin_title: Road Trip Through the Nice & Grasse Backcountry

visits.text: Tourrettes-sur-Loup, nicknamed the "City of Violets," is a medieval village perched on a rocky spur above two valleys. Pass through one of the two access gates to discover the semicircular Grand Rue: the 15th-century stone houses press against each other as if protecting themselves from the void, their flower-covered facades adorned with geranium planters. Populated since prehistoric times, the village is first mentioned under the name "castrum de Torretis" in the 11th century. The current 15th-century castle, built by the Villeneuve family, now houses the town hall.

The Bastide aux Violettes is both museum and working farm: here you discover the history of the Victoria violet, cultivated in Tourrettes since the 1880s to supply Grasse perfumeries. The last three producer families still grow organically on terraced plots. The visit is open year-round, but come from November to March to see the flowers in full bloom—their fragrance is delicate, almost ethereal.

From the orientation table below the village, the view embraces the Cassan viaduct, the valleys, and the Mediterranean coastline. The table also indicates the Pre-Alps peaks to the north. Take a few minutes here—it's one of the quietest and least-known viewpoints in the Nice hinterland.

activities.text: The first weekend of March, the Violet Festival transforms the village into a flower parade: this institution born in 1952 on the initiative of Scottish artist Victor Linton draws nearly 10,000 visitors. Floats covered with fresh violets parade through the lanes, the flower battle takes place on Place de la Libération, and the producers' market overflows with violet specialties. If you're here during this period, arrive early—the village entrance quickly becomes congested.

Taste the violet specialties offered in village shops: crystallized flowers coated in sugar, violet syrup, eau de toilette, and perfumed soaps and creams. The crystallized violet flower confection is a fragile and delicate local delicacy—they keep only a few weeks.

The medieval village hosts numerous art craftspeople: potters, ceramists, weavers, jewelers, and sculptors fill the lanes. Tourrettes has always attracted artists—the movement accelerated in the 1950s. The small Saturday morning market on Place du Scourédou is an opportunity to discover local creators.

days_plan.itin_title: Road Trip Through the Nice & Grasse Backcountry
The 15 Jewels of the French Riviera and Its Hinterland
Nice Hinterland Circuit: Perched Villages and Mountain Trails

visits.text: Long drive from Mont-Saint-Michel (3h30). Settle into Tours, gateway to the Loire Valley. Late afternoon, head to Place Plumereau—the heart of old Tours. 15th-16th century half-timbered houses frame a square lined with terraces. In the evening, it's the liveliest spot in town.

Walk to the Saint-Gatien Cathedral, built between the 13th and 16th centuries—it blends Rayonnant and Flamboyant Gothic styles. The stained glass in the rose windows is remarkable. Just next door, the 15th-16th century cloister of La Psalette is a haven of calm.

Head to Chambord this morning (1h from Tours). It's the grandest of the Loire châteaux: 156 meters of façade, 426 rooms, 282 chimneys, 77 staircases. François I launched construction in 1519—he would spend only a handful of weeks there in 32 years of reign. The jewel of the visit: the double-helix staircase, inspired by Leonardo da Vinci. Two people ascend simultaneously without ever crossing paths. Climb to the terraces—the forest of chimneys and turrets inspired Chateaubriand's image of "a woman whose hair the wind has blown about."

After lunch, head to Chenonceau (1h15 from Chambord).
