Vervins preserves remains of its old fortifications. The upper town reveals its strategic past as a border city with traces of ramparts still visible in some streets.
The Thiérache bocage rolls between meadows and hedges. Red brick villages dot this preserved rural landscape where fortified churches dominate the green horizons.
Follow the signposted tourist route of fortified churches on the Pays de Thiérache map. This orange circuit crosses the valleys of Brune and Serre linking the most spectacular buildings.

activities.text: The Thiérache region cultivates dairy farming traditions. Maroilles cheese, emblematic of the terroir, is produced in local farms. Some dairies offer visits and tastings of this strong-smelling cheese with distinctive flavor.
Walk the marked trails connecting fortified churches through the bocage. Several loops allow half-day or full-day hikes through this preserved rural heritage.
Visit craft workshops perpetuating traditional know-how. Local artisans work wood, forge iron and create pottery according to ancestral methods.

days_plan.itin_title: Aisne Circuit: Gothic Cathedrals and Picardy Nature

visits.text: The Verzenay lighthouse rises 25 meters above an ocean of vines. Built in 1909 as a champagne marketing stunt, it now houses the Museum of Vines.
Climb 101 steps to the top. The viewing platform offers 360-degree views across the Montagne de Reims and its rolling sea of vineyards.
The Verzenay windmill watches over the slopes. Owned by Maison Mumm, it stands guard above grand cru Pinot Noir parcels.

activities.text: Hike trails winding through the vineyards. These UNESCO-listed slopes reveal centuries of viticultural craftsmanship.
Visit the lighthouse tasting room, where local winemakers pour their cuvées with vineyard views stretching to the horizon.

days_plan.itin_title: Marne Road Trip: Reims, Épernay and the Champagne Route

Saône Valley.

Saint-Aubin-sur-Mer (Calvados) — seafront & beach.

activities.text: Coastal walk (Côte d'Albâtre).

days_plan.itin_title: Normandy's Classic Coast: Étretat Cliffs and Bocage Countryside Villages

visits.text: Climb the Grand'rue up to the Basilica of Sainte-Marie-Madeleine, a UNESCO World Heritage site. Built in the 12th century, it was the starting point for the Second Crusade preached by Bernard of Clairvaux in 1146 before Louis VII and an immense crowd. The narthex and its sculpted capitals—including the celebrated tympanum of the Last Judgment—are among the finest achievements of Romanesque art. Viollet-le-Duc restored it in the 19th century when it threatened to collapse.

From the esplanade behind the basilica, the panorama embraces the Morvan and the Cure valley. This is the "eternal hill"—one of four starting points for the Way of Saint James in France. Pilgrims still depart from here every spring. If you arrive late afternoon, the light on the nave is spectacular: at summer solstice, the patches of light from the stained glass windows align perfectly down the center of the nave.

Walk back down through the ramparts and ancient gates. The golden stone houses along the Grand'rue shelter art galleries, craftsmen's workshops, and bookshops. Vézelay has 400 inhabitants but attracts nearly a million visitors annually—arrive early morning or late afternoon to appreciate it in peace.

activities.text: Walk down to Saint-Père-sous-Vézelay (2 km below). The village has a beautiful 13th-century Gothic church and old washhouses by the Cure river. The marked trail connecting the two villages crosses through vineyards and offers lovely perspectives on the hill.

Take the loop of viewpoints around the hill (marked trail, 1.5 hours). You'll pass through sunken lanes bordered with boxwood, viewpoints over the valley, and remnants of old vineyards. This circuit partly follows pilgrimage routes—you might encounter pilgrims with their scallop shells.

days_plan.itin_title: Nièvre, France: Morvan Hills and Ducal Castle Heritage Routes
Yonne Circuit: Northern Burgundy & Chablis Wine Country
Burgundy's 15 Most Beautiful Villages
Burgundy Road Trip: Cathedrals, Vineyards & Medieval Villages

visits.text: The old town of Vic-sur-Cère nestles against the east slope of the valley at 680 metres altitude, sheltered by a spectacular rocky and wooded chaos. Built of lava stone and roofed with schist slabs, this spa village retains the authentic charm of Cantal mountain towns. A renowned climatic resort, it offers a green setting ideal for recharging.

The Rocher des Pendus towers over Vic-sur-Cère and offers an exceptional viewpoint over the upper valley. The nearby Puy Griou rises before the Élancèze and the Peyre-Arse in a grandiose panorama over the Cantal summits. This perspective reveals the full majesty of the volcanic cirque.

activities.text: The Cascades du Pas de Cère tumble through a spectacular canyon where tumultuous water has carved the volcanic rock. The fitted trail follows the gorges in a cool, green atmosphere, revealing successive waterfalls in an impressive mineral setting. The roar of water echoes between the basaltic walls.

The Pas de Cère canyon offers thrill-seekers a canyoning descent through the wild gorges. Jumps, natural slides and rappels follow one another in the riverbed between vertical walls of black lava. Local outfitters supervise this spectacular aquatic activity.

days_plan.itin_title: Cantal Road Trip: Auvergne Volcanoes and Mountain Traditions

visits.text: The Georges de La Tour museum, inaugurated in 2003 in a contemporary building, pays tribute to the master of chiaroscuro born in Vic in 1593. The permanent exhibition presents a collection of 17th-century paintings, contextualizing La Tour's work within the Baroque movement. Temporary exhibitions explore influences and contemporaries. Didactic multimedia spaces explain the painter's revolutionary technique: controlled light, mystical atmospheres, and humble models treated with nobility.

The Saint-Marien collegiate church from the 13th-15th centuries preserves Renaissance stained glass windows from the 16th century and remarkable liturgical furniture: 18th-century stalls, 1749 Wegmann organ, and baroque paintings. Its squat 50-meter bell tower dominates the mechanical tile roofs of the village, a sandstone sentinel watching over the former salt marshes that made Vic's fortune from the 11th to 17th century with 27 salt works producing 60,000 tons of salt per year.

activities.text: Discover the history of salt in the Saulnois region, land of 400 salt springs exploited since Gallic then Gallo-Roman times. The salt marshes of Vic, Moyenvic, and Marsal produced 'white gold' in the Middle Ages by solar evaporation in lead pans. A little-known heritage that shaped the Lorraine economy until the 18th century, controlled by the bishops of Metz who drew immense revenues from it. The flat country of shimmering ponds as far as the eye can see preserves this unique landscape.

Stroll through the streets of Vic-sur-Seille, a characterful village of 1,200 inhabitants with houses of pink Vosges sandstone. Ancient salt works including Gallic briquetages, 16th-17th-century Renaissance mansions, sandstone fountains, and wash houses tell the story of the prosperity of a city that exported its 'white gold' by river to Nancy, Metz, and even Burgundy. Partially preserved medieval ramparts.

days_plan.itin_title: Moselle Circuit 10 Days: Metz, Bitche Citadel and Chagall Stained Glass

visits.text: The Hall des Sources (1903) displays its Art Nouveau metal structure, a true vessel of glass and steel designed by Charles Le Cœur. Its ironwork signed by Émile Robert reproduces the park's thistles, while five thermal springs gush forth: Célestins, Lucas, Hôpital, Chomel and Grande Grille. Mineral water can be tasted free of charge at the bars installed in this luminous hall.

The Vichy Opera (inaugurated in 1903) houses an Art Nouveau hall with 1486 seats decorated by Léon Rudnicki. Inside the dome, the faces of famous artists like Sarah Bernhardt adorn the ceiling. This Congress Palace-Opera, classified as UNESCO World Heritage, combines today historic and contemporary spaces in a unique setting in France.

The Thermes des Dômes, recognizable by their neo-Moorish dome, rank Vichy among major thermal resorts. Built between 1899 and 1903, these first-class thermal baths offer 46 treatment cabins in a preserved Belle Époque decor. The waters rich in sodium bicarbonate have treated digestive ailments and rheumatism for 2000 years.

The Parc des Sources extends around the eponymous Hall, created in 1812 by decree of Napoleon I. Its wrought-iron covered galleries (1899-1903) connect the park to the Congress Palace-Opera. The 700-meter-long promenades allow strolling in this green lung where thermal architecture dialogues with nature.

The Napoleon III Park (1861) extends its 7.8 hectares of English gardens along a 2.5 km dike protecting from Allier floods. Rare and exotic species (cedars, sequoias, Judas trees) populate this romantic haven. The imperial chalets in colonial style, built between 1863 and 1864, testify to Napoleon III's stays who made Vichy the queen of spa towns.

activities.text: The Grand Café Casino, nestled in the Parc des Sources near the Opera, invites you to try your luck at poker, roulette or one of the 100 slot machines. This Belle Époque establishment perpetuates the playful tradition of the thermal resort, attracting spa guests and visitors in a festive and friendly atmosphere.

The thermal quarter reveals its Art Nouveau facades and covered passages with film-like allure. Rue Alquié aligns its English-style houses, Rue de Belgique exhibits its baroque facades, while the Napoleon III chalets of colonial inspiration recall the splendor of the Second Empire. Vichy architecture offers an assumed bourgeois side, legacy of the thermal golden age.

The Allier riverbanks invite strolling along the artificial lake created in 1963. Plage des Célestins, guinguettes and green spaces offer relaxation and leisure by the water. The promenade continues on 4 km of developed cycle paths, perfect for enjoying the golden hour by the river.

days_plan.itin_title: Allier Road Trip: Vichy, Thermal Baths and Bourbonnais Bocage

visits.text: Viens celebrated its millennium in 1990—and the village wears this age well. On the border with the Alpes-de-Haute-Provence, it developed around a fortress built around 990, first mentioned in 1005 under the name "Vegnis." Enter the village through the Porte Sarrasine (also called Tour de la Pousterle), pierced in the ramparts from the 11th to 13th centuries. It allows passage of only one person at a time, and its crown of machicolations was partially destroyed around 1960. The Clock Tower above it, with its square belfry, belongs to the 14th-century enclosure. In the narrow lanes, you'll encounter fine dwellings from the 13th to 16th centuries with mullioned windows and cross-bars. A Rue de la Juiverie recalls that a Jewish community found refuge here in the 14th century.

Viens' Renaissance château (16th century, renovated in the 18th and 19th centuries) now serves as the village town hall. Passing through its portal, you'll discover a magnificent octagonal Renaissance staircase—the most remarkable architectural piece in the village. The town hall opens its doors during the day. The Church of Saint-Hilaire (12th century, remodeled in the 17th) is listed as a Historic Monument for its late-13th-century square bell tower with twin bays and Lombard bands.

activities.text: Tour of the ramparts and viewpoint over the Albion plateau from the Montée de la Pousterle (20 min). The view extends over the Lure gorges and the Luberon massif.

Rural path toward the terraces and fields (short 1h loop). The surroundings of Viens are located on the border of the Alpes-de-Haute-Provence, with lavender fields beginning to appear on the surrounding plateaus.

days_plan.itin_title: The 15 Most Beautiful Villages of the Luberon

visits.text: Port d'Albret marine lake: Created artificially between 1966 and 1975 over 40 hectares, this body of water stretches between Vieux-Boucau and Soustons. The central wooded islet nicknamed Temptation Island remains inaccessible but provides a bird refuge. The name Vieux-Boucau means old mouth, a memory of when the Adour flowed out here before 1578.

Promenade du Mail: Connecting downtown to the lake's outlet, this alley lined with maritime pines features restaurants, bars and ice cream shops. A summery laid-back atmosphere reigns on this pedestrian artery. Unobstructed view of the lake and water activities. Spectacular sunsets from the jetty.

The three beaches: Grand Plage in the town center (urban then wild), Sablères beach to the north (high dunes, naturism tolerated), and the marine lake beach with calm, warm waters. Each beach offers a different atmosphere. Lake waters are 3-4°C warmer than the ocean, ideal for families.

activities.text: Swimming in the marine lake: Lifeguarded beach in season with calm waters maintained at constant level. Pleasant temperature higher than the ocean. Ideal for young children and water sports beginners. Inflatable games installed in summer. Lifeguard station and restrooms on site.

Water activities on the lake: Rental of pedal boats, paddleboards, kayaks and small sailboats. The lake's protected waters allow safe practice for beginners. Sailing lessons for children and adults in July-August. Family-friendly and convivial atmosphere.

Vieux-Boucau markets: Daily morning market except Sunday with local producers. Special evening markets in summer (crafts, clothing). Festive atmosphere with musical entertainment. Landes specialties and fresh products. Set-up from 6pm for evening markets.

Golf de Pinsolle nearby: 18-hole course in a setting of pines and ponds. Accessible to players of all levels. Driving range, pro shop and restaurant on site. Greenfees vary by season. Booking advised in high season.

days_plan.itin_title: Landes Road Trip: Atlantic Coast, Pine Forests & Gascon Heritage

visits.text: Before plunging into the gorge, stop at the Arbois viewpoint, accessible from the D531. You'll be looking down into the Bourne canyon from a purpose-built platform—300 meters of empty space below your feet. The gorge was carved 3 to 4 million years ago, then widened by successive glaciers. The white, compact Urgonian limestone creates these strikingly vertical cliffs.

Stop in Pont-en-Royans before entering the gorge. Its suspended houses clinging to the cliff face above the Bourne, as if by miracle, are a striking sight. The village is small—20 minutes is enough to look around, but take time to photograph the facades from the bridge.

If you enjoy the underground world, detour to the Choranche caves. The fistulous stalactites—hollow calcite tubes that are long and thin as straws—are among the finest in Europe. The guided tour lasts about an hour through a landscape of underground lakes and translucent formations.

At the Pont de Goule Noire, stop to observe the powerful resurgence gushing from the cave—it's water from the Autrans-Méaudre plateau re-emerging here after a long underground journey. The spectacle is all the more impressive when water levels are high in spring.

activities.text: Drive the Route des Gorges de la Bourne between Pont-en-Royans and Villard-de-Lans. These 24 km carved into the cliff face required 11 years of work, completed in 1872—workers suspended in mid-air literally sculpted the roadway into the cliff. Every bend reveals a new tableau: monumental cliffs, tunnels cut through the rock, waterfalls, and the Bourne snaking below. The road has a 3-star Michelin rating. Warning: narrow, winding mountain road—adjust your speed and avoid peak hours in summer.

In late afternoon at Villard-de-Lans, take a short hike on the plateau. The alpine pastures and lapiaz (rock surfaces sculpted by erosion) give the landscape a lunar quality. The village itself has been a climatic health resort since the 1920s, with a pleasant center for drinks on a terrace.

days_plan.itin_title: Royans-Vercors Road Trip: Hilltop Villages and Gorges
From the Alps to the Ardèche Gorges: Lakes, Peaks and Mediterranean Hills

visits.text: Villars sits on an isolated hillock between the Vaucluse Mountains and the Grand Luberon, 8 km from Apt. The village's establishment dates back to the late 11th century; a 12th or 13th-century square defensive tower formed the core of the castrum around which it developed. The commune's territory includes twelve hamlets corresponding to the old Gallo-Roman villæ. It's a characterful village that has preserved its authenticity: fountain, shaded squares, and typically Provençal stone houses.

The old center preserves traces of the fortified town—arched passages and narrow lanes—and some fine 16th and 17th-century houses. The Château Saint-Pons wine estate in Villars offers a marked vineyard trail around its parcels in the AOC Ventoux appellation.

activities.text: Rural walk toward the hillsides and dry-stone walls—shepherds' shelters and bories dot the paths around the village. The local economy still cultivates cherry trees (cœurs de pigeon and griottes), apricots and lavender: the landscapes change with the seasons.

Photography route at sunset over the Claparèdes plateau—the raking light on the garrigue and lavandin fields is particularly beautiful in June-July. The plateau overlooks the Calavon valley and extends toward the Colorado Provençal de Rustrel, 5 km away.

days_plan.itin_title: The 15 Most Beautiful Villages of the Luberon

visits.text: The Bird Park of Villars-les-Dombes brings together over 3,000 birds of 300 different species in a green setting of 35 hectares. Immersive aviaries allow close encounters with the rarest species.
Walk the interpretation trail at Chapelier Pond where two ornithological observatories offer prime viewpoints for watching the wild birdlife of the Dombes.
Notre-Dame des Dombes Abbey stands with its Cistercian buildings amid the ponds. The monks' footpath invites a meditative stroll through this landscape of water and sky.
Discover the educational farm where farmyard animals mingle with cows and horses. Children participate in daily care and bread-making workshops.
The land of a thousand ponds spreads its water mirrors as far as the eye can see. These artificial bodies of water dug by monks in the Middle Ages shape a landscape unique in France.

activities.text: Rent bikes and ride the Fairy of a Thousand Ponds circuit, winding between bodies of water where clouds are reflected.
Take off in a hot air balloon to contemplate from the sky this aquatic checkerboard dotted with farms with red-tiled roofs.
Try canicross hiking with sled dogs who guide you along the paths bordering the ponds.
Horse-drawn carriages offer guided tours through the Dombes, set to the tranquil pace of Percheron horses.

days_plan.itin_title: Grand Tour of the French Alps: From the Thousand Ponds to Mont Blanc

visits.text: The Château de Villebois-Lavalette crowns its hilltop with seven round towers visible for miles. Medieval fortress reworked during the Renaissance, two-storey Romanesque chapel. Guided or self-guided tour with booklet.
The 17th-century covered market hall stands against the castle walls. Chestnut timber frame, stone pillars. On Saturday mornings, the market comes alive: cheeses, poultry, garden vegetables.
The village retains some fine old houses, a restored Romanesque church, and an end-of-the-world feel. At the far edge of the Charente, the Périgord is only a few kilometres away.

activities.text: Guided tour of the Château de Villebois-Lavalette. The association's guides bring history to life: two-storey Romanesque chapel (pilgrims below, lords above), medieval towers, 17th-century residence. Family escape games available.
Saturday morning market. Under the 17th-century hall, local producers and village atmosphere. Goat's cheese, free-range chickens, seasonal vegetables, honey. A real country market.
Hike around Villebois. Waymarked trails through wooded hills. Mixed-farming landscapes, isolated farms, views of the castle. Several loops from 5 to 12 km.
Drive back to Angoulême on back roads. Through southern Charente: vineyards, honey-stone villages, rolling hills. 40 km in about an hour at a gentle pace.

days_plan.itin_title: Charente Circuit: Cognac and Romanesque Heritage in 9 Days

visits.text: Villecroze comes down first to an exceptional natural site: a tufa cliff carved by water for 700,000 years, in which troglodyte caves have been occupied since prehistoric times. Benedictine monks from the Abbey of Saint-Victor in Marseille took refuge here during Saracen raids in the 10th century. In the second half of the 16th century, the Lord of Villecroze transformed them into a stronghold, piercing mullioned windows and arrow slits — they have belonged to the commune since 1633 and are classified as a Natural Monument of artistic character. The guided tour (reservation required) covers 5 levels, passes behind the waterfall and reveals limestone columns formed by seeping waters. Warning: closed-toe shoes required, accessible to children 6 and up.

The 2-hectare municipal park at the cliff's base hosts the 35-meter waterfall descending from Tourtour — its spring, rich in limestone, helped form the tufa cliff from which the caves hang. The park was created in the 1930s and shelters many Mediterranean species: cedars, fig trees, olive trees, palms, magnolias. It's one of the coolest spots in the Haut-Var in summer. After the waterfall and caves, stroll through the old medieval village: its arcades, seven fountains and flower-filled lanes reveal an authentic atmosphere.

The 11th-century Chapel of Saint-Victor, south of the village, hosts the Villecroze Music Academy. High-quality free concerts are given here in summer — check dates with the tourist office; some are held in the evening in the chapel or in the open-air theater with its sculpted cypresses.

activities.text: On Thursday morning, the Villecroze market sets up under the plane trees on the main square. Local producers of black truffle (in season, November through March), olive oil, saffron, honey and goat cheese — all the flavors of the Haut-Var are represented. Don't leave without a piece of fresh goat cheese.

days_plan.itin_title: The 10 Most Beautiful Villages of the Haut-Var

places.name: Villedieu Les Poeles

visits.text: Cornille Havard Foundry perpetuates nine centuries of bell-making tradition. The listed 19th-century workshop produces over a hundred monumental bells annually. This is where the bells installed in Notre-Dame Cathedral during its 2013 restoration were cast, their voices now ringing across Paris.

Step into the Copper Workshop, France's last atelier to work copper using traditional coppersmithing and boilermaking techniques. Craftsmen shape saucepans, jam-making basins, and decorative objects with hammers, just as the 'Sourdins' did centuries ago—a nickname earned because the metalworkers were deafened by the constant clangor.

The Knights of Malta Commandery recalls Villedieu's five centuries as an independent enclave of the Order of Saint John of Jerusalem. The interior courtyard, medieval buildings, and herb garden recreate the atmosphere of this 12th-century commandery.

Enter the Cour du Foyer, one of many interior courtyards that give the town its character. These passages between granite houses once sheltered copper craftsmen's workshops. About twenty such courts survive, connected by a maze of lanes.

Notre-Dame Church of Villedieu blends Romanesque and Gothic styles. Its 15th-century porch and octagonal bell tower dominate the town. Inside, the carved wooden pulpit and stained glass windows illustrate the town's religious history.

Villedieu bears the 'City and Crafts of Art' label. Beyond copper and bells, the House of Pewter demonstrates the working of this metal using ancestral methods: casting, turning, chasing. The pieces produced here are unique creations.

activities.text: Wander the old town lanes following the courts and workshops circuit. Each vaulted passage reveals a cobbled courtyard where copper once rang against anvils from dawn to dusk.
Villedieu's market runs every Tuesday morning on place de la République. Norman farm products, farmhouse cider, Villedieu andouille sausage, and local cheeses fill the stalls.

Witness a bronze pour at Cornille Havard foundry. Guided tours let you follow every stage of bell-making: from the earthen mold to the final unveiling, when the bell emerges from its centuries-old cocoon.

days_plan.itin_title: Normandy's Classic Coast: Étretat Cliffs and Bocage Countryside Villages
Normandy's Bay of Mont-Saint-Michel: Coastal Drives & the Sacred Abbey
Manche Road Trip: Cotentin Peninsula & Mont-Saint-Michel

map_keywords: Commanderie des Chevaliers de Malte Villedieu-les-Poêles

visits.text: Lake Villefort's 127 hectares nestle in the Cévenol mountains, born from a dam built between 1956 and 1964 on the Bayard valley. Its Blue Flag-certified waters are bordered by developed beaches and an 8.5-km loop trail.

The Château de Castanet, a 16th-century historic monument, was preserved during the dam's flooding. Its name comes from the Occitan "castan" (chestnut tree). Open for self-guided visits in summer, with temporary exhibitions in the vaulted rooms.

La Garde-Guérin, listed among France's Most Beautiful Villages, is a medieval castrum perched at 900 meters elevation. Founded in the 12th century by the Bishop of Mende, this fortified settlement protected travelers on the Voie Régordane. Its cobbled streets, lined with mullioned-window houses and slate roofs, remain intact.

Climb the five levels of La Garde-Guérin's keep, a 12th-century watchtower standing 21.5 meters high. From the summit, your gaze plunges into the Chassezac gorges 400 meters below and extends across the Vivarais and Gévaudan uplands.

The Chassezac gorges, a 7-km granite canyon, cut 400 meters below the plateau. From the viewpoint accessible by trail, the view into the sheer walls is breathtaking. Locals call it "the Devil's lair."

activities.text: Swimming and water sports on Lake Villefort: sailing, windsurfing, canoe-kayak, pedal boats. Lifeguarded beach in July-August.
Villefort's via ferrata unfolds on cliffs above the lake. Two routes (easy and difficult) link ziplines and suspension bridges, with the lake as backdrop.
Fishing on Lake Villefort: brown trout, rainbow trout, and perch populate its waters. Accessible banks and calm zones make it a prized mountain fishing spot.

days_plan.itin_title: Lozère Circuit: Tarn Gorges and Cévennes

visits.text: UNESCO-listed Villefranche-de-Conflent unfolds its pink marble ramparts dating from the 11th century. Vauban created a unique double covered walkway with bastions and fortified gates.

Fort Libéria towers 180 meters above the town. Built by Vauban in 1681, it's reached via an underground staircase of 734 steps. A garrison of 100 men, chapel, powder magazines, and dungeons unfold in succession.
The Canalettes Caves reveal limestone draperies, eccentric formations, and millennial concretions. From the Cupules corridor to the White Room, water has sculpted wonders.
The Church of Saint James preserves its 11th-century Romanesque architecture. Sculpted portal and capitals testify to Catalan Romanesque art.

activities.text: Board the Yellow Train. This narrow-gauge line climbs to 1,600 meters altitude through bridges, tunnels, and spectacular Pyrenean landscapes.
Stroll through the cobbled streets. Pink marble facades, artisan shops, and café terraces create a medieval atmosphere.
Hike toward the Canigou massif. Several trails start from Villefranche toward the peaks and high-altitude lakes.

days_plan.itin_title: Pyrénées-Orientales Road Trip: French Catalonia and the Vermilion Coast

visits.text: The Citadelle Saint-Elme has guarded the roadstead entrance since 1557, commissioned by Duke Emanuel Philibert of Savoy to defend his duchy against Barbary raids. Cross the drawbridge: the five-pointed star fortress houses shaded gardens, small municipal museums and the town hall today. The Italian-style military architecture, with its thick walls and moats, is remarkably well preserved.

The Chapelle Saint-Pierre, known as the Cocteau Chapel, is one of Jean Cocteau's major works on the French Riviera. This Parisian poet, filmmaker and artist had stayed for years at the Hôtel Welcome facing the chapel — he undertook its decoration in June 1956 as a gesture of friendship toward the fishermen who had entrusted him with the building. The decor was inaugurated on June 30, 1957, with Charlie Chaplin in attendance. Five fresco panels illustrate the life of Saint Peter, the gypsies of Saintes-Maries and the demoiselles of Villefranche. Listed as a historic monument in 1996.

The Rue Obscure is one of the French Riviera's most singular passages: 130 meters of 13th-century vaulted gallery plunged in darkness, running along the coast beneath the old town's houses. This former patrol path served as a shelter for residents during World War II bombardments. Enter from Place du Conseil to experience the atmosphere.

The Église Saint-Michel, perched atop the old town's stairs, unfolds its 17th-century Baroque splendor beneath imposing vaults. The historic organ in the gallery dates from the 18th century. Have a look at the polychrome wooden recumbent Christ — a remarkable piece of Niçoise Baroque.

Continue to the Darse, the former military port of the Kings of Sardinia: the quiet basin where remnants of the rope factory remain contrasts with the bustle of the marina. It's a calm spot, often overlooked by tourists, ideal for watching sailboats and traditional pointus.

activities.text: Spread your towel on Plage des Marinières: fine sand and calm waters, sheltered by the natural roadstead — one of the deepest in the Mediterranean at 55 meters. The view of the Saint-Jean-Cap-Ferrat peninsula is superb. Arrive on a weekday if possible; weekends fill up quickly.

Settle on a terrace along Quai de l'Amiral Courbet: pink and orange 18th-century facades reflect in the water, fishing boats return in the morning with their catch, cafe terraces come alive in the evening. This is classic Villefranche — cartoon-like and utterly real at once.

Walk the ramparts along the Chemin de Ronde: the walls plunge straight into the sea, the view of the roadstead is striking, and late-afternoon light transforms the facades into painting. Allow 20 minutes for a leisurely walk.

The Villefranche roadstead is one of the deepest in the Mediterranean: the seafloor attracts divers and freedivers, with often excellent visibility. Operators offer diving baptisms departing from the port — a memorable experience in this sheltered roadstead.

Drive up to the Basse Corniche for panoramas: the view of Villefranche roadstead and the Saint-Jean-Cap-Ferrat peninsula, framed between hills and blue sky, is the emblematic French Riviera postcard. If you have time, continue to the Moyenne Corniche which climbs even higher.

days_plan.itin_title: Alpes-Maritimes Road Trip: From the French Riviera to Perched Villages
The 15 Jewels of the French Riviera and Its Hinterland

visits.text: Villefranche-sur-Saône, economic capital of Beaujolais, charms with its Rue Nationale lined with Renaissance facades and inner courtyards. Founded in the 12th century by the Lords of Beaujeu, the town prospered through trade and viticulture. The arcaded houses, sculpted corbels, and hidden courtyards testify to past wealth. The 500-meter-long main street concentrates architectural treasures accumulated over centuries.

Notre-Dame des Marais Collegiate Church stands out with its impressive facade and 55-meter-high bell tower dominating the city. Built from the 13th to 15th centuries, it showcases the evolution of Gothic style. The ribbed vaults, stained glass windows, and carved capitals create a majestic interior space. The recently restored organ ranks among the finest in the region.

The Municipal Museum Paul Dini houses an exceptional collection of modern and contemporary art. Works by Renoir, Dufy, Picasso, and local artists illustrate artistic movements of the 20th century. Temporary exhibitions showcase emerging artists and current trends. The building itself, a beautifully restored 18th-century mansion, is worth the visit.

The Covered Market, built in the 19th century, buzzes with activity every morning except Monday. The metal and glass structure shelters stalls overflowing with local products: Beaujolais wines, regional charcuterie, mountain cheeses, seasonal vegetables. Exchanges between producers and customers perpetuate the tradition of direct sales. The festive atmosphere and authentic smells create a total immersion in Beaujolais gastronomy.

activities.text: Explore the inner courtyards hidden behind Renaissance facades of Rue Nationale. These secret spaces, once economic heart of the city, reveal spiral staircases, sculpted galleries, and ancient wells. Some courtyards have been magnificently restored and now house artisan shops and artist studios. Guided tours reveal the history of merchant families who built their fortunes here.

Stroll along the Saône on the newly developed waterfront. The shaded promenade offers views of the river and surrounding hills. Play areas, picnic areas, and flowered gardens invite rest. Swans and ducks enliven the peaceful waters. The path connects to walking trails extending along the riverbanks.

Visit during the Beaujolais Nouveau festival in November, when the city becomes the epicenter of worldwide celebrations. Producers set up stands offering tastings of the new vintage. Concerts, wine-related games, and fireworks animate the streets. The festive atmosphere attracts thousands of visitors who share a glass around street braziers.

Take a walking tour of street art that has transformed many facades in recent years. Local and international artists created murals depicting Beaujolais history, wine culture, and urban life. The works, scattered throughout the city, offer a contemporary artistic journey contrasting with Renaissance heritage. Maps available at the tourist office guide visitors through this open-air gallery.

days_plan.itin_title: Ain Road Trip: Bresse Plains and Bugey Mountains
Rhône Road Trip: Lyon, Golden Stone Villages of Beaujolais & Condrieu Vineyards

visits.text: Gustave Roussy is Europe's leading cancer center and in the global top 5 — 50,000 patients per year, including 3,500 children. It all began in 1921 when Professor Gustave Roussy created France's very first consultation dedicated to cancer patients at the neighboring Paul-Brousse hospital. In 1925, the Seine departmental council created the regional center in Villejuif. The campus is still there, never relocated, now surrounded by a biocluster bringing together 5 hospitals, 5 universities, and 48 research teams. It's not a museum — but passing by reminds you that this ordinary suburb houses an institution treating cancers no one else knows how to treat.

The Saint-Cyr-Sainte-Julitte church is a historic monument with parts dating back to the 12th century. Romanesque bell tower, Gothic additions — the church recalls that Villejuif was already a village before hospitals and laboratories took it over in the 20th century.
Parc Paul Hochart covers 26 hectares — the main green space in one of Val-de-Marne's densest towns. Lawns, ponds, paths: a 45-minute walk is enough to make the circuit.

activities.text: The shopping streets around Léo Lagrange metro station concentrate groceries, restaurants, and markets reflecting Villejuif's strong cultural diversity — communities from sub-Saharan Africa, North Africa, Asia. Easy culinary change of scenery 15 minutes from central Paris.

For the curious: the Campus Grand Parc under construction around Gustave Roussy aims to become the world's first biocluster dedicated to oncology. The surroundings are freely accessible — you can see how a neighborhood transforms around a scientific institution.

days_plan.itin_title: Discovering Val-de-Marne in 10 Days
Val-de-Marne Road Trip: Castles and River Marne Banks

visits.text: On the banks of the young Seine, Villenauxe-la-Grande represents authentic rural Champagne. This peaceful agricultural town has preserved its parish church, stone washhouses and traditional houses that witness a life paced by seasons and field work. The modest but informative Heritage House traces local history and Champagne traditions — viticulture, livestock farming, crafts. Restful stop in preserved countryside.

activities.text: Seine riverbanks arranged as shaded promenade: flat paths following river meanders through wet meadows, willow groves and rustling reeds. Anglers find quiet spots to catch pike, zander and carp in fish-rich waters. Ideal family route for picnics, wildlife watching (grey herons, moorhens, kingfishers) and waterside flora. Absolute rural calm, far from tourist bustle.

days_plan.itin_title: Aube Circuit: Champagne, Medieval Heritage and Orient Forest Lakes

visits.text: LaM (Lille Métropole Museum of Modern, Contemporary and Outsider Art) houses over 8,000 works by Braque, Picasso, Modigliani, Klee and Miró. Its outsider art collection, France's largest, comes from the donation of the L'Aracine association.

LaM's sculpture park spreads across 23,000 m² of greenery. You'll encounter a monumental mobile by Alexander Calder, a sculpture by Picasso, and works by Richard Deacon, nestled among trees and lawns.
The Château de Flers, a 17th-century Flemish manor surrounded by moats, houses the tourism office. Its brick and stone façade sits within a peaceful small park where ducks patrol.
The Asnapio archaeological park recreates outdoor habitats from the Neolithic to the Middle Ages using period techniques: Gallic houses, Gallo-Roman farms and medieval constructions.
The Windmill Museum, set beside the chain of lakes, traces the history of water and windmills in the Flemish region through models, tools and archival documents.

activities.text: Circle Lac du Héron on foot or by bike: 4.6 km of easy trails through a 110-hectare regional nature reserve. Grey herons, grebes and kingfishers inhabit the shores.
The Forum of Sciences features a planetarium and interactive exhibitions on science and nature. Shows under the planetarium dome last about 45 minutes.
Visit the Héron farm beside the park to discover its heritage orchard: over 700 varieties of regional apples, 450 pear varieties, and educational beehives.

days_plan.itin_title: Northern France Circuit: Lille, Belfries and Flemish Heritage

places.name: Villeneuve Les Avignon

visits.text: Fort Saint-André crowns Mont Andaon facing Avignon. This 14th-century royal fortress marked the border between the Kingdom of France and papal lands. Its twin towers frame a fortified gate. From the ramparts, the panorama covers the Palais des Papes, Mont Ventoux, and the Alpilles.

Step through the gates of the Chartreuse du Val de Bénédiction, France's largest charterhouse. Founded in 1352 by Pope Innocent VI, it sprawls across five acres with three cloisters, Matteo Giovannetti frescoes in the chapel, and the founding pope's mausoleum.
The Tour Philippe-le-Bel guarded the western end of Avignon's bridge on the French side. Climb its spiral staircase for a commanding view of the Rhône and Avignon's rooftops.

The gardens of Abbaye Saint-André, landscaped atop Mont Andaon, blend Italian-style terraces, centuries-old olive trees, and rose gardens. The Romanesque chapel ruins offer a silent setting facing the papal palace.
The Collégiale Notre-Dame houses a 14th-century ivory altarpiece and a copy of Enguerrand Quarton's Pietà, a masterpiece of medieval Provençal painting.

activities.text: Follow the path connecting Fort Saint-André to Tour Philippe-le-Bel along the ramparts. The walk winds through quiet medieval lanes with views of both banks of the Rhône.
In summer, the Chartreuse hosts artist residencies and performances in its cloisters. Check the schedule for the Centre National des Écritures du Spectacle based there.
Settle onto a terrace in Villeneuve's heights to watch the sunset over Avignon. The low-angled light sets the Palais des Papes' golden stone ablaze.

days_plan.itin_title: Vaucluse: Avignon, Luberon Villages & Ventoux Routes

map_keywords: Chartreuse du Val de Bénédiction Villeneuve-lès-Avignon

visits.text: The Pont Vieux (or Pont des Cieutat) dates from 1282. Its irregular arches have spanned the Lot River for over seven centuries, once defended by three fortified towers.
The Chapelle Notre-Dame-du-Bout-du-Pont in rosy brick welcomed river boatmen. This pilgrimage chapel stands sentinel at the medieval bridge entrance.
Place Lafayette forms the heart of this bastide founded in 1264. Its covered arcades shelter cafés and shops around the market hall.
The Porte de Paris and Porte de Pujols are twin square towers that guarded the bastide's entrance, complete with portcullises and machicolations.
The Romano-Byzantine Église Sainte-Catherine consecrated in 1937 preserves splendid 14th- and 15th-century stained glass from the earlier church.
Admire the half-timbered houses in the old center. Their overhanging upper floors gained space without increasing property taxes.

activities.text: Visit the Place Lafayette market to sample Agen prunes, farmhouse cheeses, and seasonal vegetables.
Head to the waterfront activity center to rent a canoe, pedal boat, or electric boat on the Lot River.
Walk the Lot riverbanks along the towpath. These trails once served to haul barges loaded with wine and timber.

days_plan.itin_title: Lot-et-Garonne Road Trip: Medieval Bastides & Villages

visits.text: Basilica of the Sacred Heart (Romanesque).
Chapel of the Visitation.
Tower of Charles the Bold.

activities.text: Roman gates.
Bonaparte High School.

days_plan.itin_title: Saône Valley, France: Medieval Abbeys, Wine and Comtois Heritage

visits.text: The Canadian National Memorial at Vimy Ridge rises from the ridge captured in April 1917. Two 27-meter pylons bear the names of 11,285 Canadian soldiers who vanished in France with no known grave.
Walk through reconstructed trenches in their original 1917 positions. At some points, Canadian and German lines were separated by a mere 25 meters of no man's land.
Descend into the Grange Tunnel, one of 14 underground passages dug before the battle. The longest stretched 1,700 meters through solid chalk.
Notre-Dame-de-Lorette necropolis crowns a 165-meter hill as France's largest military cemetery. More than 40,000 soldiers rest in these fields.
The Ring of Memory forms a 345-meter ellipse. On 500 golden panels, 580,000 names of soldiers from all nations are inscribed in strict alphabetical order—no rank, no nationality, just names.

activities.text: Visit the Souchez interpretation center to search the soldiers' database. Each file records name, birth date, recruitment office, and burial location.
Vimy Memorial Park covers 107 hectares of undulating ground. The crater-pocked earth preserves shell scars, and sections remain off-limits due to unexploded ordnance.
Explore the ruins of Mont-Saint-Éloi Abbey. Its two 44-meter towers, shattered by 1914–18 bombardment, command views across the plain toward Vimy and Lorette.

days_plan.itin_title: Pas-de-Calais Road Trip: Opal Coast & Historic Belfries

visits.text: Vincennes keep stands 52 meters tall — the tallest royal keep in Europe still standing. Charles V had it built in 10 years starting in 1362, during the Hundred Years' War, with walls 3.25 meters thick. He was born here, in this very fortress, on January 21, 1338 — and made it his government headquarters. Later, the tower became a state prison: Fouquet was arrested there by d'Artagnan in 1661, Diderot wrote there, Mirabeau was locked up from 1777 to 1780 at his own father's request, the Marquis de Sade served two stints. Pencil graffiti left by prisoners of war during World War II are still visible on the walls — look for them. Climb to the terrace: Paris on one side, the woods on the other. Allow 2 hours for the keep and the Gothic Sainte-Chapelle, begun in 1379 and completed only in 1552.

The Bois de Vincennes covers 995 hectares — twice the size of the Bois de Boulogne. Its four lakes, riding trails, and lawns make it Paris's largest green space. The zoo, completely redesigned in 2014, abandoned old cages for "biozones" replicating natural habitats across 14.5 hectares — allow half a day if you go in.

activities.text: At Lac Daumesnil, vendors rent rowboats by the day for navigating between Île de Reuilly and Île de Bercy. On Sundays, the island kiosks draw families. It's one of the rare places in Île-de-France where you can row outdoors 13 km from Notre-Dame.

Inside the castle grounds, spot the royal courtyard built by Louis Le Vau starting in 1654 for Louis XIV — the symmetrical King's and Queen's pavilions foreshadow Versailles style. Construction was completed just in time for Louis XIV and Marie-Thérèse of Austria to stop there on June 19, 1661, returning from their wedding in Saint-Jean-de-Luz.

days_plan.itin_title: Vosges Mountains, France: Alpine Lakes, Spa Towns and Forest Heritage

visits.text: Vogüé is classified among the Most Beautiful Villages of France—and you understand why arriving via the road along the Ardèche: the village rises in an amphitheater on the cliff above the river, with the castle dominating everything. The Château des Marquis de Vogüé has been inhabited by the same family since the 11th century—one of Vivarais's oldest dynasties, a member of which participated in the 1191 Siege of Acre during the Third Crusade. The castle tour (open April to November) includes the Vivarais Estates hall, the 12th-century keep with its kitchens, the chapel with stained glass by Alfred Manessier created in 1980, and the hanging garden called the marquise's garden. Allow at least an hour.

Get lost in the lower village lanes: La Tourasse, one of the first houses of the Vogüé lords, is a tower 15 meters high still standing. Rue des Puces is one of the narrowest streets in France. The medieval arcades supporting the houses give the ensemble a particular atmosphere, especially early morning when tourists haven't yet arrived.

Taking the path that climbs behind the castle, you reach in 40 minutes the Chapelle Saint-Cérice called La Gleyzette—a Romanesque chapel perched on the cliff with plunging views over the castle, village and narrow Ardèche course. The hiking trail continues to a panorama over the Auzon valley. The 1877 railway viaduct, visible below, is now converted to a greenway.

activities.text: The Via Ardèche, a greenway developed on the old railway line, links Vogüé to Gropierre over 25 km. Bike rental is available in the area. The section from Vogüé is particularly pleasant as it follows the Ardèche with viewpoints over the gorges. If you don't do the full 25 km, settle for a 5 to 6 km round trip.

Below Vogüé bridge, the beach supervised July-August attracts swimmers and canoeists. This is the first official launch point for descending the Ardèche Gorges from upstream. Local operators offer water equipment rentals.

days_plan.itin_title: The 18 Most Beautiful Villages of Ardèche

visits.text: Vouvant, listed among France's Most Beautiful Villages, is Vendée's only fortified town. Over a kilometer of ramparts encircle this medieval citadel built on a rocky promontory above the river La Mère.
Climb the 122 steps of Tour Mélusine, a circular 13th-century keep rising 36 meters high. Two vaulted domed chambers and a 360° panorama over forest and bocage reward the ascent.

The Romanesque Church of Notre-Dame-de-l'Assomption boasts a sculpted portal of exceptional richness for the Bas-Poitou region. Inside, the Lusignan chapel reportedly houses the tomb of Lord Geoffroy II.
Pass through the Poterne, the only fortified gate to survive the centuries. From there, flower-filled lanes descend toward the river between lime-washed houses and artists' studios.
Explore the Antre de Mélusine, a museum space dedicated to the legend of the fairy who was half-woman, half-serpent. According to the tale, she built Vouvant castle in a single night.

activities.text: Hike through the Mervent-Vouvant forest, Vendée's largest woodland with 5,000 hectares of oaks and beeches. The Orée de la Forêt trail starts from Place du Bail.
Descend the river La Mère by kayak or canoe. Calm waters snake between wooded cliffs and meanders that made Vouvant a strategic defensive site since the 11th century.
Choose the Bocage Loop, a family-friendly 4-km circuit departing from Place du Bail. The path crosses the village, plains, and Vendée bocage in 1h30.

days_plan.itin_title: Vendée Tour: Noirmoutier Island, Puy du Fou & Coast

visits.text: The Grange du Massacre marks where, on March 1, 1562, François de Guise and his troops attacked Protestants gathered for worship. Between 25 and 80 deaths ignited the Wars of Religion that would ravage France for decades.
The Protestant Museum occupies the former temple. Panels, display cases, and video presentations trace the Protestant community's history and the Wars of Religion through the 1598 Edict of Nantes.
The Protestant temple was inaugurated in 1889, built against the massacre barn. A commemorative plaque recalls the tragic events of 1562.
Notre-Dame church displays Romanesque architecture. The circular 19th-century market hall testifies to the town's commercial vitality.

activities.text: Explore Wassy's historic center. The former theater on Place Marie-Stuart dates from when the town became a sub-prefecture in 1790.
Head to nearby Lac des Leschères. Hiking trails and cycling paths crisscross the surrounding countryside.

days_plan.itin_title: Haute-Marne Discovery: Langres & Historic Heritage

visits.text: The 1.5-kilometer promenade borders the beach, lined with Belle Époque villas and blue-and-white beach cabins. It captures the atmosphere of early seaside bathing culture.
Wimereux preserves 300 Anglo-Norman Belle Époque villas. Bay windows, ornate balconies, colorful ceramics—each residence displays unique character.
The Opal Coast's oldest seaside resort developed after 1862 with the railway's arrival. Tramway, casino, and golf course attracted aristocracy and bourgeoisie.
Notre-Dame Church with its stained glass and architecture testifies to the resort's 19th-century prosperity.

activities.text: Head to Pointe aux Oies, bordered by dunes. On clear days, the English coast materializes offshore. This wild space is prime territory for spotting grey seals and harbor seals.
Wimereux Golf Course, created in 1901 and rebuilt after 1918, offers an 18-hole course with stunning sea views.
Practice water sports: kitesurfing, windsurfing, land yachting. The wind sweeping the coast creates ideal conditions.
Follow the Imperial Trail, a 14-kilometer loop starting from the Column of the Grande Armée. A walk between cliffs and dunes.

days_plan.itin_title: Hauts-de-France: Opal Coast Cliffs, Medieval History & Great War Sites
Pas-de-Calais Road Trip: Opal Coast & Historic Belfries

visits.text: Cap Blanc-Nez rises 134 meters in chalk cliffs above the Channel. On clear days, the English coast appears just 35 kilometers away. The Dover Patrol Monument honors 2,000 Franco-British soldiers.
Ten kilometers south, Cap Gris-Nez—45-meter clay and sandstone cliffs—marks the closest point to England. The two capes form a Grand Site de France since 2011.
Between the capes, Wissant Bay stretches 12 kilometers of fine sand. This resort is renowned for kitesurfing and windsurfing, benefiting from a venturi effect created by the capes.
Three kilometers from Cap Gris-Nez, Audresselles has kept its fishermen's houses with blue shutters and tarred foundations. Colorful flobarts—traditional beaching boats—still put to sea.
The crans—notches in the cliffs—dot the coast: Cran aux Oeufs, Cran Poulet, Cran du Noirda. These wild pebble coves reveal fossils and are perfect for shore fishing.

activities.text: The GR120 trail links both capes in 16 kilometers along the clifftops. Limestone grasslands shelter orchids, rock roses, and bitter gentian.
In Wissant, schools offer kitesurfing and stand-up paddle lessons. The immense beach at low tide lets you escape the crowds.
From the summit of Cap Blanc-Nez, watch for grey seals in the waters below. The site is also a major observation point for migratory birds in spring and autumn.

days_plan.itin_title: Hauts-de-France: Opal Coast Cliffs, Medieval History & Great War Sites
Pas-de-Calais Road Trip: Opal Coast & Historic Belfries

visits.text: Start with the Abbey Church of Saints Peter and Paul, founded around 660 by Austrasian nobles on an island in the Lauter River — an actual island, since the monastery sat between two river arms linked by a canal whose location corresponds to today's Rue Stanislas. Rebuilt in Gothic style between 1262 and 1293 under Abbot Edelin, it's the second-largest Gothic church in Alsace after Strasbourg Cathedral. Don't miss the Saint Christopher fresco covering an entire nave wall: 11 meters tall, it's the largest medieval full-figure painting in France and Germany. The Christ of Wissembourg stained glass has been moved to the Strasbourg museum, but the 12th-century Romanesque rose window depicting the Virgin and Child remains in place.

Wander through the historic center crossed by the Lauter and its canals. The 15th and 16th-century half-timbered houses line streets that quickly give the impression of straddling the Franco-German border — which isn't far from the truth, since Germany is two kilometers away. Spot the statue of King Dagobert along your way, legendary founder of the abbey according to an apocryphal 12th-century charter. Wissembourg is also the home of Hans Trapp, the Alsatian version of the Bogeyman, whose legend draws from a notorious local lord who once dammed the Lauter to drain the town.

Walk the circuit of medieval ramparts — they date from the 13th to 18th centuries and encircle the old town for several hundred meters. The tower known as Schartenturm (15th century) to the southwest and the Powder Tower (13th century) are the best preserved. From the walkway, the view over Alsatian rooftops and the Lauter winding below is worth the climb.

activities.text: Follow the pedestrian loop along ramparts and canals — about 3 to 4 kilometers of easy walking that covers the essentials of the old town. If you're there in the morning, local bakers offer Bretzel and rye bread before 8 a.m. Plan an hour for a leisurely stroll.

Climb the wooded heights northwest of town for panoramic views of the Palatinate Forest on the German side and Alsatian vineyards on the French side. Marked trails from the Club Vosgien depart directly from the town center and lead to several viewpoints in under an hour.

places.name: Yenne

visits.text: Yenne is a medieval town built between the Rhône and the Dent du Chat peak. A marked trail with 29 points of interest winds through the historic center: the Chambéry Gate, Novalaise Gate, salt granary, and noble houses from the 15th-18th centuries reveal layers of history in weathered stone.
Step inside Yenne's church, a listed Historic Monument. Its baroque furnishings blend with older elements in a hushed atmosphere that invites contemplation.

Follow the Balcon de Yenne trail—part of the Santiago de Compostela pilgrimage route—up to the Notre-Dame de la Montagne viewpoint. The vista plunges into the Pierre-Châtel gorge, a protected natural site where the Rhône squeezes between cliffs at the border of Ain and Savoie.

The Pierre-Châtel gorge shelters ruins of a 13th-century charterhouse that became a military fortress in the 1800s. From the trail's second viewpoint, you can clearly make out the structure nestled in the canyon below.

Head to the Jongieux-Marestel hillsides, a vineyard classified as national heritage for its scenic beauty. Terraced vines overlook the Rhône. The slopes produce Roussette de Savoie (Altesse grape) and Mondeuse, a bold red wine.

activities.text: Balcon de Yenne hike: the trail climbs through boxwood forest to three successive viewpoints over the Rhône and Pierre-Châtel gorge. Return via the hamlets of Chevru and Curtelod.
Wine tasting in the Jongieux cellars. Marestel vintages and Mondeuse reds are sampled at local producers' estates, set between manicured slopes and Lake Bourget panoramas.
Canoe-kayak on the wild upper Rhône at the foot of the Pierre-Châtel gorge. Whitewater threads through a landscape of cliffs and forests, ranked among the region's most acclaimed paddling routes.

days_plan.itin_title: Savoie from Lakes to Glaciers: Yenne to Haute-Maurienne

map_keywords: Belvédère Notre-Dame de la Montagne Yenne

places.name: Yvoire

visits.text: Start in Chamonix, nestled at the foot of Mont Blanc. The town hosted the first Winter Olympics in 1924 and boasts one of France's oldest tourist offices, established in 1907. Despite the crowds, the town center retains its alpine village charm—the wooden and stone facades along Rue du Docteur-Paccard are worth a look. If you arrive in the morning, make the most of it: after 10am, tour buses roll in and the atmosphere shifts dramatically.

Yvoire is best explored on foot—cars must stay in the parking areas at the village entrance. Founded in 1306 by Count Amadeus V of Savoy as a fortified stronghold on Lake Geneva, this walled town has preserved its ramparts, two fortified gates, and its castle (privately owned, not open to visitors). Look up at the onion-domed bell tower of Saint-Pancrace Church, clad in stainless steel with a weathercock covered in gold leaf—it's typical of Savoyard architecture. Fun fact: in 1844, writer Alfred de Bougy described Yvoire as the most execrable village imaginable—the turnaround has been total.

Don't miss the Garden of the Five Senses, set within the former castle kitchen garden since 1988. This 2,500 m² botanical maze is classified as a Remarkable Garden and contains over 1,300 plant varieties organized by sensory themes—touch, smell, taste, sight, hearing. Allow a good hour to explore it properly without rushing.

Then head down to the fishermen's harbor east of the castle. It's the most photogenic corner of the village, especially late in the day when the low-angle light turns the stonework golden. From here, Lake Geneva opens toward Switzerland, with the Jura mountains forming a dramatic backdrop.

activities.text: Take the cable car up to the Aiguille du Midi—20 minutes to climb from 1,035 m to 3,842 m altitude, making it France's highest cable car. At the summit, you get 360° views spanning the French, Swiss, and Italian Alps. If you're not afraid of heights, try the Step into the Void: a glass cage suspended over 1,000 meters of thin air. Bring warm clothes even in summer—it rarely exceeds 5°C at the top. Advance booking required, allow 2-3 hours total.

Transfer from Chamonix to Yvoire along the southern shore of Lake Geneva. The road hugs the lake after Thonon-les-Bains—stop at one of the viewpoints to admire the water and the Swiss mountains across the way. Allow 1h30 drive time, more if you make photo stops.

days_plan.itin_title: Haute-Savoie Road Trip: Alpine Lakes & Mont Blanc
Grand Tour of the French Alps: From the Thousand Ponds to Mont Blanc
From the Alps to the Ardèche Gorges: Lakes, Peaks and Mediterranean Hills

map_keywords: Pas dans le Vide Aiguille du Midi

visits.text: Zellenberg is the village you visit from below — perched at 285 meters altitude on a wine-covered hill, its huddled houses stand out from afar against sky and vines. Up close, the curved streets that hug the hill's contours form a compact labyrinth where the church and summit square offer unobstructed views of Riquewihr below and the Alsace plain to the Black Forest. The village retains traces of its old medieval enclosure wall visible at several points.

From the church square, the view embraces a wine panorama that few places in Alsace equal: Riquewihr at 2 km, Hunawihr at 3 km, the Schoenenbourg and Sporen hillsides opposite. Circle the town via the alleys that follow the remnants of old moats — 30 to 40 minutes suffice for the entire village.

activities.text: Take the contour trail around the hill for a complete circuit on foot — 2 to 3 km of easy walking between the vines, with successive viewpoints over the Ribeauvillé valley to the north and Riquewihr hillsides to the south. The village's wine estates offer tastings of wines from surrounding grands crus — notably Schoenenbourg and Sporen.

places.name: Monaco

visits.text: Reach the Rock of Monaco-Ville via the historic lanes and the Saint-Martin gardens. This is where the Grimaldis took the fortress in 1297, disguised as Franciscan monks—an anecdote perhaps legendary, but which justifies the family motto: 'With God's Help.' The Notre-Dame-Immaculée Cathedral houses the tombs of the princes and Grace Kelly. The Palace esplanade offers a panorama over the principality and the harbor.

The Oceanographic Museum is one of the world's most important marine museums. Founded in 1889 by Prince Albert I—nicknamed the 'Prince of the Seas' for his 28 scientific expeditions—it was inaugurated in 1910. Its 85-meter-high building is constructed on the cliff face in neo-Baroque style. Jacques Cousteau was its director from 1957 to 1988. Its aquariums house more than 6,000 specimens of 350 species. Allow at least 1.5 hours.

Casino Square and the Monte-Carlo Casino: Belle Époque masterpiece designed by Charles Garnier (the architect of the Paris Opera) and inaugurated in 1863. The facades and vestibule can be visited freely. The grand gaming hall is accessible in the evening (ID required, smart dress code). Even if you don't enter, the square deserves a stop for its gardens and palaces.

activities.text: Stroll along Port Hercule: view the yachts (some exceed 100 meters) and spot the Formula 1 circuit that runs through the city streets every May. The track passes by the casino corner and the tunnel under the rock—one of the rare urban circuits where speed reaches 290 km/h.
Exotic Gardens and the Observatory Cave: the garden perched 100 meters above the city offers a 360° view over the principality. The adjacent prehistoric cave has yielded bones 200,000 years old.
